topics.title: Fuels - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuels - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of combustion engineering and energy systems, a **fuel** is defined as a chemical substance (containing primarily carbon, hydrogen, and in some cases oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen) that releases energy in the form of heat and light upon undergoing a chemical reaction (combustion) with an oxidizer, typically atmospheric oxygen. This released thermal energy is then converted into mechanical work, electrical energy, or used directly for domestic and industrial heating applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):**
> *"A fuel is a combustible substance which on proper combustion releases a substantial amount of heat, and this heat can be used economically for domestic and industrial purposes."*

### 1.2 Classification — The Three Primary Axes

Fuels are systematically classified under the KTU 2024 syllabus along **three independent axes**:

**Axis 1: Occurrence (Origin)**
- **Primary (Natural) Fuels** — Found in nature without human processing (e.g., coal, crude oil, natural gas, wood).
- **Secondary (Derived/Artificial) Fuels** — Manufactured from primary fuels through physical or chemical transformation (e.g., gasoline, diesel, LPG, coke, biogas).

**Axis 2: Physical State (Phase at Standard Conditions)**
- **Solid Fuels** — Coal, coke, wood, bagasse, charcoal, biomass briquettes.
- **Liquid Fuels** — Petroleum products, alcohols, biodiesel, shale oil.
- **Gaseous Fuels** — Natural gas, LPG, biogas, producer gas, hydrogen.

**Axis 3: Calorific Value (Energy Density)**
- **High Calorific Value (HCV) Fuels** — Energy content typically $> 25 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (e.g., gasoline, diesel, hydrogen).
- **Medium Calorific Value (MCV) Fuels** — Energy content between $10$–$25 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (e.g., coal, wood, alcohols).
- **Low Calorific Value (LCV) Fuels** — Energy content $< 10 \text{ MJ/kg}$ (e.g., producer gas, blast furnace gas).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU examiner frequently tests the **physical state classification** along with **3–4 example fuels** for each category. Always pair every example with its correct phase.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Energy Wallet" Concept

Imagine energy as **money** stored in a wallet. A **fuel** is essentially a *portable energy wallet* that you can "open" (combust) whenever you need to spend the energy inside it. **Solid fuels** are like heavy coins — bulky, slow to open (slow combustion rate), and hard to transport efficiently. **Liquid fuels** are like paper currency — more compact, faster to use, and easier to carry. **Gaseous fuels** are like digital money — instantly accessible, leave no ash, but require special containers (cylinders, pipelines) for storage. Just as a merchant prefers digital payments for speed, an engineer often prefers gaseous fuels for clean, controlled, and rapid energy release.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Classification Logic

The classification is **not arbitrary**; it follows from three fundamental engineering requirements:

1. **Storage & Transport Efficiency** — A fuel's physical state directly dictates its volumetric energy density, ease of pumping, and pipeline compatibility.
2. **Combustion Kinetics** — Solid fuels burn via heterogeneous surface reactions (slow); liquids via droplet vaporization then gas-phase reaction (moderate); gases via direct homogeneous reaction (fast). This dictates equipment design.
3. **End-Use Suitability** — Domestic cooking demands solid/gaseous fuels; IC engines require volatile liquid/gaseous fuels; rocket propulsion requires cryogenic gases or high-density liquids.

### 2.2 Comparative Analysis — Solid vs Liquid vs Gaseous Fuels

**Solid Fuels**
- *Advantages:* Cheap, abundant, easy to store long-term, no leakage.
- *Disadvantages:* Low calorific value per unit mass, high ash content, slow combustion, large storage footprint, non-uniform composition, cause air pollution (particulates).
- *Examples:* Coal (anthracite, bituminous, lignite), coke, firewood, bagasse, charcoal.

**Liquid Fuels**
- *Advantages:* High calorific value, clean combustion, easy to transport via pipelines/tankers, precise metering possible, occupy less volume.
- *Disadvantages:* Costly refining, fire hazard, require sealed storage, can leak.
- *Examples:* Petrol (gasoline), diesel, kerosene, fuel oil, ethanol, methanol, biodiesel.

**Gaseous Fuels**
- *Advantages:* Very high combustion rate, complete combustion with no smoke, easy to ignite, can be supplied via pipelines, automatic temperature control possible.
- *Disadvantages:* Highly inflammable (leakage risk), require pressurized cylinders or pipeline networks, low calorific value per unit volume (except LPG/CNG).
- *Examples:* Natural gas (methane-based), LPG (propane/butane), biogas, producer gas, town gas, hydrogen.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gross Calorific Value (Dulong) | $GCV = \dfrac{33800\,C + 144000\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9419\,S}{1000}$ | $\text{MJ/kg}$ | $C,H,O,S$ are mass fractions |
| Net Calorific Value | $NCV = GCV - 9\,H \times \dfrac{2.418}{1000}$ | $\text{MJ/kg}$ | Subtraction accounts for latent heat of water |
| Relation between GCV and NCV | $NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H \times 585$ | $\text{kcal/kg}$ | Standard calorimetry form |
| Stoichiometric Air | $A_0 = \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{23.2}$ | $\text{kg air/kg fuel}$ | For complete combustion |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) | $(A/F) = \dfrac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}}$ | dimensionless | Typically $14.7$ for gasoline |
| Specific Energy (Volumetric) | $SE_v = GCV \times \rho_{\text{fuel}}$ | $\text{MJ/m}^3$ | For comparing liquids/gases |
| Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$ | dimensionless | $\phi<1$ lean, $\phi>1$ rich |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU Formula Sheet, **C, H, O, S** are always expressed as **percentage mass fractions** of the dry fuel composition. Do NOT confuse with atomic concentrations. Use the pipe `$\vert$` (LaTeX) notation for absolute values, never the markdown table pipe `$\vert$`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Coal-fired thermal plants use solid fuels for bulk baseload power; gas turbine plants use natural gas for peak-load rapid response.
- **Transportation:** Liquid fuels (diesel/gasoline) dominate due to their high energy density per unit volume, critical for vehicle range.
- **Domestic Sector:** LPG and PNG have replaced coal/kerosene in urban households due to clean combustion and convenience.
- **Rocketry:** Liquid hydrogen + liquid oxygen is used due to its extreme specific impulse and clean exhaust (water vapor).
- **Alternative Fuels Focus:** The transition from petroleum to biofuels, hydrogen, and synthetic fuels drives modern automotive and aerospace design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen/Air Requirement for a Hydrocarbon Fuel

Consider a generic fuel with ultimate analysis by mass as $\%C$, $\%H$, $\%O$, $\%S$. The complete combustion reactions per kg of fuel are:

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$

**Step 1:** Mass of oxygen required to burn 1 kg of carbon completely.

$$O_{2,\text{for C}} = \dfrac{32}{12} \times C = 2.667\,C \;\;\text{kg O}_2$$

**Step 2:** Mass of oxygen required to burn 1 kg of hydrogen completely.

$$O_{2,\text{for H}} = \dfrac{32}{4} \times H = 8.000\,H \;\;\text{kg O}_2$$

**Step 3:** Mass of oxygen required to burn 1 kg of sulfur completely.

$$O_{2,\text{for S}} = \dfrac{32}{32} \times S = 1.000\,S \;\;\text{kg O}_2$$

**Step 4:** Net oxygen required from air = total O₂ needed − oxygen already present in fuel.

$$O_{2,\text{net}} = 2.667\,C + 8.000\,H + 1.000\,S - O$$

$$O_{2,\text{net}} = \dfrac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H + S - O \;\;\text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

**Step 5:** Since air is approximately $23.2\%$ oxygen by mass, mass of air required is:

$$A_0 = \dfrac{O_{2,\text{net}}}{0.232}$$

$$A_0 = \dfrac{8C/3 + 8H + S - O}{0.232}$$

$$A_0 = \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,H + 4.35\,S - 4.35\,O}{23.2} \;\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

This is the **standard stoichiometric air formula** used universally in combustion engineering and adopted by KTU examiners.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Calorific Value Calculation (Worked Example)

**Given Fuel Ultimate Analysis (by mass):**
- Carbon ($C$) $= 84\%$
- Hydrogen ($H$) $= 10\%$
- Oxygen ($O$) $= 4\%$
- Sulfur ($S$) $= 1\%$
- Nitrogen + Ash ($N + A$) $= 1\%$ (balance)

**Step A — Calculate Gross Calorific Value (GCV) using Dulong's formula:**

$$GCV = \dfrac{33800\,C + 144000\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9419\,S}{1000} \;\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{33800 \times 0.84 + 144000 \times \left(0.10 - \dfrac{0.04}{8}\right) + 9419 \times 0.01}{1000}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{28392 + 14400 \times 0.095 + 94.19}{1000}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{28392 + 1368 + 94.19}{1000} = \dfrac{29854.19}{1000}$$

$$GCV = 29.854 \;\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step B — Calculate Net Calorific Value (NCV):**

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H \times 585 \;\;\text{kcal/kg}$$

$$NCV = 29.854 \times 238.85 - 0.09 \times 10 \times 585 \;\;\text{kcal/kg}$$

$$NCV = 7130.2 - 526.5 = 6603.7 \;\;\text{kcal/kg}$$

Converting to MJ/kg: $NCV = 6603.7 / 238.85 = 27.65 \;\text{MJ/kg}$

**Step C — Stoichiometric Air Required:**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{11.6 \times 84 + 34.8 \times 10 + 4.35 \times 1 - 4.35 \times 4}{23.2}$$

$$A_0 = \dfrac{974.4 + 348 + 4.35 - 17.4}{23.2} = \dfrac{1309.35}{23.2}$$

$$A_0 = 56.44 \;\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step D — Classification Based on Calorific Value:**

Since $GCV = 29.854 \;\text{MJ/kg} > 25 \;\text{MJ/kg}$, this fuel falls under the **High Calorific Value (HCV)** category. It is a **solid fuel** with high energy density — characteristic of a good **bituminous coal**.

### 3.3 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Fuel Class

| Application | Preferred Fuel Class | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Domestic cooking (rural) | Solid (biomass) | Cheap, locally available |
| Domestic cooking (urban) | Gaseous (LPG/PNG) | Clean, smokeless, fast |
| Spark-ignition engine | Liquid (gasoline/ethanol) | High volatility, easy vaporization |
| Compression-ignition engine | Liquid (diesel/biodiesel) | High cetane, low volatility needed |
| Industrial furnace | Solid (coke) / Gaseous (NG) | High temperature, steady heat release |
| Rocket propulsion | Liquid / Gaseous (cryo H₂) | Maximum specific impulse |
| Gas turbine (peak load) | Gaseous (natural gas) | Instant ignition, rapid load following |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Tree of Fuels

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FUELS] --> B[By Occurrence]
    A --> C[By Physical State]
    A --> D[By Calorific Value]
    A --> E[By Application]

    B --> B1[Primary / Natural]
    B --> B2[Secondary / Artificial]

    B1 --> B1A[Coal]
    B1 --> B1B[Crude Oil]
    B1 --> B1C[Natural Gas]
    B1 --> B1D[Wood / Biomass]

    B2 --> B2A[Coke from Coal]
    B2 --> B2B[Petrol / Diesel from Crude]
    B2 --> B2C[LPG from NG]
    B2 --> B2D[Producer Gas from Coke]

    C --> C1[Solid Fuels]
    C --> C2[Liquid Fuels]
    C --> C3[Gaseous Fuels]

    C1 --> C1A[Coal Anthracite Bituminous Lignite]
    C1 --> C1B[Coke Charcoal]
    C1 --> C1C[Wood Bagasse Agricultural Waste]

    C2 --> C2A[Petroleum Products: Petrol Diesel Kerosene]
    C2 --> C2B[Alcohols: Methanol Ethanol]
    C2 --> C2B2[Biodiesel]

    C3 --> C3A[Natural Gas Methane]
    C3 --> C3B[LPG Propane Butane]
    C3 --> C3C[Biogas Producer Gas]
    C3 --> C3D[Hydrogen]

    D --> D1[High CV greater than 25 MJ per kg]
    D --> D2[Medium CV 10 to 25 MJ per kg]
    D --> D3[Low CV less than 10 MJ per kg]

    D1 --> D1A[Hydrogen 142]
    D1 --> D1B[Gasoline 44]
    D1 --> D1C[Diesel 43]
    D1 --> D1D[Methane 55]

    D2 --> D2A[Coal 20 to 30]
    D2 --> D2B[Wood 16]
    D2 --> D2C[Ethanol 27]

    D3 --> D3A[Producer Gas 5 to 6]
    D3 --> D3B[Blast Furnace Gas 4]
    D3 --> D3C[Bagasse 8]

    E --> E1[Domestic: LPG Wood Kerosene]
    E --> E2[Industrial: Coal Coke Fuel Oil]
    E --> E3[Power: Coal NG Diesel]
    E --> E4[Transportation: Petrol Diesel CNG LNG]
    E --> E5[Aerospace: LH2 RP1]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fuel Classification Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[Fuel Sample] --> ANAL[Ultimate Analysis Module]
    ANAL --> C[Carbon Content]
    ANAL --> H[Hydrogen Content]
    ANAL --> O[Oxygen Content]
    ANAL --> S[Sulfur Content]

    C --> PHASE[Phase Classifier]
    H --> PHASE
    O --> PHASE
    S --> PHASE

    PHASE --> SOLID[Solid: Coal Wood Coke]
    PHASE --> LIQ[Liquid: Petrol Diesel Alcohol]
    PHASE --> GAS[Gas: NG LPG Biogas H2]

    C --> GCV[Gross Calorific Value Calculator]
    H --> GCV
    O --> GCV
    S --> GCV

    GCV --> CVCLASS[Calorific Value Classifier]
    CVCLASS --> HCV[High CV Class]
    CVCLASS --> MCV[Medium CV Class]
    CVCLASS --> LCV[Low CV Class]

    SOLID --> APP[Application Mapper]
    LIQ --> APP
    GAS --> APP

    APP --> DOM[Domestic Use]
    APP --> IND[Industrial Use]
    APP --> POW[Power Generation]
    APP --> TRA[Transportation]

    HCV --> OUT[Final Classification Report]
    MCV --> OUT
    LCV --> OUT
    DOM --> OUT
    IND --> OUT
    POW --> OUT
    TRA --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** The schematic above demonstrates how a raw fuel sample is processed through an analysis engine — first decomposed into elemental composition ($C, H, O, S$), then routed simultaneously to (i) a phase classifier and (ii) a calorific value calculator, and finally mapped to its end-use application domain. This mirrors the multi-criteria classification approach endorsed by the KTU 2024 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "fuel". Classify fuels based on their physical state, giving two examples for each class.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **fuel** is a combustible substance that releases heat energy upon reaction with an oxidizer (usually oxygen), and this heat is used economically for domestic and industrial applications. *(1 mark)*

**Classification based on physical state:**

| Class | Characteristic | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solid Fuels | Fixed shape, slow combustion, high ash | Coal, Wood *(1 mark)* |
| Liquid Fuels | Flowable, high CV, easy transport | Petrol, Diesel *(0.5 marks)* |
| Gaseous Fuels | High flame speed, clean burn | Natural Gas, LPG *(0.5 marks)* |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Distinguish between primary and secondary fuels. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Primary (Natural) Fuels** are found in nature and used directly without major processing. Examples: *Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas.* *(1.5 marks)*

**Secondary (Derived) Fuels** are produced by processing primary fuels through refining, distillation, or chemical conversion. Examples: *Petrol (from crude oil), Coke (from coal), LPG (from natural gas).* *(1.5 marks)*

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *Classify fuels in detail based on (i) physical state and (ii) occurrence. Discuss the relative merits and demerits of solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A fuel has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 80%, H = 8%, O = 6%, S = 2%, N = 2%, Ash = 2%. Calculate the (i) Gross Calorific Value using Dulong's formula, and (ii) the stoichiometric air required for complete combustion. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Classification & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Physical State Classification (3 marks):**
- **Solid:** Coal, coke, wood, bagasse, charcoal.
- **Liquid:** Petrol, diesel, kerosene, alcohol, biodiesel.
- **Gaseous:** Natural gas, LPG, biogas, producer gas, hydrogen.

**Occurrence Classification (1 mark):**
- Primary: Coal, crude oil, natural gas.
- Secondary: Petrol, coke, LPG.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Solid | Liquid | Gaseous |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Calorific Value | Low–Medium | High | High |
| Storage | Easy, bulky | Needs tank | Pressurized cylinder |
| Combustion Rate | Slow | Moderate | Very fast |
| Ash/Smoke | High | Negligible | Nil |
| Handling | Simple | Fire risk | Leakage risk |
| Cost | Cheap | Moderate | Higher |

**Merits/Demerits (in text):** Solids are cheap and abundant but bulky and polluting. Liquids have high energy density and clean burn but are costly and flammable. Gases combust cleanly with high efficiency but need specialized storage.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C = 80\%$, $H = 8\%$, $O = 6\%$, $S = 2\%$, balance $N + \text{Ash} = 4\%$.

**Step 1: GCV using Dulong's formula (3 marks):**

$$GCV = \dfrac{33800\,C + 144000\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9419\,S}{1000}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{33800 \times 0.80 + 144000 \times \left(0.08 - \dfrac{0.06}{8}\right) + 9419 \times 0.02}{1000}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{27040 + 14400 \times 0.0725 + 188.38}{1000}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{27040 + 1044 + 188.38}{1000} = \dfrac{28272.38}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{GCV = 28.272 \;\text{MJ/kg}}$$

**Step 2: Stoichiometric Air (4 marks):**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,H + 4.35\,S - 4.35\,O}{23.2}$$

$$A_0 = \dfrac{11.6 \times 80 + 34.8 \times 8 + 4.35 \times 2 - 4.35 \times 6}{23.2}$$

$$A_0 = \dfrac{928 + 278.4 + 8.7 - 26.1}{23.2} = \dfrac{1189}{23.2}$$

$$\boxed{A_0 = 51.25 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**Classification Insight (1 mark):** Since $GCV = 28.27 \;\text{MJ/kg} > 25 \;\text{MJ/kg}$, this is a **High Calorific Value Solid Fuel** (high-grade bituminous coal).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the classification of fuels based on calorific value. Compare the calorific value of at least one solid, one liquid, and one gaseous fuel with their typical applications. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *What is the difference between Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and Net Calorific Value (NCV)? A fuel has GCV = 30 MJ/kg and hydrogen content = 12% by mass. Calculate its NCV. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification by Calorific Value (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Calorific value is the amount of heat released per unit mass (or volume) of a fuel upon complete combustion. *(1 mark)*

**Three Categories (3 marks):**
- **High Calorific Value Fuels ($>25 \;\text{MJ/kg}$):** Hydrogen ($142 \;\text{MJ/kg}$), Gasoline ($44 \;\text{MJ/kg}$), Natural Gas ($55 \;\text{MJ/kg}$), Diesel ($43 \;\text{MJ/kg}$).
- **Medium Calorific Value Fuels ($10$–$25 \;\text{MJ/kg}$):** Coal ($20$–$30 \;\text{MJ/kg}$), Wood ($16 \;\text{MJ/kg}$), Ethanol ($27 \;\text{MJ/kg}$).
- **Low Calorific Value Fuels ($<10 \;\text{MJ/kg}$):** Producer Gas ($5 \;\text{MJ/kg}$), Blast Furnace Gas ($4 \;\text{MJ/kg}$).

**Comparison + Applications (3 marks):**

| Fuel | Type | Approx GCV (MJ/kg) | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bituminous Coal | Solid | $25$–$30$ | Thermal power plants |
| Gasoline | Liquid | $44$ | Spark-ignition car engines |
| Natural Gas (Methane) | Gaseous | $55$ | Domestic stoves, gas turbines |

---

**(b) GCV vs NCV + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Difference (3 marks):**
- **GCV (Higher Heating Value):** Heat released assuming **all water vapor** produced during combustion is condensed and the latent heat is recovered.
- **NCV (Lower Heating Value):** Heat released assuming water vapor **escapes** with the products (latent heat is NOT recovered).
- **Relation:** $NCV < GCV$; the difference is approximately $0.09 \times H \times 585 \;\text{kcal/kg}$ where $H$ is the % hydrogen.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Given:** $GCV = 30 \;\text{MJ/kg}$, $H = 12\%$.

Convert GCV to kcal/kg: $GCV = 30 \times 238.85 = 7165.5 \;\text{kcal/kg}$.

Latent heat loss per kg of fuel:

$$\Delta H = 0.09 \times H \times 585 = 0.09 \times 12 \times 585 = 631.8 \;\text{kcal/kg}$$

Therefore:

$$NCV = GCV - 631.8 = 7165.5 - 631.8 = 6533.7 \;\text{kcal/kg}$$

Converting back to MJ/kg:

$$\boxed{NCV = \dfrac{6533.7}{238.85} = 27.36 \;\text{MJ/kg}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose Marks Here!**
> 1. **Unit Trap:** Students often forget to convert GCV in MJ/kg to kcal/kg (multiply by 238.85) before applying the latent heat correction in the kcal-based formula. This yields a wrong NCV by a factor of $\sim 240$. Always check units first.
> 2. **Dulong's Formula Trap:** $C, H, O, S$ must be entered as **mass fractions** (e.g., $0.80$ for $80\%$), NOT as integers ($80$). The $1000$ in the denominator handles the percentage conversion.
> 3. **Classification Confusion:** Students frequently misclassify LPG as a liquid fuel. **LPG is a GAS** stored as a liquid under pressure — its natural state is gaseous, hence it is classified under gaseous fuels.
> 4. **Ash/Ash-Free Confusion:** Dulong's formula gives GCV on a **dry, ash-free basis** strictly. For coals with high ash, additional corrections are required.
> 5. **Equivalence vs Air-Fuel Ratio:** Do not invert the equivalence ratio formula. $\phi = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} / (A/F)_{\text{actual}}$ — rich mixture means $\phi > 1$.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- A **fuel** is a combustible substance releasing heat on reaction with oxygen; classification is along **occurrence, physical state, calorific value, and application**.
- **Primary fuels** = found in nature (coal, crude oil, NG); **Secondary fuels** = derived/derived (petrol, coke, LPG).
- **Physical State:** Solid (coal, wood) | Liquid (petrol, diesel, ethanol) | Gaseous (NG, LPG, biogas, H₂).
- **CV Classes:** HCV ($>25 \;\text{MJ/kg}$), MCV ($10$–$25 \;\text{MJ/kg}$), LCV ($<10 \;\text{MJ/kg}$).
- **Hydrogen has the highest CV** ($\sim 142 \;\text{MJ/kg}$) of all conventional fuels — a key reason for its importance in alternative fuels.
- **Dulong's formula:** $GCV = [33800\,C + 144000(H - O/8) + 9419\,S] / 1000$ in $\text{MJ/kg}$ — must be memorized.
- **NCV** is always **less than GCV** by the latent heat of water vapor produced from hydrogen.
- **Stoichiometric air** for hydrocarbon fuel $\approx 11.6\,C + 34.8\,H + 4.35\,S - 4.35\,O$ over $23.2 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$.
- **LPG = gas**, not liquid, even though stored as a compressed liquid.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, always present a **comparison table** — it guarantees full structure marks.
- Always mention the **units** explicitly in numerical answers to avoid deduction of partial marks.
- **NCV is the practical value** used in engineering (since exhaust gases are hot, water does not condense).
- **Ash content** is the major pollutant in solid fuels; modern systems prefer gasification of solid fuels to gaseous fuels for cleaner combustion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Alternative Fuels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Fuels (KTU 2024 Definition):** Alternative fuels are non-conventional, non-fossil-derived or partially fossil-derived energy carriers that can substitute conventional petroleum-based fuels (gasoline, diesel) for transportation, power generation, or industrial applications. They are characterized by their potential to reduce **greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions**, **urban air pollutants**, and **geopolitical fuel dependency**.

The term *Alternative Fuels* broadly encompasses solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels (including electricity and hydrogen) that are produced from renewable resources, waste streams, or unconventional energy sources.

## 1.2 Classification Framework – The "Types" Topic

For KTU 2024 Module 1, the **Types** of alternative fuels are classified into three primary categories based on their physical state and energy source:

| **Classification Basis** | **Category** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Bio-derived | Ethanol, Biodiesel, Biogas |
| Origin | Fossil-derived (cleaner) | CNG, LNG, LPG, DME |
| Origin | Synthetic/Hydrogen | H₂, Synthetic Diesel, NH₃ |
| Physical State | Gaseous | CNG, LNG, LPG, H₂, Biogas |
| Physical State | Liquid | Ethanol, Biodiesel, Methanol, DME |
| Physical State | Solid (Renewable Electricity) | Battery Electric, Solar, Wind |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Focus:** Students must remember the classification based on **renewable vs. non-renewable**, as well as the **first-generation, second-generation, and third-generation biofuels** taxonomy.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the fuel tank of your vehicle as a "lunchbox." For over a century, we've been packing the same "sandwich" — crude oil refined into petrol/diesel. Alternative fuels are simply exploring the entire menu of the canteen — sandwiches (biofuels), salads (hydrogen), smoothies (electricity), and protein shakes (synthetic fuels) — to find options that are **cheaper, healthier for the planet, and locally sourced**.

The *Healthier* aspect refers to lower **CO₂ emissions per km** driven. The *locally sourced* aspect refers to energy security — a country can grow its own fuel (sugarcane → ethanol) instead of importing crude oil.

## 1.4 Standard Units and Metrics (To Remember)

- **Energy Density (MJ/kg)** – energy per unit mass.
- **Energy Density (MJ/L)** – energy per unit volume (critical for vehicles).
- **Cetane Number (CN)** – diesel knock resistance.
- **Octane Number (RON/MON)** – gasoline knock resistance.
- **Calorific Value (CV)** – **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** and **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** measured in **kJ/kg** or **MJ/kg**.
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** – mass of air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of fuel.
- **Auto-ignition Temperature (°C)** – minimum temperature for spontaneous combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Calorific Value of Petrol (reference)** ≈ **44 MJ/kg**
> - **Calorific Value of Diesel (reference)** ≈ **45.5 MJ/kg**
> - **Air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume** (mass basis: 23.2% O₂, 76.8% N₂).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Types of Alternative Fuels – Theory

### 2.1.1 First-Generation Biofuels
These are produced directly from food crops (sugar, starch, vegetable oil). They raise the **food vs. fuel** debate.

- **Bioethanol (C₂H₅OH):** Produced by fermentation of sugarcane, corn, or molasses. Blended with petrol (E10, E20, E85).
- **Biodiesel (FAME – Fatty Acid Methyl Esters):** Produced by **transesterification** of vegetable oils (soybean, palm, sunflower, Jatropha) or animal fats with methanol. Blended with diesel (B5, B20, B100).
- **Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂):** Produced by **anaerobic digestion** of organic waste (agricultural residue, animal dung, municipal solid waste).

### 2.1.2 Second-Generation Biofuels
Produced from non-food lignocellulosic biomass (wood, grass, agricultural waste). Avoid the food-fuel conflict.

- **Cellulosic Ethanol**
- **Bio-diesel from Jatropha/Algae (algae can also be 3rd generation)**
- **Biomass-to-Liquid (BTL)** – Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.

### 2.1.3 Third-Generation Biofuels
Derived from **algae**, which have very high photosynthetic efficiency and do not require arable land.

- **Algal biodiesel**
- **Bio-hydrogen**

### 2.1.4 Gaseous Alternative Fuels
- **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG):** Mostly **methane (CH₄)**, stored at **200–250 bar**.
- **Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG):** CH₄ cooled to **−162 °C** for storage.
- **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG):** Mixture of **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)**.
- **Hydrogen (H₂):** The ultimate clean fuel — produces only **H₂O** on combustion.
- **Biogas/Bio-methane.**
- **Dimethyl Ether (DME – CH₃OCH₃).**

### 2.1.5 Liquid Synthetic Fuels
- **Methanol (CH₃OH)** – can be produced from natural gas, coal, or biomass.
- **Ethanol** – as above.
- **Synthetic Diesel / Gasoline** – via **Fischer-Tropsch (FT)** process from syngas (CO + H₂).

### 2.1.6 Electricity as a "Fuel" (E-mobility)
- **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs):** Store electricity in Li-ion batteries.
- **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs):** Combine ICE + battery.
- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs):** Use **Hydrogen Fuel Cells (PEMFC)** to generate electricity on-board.

### 2.1.7 Solar and Wind (Indirect Alternative Fuels)
- **Photovoltaic (PV) electricity** – directly powers BEVs.
- **Solar fuels** – synthetic fuels produced using solar energy (e.g., **Solar-to-Hydrogen** via electrolysis).

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Type

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need alternatives to petrol/diesel?**
> - **Finite Reserves** of crude oil (estimated **50–60 years** left at current consumption).
> - **Tailpipe Emissions** cause smog, asthma, cancer.
> - **CO₂ Emissions** drive climate change.
> - **Import Bills** drain foreign exchange reserves (especially for India, which imports **>85%** of its crude oil).

> [!NOTE]
> **How are they produced?**
> - **Biochemical route:** Fermentation (sugar → ethanol), Anaerobic digestion (waste → biogas).
> - **Thermochemical route:** Pyrolysis, Gasification (biomass → syngas → FT diesel), Transesterification (oil → biodiesel).
> - **Electrolytic route:** Water electrolysis (renewable electricity → green H₂).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis) | dimensionless |
| $$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2O$$ | General hydrocarbon combustion | — |
| $$LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left(\frac{9H}{100}\right)$$ | Relation between LHV and HHV for hydrocarbon fuels | MJ/kg |
| $$C_{16}H_{32}O_2 + CH_3OH \xrightarrow{NaOH} 3\,C_{17}H_{34}O_2 + Glycerol$$ | Transesterification (simplified) | — |
| $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{Zymase} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$ | Fermentation of glucose to ethanol | — |
| $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$ | Otto cycle thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| $$Energy\,Density\,(vol) = \rho \cdot LHV$$ | Volumetric energy density | MJ/L |
| $$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{P \cdot V_{tank}}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$$ | Hydrogen mass in a compressed tank | kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick to Remember Stoichiometric AFR for Pure Hydrocarbons:**
> For a fuel **CₓHᵧO_z**, the mass of O₂ required is:
> $$m_{O_2} = 32\left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right) \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kmol fuel}$$
> Then: $$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{137.28 \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right)}{12x + y + 16z}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transport Sector:** Reducing **Particulate Matter (PM)** and **NOx** in cities (e.g., Delhi's CNG bus program).
- **Agriculture:** Jatropha cultivation in wasteland provides rural income + biodiesel.
- **Power Generation:** Biogas plants provide decentralized electricity in villages.
- **Aviation/Marine:** Synthetic jet fuels from FT synthesis for carbon-neutral flying.
- **Industrial Process Heat:** Hydrogen replacing coke in steel-making (green steel).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Connect:** India's **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP)** targets **20% ethanol blending (E20)** by **2025–26 (originally ESY 2030, advanced)**. **SATAT** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) scheme targets **15 MMTPA of compressed biogas** by 2023.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

Ethanol is a first-generation biofuel commonly blended with petrol. The derivation is a **KTU favourite**.

**Step 1:** Write the balanced combustion equation with air.

$$C_2H_5OH_{(l)} + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O_{(l)}$$

*Logic: One molecule of ethanol contains 2 C, 6 H, and 1 O atom. Combustion requires 2 C → 2 CO₂ (needs 4 O), 6 H → 3 H₂O (needs 3 O). Total O needed = 4 + 3 = 7. Ethanol itself supplies 1 O atom, so external O₂ needed = (7−1)/2 = 3 molecules.*

**Step 2:** Introduce nitrogen (since air is 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume → 1 mol O₂ comes with 3.76 mol N₂).

$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 + 3 \times 3.76\,N_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + 11.28\,N_2$$

**Step 3:** Compute molecular weights.

- Molecular weight of **C₂H₅OH** = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = **46 kg/kmol**
- Molecular weight of **O₂** = 32 kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of **N₂** = 28 kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of **air** (per mole O₂) = 32 + 3.76(28) = **137.28 kg**

**Step 4:** Compute the mass of air required.

$$m_{air} = 3 \times 137.28 = 411.84 \text{ kg air / kmol fuel}$$

**Step 5:** Compute stoichiometric AFR.

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{411.84}{46} = 8.95$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Stoichiometric AFR of ethanol = **8.95 : 1** (mass basis). Compare to petrol ≈ 14.7 and diesel ≈ 14.5. Ethanol requires much more air per kg of fuel due to its oxygen content.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LHV of Biogas (Methane-Dominant)

Assume biogas composition: **60% CH₄, 40% CO₂** (typical from anaerobic digestion of cattle dung).

**Step 1:** Write HHV of each component.

- HHV of **CH₄** = **55.5 MJ/kg**
- CO₂ is inert (does not combust), HV = **0 MJ/kg**

**Step 2:** Convert volumetric composition to mass composition.

Basis: 1 kmol of biogas = 0.6 kmol CH₄ + 0.4 kmol CO₂

- Mass of CH₄ = 0.6 × 16 = **9.6 kg**
- Mass of CO₂ = 0.4 × 44 = **17.6 kg**
- Total mass = 9.6 + 17.6 = **27.2 kg**

**Step 3:** Calculate mass-weighted HHV.

$$HHV_{biogas} = \frac{(0.6 \times 16 \times 55.5) + (0.4 \times 44 \times 0)}{27.2}$$

$$HHV_{biogas} = \frac{9.6 \times 55.5}{27.2} = \frac{532.8}{27.2} \approx 19.59 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4:** Convert HHV to LHV (assuming water leaves as vapour, h_fg of water at 25 °C = **2.44 MJ/kg**).

For methane, hydrogen mass fraction = 4/16 = 0.25. In our biogas, effective hydrogen mass fraction is reduced. Approximating LHV of biogas:

$$LHV_{biogas} \approx 0.9 \times HHV_{biogas} \approx 0.9 \times 19.59 \approx 17.6 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Biogas (60:40) has LHV ≈ **17.6 MJ/kg** — only about **40% of pure methane's** LHV. This is why **CO₂ scrubbing** is essential before using biogas as a vehicle fuel.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Density of Hydrogen (Compressed vs. Liquid)

Hydrogen has the **highest energy per unit mass (LHV ≈ 120 MJ/kg)** but the **lowest energy per unit volume** unless highly compressed or liquefied.

**Step 1:** Calculate energy density by mass (gravimetric).

$$LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Density of hydrogen gas at STP.

$$\rho_{H_2, STP} = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.0899 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 3:** Energy density by volume at STP.

$$E_{vol, STP} = 120 \times 0.0899 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0108 \text{ MJ/L} = 10.8 \text{ kJ/L}$$

**Step 4:** Compress to 700 bar (typical FCEV tank).

At 700 bar and ambient temperature, hydrogen density ≈ **40 kg/m³ = 0.04 kg/L**.

$$E_{vol, 700\,bar} = 120 \times 0.04 = 4.8 \text{ MJ/L}$$

**Step 5:** Compare to petrol.

$$E_{vol, petrol} = 34 \text{ MJ/L} \text{ (approx)}$$

$$Ratio = \frac{4.8}{34} \approx 0.141$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Even at 700 bar, hydrogen occupies **~7× the volume** of petrol for the same energy. This is the **#1 challenge** for hydrogen vehicles — storage volume, not weight.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of Major Alternative Fuels

| **Fuel** | **Formula** | **LHV (MJ/kg)** | **Density (kg/L)** | **Octane/Cetane** | **AFR_stoich** | **Renewable?** | **Feedstock** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Petrol (baseline) | C₄–C₁₂ mix | 44.0 | 0.74 | 92 RON | 14.7 | ❌ | Crude oil |
| Diesel (baseline) | C₁₂–C₂₀ mix | 45.5 | 0.84 | 50 CN | 14.5 | ❌ | Crude oil |
| Bioethanol | C₂H₅OH | 26.9 | 0.789 | 110 RON | 8.95 | ✅ | Sugarcane, corn |
| Biodiesel (FAME) | C₁₇H₃₄O₂ | 37.2 | 0.88 | 50–60 CN | 12.5 | ✅ | Vegetable oil, algae |
| Biogas (60% CH₄) | CH₄+CO₂ | 17.6 | 1.15 (g/L) | 130 RON | ~10 | ✅ | Organic waste |
| CNG (Methane) | CH₄ | 50.0 | 0.20 (g/L) | 120 RON | 17.2 | ❌ (fossil) | Natural gas |
| Hydrogen | H₂ | 120.0 | 0.04 (g/L) @ 700 bar | 130+ RON | 34.3 | ✅ (if green) | Water electrolysis |
| LPG | C₃H₈/C₄H₁₀ | 46.0 | 0.51 | 111 RON | 15.6 | ❌ (fossil) | Refinery, NG |
| Methanol | CH₃OH | 20.0 | 0.792 | 108 RON | 6.4 | ✅/❌ | NG, coal, biomass |
| DME | CH₃OCH₃ | 28.9 | 0.67 (liq) | 55–60 CN | 9.0 | ✅/❌ | NG, coal, biomass |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Octane Number** with **Cetane Number**. Petrol engines need HIGH octane (resists knock); diesel engines need HIGH cetane (easy to ignite). Ethanol has **high octane** but is unsuitable for diesels without modification.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **List and briefly explain any three types of first-generation biofuels.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Bioethanol** – Produced by **fermentation of sugar/starch** (sugarcane, corn, molasses) using yeast. Used as a petrol-blending fuel (E10, E20, E85). Has high octane number (~110 RON) but low energy density.
2. **Biodiesel (FAME)** – Produced by **transesterification** of vegetable oils (soybean, palm, Jatropha) or animal fats with methanol in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH/KOH). Used as a diesel blend (B5, B20). Cetane number ~50–60.
3. **Biogas** – Produced by **anaerobic digestion** of organic waste (cattle dung, agricultural residue, MSW) in the absence of oxygen. Composition: **55–70% CH₄ + 30–45% CO₂**. Used for cooking, power generation, and as a vehicle fuel (after upgrading to bio-methane).

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> **Differentiate between first, second, and third-generation biofuels with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **1st Gen** | **2nd Gen** | **3rd Gen** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Sugar, starch, edible oil | Lignocellulose, waste biomass | Algae, aquatic biomass |
| **Examples** | Sugarcane ethanol, soybean biodiesel | Cellulosic ethanol, BTL diesel | Algal biodiesel, bio-hydrogen |
| **Food vs Fuel** | Yes, conflict | No conflict | No conflict |
| **Land requirement** | High (arable) | Moderate (wasteland) | Minimal (ponds) |
| **Maturity** | Commercial | Pilot | R&D |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High | Very high |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Classify alternative fuels in detail. Discuss the production, properties, advantages, and limitations of (a) bioethanol and (b) biodiesel.**

#### Part (a) – Bioethanol (7 Marks)

**1. Classification (2 marks):**
Bioethanol (C₂H₅OH) is a **first-generation liquid biofuel** derived from the **fermentation of sugars and starches**. It belongs to the **alcohol** family of alternative fuels.

**2. Production Process (2 marks):**
The process involves three stages:
- **Feedstock preparation:** Sugarcane juice, molasses, or starchy grains (corn, wheat) are crushed and hydrolyzed to release fermentable sugars (glucose, sucrose).
- **Fermentation:** Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) converts sugars to ethanol and CO₂ under anaerobic conditions at ~30 °C:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$
- **Distillation & Dehydration:** The dilute ethanol (~10% v/v) is distilled to ~95% (azeotrope) and then dehydrated using **molecular sieves** to get anhydrous ethanol (>99.5%) for blending with petrol.

**3. Properties (1.5 marks):**
- LHV = **26.9 MJ/kg** (≈ 60% of petrol)
- Octane Number = **110–115 RON** (excellent knock resistance)
- Stoichiometric AFR = **8.95 : 1**
- Oxygen content = **35%** by mass (reduces CO and PM emissions)
- Miscible with petrol in all proportions
- Hygroscopic (absorbs water → phase separation issues)

**4. Advantages (1 mark):**
- Domestic production reduces oil import bills.
- Octane booster → improved engine efficiency.
- 30–50% reduction in **CO, HC, and PM** tailpipe emissions.
- Renewable feedstock (India's EBP targets E20 by 2025–26).

**5. Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Lower energy density → reduced fuel economy (~30% lower km/litre).
- Corrosive to rubber and certain plastics in older fuel systems.
- Food vs fuel debate (1st generation).
- Requires modified engine for high blends (E85 needs FFV).

#### Part (b) – Biodiesel (7 Marks)

**1. Classification (1.5 marks):**
Biodiesel, chemically known as **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)**, is a **first-generation liquid biofuel** derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oil. It is a **diesel-substitute** fuel.

**2. Production Process – Transesterification (2.5 marks):**
The vegetable oil (triglyceride) reacts with **methanol in the presence of NaOH/KOH catalyst** at 60–70 °C to produce FAME and glycerol.

$$Triglyceride + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightleftharpoons{NaOH} 3\,FAME + Glycerol$$

**Simplified chemical equation:**

$$C_{16}H_{32}O_2 + CH_3OH \xrightarrow{NaOH} C_{17}H_{34}O_2 + H_2O$$

Steps:
- **Feedstock pretreatment:** Degumming, deacidification.
- **Reaction:** Oil + methanol + catalyst (1% wt NaOH) at 60 °C for 1–2 hours.
- **Separation:** Glycerol (bottom layer) is drained; FAME is washed and dried.
- **Purification:** Removal of residual methanol, catalyst, soap.

**3. Properties (1.5 marks):**
- LHV = **37.2 MJ/kg** (~ 82% of diesel)
- Cetane Number = **50–60** (similar to diesel)
- Density = **0.88 kg/L**
- Flash point = **> 130 °C** (safer to handle than diesel)
- Oxygen content ≈ **11%** by mass
- Viscosity higher than diesel (may require preheating in cold climates).

**4. Advantages (1 mark):**
- Renewable, biodegradable, non-toxic.
- ~50–60% reduction in **PM, CO, and HC** emissions.
- Higher cetane → smoother combustion.
- Can be used in existing diesel engines with **little or no modification** (up to B20).
- Lubricity improver → reduces engine wear.

**5. Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Higher NOₓ emissions (~10% increase).
- Cold-flow issues (cloud point, pour point problems in winter).
- Slightly higher viscosity → injector fouling long term.
- Solvent action on rubber seals (must use Viton/FKM).

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**Discuss the types of gaseous alternative fuels. Explain in detail the (a) production, storage, and applications of hydrogen as an alternative fuel, and (b) compare CNG, LNG, and LPG as vehicle fuels.**

#### Part (a) – Hydrogen (7 Marks)

**1. Why Hydrogen? (1 mark):**
- Zero-carbon fuel: combustion product is **H₂O only**.
- Highest energy-to-mass ratio: **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** (≈ 2.7× petrol).
- Can be produced from water using renewable electricity → **truly sustainable**.

**2. Production Methods (3 marks):**
- **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):** CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂ — produces **grey hydrogen** (CO₂ released).
- **Electrolysis:** 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ — using renewable electricity produces **green hydrogen** (zero CO₂).
- **Partial Oxidation, Biomass Gasification, Photoelectrochemical** — emerging methods.
- **Colour codes:** Grey (fossil), Blue (fossil + CCS), Green (renewable), Pink (nuclear).

**3. Storage (2 marks):**
- **Compressed gas (CGH₂):** at 350 bar or 700 bar in composite tanks.
- **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂):** cryogenic storage at **−253 °C**.
- **Metal hydrides / LOHC (Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers):** chemical storage in toluene/methylcyclohexane.
- **Solid-state storage** in carbon nanotubes, MOFs — under research.

**4. Applications (1 mark):**
- **FCEVs** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) — fuel cells generate electricity on-board.
- Industrial: ammonia synthesis, petroleum refining, methanol production.
- Rocket propellant (liquid H₂ + LOX).
- Stationary fuel cells for power generation.

#### Part (b) – Comparison of CNG, LNG, LPG (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Property** | **CNG** | **LNG** | **LPG** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Main component** | CH₄ (>90%) | CH₄ (>90%) | C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀ |
| **State (storage)** | Gas (high pressure) | Cryogenic liquid | Liquid (moderate pressure) |
| **Storage pressure** | **200–250 bar** | Atmospheric (−162 °C) | ~7 bar |
| **Storage temp** | Ambient | −162 °C | Ambient |
| **LHV (MJ/kg)** | 50 | 50 | 46 |
| **Density (kg/L)** | 0.20 (at 200 bar) | 0.41 | 0.51 |
| **Energy density (MJ/L)** | ~10 | ~22 | ~25 |
| **Octane number** | 120 | 120 | 111 |
| **Range in vehicle** | 250–300 km | 600+ km (heavy vehicles) | 350–400 km |
| **Refuelling time** | 5–10 min | 5–10 min | 5 min |
| **Infrastructure** | Compressors expensive | Cryogenic tanks expensive | Cylinders widely available |
| **Use case** | City buses, cars | Heavy trucks, ships | Domestic + light vehicles |

**Advantages & Limitations (3 marks):**
- **CNG:** Cheaper than petrol/diesel; lower emissions; but bulky tanks reduce boot space; limited range.
- **LNG:** Higher energy density → suited for long-haul trucks, marine; expensive cryogenic infrastructure.
- **LPG:** Convenient domestic availability; moderate cost; but fossil-derived and limited renewability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "CNG is renewable" → **WRONG.** CNG is **fossil-derived** (natural gas). Only **biogas upgraded to bio-CNG** is renewable.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **LNG with LPG.** LNG is **pure methane** at −162 °C; LPG is **propane+butane** at room temperature under low pressure.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the **stoichiometric AFR** in derivation questions. Always show the molecular weight of air (137.28) explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "biodiesel = vegetable oil" → **WRONG.** Biodiesel is **FAME**, the *methyl ester* of vegetable oil, produced via transesterification. Raw vegetable oil is **NOT biodiesel**.
> - **Mark Loss Zones:** Missing units in formulas (−1 mark); not balancing chemical equations (−1 mark); not mentioning catalyst in transesterification (−0.5 mark); confusing octane and cetane (−1 mark).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Types of Alternative Fuels" (Module 1)**

### 🎯 Core Classifications
- **First-gen biofuels:** Food crops → ethanol, biodiesel, biogas.
- **Second-gen biofuels:** Lignocellulosic waste → cellulosic ethanol, BTL.
- **Third-gen biofuels:** Algae → algal biodiesel, bio-H₂.
- **Gaseous:** CNG (CH₄, 200 bar), LNG (CH₄, −162 °C), LPG (C₃+C₄), H₂.
- **Liquid synthetic:** Methanol, DME, FT diesel.
- **Electric:** BEV, PHEV, FCEV.

### 🔑 Critical Numbers to Memorize
- LHV of H₂ = **120 MJ/kg** (highest of any fuel).
- LHV of CNG = **50 MJ/kg**; Petrol = **44 MJ/kg**; Diesel = **45.5 MJ/kg**.
- Stoichiometric AFR: Petrol ≈ **14.7**; Diesel ≈ **14.5**; Ethanol ≈ **8.95**; H₂ ≈ **34.3**.
- Molecular weight of air per mole O₂ = **137.28 kg**.
- N₂/O₂ molar ratio in air = **3.76**.

### ⚗️ Chemical Equations (Must Memorize)
- **Combustion of methane:** CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O.
- **Combustion of ethanol:** C₂H₅OH + 3O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O.
- **Combustion of hydrogen:** 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O.
- **Fermentation:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂.
- **Transesterification:** Triglyceride + 3CH₃OH → 3FAME + Glycerol (with NaOH catalyst).

### 🚗 Vehicle-Application Map
- **Petrol engines (SI):** Petrol, Ethanol (E10–E100), LPG, CNG (with kit), H₂ (with kit), Methanol.
- **Diesel engines (CI):** Diesel, Biodiesel (B5–B100), DME, FT diesel, H₂ (with dual fuel).
- **Electric drivetrain:** BEV (battery), FCEV (H₂ fuel cell), PHEV (both).

### 🌱 Renewable vs Non-Renewable
- **Renewable:** Bioethanol, biodiesel, biogas, green H₂, electricity from solar/wind, algal fuel.
- **Non-renewable (fossil-derived):** CNG, LNG, LPG, grey/blue H₂, methanol/DME from coal/NG.

### 🇮🇳 India-Specific Points
- **EBP (Ethanol Blending Programme):** E20 by 2025–26.
- **SATAT:** Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation — 15 MMTPA CBG target.
- **National Green Hydrogen Mission:** 5 MMTPA green H₂ by 2030.
- **India's crude oil import dependence:** > **85%**.

### ❌ Common Pitfalls (Re-read Before Exam)
- Don't confuse **LNG vs LPG** (temperature/pressure differences).
- Don't call **CNG renewable** — only **bio-CNG** is.
- **Biodiesel ≠ vegetable oil** — biodiesel is **FAME**.
- **Hydrogen's challenge is volumetric energy density**, not gravimetric.
- **Octane for SI engines, Cetane for CI engines** — never mix them up.
- **DME has high cetane (55–60)** — good diesel substitute despite being a gas at STP.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "B-C-L-H-E" to remember the 5 main categories of Alternative Fuels:**
> **B**iofuels → **C**NG/LNG/LPG → **L**iquid synthetic (methanol, DME) → **H**ydrogen → **E**lectricity (BEV/FCEV).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combustion Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combustion Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Combustion Equation**: A balanced chemical equation that represents the complete oxidation of a fuel (containing Carbon, Hydrogen, Sulfur, etc.) with oxygen (the oxidizer), yielding products of combustion such as CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, N₂, and excess O₂ when excess air is supplied.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **combustion equation** is the stoichiometric representation of the reaction:
$$\text{Fuel} + \nu_{O_2} \cdot O_2 \longrightarrow \sum \text{Products}$$

where $\nu_{O_2}$ denotes the theoretical moles of oxygen required for complete combustion of one mole (or one kilogram) of fuel.

The general stoichiometric combustion equation for a hydrocarbon fuel **CₐHᵦOᵧNδSε** with dry air (modeled as 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume, i.e., 1 mol O₂ accompanied by **3.76 mol N₂**) is:

$$C_aH_bO_cN_dS_e + \left(a + \frac{b}{4} + e - \frac{c}{2}\right) O_2 + 3.76\left(a + \frac{b}{4} + e - \frac{c}{2}\right) N_2 \longrightarrow a\,CO_2 + \frac{b}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{d}{2}\,N_2 + e\,SO_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Board Principle**: The molecular weight of air is taken as **29 kg/kmol**, and oxygen as **32 kg/kmol**. The volumetric ratio of N₂ to O₂ in air is universally taken as **79/21 = 3.7619 ≈ 3.76**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe of Fire"

Imagine combustion as a **baking recipe**: flour, sugar, and eggs must be measured in **exact stoichiometric proportions** to bake a perfect cake. If you add too much flour (excess fuel) or too little (excess air), the cake (combustion) will be incomplete. A **combustion equation** is the chemist's "recipe card" that tells the engineer exactly how much oxygen to invite to the party so that every carbon atom pairs with two oxygen atoms to form CO₂, and every hydrogen atom pairs with half an oxygen molecule to form H₂O.

When you burn a candle (paraffin wax, roughly C₂₅H₅₂) in a closed room with **insufficient oxygen**, you get **carbon monoxide (CO)** and **soot (C)** — incomplete combustion. With **just enough** oxygen, you get CO₂ and H₂O. With **excess air**, you still get CO₂ and H₂O, but they are diluted by leftover O₂ and N₂, lowering the flame temperature.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Molecular weight of air**: $M_{air} = 28.97 \approx \mathbf{29 \text{ kg/kmol}}$
- **Molecular weight of dry air components**: O₂ = 32 kg/kmol, N₂ = 28 kg/kmol
- **Universal Gas Constant**: $R_u = \mathbf{8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}}$
- **Standard atmospheric conditions (KTU Board convention)**: $T_0 = 298 \text{ K}, P_0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Volumetric O₂ in air**: **21%**, Volumetric N₂ in air: **79%**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Combustion Equation Construction

### Step 1: Identify the Fuel Molecular Formula
The fuel is typically expressed in terms of its elemental composition:
- **Solid/Liquid fuel**: Ultimate analysis gives mass fractions of C, H, S, O, N (dry basis)
- **Gaseous fuel**: Molecular formula like CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, C₈H₁₈

### Step 2: Apply Atom Conservation (Why It Works)
Each atom entering the reactor must exit it. This is the **Law of Conservation of Mass** — atoms are neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. We equate moles of each element on both sides:

- **Carbon balance**: 1 mol C in fuel → 1 mol CO₂ in products
- **Hydrogen balance**: 2 mol H → 1 mol H₂O
- **Sulfur balance**: 1 mol S → 1 mol SO₂
- **Nitrogen balance**: N₂ is inert and passes through
- **Oxygen balance**: Total O atoms on LHS = Total O atoms on RHS

### Step 3: Introduce the Air-Supply Coefficient
Real furnaces always supply more than the theoretical (stoichiometric) oxygen. We define:

$$\lambda = \frac{\text{Actual Air Supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air Required}} = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$

When $\lambda = 1.0$ → Stoichiometric (theoretical) combustion
When $\lambda > 1.0$ → Excess air supplied
When $\lambda < 1.0$ → Deficiency of air (incomplete combustion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Excess Air (eA)**: $eA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100\%$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Moles of O₂ per mole of fuel CₐHᵦOᵧNδSε | $n_{O_2} = a + \dfrac{b}{4} + e - \dfrac{c}{2}$ | kmol | Theoretical O₂ |
| 2 | Moles of N₂ accompanying air | $n_{N_2} = 3.76 \cdot n_{O_2}$ | kmol | With 21/79 ratio |
| 3 | Stoichiometric A/F (mass basis) | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{n_{O_2} \cdot M_{air}}{M_{fuel}}$ | kg air / kg fuel | Multiply numerator by 29 |
| 4 | Stoichiometric A/F (mole basis) | $(A/F)_{mole} = \dfrac{n_{O_2} \cdot (1+3.76)}{1}$ | kmol air / kmol fuel | For gaseous fuels |
| 5 | Actual moles of O₂ supplied | $n_{O_2,act} = \lambda \cdot n_{O_2,stoich}$ | kmol | Depends on λ |
| 6 | Excess O₂ in products | $n_{O_2,excess} = (\lambda - 1) \cdot n_{O_2,stoich}$ | kmol | When λ > 1 |
| 7 | Excess air percentage | $eA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100$ | % | Board favorite |
| 8 | Air-Fuel ratio on mass basis | $(A/F) = \lambda \cdot (A/F)_{stoich}$ | kg/kg | |
| 9 | Fuel-Air ratio | $F/A = \dfrac{1}{(A/F)}$ | kg/kg | Inverse |
| 10 | Combustion efficiency (CO₂ basis) | $\eta_{CO_2} = \dfrac{[CO_2]}{[CO_2] + [CO]}$ | — | Orsat dry analysis |
| 11 | Theoretical air (mass) per kg fuel | $A_{stoich} = 8.89\,C + 26.7\,H + 3.33\,S - 3.33\,O$ | kg air / kg fuel | C,H,S,O are mass fractions |
| 12 | Mass of N₂ per kg fuel | $m_{N_2} = 3.76 \cdot n_{O_2} \cdot 28$ | kg | When N₂ per O₂ = 3.76 |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Combustion equations are the **backbone of every thermal design**:

- **Boiler design**: Determining the quantity of flue gases to size the chimney and economizer
- **IC engine calibration**: Setting the carburetor jet or common-rail injection timing
- **Gas turbine combustor**: Calculating the inlet air-fuel ratio to maintain turbine inlet temperature (TIT) within material limits
- **Pollution control**: Predicting the volume of NOₓ, SO₂, and CO emissions for environmental compliance
- **Rocket propulsion**: Propellant combinations like LOX/LH₂ or LOX/RP-1 rely on these exact stoichiometric equations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip**: 90% of combustion equation problems in the 2024 Scheme exam require you to (a) write the balanced equation, (b) find A/F ratio on **mass basis**, and (c) compute **flue gas composition on dry basis** with given excess air.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Stoichiometric Combustion of Methane (CH₄)

**Problem**: Write the stoichiometric combustion equation of methane with air. Determine the stoichiometric A/F ratio on mass basis.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

The basic reaction of methane with pure oxygen is:

$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O \quad \text{(1)}$$

Now, since air contains 79% N₂ and 21% O₂ by volume, every mole of O₂ is accompanied by 3.76 moles of N₂. Therefore, the **combustion with air** becomes:

$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 2(3.76)\,N_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2$$

$$\boxed{CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 7.52\,N_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2} \quad \text{(2)}$$

**Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio on Mass Basis**:

$$\text{Mass of air} = (2 \times 32) + (7.52 \times 28) = 64 + 210.56 = 274.56 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Mass of fuel (CH}_4) = 1 \times 16 = 16 \text{ kg}$$

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{274.56}{16} = \mathbf{17.16 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Combustion of Octane (C₈H₁₈) with 150% Theoretical Air

**Problem**: Liquid octane is burned with 150% theoretical air. Write the balanced equation and determine the actual A/F ratio on mass basis.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

The stoichiometric reaction with pure O₂:

$$C_8H_{18} + \left(8 + \frac{18}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O$$

$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 \longrightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O$$

Now with stoichiometric air:

$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 12.5(3.76)\,N_2 \longrightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47\,N_2$$

With **150% theoretical air** ($\lambda = 1.50$), actual O₂ supplied is $1.5 \times 12.5 = 18.75$ mol, and N₂ is $1.5 \times 47 = 70.5$ mol.

Oxygen consumed in reaction = 12.5 mol
Oxygen remaining in products = $18.75 - 12.5 = 6.25$ mol

$$\boxed{C_8H_{18} + 18.75\,O_2 + 70.5\,N_2 \longrightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 6.25\,O_2 + 70.5\,N_2}$$

**Actual A/F on Mass Basis**:

$$\text{Mass of air} = 18.75 \times 32 + 70.5 \times 28 = 600 + 1974 = 2574 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Mass of fuel} = (8 \times 12) + (18 \times 1) = 96 + 18 = 114 \text{ kg}$$

$$(A/F)_{actual} = \frac{2574}{114} = \mathbf{22.58 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Combustion from Ultimate Analysis

**Problem**: A coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 70%, H = 8%, S = 3%, O = 6%, N = 5%, Ash = 8%. Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of coal.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

Using the empirical mass-based formula:

$$A_{stoich} = 8.89\,C + 26.7\,H + 3.33\,S - 3.33\,O$$

Substituting the mass fractions:

$$A_{stoich} = (8.89 \times 0.70) + (26.7 \times 0.08) + (3.33 \times 0.03) - (3.33 \times 0.06)$$

$$A_{stoich} = 6.223 + 2.136 + 0.0999 - 0.1998$$

$$\boxed{A_{stoich} = 8.26 \text{ kg air / kg coal}}$$

**Mass of N₂ per kg coal**:

$$m_{N_2} = 3.76 \times n_{O_2} \times 28 / 32$$

Where $n_{O_2}$ per kg coal = $A_{stoich}/M_{air} = 8.26/29 = 0.2848$ kmol... or directly from the O₂ requirement:

$$n_{O_2,stoich} = \frac{6.223 + 2.136/2 + 0.0999 - 0.1998}{32} = \frac{7.189}{32} = 0.2247 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

$$m_{N_2} = 0.2247 \times 3.76 \times 28 = \mathbf{23.65 \text{ kg N}_2/\text{kg coal}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU 2024 Patterns

| Pattern | What KTU Asks | Typical Marks |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Balanced equation + A/F | Write the combustion equation for a gaseous fuel with given excess air; compute mass A/F | 7 |
| Ultimate analysis | Compute theoretical air from mass percentages | 7 |
| Orsat analysis | From dry flue gas composition, back-calculate A/F ratio and excess air | 14 (Module 2 territory, but foundation here) |
| Air-Fuel ratio comparison | Compare stoich and actual A/F for different hydrocarbons (CH₄ vs C₈H₁₈ vs CH₃OH) | 7 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Insight — Why divide H by 4?**
> Because 4 hydrogen atoms require 1 molecule of O₂ to form 2 molecules of H₂O:
> $4H + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$. So per H atom, we need $\frac{1}{4}$ molecule of O₂.
> Per mole of H₂, we need $\frac{1}{2}$ molecule of O₂.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Combustion Equation Formation — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below illustrates the logical workflow that an engineer follows when constructing a combustion equation from a given fuel composition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Input: Molecular formula or Ultimate Analysis] --> B{Identify Fuel State}
    B -->|Gaseous| C1[Read mole fractions: CH4, C2H6, C3H8, etc.]
    B -->|Solid or Liquid| C2[Read mass percentages: C, H, S, O, N, Ash]
    C1 --> D[Compute stoichiometric O2 requirement]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E1[For CxHy: nO2 = x + y/4]
    D --> E2[Add sulfur: nO2 = x + y/4 + s]
    D --> E3[Subtract fuel oxygen: nO2 = x + y/4 + s - z/2]
    E1 --> F[Apply excess air factor lambda]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Excess Air Specified?}
    G -->|Yes, percent eA| H1[Actual O2 = nO2 stoichiometric times 1 plus eA/100]
    G -->|No, lambda=1| H2[Stoichiometric reaction only]
    H1 --> I[Add N2: multiply O2 by 3.76]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Write product side: CO2, H2O, SO2, N2, excess O2]
    J --> K[Verify atom balance C, H, O, N, S]
    K --> L[Compute mass A over F ratio]
    L --> M[Final Balanced Combustion Equation]
```

## 4.2 Process Schematic — Combustion Reaction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REACTANTS
        R1[Fuel: CxHyOzNdSe]
        R2[Theoretical O2]
        R3[N2 from air]
    end
    subgraph COMBUSTOR[Combustion Chamber]
        X[Ignition Source or Catalyst]
    end
    subgraph PRODUCTS[Flue Gas Products]
        P1[CO2]
        P2[H2O vapor]
        P3[SO2 if sulfur present]
        P4[N2 inert]
        P5[Excess O2 if lambda greater than 1]
    end
    R1 --> X
    R2 --> X
    R3 --> X
    X --> P1
    X --> P2
    X --> P3
    X --> P4
    X --> P5
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Air-Fuel Ratio Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AF[Air-Fuel Ratio Family] --> A1[Stoichiometric A over F]
    AF --> A2[Actual A over F]
    AF --> A3[Equivalence Ratio phi]
    A1 --> B1[Lambda equals 1]
    A2 --> B2[Lambda greater than 1: Lean burn]
    A2 --> B3[Lambda less than 1: Rich burn]
    A3 --> B4[Phi equals 1 over lambda]
    B2 --> C1[Excess air: eA percent]
    B3 --> C2[Deficiency of air]
    C1 --> D1[Excess O2 appears in flue gas]
    C2 --> D2[CO and soot appear in flue gas]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip**: The first Mermaid diagram is your **algorithm** for solving any combustion equation KTU throws at you. Memorize this flow as if it were a recipe — Fuel → Identify state → Compute O₂ → Apply λ → Add N₂ → Balance → Compute A/F.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define: (i) Stoichiometric air, (ii) Excess air, (iii) Air-Fuel ratio.**

**Model Answer**:

**(i) Stoichiometric Air**: The minimum quantity of air required to supply just enough oxygen for the complete combustion of a unit mass or mole of fuel. Beyond this point, no more oxygen is chemically needed; any additional air remains unreacted. Mathematically, it is the air corresponding to $\lambda = 1.0$.

**(ii) Excess Air**: The quantity of air supplied **above** the stoichiometric requirement, expressed either as a percentage or as a ratio. It is calculated as:

$$eA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100\%$$

Excess air is intentionally provided in practical furnaces and boilers (typically 5–25%) to ensure complete combustion and avoid CO formation.

**(iii) Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)**: The ratio of the mass of air supplied to the mass of fuel burned. On a stoichiometric basis for octane, it is approximately **15.13 kg air/kg fuel**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**For combustion of methane CH₄ with 100% excess air, write the balanced equation.**

**Model Answer**:

Stoichiometric O₂ for CH₄ = 2 mol
With 100% excess air, actual O₂ = $2 \times 2 = 4$ mol
N₂ = $4 \times 3.76 = 15.04$ mol

$$\boxed{CH_4 + 4\,O_2 + 15.04\,N_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 2\,O_2 + 15.04\,N_2}$$

**Verification**: 
- C: LHS = 1, RHS = 1 ✓
- H: LHS = 4, RHS = 4 ✓
- O: LHS = 8, RHS = 2 + 2 + 4 = 8 ✓
- N: LHS = 15.04, RHS = 15.04 ✓

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**A coal sample has the following composition by mass: C = 76%, H = 5%, S = 2%, O = 7%, N = 3%, Ash = 7%. The coal is burned with 30% excess air. Calculate:**

**(a)** The stoichiometric air required per kg of coal. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The actual A/F ratio on mass basis, and the flue gas composition on dry basis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1**: Identify the mass fractions: $C = 0.76$, $H = 0.05$, $S = 0.02$, $O = 0.07$.

**Step 2**: Compute stoichiometric O₂ per kg of coal using mass fractions:

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - \frac{8}{3}O$$

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = \frac{8}{3}(0.76) + 8(0.05) + (0.02) - \frac{8}{3}(0.07)$$

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = 2.0267 + 0.40 + 0.02 - 0.1867$$

$$m_{O_2,stoich} = \mathbf{2.26 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}}$$

**Step 3**: Convert O₂ mass to air mass using $M_{air}/M_{O_2} = 29/32$:

$$A_{stoich} = 2.26 \times \frac{29}{32} = 2.26 \times 0.90625$$

$$\boxed{A_{stoich} = 2.048 \text{ kg air/kg coal}}$$

Or using the equivalent formula:

$$A_{stoich} = 8.89C + 26.7H + 3.33S - 3.33O$$

$$A_{stoich} = 8.89(0.76) + 26.7(0.05) + 3.33(0.02) - 3.33(0.07)$$

$$A_{stoich} = 6.7564 + 1.335 + 0.0666 - 0.2331 = \mathbf{7.92 \text{ kg air/kg coal}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Which Formula?**: KTU board examiners often accept both formulas above, BUT if you mix the coefficients (e.g., use $8C$ instead of $\frac{8}{3}C$ for oxygen), you will lose 2 marks. The **2.26 kg O₂/kg coal** value uses the O₂-mass formula; the **7.92 kg air/kg coal** uses the air-mass formula. Both are equivalent.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1**: Actual air supplied = $1.30 \times 7.92 = 10.30$ kg air/kg coal.

**Step 2**: Mass A/F ratio:

$$(A/F)_{actual} = \mathbf{10.30 \text{ kg air/kg coal}}$$

**Step 3**: Determine moles of products per kg of coal.

Moles of C per kg coal = $0.76 / 12 = 0.0633$ kmol → produces $0.0633$ kmol CO₂
Moles of H per kg coal = $0.05 / 1 = 0.05$ kmol → produces $0.05/2 = 0.025$ kmol H₂O
Moles of S per kg coal = $0.02 / 32 = 0.000625$ kmol → produces $0.000625$ kmol SO₂

Moles of O₂ stoichiometric = $2.26/32 = 0.0706$ kmol
Moles of O₂ actual = $1.30 \times 0.0706 = 0.0918$ kmol
Moles of N₂ actual = $3.76 \times 0.0918 = 0.3453$ kmol
Moles of O₂ excess = $0.0918 - 0.0706 = 0.0212$ kmol

**Step 4**: Dry basis (excluding H₂O):

Total dry moles = CO₂ + SO₂ + N₂ + excess O₂
$= 0.0633 + 0.000625 + 0.3453 + 0.0212 = 0.4304$ kmol

**Composition on dry basis (mole %)**:

| Component | Moles | Percentage |
|-----------|-------|------------|
| CO₂ | 0.0633 | **14.71%** |
| SO₂ | 0.000625 | **0.15%** |
| N₂ | 0.3453 | **80.23%** |
| O₂ | 0.0212 | **4.91%** |
| **Total Dry** | 0.4304 | **100.00%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: When asked for **dry basis**, students often **forget to exclude H₂O**. If you include H₂O, your CO₂ percentage will be wrong (it will read ~12% instead of ~14.7%). This single mistake can cost you 2–3 marks. Always clarify "wet" vs "dry" basis before computing percentages.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Methane (CH₄) is burned with air. The dry flue gas analysis by volume gives: CO₂ = 9.0%, O₂ = 3.0%, N₂ = 88.0%. Determine:**

**(a)** The stoichiometric A/F ratio and the actual A/F ratio on mass basis. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The percentage of excess air supplied, and the air-fuel ratio on mole basis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1**: Write the actual combustion equation on the basis of 100 moles of dry flue gas:

- CO₂ = 9.0 mol
- O₂ = 3.0 mol
- N₂ = 88.0 mol

**Step 2**: From C-balance: Moles of CH₄ burned = moles of CO₂ produced = **9.0 mol**.

**Step 3**: From N₂ balance: Total N₂ supplied = 88.0 mol. Since N₂ comes only from air, total O₂ supplied from air:

$$n_{O_2,supplied} = \frac{88.0}{3.76} = 23.404 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4**: Oxygen balance — Total O₂ supplied = O₂ in CO₂ + O₂ in H₂O + O₂ excess:
$2(9.0) + \frac{1}{2}n_{H_2O} + 2(3.0) = 2(23.404)$
$18.0 + 0.5\,n_{H_2O} + 6.0 = 46.808$
$0.5\,n_{H_2O} = 22.808$
$n_{H_2O} = 45.616$ mol

**Step 5**: Stoichiometric A/F on mass basis.

Stoichiometric O₂ for 9 mol CH₄ = $2 \times 9 = 18$ mol
Stoichiometric air moles = $18 \times 4.76 = 85.68$ mol
Stoichiometric air mass = $85.68 \times 29 = 2484.72$ g
Fuel mass = $9 \times 16 = 144$ g

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{2484.72}{144} = \mathbf{17.255 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**Actual A/F**: 

Actual air supplied = $23.404 \times 4.76 = 111.40$ mol
Actual air mass = $111.40 \times 29 = 3230.6$ g

$$(A/F)_{actual} = \frac{3230.6}{144} = \mathbf{22.43 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1**: Excess air percentage:

$$eA = \left(\frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} - 1\right) \times 100$$

$$eA = \left(\frac{22.43}{17.255} - 1\right) \times 100 = (1.30 - 1) \times 100 = \mathbf{30\%}$$

**Step 2**: Air-fuel ratio on mole basis:

$$(A/F)_{mole} = \frac{111.40}{9.0} = \mathbf{12.38 \text{ kmol air/kmol fuel}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall — The "Per Mole vs Per Mass" Confusion**: When the question says **"A/F ratio on mole basis"**, the student must report kmol-air per kmol-fuel (a number like 12.38). Reporting 22.43 (the mass basis) here is wrong and will lose 3 marks. Always re-read the unit requested.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Combustion Equations**

- ✅ Stoichiometric O₂ for **CₓHᵧS_zO_w**: $n_{O_2} = x + \dfrac{y}{4} + z - \dfrac{w}{2}$
- ✅ **N₂ per O₂ in air = 3.76** (universally accepted in KTU exams)
- ✅ **Molecular weight of air = 29 kg/kmol**, O₂ = 32 kg/kmol, N₂ = 28 kg/kmol
- ✅ Stoichiometric air (mass per kg fuel): $8.89\,C + 26.7\,H + 3.33\,S - 3.33\,O$ (where C, H, S, O are mass fractions)
- ✅ Excess air: $eA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100\%$
- ✅ Excess O₂ in products = $(\lambda - 1) \times n_{O_2,stoich}$
- ✅ **Dry basis** excludes H₂O; **Wet basis** includes it — always clarify
- ✅ Atom conservation: C → CO₂, H → H₂O, S → SO₂, N → N₂ (inert)
- ✅ Methane combustion: $(A/F)_{stoich} = 17.16$ kg/kg
- ✅ Octane combustion: $(A/F)_{stoich} = 15.13$ kg/kg
- ✅ For Orsat/back-calculations, take dry basis = 100 moles for ease of percentage computation
- ✅ **Common Pitfall**: Students forget to multiply O₂ moles by $(1 + 3.76) = 4.76$ when computing total air moles — KTU deducts 1 mark for this.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall**: When reporting "A/F ratio," always state the **basis** (mass or mole) and **condition** (stoichiometric or actual) — KTU answer scripts that don't specify these lose 1–2 marks.

---

**End of Module 1 — Combustion Equations Notes**
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (PEMUT411) syllabus and NEP 2020 outcome-based assessment framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stoichiometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stoichiometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stoichiometry** (from the Greek words *stoicheion* = "element" and *metron* = "measure") is the quantitative study of the relative amounts of **reactants and products** participating in a chemical reaction, governed by the **Law of Conservation of Mass**. In the specific context of *Alternative Fuels and Energy Systems*, combustion stoichiometry is the branch of stoichiometry that deals with calculating the **exact quantities of oxidizer (air/oxygen) required** to completely burn a given fuel, the resulting composition of **flue gases (products of combustion)**, and the key performance parameter called the **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)**.

The combustion of a generic fuel — applicable to virtually all conventional and alternative fuels (hydrogen, methane, ethanol, biodiesel, methanol, LPG, CNG, biogas, etc.) — is expressed as:

$$C_x H_y O_z N_w S_v + a\,O_2 + 3.76\,a\,N_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{w}{2}\,N_2 + v\,SO_2$$

where the subscript letters *x, y, z, w, v* denote the number of atoms of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur in **one mole** of the fuel molecule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** Stoichiometry forms the *foundational prerequisite* for all subsequent modules dealing with combustion thermodynamics, flame temperature, flame speed, and emissions from alternative fuels. Mastery of air–fuel ratio (A/F) and equivalence ratio (φ) calculations is *non-negotiable* for Part-B problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe for Fire"

Imagine you are baking a cake. The recipe states that for every 200 g of flour, you need exactly 100 g of sugar, 2 eggs, and 100 mL of milk. If you add *less* sugar, the cake is bland (incomplete reaction); if you add *too much* sugar, the excess simply sits in the bowl unused (excess reactant). Combustion works **identically**:

| Baking Analogy | Combustion Stoichiometry |
|---|---|
| Flour (base ingredient) | **Fuel** (e.g., methane, ethanol) |
| Sugar, eggs, milk | **Oxygen** (the oxidizer from air) |
| Excess sugar left in bowl | **Excess air** in flue gas |
| Perfect cake (all ingredients used) | **Stoichiometric (theoretical) combustion** |
| Cake texture & taste (output) | **Combustion products** (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) |

Thus, stoichiometry is simply the **engineering recipe card** for burning fuels cleanly and completely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used Throughout Stoichiometry:**
> - **Molar mass of air** = **28.97 kg/kmol** (≈ 29 kg/kmol)
> - **Molar mass of O₂** = **32 kg/kmol**
> - **Molar mass of N₂** = **28 kg/kmol**
> - **Molecular composition of dry air** = 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ (mole basis) ⇒ 1 kmol O₂ is accompanied by **3.76 kmol N₂**
> - **Universal Gas Constant, R** = **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The entire discipline of combustion stoichiometry rests on **three universal pillars**:

**Pillar 1 — Law of Conservation of Mass (Antoine Lavoisier, 1789)**
*In a chemical reaction, the total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products.*

This is the bedrock principle. Every stoichiometric balance equation is essentially a **mass-bookkeeping ledger**.

**Pillar 2 — Mass Balance for Each Element (Atomic Balance)**
For every element participating in combustion (C, H, O, N, S), the number of atoms entering the reaction zone must equal the number leaving it. This yields the stoichiometric coefficient *a* (the moles of O₂ required per mole of fuel).

**Pillar 3 — Stoichiometric vs. Actual Combustion**
- *Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Combustion*: Exactly the chemically required air is supplied. No free O₂ remains in the products; no fuel is left unburned.
- *Actual Combustion with Excess Air*: More air than stoichiometric is supplied (always done in practice to ensure complete combustion and avoid CO formation). Excess air is quantified as:

$$\%\,\text{Excess Air} = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}} - (A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}} \times 100$$

### 2.2 The Master Coefficient — Moles of Oxygen Required (a)

For the general fuel formula $C_x H_y O_z N_w S_v$, balancing the oxygen atoms on both sides of the combustion equation:

$$2a = 2x + \frac{y}{2} + 2v - z$$

Solving for *a*:

$$a = x + \frac{y}{4} + v - \frac{z}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The coefficient *a* is the most-used parameter in all of combustion stoichiometry.** It tells you exactly how many kmol of O₂ are required to burn 1 kmol of fuel. The total stoichiometric air required per kmol of fuel is therefore $a(1 + 3.76) = 4.76\,a$ kmol of air.

### 2.3 The Equivalence Ratio (φ) — A Crucial Dimensionless Parameter

The **equivalence ratio** is the standard KTU-asked parameter for comparing fuels:

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$$

- **φ = 1** → Stoichiometric mixture
- **φ < 1** → Lean mixture (excess air)
- **φ > 1** → Rich mixture (deficient air)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Condition / Notes** |
|:-:|---|---|:-:|---|
| 1 | Moles of O₂ per mole of fuel | $a = x + y/4 + v - z/2$ | kmol O₂ / kmol fuel | For $C_x H_y O_z N_w S_v$ |
| 2 | Stoichiometric air (moles) | $A_{\text{stoich}} = 4.76\,a$ | kmol air / kmol fuel | Based on 21% O₂ – 79% N₂ by volume |
| 3 | Stoichiometric air (mass) | $m_{\text{air,stoich}} = 4.76\,a \times 29$ | kg air / kmol fuel | Uses **M_air = 29 kg/kmol** |
| 4 | Stoichiometric A/F (mass) | $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{4.76\,a \times 29}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kg air / kg fuel | **Most asked KTU formula** |
| 5 | Stoichiometric F/A (mass) | $(F/A)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | kg fuel / kg air | Reciprocal of A/F |
| 6 | Stoichiometric A/F (mole) | $(A/F)_{\text{mole}} = 4.76\,a$ | kmol air / kmol fuel | Purely molecular basis |
| 7 | Excess air (%) | $\%EA = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{act}} - (A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}} \times 100$ | % | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 8 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} / (A/F)_{\text{act}}$ | dimensionless | $\phi < 1$ : lean ; $\phi > 1$ : rich |
| 9 | Actual moles of O₂ supplied | $a_{\text{act}} = a \times (1 + EA/100)$ | kmol O₂ / kmol fuel | Direct from excess air |
| 10 | N₂ in products | $N_2^{\text{prod}} = 3.76\,a_{\text{act}} + w/2$ | kmol N₂ / kmol fuel | Includes fuel-bound N |
| 11 | O₂ in products (wet basis) | $O_2^{\text{prod}} = a_{\text{act}} - a$ | kmol O₂ / kmol fuel | Only with excess air |
| 12 | CO₂ in products | $CO_2^{\text{prod}} = x$ | kmol / kmol fuel | From fuel carbon balance |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stoichiometric calculations are the **lifeblood of every thermal engineering system**:

- **Internal Combustion (IC) Engines**: A/F ratio directly controls power output, fuel economy, and exhaust emissions (CO, NOx, unburnt HC).
- **Gas Turbines & Jet Engines**: Off-stoichiometric operation alters flame stability and turbine inlet temperature.
- **Industrial Boilers & Furnaces**: Excess air optimization minimizes stack heat losses while ensuring complete combustion.
- **Hydrogen Fuel Cells & Rocket Propulsion**: Stoichiometry dictates the minimum oxidizer tankage and propellant mixture ratio.

> [!NOTE]
