> **KTU Practical Note:** Hydrogen has the **highest stoichiometric A/F (≈ 34.3)**, while methanol has one of the **lowest (≈ 6.43)**. This 5× spread dictates radically different induction system designs in engines running on these alternative fuels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Oxygen Coefficient *a* from Atomic Balance

**Given:** A generic fuel of empirical formula $C_x H_y O_z N_w S_v$ is burned completely with theoretical air.

**Step 1 — Write the unbalanced combustion equation:**

$$C_x H_y O_z N_w S_v + a\,O_2 + 3.76\,a\,N_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{w}{2}\,N_2 + v\,SO_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply atomic balance on Oxygen (O):**
Atoms of O on LHS = $z$ (from fuel) $+ 2a$ (from O₂)
Atoms of O on RHS = $2x$ (in CO₂) $+ \frac{y}{2}$ (in H₂O) $+ 2v$ (in SO₂)

Setting them equal:

$$z + 2a = 2x + \frac{y}{2} + 2v$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for the coefficient *a*:**

$$2a = 2x + \frac{y}{2} + 2v - z$$

$$\boxed{a = x + \frac{y}{4} + v - \frac{z}{2}}$$

> **Logic check:** The fuel brings in *z* oxygens "for free", so the net O₂ needed is reduced by *z/2*. Conversely, hydrogen demands *y/4* mole of O₂ per atom pair, and sulfur needs one O atom (½ O₂) per atom.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass-Based Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio

**Given:** A fuel of molar mass $M_{\text{fuel}}$ (kg/kmol) requires *a* kmol of O₂ per kmol of fuel.

**Step 1 — Mass of air required (theoretical):**
The total stoichiometric air per kmol of fuel = $4.76\,a$ kmol air.
Mass of this air = $4.76\,a \times M_{\text{air}}$ = $4.76\,a \times 29$ kg.

**Step 2 — Mass of fuel per kmol:** = $M_{\text{fuel}}$ kg.

**Step 3 — Form the mass A/F ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich, mass}} = \frac{\text{Mass of stoichiometric air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}} = \frac{4.76\,a \times 29}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{137.96\,a}{M_{\text{fuel}}}}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) Combustion

Ethanol is a primary alternative biofuel. Let's determine *every* stoichiometric parameter.

**Step 1 — Identify the fuel formula:**
Ethanol = $C_2H_6O$ (in KTU 2024 style, written as $C_2H_5OH$). Thus:
- $x = 2$, $y = 6$, $z = 1$, $w = 0$, $v = 0$
- $M_{\text{fuel}} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46$ kg/kmol

**Step 2 — Calculate the oxygen coefficient *a*:**

$$a = x + \frac{y}{4} + v - \frac{z}{2} = 2 + \frac{6}{4} + 0 - \frac{1}{2}$$

$$a = 2 + 1.5 - 0.5 = 3.0 \;\text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kmol fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Write the balanced stoichiometric equation:**

$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 + 3(3.76)\,N_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + 11.28\,N_2$$

Verification of atom balance:
- C: 2 = 2 ✓
- H: 6 = 3×2 = 6 ✓
- O: 1 + 6 = 7 (LHS); 4 + 3 = 7 (RHS) ✓

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric air (molar):**

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = 4.76 \times 3 = 14.28 \;\text{kmol air / kmol fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric air (mass):**

$$m_{\text{air,stoich}} = 14.28 \times 29 = 414.12 \;\text{kg air / kmol fuel}$$

**Step 6 — Mass-based stoichiometric A/F ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{414.12}{46} = 9.00 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

**Step 7 — Now consider 30% excess air** (typical engine operating condition):

$$a_{\text{act}} = 3.0 \times (1 + 0.30) = 3.9 \;\text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kmol fuel}$$

**Step 8 — Actual air supplied (mass):**

$$m_{\text{air,act}} = 4.76 \times 3.9 \times 29 = 538.36 \;\text{kg air / kmol fuel}$$

**Step 9 — Actual A/F (mass basis):**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{act}} = \frac{538.36}{46} = 11.70 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

**Step 10 — Equivalence ratio:**

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{act}}} = \frac{9.00}{11.70} = 0.769$$

Since φ < 1, the mixture is confirmed **lean** (excess air present).

**Step 11 — Flue gas composition (per kmol fuel with 30% excess air):**
- CO₂ produced = 2 kmol
- H₂O produced = 3 kmol
- O₂ in products = $3.9 - 3.0 = 0.9$ kmol (this is the unreacted excess oxygen)
- N₂ in products = $3.76 \times 3.9 = 14.66$ kmol
- **Total dry flue gas** = $2 + 0.9 + 14.66 = 17.56$ kmol

**Step 12 — Dry flue gas composition (% by volume):**

$$CO_2\% = \frac{2}{17.56} \times 100 = 11.39\%$$

$$O_2\% = \frac{0.9}{17.56} \times 100 = 5.13\%$$

$$N_2\% = \frac{14.66}{17.56} \times 100 = 83.48\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of dry-basis analysis (Orsat method):** The sum $11.39 + 5.13 + 83.48 = 100\%$ ✓
> These three numbers are typical *Orsat readings* you would measure with a gas analyzer in a boiler flue gas test.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Stoichiometry Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Fuel Formula Identification] --> B[Step2 Extract Atoms x y z w v]
    B --> C[Step3 Calculate Coefficient a]
    C --> D[Step4 Write Balanced Combustion Equation]
    D --> E[Step5 Compute Stoichiometric Air kmol and kg]
    E --> F[Step6 Compute Stoichiometric A to F Ratio]
    F --> G{Step7 Is Excess Air Specified?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Step8 Apply Excess Air Factor]
    G -- No --> I[Output Stoichiometric Parameters Only]
    H --> J[Step9 Compute Actual A to F Ratio]
    J --> K[Step10 Compute Equivalence Ratio phi]
    K --> L[Step11 Determine Flue Gas Composition]
    L --> M[Step12 Calculate Orsat Dry Analysis]
    M --> N[Final Output Mass and Energy Basis Ready]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Stoichiometric Data for Major Alternative Fuels

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Hydrogen H2] --> nodeB[Highest A to F equals 34.3]
    nodeC[Methane CH4 CNG] --> nodeD[A to F equals 17.2]
    nodeE[Ethanol C2H5OH] --> nodeF[A to F equals 9.0]
    nodeG[Methanol CH3OH] --> nodeH[A to F equals 6.43]
    nodeI[Biodiesel C12H23 typical] --> nodeJ[A to F equals 32.6]
    nodeK[Gasoline C8H18 reference] --> nodeL[A to F equals 15.1]
    nodeM[Diesel C12H23 reference] --> nodeN[A to F equals 14.7]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each terminal node shows the *stoichiometric mass A/F ratio* for that fuel. Note how hydrogen's A/F (34.3) is nearly **5×** that of methanol (6.43). This is why hydrogen vehicles need *very large* intake systems, while methanol engines run fuel-rich relative to gasoline.

### 4.3 Schematic Map — Inputs and Outputs of the Stoichiometry Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput1[Fuel Chemical Formula] --> nodeCore[Stoichiometry Engine]
    nodeInput2[Molar Mass of Fuel] --> nodeCore
    nodeInput3[Excess Air Percent] --> nodeCore
    nodeCore --> nodeOut1[Balanced Equation]
    nodeCore --> nodeOut2[Moles of O2 Required]
    nodeCore --> nodeOut3[Stoichiometric A to F]
    nodeCore --> nodeOut4[Actual A to F]
    nodeCore --> nodeOut5[Equivalence Ratio phi]
    nodeCore --> nodeOut6[Flue Gas Composition]
    nodeCore --> nodeOut7[Dry Orsat Analysis]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the terms (i) Stoichiometric Air, (ii) Excess Air, and (iii) Equivalence Ratio as applied to combustion of alternative fuels.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Stoichiometric Air** is the *minimum* quantity of air that contains just enough oxygen to completely burn all the combustible elements (C, H, S) in the fuel, with no oxygen left over in the products. For a fuel $C_x H_y O_z S_v$, the stoichiometric air required is $4.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4} + v - \frac{z}{2}\right)$ kmol per kmol of fuel.

(ii) **Excess Air** is the *additional* air supplied beyond the stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage of the stoichiometric air. It is invariably used in practical systems to ensure complete combustion, suppress CO formation, and account for imperfect fuel–air mixing. Typical values: 5–10% in gas turbines, 15–25% in boilers, up to 30–40% in IC engines.

(iii) **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** is the dimensionless ratio of the actual fuel–air ratio to the stoichiometric fuel–air ratio. Mathematically, $\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$. When φ = 1, combustion is stoichiometric; φ < 1 indicates a *lean* mixture; φ > 1 indicates a *rich* mixture.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Why is the stoichiometric air requirement for hydrogen (34.3 kg/kg) much higher than for gasoline (15.1 kg/kg)?**

**Model Answer:**

The mass-based stoichiometric A/F ratio is given by $\frac{4.76 \times a \times 29}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$. For hydrogen ($H_2$): $a = 0.5$, $M_{\text{fuel}} = 2$, giving A/F = 34.3. For gasoline ($C_8H_{18}$): $a = 12.5$, $M_{\text{fuel}} = 114$, giving A/F = 15.1.

The **two-fold reason** is:

1. **Hydrogen has an extremely low molar mass (2 kg/kmol)**. Although it needs only 0.5 kmol of O₂ per kmol of fuel, this translates to a *very small fuel mass* denominator, making the A/F ratio numerically large.

2. **Hydrogen's energy is carried by H atoms only**, requiring 8 kg of O₂ per kg of H₂ to form H₂O. Gasoline, in contrast, carries its energy partly in carbon (which needs only 2.67 kg O₂ per kg of C) — making it more "oxygen-efficient" per unit mass.

This high A/F is a major engineering challenge for hydrogen-fueled engines and demands large displacement induction systems.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **stoichiometric mass air–fuel ratio** for a general hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y O_z$ in terms of its molar mass. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sample of **methanol (CH₃OH)** is burned with **40% excess air**. Calculate:
- (i) the actual air–fuel ratio on mass basis,
- (ii) the equivalence ratio, and
- (iii) the percentage composition of dry flue gases by volume. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Solution to (a):** *Derivation of stoichiometric A/F ratio*

**Step 1:** The balanced stoichiometric combustion of $C_x H_y O_z$ is:

$$C_x H_y O_z + a\,O_2 + 3.76\,a\,N_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + 3.76\,a\,N_2$$

**Step 2:** Balancing the oxygen atoms:

$$2a + z = 2x + \frac{y}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad a = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of stoichiometric air per kmol of fuel:

$$m_{\text{air,stoich}} = (a + 3.76a) \times M_{\text{air}} = 4.76\,a \times 29 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 4:** Mass of one kmol of fuel:

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = 12x + y + 16z = M_{\text{fuel}} \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 5:** Form the ratio:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{4.76 \times \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right) \times 29}{M_{\text{fuel}}}}$$

**Solution to (b):** *Methanol CH₃OH combustion with 40% excess air*

**Step 1 — Identify fuel properties:**
Methanol: $CH_3OH = CH_4O$ → $x = 1$, $y = 4$, $z = 1$, $w = 0$, $v = 0$
$M_{\text{fuel}} = 12 + 4 + 16 = 32$ kg/kmol

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric oxygen coefficient:**

$$a = 1 + \frac{4}{4} - \frac{1}{2} = 1 + 1 - 0.5 = 1.5 \;\text{kmol O}_2$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric A/F ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{4.76 \times 1.5 \times 29}{32} = \frac{207.06}{32} = 6.471 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

**Step 4 — With 40% excess air, actual O₂ supplied:**

$$a_{\text{act}} = 1.5 \times 1.40 = 2.10 \;\text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kmol fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Actual air–fuel ratio (mass):**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{act}} = \frac{4.76 \times 2.10 \times 29}{32} = \frac{289.88}{32} = 9.059 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{act}} = 9.06 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

**Step 6 — Equivalence ratio:**

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{act}}} = \frac{6.471}{9.059} = 0.714$$

Since φ < 1, the mixture is **lean** (excess air present).

**Step 7 — Flue gas composition per kmol of methanol:**

The balanced equation with 40% excess air:

$$CH_3OH + 2.10\,O_2 + 7.896\,N_2 \longrightarrow 1\,CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 0.60\,O_2 + 7.896\,N_2$$

- CO₂ = 1 kmol
- H₂O = 2 kmol (excluded in *dry* analysis)
- O₂ = 2.10 − 1.50 = 0.60 kmol
- N₂ = 7.896 kmol

**Step 8 — Dry flue gas total:**

$$n_{\text{dry}} = 1 + 0.60 + 7.896 = 9.496 \;\text{kmol}$$

**Step 9 — Dry composition (% by volume):**

$$CO_2\% = \frac{1}{9.496} \times 100 = 10.53\%$$

$$O_2\% = \frac{0.60}{9.496} \times 100 = 6.32\%$$

$$N_2\% = \frac{7.896}{9.496} \times 100 = 83.15\%$$

$$\boxed{CO_2 = 10.53\%,\quad O_2 = 6.32\%,\quad N_2 = 83.15\%}$$

(Sum = 100.00% ✓)

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **significance of stoichiometric air calculation** in the design of alternative fuel engines. Discuss how the stoichiometric A/F ratio varies for **hydrogen, methane, ethanol, and biodiesel**, with appropriate numerical values. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **biogas** with volumetric composition **60% CH₄ and 40% CO₂** is burned with **20% excess air**. Calculate:
- (i) the stoichiometric air required per kmol of biogas,
- (ii) the actual A/F ratio (mass basis), and
- (iii) the moles of each product gas per kmol of biogas. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Solution to (a):** *Significance of stoichiometric air calculation*

The stoichiometric air calculation is the *first and most critical* step in designing any combustion-based alternative fuel system because:

1. **Engine Calibration**: It defines the optimal fuel delivery quantity for a given air mass flow, governing the electronic control unit (ECU) mapping in modern engines.

2. **Emissions Control**: Deviation from stoichiometric A/F (running lean or rich) directly affects CO, NOx, and unburnt hydrocarbon emissions. Three-way catalytic converters work *only* near φ = 1.

3. **Combustion Efficiency**: Insufficient air → unburnt fuel & CO; excessive air → heat loss & reduced flame temperature.

**Stoichiometric A/F values for major alternative fuels:**

| **Fuel** | **Formula** | **a (kmol O₂/kmol fuel)** | **M (kg/kmol)** | **(A/F)_stoich** |
|---|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Hydrogen | $H_2$ | 0.5 | 2.0 | 34.30 |
| Methane (CNG) | $CH_4$ | 2.0 | 16.0 | 17.19 |
| Ethanol | $C_2H_5OH$ | 3.0 | 46.0 | 9.00 |
| Biodiesel (typical $C_{19}H_{36}O_2$) | — | 27.0 | 312.0 | 12.10 |

**Trend analysis:** As molecular weight and carbon content increase, the A/F ratio generally decreases. Hydrogen is the *extreme outlier* with a stoichiometric A/F about 4× that of ethanol, due to its high hydrogen density and lack of "free" fuel-bound oxygen.

**Solution to (b):** *Biogas (60% CH₄ + 40% CO₂) with 20% excess air*

**Step 1 — Per kmol of biogas basis:**
- CH₄ in 1 kmol biogas = 0.60 kmol
- CO₂ in 1 kmol biogas = 0.40 kmol (CO₂ is *inert*; it does not combust)

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ required (only CH₄ burns):**
For CH₄: $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$ → requires 2 kmol O₂ per kmol CH₄

$$a_{\text{stoich, total}} = 0.60 \times 2.0 = 1.20 \;\text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kmol biogas}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air (kmol):**

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = 4.76 \times 1.20 = 5.712 \;\text{kmol air / kmol biogas}$$

**Step 4 — Actual O₂ supplied with 20% excess:**

$$a_{\text{act}} = 1.20 \times 1.20 = 1.44 \;\text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kmol biogas}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of air supplied:**

$$m_{\text{air,act}} = 4.76 \times 1.44 \times 29 = 198.77 \;\text{kg air / kmol biogas}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of 1 kmol of biogas:**

$$m_{\text{biogas}} = 0.60 \times 16 + 0.40 \times 44 = 9.6 + 17.6 = 27.2 \;\text{kg/kmol}$$

**Step 7 — Actual A/F ratio (mass):**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{act}} = \frac{198.77}{27.2} = 7.31 \;\text{kg air / kg biogas}$$

**Step 8 — Product composition (per kmol biogas):**

The complete balanced equation:

$$0.60\,CH_4 + 0.40\,CO_2 + 1.44\,O_2 + 5.414\,N_2 \longrightarrow 0.60\,CO_2 + 1.20\,H_2O + 0.24\,O_2 + 5.414\,N_2 + 0.40\,CO_2$$

- **CO₂ produced** = 0.60 (from CH₄) + 0.40 (from inlet CO₂) = **1.00 kmol**
- **H₂O produced** = **1.20 kmol**
- **O₂ in products** = 1.44 − 1.20 = **0.24 kmol**
- **N₂ in products** = **5.414 kmol**

Total dry products = 1.00 + 0.24 + 5.414 = 6.654 kmol

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the inert CO₂ in biogas**: It does *not* combust but *contributes* to CO₂ in the products. Missing this drops 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using 4.76 instead of (1+3.76) = 4.76** is correct, but using the *mass* fraction 23.3% O₂ in air is a frequent error that gives wrong results.
> 3. **Not distinguishing mass A/F from mole A/F**: Always state the basis explicitly. The KTU question will specify "mass basis" — defaulting incorrectly loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Orsat analysis mistake**: H₂O must be *excluded* for "dry" flue gas analysis. Including it gives an incorrect dry-basis percentage.
> 5. **Rounding errors**: Round only at the *final* step. Intermediate values should retain 3–4 significant digits.
> 6. **Unit errors**: Molar mass of air must be **29 kg/kmol** (not 28.97) unless otherwise specified. Mismatched units cost 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Stoichiometry** = quantitative combustion analysis based on **mass conservation**.
- Generic fuel formula to remember: **$C_x H_y O_z N_w S_v$**.
- Master formula: $\boxed{a = x + y/4 + v - z/2}$
- Stoichiometric air (kmol) = **$4.76\,a$** per kmol of fuel.
- Stoichiometric air (kg) = **$4.76\,a \times 29$** per kmol of fuel.
- Mass A/F = **$\frac{4.76\,a \times 29}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$** ← the single most important KTU formula.
- Mole A/F = **$4.76\,a$** (not divided by fuel mass).
- Excess air (decimal) applied to *a*, not to the A/F ratio directly.
- $\phi = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} / (A/F)_{\text{act}}$ — lean if φ < 1, rich if φ > 1.
- For combustion products, CO₂, H₂O, SO₂ come from the fuel; O₂ appears only with excess air; N₂ comes from both air *and* fuel-bound nitrogen.
- **Dry basis** (Orsat) excludes H₂O; **wet basis** includes it.
- A/F ratio hierarchy (mass): **H₂ (34.3) > CH₄ (17.2) > Gasoline (15.1) > Diesel (14.7) > Biodiesel (12.1) > Ethanol (9.0) > Methanol (6.4)**.
- Inert gases (CO₂, N₂, H₂O vapor) in the fuel do not consume O₂ but do contribute to product composition.
- KTU 2024 emphasises **equivalence ratio** and **Orsat analysis** — practice both.
- Always balance the equation *first*, then read off coefficients for all subsequent calculations.
- Numerical answers should retain at least 3 significant figures; round only at the end.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combustion Analysis of Fossil Fuels by Mass and Volume - Calculation of Air Quantity, Fuels Properties - Calorific Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combustion Analysis of Fossil Fuels by Mass and Volume - Calculation of Air Quantity, Fuels Properties - Calorific Value

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Combustion — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Combustion** is defined as a rapid, self-sustaining **exothermic chemical reaction** between a fuel and an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen, O₂), accompanied by the evolution of **heat, light, and combustion products** (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, N₂, etc.).

In the context of **fossil fuels**, combustion is the controlled oxidation of hydrocarbons, carbon, hydrogen, and sulfur contained in coal, petroleum products, and natural gas. The analysis of this process is carried out in **two distinct ways**:

1. **Analysis by Mass** (Solid & Liquid Fuels) — Based on the *Ultimate Analysis* giving the mass percentages of C, H, O, N, S, and ash.
2. **Analysis by Volume** (Gaseous Fuels & Flue Gas) — Based on the volumetric composition of fuel gas or *Orsat Analysis* of dry flue gas.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Controlled Bonfire" Picture

Imagine a **brick kiln** in a pottery village. The kiln-master controls exactly how much wood, how much air enters, and measures the smoke coming out. Too little air → unburnt fuel (smoke, CO, soot → pollution + wasted energy). Too much air → heat is "diluted" because the extra N₂ and O₂ absorb the heat and carry it out through the chimney.

> **The same engineering principle governs every internal combustion engine, furnace, and boiler:** the *exact stoichiometric* (chemically perfect) amount of air is the "sweet spot." Combustion analysis is the science of finding this sweet spot.

## 1.3 Fundamental Composition of Atmospheric Air (Standard Reference Data)

> [!NOTE]
> The mass and volumetric proportions of air are **the most-used constants in the entire combustion chapter**. Memorize them.

| Component | By Volume (%) | By Mass (%) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Oxygen (O₂) | 21.0 | 23.2 |
| Nitrogen (N₂) | 79.0 | 76.8 |

- Molecular weight of air = **28.97 kg/kmol**
- Mass ratio: 1 kg of O₂ is supplied with $\frac{76.8}{23.2}$ = **3.31 kg of N₂**
- Volumetric ratio: 1 m³ of O₂ is supplied with $\frac{79}{21}$ = **3.76 m³ of N₂**

## 1.4 Stoichiometric Combustion Reactions (The "Building Blocks")

The entire combustion chapter rests on **three single-element reactions**:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad + \quad \Delta H \; (\text{= } -393.5 \; \text{MJ/kmol})$$

$$\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O} \quad + \quad \Delta H \; (\text{= } -483.7 \; \text{MJ/kmol O}_2)$$

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad + \quad \Delta H \; (\text{= } -296.8 \; \text{MJ/kmol})$$

These reactions are the **"ABC" of combustion** — every fuel, whether coal or natural gas, is broken down into these three atoms (C, H, S) and reacted according to these stoichiometric laws.

## 1.5 Definition of Calorific Value (CV)

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Calorific Value (CV)**, also called **Heat of Combustion (HoC)**, is the total amount of heat released when **one unit mass (or volume) of a fuel is completely burnt** and the products of combustion are cooled back to the reference temperature (usually **25 °C or 15 °C**).

Two variants exist:

- **Higher Calorific Value (HCV) / Gross CV (GCV):** The water vapour in the products is assumed to be **condensed** (latent heat recovered).
- **Lower Calorific Value (LCV) / Net CV (NCV):** The water vapour **escapes uncondensed** (latent heat is lost). LCV < HCV.

> **Real-World Note:** Industrial boilers and IC engines always use **LCV** because flue gases always leave the system hot and dry — the latent heat of condensation is never practically recovered.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Two Methods of Analysis?

The **physical state of the fuel** dictates the method:

- **Solid & Liquid fuels** (coal, diesel, fuel oil) → naturally measured in **kilograms (mass basis)**. The ultimate analysis gives C, H, O, N, S in **mass %**.
- **Gaseous fuels** (natural gas, biogas, producer gas) → naturally measured in **m³ (volume basis)**. Combustion reactions proceed in **molar proportions**, and by Avogadro's law, equal volumes of gases at the same T & P contain equal number of molecules → volumetric analysis is directly proportional to molar analysis.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Mass-Based Combustion (Solid/Liquid Fuels)

For **1 kg of fuel** with ultimate analysis C, H, O, N, S (in mass %):

**Step 1:** Calculate mass of each combustible element per kg of fuel.
- Mass of C = $\frac{C}{100}$ kg
- Mass of H = $\frac{H}{100}$ kg
- Mass of S = $\frac{S}{100}$ kg

**Step 2:** Calculate mass of O₂ theoretically required.

- For C: $\frac{32}{12} = \frac{8}{3}$ kg O₂ per kg C
- For H: $\frac{32}{4} = 8$ kg O₂ per kg H
- For S: $\frac{32}{32} = 1$ kg O₂ per kg S

$$\boxed{\text{O}_2 \text{ min (kg/kg fuel)} = \frac{8}{3}\left(\frac{C}{100}\right) + 8\left(\frac{H}{100}\right) + 1\left(\frac{S}{100}\right) - \frac{O}{100}}$$

> **Why subtract O/100?** The oxygen already present in the fuel can partially oxidize the C and H, reducing external O₂ demand. This is a "credit" to the fuel.

**Step 3:** Convert O₂ to theoretical air.

$$m_{\text{air, theo}} = \frac{\text{O}_2 \text{ min}}{0.232} \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4:** Actual air with excess air factor ($\alpha$):

$$m_{\text{air, actual}} = \alpha \times m_{\text{air, theo}}$$

Excess air % = $(\alpha - 1) \times 100$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic for Volume-Based Combustion (Gaseous Fuels)

For **1 m³ of fuel gas** with components CH₄, C₂H₆, CO, H₂, O₂, N₂, CO₂ (in volume %):

For a generic hydrocarbon **C_nH_m** burning in 1 m³:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \left(n + \frac{m}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{m}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

| Fuel Component | O₂ Required (m³/m³) | CO₂ Produced (m³/m³) | H₂O Produced (m³/m³) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CO | 0.5 | 1.0 | 0 |
| H₂ | 0.5 | 0 | 1.0 |
| CH₄ | 2.0 | 1.0 | 2.0 |
| C₂H₆ | 3.5 | 2.0 | 3.0 |
| C₃H₈ | 5.0 | 3.0 | 4.0 |
| C₄H₁₀ | 6.5 | 4.0 | 5.0 |

Then:

$$V_{\text{O}_2 \text{ min}} = \sum (\text{Fuel \%} \times \text{O}_2 \text{ per m}^3)$$

$$V_{\text{air, theo}} = \frac{V_{\text{O}_2 \text{ min}}}{0.21} \; \text{m}^3/\text{m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

## 2.4 Calorific Value — Theoretical Background

**Dulong's Formula** (the workhorse for solid fuels):

$$\text{GCV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right] \; \text{kcal/kg}$$

Converting kcal → kJ (× 4.186):

$$\text{GCV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[33800\,C + 144400\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9370\,S\right] \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

> **The "magic" of Dulong's formula:** It uses the **heat of combustion of pure elements** (C = 8080 kcal/kg, H₂ = 34500 kcal/kg, S = 2240 kcal/kg) and **subtracts the oxygen already combined in the fuel** as if it were "stealing" combustion energy from hydrogen (because H + O = H₂O — already partially oxidized, contributes no heat).

**Relation between HCV and LCV:**

$$\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - \left(\frac{9H}{100}\right) \times h_{fg}$$

where $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water at the reference condition:
- At **25 °C**: $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg = 583 kcal/kg
- At **100 °C**: $h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg = 540 kcal/kg

The factor **9** comes from: 1 kg H₂ + 8 kg O₂ → 9 kg H₂O. So per kg of hydrogen burned, 9 kg of water is formed.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Summary** — These equations carry 80 % of the marks in Module 1.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Min. O₂ (mass) | $O_2 = \tfrac{8}{3}\tfrac{C}{100} + 8\tfrac{H}{100} + \tfrac{S}{100} - \tfrac{O}{100}$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | Solid/Liquid fuels |
| 2 | Theoretical air (mass) | $m_a = O_2 / 0.232$ | kg air/kg fuel | Use 0.232, not 0.23 |
| 3 | Theoretical air (volume) | $V_a = V_{O_2} / 0.21$ | m³ air/m³ fuel | For gaseous fuels |
| 4 | Air-Fuel ratio (mass) | $(A/F) = m_a / 1$ | dimensionless | For 1 kg fuel |
| 5 | Dulong GCV (kJ) | $GCV = \tfrac{1}{100}\left[33800C + 144400(H - O/8) + 9370S\right]$ | kJ/kg | Indian textbooks |
| 6 | Dulong GCV (kcal) | $GCV = \tfrac{1}{100}\left[8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S\right]$ | kcal/kg | Older/USA texts |
| 7 | HCV to LCV | $LCV = HCV - (9H/100) \cdot h_{fg}$ | same as HCV | $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg @ 25 °C |
| 8 | Excess air % | $EA = \tfrac{O_{2,flue}}{O_{2,used}} \cdot 100$ | % | Orsat analysis basis |
| 9 | C_nH_m — O₂ reqd | $n + m/4$ | m³ O₂/m³ fuel | Per m³ of gas |
| 10 | Stoichiometric C | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | — | 1 kmol C needs 1 kmol O₂ |
| 11 | Stoichiometric H | $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | — | 1 kmol O₂ needs 2 kmol H₂ |
| 12 | N₂ from air (vol) | $N_2 = 3.76 \times O_{2,supplied}$ | m³ | Volumetric ratio |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in:
> - **Thermal Power Plants** (boiler efficiency, heat balance)
> - **IC Engine design** (BSFC, air-fuel ratio tuning, exhaust emissions)
> - **Gas Turbine combustor design** (TIT, excess air optimization)
> - **Industrial furnace** design (reverberatory, blast furnace, rotary kiln)
> - **Pollution Control** (minimize CO and unburnt HC by adjusting excess air)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for Carbon

Consider 1 kg of pure carbon burning completely:

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

- Molecular weight of C = **12 kg/kmol**
- Molecular weight of O₂ = **32 kg/kmol**

By stoichiometry: 12 kg C reacts with 32 kg O₂.

Therefore, 1 kg C requires $\tfrac{32}{12} = \tfrac{8}{3} = 2.667$ kg O₂.

**Air required for 1 kg C:**

Since air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass, the mass of air needed is:

$$m_{air} = \frac{2.667}{0.232} = 11.494 \; \text{kg air per kg C}$$

This is the **canonical answer** — every mass-based combustion problem references this number.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for Hydrogen

For 1 kg of H₂ burning:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

- 4 kg H₂ reacts with 32 kg O₂

Therefore, 1 kg H₂ requires $\tfrac{32}{4} = 8$ kg O₂.

**Air required for 1 kg H₂:**

$$m_{air} = \frac{8}{0.232} = 34.48 \; \text{kg air per kg H}_2$$

> **Key Insight:** Hydrogen requires **3 times more air per kg** than carbon because of the high H-to-mass ratio and 4 electrons per molecule of H₂.

## 3.3 Derivative: Mass of Water Formed per kg of Fuel

For a fuel containing H % hydrogen (by mass):
- Mass of H in 1 kg fuel = H/100 kg
- 4 kg H₂ produces 36 kg H₂O
- So 1 kg H₂ produces 9 kg H₂O

$$\text{Mass of H}_2\text{O formed} = 9 \times \frac{H}{100} \; \text{kg per kg fuel}$$

This is **the crucial link** between HCV and LCV. The latent heat lost = mass of water formed × $h_{fg}$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Dulong's Formula from First Principles

Dulong's formula is constructed by **linear superposition** of the heats of combustion of the constituent elements, minus the energy "lost" because some of the hydrogen is already combined with the fuel's intrinsic oxygen (forming chemically bound water).

**Step 1:** Heat from carbon:
$$Q_C = \frac{C}{100} \times 8080 \; \text{kcal/kg fuel} \; (\text{at } 8080 \; \text{kcal per kg C})$$

**Step 2:** Heat from hydrogen *actually available* for combustion:
- Total H₂ present = H/100
- H₂ already combined with O in the fuel = O/100 / 8 (since 1 kg O combines with 1/8 kg H₂ to form 1/8 kg H₂O — no, corrected: 16 kg O combines with 2 kg H₂, so 1 kg O combines with 1/8 kg H₂)
- **Available H₂** = (H/100) − (O/100)/8 = (H − O/8)/100

$$Q_H = \left(\frac{H - O/8}{100}\right) \times 34500$$

**Step 3:** Heat from sulfur:

$$Q_S = \frac{S}{100} \times 2240$$

**Step 4:** Sum:

$$\boxed{\text{GCV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right] \; \text{kcal/kg}}$$

> **Note:** This formula gives **GCV**. For LCV, subtract the latent heat of water.

## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example — Mass Basis (A KTU Board-Style Problem)

> **[Worked Example 3.5.1]**
> A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
> **C = 78 %, H = 5.2 %, O = 7.5 %, N = 1.8 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 7.0 %**
>
> Calculate:
> (a) Minimum (theoretical) mass of air required for complete combustion per kg of fuel.
> (b) Actual mass of air supplied if **30 % excess air** is used.
> (c) The GCV using Dulong's formula (in kJ/kg).
> (d) The LCV at 25 °C reference.

### Solution (a): Theoretical Air

**Step A1:** Write the general O₂ demand equation:

$$O_{2,min} = \frac{8}{3}\left(\frac{C}{100}\right) + 8\left(\frac{H}{100}\right) + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}$$

**Step A2:** Substitute the values:

$$O_{2,min} = \frac{8}{3}\left(\frac{78}{100}\right) + 8\left(\frac{5.2}{100}\right) + \frac{0.5}{100} - \frac{7.5}{100}$$

Let us evaluate each term individually:

- $\frac{8}{3} \times 0.78 = 2.080$
- $8 \times 0.052 = 0.416$
- $0.5 / 100 = 0.005$
- $7.5 / 100 = 0.075$

$$O_{2,min} = 2.080 + 0.416 + 0.005 - 0.075 = 2.426 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step A3:** Convert to theoretical air using 23.2 % O₂ by mass:

$$m_{air,theo} = \frac{2.426}{0.232} = 10.457 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

### Solution (b): Actual Air with 30 % Excess

$$m_{air,actual} = 1.30 \times 10.457 = 13.594 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

### Solution (c): Gross Calorific Value (GCV)

Using Dulong's formula in **kJ/kg**:

$$\text{GCV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[33800\,C + 144400\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9370\,S\right]$$

Compute each bracket term:

- $33800 \times 78 = 2,636,400$
- $144400 \times (5.2 - 7.5/8) = 144400 \times (5.2 - 0.9375) = 144400 \times 4.2625 = 615,505$
- $9370 \times 0.5 = 4,685$

$$\text{GCV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[2,636,400 + 615,505 + 4,685\right] = \frac{3,256,590}{100} = 32,565.9 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

### Solution (d): Lower Calorific Value (LCV)

Using $h_{fg}$ at 25 °C = 2442 kJ/kg:

$$\text{LCV} = \text{GCV} - \frac{9H}{100} \times h_{fg} = 32,565.9 - \frac{9 \times 5.2}{100} \times 2442$$

$$= 32,565.9 - 0.468 \times 2442 = 32,565.9 - 1142.9 = 31,423.0 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

> **Sanity Check:** LCV < GCV ✓. LCV/GCV ratio = 96.5 %, typical for bituminous coal (95–97 %).

## 3.6 Comprehensive Worked Example — Volume Basis (Gaseous Fuel)

> **[Worked Example 3.6.1]**
> A producer gas has the following composition by volume:
> **CO = 22 %, H₂ = 10 %, CH₄ = 3 %, CO₂ = 5 %, N₂ = 55 %, O₂ = 5 %**
>
> Calculate the theoretical volume of air required for complete combustion of 1 m³ of this gas.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify only the *combustible* components (CO, H₂, CH₄). The O₂ present in the fuel reduces external O₂ demand.

**Step 2:** O₂ required per m³ of each combustible:

| Component | % | O₂ reqd (m³/m³) | O₂ contribution |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CO | 22 | 0.5 | $22 \times 0.5 = 11.00$ |
| H₂ | 10 | 0.5 | $10 \times 0.5 = 5.00$ |
| CH₄ | 3 | 2.0 | $3 \times 2.0 = 6.00$ |
| **Total O₂ reqd** | — | — | **22.00** |
| O₂ present in fuel | 5 | — | $-5.00$ (credit) |
| **Net O₂ from air** | — | — | **17.00** |

$$V_{O_2, min} = 17.00 \; \text{m}^3 \text{ O}_2 \text{ per m}^3 \text{ fuel gas}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to air volume (air = 21 % O₂ by volume):

$$V_{air,theo} = \frac{17.00}{0.21} = 80.95 \; \text{m}^3 \text{ air per m}^3 \text{ fuel gas}$$

> **Sanity Check:** Producer gas has very high air demand (~81 m³) because of its low calorific value — exactly why producer gas is a *low-energy* industrial fuel.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Linking Combustion Concepts

| Parameter | Solid Fuel (Mass) | Gaseous Fuel (Volume) | Flue Gas (Volume) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Input Basis | 1 kg fuel | 1 m³ fuel gas | 1 m³ dry flue gas |
| Air constant | $1/0.232$ | $1/0.21$ | $3.76$ (N₂/O₂) |
| Stoichiometry | kg basis | kmol basis | N₂ balance |
| CV formula | Dulong | Sum of (C_nH_m) HCs | Not applicable |
| Excess air | $m_{actual}/m_{theo}$ | $V_{actual}/V_{theo}$ | $O_{2,fg}/O_{2,used}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: Combustion Analysis Workflow

This block-level diagram maps the **entire decision tree** from fuel sample to final CV and air-quantity determination. It is designed to be the **only diagram you need** for Module 1 revisions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Fossil Fuel Sample] --> B{Physical State?}
    B -->|Solid or Liquid| C[Ultimate Analysis by Mass]
    B -->|Gaseous| D[Volumetric Composition]
    C --> E[Identify C, H, O, S percentages]
    D --> F[Identify CH4, C2H6, CO, H2, CO2, N2 percentages]
    E --> G[Apply Stoichiometry: C, H, S, O reactions]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Calculate Minimum O2 Requirement]
    H --> I[Divide by 0.232 for Mass Basis]
    H --> J[Divide by 0.21 for Volume Basis]
    I --> K[Theoretical Air kg per kg fuel]
    J --> L[Theoretical Air m3 per m3 fuel]
    K --> M[Apply Excess Air Factor]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Actual Air Supplied]
    E --> O[Apply Dulong Formula]
    F --> P[Sum Individual Component Heat Values]
    O --> Q[Gross Calorific Value GCV]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Subtract 9 times H over 100 times hfg]
    R --> S[Lower Calorific Value LCV]
    N --> T[Air Fuel Ratio]
    Q --> U[Heat Energy Output]
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[End: Combustion System Design]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Bomb Calorimeter (GCV Measurement)

The **bomb calorimeter** is the standard experimental apparatus for measuring GCV in the lab. The functional block diagram below shows the energy balance and component interaction.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        P[Precision Pressure Gauge] --> B
        F[Fuel Pellet 1 to 2 g] --> B
        O2[Pure Oxygen at 25 to 30 atm] --> B
    end
    B[Bomb Vessel Stainless Steel] --> W[Ignition Wires]
    W --> SP[Spark 15 to 30 V]
    SP --> RB[Rapid Combustion in Bomb]
    RB --> H2O[Water Jacket Surrounding Bomb]
    H2O --> T1[Initial Temperature T1]
    H2O --> T2[Final Temperature T2]
    T1 --> DELTA[Delta T = T2 minus T1]
    T2 --> DELTA
    DELTA --> CALC[GCV = W times Delta T divided by m]
    CALC --> OUT[Output: Gross Calorific Value]
    CALC --> LCVSUB[LCV = GCV minus 9 times H over 100 times hfg]
    LCVSUB --> OUT2[Output: Net Calorific Value]
```

## 4.3 Architecture: Orsat Flue Gas Analysis (Excess Air Determination)

```mermaid
graph TD
    GAS[Sample Probe from Flue] --> C1[Gas Measuring Burette]
    C1 --> ABS1[Absorption Bulb 1: KOH absorbs CO2]
    ABS1 --> C2[Reading: CO2 percent]
    C2 --> ABS2[Absorption Bulb 2: Pyrogallol absorbs O2]
    ABS2 --> C3[Reading: O2 percent]
    C3 --> ABS3[Absorption Bulb 3: Cuprous Chloride absorbs CO]
    ABS3 --> C4[Reading: CO percent]
    C4 --> N2[By Difference: N2 = 100 minus CO2 minus O2 minus CO]
    N2 --> MAT[N2 from air = 3.76 times O2 supplied]
    MAT --> SOLVE[Solve: O2 supplied = O2 used + O2 in flue]
    SOLVE --> EA[Excess Air percent]
    SOLVE --> EFF[Combustion Efficiency]
```

## 4.4 Concept Linkage Diagram: Mass vs Volume Analysis

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Coal, Coke, Fuel Oil] -->|Mass Basis| M[Theoretical Air kg/kg]
    A -->|Dulong| M2[GCV kJ/kg]
    B[Natural Gas, Biogas, Producer Gas] -->|Volume Basis| V[Theoretical Air m3/m3]
    B -->|Sum of HCs| V2[GCV kJ/m3]
    C[Orsat Flue Gas] -->|N2 Balance| X[Excess Air percent]
    M --> D[Boiler Design]
    V --> D
    M2 --> E[Energy Content of Fuel]
    V2 --> E
    X --> D
    E --> F[Engine and Furnace Performance]
    D --> F
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

### **Q1. [3 Marks]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
**(a) Calorific Value (CV)**
**(b) Higher Calorific Value (HCV)**
**(c) Lower Calorific Value (LCV)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Calorific Value (CV):** Calorific value of a fuel is the amount of heat released per unit mass (or volume) of the fuel when it undergoes complete combustion with oxygen, and the products of combustion are cooled to a reference temperature (typically 25 °C).
*Example:* The CV of methane (CH₄) is approximately 55.5 MJ/kg.

**(b) Higher Calorific Value (HCV) / Gross Calorific Value (GCV):** It is the total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is burnt completely and the products of combustion (especially H₂O) are cooled sufficiently to allow the water vapour to condense. Thus, the **latent heat of condensation of water is included**.
*Example:* HCV of gasoline ≈ 47 MJ/kg.

**(c) Lower Calorific Value (LCV) / Net Calorific Value (NCV):** It is the *useful* heat available when 1 kg of fuel is burnt and the combustion products leave the system as hot dry gases — the water vapour **does not condense**, so the latent heat of vaporization is **lost**. *Example:* LCV of gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg.

> **Examiner's Key Concept:** HCV – LCV = (9H/100) × h_fg. **Memorize this for KTU.**

---

### **Q2. [3 Marks]**
**A fuel contains 86 % C, 14 % H₂ by mass. Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel. Take mass of O₂ in air as 23.2 %.**

#### Model Answer

**Step 1:** O₂ required for C = (8/3) × (86/100) = 2.293 kg
**Step 2:** O₂ required for H₂ = 8 × (14/100) = 1.120 kg
**Step 3:** Total O₂ = 2.293 + 1.120 = **3.413 kg O₂/kg fuel**
**Step 4:** Theoretical air = 3.413 / 0.232 = **14.71 kg air/kg fuel**

> **Marks Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Stoichiometry 1 mark, Substitutions 1 mark, Final answer with units 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Q A **or** Q B.

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A coal sample has the following composition by mass:
C = 82 %, H = 4.5 %, O = 6.0 %, N = 1.5 %, S = 1.0 %, Ash = 5.0 %.
Calculate:
(i) The minimum mass of air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of fuel.
(ii) The actual mass of air required if 25 % excess air is supplied.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Estimate the **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** and **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)** of the same coal using Dulong's formula. Take latent heat of vaporization of water as 2442 kJ/kg.

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Given:** C = 82 %, H = 4.5 %, O = 6.0 %, S = 1.0 %.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ demand (per kg fuel):**

$$O_{2,min} = \frac{8}{3}\left(\frac{82}{100}\right) + 8\left(\frac{4.5}{100}\right) + \frac{1.0}{100} - \frac{6.0}{100}$$

$$O_{2,min} = 2.1867 + 0.3600 + 0.0100 - 0.0600 = 2.4967 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical air:**

$$m_{air,theo} = \frac{2.4967}{0.232} = 10.762 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Actual air with 25 % excess:**

$$m_{air,actual} = 1.25 \times 10.762 = 13.452 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

> **Final Answer (a):** (i) 10.76 kg air/kg fuel, (ii) 13.45 kg air/kg fuel.

#### **Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Apply Dulong's formula (kJ/kg):**

$$\text{GCV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[33800\,C + 144400\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9370\,S\right]$$

Compute each term:

- $33800 \times 82 = 2,771,600$
- $144400 \times (4.5 - 6.0/8) = 144400 \times (4.5 - 0.75) = 144400 \times 3.75 = 541,500$
- $9370 \times 1.0 = 9,370$

$$\text{GCV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[2,771,600 + 541,500 + 9,370\right] = \frac{3,322,470}{100} = 33,224.7 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute LCV:**

$$\text{LCV} = \text{GCV} - \frac{9H}{100} \times h_{fg}$$

$$= 33,224.7 - \frac{9 \times 4.5}{100} \times 2442 = 33,224.7 - 988.6 = 32,236.1 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

> **Final Answer (b):** GCV = 33,225 kJ/kg, LCV = 32,236 kJ/kg.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A gaseous fuel has the following composition by volume:
**CH₄ = 40 %, C₂H₆ = 25 %, CO = 10 %, H₂ = 15 %, CO₂ = 4 %, N₂ = 6 %.**
Calculate the **theoretical volume of air** required for complete combustion of 1 m³ of this fuel gas.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If **20 % excess air** is supplied, determine:
(i) The actual volume of air supplied.
(ii) The actual volume of CO₂ and H₂O produced in combustion (per m³ of fuel).
(iii) The calorific value of the fuel, given individual HVs: CH₄ = 855 kJ/mol, C₂H₆ = 1428 kJ/mol, CO = 285 kJ/mol, H₂ = 240 kJ/mol.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — O₂ required per m³ of each combustible:**

| Gas | Volume % | O₂ per m³ | O₂ Contribution (m³) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CH₄ | 40 | 2.0 | 80.0 |
| C₂H₆ | 25 | 3.5 | 87.5 |
| CO | 10 | 0.5 | 5.0 |
| H₂ | 15 | 0.5 | 7.5 |
| **Total O₂ needed** | — | — | **180.0** |

**Step 2 — Subtract fuel's intrinsic O₂ (here 0 %):**

$$V_{O_2, min} = 180.0 / 100 = 1.80 \; \text{m}^3 \text{ O}_2/\text{m}^3 \text{ fuel gas}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to theoretical air (21 % O₂ by volume):**

$$V_{air,theo} = \frac{1.80}{0.21} = 8.571 \; \text{m}^3 \text{ air per m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

> **Final Answer (a):** 8.57 m³ air/m³ fuel gas.

#### **Solution to Q-B(b):**

**(i) Actual air with 20 % excess:**

$$V_{air,actual} = 1.20 \times 8.571 = 10.286 \; \text{m}^3 \text{ air/m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

**(ii) Products of combustion (per m³ of fuel):**

| Product | Source | Volume (m³) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| CO₂ | CH₄ (1.0 × 0.40) + C₂H₆ (2.0 × 0.25) + CO (1.0 × 0.10) | $0.40 + 0.50 + 0.10 = 1.00$ |
| H₂O | CH₄ (2.0 × 0.40) + C₂H₆ (3.0 × 0.25) + H₂ (1.0 × 0.15) | $0.80 + 0.75 + 0.15 = 1.70$ |

**(iii) Calorific Value (per m³ of fuel):**

- 1 m³ at NTP = $\frac{1000}{22.4} = 44.64$ mol of gas
- Multiply each component's HV by its volume fraction × total moles

$$\text{CV} = 44.64 \times \left[(0.40)(855) + (0.25)(1428) + (0.10)(285) + (0.15)(240)\right]$$

$$= 44.64 \times \left[342.0 + 357.0 + 28.5 + 36.0\right] = 44.64 \times 763.5$$

$$= 34,082.6 \; \text{kJ/m}^3 \text{ of fuel}$$

> **Final Answer (b):** (i) 10.29 m³ air/m³, (ii) CO₂ = 1.00 m³, H₂O = 1.70 m³, (iii) CV = 34,083 kJ/m³.

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mismatch:** Using **0.23** instead of **0.232** for air's O₂ content loses **1 mark** per question. Always use **0.232** (mass) or **0.21** (volume).
> 2. **Sign of O in Dulong's formula:** Many students write "+ O/8" instead of "− O/8". This flips the meaning — *intrinsic oxygen* **reduces** the effective hydrogen. The correct sign is **negative**.
> 3. **Confusing HCV and LCV:** IC engine, gas turbine and furnace problems use **LCV**, but Dulong gives **GCV**. Always subtract the latent heat term when the question says "net" or "lower".
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract fuel-intrinsic O₂:** For gaseous fuels, the O₂ component *reduces* air demand. Forgetting this gives a 5–10 % error.
> 5. **Not writing units:** KTU strictly awards a mark for correct units. kg/kg, m³/m³, kJ/kg — never omit them.
> 6. **Final rounding:** Round off only at the **final answer**, not in intermediate steps. Otherwise small errors compound.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this page before the exam!)**

- [x] Air contains **23.2 % O₂ by mass** and **21 % O₂ by volume**.
- [x] N₂/O₂ ratio by volume = **3.76**; by mass = **3.31**.
- [x] **1 kg C** needs **2.667 kg O₂** (or **11.49 kg air**).
- [x] **1 kg H₂** needs **8 kg O₂** (or **34.48 kg air**).
- [x] **1 kg S** needs **1 kg O₂** (or **4.31 kg air**).
- [x] 1 kg H₂ burning gives **9 kg of H₂O** → latent heat loss = 9 × h_fg.
- [x] **Dulong's formula** (kJ/kg): $\frac{1}{100}[33800\,C + 144400(H - O/8) + 9370\,S]$.
- [x] **Dulong's formula** (kcal/kg): $\frac{1}{100}[8080\,C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240\,S]$.
- [x] **HCV – LCV** = (9H/100) × 2442 kJ/kg (at 25 °C reference).
- [x] For hydrocarbon **C_nH_m**: O₂ required = (n + m/4) m³/m³, CO₂ = n, H₂O = m/2.
- [x] For flue gas: N₂ from air = 3.76 × O₂ supplied.
- [x] Excess air = (O₂ in flue / O₂ used) × 100 (vol basis).
- [x] **Stoichiometric air** is *the minimum air*; **excess air** is supplied in practice (5–25 % for gas, 10–50 % for solid fuels).
- [x] Dulong's formula is **not valid** for gaseous fuels — use component-wise summation.
- [x] **Bomb calorimeter** measures GCV; LCV is obtained by subtracting the latent heat of water formed.
- [x] The **Orsat apparatus** measures **dry** flue gas (CO₂, CO, O₂, N₂) — water is condensed before analysis.
- [x] Higher CV → Higher engine efficiency (in theory), but practically, **LCV** governs all combustion calculations.
- [x] KTU 2024 standard: **answer both Part B options mentally; choose the one with higher confidence**, since both carry 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash and Fire Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flash and Fire Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 MODULE 1: Flash and Fire Point — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FLASH POINT** is the **lowest temperature** at which the vapours of a fuel, when heated in a specified apparatus (under defined conditions), momentarily ignite (flash) when a small flame (pilot flame/test flame) is passed across its surface. The ignition is **transient** and the vapours do **NOT** continue to burn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIRE POINT** (also called the **BURN POINT**) is the **lowest temperature** at which the vapours of a fuel, when heated under identical conditions, **not only flash** but also **continue to burn steadily** for at least **5 seconds** when ignited by the pilot flame.

### Standard Units Used in KTU Examinations
- **Temperature Scale:** Degrees Celsius (°C) — most common in Indian Standards (IS 1448)
- **Apparatus Pressure:** **Atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa)**
- **Heating Rate:** As prescribed by IS/ASTM standards (typically **5–6 °C per minute** for open cup)

> [!NOTE]
> The flash and fire points are **safety-critical properties** for any fuel — they dictate how the fuel must be stored, transported, and handled. This is doubly important for **alternative fuels** like ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, and hydrogen, which have very different flash/fire characteristics than diesel or petrol.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are cooking with oil in a frying pan on a stove:

- **Flash Point Analogy:** At a certain lower temperature, you can see a brief **"poof"** of blue flame above the oil when a matchstick is brought close, but the flame **does NOT continue** — it disappears instantly. This is the **flash**. The oil is releasing enough vapour to ignite, but not enough to sustain combustion.

- **Fire Point Analogy:** At a **slightly higher temperature**, the vapours become so abundant that when you bring the match, the oil **catches fire and continues to burn** for several seconds on its own. This sustained burning is the **fire point**.

> 🔑 **Key Takeaway:** The flash point tells you *"when does it become dangerous?"* The fire point tells you *"when does it become a sustained fire hazard?"*

For fuels like petrol (gasoline), the flash point is around **−43 °C** (extremely low → highly hazardous at room temperature).
For diesel, it's around **52–96 °C** (safer to store).
For biodiesel, it's typically **130–170 °C** (very safe to store, but problematic for cold-start in engines).

---

## 1.3 Distinction Between Flash Point and Fire Point

| Property | Flash Point | Fire Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Lowest temp for momentary ignition | Lowest temp for sustained combustion (≥5 s) |
| **Vapour Quantity** | Just enough to flash | Sufficient to sustain burning |
| **Temperature Value** | **Lower** | **Higher** (typically **5–30 °C above flash point**) |
| **Fire Risk** | Momentary hazard | Sustained fire hazard |
| **Typical Test** | Open/Closed cup | Open cup (Cleveland) |
| **Numerical Relation** | $T_{fire} > T_{flash}$ | $T_{fire} \approx T_{flash} + (5 \text{ to } 30)\,°\text{C}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The difference between fire point and flash point is often called the **"fire–flash gap"** or **"fire point margin."** A **narrow gap** indicates a **highly volatile, dangerous fuel** (like petrol), while a **wide gap** indicates a **less volatile, safer fuel** (like heavy fuel oil).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The physics of flash and fire point revolves around the **vapour pressure equilibrium** above the liquid fuel surface as temperature rises.

### Step-by-Step Theory (Logical Breakdown)

1. **Initial State (Room Temperature):**
   - The liquid fuel has a low vapour pressure.
   - The concentration of fuel vapours in the air-fuel mixture above the liquid is **below the Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)**.
   - No ignition is possible — even with a pilot flame.

2. **Heating Phase (Gradual Temperature Rise):**
   - As temperature increases, **molecules gain kinetic energy** and escape from the liquid surface (evaporation increases).
   - Vapour pressure rises **exponentially** following the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation**.

3. **Approaching Flash Point:**
   - At a specific temperature, the vapour concentration above the liquid just reaches the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)**.
   - When the pilot flame is dipped, the vapours ignite momentarily — this is the **FLASH POINT**.

4. **Approaching Fire Point:**
   - With further heating, vapour concentration crosses **well above the LFL**, entering the **flammable range** (between LFL and UFL).
   - The mixture can now **sustain combustion** without continued external heating at the surface.
   - The fuel continues to burn for **at least 5 seconds** — this is the **FIRE POINT**.

5. **Beyond Fire Point:**
   - The fuel burns continuously and may reach the **spontaneous ignition temperature** if no flame is needed, but here a flame IS used.

---

## 2.2 The Clausius-Clapeyron Foundation

The vapour pressure above the liquid at any temperature is governed by:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Where:
- $P_1, P_2$ = Vapour pressures at temperatures $T_1, T_2$ (in Kelvin)
- $\Delta H_{vap}$ = Latent heat of vaporization (J/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

> 🔑 **Engineering Insight:** Fuels with **lower $\Delta H_{vap}$** (like gasoline hydrocarbons) reach the LFL at **lower temperatures** → **lower flash points** → **more hazardous**.

---

## 2.3 Open Cup vs. Closed Cup — The Apparatus Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why does an open cup method give a higher flash point than a closed cup method?"*

### Cleveland Open Cup (COC) — ASTM D92 / IS 1448 (P:36)
- Fuel is heated in an **open** brass cup.
- Vapours can **escape freely** into the atmosphere.
- The pilot flame is swept **across the surface** at regular intervals.
- **Result:** Higher flash point reading (vapours lost → need more heating to reach LFL).

### Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) — ASTM D93 / IS 1448 (P:21)
- Fuel is heated in a **closed** cup with a lid.
- Vapours are **trapped** → equilibrium builds up faster.
- Pilot flame is dipped through a shutter in the lid.
- **Result:** Lower flash point reading (vapours accumulate → reach LFL sooner).

### Abel Closed Cup — IS 1448 (P:20) — Used for **Petroleum Products below 80°C**
- Specifically designed for **low-flash fuels** like petrol, naphtha.
- Has a water bath to ensure **uniform, slow heating**.

### Tag Closed Cup — ASTM D56
- Used for **low-viscosity fuels** with flash points **below 93 °C**.

### Summary Table — Apparatus Comparison

| Apparatus | Cup Type | Typical Use | Flash Point Reading | Vapour Escape |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Abel** | Closed | Petrol, naphtha | Lowest | None (sealed) |
| **Tag** | Closed | Low-flash fuels | Low | Minimal |
| **Pensky-Martens** | Closed | Diesel, kerosene, biodiesel | Moderate | None (sealed) |
| **Cleveland** | Open | Lubricants, heavy oils, biodiesel | Highest | Free escape |

---

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clausius-Clapeyron** | $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K, $\Delta H$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Predicts vapour pressure vs. temperature |
| **Fire–Flash Gap** | $\Delta T_{fire-flash} = T_{fire} - T_{flash}$ | $\Delta T$ in °C, typical = 5–30 °C | Indicates volatility/safety margin |
| **Antoine Equation** | $\log_{10}(P) = A - \frac{B}{C + T(°C)}$ | $P$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C, $A,B,C$ = constants | Accurate vapour pressure prediction |
| **Flammability Range** | $LFL < \phi < UFL$ | $\phi$ = fuel-air ratio | Defines combustion range |
| **Conversion** | $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | — | Required for absolute temperature |
| **Vapour Density Relation** | $\rho_{vapour} \propto \frac{PM}{RT}$ | $M$ = molar mass | Vapour accumulation rate |

> [!NOTE]
> The Clausius-Clapeyron equation is the **theoretical backbone** of flash point physics. In KTU problems, you may be asked to estimate one temperature given another, using the latent heat of vaporization.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

1. **Storage & Transport Safety:** Fuels with low flash points (petrol, ethanol) need **flameproof storage**, special tanker designs, and **grounding** to prevent static discharge.
2. **Insurance & Hazard Classification:** Flash point determines the **NFPA (National Fire Protection Association) rating** and **transport hazard class** (Class 3 flammable liquids for flash point < 93 °C).
3. **Engine Cold-Start Performance:** For IC engines, a **low flash point** is desirable because the fuel vaporizes easily at low temperatures, ensuring **easy starting in cold weather**. This is why petrol (flash point ~−43 °C) starts better in winter than diesel (flash point ~55 °C).
4. **Alternative Fuel Selection:** Biodiesel has a **high flash point (~130–170 °C)** → **very safe to store** but **poor cold-start**. Ethanol has a **low flash point (~13 °C)** → **highly flammable** but **excellent cold-start**.
5. **Refinery Process Control:** Flash point helps determine the **distillation cut** of crude oil fractions.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Clausius-Clapeyron for Vapour Pressure

We will derive the relation that connects vapour pressure and temperature, which is the **theoretical basis** for flash and fire point.

### Starting Point: Phase Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium between liquid and vapour, the molar Gibbs free energy of both phases is equal:

$$G_{liquid}(T, P) = G_{vapour}(T, P)$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to temperature at constant pressure:

$$dG_{liquid} = dG_{vapour}$$

Using the fundamental thermodynamic relation $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$, and at constant pressure ($dP = 0$):

$$-S_{liquid}\,dT = -S_{vapour}\,dT$$

Rearranging:

$$(S_{vapour} - S_{liquid})\,dT = 0$$

Wait — this gives zero. We need to consider **pressure dependence** as well. Let's do the proper derivation using the **Clapeyron equation**.

### Step 1: Clapeyron Equation (General Phase Change)

For any phase transition, the slope of the coexistence curve is given by:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V} = \frac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$

Where:
- $\Delta S$ = entropy change of phase change
- $\Delta H$ = enthalpy change (latent heat) of phase change
- $\Delta V$ = volume change during phase change
- $T$ = absolute temperature

### Step 2: Apply Assumptions for Liquid → Vapour Transition

For vaporization, we assume:
- Vapour behaves as an **ideal gas**: $V_{vapour} = \frac{RT}{P}$
- Molar volume of liquid is **negligible** compared to vapour: $V_{vapour} \gg V_{liquid}$

So:

$$\Delta V = V_{vapour} - V_{liquid} \approx V_{vapour} = \frac{RT}{P}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Clapeyron Equation

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot (RT/P)} = \frac{P \cdot \Delta H_{vap}}{R \cdot T^2}$$

### Step 4: Separate Variables and Integrate

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2 (assuming $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant over the temperature range — a standard assumption for short temperature intervals):

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

### Step 5: Final Form (Clausius-Clapeyron Equation)

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$

This is the **final form** of the Clausius-Clapeyron equation, which describes how vapour pressure changes with temperature — the very relationship that determines **when flash point and fire point are reached**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example Problem (KTU Style)

> **[Numerical Problem — 7 Marks Style]**
> The vapour pressure of a biodiesel sample is **1.2 kPa at 150 °C**. The latent heat of vaporization of biodiesel is **52,000 J/mol**. At what temperature will the vapour pressure reach **5.0 kPa** (the threshold approximately corresponding to flash point conditions)? Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

### Given Data
- $P_1 = 1.2$ kPa
- $T_1 = 150\,°\text{C} = 150 + 273.15 = 423.15$ K
- $P_2 = 5.0$ kPa
- $\Delta H_{vap} = 52{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T_2 = ?$

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write the Clausius-Clapeyron equation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values:

$$\ln\left(\frac{5.0}{1.2}\right) = -\frac{52{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{423.15}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the left side:

$$\ln(4.1667) = 1.4271$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the coefficient:

$$-\frac{52{,}000}{8.314} = -6254.51 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$1.4271 = -6254.51\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{423.15}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Divide both sides:

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{423.15} = \frac{1.4271}{-6254.51} = -2.2815 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $\frac{1}{T_2}$:

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{423.15} - 2.2815 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{423.15} = 2.3632 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 2.3632 \times 10^{-3} - 0.2282 \times 10^{-3} = 2.1350 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 8:** Invert to find $T_2$:

$$T_2 = \frac{1}{2.1350 \times 10^{-3}} = 468.38 \text{ K}$$

**Step 9:** Convert back to °C:

$$T_2 = 468.38 - 273.15 = 195.23\,°\text{C}$$

### ✅ Final Answer

> The vapour pressure reaches **5.0 kPa at approximately 195 °C** — consistent with the typical flash point range of biodiesel (130–170 °C for closed cup, 150–195 °C for open cup).

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Flash & Fire Points of Conventional vs. Alternative Fuels

| Fuel | Type | Flash Point (°C, Closed Cup) | Fire Point (°C) | Fire–Flash Gap (°C) | Hazard Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Petrol (Gasoline)** | Conventional | **−43** | ≈ −38 | ~5 | Class I (Highly flammable) |
| **Diesel** | Conventional | **52–96** | ≈ 75–110 | ~10–20 | Class II/III |
| **Kerosene** | Conventional | **38–72** | ≈ 60–85 | ~15–20 | Class II |
| **Ethanol (E100)** | Alternative | **13** | ≈ 18 | ~5 | Class IB (Highly flammable) |
| **Methanol** | Alternative | **11** | ≈ 16 | ~5 | Class IB (Highly flammable) |
| **Biodiesel (B100, FAME)** | Alternative | **130–170** | ≈ 160–190 | ~20–30 | Class IIIB (Combustible) |
| **Hydrogen (H₂)** | Alternative | **< −50** (gas) | N/A (gas) | N/A | Extremely hazardous |
| **CNG / LNG** | Alternative | **< −150** (gas) | N/A (gas) | N/A | Extremely hazardous |
| **Vegetable Oil (Straight)** | Alternative | **~280** | ~310 | ~30 | Class IIIB (Low hazard) |

> 🔑 **Engineering Insight from Matrix:** Hydrogen and CNG have **no meaningful flash point in the liquid state** because they are gases at room temperature — they form flammable mixtures with air at almost any temperature, which is why they require **completely sealed systems**.

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Relation — Fire Point Margin Significance

The **fire–flash gap** is a critical safety metric. A small gap indicates:

$$\text{Small } \Delta T_{fire-flash} \implies \text{High volatility} \implies \text{High fire hazard}$$

Mathematically, the fire point is empirically related to the flash point for **hydrocarbon fuels** by:

$$T_{fire} \approx T_{flash} + k$$

Where $k$ is typically **5–30 °C** depending on the fuel class:
- **Paraffins** (e.g., hexane): $k \approx 5$ °C (narrow gap)
- **Aromatics** (e.g., benzene): $k \approx 5$ °C (narrow gap)
- **Heavy oils**: $k \approx 25$ °C (wide gap)

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]
**Define flash point and fire point. What is the significance of the fire point in fuel handling?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Flash Point (1 Mark):** Flash point is the **lowest temperature** at which the vapours of a fuel, when heated in a specified apparatus, **momentarily ignite** when a small pilot flame is brought near the surface. The ignition is **transient** and does not sustain.

**Fire Point (1 Mark):** Fire point is the **lowest temperature** at which the fuel vapours **not only flash** but also **continue to burn steadily** for at least **5 seconds** after ignition by the pilot flame.

**Significance (1 Mark):** The fire point indicates the **sustained fire hazard** of a fuel. Above the fire point, the fuel can support **continuous combustion** without external heat input, posing a serious risk during storage, transport, and handling. It is critical for **insurance classification** and **safety protocols**.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Why does the closed cup method give a lower flash point reading than the open cup method?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

In the **closed cup method** (Pensky-Martens or Abel), the fuel is heated in a **sealed cup** with a lid. The vapours generated are **trapped inside** the cup, leading to **rapid accumulation** of fuel-air mixture above the liquid surface (1 Mark).

As a result, the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** is reached at a **lower temperature** because vapour concentration builds up efficiently without any loss to the surroundings (1 Mark).

In contrast, the **open cup method** (Cleveland) allows vapours to **escape freely** into the atmosphere. To achieve the LFL at the surface, a **higher temperature** is required, resulting in a **higher flash point reading** (1 Mark).

Typical difference: **5–15 °C** higher for open cup compared to closed cup for the same fuel.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model]

**(a)** Define flash point and fire point with a neat sketch. Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of the **Cleveland Open Cup (COC) apparatus** used for flash and fire point determination. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fuel has a vapour pressure of **0.8 kPa at 120 °C** and a latent heat of vaporization of **45,000 J/mol**. Estimate the temperature at which its vapour pressure reaches **3.5 kPa** (approximately the flash point condition). Use the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Comment on the safety of this fuel. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Flash Point (1 Mark):**
Flash point is the lowest temperature at which the vapour of a fuel, when heated in a Cleveland Open Cup apparatus, momentarily ignites (flashes) when a pilot flame is passed across the surface.

**Definition of Fire Point (1 Mark):**
Fire point is the lowest temperature at which the vapours continue to burn sustainably for at least 5 seconds after the pilot flame is applied.

**Construction of Cleveland Open Cup Apparatus (3 Marks):**
- A **brass cup** (capacity ~70 mL) holds the fuel sample.
- The cup is placed in a **heating plate** (electric or gas).
- A **thermometer** is inserted to measure the fuel temperature.
- A **pilot flame** (small gas jet) is mounted on a swiveling arm that can be moved across the cup surface.
- The cup is exposed to **atmospheric air** (open design — no lid).

**Working (2 Marks):**
1. The fuel is filled in the cup up to the marked level.
2. The fuel is heated at a rate of **5–6 °C per minute**.
3. At every **2 °C rise**, the pilot flame is swept across the surface.
4. The temperature at which a momentary flash appears = **Flash Point**.
5. Heating is continued; the temperature at which the fuel **burns continuously for ≥5 seconds** = **Fire Point**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 0.8$ kPa, $T_1 = 120\,°\text{C} = 393.15$ K
- $P_2 = 3.5$ kPa, $T_2 = ?$
- $\Delta H_{vap} = 45{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (1 Mark):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Substitute Values (2 Marks):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{3.5}{0.8}\right) = -\frac{45{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{393.15}\right)$$

$$\ln(4.375) = -5412.8 \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - 2.5434 \times 10^{-3}\right)$$

$$1.4759 = -5412.8 \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - 2.5434 \times 10^{-3}\right)$$

**Solve for $T_2$ (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - 2.5434 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{1.4759}{-5412.8} = -2.7267 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 2.5434 \times 10^{-3} - 2.7267 \times 10^{-4} = 2.2707 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{1}{2.2707 \times 10^{-3}} = 440.39 \text{ K} = 167.24\,°\text{C}$$

**Final Answer (1 Mark):**
$$T_2 \approx 167\,°\text{C}$$

**Safety Comment (1 Mark):**
A flash point of ~167 °C indicates a **moderately safe fuel** (similar to biodiesel range). It can be stored under normal conditions but should be kept away from high-temperature surfaces and open flames.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **differences between open cup and closed cup methods** for flash point determination. Discuss the **Abel apparatus** and its application. Why is the Abel apparatus preferred for petrol? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the flash and fire points of **petrol, diesel, ethanol, and biodiesel**. Explain why **hydrogen and CNG** do not have a meaningful flash point. Relate this to engine cold-start performance. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Open Cup vs. Closed Cup (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Open Cup (COC) | Closed Cup (PMCC/Abel) |
|---|---|---|
| Vapour escape | Free to atmosphere | Trapped inside cup |
| Flash point reading | Higher (5–15 °C) | Lower |
| Heating rate | 5–6 °C/min | Slower (1–2 °C/min for Abel) |
| Application | Heavy oils, lubricants, biodiesel | Petrol, diesel, kerosene |
| Air circulation | Yes | No (sealed) |

**Abel Apparatus (2 Marks):**
- It is a **closed cup apparatus** used for fuels with flash points **below 80 °C**.
- The fuel cup is immersed in a **water bath** for uniform, slow heating.
- A **shutter mechanism** allows the pilot flame to dip into the cup at regular intervals.
- A **thermometer** measures the fuel temperature precisely.

**Why Abel is Preferred for Petrol (2 Marks):**
- Petrol has an **extremely low flash point (~−43 °C)**, which requires a **closed system** to prevent vapour loss and ensure accurate measurement.
- The **water bath** in the Abel apparatus provides **gentle, uniform heating**, preventing premature vaporization.
- The **sealed design** allows the LFL to be reached at the correct low temperature without vapours escaping.
- It is the **standard IS 1448 (P:20)** method for petroleum products below 80 °C in India.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Fuel | Flash Point (°C) | Fire Point (°C) | Cold-Start Performance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Petrol | −43 | ≈ −38 | Excellent |
| Diesel | 55–96 | ≈ 75–110 | Poor (needs glow plugs) |
| Ethanol | 13 | ≈ 18 | Excellent |
| Biodiesel | 130–170 | ≈ 160–190 | Very poor |

**Why Hydrogen and CNG Have No Meaningful Flash Point (2 Marks):**
- Hydrogen and CNG are **gaseous at room temperature and atmospheric pressure**.
- They do not have a **liquid phase** under normal conditions, so the concept of "heating a liquid to produce vapours" is **not applicable**.
- They form **flammable mixtures with air at virtually any temperature** above their very low auto-ignition temperatures (H₂: 500 °C; CNG: 540 °C).
- Therefore, their **safety is governed by leak prevention and flammability limits**, not by flash/fire point.

**Relation to Cold-Start Performance (2 Marks):**
- A **low flash point** means the fuel vaporizes easily at low ambient temperatures.
- Petrol (−43 °C) and ethanol (13 °C) **vaporize readily** in cold weather → **excellent cold-start**.
- Diesel (55–96 °C) does **not vaporize well** in cold weather → **poor cold-start**, requires **glow plugs** or **block heaters**.
- Biodiesel (130–170 °C) is even worse in cold weather → requires **blending with diesel** for cold-start capability.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and lose marks for):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Flash Point and Fire Point definitions** — Remember: Flash = *momentary*; Fire = *sustained ≥5 seconds*. (Lose 1–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. The equation REQUIRES absolute temperature. (Lose 2–3 marks)
>
> 3. **Confusing Open Cup and Closed Cup** — Students often write "open cup gives lower flash point," which is WRONG. Open cup gives HIGHER flash point. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 4. **Ignoring the sign convention** in Clausius-Clapeyron: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$. The negative sign is CRITICAL. (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 5. **Not specifying the apparatus** in definitions (e.g., saying "flash point is..." without mentioning "Cleveland Open Cup" or "Pensky-Martens"). Examiners expect apparatus context. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 6. **Forgetting the 5-second criterion** for fire point — it's a specific time, not just "sustained burning."

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Flash Point** = Lowest temperature for **momentary** ignition (flash).
- ✅ **Fire Point** = Lowest temperature for **sustained burning (≥5 seconds)**.
- ✅ **Fire Point > Flash Point** always (by 5–30 °C).
- ✅ **Closed cup** method (Pensky-Martens, Abel) gives **lower** flash point than open cup (Cleveland).
- ✅ **Abel apparatus** → for fuels with flash point **< 80 °C** (e.g., petrol).
- ✅ **Cleveland Open Cup** → for high flash point fuels (oils, biodiesel).
- ✅ **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** governs vapour pressure vs. temperature:

$$\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

- ✅ **Petrol** flash point ≈ **−43 °C** (extremely hazardous).
- ✅ **Diesel** flash point ≈ **52–96 °C** (moderately safe).
- ✅ **Ethanol** flash point ≈ **13 °C** (highly flammable).
- ✅ **Biodiesel** flash point ≈ **130–170 °C** (very safe storage).
- ✅ **Hydrogen & CNG** have **no meaningful flash point** (gaseous fuels).
- ✅ **Lower flash point = Better cold-start** but **higher fire hazard**.
- ✅ **Fire–Flash Gap** indicates volatility; small gap → high volatility → high hazard.
- ✅ Always use **absolute temperature (Kelvin)** in Clausius-Clapeyron.
- ✅ **Heating rate** in open cup: **5–6 °C/min**; in closed cup: **1–2 °C/min** (Abel).
- ✅ Flash point determines **NFPA hazard class** and **transport regulations**.
- ✅ The fire point is **always higher** than the flash point for any fuel.
- ✅ **Alternative fuels** like ethanol and methanol are **more hazardous** (low flash) than biodiesel (high flash).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Octane Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Octane Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Octane Number (ON)**, also called **Octane Rating (OR)**, is a dimensionless, standardized scalar metric that quantifies the **resistance of a spark-ignition (SI) engine fuel to auto-ignition (detonation or "knock")** during the combustion process. It is determined by comparing the knocking behavior of an unknown fuel against a binary reference blend of two pure paraffinic hydrocarbons under strictly controlled engine operating conditions.

The two reference hydrocarbons used in the ASTM standard (CFR – Cooperative Fuel Research engine test method) are:

| Reference Hydrocarbon | Chemical Name | Structure | Assigned Octane Number |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Iso-octane** | 2,2,4-Trimethylpentane | C₈H₁₈ (branched) | **100** (excellent knock resistance) |
| **n-Heptane** | Normal heptane | C₇H₁₆ (straight chain) | **0** (highly prone to knock) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Fundamental Rule (KTU 2024 Syllabus Statement):**  
> The octane number of any test fuel is numerically equal to the **percentage by volume of iso-octane** in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane that exhibits **identical knock intensity** in a standardized CFR engine test.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$
ON = \text{Vol}_{\%} \, \text{ of iso-octane in the matching reference blend}
$$

### 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

Imagine two students taking the same high-pressure mathematics examination:

- **Student A (Iso-octane, ON = 100):** Calm, composed, and works through difficult problems in a measured, sequential order without panicking — even when the pressure builds up.
- **Student B (n-Heptane, ON = 0):** Easily stressed, becomes erratic, and produces sudden, uncontrolled bursts of answers (analogous to premature, uncontrolled combustion = "knock").

**Octane Number** is essentially a "**composure score**" of a fuel under high pressure and temperature inside a combustion chamber. A high ON fuel resists premature ignition; a low ON fuel "panics" and ignites spontaneously before the spark plug fires.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engine Knocking (Detonation):** The audible metallic "pinging" sound produced when the air–fuel mixture auto-ignites in multiple uncontrolled flame fronts instead of the controlled, smooth propagation from the spark plug. It causes **loss of power, overheating, and severe mechanical damage** to pistons, rings, and bearings.

### 1.3 Why Octane Number Matters in Engineering

Modern, high-compression-ratio engines (typical CR of 9:1 to 13:1) demand fuels with high knock resistance. Using a fuel with an octane number **lower than the engine's requirement (Minimum Octane Requirement, MOR)** leads to:

1. Mechanical failure of engine components
2. Reduced thermal efficiency
3. Increased exhaust emissions (HC, CO, NOx)
4. Loss of power output
5. Increased fuel consumption (rich mixture compensation)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Standard Test Engines Used Worldwide:**  
> - **CFR Engine (ASTM D2699 / D2700)** – Primary reference method (USA)  
> - **BASF Engine, Waukesha Engine** – Modified CFR variants  
> - **On-Board Octane Rating Sensors** – Modern automotive knock sensors (real-time KN feedback to ECU)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Standardized Test Methods

The octane number of a fuel is **not an intrinsic thermodynamic property**; it is an **empirical, engine-test-derived number** that depends strictly on the test conditions. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

#### 2.1.1 Research Octane Number (RON) – ASTM D2699
- Engine speed: **600 rpm** (mild conditions)
- **No** intake air swirl
- Variable compression ratio (knock is matched)
- Cooled mixture, low heat soak
- Represents fuel behavior under **low-to-moderate load** (typical city driving)
- Generally **higher** value than MON for the same fuel

#### 2.1.2 Motor Octane Number (MON) – ASTM D2700
- Engine speed: **900 rpm** (harsh conditions)
- **High** intake air swirl (mixture pre-heated)
- Heated intake manifold (150 °C)
- Represents fuel behavior under **high-speed, high-temperature** operation (highway, full-throttle)
