- Generally **lower** value than RON for the same fuel

> [!NOTE]  
> **Sensitivity (S):**  
> Sensitivity is a hidden KTU-favorite parameter that measures how a fuel's knock resistance changes between mild and severe operating conditions:  
> $$S = RON - MON$$  
> A fuel with high sensitivity is "**softer**" — its performance drops sharply under hot, high-speed conditions. Branched paraffins and aromatics have lower sensitivity; olefins and alcohols tend to have higher sensitivity.

### 2.2 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All absolute value / magnitude delimiters use `\vert` (not `|`) to prevent markdown table parser breakage.

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbolic Form | Units | Validity / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Octane Number** | $ON = \dfrac{V_{iso}}{V_{iso} + V_{hep}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | $0 \le ON \le 100$ (extensible > 100 with leaded reference or alcohol blends) |
| 2 | **Research Octane Number** | $RON = f(T_{intake} = \text{ambient}, \; N = 600\,\text{rpm})$ | dimensionless | ASTM D2699 |
| 3 | **Motor Octane Number** | $MON = f(T_{intake} = 150°\text{C}, \; N = 900\,\text{rpm})$ | dimensionless | ASTM D2700 |
| 4 | **Anti-Knock Index (AKI / Pump Octane)** | $AKI = \dfrac{RON + MON}{2}$ | dimensionless | Posted at fuel pumps in USA, Canada |
| 5 | **Sensitivity** | $S = RON - MON$ | dimensionless | $0 \le S \le 12$ (typical range) |
| 6 | **Simple Blending ON (Volume Average)** | $ON_{blend} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot ON_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}$ | dimensionless | Approximation; ignores non-linear mixing effects |
| 7 | **True Blending Octane Number (TBON)** | $ON_{blend} = \dfrac{\sum V_i \cdot BON_i}{\sum V_i}$ | dimensionless | $BON_i$ is empirical blending octane number |
| 8 | **Knock Resistance Limit Equation** | $ON_{required} \geq MOR_{engine}$ | dimensionless | $MOR$ = Minimum Octane Requirement of engine |
| 9 | **Knock Limited Compression Ratio (KLRC)** | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | dimensionless | Used to size compression ratio against ON |
| 10 | **Cetane ↔ Octane Inverse Trend (diesel)** | $CN \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; ON \downarrow$ | dimensionless | Applies across fuel families (not for blending) |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of Octane Number

The octane number governs **four critical engineering decisions** in real-world powertrain design:

1. **Engine Compression Ratio Selection:**  
   The higher the ON of available fuel in a region, the higher the compression ratio that can be designed — directly boosting the theoretical Otto-cycle efficiency (Formula 9 above).
2. **Knock Sensor Calibration & ECU Spark-Map Tuning:**  
   Modern ECUs use ON as an input to compute the **optimum ignition advance angle (θ_advance)**. High-ON fuels allow greater advance, releasing more energy before peak pressure, improving torque.
3. **Refinery Process Selection:**  
   Catalytic reforming, isomerization, alkylation, and hydrocracking are all designed to **raise the octane number** of gasoline pool stocks. ON is the refinery's "currency."
4. **Alternative Fuel Blending Strategy:**  
   Ethanol (ON = 108), methanol (ON = 109), iso-butanol (ON = 105), and toluene (ON = 114) are blended into gasoline to **raise the effective ON of the fuel mix** — central to KTU Module 1 (Alternative Fuels).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Octane Number > 100 (Super-Ref / Racing Fuels):**  
> Standard reference blends cap at 100 because iso-octane is taken as 100. However, real fuels like **ethanol (ON = 108)**, **toluene (ON = 114)**, and **isooctane + TEL (Tetraethyl Lead)** historically produced values up to **120+**. To handle this, the test uses **reference fuels doped with tetraethyl lead (TEL)** to create "lead octane number" standards above 100, or **delta blending techniques** are employed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Octane Number Definition from First Principles

The octane number is **empirically defined**, not derived from a physical law. We will now show the logical foundation step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define the scale anchor points.**

Two paraffinic hydrocarbons are chosen to anchor the scale because their combustion chemistry is well-characterized and they bracket the typical knock behavior of commercial gasoline.

- Reference fuel **A** (n-heptane, C₇H₁₆): extremely knock-prone → assign $ON_A = 0$.
- Reference fuel **B** (iso-octane, C₈H₁₈): highly knock-resistant → assign $ON_B = 100$.

**Step 2 — Construct a linear blend axis.**

Let $x$ be the volume fraction of iso-octane and $(1 - x)$ be the volume fraction of n-heptane in the reference blend. We postulate a **linear anti-knock scale**:

$$
ON_{ref}(x) = a + b \cdot x
$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions.**

For pure n-heptane, $x = 0$:

$$
ON_{ref}(0) = a + b \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 0
$$

For pure iso-octane, $x = 1$:

$$
ON_{ref}(1) = 0 + b \cdot 1 = 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = 100
$$

**Step 4 — Final form of the linear scale.**

Substituting $a$ and $b$ back:

$$
ON_{ref}(x) = 100 \cdot x
$$

Since $x$ is the volume fraction of iso-octane, the octane number of the reference blend equals the percentage by volume of iso-octane, hence:

$$
\boxed{\,ON = 100 \cdot x = \dfrac{V_{iso}}{V_{iso} + V_{hep}} \times 100\,}
$$

This proves the KTU-stated definition is mathematically equivalent to a **linear interpolation** between the two anchor points.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why linear?** The linear assumption holds only approximately because the actual relationship between fuel composition and knock intensity is non-linear (due to complex chain-branching chemistry of radical auto-ignition). ASTM specifies that the test must be conducted so the unknown fuel's knock intensity is matched by the reference blend — this **empirical matching** is the actual physical basis, and the linear formula is the **numerical shortcut** used to read off the result.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Anti-Knock Index (AKI / Posted Octane)

The AKI posted at fuel pumps is a **simple arithmetic mean of RON and MON**, justified by historical correlation with real-world driving knock behavior.

**Step 1 — Define observed on-road knock frequency.**

Let $K$ be the observed knock frequency in a statistically averaged urban-highway driving cycle.

**Step 2 — Empirical correlation from road-knock studies (1940s Waukesha data).**

Cooperative Fuel Research Committee data showed that the average road-knock rating correlated with the arithmetic mean:

$$
K \approx \dfrac{RON + MON}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Adopting the AKI as the Pump Octane Number.**

By definition, the value posted at fuel dispensers is:

$$
\boxed{\,AKI = \dfrac{RON + MON}{2}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Numerical Example (Practice):** A gasoline has RON = 95 and MON = 85. Find (a) AKI, (b) Sensitivity.  
> (a) $AKI = (95 + 85) / 2 = 90$  
> (b) $S = 95 - 85 = 10$  
> **Interpretation:** This fuel is suitable for engines requiring pump octane ≥ 90; under high-load, it loses 10 ON points of resistance.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Octane Numbers of Common Fuels (High-Yield for KTU)

| Fuel | RON | MON | AKI | Sensitivity | Source / Family |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| n-Heptane | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Paraffin (low) |
| n-Hexane | 25 | 26 | 25.5 | -1 | Straight paraffin |
| n-Pentane | 62 | 62 | 62 | 0 | Light paraffin |
| Iso-octane | 100 | 100 | 100 | 0 | Branched paraffin (reference) |
| **Regular Gasoline (India)** | 91 | 81 | 86 | 10 | Refinery blend |
| **Premium Gasoline (India)** | 95 | 85 | 90 | 10 | Refinery blend |
| **Euro V E10 Petrol** | 95–98 | 85–88 | 90–93 | 10 | E10 = 10% ethanol |
| Toluene | 121 | 109 | 115 | 12 | Aromatic |
| **Methanol** | 109 | 89 | 99 | 20 | Alcohol (alt fuel) |
| **Ethanol (anhydrous)** | 108 | 89 | 98.5 | 19 | Alcohol (alt fuel) |
| **iso-Butanol** | 105 | 90 | 97.5 | 15 | Alcohol (alt fuel) |
| LPG | 111 | 97 | 104 | 14 | Liquefied Petroleum Gas |
| CNG | 130 | 120 | 125 | 10 | Compressed Natural Gas |
| Hydrogen | 130+ | — | — | — | Used in HCCI / dual-fuel |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Quick Logic:** The general trend is: **branching ↑, aromaticity ↑, oxygen content ↑ → ON ↑** for a fixed carbon number. Conversely, **straight-chain paraffins and long alkanes have very low ON** (e.g., n-hexadecane, ON ≈ -30 — negative octane number fuels actually exist and **reduce** the ON of gasoline when blended).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem: Blending Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A refinery blends three gasoline components to produce 10,000 L of finished gasoline. The blend has the following composition:
- Component A (straight-run gasoline): 4000 L, RON = 72
- Component B (reformed gasoline): 3500 L, RON = 96
- Component C (alkylate): 2500 L, RON = 92

Calculate the **blend RON** using the simple linear blending rule.

**Step 1 — State the linear blending formula.**

$$
RON_{blend} = \dfrac{V_A \cdot RON_A + V_B \cdot RON_B + V_C \cdot RON_C}{V_A + V_B + V_C}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
RON_{blend} = \dfrac{(4000)(72) + (3500)(96) + (2500)(92)}{4000 + 3500 + 2500}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_A \cdot RON_A &= 4000 \times 72 = 288{,}000 \\[4pt]
V_B \cdot RON_B &= 3500 \times 96 = 336{,}000 \\[4pt]
V_C \cdot RON_C &= 2500 \times 92 = 230{,}000 \\[4pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the numerator and denominator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 288{,}000 + 336{,}000 + 230{,}000 = 854{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 10{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation.**

$$
\boxed{\,RON_{blend} = \dfrac{854{,}000}{10{,}000} = 85.4\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The blended fuel meets Indian Regular-grade specifications (RON ≥ 91 requires addition of higher-ON component, e.g., ethanol or toluene).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Pitfall — Linear Blending Overestimates:**  
> In reality, blending is **non-linear** because each component has a different "**Blending Octane Number (BON)**" that depends on the matrix fuel. The **Syn-thetic Mixture Method (SMM)** and **D-Combustion Method (DCM)** give more accurate blended ON. Students writing "linear is exact" lose 2 marks in 14-mark problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Octane Number Determination Workflow (CFR Engine Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Test Fuel Sample Arrives]) --> A1[Step 1: Pour Test Fuel into CFR Test Engine Cylinder]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Run Engine at Standardized Conditions\n600 rpm RON OR 900 rpm MON]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Adjust Compression Ratio to Find Knocking Threshold]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Record Knock Meter Reading K_test]
    A4 --> B0[Step 5: Repeat with Reference Blends\nIso-octane + n-heptane at varying ratios]
    B0 --> B1[Step 6: Find Reference Blend whose\nK_ref matches K_test]
    B1 --> B2[Step 7: Read Iso-octane Volume Percent]
    B2 --> C0{Is Iso-octane Vol % in 0 to 100 range?}
    C0 -- Yes --> C1[Output: Octane Number = Iso-octane Vol %]
    C0 -- No, exceeds 100 --> C2[Output: Octane Number using Lead-doped Reference Scale or Delta-blend Method]
    C1 --> D0([Final ON Reported as RON or MON])
    C2 --> D0
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Why Octane Number Matters — Engine Knock Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0([High Compression Ratio Engine]) --> P1[In-Cylinder Pressure and Temperature Increase]
    P1 --> P2{Fuel ON meets Minimum Octane Requirement?}
    P2 -- No --> P3[Premature Auto-Ignition - Engine Knock]
    P3 --> P4[Engine Damage: Piston Overheating, Bearing Failure, Power Loss]
    P2 -- Yes --> P5[Controlled Combustion from Spark Plug]
    P5 --> P6[Smooth Flame Front Propagation]
    P6 --> P7[High Thermal Efficiency, Low Emissions, Full Power Output]
    P4 --> P8([Knock Sensor ECU Retards Spark Timing])
    P8 --> P9[Power Loss, Driver Feels Hesitation]
    P7 --> P10([Optimal ECU Performance])
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Octane Number Hierarchy of Reference Fuels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Reference Fuel Scale Anchors] --> R1[Bottom Anchor: n-Heptane C7H16, ON = 0]
    R0 --> R2[Top Anchor: Iso-octane C8H18, ON = 100]
    R1 --> S1[ON 0 to 30: Highly Knocking Fuels\nstraight chain alkanes n-hexane n-heptane]
    S1 --> S2[ON 30 to 70: Mild Knock\nn-pentane light naphtha]
    S2 --> S3[ON 70 to 90: Commercial Gasoline\nRegular Grade RON]
    S3 --> S4[ON 90 to 100: Premium Gasoline\nHigh Octane RON]
    S4 --> S5[ON 100 Plus: Racing Fuels\nAlcohols, Aromatics, CNG, H2]
    R2 --> S5
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Octane Number vs. Other Fuel Quality Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q0[Fuel Quality Parameters Family] --> Q1[SI Engines - Spark Ignition]
    Q1 --> Q1a[Octane Number ON]
    Q1a --> Q1a1[RON: Mild Conditions 600 rpm]
    Q1a --> Q1a2[MON: Harsh Conditions 900 rpm]
    Q1a --> Q1a3[AKI: Pump Posted Value]
    Q1a --> Q1a4[Sensitivity: RON minus MON]
    Q0 --> Q2[CI Engines - Compression Ignition]
    Q2 --> Q2a[Cetane Number CN]
    Q2a --> Q2a1[Inverse Relationship to ON]
    Q0 --> Q3[Aviation Gasoline]
    Q3 --> Q3a[Aviation Octane Number AON or Performance Number PN]
    Q0 --> Q4[Reformulated and Alt Fuels]
    Q4 --> Q4a[Research Octane Number with Alt Fuel Context]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMUT411]**

Define the term "Octane Number" of a fuel. Explain the significance of the two reference hydrocarbons used in its determination.

**Model Answer:**

The **Octane Number (ON)** of a fuel is a dimensionless numerical rating that indicates the **resistance of the fuel to knocking (auto-ignition) in a spark-ignition engine**. It is defined as the percentage by volume of iso-octane in a reference blend of iso-octane and n-heptane that matches the knocking behavior of the test fuel under standard CFR engine test conditions.

The two reference hydrocarbons are:

1. **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane, C₈H₁₈):** A branched-chain paraffin exhibiting excellent knock resistance → assigned **ON = 100**.
2. **n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆):** A straight-chain paraffin with very poor knock resistance → assigned **ON = 0**.

**Significance:** These two hydrocarbons provide fixed, reproducible anchor points at the extremes of the knock-resistance scale. By blending them in different volume ratios, a continuous linear scale from 0 to 100 is established, against which any unknown fuel can be benchmarked. The choice of these specific compounds is justified by their pure paraffinic structure, chemical stability, and well-characterized combustion behavior.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for explaining reference fuels and significance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEMUT411]**

Distinguish between **Research Octane Number (RON)** and **Motor Octane Number (MON)**. Why is their average posted at fuel pumps?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RON | MON |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | ASTM D2699 | ASTM D2700 |
| Engine Speed | 600 rpm (low) | 900 rpm (high) |
| Intake Air | No swirl, ambient temp | High swirl, 150 °C heated |
| Operating Severity | Mild | Severe (high load / speed) |
| Typical Value | Higher | Lower (8–12 points) |
| Represents | City driving | Highway full-throttle |

**Why the average is posted:**

The posted value at the fuel dispenser is the **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)**, calculated as:

$$
AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2}
$$

The AKI is used because:

1. **Neither RON nor MON alone** represents real-world driving — actual driving cycles alternate between mild and severe conditions.
2. Cooperative Fuel Research Committee field studies (1940s) showed that the **arithmetic mean of RON and MON** correlates best with observed on-road knock frequency in mixed urban-highway use.
3. The AKI is mandated by ASTM and posted at fuel stations in the USA, Canada, and increasingly in India (Bharat Stage VI norms).

*(3 marks: 2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for AKI justification)*

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine test method** for determining the octane number of a gasoline sample. Clearly state the standard reference conditions, the role of the knock meter, and how the final octane number value is reported.

**(b)** [7 marks] The Research Octane Number (RON) and Motor Octane Number (MON) of three gasoline samples are:

| Sample | RON | MON |
|---|---|---|
| P | 95 | 85 |
| Q | 92 | 80 |
| R | 88 | 78 |

For each sample, calculate the **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** and **Sensitivity (S)**. Comment on which fuel is most suitable for (i) a city commuter car with 8:1 CR, and (ii) a high-performance sport bike with 12:1 CR.

---

### **Question A — Model Solution**

#### (a) CFR Engine Test Method for Octane Number

**Step 1 — Engine Description:**  
The CFR engine is a single-cylinder, variable-compression-ratio, four-stroke spark-ignition engine designed specifically for fuel rating. The compression ratio can be smoothly varied during operation (typically 4:1 to 18:1) using a hand-wheel that moves the cylinder block relative to the crankcase.

**Step 2 — Test Conditions:**  
- For RON: 600 rpm, ambient intake air, no swirl, standard ignition timing.  
- For MON: 900 rpm, intake air pre-heated to **150 °C**, high swirl, advanced ignition timing.

**Step 3 — Procedure:**

1. The test fuel is fed to the engine.
2. The compression ratio is gradually increased until the fuel **just begins to knock** — this is detected by the **knock meter** (a piezo-electric pressure transducer coupled to a tuned electronic filter that responds to the characteristic high-frequency pressure oscillations of detonation, typically in the 5–10 kHz band).
3. The compression ratio at onset of knock is recorded.
4. The test fuel is replaced with a **reference blend** (iso-octane + n-heptane) of known octane number.
5. The engine is run on the reference blend at the **same compression ratio**; the reference blend's octane number is **adjusted by changing the iso-octane/n-heptane ratio** until its knock intensity matches that of the test fuel.
6. The matching reference blend's iso-octane volume percentage is reported as the **Octane Number**.

**Step 4 — Reporting:**  
The result is reported as either **RON** or **MON** depending on the test conditions used. For super-octane fuels (ON > 100), leaded reference fuels or **delta-blend** techniques are used.

*(7 marks: 1 mark engine, 1 mark conditions, 3 marks procedure, 1 mark knock meter, 1 mark reporting)*

---

#### (b) AKI and Sensitivity Calculation

Using the formulas $AKI = (RON + MON) / 2$ and $S = RON - MON$:

**Sample P (RON = 95, MON = 85):**

$$
AKI_P = \frac{95 + 85}{2} = 90
$$

$$
S_P = 95 - 85 = 10
$$

**Sample Q (RON = 92, MON = 80):**

$$
AKI_Q = \frac{92 + 80}{2} = 86
$$

$$
S_Q = 92 - 80 = 12
$$

**Sample R (RON = 88, MON = 78):**

$$
AKI_R = \frac{88 + 78}{2} = 83
$$

$$
S_R = 88 - 78 = 10
$$

**Tabulated Results:**

| Sample | RON | MON | AKI | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P | 95 | 85 | 90 | 10 |
| Q | 92 | 80 | 86 | 12 |
| R | 88 | 78 | 83 | 10 |

**Comments on Engine Suitability:**

**(i) City Commuter Car (8:1 CR — low-to-moderate load):**  
Typical city driving involves low-speed, low-load operation where **RON is the dominant indicator** of knock resistance. Sample **P (RON = 95)** offers the maximum safety margin against knock and is the best choice. Sample Q (RON = 92) is acceptable. Sample R (RON = 88) is marginal and may knock under hard acceleration in summer.

**(ii) High-Performance Sport Bike (12:1 CR — high load, high speed):**  
High-compression engines operate continuously in the **MON-dominant regime** (high cylinder temperatures, high swirl, high heat soak). The required pump octane (AKI) is **≥ 95–98**. None of the samples meet this requirement directly. However, sample **P (AKI = 90)** is the best of the three; in practice, the rider would need **premium racing fuel or ethanol blend (E85)** for sustained high-CR operation. The high sensitivity of Q (S = 12) makes it especially unsuitable for hot-running motorcycle engines.

*(7 marks: 3 marks calculation, 2 marks AKI table, 2 marks suitability comment)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the effect of the following fuel structural properties on octane number:  
(i) **Chain branching** in paraffins, (ii) **Aromaticity**, (iii) **Carbon chain length**, (iv) **Oxygenate content (alcohol blending)**. Support your answer with specific molecular examples.

**(b)** [7 marks] A refinery produces 4 components with the following properties and intends to blend them to make 12,000 L of gasoline. Determine the **blended RON**. State the result's compliance with the Indian **BS-VI Regular Grade (RON ≥ 91)** specification.

| Component | Volume (L) | RON |
|---|---|---|
| LGO (Light Gas Oil) | 2000 | 60 |
| Naphtha | 4000 | 70 |
| Reformate | 4500 | 98 |
| Alkylate | 1500 | 92 |

---

### **Question B — Model Solution**

#### (a) Effect of Fuel Structural Properties on Octane Number

**(i) Effect of Chain Branching:**  
**As branching increases, octane number increases.**  
- n-Heptane (straight chain) → ON = 0  
- Iso-octane (highly branched) → ON = 100  
- n-Butane (straight) → ON = 92; iso-butane (branched) → ON = 102  

**Reason:** Branching shortens the carbon chain length, increases the C–H bond dissociation energy, and disrupts the chain-branching radical reactions that lead to auto-ignition (knock). Branched paraffins are therefore **more knock-resistant**.

**(ii) Effect of Aromaticity:**  
**Aromatic content dramatically raises octane number.**  
- Toluene (C₇H₈): RON = 121  
- Benzene (C₆H₆): RON = 101  
- m-Xylene: RON = 117  

**Reason:** The resonance-stabilized aromatic ring structure is highly resistant to radical chain-branching auto-ignition kinetics. However, aromatics have **low MON (severe knock resistance is moderate)** and produce soot; their use is regulated (max 35% in Indian BS-VI).

**(iii) Effect of Carbon Chain Length:**  
**As carbon chain length increases (within the same structural family), octane number decreases.**  
- n-Pentane (C₅H₁₂): RON = 62  
- n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆): RON = 0  
- n-Decane (C₁₀H₂₂): RON ≈ -45 (negative!)  

**Reason:** Longer straight chains provide more C–C bonds that can undergo low-temperature radical decomposition, initiating auto-ignition. Refineries therefore **crack** long chains into shorter branched ones to raise ON.

**(iv) Effect of Oxygenate Content (Alcohols):**  
**Adding oxygenates (alcohols, ethers) raises the octane number of the blend.**  
- Methanol (CH₃OH): RON = 109  
- Ethanol (C₂H₅OH): RON = 108  
- iso-Butanol (C₄H₉OH): RON = 105  
- MTBE (Methyl tert-butyl ether): RON = 118  

**Reason:** Alcohols have:
- **High latent heat of vaporization** → cools the intake charge, reducing in-cylinder temperature and delaying auto-ignition.
- **Oxygen in the molecule** → leaner effective combustion, more complete burn, lower flame temperature.
- **Inherently branched, compact structure** → intrinsically knock-resistant.

*Note:* Methanol and ethanol have very high **sensitivity** (RON − MON = 20), meaning they lose much of their advantage under MON conditions. MTBE has very low sensitivity (RON − MON ≈ 5), making it an excellent "anti-knock blender" with stable behavior.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per property + 1 mark reason synthesis)*

---

#### (b) Blended RON Calculation

**Step 1 — State the linear blending formula.**

$$
RON_{blend} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot RON_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual contributions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 \cdot RON_1 &= 2000 \times 60 = 120{,}000 \\
V_2 \cdot RON_2 &= 4000 \times 70 = 280{,}000 \\
V_3 \cdot RON_3 &= 4500 \times 98 = 441{,}000 \\
V_4 \cdot RON_4 &= 1500 \times 92 = 138{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum numerator and denominator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 120{,}000 + 280{,}000 + 441{,}000 + 138{,}000 = 979{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2000 + 4000 + 4500 + 1500 = 12{,}000 \,\text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Final blended RON.**

$$
\boxed{\,RON_{blend} = \frac{979{,}000}{12{,}000} \approx 81.58\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance Check:**

- Required: RON ≥ 91 (BS-VI Regular Grade).
- Calculated: RON ≈ 81.58.

**Result: The blend DOES NOT comply with BS-VI Regular Grade.** The RON is 9.42 points short. 

**Remedial Suggestion (for full marks):**  
To meet RON ≥ 91, the refinery can:
1. **Increase the reformate fraction** (RON = 98) by displacing some LGO.
2. **Add an oxygenate** such as 10% ethanol (which raises RON by ~2–3 points per 10% blend).
3. **Add toluene** (RON = 121) as an aromatic high-octane booster.
4. **Use alkylation technology** to convert iso-butanes into high-ON iso-octane–class alkylate.

*(7 marks: 2 marks formula, 3 marks arithmetic, 1 mark compliance verdict, 1 mark remediation)*

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on Octane Number Questions**  
>  
> 1. **Forgetting to state "dimensionless"** for the octane number → lose 1 mark.  
> 2. **Confusing RON with MON** in the AKI formula → cascades into a wrong answer.  
> 3. **Reporting AKI instead of RON** in numerical blending problems → wrong variable.  
> 4. **Assuming linear blending is exact** in real-world refinery problems → state "this is a first-order approximation; true blending uses BON tables."  
> 5. **Writing the CFR engine is a "diesel engine"** — it is a spark-ignition, single-cylinder, **variable-compression-ratio** engine.  
> 6. **Mixing up octane number with cetane number** — ON is for SI (gasoline), CN is for CI (diesel); they are inversely related.  
> 7. **Not mentioning knock meter / piezoelectric transducer** in CFR procedure → lose 1 mark.  
> 8. **Ignoring unit consistency** in blending problems (volumes in L, RON dimensionless) → irrelevant to numerical answer, but method marks are reduced.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ⭐ **Octane Number (ON)** is the percentage by volume of iso-octane in a reference blend of iso-octane and n-heptane that matches the knock intensity of the test fuel in a CFR engine.
- ⭐ **Reference scale anchors:** n-heptane = 0, iso-octane = 100.
- ⭐ **RON (ASTM D2699):** 600 rpm, no swirl, ambient air — mild city-driving simulation.
- ⭐ **MON (ASTM D2700):** 900 rpm, 150 °C intake, high swirl — severe high-load simulation.
- ⭐ **AKI (Pump Octane)** = (RON + MON) / 2.
- ⭐ **Sensitivity S** = RON − MON; typical range 0–12.
- ⭐ **CFR Engine:** Single-cylinder, variable-compression-ratio SI engine with a piezo-knock meter.
- ⭐ **Branching ↑ → ON ↑; Aromaticity ↑ → ON ↑; Chain length ↑ → ON ↓; Oxygenate content ↑ → ON ↑.**
- ⭐ **Octane > 100 fuels** (alcohols, toluene, CNG, H₂) measured by leaded reference scale or delta-blend method.
- ⭐ **Blending formula** (linear): $ON_{blend} = \sum(V_i \cdot ON_i) / \sum V_i$ — first-order approximation only.
- ⭐ **Inverse relationship with Cetane Number:** ON ↑ ⇒ CN ↓ — fuels cannot simultaneously be high in both.
- ⭐ **Why we care:** Higher ON allows higher CR → higher Otto efficiency → more power; wrong ON causes engine knock and damage.
- ⭐ **KTU-favorite trick question:** "Is the octane number a thermodynamic property?" → Answer: **No**, it is an empirical engine-test number.
- ⭐ **Common alternative fuel ONs to remember:** Ethanol = 108, Methanol = 109, iso-Butanol = 105, LPG = 111, CNG = 130, Toluene = 121.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cetane Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cetane Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cetane Number (CN)** is a dimensionless, inverse-metric measure of the **ignition quality of a diesel fuel** — quantifying the readiness of the fuel to auto-ignite (self-ignite) when sprayed as a fine mist into the hot, highly compressed air charge inside a **Compression Ignition (CI) engine** cylinder.

Mathematically, it is defined as the **percentage by volume of n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** in a reference blend — consisting of n-cetane and α-methylnaphthalene (C₁₁H₁₀) — that exhibits the **same ignition delay (ignition lag) period** as the test fuel, under standard, controlled engine conditions specified by **ASTM D613 (Cetane Number Engine Method)** or correlated by **ASTM D6890 / D7170 (Constant Volume Combustion Chamber methods)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Standard Definition:** *"The Cetane Number of a fuel is the percentage of cetane (n-hexadecane) in a mixture of cetane and α-methylnaphthalene that has the same ignition lag as the test fuel when evaluated in a standard CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) diesel test engine under prescribed conditions."*

### 1.2 The Two Reference Benchmark Fuels

| Property | **n-Cetane (C₁₆H₃₄)** | **α-Methylnaphthalene (C₁₁H₁₀)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Assigned CN** | **100** (High ignitibility) | **0** (High resistance to ignition) |
| **Chemical Family** | Straight-chain saturated alkane (paraffin) | Aromatic hydrocarbon |
| **Structure** | CH₃–(CH₂)₁₄–CH₃ | Naphthalene ring + 1 methyl group |
| **Ignition Delay** | Very short (ignites almost instantly) | Very long (resists auto-ignition) |
| **Boiling Point** | **287 °C (549 °F)** | **245 °C (473 °F)** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Matchstick vs. Wet Wood" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to start a campfire:

- **n-Cetane (CN = 100)** behaves like a **box of dry matchsticks** — the moment they touch a hot surface (compressed hot air), they burst into flame almost instantly. Short ignition delay.
- **α-Methylnaphthalene (CN = 0)** behaves like **wet, green wood** — you can place it on hot coals, and it will simply smolder, hiss steam, and stubbornly resist igniting. Long ignition delay.
- **A real diesel fuel** lies somewhere in between, and the **Cetane Number tells you exactly where** on this "match-to-wet-wood" spectrum it falls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** High CN = "Impatient" fuel (ignites the moment it gets hot). Low CN = "Stubborn" fuel (refuses to ignite easily). This inverse relationship with octane number (where higher = more resistance to *unwanted* premature ignition in SI engines) is a favorite KTU conceptual trap question.

### 1.4 Formal Ignition Delay (The Physical Quantity CN Actually Measures)

**Ignition delay (or ignition lag), τ_id**, is the time interval — expressed in **degrees of crank angle (°CA)** or milliseconds (ms) — between:
- **Start of fuel injection** (needle lift), and
- **Start of combustion** (point of rapid pressure rise in the cylinder).

The CN is inversely proportional to the **shortest, smoothest combustion lag** exhibited by a fuel.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Always remember that CN does NOT measure the energy content of the fuel. Two fuels with identical CN can have wildly different calorific values. CN measures **how fast** the fuel ignites, not **how much energy** it releases.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain — Why Cetane Number Exists

A diesel (CI) engine has **no spark plug**. Combustion is initiated purely by the high temperature of compressed air (typically **500 °C – 900 °C** in the cylinder after compression). The fuel must therefore **auto-ignite** the instant it is injected. This imposes strict chemical requirements on the fuel, which CN was invented to quantify.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Air is compressed** in the cylinder to a high pressure (typically **30 – 55 bar**) and temperature (**700 – 900 °C**).
2. **Fuel is injected** as a high-pressure spray through the injector nozzle into this hot air.
3. The fuel must **mix with air, vaporize, heat up, and self-ignite** within a precisely controlled time window.
4. The duration of step 3 = **ignition delay (τ_id)**.
5. If τ_id is **too long** → fuel accumulates, then ignites all at once → **diesel knock**, pressure spikes, rough running, smoke, poor emissions.
6. If τ_id is **just right (short & smooth)** → controlled, progressive combustion → smooth power, high efficiency, low emissions.
7. **CN quantifies how short and smooth step 3 will be.**

### 2.2 The Mathematical Foundation — How CN is Determined

For a reference blend where volume fraction of n-cetane = $V_c$ and volume fraction of α-methylnaphthalene = $V_m$ (with $V_c + V_m = 100$):

$$\text{CN of Test Fuel} = \text{CN of Reference Blend} = V_c \, (\\%)$$

In the **ASTM D613 engine test method**:
- The test fuel's ignition delay is measured in a **standard CFR diesel engine** at a fixed compression ratio, intake air temperature of **150 °F (65.5 °C)**, and engine speed of **900 ± 9 rpm**.
- The injection timing is then **varied** to find the "Hand-Switch" position where ignition occurs **13° CA (crank angle) after Top Dead Centre (TDC)**.
- Reference blends are bracketed (one with shorter, one with longer delay), and **linear interpolation** is used to assign the CN.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The following table consolidates every formula, constant, and limit you are likely to need for a Cetane Number question in either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (14 marks).

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Value | Units | Notes & KTU Caveats |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Definition of CN** | $\text{CN} = \%\,\,\text{n-cetane in blend}$ | dimensionless | Blend of n-C₁₆H₃₄ + α-methylnaphthalene |
| 2 | **Reference fuel — high** | n-Hexadecane (C₁₆H₃₄), CN = 100 | — | Straight-chain paraffin |
| 3 | **Reference fuel — low** | α-Methylnaphthalene, CN = 0 | — | Aromatic ring compound |
| 4 | **Typical diesel fuel CN** | 45 – 55 (regular), 55 – 70 (premium) | — | Indian diesel (BIS IS 1460) : **min 48** |
| 5 | **CN of biodiesel (B100, e.g., soybean)** | 46 – 52 | — | Slightly lower than petro-diesel; varies by feedstock |
| 6 | **CN of ethanol** | **~ 8 – 11** (very low) | — | Ethanol is a **gasoline-range fuel**, not a diesel fuel |
| 7 | **CN of dimethyl ether (DME)** | 55 – 60 | — | Excellent alternative CI fuel |
| 8 | **CN of n-butanol** | ~ 25 (still low) | — | Longer-chain alcohols improve CN |
| 9 | **CN of Fischer–Tropsch diesel** | 70 – 85 (very high) | — | Synthetic, near-zero sulfur, paraffinic |
| 10 | **CN of LPG / CNG** | N/A (gaseous, no CN defined) | — | Gases use **Methane Number** instead |
| 11 | **CN of crude palm oil (CPO)** | ~ 40 – 45 | — | Direct use possible in pre-chamber diesels |
| 12 | **Cetane Index (CI)** — *calculated, not measured* | $\text{CI} = 454.74 - 1641.416 \cdot D + 774.74 \cdot D^2 - 0.554 \cdot T_{50} + 97.803 \cdot \log_{10}(T_{50})$ | — | ASTM D976 (older), D4737 (modern, 4-variable) |
| 13 | **Cetane Improver — 2-EHN (2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate)** | $\text{Dose} \approx 0.05 - 0.4 \,\%\,\text{vol}$ | vol% | Most common commercial CN booster |
| 14 | **CN boost per 0.1 % 2-EHN** | +3 to +5 CN points | — | Diminishing returns above 0.3 % |
| 15 | **Diesel knock limit (rough)** | $\text{CN} < 40 \Rightarrow$ severe knock | — | Threshold varies with engine design |
| 16 | **Effect on ignition delay** | $\tau_{id} \propto 1/\text{CN}$ (qualitative) | ms or °CA | Approximate inverse relation |
| 17 | **Engine speed (ASTM D613)** | $900 \pm 9$ | rpm | Standardized test condition |
| 18 | **Intake air temperature (ASTM D613)** | $150 \pm 2$ (i.e. 65.5 °C) | °F | Standardized test condition |
| 19 | **Compression ratio range in CFR engine** | Adjustable, ~ 7 – 35 | :1 | Used to find matching delay |
| 20 | **BSFC optimum CN** | ~ 50 – 55 | — | Above this, marginal BSFC gains |

> **Notation used in table:** $D$ = density of fuel at 15 °C (g/mL), $T_{50}$ = temperature at which 50 vol% of the fuel is distilled (°F).  **Critical: Use `\vert` in any inline `CN < 40` in your answer sheet to avoid LaTeX rendering issues.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Cetane Number Concept

| Engineering Field / Application | Why CN matters |
|---|---|
| **Refinery fuel blending** | Optimizes yield of diesel pool by blending high-CN paraffinic streams with low-CN cracked streams. |
| **Diesel engine cold-start design** | Low CN fuels cause white smoke, misfire, and unburned HC emissions at low ambient temperatures. |
| **Emissions control (BS-VI / Euro VI)** | Higher CN → shorter, more complete combustion → lower CO, HC, PM, NOₓ noise. |
| **Biodiesel quality control** | EN 14214 (European) and IS 15607 (Indian) standards mandate **CN ≥ 51 (EN)** and **CN ≥ 47 (BIS)**. |
| **Alternative fuel R&D** | DME, FAME, HVO, GTL, BTL are routinely characterized and ranked by CN. |
| **Additive industry** | Multi-million-dollar global market for cetane improver additives (2-EHN, DTBP, etc.). |
| **Military / aviation diesel** | JP-8, JP-5 require high CN (typically ≥ 45) for reliable operation in extreme conditions. |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world link:** When you read in the news that "BS-VI diesel is now available across India", one of the unstated but critical changes has been the **upward shift in minimum cetane number from 48 (BS-IV) to 51 (BS-VI)** — directly reducing PM and NOₓ emissions from every commercial truck and bus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation — The Linear Interpolation Algorithm for CN

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation shows how a Cetane Number is *actually assigned* in the laboratory. KTU frequently asks a Part A question: *"How is the cetane number of a test fuel determined experimentally?"* — so this is **extremely high yield**.

**Setup:** A test fuel of unknown CN is to be evaluated. Two reference blends are prepared:

- **Blend 1:** Volume fraction of n-cetane = $V_1$ %, giving CN₁ = $V_1$. This blend has a measured ignition delay of $\tau_1$ (in °CA).
- **Blend 2:** Volume fraction of n-cetane = $V_2$ % (where $V_2 > V_1$), giving CN₂ = $V_2$. This blend has a measured ignition delay of $\tau_2$ (in °CA).
- The test fuel is injected and its ignition delay $\tau_{\text{test}}$ is measured.

**Assumption (Standard ASTM D613):** Ignition delay varies **linearly** with cetane number over the narrow bracketing range.

**Step 1:** State the linear relation between $\tau$ and CN.

$$
\tau = m \cdot \text{CN} + c
$$

where $m$ is the slope (negative, since higher CN → lower delay) and $c$ is the intercept.

**Step 2:** Write the equation for each of the two reference blends.

$$
\tau_1 = m \cdot \text{CN}_1 + c
$$
$$
\tau_2 = m \cdot \text{CN}_2 + c
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the two equations to eliminate $c$ and solve for $m$.

$$
\tau_2 - \tau_1 = m \cdot (\text{CN}_2 - \text{CN}_1)
$$
$$
m = \dfrac{\tau_2 - \tau_1}{\text{CN}_2 - \text{CN}_1}
$$

**Step 4:** Since CN₂ > CN₁, the better-igniting blend has a *shorter* delay, so $\tau_2 < \tau_1$, making $m$ negative (as expected).

**Step 5:** Solve the test-fuel equation for CN_test.

$$
\tau_{\text{test}} = m \cdot \text{CN}_{\text{test}} + c
$$
$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{test}} - c}{m}
$$

**Step 6:** Eliminate $c$ using Blend 1's equation: $c = \tau_1 - m \cdot \text{CN}_1$.

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{test}} - \tau_1 + m \cdot \text{CN}_1}{m}
$$
$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = \text{CN}_1 + \dfrac{\tau_{\text{test}} - \tau_1}{m}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute the expression for $m$ from Step 3.

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = \text{CN}_1 + \dfrac{(\tau_{\text{test}} - \tau_1) \cdot (\text{CN}_2 - \text{CN}_1)}{\tau_2 - \tau_1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU-Board Working Formula:**
> $$\boxed{\;\text{CN}_{\text{test}} \;=\; \text{CN}_1 \;+\; \dfrac{(\tau_{\text{test}} - \tau_1)\,(\text{CN}_2 - \text{CN}_1)}{\tau_2 - \tau_1}\;}$$

**Step 8 (Worked Numerical Example — for your KTU problem-solving practice):**

Given:
- Blend 1: CN₁ = 40, τ₁ = 6.0 °CA
- Blend 2: CN₂ = 60, τ₂ = 4.0 °CA
- Test fuel: τ_test = 5.2 °CA

Substitute into the boxed formula:

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = 40 + \dfrac{(5.2 - 6.0)\,(60 - 40)}{4.0 - 6.0}
$$
$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = 40 + \dfrac{(-0.8)\,(20)}{(-2.0)}
$$
$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = 40 + \dfrac{-16.0}{-2.0}
$$
$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = 40 + 8.0 = \mathbf{48}
$$

**Result:** The test fuel has a **Cetane Number = 48** — which lies between 40 and 60, and (intuitively) is closer to 50 because its delay of 5.2 °CA is closer to the midpoint of 4.0 and 6.0.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Various Engine & Emissions Parameters Respond to CN

| Engine / Performance Parameter | Effect of **Increasing** CN | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignition delay (τ_id)** | ↓ Decreases (shorter) | Smoother start of combustion |
| **Peak cylinder pressure (P_max)** | ↓ Decreases, occurs closer to TDC | Less mechanical stress on bearings, lower noise |
| **Maximum rate of pressure rise (dP/dθ)_max** | ↓ Decreases sharply | Reduced **diesel knock**, quieter engine |
| **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** | ↓ Decreases slightly | Better thermal efficiency (small gain) |
| **Cold-start capability** | ↑ Improves | Less cranking, less white smoke |
| **CO emissions** | ↓ Decreases | More complete combustion |
| **Unburned HC emissions** | ↓ Decreases | Less wall-quenching, less fuel-rich pockets |
| **Particulate Matter (PM) / Smoke** | ↓ Decreases | Less diffusion-controlled over-rich combustion |
| **NOₓ emissions** | **↑ Slightly increases** | Earlier, hotter combustion → more thermal NOₓ |
| **Engine noise** | ↓ Decreases | Lower (dP/dθ)_max = softer combustion |
| **Power output** | ≈ Marginal change | CN mostly affects *timing* of combustion, not energy released |
| **Engine warm-up period** | ↓ Decreases | Faster attainment of stable combustion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently write *"Higher CN always improves engine performance and reduces all emissions."* This is **wrong** on NOₓ. The earlier, hotter combustion associated with high CN actually **increases NOₓ** formation because of higher peak in-cylinder temperatures. This is a famous 3-mark KTU Part A trap.

### 3.3 The Cetane Number vs. Octane Number Comparison Matrix

| Property | **Cetane Number (CN)** | **Octane Number (ON)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine type** | Compression Ignition (CI / Diesel) | Spark Ignition (SI / Petrol) |
| **What it measures** | Ease of *auto-ignition* (we want it to ignite fast) | Resistance to *auto-ignition* (we want it to NOT ignite early) |
| **Desired value** | **Higher** (faster, smoother combustion) | **Higher** (more resistance to knock) |
| **Reference high** | n-Cetane (C₁₆H₃₄), **CN = 100** | iso-Octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane), **ON = 100** |
| **Reference low** | α-Methylnaphthalene, **CN = 0** | n-Heptane, **ON = 0** |
| **Mechanism measured** | Ignition delay (τ_id) | Knock intensity (audible, or via knock sensor) |
| **Test method** | ASTM D613 (engine) | ASTM D2700 (RON), ASTM D2699 (MON) |
| **Kicks in when** | Too LOW → diesel knock, smoke, rough running | Too LOW → SI engine knock, power loss, damage |
| **Kicks in when too high** | May marginally increase NOₓ, increase cost | Hard to cold-start, hot-weather running issues |
| **Chemistry preference** | **Long straight chains** (paraffins) | **Branched chains & aromatics** |
| **Hydrocarbon class** | n-alkanes → high CN | aromatics → high ON |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *Paraffins Party in Diesels; Aromatics are Aces in SI.* (Paraffins = long straight chains → high CN. Aromatics → high ON.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Cetane Number Determination Procedure (ASTM D613)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START: Test Fuel Arrives in Lab]) --> A1[Mount in CFR Diesel Engine]
    A1 --> A2[Set Engine Speed to 900 rpm]
    A2 --> A3[Set Intake Air Temp to 150 degF]
    A3 --> A4[Set Injection Timing to Standard Reference]
    A4 --> A5[Measure Ignition Delay tau_test in crank angle degrees]
    A5 --> A6{Is tau_test Within Calibration Range?}
    A6 -- No --> A7[Re-calibrate Engine or Adjust Compression Ratio]
    A7 --> A5
    A6 -- Yes --> A8[Prepare Blend 1: Low Cetane Reference, CN1]
    A8 --> A9[Measure tau1 for Blend 1]
    A9 --> A10[Prepare Blend 2: High Cetane Reference, CN2]
    A10 --> A11[Measure tau2 for Blend 2]
    A11 --> A12[Apply Linear Interpolation Formula]
    A12 --> A13["CN_test = CN1 + (tau_test - tau1)(CN2 - CN1) / (tau2 - tau1)"]
    A13 --> A14[Report Final CN with 2 Decimal Places]
    A14 --> A15([END: Cetane Number Determined])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Architecture of a CI Engine Combustion Event in Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FUEL INJECTION BEGINS] --> A2[Ignition Delay Period tau_id]
    A2 --> A3[FUEL CHEMICALLY PREPARES: Atomize, Vaporize, Mix, Heat]
    A3 --> A4{Critical Auto-Ignition Conditions Reached?}
    A4 -- NO --> A3
    A4 -- YES --> A5[START OF COMBUSTION SOC]
    A5 --> A6[Premixed Combustion Phase: Rapid Heat Release]
    A6 --> A7[Mixing Controlled Combustion Phase: Diffusion Flame]
    A7 --> A8[Late Combustion and Burnout]
    A8 --> A9([END: Combustion Event])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — The Chain of Causation: Molecular Structure → CN → Engine Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Paraffinic Straight Chain C16H34] --> H1[Highest CN: 100]
    M2[Long Chain n-Alkanes C12 to C20] --> H2[High CN: 70 to 90]
    M3[Isoparaffins and Cycloparaffins] --> H3[Moderate CN: 40 to 65]
    M4[Aromatics Benzene Naphthalene] --> H4[Low CN: 0 to 30]
    H1 --> E1[Smooth Combustion, Low Smoke, Low Noise, Slight NOX Rise]
    H2 --> E2[Good CI Engine Performance]
    H3 --> E3[Acceptable Performance, May Show Mild Diesel Knock]
    H4 --> E4[Long Ignition Delay, Hard Starting, Diesel Knock, White Smoke]
    E1 --> F1[Premium Diesel Fuel Grade]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F2[Regular Diesel Fuel Grade]
    E4 --> F3[Requires Cetane Improver Additive]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Relationship Between Cetane Number, Engine, and Chemistry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Raw Crude Oil Enters Refinery] --> S1[Atmospheric Distillation Column]
    S1 --> S2[Diesel Boiling Cut 180 to 360 degC Obtained]
    S2 --> S3[Cracking Hydrotreating Blending Operations]
    S3 --> S4{Cetane Number Check vs BIS Standard min 48}
    S4 -- CN BELOW 48 --> S5[Add Cetane Improver 2-EHN or DTBP]
    S5 --> S6[Re-test CN]
    S6 --> S4
    S4 -- CN ABOVE 48 --> S7[Quality Cleared]
    S7 --> S8[Diesel Dispatched to Retail Outlet]
    S4 -- CN ABOVE 60 --> S9[Premium Grade Diesel Branded]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide (for KTU descriptive questions):** Use these four diagrams in your answer sheets to score full marks on visualization-based questions. KTU examiners award 2 to 3 bonus marks for a well-labelled flow diagram accompanying a textual answer, particularly in Module 1 questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Cetane Number. What are the reference fuels used and why?** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

Cetane Number (CN) is a measure of the **ignition quality of a diesel fuel**, defined as the percentage by volume of **n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** in a blend of n-cetane and α-methylnaphthalene that has the **same ignition delay** as the test fuel, evaluated in a standard CFR engine under ASTM D613 conditions. n-cetane (CN = 100) is assigned the highest value because as a **straight-chain paraffin**, it ignites very rapidly in hot compressed air. α-Methylnaphthalene (CN = 0), being an **aromatic**, resists auto-ignition, providing the low end of the scale.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between Cetane Number and Octane Number. Why can they not be interchanged?** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~75 words):**

| Aspect | Cetane Number (CN) | Octane Number (ON) |
|---|---|---|
| Engine type | **CI (Diesel)** | **SI (Petrol)** |
| Measures | Ease of **auto-ignition** | Resistance to **auto-ignition** |
| Reference high | n-Cetane (CN = 100) | iso-Octane (ON = 100) |
| Reference low | α-Methylnaphthalene (CN = 0) | n-Heptane (ON = 0) |

They cannot be interchanged because **CI engines *require* rapid self-ignition** (so high CN is desired), while **SI engines must *resist* premature self-ignition** to avoid knock (so high ON is desired). The desired property is fundamentally opposite.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions in ESE carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Model Solution** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the determination of Cetane Number of a diesel fuel using the ASTM D613 engine method. Describe the reference fuels, test conditions, and the linear interpolation formula with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A test fuel gives an ignition delay of **5.2 °CA** in a standard CFR engine. Reference blend 1 (CN = 40) gives **6.0 °CA** and Reference blend 2 (CN = 60) gives **4.0 °CA**. Determine the Cetane Number of the test fuel using linear interpolation. Discuss the significance of the obtained value. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The Cetane Number (CN) of a fuel is determined experimentally using the **ASTM D613 standard test method** in a **Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) diesel engine**. The test compares the ignition delay of the unknown fuel against a series of reference fuel blends of known CN.

**Reference Fuels (1 mark):**
- **n-Cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄):** A straight-chain paraffin, assigned CN = **100** because it ignites almost instantaneously in compressed hot air.
- **α-Methylnaphthalene (C₁₁H₁₀):** An aromatic hydrocarbon, assigned CN = **0** because it has a very long ignition delay and resists auto-ignition.

**Standardized Test Conditions (1 mark):** The CFR engine is operated at:
- Engine speed: **900 ± 9 rpm**
- Intake air temperature: **150 ± 2 °F (65.5 °C)**
- Compression ratio: Variable (used to bracket the ignition delay)
- Injection pressure: Standardized (~ 100 – 150 bar)
- Hand-switch (start of combustion): **13° CA after TDC**

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. The test fuel is injected, and the **ignition delay τ_test** is measured in **degrees of crank angle (°CA)** between injector needle lift and the start of rapid pressure rise.
2. Two reference blends — one of **lower CN (CN₁)** and one of **higher CN (CN₂)** — are prepared such that their ignition delays **bracket** τ_test (i.e., τ₁ > τ_test > τ₂, since higher CN → lower delay).
3. The CN of the test fuel is then calculated by **linear interpolation**.

**Formula and Diagram (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = \text{CN}_1 + \dfrac{(\tau_{\text{test}} - \tau_1)(\text{CN}_2 - \text{CN}_1)}{\tau_2 - \tau_1}\;}
$$

```
         |   //
    tau  |  /  <-  Linear Region
         | /
         |/________
              CN
         0       100
```

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Test fuel ignition delay: τ_test = 5.2 °CA
- Reference blend 1: CN₁ = 40, τ₁ = 6.0 °CA
- Reference blend 2: CN₂ = 60, τ₂ = 4.0 °CA

**Step 1 — Check bracketing (1 mark):** Since CN₂ > CN₁, we have τ₂ < τ₁. We verify: 4.0 < 5.2 < 6.0 ✓. The test fuel lies between the two reference blends.

**Step 2 — Apply the linear interpolation formula (2 marks):**

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = \text{CN}_1 + \dfrac{(\tau_{\text{test}} - \tau_1)(\text{CN}_2 - \text{CN}_1)}{\tau_2 - \tau_1}
$$

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = 40 + \dfrac{(5.2 - 6.0)\,(60 - 40)}{4.0 - 6.0}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator (1 mark):**

$$
(5.2 - 6.0) \times (60 - 40) = (-0.8) \times (20) = -16.0
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator (1 mark):**

$$
4.0 - 6.0 = -2.0
$$

**Step 5 — Final computation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = 40 + \dfrac{-16.0}{-2.0} = 40 + 8.0 = \mathbf{48}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** A CN of **48** is at the lower edge of acceptable commercial diesel specification (BIS IS 1460 requires minimum **48**, while BS-VI mandates **51**). The fuel would be suitable for use, but with the addition of a cetane improver (e.g., **2-EHN at 0.1–0.2 vol%**), its CN could be raised to 51–55, giving **smoother combustion, easier cold-start, lower smoke, and reduced engine knock**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Full-Model Solution** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2021 / Internal Choice Pattern]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss the effect of Cetane Number on the **performance** and **emission characteristics** of a Compression Ignition engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Cetane Numbers of the following alternative fuels and explain the chemical reason for the differences: (i) n-Butanol, (ii) Biodiesel (FAME, e.g., soy methyl ester), (iii) Dimethyl Ether (DME), (iv) Fischer–Tropsch Synthetic Diesel, (v) Ethanol. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Performance Effects (4 marks):**
1. **Ignition Delay (τ_id):** Higher CN → shorter, more reproducible ignition delay → combustion starts closer to TDC → less work lost to compression of unburned charge.
2. **Starting, Especially Cold Starting:** Low CN fuels have long delays; they pool on cold cylinder walls → **white smoke (unburned fuel)** and **misfire**. High CN improves cold-start markedly.
3. **Engine Noise (Diesel Knock):** Low CN → long delay → accumulated fuel ignites all at once → sharp pressure rise → audible "knock". High CN reduces (dP/dθ)_max → quieter engine.
4. **Power & BSFC:** Power output is not strongly affected (depends on heating value), but BSFC improves slightly with higher CN due to more complete combustion and reduced heat losses.

**Emission Effects (3 marks):**
1. **CO and HC:** Decrease with higher CN (more complete combustion, less wall-quenching).
2. **Particulate Matter (PM/Soot):** Decreases with higher CN (less over-rich fuel-rich zone accumulation).
3. **NOₓ:** **Slightly increases** with higher CN (earlier, hotter combustion → more thermal NOₓ). This is a trade-off and is the basis of EGR and retarded-injection strategies in modern BS-VI engines.
4. **CO₂ (regulated):** Roughly proportional to fuel burned, so CN has minor effect.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

| # | Alternative Fuel | Approx. CN | Chemical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **n-Butanol** | **~ 25** | Short C₄ chain, presence of polar **–OH group** raises auto-ignition resistance; not paraffinic enough. |
| (ii) | **Biodiesel (FAME, soy methyl ester)** | **~ 48 – 52** | Long C₁₆–C₁₈ fatty acid methyl ester chains; predominantly saturated → high CN. Lower saturation → lower CN. |
| (iii) | **Dimethyl Ether (DME, CH₃–O–CH₃)** | **~ 55 – 60** | Small ether molecule with **very low auto-ignition temperature (~ 235 °C)**; ignites very readily. Excellent CI fuel, though it needs low-pressure fuel system changes. |
| (iv) | **Fischer–Tropsch Synthetic Diesel** | **~ 70 – 85** | Almost 100% **n-paraffins** (no aromatics, no sulfur) → exceptionally high CN. Premium "designer" diesel. |
| (v) | **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** | **~ 8 – 11** | Short C₂ chain, **polar –OH group**, **high latent heat of vaporization**, low cetane. Not suitable as a pure CI fuel; used in **diesel-ethanol dual-fuel mode**. |

**Summary Statement (1 mark):** The general rule is — **longer, straighter, more saturated hydrocarbon chains → higher CN; presence of –OH, C=C double bonds, aromatic rings, and short chain length → lower CN.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Cetane Number Questions**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Cetane with Octane references:** Writing "iso-octane is the high reference for CN" is an immediate 1-mark deduction. Memorize: **n-Cetane (CN=100) / α-Methylnaphthalene (CN=0); iso-Octane (ON=100) / n-Heptane (ON=0)**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention ASTM D613:** The examiner's checklist almost always includes the test method. **Always write "ASTM D613 engine method"** at least once in any CN-related answer.
>
> 3. **Claiming higher CN = lower NOₓ:** This is wrong. Higher CN → earlier/hotter combustion → higher NOₓ. This is a 1- to 2-mark trap question in Part A.
>
> 4. **Linear interpolation sign errors:** If you mix up which delay corresponds to which CN, the final answer comes out negative or above 100. Always **verify that CN₂ > CN₁ ⇒ τ₂ < τ₁** before applying the formula.
>
> 5. **Not stating units of ignition delay:** Always write **"°CA (degrees of crank angle)"** or **"milliseconds (ms)"** when quoting or computing τ_id. No unit = 0.5 mark lost in 7-mark sub-questions.
>
> 6. **Confusing Cetane Number with Cetane Index:** Cetane Number is **measured in an engine**; Cetane Index is **calculated from density and distillation data** (ASTM D976 or D4737). They are *correlated*, not identical.
>
> 7. **Missing the "alternative fuels" angle:** PEMUT411 is an **Alternative Fuels** course. A purely petro-diesel answer will be marked incomplete. Always add a sentence on **biodiesel, DME, FAME, FT-diesel, alcohols** for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Definition:** CN = % n-cetane in a reference blend (n-cetane + α-methylnaphthalene) having the same ignition delay as the test fuel.
- ✅ **Two Reference Fuels:**
  * **n-Cetane (C₁₆H₃₄)** — straight-chain paraffin — **CN = 100** (high ignitibility).
  * **α-Methylnaphthalene (C₁₁H₁₀)** — aromatic — **CN = 0** (high resistance).
- ✅ **What CN Measures:** **Ignition delay (τ_id)**, in **°CA** or **ms** — the interval between fuel injection and start of combustion.
- ✅ **Standard Test Method:** **ASTM D613**, in a CFR diesel engine at **900 rpm, 150 °F intake air**.
- ✅ **Linear Interpolation Formula:**
  $$\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = \text{CN}_1 + \dfrac{(\tau_{\text{test}} - \tau_1)(\text{CN}_2 - \text{CN}_1)}{\tau_2 - \tau_1}$$
- ✅ **Cetane Number vs. Octane Number:** CN for **diesel/CI**, ON for **petrol/SI** — **opposite** desired trends.
- ✅ **Typical CN values:**
  * Petro-diesel: **45 – 55**
  * Biodiesel (FAME): **~ 48 – 52**
  * DME: **~ 55 – 60**
  * Fischer–Tropsch diesel: **~ 70 – 85**
  * Ethanol: **~ 8 – 11** (very low, unsuitable for pure CI use)
- ✅ **Effects of HIGH CN:** Smooth combustion, low noise, easy cold-start, low CO, low HC, low PM, slightly **higher** NOₓ.
- ✅ **Effects of LOW CN:** Diesel knock, white smoke, hard starting, rough running, higher CO/HC/PM, lower NOₓ.
- ✅ **Cetane Index (CI):** **Calculated** from density and T₅₀ distillation temperature (ASTM D976/D4737) — correlated with CN but not a replacement for engine test.
- ✅ **Cetane Improvers (Additives):** **2-EHN (2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate)**, **DTBP (Di-tert-butyl peroxide)** — added at **0.05 – 0.4 vol%** to boost CN by **3 – 8 points**.
- ✅ **Indian Standard (BIS IS 1460):** Minimum **CN = 48** for regular diesel; **BS-VI specification** mandates **CN ≥ 51**.
- ✅ **Biodiesel Standard (IS 15607 / EN 14214):** **CN ≥ 47 (BIS) / CN ≥ 51 (EN)**.
- ✅ **Gaseous alternative fuels** (LPG, CNG, H₂) have **no Cetane Number** defined — they use **Methane Number** instead.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb for alternative fuels:** Longer C-chain, more saturated, no –OH group, no aromatic ring → higher CN.
- ✅ **Always quote ASTM D613** when asked about CN determination.
- ✅ **Avoid the NOₓ trap:** Higher CN does NOT reduce all emissions — it *increases* NOₓ slightly.

> **End of Notes — Cetane Number | Module 1, PEMUT411 (Alternative Fuels and Energy Systems) | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties for SI Engine Fuels and CI Engine Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Properties for SI Engine Fuels and CI Engine Fuels # Properties for SI Engine Fuels and CI Engine Fuels ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **SI (Spark Ignition) Engine Fuels** are volatile, liquid hydrocarbon-based fuels (predominantly **gasoline/petrol**) that require an external ignition source (spark plug) to initiate combustion. The combustion process is governed by homogeneous charge preparation, where the fuel is **vaporized and mixed with air before entering the combustion chamber**. **CI (Compression Ignition) Engine Fuels** are relatively less volatile, heavier liquid hydrocarbon fuels (predominantly **diesel**) that self-ignite when injected into hot, highly compressed air. The combustion process is governed by **heterogeneous charge preparation**, where atomized fuel droplets ignite upon contact with air at temperatures exceeding the auto-ignition threshold. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A fuel's suitability for either SI or CI engine is determined by a set of **physical, chemical, and performance-based properties**. The properties are not the same for both engine types because the combustion initiation mechanisms differ fundamentally — SI uses spark, CI uses compression heat. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two different cooking scenarios: - **SI Engine Analogy** = Lighting a candle with a matchstick. You prepare a small wick (fuel-air mixture), and you need an **external spark** to light it. The fuel must be **highly volatile** (easy to vaporize) so it can mix well with air, just as a candle wick needs to draw wax up easily. - **CI Engine Analogy** = Using a fire piston (traditional fire-starter). You compress air so intensely that it becomes hot enough to ignite tinder **on its own**. Here, the fuel must be **less volatile** but must ignite **spontaneously at high temperature and pressure** — just like tinder that catches fire under sudden compression. ### Classification of Fuel Properties The properties are broadly classified into **three categories**: > [!NOTE] > **Category 1 — Physical Properties:** Density, Viscosity, Boiling Range, Flash Point, Fire Point, Cloud Point, Pour Point, Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) > > **Category 2 — Chemical Properties:** Calorific Value, Auto-Ignition Temperature, Chemical Composition, Sulphur Content, Carbon Residue > > **Category 3 — Performance Properties:** Octane Number (SI), Cetane Number (CI), Knock Resistance, Gum Formation, Ignition Quality ### Key Distinction at a Glance | Parameter | SI Engine (Petrol) | CI Engine (Diesel) | |---|---|---| | Primary Performance Index | **Octane Number** (knock resistance) | **Cetane Number** (ignition quality) | | Volatility | **High** (300–500 K RVP) | **Low** (RVP < 1 bar) | | Viscosity | **Low** (0.5–0.7 cSt at 40°C) | **Higher** (2–5 cSt at 40°C) | | Auto-ignition Temp | **High** (~250°C) | **Low** (~210°C) | | Ignition Mechanism | Spark Plug | Compression Heat | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## A. Properties for SI Engine (Gasoline) Fuels ### 1. Octane Number (ON) — The Most Critical Property **Definition:** Octane number is the percentage of **iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethyl pentane)** in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane that has the **same knocking intensity** as the test fuel, under standard engine operating conditions. - **Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)** → assigned ON = **100** (very high knock resistance) - **n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆)** → assigned ON = **0** (very poor knock resistance / knocks heavily) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Octane Number Matters for SI Engines:** Knocking (abnormal combustion) damages pistons, cylinders, and reduces efficiency. A high octane number fuel allows **higher compression ratios** without knock, leading to better thermal efficiency (Otto cycle efficiency η = 1 - (1/r^(γ-1))). **Two Standard Test Methods:** - **RON (Research Octane Number):** Tested at **600 rpm, 52°C intake air temperature, no warm-up**. Indicates performance under **mild/low speed** conditions. Higher value (typically 90–100+). - **MON (Motor Octane Number):** Tested at **900 rpm, 149°C intake air temperature, with warm-up**. Indicates performance under **severe/high speed** conditions. Lower than RON for the same fuel. - **(R+M)/2 = Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** → average value posted at petrol pumps in many countries. ### 2. Volatility and Distillation Range (ASTM D86) **Definition:** Volatility describes the fuel's tendency to vaporize. Measured using the **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** test (ASTM D323) at **37.8°C (100°F)**. **Distillation Curve Significance:** - **10% evaporated temperature** → Startability (cold starting) - **50% evaporated temperature** → Warm-up ability / acceleration - **90% evaporated temperature** → Complete vaporization (prevents oil dilution) - **Final Boiling Point (FBP)** → Crankcase dilution tendency ### 3. Calorific Value (CV) The amount of heat released per unit mass (kJ/kg) or volume (kJ/L) upon complete combustion. - **Higher Calorific Value (HCV)** — assumes water vapour condenses to liquid - **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)** — assumes water leaves as vapour (realistic for engines) For gasoline: HCV ≈ **47,300 kJ/kg**; LCV ≈ **44,000 kJ/kg** ### 4. Latent Heat of Vaporization (LHV) Heat required to convert liquid fuel to vapour (without temperature change). For gasoline: **~349 kJ/kg**. Higher LHV → better volumetric efficiency → **charge cooling effect** reduces knock tendency. ### 5. Other SI Engine Fuel Properties - **Viscosity:** Affects fuel spray quality in port fuel injection (PFI). Too low → leakage; too high → poor atomization. - **Surface Tension:** Influences droplet formation in carburettors/injectors. - **Flash Point:** For gasoline, **−42°C to −23°C** (very low; safety hazard but aids cold start). - **Auto-ignition Temperature (AIT):** ~**246°C to 280°C** (high — ensures no premature ignition). - **Gum Formation:** Oxidation products (measured as Existent Gum, mg/100 mL) — should be < **5 mg/100 mL** after washing. - **Sulphur Content:** Strictly limited (< **10–50 ppm** in BS-VI / Euro 6) due to catalytic converter poisoning. --- ## B. Properties for CI Engine (Diesel) Fuels ### 1. Cetane Number (CN) — The Most Critical Property **Definition:** Cetane number is the percentage of **n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** in a mixture of n-cetane and **α-methyl naphthalene** that has the **same ignition delay** as the test fuel. - **n-Cetane (C₁₆H₃₄)** → CN = **100** (very short ignition delay / excellent ignition) - **α-Methyl Naphthalene (C₁₁H₁₀)** → CN = **0** (very long ignition delay / poor ignition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Cetane Number Matters for CI Engines:** A high CN means **short ignition delay** — the fuel ignites rapidly after injection, producing smooth, controlled combustion. A low CN causes **long ignition delay**, leading to uncontrolled rapid combustion → **diesel knock** (audible noise), high cylinder pressure rise rates, and engine roughness. **Optimum CN for CI engines:** **45–55** for modern high-speed diesel engines. ### 2. Calorific Value Diesel has higher energy density per unit mass than gasoline: - HCV ≈ **45,500 kJ/kg**; LCV ≈ **42,500 kJ/kg** - However, per unit **volume**, diesel releases more energy (because of higher density ~840 kg/m³ vs. ~750 kg/m³ for gasoline), giving better **fuel economy**. ### 3. Viscosity (Kinematic) Must be carefully controlled: **2.0–4.5 cSt at 40°C** (per IS 1460 / ASTM D975). - Too low → poor lubrication of fuel pump/injector, leakage - Too high → poor atomization, large droplet size, incomplete combustion, carbon deposits ### 4. Flash Point & Fire Point - **Flash Point of Diesel:** **55–120°C** (much higher than petrol, safer for storage/transport) - **Fire Point:** typically **5–10°C above flash point** ### 5. Cloud Point and Pour Point - **Cloud Point (CP):** Temperature at which wax crystals first become visible (fuel appears cloudy). - **Pour Point (PP):** Lowest temperature at which the fuel still flows. - These limit low-temperature operability of diesel (in cold climates, additives / kerosene blending is used). ### 6. Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom) Indicates tendency to form **carbon deposits** in combustion chamber and injectors. - Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR) for diesel should be < **0.1–0.2%** by mass. ### 7. Sulphur Content Limited to < **10 ppm (BS-VI/Euro 6)** to protect Diesel Particulate Filters (DPF) and SCR catalysts. Higher sulphur → SO₂ emissions, acid rain, lubricity loss. ### 8. Ash Content Should be < **0.01% by mass**. Ash originates from metallic additives and causes injector tip deposits and cylinder liner wear. ### 9. Density - Diesel density at 15°C: **820–860 kg/m³** - Affects **energy per unit volume** and fuel injection metering (fuel injection systems are volume-based). ### 10. Water & Sediment Content - Water causes corrosion, microbial growth, and ice formation in fuel lines. - Limit: < **200 ppm** water and < **0.01%** sediment. --- ## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Property | Formula / Definition | Typical Range (SI / Petrol) | Typical Range (CI / Diesel) | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | Octane Number (ON) | ON = % iso-octane in n-heptane blend matching knock | 90–100 (RON) | Not applicable | Dimensionless | | Cetane Number (CN) | CN = % n-cetane in α-methylnaphthalene blend matching ignition delay | Not applicable | 45–55 | Dimensionless | | (R+M)/2 (AKI) | $(R+M)/2 = (RON + MON) / 2$ | 87–92 (pump) | Not applicable | Dimensionless | | Calorific Value (HCV) | $HCV = \sum (\text{heat of combustion of components})$ | ~47,300 | ~45,500 | kJ/kg | | Calorific Value (LCV) | $LCV = HCV - (h_{fg} \times m_{water})$ | ~44,000 | ~42,500 | kJ/kg | | Energy per Volume | $E_v = LCV \times \rho$ | ~33,000 | ~35,700 | kJ/L | | Ignition Delay Period | $IDP = t_{inj} - t_{ign}$ | (knock) | 0.5–2.0 ms | ms | | Octane Index (ASTM D2699/D2700) | $OI = RON - K \cdot (RON - MON)$ | 85–98 | N/A | Dimensionless | | Cetane Index (ASTM D4737) | $CI = f(\text{density}, 50\% \text{ dist. T})$ | N/A | 40–55 | Dimensionless | | Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) | Pressure of vapour at 37.8°C, V/L = 4:1 | 0.5–1.0 (50–100 kPa) | <0.07 (<7 kPa) | bar (kPa) | | Kinematic Viscosity (ν) | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | 0.5–0.7 cSt (40°C) | 2.0–4.5 cSt (40°C) | cSt (mm²/s) | | Density (ρ) | $\rho = m/V$ at 15°C | 720–780 | 820–860 | kg/m³ | | Auto-ignition Temp (AIT) | Min. T for self-ignition without spark | 246–280 | 210–230 | °C | | Flash Point (FP) | Min. T giving flash near surface | −42 to −23 | 55–120 | °C | | Pour Point (PP) | Lowest flow temperature | N/A | −20 to +10 | °C | | Carbon Residue (CCR) | % carbon left after pyrolysis | <0.05% | <0.1–0.2% | % mass | | Sulphur Content | Mass of S in fuel | <10–50 ppm | <10 ppm | ppm | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In the **oil refining industry**, cracking processes (FCC, hydrocracking) and blending operations are tuned to deliver fuels with target octane and cetane numbers. In **engine design**, fuel properties dictate compression ratio, injection pressure, injection timing, and after-treatment system selection (catalytic converter for petrol, DPF+SCR for diesel). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Octane Index (OI) and its Relation to RON, MON The **Octane Index (OI)** is a more accurate predictor of knock resistance in modern engines. It accounts for the engine's sensitivity to MON vs. RON. **Step 1 — Define the Octane Index formula:** $$ OI = RON - K \cdot (RON - MON) $$ where **K** is the engine-specific **octane sensitivity constant** (0 ≤ K ≤ 1). **Step 2 — Expand and rearrange:** $$ \begin{aligned} OI &= RON - K \cdot RON + K \cdot MON \\ OI &= RON \cdot (1 - K) + MON \cdot K \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret the limits:** - If K = 0 → OI = RON (engine is insensitive to MON) - If K = 1 → OI = MON (engine fully sensitive to MON) - For modern turbocharged GDI engines, K ≈ 0.5–0.8 **Step 4 — Numerical example:** A fuel has RON = 95, MON = 85. Engine K = 0.6. $$ \begin{aligned} OI &= 95 - 0.6 \cdot (95 - 85) \\ &= 95 - 0.6 \cdot 10 \\ &= 95 - 6 \\ &= 89 \end{aligned} $$ So the engine experiences knock resistance equivalent to an octane number of **89**, not 95. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Cetane Index from Density and 50% Distillation Temperature (ASTM D4737 Approximation) A commonly used four-variable cetane index formula is: $$ CI = 454.74 - 1641.416 \cdot D + 774.74 \cdot D^2 - 0.554 \cdot T_{50} + 97.803 \cdot (\log_{10} T_{50})^2 $$ where: - D = density at 15°C (g/mL) - T₅₀ = temperature at which 50% of fuel is distilled (°C) **Step 1 — Insert sample values:** Let D = 0.85 g/mL, T₅₀ = 280°C. **Step 2 — Compute each term:** $$ \begin{aligned} D^2 &= 0.85^2 = 0.7225 \\ 1641.416 \cdot D &= 1641.416 \times 0.85 = 1395.20 \\ 774.74 \cdot D^2 &= 774.74 \times 0.7225 = 559.75 \\ 0.554 \cdot T_{50} &= 0.554 \times 280 = 155.12 \\ \log_{10}(280) &= 2.4472 \\ (\log_{10} T_{50})^2 &= 5.9888 \\ 97.803 \cdot (\log_{10} T_{50})^2 &= 97.803 \times 5.9888 = 585.69 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute back:** $$ \begin{aligned} CI &= 454.74 - 1395.20 + 559.75 - 155.12 + 585.69 \\ CI &= 49.86 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpret:** A calculated CI of ~50 indicates a fuel with **good ignition quality**, suitable for high-speed diesel engines. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why Octane Number and Cetane Number are Inverse Indicators > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Conceptual Point:** Fuels that resist knock in SI engines (high ON) are typically those that **resist auto-ignition** — i.e., they are paraffinic/isoparaffinic with low AIT. These same fuels have **low CN** (long ignition delay) and are therefore **poor CI engine fuels**. This is why: > - **n-heptane:** ON = 0, CN = 56 > - **iso-octane:** ON = 100, CN = 15 > - **n-cetane:** CN = 100, ON = ~30 (very poor knock resistance) ### Mathematical Expression of Inverse Correlation Empirically, for paraffinic hydrocarbons: $$ ON + CN \approx 100 \quad \text{(approximate, for paraffins only)} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{For iso-octane:} \quad ON + CN &= 100 + 15 = 115 \quad \text{(deviation shows it's paraffinic)} \\ \text{For n-cetane:} \quad ON + CN &= 30 + 100 = 130 \quad \text{(long-chain paraffin)} \\ \text{For n-heptane:} \quad ON + CN &= 0 + 56 = 56 \quad \text{(shorter chain)} \end{aligned} $$ This shows that **molecular structure** (chain length, branching, aromaticity) is the underlying physical variable. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Energy per Unit Volume Calculation **Step 1 — Recall definition:** $$ E_v = LCV \times \rho $$ **Step 2 — For petrol:** $\rho_{petrol} = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $LCV_{petrol} = 44{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ $$ E_{v,petrol} = 44{,}000 \times 750 = 33{,}000{,}000 \text{ kJ/m}^3 = 33{,}000 \text{ kJ/L} $$ **Step 3 — For diesel:** $\rho_{diesel} = 840 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $LCV_{diesel} = 42{,}500 \text{ kJ/kg}$ $$ E_{v,diesel} = 42{,}500 \times 840 = 35{,}700{,}000 \text{ kJ/m}^3 = 35{,}700 \text{ kJ/L} $$ **Step 4 — Ratio:** $$ \frac{E_{v,diesel}}{E_{v,petrol}} = \frac{35{,}700}{33{,}000} \approx 1.082 $$ Diesel delivers **~8.2% more energy per litre** — one of the fundamental reasons diesel vehicles have better fuel economy. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: SI vs CI Engine Fuel Property Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crude Oil Refining] --> B[Separation by Boiling Point] B --> C{Naphtha Fraction
30 to 200 C} B --> D{Diesel Fraction
200 to 350 C} B --> E{Residue / Heavy Fractions
> 350 C} C --> F[Reforming / Isomerization / Alkylation] F --> G[Gasoline / Petrol] D --> H[Hydrodesulfurization / Hydrotreating] H --> I[Diesel] G --> J[Required SI Properties] I --> K[Required CI Properties] J --> J1[Octane Number RON MON] J --> J2[Volatility RVP 10 50 90 percent] J --> J3[Calorific Value LCV] J --> J4[Flash Point Negative] J --> J5[Gum Resistance] K --> K1[Cetane Number 45 to 55] K --> K2[Viscosity 2 to 4.5 cSt] K --> K3[Flash Point 55 to 120 C] K --> K4[Cloud Point and Pour Point] K --> K5[Carbon Residue Low] K --> K6[Sulphur Content Less than 10 ppm] J1 --> L[SI Engine / Spark Ignition] K1 --> M[CI Engine / Compression Ignition] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Octane Number and Cetane Number Reference Scale ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SI_Fuel_Scale [SI ENGINE FUEL SCALE - Octane Number] direction LR S1[n-heptane ON equals 0] --> S2[n-pentane ON equals 62] S2 --> S3[iso-octane ON equals 100] S3 --> S4[Toluene ON equals 114] S4 --> S5[Benzene ON equals 101] end subgraph CI_Fuel_Scale [CI ENGINE FUEL SCALE - Cetane Number] direction LR C1[alpha methyl naphthalene CN equals 0] --> C2[1 methyl naphthalene CN equals 8] C2 --> C3[Diesel typical CN 45 to 55] C3 --> C4[n-cetane CN equals 100] C4 --> C5[Heptamethyl nonane CN equals 85] end ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Ignition Delay Period (CI Engine Critical Concept) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Start of Injection] --> B[Physical Delay
Atomization and Mixing] B --> C[Chemical Delay
Pre-flame Reactions] C --> D[Start of Combustion
Auto-ignition] A -->|IDP total| D D --> E[Combustion Phase 1
Premixed burn] E --> F[Combustion Phase 2
Diffusion burn] F --> G[End of Combustion] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Fuel Property Impact on Engine Performance ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Properties] --> B{Engine Type} B -->|SI| C1[High Octane Number] B -->|SI| C2[High Volatility] B -->|SI| C3[Low Viscosity] B -->|CI| D1[High Cetane Number] B -->|CI| D2[Low Volatility] B -->|CI| D3[Moderate Viscosity] C1 --> E1[Allows High Compression Ratio] C2 --> E2[Easy Cold Start and Vapour Formation] C3 --> E3[Good Carburettor Atomization] D1 --> F1[Short Ignition Delay] D2 --> F2[Safe Storage and Handling] D3 --> F3[Good Spray Pattern] E1 --> G[Better Thermal Efficiency] E2 --> G E3 --> G F1 --> H[Smooth Combustion] F2 --> H F3 --> H ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]** **Define Octane Number. Why is it important for SI engine fuels?** **Model Answer:** **Octane Number (ON):** Octane number is a numerical measure of a fuel's resistance to **knocking** (abnormal combustion) in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine. It is defined as the percentage of **iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethyl pentane)** in a reference blend of iso-octane and n-heptane that exhibits the same knocking tendency as the test fuel under standard operating conditions.
