By convention, iso-octane has ON = 100 (excellent knock resistance) and n-heptane has ON = 0 (very poor knock resistance). **Importance for SI Engines:** 1. **Knock Prevention:** A high ON prevents auto-ignition of end-gas before flame arrival, protecting engine components. 2. **Higher Compression Ratio (r):** Fuels with high ON allow engines to be designed with higher r, increasing **Otto cycle efficiency** $\eta_{otto} = 1 - (1/r^{\gamma-1})$. 3. **Performance:** Higher ON fuels (e.g., 95 RON) deliver better power output and thermal efficiency. 4. **Emission Control:** Smooth combustion reduces unburnt hydrocarbons and NOx spikes caused by knock. 5. **Engine Reliability:** Avoids piston/crown damage, ring breakage, and bearing damage caused by severe knock. **[3 marks — Expected: Definition (1.5) + any 3 importance points (1.5)]** --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]** **What is Cetane Number? How does it differ from Octane Number?** **Model Answer:** **Cetane Number (CN):** Cetane number is a measure of a fuel's **ignition quality** in a Compression Ignition (CI) engine. It is defined as the percentage of **n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** in a reference blend of n-cetane and **α-methyl naphthalene** that has the same ignition delay as the test fuel. n-Cetane has CN = 100 (short ignition delay); α-methyl naphthalene has CN = 0 (long ignition delay). **Key Differences from Octane Number:** | Aspect | Octane Number | Cetane Number | |---|---|---| | Engine Type | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) | | Reference Fuels | iso-octane / n-heptane | n-cetane / α-methyl naphthalene | | Measures | Knock resistance | Ignition quality (ignition delay) | | High Value Means | Better resistance to auto-ignition | Faster self-ignition | | Typical Range | 90–100+ RON | 45–55 for diesel | | Opposite Relationship | High ON → Low CN | High CN → Low ON | **[3 marks — Expected: Definition (1.5) + Comparison table (1.5)]** --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1, Question A]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various properties required for **SI engine fuels**, with reference to their significance and typical values. **(b) [7 Marks]** A gasoline fuel has **RON = 96** and **MON = 86**. The engine has an octane sensitivity constant **K = 0.65**. - (i) Calculate the **Octane Index (OI)** of the fuel. - (ii) Calculate the **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)**. - (iii) Comment on whether the fuel is suitable for a high-performance engine requiring OI ≥ 90. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Properties Required for SI Engine Fuels [7 marks]** 1. **Octane Number (RON & MON) [1.5 marks]** - Most critical property; resistance to knock. - RON tested at 600 rpm, low severity. MON at 900 rpm, high severity. - Typical: RON 91–98 for commercial petrol, 100+ for racing fuels. 2. **Volatility [1 mark]** - Measured by RVP (Reid Vapour Pressure) at 37.8°C. - Typical RVP: 50–100 kPa. Required for cold start and vaporization. 3. **Distillation Range (ASTM D86) [1 mark]** - 10%, 50%, 90% evaporated temperatures indicate startability, warm-up, and complete vaporization. - Typical: T10 ≈ 50°C, T50 ≈ 100°C, T90 ≈ 180°C. 4. **Calorific Value [0.5 marks]** - LCV ≈ 44,000 kJ/kg; energy per litre ≈ 33,000 kJ/L. 5. **Latent Heat of Vaporization [0.5 marks]** - ~349 kJ/kg. Provides charge cooling → reduces knock tendency. 6. **Flash Point and Fire Point [0.5 marks]** - Flash point of petrol: −42 to −23°C (safety consideration; very low). 7. **Auto-Ignition Temperature [0.5 marks]** - 246–280°C; high enough to prevent pre-ignition but not knock. 8. **Viscosity, Surface Tension, Gum Content, Sulphur [1 mark]** - Affect spray, storage stability, catalyst life. 9. **Density [0.5 marks]** - 720–780 kg/m³; affects volumetric energy content and air-fuel ratio metering. **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]** **Given:** RON = 96, MON = 86, K = 0.65. **(i) Octane Index (OI): [3 marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} OI &= RON - K \cdot (RON - MON) \\ OI &= 96 - 0.65 \cdot (96 - 86) \\ OI &= 96 - 0.65 \cdot 10 \\ OI &= 96 - 6.5 \\ OI &= 89.5 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Anti-Knock Index (AKI) = (R+M)/2: [2 marks]** $$ AKI = \frac{96 + 86}{2} = \frac{182}{2} = 91 $$ **(iii) Suitability Comment: [2 marks]** Required OI ≥ 90. Calculated OI = 89.5, which is **just below the threshold by 0.5 units**. The fuel is **borderline / marginally unsuitable** for the high-performance engine. To meet the requirement: - Either upgrade to a fuel with RON ≥ 97 (at same MON), or - Use a fuel with K < 0.5 (more paraffinic, less sensitive to MON). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1, Question B]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various properties required for **CI engine fuels**, with their significance and typical values. **(b) [7 Marks]** A diesel fuel has the following properties: - Density at 15°C = **0.85 g/mL** - 50% Distilled at T₅₀ = **285°C** - LCV = **42,500 kJ/kg** - Viscosity at 40°C = **3.5 cSt** Calculate: - (i) **Cetane Index** using the ASTM D4737 approximate formula. - (ii) **Energy per unit volume** of the fuel in kJ/L. - (iii) Comment on whether the fuel is suitable for a high-speed diesel engine (acceptable CN range: 45–55). --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Properties Required for CI Engine Fuels [7 marks]** 1. **Cetane Number [1.5 marks]** - Primary indicator of ignition quality. - Optimum CN: 45–55 (for modern high-speed engines). - Higher CN → shorter ignition delay → smoother combustion. 2. **Calorific Value [1 mark]** - LCV ≈ 42,500 kJ/kg; HCV ≈ 45,500 kJ/kg. - Higher energy per volume than petrol → better fuel economy. 3. **Viscosity [1 mark]** - 2.0–4.5 cSt at 40°C. - Affects injector spray atomization and fuel pump lubrication. 4. **Flash Point and Fire Point [0.5 marks]** - Flash point: 55–120°C (much safer than petrol). 5. **Cloud Point and Pour Point [0.5 marks]** - Cloud point: 0 to −15°C (wax formation). - Pour point: 5 to 10°C below cloud point. - Limit low-temperature operability. 6. **Carbon Residue (CCR/Ramsbottom) [0.5 marks]** - Indicates carbon deposit forming tendency. - Should be < 0.1–0.2% for high-speed diesel. 7. **Sulphur Content [0.5 marks]** - < 10 ppm (BS-VI) to protect DPF, SCR, and reduce acid rain. 8. **Ash, Water, Sediment [0.5 marks]** - Ash < 0.01%; water < 200 ppm; sediment < 0.01%. 9. **Density [0.5 marks]** - 820–860 kg/m³ at 15°C; affects volumetric metering and energy density. 10. **Volatility [0.5 marks]** - RVP < 0.07 bar (very low); high flash point aids storage safety. **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]** **Given:** D = 0.85 g/mL, T₅₀ = 285°C, LCV = 42,500 kJ/kg, viscosity = 3.5 cSt. **(i) Cetane Index using ASTM D4737 formula: [3 marks]** $$ CI = 454.74 - 1641.416D + 774.74D^2 - 0.554T_{50} + 97.803(\log_{10} T_{50})^2 $$ **Step-by-step:** $$ \begin{aligned} D^2 &= 0.85^2 = 0.7225 \\ 1641.416 \cdot 0.85 &= 1395.20 \\ 774.74 \cdot 0.7225 &= 559.75 \\ 0.554 \cdot 285 &= 157.89 \\ \log_{10}(285) &= 2.4548 \\ (\log_{10} 285)^2 &= 6.0261 \\ 97.803 \cdot 6.0261 &= 589.43 \end{aligned} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} CI &= 454.74 - 1395.20 + 559.75 - 157.89 + 589.43 \\ CI &= 50.83 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Energy per unit volume: [2 marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} \rho &= 0.85 \text{ g/mL} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\ E_v &= LCV \times \rho \\ E_v &= 42{,}500 \times 850 \\ E_v &= 36{,}125{,}000 \text{ kJ/m}^3 \\ E_v &= 36{,}125 \text{ kJ/L} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Suitability Comment: [2 marks]** CI = 50.83, which lies **within the acceptable range of 45–55** for high-speed diesel engines. The fuel is **suitable** for the application. With density at 0.85 g/mL and viscosity at 3.5 cSt (within 2.0–4.5 cSt), the fuel also meets volumetric and atomization requirements. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes where KTU students LOSE MARKS:** > > 1. **Confusing ON and CN reference compounds:** Students often write "octane" instead of **iso-octane** (2,2,4-trimethyl pentane) for ON reference. For CN, they forget **n-cetane** is n-hexadecane (C₁₆H₃₄) and second reference is **α-methyl naphthalene**, NOT methylnaphthalene. > > 2. **Mixing up RON and MON test conditions:** RON is at 600 rpm, low severity. MON is at 900 rpm, high severity. A common error is reversing them. > > 3. **Sign errors in Octane Index formula:** $OI = RON - K(RON - MON)$ — students often write $OI = RON - K \cdot MON$ which is wrong. > > 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always state Cetane Index is **dimensionless**, energy is in **kJ/L or kJ/kg**, viscosity in **cSt (mm²/s)**. > > 5. **In Part A answers, writing only 2 points when 3 are needed for full 3 marks.** > > 6. **Not stating "why" a property is required** — examiners award marks for *significance*, not just listing properties. > > 7. **Formula copying errors** in Cetane Index calculation: be careful with $(log₁₀ T₅₀)²$ — square the **logarithm**, not T₅₀. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **SI Engine → Octane Number** is the key property (resistance to knock). **CI Engine → Cetane Number** is the key property (ignition quality). - **Reference fuels:** ON uses **iso-octane (100)** and **n-heptane (0)**; CN uses **n-cetane (100)** and **α-methyl naphthalene (0)**. - **RON** = mild test (600 rpm); **MON** = severe test (900 rpm); **AKI = (R+M)/2**. - **Octane Index** $OI = RON - K(RON - MON)$; accounts for engine-specific sensitivity. - **Octane Number ↑ → Compression Ratio ↑ → Thermal Efficiency ↑** (Otto cycle: $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$). - **Cetane Number ↑ → Ignition Delay ↓ → Combustion Smoother, Less Diesel Knock.** - **Volatility:** Petrol is HIGH volatility (RVP 50–100 kPa) → cold start easy but vapor lock risk. Diesel is LOW volatility (RVP < 7 kPa) → safer storage. - **Flash Point:** Petrol = −42 to −23°C; Diesel = 55–120°C. Diesel is much safer. - **Auto-ignition Temperature:** Petrol ≈ 250°C; Diesel ≈ 210°C. **Diesel ignites at lower T** due to higher CN. - **Calorific Value:** Petrol LCV ≈ 44,000 kJ/kg; Diesel LCV ≈ 42,500 kJ/kg (less per kg), but **diesel has higher energy per litre** (~36,000 kJ/L vs 33,000 kJ/L for petrol) due to higher density. - **Viscosity:** Petrol 0.5–0.7 cSt; Diesel 2.0–4.5 cSt (40°C). - **Cloud Point & Pour Point** apply only to diesel; limit cold-weather operation. - **Sulphur Limit:** < 10 ppm in both modern petrol and diesel (BS-VI / Euro 6) — protects catalysts and after-treatment. - **Carbon Residue, Ash, Water, Sediment** are quality/contamination limits; primarily tested for diesel. - **Inverse relationship** between ON and CN: paraffinic fuels → high CN, low ON; aromatic fuels → high ON, low CN. - **Distillation 10/50/90%** points govern startability, warm-up, and oil-dilution tendency for petrol. - **Latent heat of vaporization** of petrol provides **charge cooling** → reduces knock tendency → another reason why high LHV fuels resist knock. - **Modern trends:** Direct Injection Spark Ignition (DISI) engines require **RON 95+**; Common Rail Diesel engines require **CN 50+** for smooth combustion.

topics.title: Fuel Properties Affecting Engine Performance; Emissions - Main Pollutants and Its Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Properties Affecting Engine Performance; Emissions - Main Pollutants and Its Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fuel Properties Affecting Engine Performance — Definition

In the context of internal combustion (IC) engines, **fuel properties** are intrinsic physical and chemical characteristics of a fuel that govern how it ignites, combusts, and releases energy inside the cylinder. These properties directly dictate engine startability, knocking tendency, power output, fuel economy, and the quantity and type of pollutants generated in the exhaust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Fuel properties are the measurable physical and chemical parameters of a fuel that influence combustion behavior, engine efficiency, power output, emissions, and storage/handling safety in spark-ignition (SI) and compression-ignition (CI) engines.*

The principal properties of significance to engine performance are:

| Sl. No. | Property | Engine Type Most Affected |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Calorific Value (CV) | Both SI & CI |
| 2 | Octane Number (ON) | SI Engines |
| 3 | Cetane Number (CN) | CI Engines |
| 4 | Flash Point & Fire Point | Safety / Storage |
| 5 | Viscosity | CI Engines (fuel injection) |
| 6 | Density | Injection system calibration |
| 7 | Volatility / Distillation Range | SI Engines (cold start) |
| 8 | Self-Ignition Temperature | CI / Safety |
| 9 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio | Combustion efficiency |
| 10 | Latent Heat of Vaporization | Volumetric efficiency, cold start |
| 11 | Chemical Structure (Paraffin, Olefin, Naphthene, Aromatic) | Both |
| 12 | Sulfur Content | Emissions, corrosion |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Cooking on Different Stoves"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a fuel like an ingredient you cook with. *Calorific value* is the "energy density" of the food (like the calorie content of a nut vs. a cucumber). *Octane number* is the "resistance to burning too eagerly" — like a steak that waits on the pan instead of bursting into flames like paper (low octane). *Cetane number* is the "ease of self-ignition" — like how easily a charcoal catches fire by itself. Choosing the wrong fuel for the wrong "stove" (engine) means either raw food (unburnt fuel) or burnt food (knock/damage). The cleaner the fuel chemistry (less sulfur, more paraffins), the cleaner the exhaust — just like using fresh ingredients produces less smoke.

### 1.3 Emissions — Main Pollutants Definition

**Engine exhaust emissions** are the unwanted by-products of the combustion process that escape from the tailpipe into the atmosphere. The four regulated "criteria pollutants" from IC engines are:

1. **CO — Carbon Monoxide** (incomplete combustion)
2. **HC — Unburnt Hydrocarbons** (incomplete combustion / wall quenching)
3. **NOx — Oxides of Nitrogen** (high-temperature oxidation of N₂)
4. **PM — Particulate Matter** (soot, mostly in diesel)

Plus unregulated species: **CO₂** (greenhouse gas), **SOx**, **Lead (Pb)**, **Aldehydes**, **PAHs (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The single largest contributor to urban air pollution globally is road transport. The Euro 6 / BS-VI norms limit CO, HC, NOx, and PM simultaneously — making understanding each pollutant and its control mechanism a high-weightage topic for KTU examinations.

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for gasoline:** **14.7 : 1** (by mass)
- **Stoichiometric AFR for diesel (C₁₂H₂₃):** **~14.5 : 1**
- **Stoichiometric AFR for methane (CH₄):** **17.2 : 1**
- **Auto-ignition temperature of gasoline:** **~250 °C (482 °F)**
- **Auto-ignition temperature of diesel:** **~210 °C (410 °F)**
- **Density of gasoline:** **~740 kg/m³**
- **Density of diesel:** **~840 kg/m³**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Fuel-Property Analysis (Step-by-Step Logic)

#### 2.1.1 Calorific Value (CV)
- **Definition:** Energy released per unit mass (kJ/kg) or per unit volume (kJ/L) when fuel is completely burned.
- **Higher Calorific Value (HCV / GCV):** Heat recovered when water vapor in products condenses (latent heat recovered).
- **Lower Calorific Value (LCV / NCV):** Heat recovered when water leaves as vapor (latent heat lost).
- **Why it matters:** Higher CV → more work per kg → higher indicated thermal efficiency, smaller fuel tank.

$$
\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = 9 \times H \times 2442 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

where $H$ is the mass fraction of hydrogen in the fuel and **2442 kJ/kg** is the latent heat of steam at 25 °C.

#### 2.1.2 Octane Number (ON) — for SI Engines
- **Definition:** Measure of resistance to **knocking** (auto-ignition of end-gas).
- **Reference fuels:** Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈) = 100 (high resistance), n-heptane (C₇H₁₆) = 0 (low resistance).
- **Motor Octane Number (MON):** Tested at 900 rpm, high inlet temperature (more severe).
- **Research Octane Number (RON):** Tested at 600 rpm, low inlet temperature.
- **(R+M)/2 = Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** — posted on fuel pumps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Knocking** is the auto-ignition of the unburnt end-gas ahead of the flame front, producing a sharp metallic pinging sound. Severe knock causes piston damage within seconds.

#### 2.1.3 Cetane Number (CN) — for CI Engines
- **Definition:** Measure of **ignition delay** — the time between fuel injection and start of combustion.
- **Reference fuels:** n-cetane (C₁₆H₃₄) = 100 (short delay), α-methylnaphthalene = 0 (long delay).
- **High CN** = short ignition delay = smoother cold start, less white smoke.
- **Low CN** = long ignition delay = rapid pressure rise, diesel knock, more HC & PM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Relationship:** Octane Number and Cetane Number are **inversely related** for hydrocarbons. Aromatics (e.g., toluene) → high ON, low CN. n-paraffins (e.g., n-hexadecane) → low ON, high CN.

#### 2.1.4 Flash Point and Fire Point
- **Flash Point (FP):** Lowest temperature at which fuel vapor forms an ignitable mixture with air (a flash appears, but not sustained).
- **Fire Point:** Lowest temperature at which vapor burns **continuously** for ≥ 5 seconds.
- **Significance:** Determines safety in storage, transport, classification (Class A, B, C liquids).
- **Gasoline FP:** ~ -43 °C (highly volatile, hazardous). **Diesel FP:** ~ 52–96 °C (safer).

#### 2.1.5 Viscosity
- **Definition:** Internal resistance to flow (Pa·s or cSt).
- **Engine effect:** Must be low enough to atomize in injectors; high enough to lubricate injection pump.
- **Diesel viscosity (kinematic):** 2–5 cSt at 40 °C.
- **Too low:** Pump leakage, power loss. **Too high:** Poor atomization, large droplet size → incomplete combustion → smoke, deposits.

#### 2.1.6 Volatility / Distillation Range
- **T10, T50, T90:** Temperatures at which 10%, 50%, 90% of fuel evaporates.
- **T10 (low end):** Cold start ability. **T50 (mid):** Warm-up, acceleration. **T90 (high end):** Crankcase dilution, deposits.
- **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP):** For gasoline, 45–90 kPa typical.

#### 2.1.7 Self-Ignition Temperature
- **SI engines:** Must be **higher** than peak cylinder temperature (to avoid pre-ignition/knock).
- **CI engines:** Must be **lower** (so compression heating ignites it).

#### 2.1.8 Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio
- **Definition:** Mass of air required to completely burn 1 kg of fuel (no O₂ left, no CO left).
- **For a generic hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ:**

$$
\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{34.56 \left(\dfrac{x}{4} + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)}{12x + y}
$$

#### 2.1.9 Latent Heat of Vaporization
- High latent heat → cools intake charge → **increases volumetric efficiency** but causes **cold-start problems** (fuel condenses on cold walls).
- Alcohols have very high latent heat → poor cold start.

#### 2.1.10 Chemical Structure
- **Paraffins (alkanes):** High CN, low ON, clean burning.
- **Olefins (alkenes):** Moderate, tend to form gum.
- **Naphthenes (cycloalkanes):** Moderate, good for gasoline.
- **Aromatics (benzene, toluene):** High ON, low CN, high soot tendency (especially in diesel).

#### 2.1.11 Sulfur Content
- Forms SO₂ / SO₃ → acid rain.
- Poisons catalytic converters and NOx adsorbers.
- **BS-VI limit:** **< 10 ppm** in both petrol and diesel (India).

### 2.2 Detailed Emissions Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Carbon Monoxide (CO)
- **Formation:** Incomplete oxidation of C → C + ½O₂ → CO.
- **Favored by:** Rich mixture, low temperature, low residence time, poor mixing.
- **Why CO is toxic:** Binds hemoglobin ~200× stronger than O₂ → reduces blood oxygen carrying capacity.
- **Control:** Lean burn engines, oxidation catalyst (2 CO + O₂ → 2 CO₂), better combustion chamber design.

#### 2.2.2 Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)
- **Sources:**
  1. **Wall quenching** — flame extinguished within ~0.5 mm of cold walls.
  2. **Crevice volumes** — trapped in piston ring grooves, head gasket crevice.
  3. **Fuel absorption/desorption** in oil film.
  4. **Incomplete combustion** in locally rich zones.
- **Control:** Oxidation catalyst, exhaust gas recirculation (EGR), close combustion chamber design, fuel injection improvements.

#### 2.2.3 Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)
- **Three types:** NO (~95%), NO₂ (~5%), N₂O (trace).
- **Formation mechanism:** **Extended Zeldovich mechanism** (thermal NOx):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Strongly temperature-dependent** (Arrhenius), also depends on O₂ concentration and residence time.
- **Control:** **EGR** (lowers flame temperature), **three-way catalyst (TWC)**, **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** in diesel, **LNT (Lean NOx Trap)**, retarded timing, water injection.

#### 2.2.4 Particulate Matter (PM)
- **Composition:** Soot (elemental C) + adsorbed HC (SOF) + sulfate + metals.
- **PM2.5 (≤ 2.5 μm) and PM10 (≤ 10 μm)** — health hazards, penetrate lungs.
- **Favored by:** Rich zones, low temperature, high pressure, low cetane, aromatic fuels, high EGR.
- **Control:** **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)** with regeneration, oxidation catalyst, high-pressure common-rail injection, SCR, cleaner fuels.

#### 2.2.5 CO₂ (Greenhouse Gas)
- **Directly proportional to fuel consumption.**
- **Control:** Improved thermal efficiency, hybrid/electric powertrains, biofuels (carbon-neutral cycle), lightweighting.

#### 2.2.6 Sulfur Oxides (SOx)
- **S + O₂ → SO₂** and **2 SO₂ + O₂ → 2 SO₃**.
- **Control:** Ultra-low sulfur fuel (< 10 ppm), desulfurization at refinery.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Key Boundary / Note |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric AFR (hydrocarbon) | $\text{AFR}_{st} = \dfrac{34.56(x/4 + y/4)}{12x + y}$ | kg air/kg fuel | For CₓHᵧ |
| 2 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = (F/A)_{actual} \div (F/A)_{stoich}$ | — | $\phi < 1$ lean, $\phi > 1$ rich |
| 3 | Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | — | $\lambda > 1$ lean, $\lambda < 1$ rich |
| 4 | Energy from fuel | $Q = m \cdot \text{CV}$ | kJ | $m$ in kg, CV in kJ/kg |
| 5 | HCV to LCV | $\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9H \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg at 25 °C; $H$ = H mass fraction |
| 6 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{BSFC} = \dot{m}_f / P_b$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |
| 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | — | 0.25–0.40 typical |
| 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}}$ | — | 0.80–0.95 (naturally aspirated) |
| 9 | NOx formation (Arrhenius) | $\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} \propto \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | — | $E_a \approx 320$ kJ/mol |
| 10 | CO emission index | $\text{CO}_{EI} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CO}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}}$ | g/kg fuel | High at rich, cold start |
| 11 | Carbon balance (BSFC ↔ CO₂) | $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \dfrac{44}{12} \cdot x_f$ | kg/h | $x_f$ = carbon mass fraction of fuel |
| 12 | SFC to BSFC | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{bt} \cdot \text{CV}}$ | kg/kWh | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by KTU examiners in numerical problems. Memorize the stoichiometric AFR derivations and the inverse relationship between $\phi$ and $\lambda$ — they appear almost every exam cycle.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery engineers** tune fuel properties (CN, RON, RVP, S) to meet market specs and emission norms.
- **Engine calibration engineers** map ignition timing, EGR rate, and injection pressure to fuel RON/CN values.
- **Aftertreatment designers** size catalysts and DPFs based on emission indices.
- **Policy / certification engineers** validate BS-VI / Euro 6 compliance using these formulas and pollutant indices.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for a Generic Hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ

**Combustion reaction:**

$$
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 1:** O₂ required = $(x + y/4)$ moles per mole of fuel.

**Step 2:** Moles of air = O₂ moles × (1/0.21) = $\dfrac{x + y/4}{0.21}$ moles of air (since air is 21% O₂ by volume).

**Step 3:** Convert to mass using molecular weights.
- Mass of 1 mole air = **28.97 g**.
- Mass of 1 mole fuel = $(12x + y)$ g.

**Step 4:** Mass of air required = $\dfrac{(x + y/4)}{0.21} \times 28.97$ g

$$
m_{air} = \dfrac{28.97}{0.21}\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) = 137.95\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5:** Mass of fuel = $(12x + y)$ g.

**Step 6:** Form the ratio.

$$
\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{137.95\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)}{12x + y}
$$

**Step 7:** Numerical constant check.

The constant $137.95 \approx 138$ in many textbooks; if the textbook uses 32/0.233 (mass fraction of O₂ in air) directly:

$$
\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \dfrac{4 \times 32}{0.232} \cdot \dfrac{(x + y/4)}{4(12x + y)} = \dfrac{34.56(x + y/4)}{12x + y}
$$

where **34.56** comes from $(32/0.232) \times \frac{1}{4} \times 4$ — i.e., 4 moles of O₂ per 1 mole of fuel is folded into the derivation.

**Step 8 — Sanity check for gasoline (octane, C₈H₁₈, x = 8, y = 18):**

$$
\text{AFR}_{st} = \dfrac{34.56(8 + 18/4)}{12(8) + 18} = \dfrac{34.56(8 + 4.5)}{114} = \dfrac{34.56 \times 12.5}{114} = \dfrac{432}{114} \approx 3.79
$$

Wait — this is **molar ratio**. The actual mass ratio is calculated as:

$$
\text{AFR}_{st} = \dfrac{137.95 \times 12.5}{114} = \dfrac{1724.4}{114} \approx 15.13
$$

This is the **mass AFR** and is very close to the well-known value of **15.13 : 1** for octane (gasoline ≈ 14.7).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always confirm:** Mass-AFR for gasoline is **14.7 : 1** and molar-AFR is **~58 : 1**. Mixing up the two is a common KTU exam mistake.

### 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between HCV, LCV, and Hydrogen Content

**Combustion of 1 kg of fuel CₓHᵧ:**
- Mass of H₂ produced = $9H$ kg (where $H$ is the hydrogen mass fraction).
- Latent heat lost in vapor form = $9H \times h_{fg}$.

**Standard latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C (reference):** $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg.

**Therefore:**

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = 9H \times 2442 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

**Verification with methane (CH₄):**
- $H = 4/16 = 0.25$.
- HCV of CH₄ ≈ 55,530 kJ/kg. LCV ≈ 50,020 kJ/kg.
- Difference = 5,510 kJ/kg.
- Predicted: $9 \times 0.25 \times 2442 = 9 \times 0.25 \times 2442 = 5494.5$ kJ/kg.
- ✓ Matches closely.

### 3.3 Derivation: Indicated Thermal Efficiency and BSFC Relation

**Brake power output:** $P_b = \eta_{bt} \times \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}$

**Rearrange for BSFC:**

$$
\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{bt} \times \text{CV}}
$$

**Convert units:** If CV in kJ/kg and $P_b$ in kW, then $\dot{m}_f$ is in kg/s. To get BSFC in kg/kWh, multiply by 3600 s/h.

$$
\boxed{\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{bt} \times \text{CV}} \text{ kg/kWh}}
$$

**Numerical example:** $\eta_{bt} = 0.30$, CV = 43,000 kJ/kg.

$$
\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{3600}{0.30 \times 43000} = \dfrac{3600}{12900} = 0.279 \text{ kg/kWh} = 279 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A modern diesel engine achieves ~200–220 g/kWh; a gasoline SI engine ~260–300 g/kWh. Lower BSFC is always desired.

### 3.4 Derivation: Carbon Balance to Estimate CO₂ Emissions

**For 1 kg of fuel with carbon mass fraction $x_c$:**

- Mass of C in fuel = $x_c$ kg.
- Moles of C = $x_c / 12$ kmol.
- Moles of CO₂ produced = $x_c / 12$ kmol.
- Mass of CO₂ = $(x_c / 12) \times 44 = 3.67\,x_c$ kg.

**Per kg of fuel:**

$$
\boxed{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emitted} = 3.67 \times x_c \text{ kg/kg fuel}}
$$

**Per kWh (using BSFC):**

$$
\text{Specific CO}_2 = 3.67 \times x_c \times \text{BSFC} \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

**Example:** Gasoline, $x_c \approx 0.86$, BSFC = 0.28 kg/kWh.

$$
\text{CO}_2 = 3.67 \times 0.86 \times 0.28 = 0.884 \text{ kg/kWh} = 884 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

This is consistent with literature values (~850–900 g CO₂/kWh for gasoline engines).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How Each Property Affects Each Pollutant

| Fuel Property ↑ | CO | HC | NOx | PM | Knocking (SI) | Diesel Knock (CI) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Calorific Value | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (higher T) | ↑ (more energy) | — | — |
| Octane Number | — | — | — | — | ↓ (resists knock) | — |
| Cetane Number | ↓ (smooth comb.) | ↓ | — | ↓ | — | ↓ (less knock) |
| Aromatic Content | ↑ | ↑ | — | ↑↑ (soot precursor) | ↓ (high ON) | ↑ (low CN) |
| Volatility (↑) | ↓ | ↓ (better mix) | ↑ | ↓ | — | ↓ |
| Viscosity (↑) | ↑ | ↑ | — | ↑ (poor atomization) | — | ↑ |
| Sulfur Content | — | — | — | ↑ (sulfate PM) | — | — |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | ↓ | ↓ at cold start | ↓ (cooler charge) | — | ↓ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a KTU favorite — it appears in long-answer questions asking "How does cetane number affect diesel engine emissions?" Reference this table in your answers.

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has a displacement of 2.0 L, compression ratio 10:1, runs on gasoline (CV = 44,000 kJ/kg, AFR = 14.7). At wide-open throttle, it consumes 8 kg/h of fuel and delivers 50 kW of brake power. Compute: (a) BSFC, (b) $\eta_{bt}$, (c) $\eta_v$ if ambient air is at 1.013 bar, 30 °C, and volumetric flow is measured at 240 m³/h.

**Solution:**

**(a) BSFC:**

$$
\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{8}{50} = 0.16 \text{ kg/kWh} = 160 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

(This is unrealistically low — engine must be super-efficient; let's just demonstrate the math.)

**(b) $\eta_{bt}$:**

$$
\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{50 \times 3600}{8 \times 44000} = \dfrac{180000}{352000} = 0.511 \text{ or } 51.1\%
$$

**(c) $\eta_v$:**

- Air consumed: 8 × 14.7 = 117.6 kg/h.
- Air density at 1.013 bar, 30 °C = 303.15 K:

$$
\rho_{air} = \dfrac{P}{RT} = \dfrac{101300}{287 \times 303.15} = 1.164 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

- Theoretical air volumetric flow:

$$
\dot{V}_{th} = \dfrac{117.6}{1.164} = 101.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

- $\eta_v = 101.0 / 240 = 0.421$ or **42.1%**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Always use **287 J/(kg·K)** as the specific gas constant for air, and ensure temperature is in **Kelvin (K = °C + 273.15)**. Many KTU students lose 1–2 marks by using R = 8.314 (universal) instead of R_air.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Octane Number". How is it measured, and why is it critical for SI engines?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Octane Number is the percentage of iso-octane in a blend with n-heptane that matches the knocking intensity of the test fuel under standard engine conditions.
- **Measurement (1 mark):** Measured in a Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) engine. Reference fuels: iso-octane = 100, n-heptane = 0. RON tested at 600 rpm, MON at 900 rpm. (R+M)/2 is posted on the pump.
- **Critical for SI engines (1 mark):** Higher octane number resists auto-ignition (knocking) of the end-gas, allowing higher compression ratios and hence higher thermal efficiency without engine damage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the four main regulated pollutants from IC engines. Which one is dominant in SI engines and which in CI engines, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Four regulated pollutants (1 mark):** Carbon Monoxide (CO), Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC), Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx), Particulate Matter (PM).
- **SI engine dominant (1 mark):** **CO and HC** are higher in SI engines because of wall quenching, crevice volumes, and richer operating mixtures near stoichiometric for TWC efficiency.
- **CI engine dominant (1 mark):** **NOx and PM** are higher in CI engines because of high in-cylinder temperatures (NOx via Zeldovich) and heterogeneous combustion with rich local zones that form soot (PM).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the important fuel properties that affect SI engine performance. Discuss how volatility, octane number, and latent heat of vaporization influence cold start, knock, and volumetric efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Volatility (2.5 marks):**
- Volatility is measured by the **distillation curve** (T10, T50, T90) and **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP)**.
- **Cold start (1 mark):** A fuel with high front-end volatility (low T10) vaporizes easily, providing sufficient air-fuel mixture for ignition at low ambient temperatures. Gasoline T10 typically 35–60 °C.
- **Hot start / vapor lock (0.5 mark):** Too high volatility (RVP > 90 kPa) can cause vapor lock in fuel lines at high altitude/high ambient — bubbles form, fuel flow stops, engine stalls.
- **Crankcase dilution (0.5 mark):** Heavy-end volatility (T90) determines fuel wash-down of oil; high T90 causes oil dilution, increased wear.

**Octane Number (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Measure of resistance to auto-ignition (knocking). RON/MON/(R+M)/2.
- **Knock (1 mark):** End-gas auto-ignites before flame arrival, creating shock waves that produce the "ping" sound and can damage pistons. High ON resists this.
- **Compression ratio link (0.5 mark):** Higher ON allows higher CR, which improves thermal efficiency. Premium fuels (RON 95–98) are required for high-CR turbocharged engines.

**Latent Heat of Vaporization (2 marks):**
- Energy required to vaporize liquid fuel (kJ/kg). Gasoline ~ 350 kJ/kg; ethanol ~ 904 kJ/kg.
- **Volumetric efficiency (1 mark):** High LHV cools the intake charge (charge cooling effect), increasing charge density and thus $\eta_v$ and power output. This is why alcohol fuels give high performance.
- **Cold start penalty (1 mark):** On a cold engine, high LHV causes fuel to condense on cold walls, failing to form a combustible mixture → poor cold start. Ethanol-blend vehicles often need a "cold start injector" or heated fuel rail.

**Conclusion:** Volatility, ON, and LHV must be carefully balanced in fuel design for year-round, all-altitude operation.

---

**Q. (b)** With neat sketches, explain the working of a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)**. How does it simultaneously control CO, HC, and NOx? What role does the **lambda (λ) oxygen sensor** play? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** A TWC is the most widely used aftertreatment device in gasoline SI engines. It uses platinum (Pt), palladium (Pd), and rhodium (Rh) as catalysts in a ceramic honeycomb (cordierite) substrate.

**Construction (1 mark):** Stainless-steel shell, ceramic monolith substrate (~ 400 cells/in²), washcoat of alumina (γ-Al₂O₃) impregnated with Pt, Pd, Rh.

**Three Simultaneous Reactions (3 marks):**
- **Reduction reactions (Rh-catalyzed):**
  - 2 NO → N₂ + O₂
  - 2 NO₂ → N₂ + 2 O₂
  - 2 NO + 2 CO → N₂ + 2 CO₂
- **Oxidation reactions (Pt/Pd-catalyzed):**
  - 2 CO + O₂ → 2 CO₂
  - CₓHᵧ + (x + y/4) O₂ → x CO₂ + (y/2) H₂O

**Operating Window (1 mark):** TWC works efficiently only in a narrow band around stoichiometric (λ = 1.00 ± 0.01). Outside this window, efficiency drops sharply — see the characteristic TWC "lambda window" curve.

**Lambda Sensor Role (1 mark):** The oxygen sensor (zirconia-based) in the exhaust compares O₂ in exhaust to atmospheric O₂, generating a voltage (0.1 V lean, 0.9 V rich). ECU uses this as feedback to pulse-width modulate fuel injectors, maintaining λ ≈ 1.00 within the TWC window. Closed-loop control is essential.

**Conclusion:** TWC removes ~95% of CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously, but only with precise closed-loop stoichiometric control.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Compare SI and CI engine requirements in terms of fuel properties. Why is **cetane number critical for diesel** and **octane number critical for gasoline**? Explain with reference to ignition delay, knocking, and combustion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SI vs CI Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | SI Engine Requirement | CI Engine Requirement |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Octane Number | HIGH (≥ 91 RON) | LOW (irrelevant; SI knock, not CI) |
| Cetane Number | LOW (irrelevant) | HIGH (≥ 48 CN) |
| Volatility | HIGH (cold start) | LOW (avoids vapor lock, fires by compression) |
| Viscosity | LOW (1 cSt) | MODERATE (2–5 cSt) for injector lub. |
| Flash Point | LOW (safety = minor; high volatility anyway) | HIGH (> 55 °C) for safety |
| Sulfur | < 10 ppm | < 10 ppm (poisons SCR, DPF) |
| Self-Ignition Temp | HIGH (resist pre-ignition) | LOW (must ignite by compression) |

**Why Cetane Number is Critical for Diesel (2 marks):**
- Cetane Number (CN) measures the **ignition delay** — the time (in crank-angle degrees) between fuel injection start and start of combustion.
- **Short ignition delay (high CN):** Gradual, controlled pressure rise → smooth, quiet combustion → low HC, low PM, low noise.
- **Long ignition delay (low CN):** Fuel accumulates in the cylinder; when it finally ignites, the rate of pressure rise is very high → "diesel knock", high noise, high HC, high PM, possible engine damage.
- **Optimum CN for road diesel:** 48–55. Higher CN (> 60) shortens delay too much → combustion begins before proper mixing → incomplete combustion, smoke, fuel waste.

**Why Octane Number is Critical for Gasoline (2 marks):**
- SI engines rely on spark ignition at a precise moment. The remaining unburnt "end-gas" is compressed and heated.
- **If ON is low:** End-gas auto-ignites before flame front arrives → **knocking** (sharp metallic pinging), loss of power, piston/crown damage, possible pre-ignition.
- **If ON is high:** End-gas resists auto-ignition, flame propagates smoothly, full energy release at TDC, higher CR possible → higher efficiency.

**Conclusion:** The choice of "octane vs cetane" reflects the fundamentally different combustion philosophies of SI and CI engines.

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **formation mechanisms of NOx** in IC engines. Explain in detail the **three engine-level control techniques** and the **two aftertreatment techniques** used to reduce NOx emissions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**NOx Formation — Extended Zeldovich Mechanism (2.5 marks):**
- **Thermal NOx** dominates (> 90% of total NOx).
- Three reactions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \quad (\text{slow, rate-limiting}) \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Highly temperature dependent** (Arrhenius). Below ~ 1500 K, negligible. Above ~ 2000 K, exponential increase.
- **Residence time and O₂ partial pressure** also matter.
- **Peak NOx formation** occurs at slightly lean-of-stoichiometric (λ ≈ 1.05) due to abundant O₂.

**Engine-Level Controls (3 marks):**

1. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) (1.5 marks):** Recirculates a portion (5–25%) of inert exhaust gas back to the intake. This:
   - Lowers adiabatic flame temperature.
   - Reduces O₂ partial pressure.
   - **Result:** Peak NOx reduction of 50–70%. Trade-off: increased PM at high EGR.

2. **Retarded / Optimized Ignition Timing (1 mark):** Shifting spark later (after TDC) moves peak combustion pressure/temperature out of the high-NOx zone. **Cost:** 2–5% loss in BSFC, lower torque.

3. **Lean-Burn Combustion (0.5 mark):** Operating at λ > 1 lowers peak temperature. However, NOx reduction is only moderate; lean-burn requires lean-NOx aftertreatment (LNT/SCR) for full compliance.

**Aftertreatment Controls (1.5 marks):**

1. **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) — SI Engines (0.75 mark):** Reduces NO/NO₂ to N₂ over Rh; requires λ = 1.00 ± 0.01 controlled by O₂ sensor feedback. Efficiency > 95% for NOx.

2. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) — CI / Lean-Burn Engines (0.75 mark):** Aqueous urea (AdBlue/DEF) injected upstream of catalyst. NH₃ formed from urea reacts with NOx:
   - $4 \text{NH}_3 + 4 \text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4 \text{N}_2 + 6 \text{H}_2\text{O}$
   - Efficiency: 80–95%. Used in BS-VI heavy-duty diesels.

**Conclusion:** Modern engines combine all three engine-level controls with one or more aftertreatment stages to meet BS-VI / Euro 6 limits (NOx ≤ 0.06 g/km for gasoline, ≤ 0.08 g/kWh for heavy-duty diesel).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Molar vs Mass confusion** in stoichiometric AFR — write the units explicitly.
> 2. **"Octane" and "Cetane"** are not interchangeable; do not say "cetane number" for a petrol engine.
> 3. **TWC requires λ = 1**, but EGR cools combustion, so don't claim TWC works with EGR; they are different engines.
> 4. In numericals, **always convert °C to K** before using R in gas equations.
> 5. Spelling: "n-heptane", "iso-octane", "cetane", "AdBlue" — single-mark answer losing half-marks for spelling errors is common.
> 6. Always show the **energy balance** when comparing HCV vs LCV — examiners award 1 mark for the latent heat step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Calorific Value (CV):** Energy per kg fuel. **HCV** includes latent heat of water condensation; **LCV** excludes it. $\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = 9H \times 2442$ kJ/kg.
- **Octane Number (ON):** For SI engines. High ON = knock resistance. **RON + MON** averaged = (R+M)/2.
- **Cetane Number (CN):** For CI engines. High CN = short ignition delay = smooth combustion. **CN of road diesel:** 48–55.
- **Flash Point (FP):** Lowest temp for vapor ignition. Gasoline ~ -43 °C, Diesel ~ 52–96 °C. Safety classification.
- **Viscosity:** Must balance atomization and pump lubrication. Diesel: 2–5 cSt at 40 °C.
- **Volatility:** T10, T50, T90 + RVP. Affects cold start, vapor lock, oil dilution.
- **Stoichiometric AFR:** **14.7 : 1** (gasoline), **14.5 : 1** (diesel), **17.2 : 1** (methane).
- **Equivalence ratio $\phi = (F/A)_{actual} / (F/A)_{stoich}$. $\lambda = 1/\phi$.**
- **Aromatic content** raises ON but increases **soot (PM)** in diesel.
- **Sulfur < 10 ppm** mandated by BS-VI.
- **CO** ↓ with lean mixture, ↑ with rich; controlled by oxidation catalyst.
- **HC** sources: wall quenching, crevice, oil absorption; controlled by oxidation catalyst + engine design.
- **NOx** forms via Zeldovich at T > 1500 K; controlled by **EGR, retarded timing, lean burn + SCR/TWC/LNT**.
- **PM** controlled by **DPF + DOC + clean fuel + high-pressure injection**.
- **CO₂** = 3.67 × $x_c$ × BSFC kg/kWh. Reduced by efficiency improvements and biofuels.
- **TWC** = simultaneous CO + HC + NOx control, requires **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01**.
- **SCR** uses **AdBlue (urea)** to reduce NOx in lean (diesel) exhaust.
- **EGR** is the most effective in-cylinder NOx control, **DPF** is the most effective PM control.
- **BSFC** = 3600 / ($\eta_{bt}$ × CV) kg/kWh. Lower is better.
- **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ = actual / theoretical air mass flow.
- **Universal R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**; **R_air = 287 J/(kg·K)** — don't confuse.
- **KNOCK = SI engine problem**, **DIESEL KNOCK = CI engine problem** — opposite causes.
- **Higher ON** allows higher CR → higher $\eta_{th}$.
- **Higher CN** shortens ignition delay → less PM, HC, and noise.
- **Latent heat of vaporization** → charge cooling → higher $\eta_v$ (good), but worse cold start (bad).
- **Health impact:** CO binds Hb 200× stronger than O₂; PM2.5 penetrates alveoli; NOx causes smog and acid rain.
- **BS-VI norms (India, 2020+):** NOx ≤ 0.06 g/km (petrol), PM ≤ 0.0045 g/km (diesel).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Additives - Need
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Additives - Need

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Additives** are chemical compounds (typically organic or organometallic) that are blended with base fuels in very small concentrations — usually in the range of **parts per million (ppm)** to a few percent by volume or mass — in order to improve certain physical, chemical, or performance characteristics of the fuel that the parent fuel does not possess on its own.

In the context of **KTU Module 1 (Alternative Fuels and Energy Systems)**, fuel additives are critical because most alternative fuels (ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, CNG, LPG, hydrogen blends) bring inherent drawbacks such as low lubricity, poor cold-flow behavior, oxidative instability, corrosive tendencies, and higher NOₓ emissions. Additives are the *enabling bridge* that allows these fuels to meet real-world engine performance and emission norms.

### 1.2 The "Need" — Why Fuel Additives?

The need for fuel additives arises from the gap between the **ideal properties** demanded by modern engines/emission regulations and the **actual properties** of the base (especially alternative) fuels. They are needed to:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Improve Combustion Quality** – better cetane/octane rating, smoother burn.
> - **Protect Engine Components** – reduce wear, corrosion, deposits.
> - **Stabilize Fuel** – prevent oxidation, gum formation, phase separation.
> - **Reduce Emissions** – smoke, CO, NOₓ, unburnt hydrocarbons, particulate matter.
> - **Improve Handling & Storage** – anti-icing, anti-foam, demulsifiers.
> - **Enhance Cold-Start & Cold-Flow** – pour-point depressants, anti-gel agents.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Think of a fuel additive like **vitamins and medicines added to human food**.
> - The food (base fuel) gives energy.
> - But on its own, it may lack certain nutrients, may spoil easily, may not suit every person (engine), or may cause long-term health (emissions/wear) issues.
> - Vitamins (additives) are added in **tiny amounts** to:
>   - **Preserve** the food (anti-oxidants → fuel stabilizers),
>   - **Aid digestion** (anti-knock → octane boosters, cetane improvers),
>   - **Protect organs** (detergents → deposit-control additives),
>   - **Improve absorption** (lubricity improvers → protect fuel injectors).
>
> Just like too much medicine is harmful, additives are dosed carefully — usually in the **ppm range** — to avoid side effects (ash deposits, increased emissions, catalyst poisoning).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Dosing Range:** typically **50 – 500 ppm** for most performance additives, up to a few percent for some co-solvents.
- **Blending Octane Number (BON):** key metric for octane boosters.
- **Cetane Number Improver Index:** measured as **Δ CN per unit mass** of additive.
- **ASTM D975 / EN 590 / IS 1460:** standard specifications that dictate additive performance criteria.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why & How" Behind Each Need

The need for fuel additives is best understood by mapping the **deficiency** of the base fuel to the **additive function** that overcomes it.

| # | Problem in Base Fuel | Why It Happens | Additive Category Needed | How the Additive Solves It |
|---|----------------------|----------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Low octane / knocking | Low auto-ignition resistance (e.g., in ethanol-gasoline blends at lean burn) | Octane boosters (MTBE, ETBE, MMT, TML) | Increase activation energy for auto-ignition; absorb radicals that propagate knock |
| 2 | Low cetane / poor ignition (biodiesel blends) | High cetane alternatives help cold-start of CI engines | Cetane improvers (2-EHN, DIPE) | Decompose into peroxides that advance ignition timing |
| 3 | Injector deposits & wear | Low-sulfur fuels lack natural lubricity; biodiesel has solvent action | Lubricity improvers (fatty acid esters), detergents (PIB-amine) | Form a protective film on metal surfaces; solubilize deposit precursors |
| 4 | Oxidation & gum formation | Fuel reacts with dissolved O₂ during storage | Antioxidants (BHT, BHA), metal deactivators (salicylates) | Scavenge peroxy radicals; chelate trace metal ions (Cu, Fe) that catalyze oxidation |
| 5 | Phase separation (ethanol-blend fuels) | Water tolerance of ethanol-gasoline is limited | Co-solvents (higher alcohols, ethers) | Bridge polarity gap; raise miscibility of water-ethanol-hydrocarbon |
| 6 | Poor cold-flow (biodiesel) | Saturated FAMEs crystallize at low T | Cold-flow improvers, pour-point depressants (polyacrylates) | Co-crystallize with wax; modify wax crystal shape and size |
| 7 | High NOₓ, smoke, PM | Local rich/lean zones, high flame temperature | Combustion improvers (ferrocene, manganese compounds) | Catalyze radical reactions; lower local flame T via better mixing |
| 8 | Corrosion (especially methanol, biodiesel) | Water content, free fatty acids, methanol attacks metals | Corrosion inhibitors (fatty acid amines, imidazolines) | Adsorb on metal surface; form hydrophobic barrier |
| 9 | Foaming during refueling | Air entrainment in low-viscosity fuels | Anti-foam agents (silicone polymers) | Reduce surface tension of bubbles; accelerate bubble collapse |
| 10 | Icing in cold weather (CNG/LPG) | Pressure drop → Joule-Thomson cooling → ice on regulators | Anti-icing agents (alcohols, glycol ethers) | Lower freezing point of entrained water |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although this topic is predominantly descriptive, a few **quantitative** relations are high-yield for KTU 2024 numerical questions.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Additive Concentration (mass) | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{m_{add}}{m_{fuel}} \times 10^{6}$ | Parts per million by mass | ppm |
| Additive Concentration (vol) | $C_{\% v/v} = \dfrac{V_{add}}{V_{fuel}} \times 100$ | Volume percent | % |
| Octane Number Improvement | $\Delta ON = ON_{blend} - ON_{base}$ | Blending effect | Numbers (RON/MON) |
| Cetane Number Improvement | $\Delta CN = CN_{blend} - CN_{base}$ | Ignition quality gain | Numbers |
| Blending Octane Number (BON) | $ON_{blend} = ON_{base} + x_{add}\left(BON - ON_{base}\right)$ | Linear blending model | Numbers |
| Air-Fuel ratio correction | $\text{AFR}_{actual} = \text{AFR}_{st} \cdot \left(1 + \alpha \cdot C_{add}\right)$ | $\alpha$ = correction coefficient (additive-specific) | dimensionless |
| Heat release boost | $HRR_{add} = HRR_{base}\left(1 + \beta \cdot C_{add}\right)$ | Combustion catalysis gain | kJ/m³ |
| Energy density ratio | $\dfrac{\rho_{add} \cdot LHV_{add}}{\rho_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | Energy contribution of additive | dimensionless |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Refineries & Petrochemical Industries:** Octane and cetane improvers enable compliance with BS-VI / Euro-VI fuel norms.
> - **Automotive OEMs:** Detergent additives keep direct-injection engines deposit-free (a major warranty issue).
> - **Aviation & Defense:** Anti-icing and thermal stability additives are mission-critical.
> - **Renewable Energy Sector:** Additives make **biodiesel** and **bioethanol** commercially deployable by fixing cold-flow and stability issues.
> - **Hydrogen Economy:** Anti-permeation and metal-hydride-stabilizer additives enable safe storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Determining the Required Dosage of a Cetane Improver

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU 2024 Numerical):**
A base biodiesel blend (B20) has a measured **Cetane Number (CN) = 48**. Engine tests on a target engine require a **minimum CN = 52**. The available additive **2-EHN (2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate)** has a **Blending Cetane Number Improvement Index** of $k = 0.6$ CN units per **1000 ppm** of additive blended. Calculate the **minimum dosage** of 2-EHN required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:**
> - $CN_{base} = 48$
> - $CN_{required} = 52$
> - $k = 0.6$ CN units per 1000 ppm
>
> **Find:** $C_{add}$ (in ppm) of 2-EHN.

#### Step 1 — Write the Linear Blending Model for Cetane Number

For a dilute additive (ppm-level), the cetane number of the blend is assumed to vary **linearly** with the additive mass concentration:

$$
CN_{blend} = CN_{base} + k \cdot C_{add}
$$

where $C_{add}$ is the additive concentration expressed in **thousands of ppm (i.e., per 1000 ppm units)**. This linearity holds for small doses where additive–fuel interactions remain ideal.

#### Step 2 — Set Up the Required Condition

We require $CN_{blend} \geq CN_{required}$:

$$
CN_{base} + k \cdot C_{add} \geq CN_{required}
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
48 + (0.6) \cdot C_{add} \geq 52
$$

#### Step 3 — Isolate the Additive Concentration

Rearranging the inequality:

$$
0.6 \cdot C_{add} \geq 52 - 48
$$

$$
0.6 \cdot C_{add} \geq 4
$$

$$
C_{add} \geq \dfrac{4}{0.6} = 6.667
$$

Since $C_{add}$ is in units of "per 1000 ppm":

$$
C_{add} \geq 6.667 \times 1000 \text{ ppm} = 6667 \text{ ppm}
$$

#### Step 4 — Final Answer and Practical Consideration

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum dosage of 2-EHN ≈ 6667 ppm (≈ 0.67 % by volume)**
>
> **Practical Engineer's Note:** In real-world applications, the actual dosage is held slightly **above** the calculated minimum (typically a 10–20 % safety margin) to account for fuel batch variability, fuel aging, and engine duty cycles.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Fuel Type vs. Additive Need

| Base / Alternative Fuel | Inherent Deficiency | Most Needed Additive | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biodiesel (B100)** | Poor cold-flow, NOₓ rise, oxidative instability | Cold-flow improver, antioxidant | Saturated FAMEs crystallize; polyunsaturated FAMEs oxidize |
| **Ethanol (E85/E100)** | Low lubricity, corrosion, vapor-lock | Lubricity additive, corrosion inhibitor, co-solvent | Ethanol strips oil films; attacks aluminum and zinc |
| **Methanol (M85/M100)** | High latent heat (cold-start issue), corrosive | Volatility improver, corrosion inhibitor | Cold-start failure; attacks plastics, metals |
| **CNG / LNG** | Power loss, slow flame, valve seat wear | Valve seat protectors, ignition improvers (for lean burn) | Dry gas → seat recession; low flame speed → misfire |
| **LPG** | Vapor-lock in hot climates, odor masking | Anti-vapor-lock additive, odorant (ethyl mercaptan) | Pressurized fuel suffers at high ambient T |
| **Hydrogen (H₂)** | Pre-ignition, embrittlement of metals | Anti-pre-ignition additives (in blends), metal-protective coatings | High flame speed, small quenching distance |
| **Vegetable oil (raw)** | Very high viscosity (10–20× diesel) | Viscosity reducers (e.g., via transesterification or dilution) | Atomization failure in CI engines |
| **Producer gas / Syngas** | Low energy density, tar, particulates | Tar-cracking catalysts, anti-soot additives | Tar condenses; soot plugs injectors |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Octane Booster Dosage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A refinery wishes to raise a gasoline blend from **RON 87** to **RON 91** using **ETBE (Ethyl tert-butyl ether)** which has a **Blending Octane Number (BON) = 118 (RON)**. Assuming linear blending behavior, calculate the **volume percent** of ETBE required.

**Step 1 — Write the linear blending equation:**

$$
ON_{blend} = ON_{base} + x_{add}\,(BON - ON_{base})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

$$
91 = 87 + x_{add}\,(118 - 87)
$$

$$
91 - 87 = x_{add} \cdot 31
$$

$$
x_{add} = \dfrac{4}{31} = 0.1290
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percent:**

$$
x_{add} = 0.1290 \times 100 \approx 12.9\,\%\,v/v
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Required ETBE dosage ≈ 12.9 % v/v.** This is consistent with commercial oxygenate blending practice (Ethers like ETBE, MTBE are typically added at 10–22 % v/v).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fuel Additives Classification

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions** between the base fuel, the additive family, the engine system, and the emission/performance outputs. This is the Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow used to depict how each additive family couples with engine and emission subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Fuel\nPetroleum Diesel\nGasoline\nBiodiesel\nEthanol\nCNG\nHydrogen Blend] --> B{Additive Family\nRequired}
    
    B --> C1[Performance Additives]
    B --> C2[Handling Storage Additives]
    B --> C3[Stabilizer Additives]
    B --> C4[Emission Control Additives]
    B --> C5[Protective Additives]
    
    C1 --> D1[Octane Booster\nMTBE / ETBE / MMT / TML]
    C1 --> D2[Cetane Improver\n2-EHN / DIPE / DTBP]
    C1 --> D3[Combustion Improver\nFerrocene / Mn Compounds]
    C1 --> D4[Anti-Knock\nLead Replacements\nAromatic Amines]
    
    C2 --> D5[Anti-Icing Agent\nGlycol Ethers / Alcohols]
    C2 --> D6[Anti-Foam Agent\nSilicone Polymers]
    C2 --> D7[Demulsifier\nPolyol Esters]
    C2 --> D8[Anti-Static Agent\nStadis 450]
    
    C3 --> D9[Antioxidant\nBHT / BHA]
    C3 --> D10[Metal Deactivator\nSalicylates]
    C3 --> D11[Biocide\nIsothiazolinones]
    
    C4 --> D12[Deposit Control\nPIB-Amine Detergents]
    C4 --> D13[Smoke Suppressant\nBarium Compounds]
    C4 --> D14[NOx Reducer\nOrganic Ethers]
    C4 --> D15[SOF Reducer\nFuel Borne Catalysts]
    
    C5 --> D16[Corrosion Inhibitor\nImidazolines]
    C5 --> D17[Lubricity Improver\nFatty Acid Esters]
    C5 --> D18[Cold Flow Improver\nPolyacrylates]
    C5 --> D19[Co-Solvent\nHigher Alcohols]
    
    D1 --> E1[Engine Output\nBrake Power\nBSFC\nThermal Efficiency]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E2[Fuel System\nStorage Stability\nPumping Viscosity]
    D6 --> E2
    D7 --> E2
    D8 --> E2
    D9 --> E2
    D10 --> E2
    D11 --> E2
    D12 --> E3[Engine Internals\nInjector Cleanliness\nPiston Rings\nCombustion Chamber]
    D13 --> E3
    D14 --> E4[Exhaust\nNOx CO HC PM]
    D15 --> E4
    D16 --> E3
    D17 --> E3
    D18 --> E2
    D19 --> E2
    
    E1 --> F[KTU Performance Metrics]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    
    F --> G[Compliance with\nBS-VI / Euro-VI\nOEM Warranty\nStorage Standards]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Additive Blending Decision Tree

This diagram shows the **decision logic** an engineer follows when selecting an additive for an alternative fuel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Base Fuel Selected] --> Q1{Fuel Type?}
```
