## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMUT411]**
*"What is meant by the term 'fuel additive'? Briefly explain the need for fuel additives in the context of alternative fuels."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — one mark per key point):**

1. **Definition (1 Mark):** A fuel additive is a chemical compound blended with the base fuel in very small quantities (typically 50–5000 ppm) to enhance specific properties such as combustion, stability, lubricity, or emission behavior that the parent fuel does not adequately possess on its own.

2. **Need – Performance Enhancement (1 Mark):** Alternative fuels like ethanol, methanol, and biodiesel suffer from drawbacks such as low cetane number, poor cold-flow, low lubricity, and high NOₓ. Additives like cetane improvers, octane boosters, and cold-flow improvers compensate for these deficiencies and bring the fuel to engine-usable quality.

3. **Need – Stability & Engine Protection (1 Mark):** Additives are needed to prevent oxidation, gum formation, and phase separation during storage; to protect metal components from corrosion caused by alcohols/biodiesel; and to maintain injector cleanliness — all of which are critical for reliable engine operation and long service life.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEMUT411]**
*"List any three categories of fuel additives and state one example for each."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — one mark per category-example pair):**

1. **Octane Booster** — Example: **MTBE (Methyl tert-butyl ether)** or **ETBE**. Used in gasoline to suppress knock by raising the Research Octane Number (RON).

2. **Cetane Improver** — Example: **2-EHN (2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate)**. Used in diesel and biodiesel blends to shorten ignition delay and improve cold-start.

3. **Antioxidant / Stabilizer** — Example: **BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene)**. Used in biodiesel and middle distillates to prevent oxidation and gum formation during storage.

*(Other valid pairs: Lubricity improver — fatty acid methyl ester; Cold-flow improver — polyacrylate; Anti-foam — silicone polymer; Biocide — isothiazolinone; Co-solvent — iso-butanol; Smoke suppressant — barium sulfonate.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q A(a).** *"With the help of a clear classification, explain the various categories of fuel additives. Discuss in detail the role of (i) octane boosters, (ii) cetane improvers, and (iii) anti-oxidants in fuel quality improvement."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Fuel Additives (3 Marks):**
Fuel additives are broadly classified into the following functional families:
- **Performance Additives** — octane boosters, cetane improvers, combustion improvers, anti-knock agents.
- **Handling & Storage Additives** — anti-icing agents, anti-foam agents, demulsifiers, anti-static agents.
- **Stability Additives** — antioxidants, metal deactivators, biocides.
- **Emission-Control Additives** — deposit-control detergents, smoke suppressants, fuel-borne catalysts.
- **Protective Additives** — corrosion inhibitors, lubricity improvers, cold-flow improvers, co-solvents.

**(i) Octane Boosters (1.5 Marks):**
Octane boosters are added to gasoline to suppress engine knock. They work by increasing the activation energy of the auto-ignition reaction and by scavenging peroxy radicals that initiate knock. Examples include **MTBE, ETBE, TML (Tetra Methyl Lead — phased out), MMT (Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl), and aromatic amines**. The blending octane number (BON) is the key parameter; for instance, ETBE has a BON of ~118 (RON), so a small addition of ~10–15 % v/v can raise a 87-RON gasoline to ~91 RON.

**(ii) Cetane Improvers (1.5 Marks):**
Cetane improvers are added to diesel and biodiesel to reduce the ignition delay, enabling smoother cold-start and lower emissions of unburnt hydrocarbons and smoke. They typically function by decomposing at compression temperatures to release free radicals that initiate combustion earlier. **2-EHN (2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate)** is the most widely used commercial cetane improver; dosages of 500–3000 ppm can raise the CN of a fuel by 3–8 points. DIPE (Di-isopropyl ether) and DTBP (Di-tert-butyl peroxide) are also used.

**(iii) Anti-oxidants (1 Mark):**
Antioxidants inhibit the free-radical chain reactions that lead to oxidation, gum, and acid formation in fuels during storage — particularly critical for biodiesel (rich in polyunsaturated FAMEs) and for cracked gasoline. Common examples are **BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene), BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole), and hindered phenols**, used in the 10–100 ppm range. They are often paired with **metal deactivators (e.g., N,N'-disalicylidene-1,2-propanediamine)** to chelate trace Cu and Fe that catalyze oxidation.

---

> **Q A(b).** *"A B20 biodiesel blend has a Cetane Number of 49. It is to be used in a turbocharged CI engine that requires a minimum CN of 54. The available cetane improver (2-EHN) provides an improvement index of 0.7 CN units per 1000 ppm. Calculate (i) the minimum dosage of 2-EHN in ppm, and (ii) the equivalent volume percentage if the density of 2-EHN is 970 kg/m³ and the density of B20 is 860 kg/m³. Assume additive–fuel interaction is linear."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Minimum Dosage in ppm (3.5 Marks):**

Using the linear blending relation:

$$
CN_{blend} = CN_{base} + k \cdot \dfrac{C_{ppm}}{1000}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
54 = 49 + 0.7 \cdot \dfrac{C_{ppm}}{1000}
$$

$$
5 = 0.7 \cdot \dfrac{C_{ppm}}{1000}
$$

$$
C_{ppm} = \dfrac{5 \times 1000}{0.7} = 7142.86 \text{ ppm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum dosage of 2-EHN ≈ 7143 ppm (≈ 0.714 % by mass).**

**(ii) Conversion to Volume Percentage (3.5 Marks):**

For ppm by mass, the mass of additive per mass of fuel is:

$$
C_{ppm} = \dfrac{m_{add}}{m_{fuel}} \times 10^{6} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{m_{add}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{7142.86}{10^{6}} = 7.143 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Let $m_{fuel} = 1$ kg (basis). Then $m_{add} = 7.143 \times 10^{-3}$ kg.

Convert masses to volumes using densities:

$$
V_{add} = \dfrac{m_{add}}{\rho_{add}} = \dfrac{7.143 \times 10^{-3}}{970} = 7.364 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}} = \dfrac{1}{860} = 1.163 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Volume percent:

$$
\% v/v = \dfrac{V_{add}}{V_{fuel}} \times 100 = \dfrac{7.364 \times 10^{-6}}{1.163 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = 0.633\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Volume percentage of 2-EHN ≈ 0.63 % v/v.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **Q B(a).** *"Explain the need for fuel additives in alternative fuel systems. Discuss in detail the role of (i) lubricity improvers, (ii) cold-flow improvers, and (iii) co-solvents, with one example for each."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Need for Additives in Alternative Fuels (2 Marks):**
Alternative fuels (ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, CNG, LPG, hydrogen blends) often have properties that are ideal in some respects but problematic in others:
- **Ethanol/Methanol** → low lubricity, high corrosion, vapor-lock, phase separation with water.
- **Biodiesel** → poor low-temperature flow, oxidation instability, higher NOₓ, deposits.
- **CNG/LPG** → valve-seat recession, cold-start difficulty, slow flame speed.
- **Hydrogen blends** → pre-ignition, metal embrittlement.
Additives correct these shortcomings, allowing the fuels to be stored, transported, and burned in existing engines with minimal retrofitting.

**(i) Lubricity Improvers (1.5 Marks):**
Low-sulfur petrodiesel, biodiesel blends, and especially alcohol fuels have inherently low lubricity, leading to wear of fuel injection pumps and injectors. **Lubricity improvers** (e.g., **fatty acid methyl esters / oleic acid esters**) form a thin protective film on metal surfaces, reducing friction and wear. Dosages are typically 50–500 ppm.

**(ii) Cold-Flow Improvers (1.5 Marks):**
Biodiesel and high-paraffin diesel fuels crystallize (form wax) at low temperatures, blocking fuel lines and filters. **Cold-flow improvers / pour-point depressants** (e.g., **polyacrylate esters, ethylene–vinyl acetate copolymers**) co-crystallize with wax and modify the wax crystal shape, lowering the pour point and cold-filter plugging point (CFPP).

**(iii) Co-solvents (2 Marks):**
Ethanol–gasoline blends (E10, E20, E85) are vulnerable to **phase separation** when water is present, because ethanol is hygroscopic and the blend has limited water tolerance. **Co-solvents** such as **iso-butanol, n-butanol, or higher alcohols** are added in small quantities to bridge the polarity gap, keeping the fuel in a single homogeneous phase. They also raise the octane rating slightly and improve cold-start of high-percentage ethanol blends.

---

> **Q B(b).** *"A refinery is blending 10 % v/v ethanol into gasoline. The base gasoline has a RON of 86 and a Density of 740 kg/m³. Pure ethanol has a RON of 110 and a Density of 789 kg/m³. Assuming a linear blending model, calculate (i) the expected RON of the blend, and (ii) the mass-based percentage of ethanol in the final blend."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Expected RON of the Blend (3.5 Marks):**

Using the linear blending relation:

$$
ON_{blend} = ON_{base} + x_{add} \left(ON_{add} - ON_{base}\right)
$$

where $x_{add} = 0.10$ (10 % v/v):

$$
ON_{blend} = 86 + 0.10 \times (110 - 86)
$$

$$
ON_{blend} = 86 + 0.10 \times 24 = 86 + 2.4 = 88.4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected RON of the blend ≈ 88.4** (this corresponds to a typical Regular-Grade gasoline in India; commercial blending often uses non-linear models and synergy effects which can raise this by 1–3 additional RON points).

**(ii) Mass-Based Percentage of Ethanol (3.5 Marks):**

Take a basis of 1 m³ (1000 L) of blend.

Volume of ethanol = $0.10 \times 1000 = 100$ L  
Volume of base gasoline = $0.90 \times 1000 = 900$ L

Mass of ethanol:

$$
m_{ethanol} = V_{ethanol} \times \rho_{ethanol} = 100 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} \times 789 \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 78.9 \text{ kg}
$$

Mass of gasoline:

$$
m_{gasoline} = 900 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} \times 740 \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 666.0 \text{ kg}
$$

Total mass of blend:

$$
m_{total} = 78.9 + 666.0 = 744.9 \text{ kg}
$$

Mass percentage of ethanol:

$$
\%\,m/m = \dfrac{78.9}{744.9} \times 100 = 10.59\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass-based percentage of ethanol ≈ 10.59 % (m/m).**
>
> **Engineer's Note:** Because ethanol has a higher density than gasoline, the mass percentage is *slightly higher* than the volume percentage. This is a classic KTU examiner trap — students often assume they are equal.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Fuel Additives – Need" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ppm with %** — Always write units explicitly. 1 % = 10,000 ppm. A student writing 6667 instead of 0.67 % loses full marks in dosage questions.
> 2. **Linear vs. Non-linear blending** — The KTU 2024 model answers expect the **linear** model for dilute additives but ask students to mention the non-linear reality as a comment line (e.g., "in practice, blending octane numbers are non-linear"). Not mentioning this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mass vs. Volume basis** — In dosage numericals, students forget to convert between mass and volume. Always state: *ppm (m/m)*, *% v/v*, *% m/m* — and convert densities carefully.
> 4. **Naming the additive family, not just the example** — A common mistake is writing "BHT" without stating it is an **antioxidant / stabilizer**. The family name carries the conceptual credit.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Need"** — When asked about additives, students list examples but forget to *justify* the need. Every example should be tied back to a **fuel deficiency** (e.g., "low cetane → cetane improver").
> 6. **Skipping the linkage to alternative fuels** — This is a **PEMUT411** (Alternative Fuels) course. Any answer that doesn't tie the additive to a *specific alternative fuel* (biodiesel, ethanol, CNG, etc.) is marked incomplete. A generic "Fuel additive X is used in petrol" answer loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Fuel Additives: Need"**
>
> ✅ **Definition (must memorize):** Chemical compounds blended in ppm-level doses to overcome a specific deficiency of the base fuel.
>
> ✅ **Five Major Families of Additives:**
>   - Performance (octane, cetane, combustion)
>   - Handling/Storage (anti-icing, anti-foam, demulsifier)
>   - Stability (antioxidant, metal deactivator, biocide)
>   - Emission-Control (detergent, smoke suppressant, FBC)
>   - Protective (corrosion inhibitor, lubricity, cold-flow, co-solvent)
>
> ✅ **"Need" = Mapping deficiency to function.** Every additive exists because a fuel has a problem; know which problem each additive family solves.
>
> ✅ **Top 10 Additives to Memorize (with family & example):**
>   1. Octane booster → **MTBE / ETBE**
>   2. Cetane improver → **2-EHN**
>   3. Lubricity improver → **Oleic acid ester**
>   4. Antioxidant → **BHT**
>   5. Metal deactivator → **Salicylaldehyde derivative**
>   6. Co-solvent → **Iso-butanol**
>   7. Anti-foam → **Silicone polymer**
>   8. Cold-flow improver → **Polyacrylate**
>   9. Corrosion inhibitor → **Imidazoline**
>   10. Combustion improver → **Ferrocene / MMT**
>
> ✅ **Two Key Formulas (must practice):**
>   - $C_{ppm} = (m_{add}/m_{fuel}) \times 10^{6}$
>   - $ON_{blend} = ON_{base} + x_{add}(BON - ON_{base})$
>
> ✅ **Conversion Traps:**
>   - 1 % = 10,000 ppm
>   - mass-% ≠ volume-% (use density for conversion)
>   - ppm by mass ≠ ppm by volume (rare in SI, but appears in fuel specs)
>
> ✅ **Linkage to KTU Module 1 Theme:** Every additive discussion must be tied to an **alternative fuel** (biodiesel, ethanol, methanol, CNG, LPG, H₂). Generic answers score poorly.
>
> ✅ **Recent Exam Trend (2023–2024):** The university has been asking 7-mark sub-parts that combine a **definition + classification + numerical dosage** in a single question. Practice these integrated answers.
>
> ✅ **Examiner's One-Liner:** *"An additive is *needed* only if a fuel is *deficient* in some property required by the engine or regulation."* — Use this as your opening line for full-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Alternative Fuels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Fuels (KTU 2024 Definition):** Alternative fuels are non-conventional, non-fossil-derived or partially fossil-derived energy carriers that can substitute conventional petroleum-based fuels (gasoline, diesel) for transportation, power generation, or industrial applications. They are characterized by their potential to reduce **greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions**, **urban air pollutants**, and **geopolitical fuel dependency**.

The term *Alternative Fuels* broadly encompasses solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels (including electricity and hydrogen) that are produced from renewable resources, waste streams, or unconventional energy sources.

## 1.2 Classification Framework – The "Types" Topic

For KTU 2024 Module 1, the **Types** of alternative fuels are classified into three primary categories based on their physical state and energy source:

| **Classification Basis** | **Category** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Bio-derived | Ethanol, Biodiesel, Biogas |
| Origin | Fossil-derived (cleaner) | CNG, LNG, LPG, DME |
| Origin | Synthetic/Hydrogen | H₂, Synthetic Diesel, NH₃ |
| Physical State | Gaseous | CNG, LNG, LPG, H₂, Biogas |
| Physical State | Liquid | Ethanol, Biodiesel, Methanol, DME |
| Physical State | Solid (Renewable Electricity) | Battery Electric, Solar, Wind |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Focus:** Students must remember the classification based on **renewable vs. non-renewable**, as well as the **first-generation, second-generation, and third-generation biofuels** taxonomy.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the fuel tank of your vehicle as a "lunchbox." For over a century, we've been packing the same "sandwich" — crude oil refined into petrol/diesel. Alternative fuels are simply exploring the entire menu of the canteen — sandwiches (biofuels), salads (hydrogen), smoothies (electricity), and protein shakes (synthetic fuels) — to find options that are **cheaper, healthier for the planet, and locally sourced**.

The *Healthier* aspect refers to lower **CO₂ emissions per km** driven. The *locally sourced* aspect refers to energy security — a country can grow its own fuel (sugarcane → ethanol) instead of importing crude oil.

## 1.4 Standard Units and Metrics (To Remember)

- **Energy Density (MJ/kg)** – energy per unit mass.
- **Energy Density (MJ/L)** – energy per unit volume (critical for vehicles).
- **Cetane Number (CN)** – diesel knock resistance.
- **Octane Number (RON/MON)** – gasoline knock resistance.
- **Calorific Value (CV)** – **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** and **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** measured in **kJ/kg** or **MJ/kg**.
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** – mass of air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of fuel.
- **Auto-ignition Temperature (°C)** – minimum temperature for spontaneous combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Calorific Value of Petrol (reference)** ≈ **44 MJ/kg**
> - **Calorific Value of Diesel (reference)** ≈ **45.5 MJ/kg**
> - **Air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume** (mass basis: 23.2% O₂, 76.8% N₂).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Types of Alternative Fuels – Theory

### 2.1.1 First-Generation Biofuels
These are produced directly from food crops (sugar, starch, vegetable oil). They raise the **food vs. fuel** debate.

- **Bioethanol (C₂H₅OH):** Produced by fermentation of sugarcane, corn, or molasses. Blended with petrol (E10, E20, E85).
- **Biodiesel (FAME – Fatty Acid Methyl Esters):** Produced by **transesterification** of vegetable oils (soybean, palm, sunflower, Jatropha) or animal fats with methanol. Blended with diesel (B5, B20, B100).
- **Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂):** Produced by **anaerobic digestion** of organic waste (agricultural residue, animal dung, municipal solid waste).

### 2.1.2 Second-Generation Biofuels
Produced from non-food lignocellulosic biomass (wood, grass, agricultural waste). Avoid the food-fuel conflict.

- **Cellulosic Ethanol**
- **Bio-diesel from Jatropha/Algae (algae can also be 3rd generation)**
- **Biomass-to-Liquid (BTL)** – Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.

### 2.1.3 Third-Generation Biofuels
Derived from **algae**, which have very high photosynthetic efficiency and do not require arable land.

- **Algal biodiesel**
- **Bio-hydrogen**

### 2.1.4 Gaseous Alternative Fuels
- **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG):** Mostly **methane (CH₄)**, stored at **200–250 bar**.
- **Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG):** CH₄ cooled to **−162 °C** for storage.
- **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG):** Mixture of **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)**.
- **Hydrogen (H₂):** The ultimate clean fuel — produces only **H₂O** on combustion.
- **Biogas/Bio-methane.**
- **Dimethyl Ether (DME – CH₃OCH₃).**

### 2.1.5 Liquid Synthetic Fuels
- **Methanol (CH₃OH)** – can be produced from natural gas, coal, or biomass.
- **Ethanol** – as above.
- **Synthetic Diesel / Gasoline** – via **Fischer-Tropsch (FT)** process from syngas (CO + H₂).

### 2.1.6 Electricity as a "Fuel" (E-mobility)
- **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs):** Store electricity in Li-ion batteries.
- **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs):** Combine ICE + battery.
- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs):** Use **Hydrogen Fuel Cells (PEMFC)** to generate electricity on-board.

### 2.1.7 Solar and Wind (Indirect Alternative Fuels)
- **Photovoltaic (PV) electricity** – directly powers BEVs.
- **Solar fuels** – synthetic fuels produced using solar energy (e.g., **Solar-to-Hydrogen** via electrolysis).

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Type

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need alternatives to petrol/diesel?**
> - **Finite Reserves** of crude oil (estimated **50–60 years** left at current consumption).
> - **Tailpipe Emissions** cause smog, asthma, cancer.
> - **CO₂ Emissions** drive climate change.
> - **Import Bills** drain foreign exchange reserves (especially for India, which imports **>85%** of its crude oil).

> [!NOTE]
> **How are they produced?**
> - **Biochemical route:** Fermentation (sugar → ethanol), Anaerobic digestion (waste → biogas).
> - **Thermochemical route:** Pyrolysis, Gasification (biomass → syngas → FT diesel), Transesterification (oil → biodiesel).
> - **Electrolytic route:** Water electrolysis (renewable electricity → green H₂).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis) | dimensionless |
| $$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2O$$ | General hydrocarbon combustion | — |
| $$LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left(\frac{9H}{100}\right)$$ | Relation between LHV and HHV for hydrocarbon fuels | MJ/kg |
| $$C_{16}H_{32}O_2 + CH_3OH \xrightarrow{NaOH} 3\,C_{17}H_{34}O_2 + Glycerol$$ | Transesterification (simplified) | — |
| $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{Zymase} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$ | Fermentation of glucose to ethanol | — |
| $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$ | Otto cycle thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| $$Energy\,Density\,(vol) = \rho \cdot LHV$$ | Volumetric energy density | MJ/L |
| $$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{P \cdot V_{tank}}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$$ | Hydrogen mass in a compressed tank | kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick to Remember Stoichiometric AFR for Pure Hydrocarbons:**
> For a fuel **CₓHᵧO_z**, the mass of O₂ required is:
> $$m_{O_2} = 32\left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right) \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kmol fuel}$$
> Then: $$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{137.28 \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right)}{12x + y + 16z}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transport Sector:** Reducing **Particulate Matter (PM)** and **NOx** in cities (e.g., Delhi's CNG bus program).
- **Agriculture:** Jatropha cultivation in wasteland provides rural income + biodiesel.
- **Power Generation:** Biogas plants provide decentralized electricity in villages.
- **Aviation/Marine:** Synthetic jet fuels from FT synthesis for carbon-neutral flying.
- **Industrial Process Heat:** Hydrogen replacing coke in steel-making (green steel).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Connect:** India's **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP)** targets **20% ethanol blending (E20)** by **2025–26 (originally ESY 2030, advanced)**. **SATAT** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) scheme targets **15 MMTPA of compressed biogas** by 2023.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

Ethanol is a first-generation biofuel commonly blended with petrol. The derivation is a **KTU favourite**.

**Step 1:** Write the balanced combustion equation with air.

$$C_2H_5OH_{(l)} + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O_{(l)}$$

*Logic: One molecule of ethanol contains 2 C, 6 H, and 1 O atom. Combustion requires 2 C → 2 CO₂ (needs 4 O), 6 H → 3 H₂O (needs 3 O). Total O needed = 4 + 3 = 7. Ethanol itself supplies 1 O atom, so external O₂ needed = (7−1)/2 = 3 molecules.*

**Step 2:** Introduce nitrogen (since air is 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume → 1 mol O₂ comes with 3.76 mol N₂).

$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 + 3 \times 3.76\,N_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + 11.28\,N_2$$

**Step 3:** Compute molecular weights.

- Molecular weight of **C₂H₅OH** = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = **46 kg/kmol**
- Molecular weight of **O₂** = 32 kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of **N₂** = 28 kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of **air** (per mole O₂) = 32 + 3.76(28) = **137.28 kg**

**Step 4:** Compute the mass of air required.

$$m_{air} = 3 \times 137.28 = 411.84 \text{ kg air / kmol fuel}$$

**Step 5:** Compute stoichiometric AFR.

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{411.84}{46} = 8.95$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Stoichiometric AFR of ethanol = **8.95 : 1** (mass basis). Compare to petrol ≈ 14.7 and diesel ≈ 14.5. Ethanol requires much more air per kg of fuel due to its oxygen content.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LHV of Biogas (Methane-Dominant)

Assume biogas composition: **60% CH₄, 40% CO₂** (typical from anaerobic digestion of cattle dung).

**Step 1:** Write HHV of each component.

- HHV of **CH₄** = **55.5 MJ/kg**
- CO₂ is inert (does not combust), HV = **0 MJ/kg**

**Step 2:** Convert volumetric composition to mass composition.

Basis: 1 kmol of biogas = 0.6 kmol CH₄ + 0.4 kmol CO₂

- Mass of CH₄ = 0.6 × 16 = **9.6 kg**
- Mass of CO₂ = 0.4 × 44 = **17.6 kg**
- Total mass = 9.6 + 17.6 = **27.2 kg**

**Step 3:** Calculate mass-weighted HHV.

$$HHV_{biogas} = \frac{(0.6 \times 16 \times 55.5) + (0.4 \times 44 \times 0)}{27.2}$$

$$HHV_{biogas} = \frac{9.6 \times 55.5}{27.2} = \frac{532.8}{27.2} \approx 19.59 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4:** Convert HHV to LHV (assuming water leaves as vapour, h_fg of water at 25 °C = **2.44 MJ/kg**).

For methane, hydrogen mass fraction = 4/16 = 0.25. In our biogas, effective hydrogen mass fraction is reduced. Approximating LHV of biogas:

$$LHV_{biogas} \approx 0.9 \times HHV_{biogas} \approx 0.9 \times 19.59 \approx 17.6 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Biogas (60:40) has LHV ≈ **17.6 MJ/kg** — only about **40% of pure methane's** LHV. This is why **CO₂ scrubbing** is essential before using biogas as a vehicle fuel.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Density of Hydrogen (Compressed vs. Liquid)

Hydrogen has the **highest energy per unit mass (LHV ≈ 120 MJ/kg)** but the **lowest energy per unit volume** unless highly compressed or liquefied.

**Step 1:** Calculate energy density by mass (gravimetric).

$$LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Density of hydrogen gas at STP.

$$\rho_{H_2, STP} = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.0899 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 3:** Energy density by volume at STP.

$$E_{vol, STP} = 120 \times 0.0899 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0108 \text{ MJ/L} = 10.8 \text{ kJ/L}$$

**Step 4:** Compress to 700 bar (typical FCEV tank).

At 700 bar and ambient temperature, hydrogen density ≈ **40 kg/m³ = 0.04 kg/L**.

$$E_{vol, 700\,bar} = 120 \times 0.04 = 4.8 \text{ MJ/L}$$

**Step 5:** Compare to petrol.

$$E_{vol, petrol} = 34 \text{ MJ/L} \text{ (approx)}$$

$$Ratio = \frac{4.8}{34} \approx 0.141$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Even at 700 bar, hydrogen occupies **~7× the volume** of petrol for the same energy. This is the **#1 challenge** for hydrogen vehicles — storage volume, not weight.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of Major Alternative Fuels

| **Fuel** | **Formula** | **LHV (MJ/kg)** | **Density (kg/L)** | **Octane/Cetane** | **AFR_stoich** | **Renewable?** | **Feedstock** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Petrol (baseline) | C₄–C₁₂ mix | 44.0 | 0.74 | 92 RON | 14.7 | ❌ | Crude oil |
| Diesel (baseline) | C₁₂–C₂₀ mix | 45.5 | 0.84 | 50 CN | 14.5 | ❌ | Crude oil |
| Bioethanol | C₂H₅OH | 26.9 | 0.789 | 110 RON | 8.95 | ✅ | Sugarcane, corn |
| Biodiesel (FAME) | C₁₇H₃₄O₂ | 37.2 | 0.88 | 50–60 CN | 12.5 | ✅ | Vegetable oil, algae |
| Biogas (60% CH₄) | CH₄+CO₂ | 17.6 | 1.15 (g/L) | 130 RON | ~10 | ✅ | Organic waste |
| CNG (Methane) | CH₄ | 50.0 | 0.20 (g/L) | 120 RON | 17.2 | ❌ (fossil) | Natural gas |
| Hydrogen | H₂ | 120.0 | 0.04 (g/L) @ 700 bar | 130+ RON | 34.3 | ✅ (if green) | Water electrolysis |
| LPG | C₃H₈/C₄H₁₀ | 46.0 | 0.51 | 111 RON | 15.6 | ❌ (fossil) | Refinery, NG |
| Methanol | CH₃OH | 20.0 | 0.792 | 108 RON | 6.4 | ✅/❌ | NG, coal, biomass |
| DME | CH₃OCH₃ | 28.9 | 0.67 (liq) | 55–60 CN | 9.0 | ✅/❌ | NG, coal, biomass |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Octane Number** with **Cetane Number**. Petrol engines need HIGH octane (resists knock); diesel engines need HIGH cetane (easy to ignite). Ethanol has **high octane** but is unsuitable for diesels without modification.
