# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **List and briefly explain any three types of first-generation biofuels.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Bioethanol** – Produced by **fermentation of sugar/starch** (sugarcane, corn, molasses) using yeast. Used as a petrol-blending fuel (E10, E20, E85). Has high octane number (~110 RON) but low energy density.
2. **Biodiesel (FAME)** – Produced by **transesterification** of vegetable oils (soybean, palm, Jatropha) or animal fats with methanol in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH/KOH). Used as a diesel blend (B5, B20). Cetane number ~50–60.
3. **Biogas** – Produced by **anaerobic digestion** of organic waste (cattle dung, agricultural residue, MSW) in the absence of oxygen. Composition: **55–70% CH₄ + 30–45% CO₂**. Used for cooking, power generation, and as a vehicle fuel (after upgrading to bio-methane).

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> **Differentiate between first, second, and third-generation biofuels with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **1st Gen** | **2nd Gen** | **3rd Gen** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Sugar, starch, edible oil | Lignocellulose, waste biomass | Algae, aquatic biomass |
| **Examples** | Sugarcane ethanol, soybean biodiesel | Cellulosic ethanol, BTL diesel | Algal biodiesel, bio-hydrogen |
| **Food vs Fuel** | Yes, conflict | No conflict | No conflict |
| **Land requirement** | High (arable) | Moderate (wasteland) | Minimal (ponds) |
| **Maturity** | Commercial | Pilot | R&D |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High | Very high |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Classify alternative fuels in detail. Discuss the production, properties, advantages, and limitations of (a) bioethanol and (b) biodiesel.**

#### Part (a) – Bioethanol (7 Marks)

**1. Classification (2 marks):**
Bioethanol (C₂H₅OH) is a **first-generation liquid biofuel** derived from the **fermentation of sugars and starches**. It belongs to the **alcohol** family of alternative fuels.

**2. Production Process (2 marks):**
The process involves three stages:
- **Feedstock preparation:** Sugarcane juice, molasses, or starchy grains (corn, wheat) are crushed and hydrolyzed to release fermentable sugars (glucose, sucrose).
- **Fermentation:** Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) converts sugars to ethanol and CO₂ under anaerobic conditions at ~30 °C:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$
- **Distillation & Dehydration:** The dilute ethanol (~10% v/v) is distilled to ~95% (azeotrope) and then dehydrated using **molecular sieves** to get anhydrous ethanol (>99.5%) for blending with petrol.

**3. Properties (1.5 marks):**
- LHV = **26.9 MJ/kg** (≈ 60% of petrol)
- Octane Number = **110–115 RON** (excellent knock resistance)
- Stoichiometric AFR = **8.95 : 1**
- Oxygen content = **35%** by mass (reduces CO and PM emissions)
- Miscible with petrol in all proportions
- Hygroscopic (absorbs water → phase separation issues)

**4. Advantages (1 mark):**
- Domestic production reduces oil import bills.
- Octane booster → improved engine efficiency.
- 30–50% reduction in **CO, HC, and PM** tailpipe emissions.
- Renewable feedstock (India's EBP targets E20 by 2025–26).

**5. Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Lower energy density → reduced fuel economy (~30% lower km/litre).
- Corrosive to rubber and certain plastics in older fuel systems.
- Food vs fuel debate (1st generation).
- Requires modified engine for high blends (E85 needs FFV).

#### Part (b) – Biodiesel (7 Marks)

**1. Classification (1.5 marks):**
Biodiesel, chemically known as **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)**, is a **first-generation liquid biofuel** derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oil. It is a **diesel-substitute** fuel.

**2. Production Process – Transesterification (2.5 marks):**
The vegetable oil (triglyceride) reacts with **methanol in the presence of NaOH/KOH catalyst** at 60–70 °C to produce FAME and glycerol.

$$Triglyceride + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightleftharpoons{NaOH} 3\,FAME + Glycerol$$

**Simplified chemical equation:**

$$C_{16}H_{32}O_2 + CH_3OH \xrightarrow{NaOH} C_{17}H_{34}O_2 + H_2O$$

Steps:
- **Feedstock pretreatment:** Degumming, deacidification.
- **Reaction:** Oil + methanol + catalyst (1% wt NaOH) at 60 °C for 1–2 hours.
- **Separation:** Glycerol (bottom layer) is drained; FAME is washed and dried.
- **Purification:** Removal of residual methanol, catalyst, soap.

**3. Properties (1.5 marks):**
- LHV = **37.2 MJ/kg** (~ 82% of diesel)
- Cetane Number = **50–60** (similar to diesel)
- Density = **0.88 kg/L**
- Flash point = **> 130 °C** (safer to handle than diesel)
- Oxygen content ≈ **11%** by mass
- Viscosity higher than diesel (may require preheating in cold climates).

**4. Advantages (1 mark):**
- Renewable, biodegradable, non-toxic.
- ~50–60% reduction in **PM, CO, and HC** emissions.
- Higher cetane → smoother combustion.
- Can be used in existing diesel engines with **little or no modification** (up to B20).
- Lubricity improver → reduces engine wear.

**5. Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Higher NOₓ emissions (~10% increase).
- Cold-flow issues (cloud point, pour point problems in winter).
- Slightly higher viscosity → injector fouling long term.
- Solvent action on rubber seals (must use Viton/FKM).

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**Discuss the types of gaseous alternative fuels. Explain in detail the (a) production, storage, and applications of hydrogen as an alternative fuel, and (b) compare CNG, LNG, and LPG as vehicle fuels.**

#### Part (a) – Hydrogen (7 Marks)

**1. Why Hydrogen? (1 mark):**
- Zero-carbon fuel: combustion product is **H₂O only**.
- Highest energy-to-mass ratio: **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** (≈ 2.7× petrol).
- Can be produced from water using renewable electricity → **truly sustainable**.

**2. Production Methods (3 marks):**
- **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):** CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂ — produces **grey hydrogen** (CO₂ released).
- **Electrolysis:** 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ — using renewable electricity produces **green hydrogen** (zero CO₂).
- **Partial Oxidation, Biomass Gasification, Photoelectrochemical** — emerging methods.
- **Colour codes:** Grey (fossil), Blue (fossil + CCS), Green (renewable), Pink (nuclear).

**3. Storage (2 marks):**
- **Compressed gas (CGH₂):** at 350 bar or 700 bar in composite tanks.
- **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂):** cryogenic storage at **−253 °C**.
- **Metal hydrides / LOHC (Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers):** chemical storage in toluene/methylcyclohexane.
- **Solid-state storage** in carbon nanotubes, MOFs — under research.

**4. Applications (1 mark):**
- **FCEVs** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) — fuel cells generate electricity on-board.
- Industrial: ammonia synthesis, petroleum refining, methanol production.
- Rocket propellant (liquid H₂ + LOX).
- Stationary fuel cells for power generation.

#### Part (b) – Comparison of CNG, LNG, LPG (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Property** | **CNG** | **LNG** | **LPG** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Main component** | CH₄ (>90%) | CH₄ (>90%) | C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀ |
| **State (storage)** | Gas (high pressure) | Cryogenic liquid | Liquid (moderate pressure) |
| **Storage pressure** | **200–250 bar** | Atmospheric (−162 °C) | ~7 bar |
| **Storage temp** | Ambient | −162 °C | Ambient |
| **LHV (MJ/kg)** | 50 | 50 | 46 |
| **Density (kg/L)** | 0.20 (at 200 bar) | 0.41 | 0.51 |
| **Energy density (MJ/L)** | ~10 | ~22 | ~25 |
| **Octane number** | 120 | 120 | 111 |
| **Range in vehicle** | 250–300 km | 600+ km (heavy vehicles) | 350–400 km |
| **Refuelling time** | 5–10 min | 5–10 min | 5 min |
| **Infrastructure** | Compressors expensive | Cryogenic tanks expensive | Cylinders widely available |
| **Use case** | City buses, cars | Heavy trucks, ships | Domestic + light vehicles |

**Advantages & Limitations (3 marks):**
- **CNG:** Cheaper than petrol/diesel; lower emissions; but bulky tanks reduce boot space; limited range.
- **LNG:** Higher energy density → suited for long-haul trucks, marine; expensive cryogenic infrastructure.
- **LPG:** Convenient domestic availability; moderate cost; but fossil-derived and limited renewability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "CNG is renewable" → **WRONG.** CNG is **fossil-derived** (natural gas). Only **biogas upgraded to bio-CNG** is renewable.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **LNG with LPG.** LNG is **pure methane** at −162 °C; LPG is **propane+butane** at room temperature under low pressure.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the **stoichiometric AFR** in derivation questions. Always show the molecular weight of air (137.28) explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "biodiesel = vegetable oil" → **WRONG.** Biodiesel is **FAME**, the *methyl ester* of vegetable oil, produced via transesterification. Raw vegetable oil is **NOT biodiesel**.
> - **Mark Loss Zones:** Missing units in formulas (−1 mark); not balancing chemical equations (−1 mark); not mentioning catalyst in transesterification (−0.5 mark); confusing octane and cetane (−1 mark).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Types of Alternative Fuels" (Module 1)**

### 🎯 Core Classifications
- **First-gen biofuels:** Food crops → ethanol, biodiesel, biogas.
- **Second-gen biofuels:** Lignocellulosic waste → cellulosic ethanol, BTL.
- **Third-gen biofuels:** Algae → algal biodiesel, bio-H₂.
- **Gaseous:** CNG (CH₄, 200 bar), LNG (CH₄, −162 °C), LPG (C₃+C₄), H₂.
- **Liquid synthetic:** Methanol, DME, FT diesel.
- **Electric:** BEV, PHEV, FCEV.

### 🔑 Critical Numbers to Memorize
- LHV of H₂ = **120 MJ/kg** (highest of any fuel).
- LHV of CNG = **50 MJ/kg**; Petrol = **44 MJ/kg**; Diesel = **45.5 MJ/kg**.
- Stoichiometric AFR: Petrol ≈ **14.7**; Diesel ≈ **14.5**; Ethanol ≈ **8.95**; H₂ ≈ **34.3**.
- Molecular weight of air per mole O₂ = **137.28 kg**.
- N₂/O₂ molar ratio in air = **3.76**.

### ⚗️ Chemical Equations (Must Memorize)
- **Combustion of methane:** CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O.
- **Combustion of ethanol:** C₂H₅OH + 3O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O.
- **Combustion of hydrogen:** 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O.
- **Fermentation:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂.
- **Transesterification:** Triglyceride + 3CH₃OH → 3FAME + Glycerol (with NaOH catalyst).

### 🚗 Vehicle-Application Map
- **Petrol engines (SI):** Petrol, Ethanol (E10–E100), LPG, CNG (with kit), H₂ (with kit), Methanol.
- **Diesel engines (CI):** Diesel, Biodiesel (B5–B100), DME, FT diesel, H₂ (with dual fuel).
- **Electric drivetrain:** BEV (battery), FCEV (H₂ fuel cell), PHEV (both).

### 🌱 Renewable vs Non-Renewable
- **Renewable:** Bioethanol, biodiesel, biogas, green H₂, electricity from solar/wind, algal fuel.
- **Non-renewable (fossil-derived):** CNG, LNG, LPG, grey/blue H₂, methanol/DME from coal/NG.

### 🇮🇳 India-Specific Points
- **EBP (Ethanol Blending Programme):** E20 by 2025–26.
- **SATAT:** Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation — 15 MMTPA CBG target.
- **National Green Hydrogen Mission:** 5 MMTPA green H₂ by 2030.
- **India's crude oil import dependence:** > **85%**.

### ❌ Common Pitfalls (Re-read Before Exam)
- Don't confuse **LNG vs LPG** (temperature/pressure differences).
- Don't call **CNG renewable** — only **bio-CNG** is.
- **Biodiesel ≠ vegetable oil** — biodiesel is **FAME**.
- **Hydrogen's challenge is volumetric energy density**, not gravimetric.
- **Octane for SI engines, Cetane for CI engines** — never mix them up.
- **DME has high cetane (55–60)** — good diesel substitute despite being a gas at STP.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "B-C-L-H-E" to remember the 5 main categories of Alternative Fuels:**
> **B**iofuels → **C**NG/LNG/LPG → **L**iquid synthetic (methanol, DME) → **H**ydrogen → **E**lectricity (BEV/FCEV).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commonly Used Additives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Commonly Used Additives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Additives (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *fuel additive* is a chemical compound, usually in small concentrations (typically **< 1 % by mass**, occasionally up to **5–10 %** for oxygenates), which is intentionally blended with a base fuel (petroleum diesel, gasoline, biodiesel, ethanol, methanol, CNG, LPG, or hydrogen) to **enhance specific physico-chemical properties**, **improve combustion efficiency**, **reduce emissions**, **improve storage/oxidation stability**, **mitigate cold-flow limitations**, or **restore lubricity lost during desulfurization**, without materially altering the bulk energy density of the host fuel.

In the KTU 2024 context of *Alternative Fuels and Energy Systems*, additives are broadly classified as **performance improvers**, **stability improvers**, **handling improvers**, and **emission-control additives**, and they are often the practical enablers that allow neat alternative fuels (e.g., pure biodiesel, neat ethanol, FAME-rich blends) to be deployed in engines that were originally designed for conventional petroleum fuels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Vitamins for a Fuel":** Think of fuel additives the way a doctor prescribes **vitamins or supplements to a human body**. The body can technically survive without them, but the supplement *optimises performance* — strengthens immunity, improves endurance, prevents rust (oxidation), and helps the body survive cold weather. A pure alternative fuel like **neat biodiesel (B100)** is "healthy" but "lacks certain micronutrients": it is prone to **oxidation, cold-flow blockage, injector deposits, and poor low-temperature operability**. Adding **BHT, TBHQ, polymeric PPDs, and lubricity improvers** is the engineering equivalent of giving the fuel a *multivitamin + Omega-3 capsule* so it runs cleanly in a Maruti or Tata diesel engine without choking in the Kerala winter or clogging the fuel filter.

### 1.3 Why Additives Are Indispensable in Alternative Fuels

| # | Problem in Neat Alternative Fuel | Role of Additive |
|---|---------------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Biodiesel** oxidises rapidly → peroxides, acids, gums | **Antioxidants** (BHT, BHA, TBHQ, PY) |
| 2 | Biodiesel has poor **cold-flow properties** (high CP, PP, CFPP) | **Cold Flow Improvers / PPDs** |
| 3 | Biodiesel has lower **cetane number** in some feedstocks | **Cetane Improvers** (2-EHN) |
| 4 | Low-sulfur ULSD & biodiesel lose **lubricity** | **Lubricity Improvers** (fatty acids, esters) |
| 5 | Ethanol is **corrosive** to Al, Zn, Mg | **Corrosion Inhibitors** |
| 6 | Ethanol absorbs **water** → phase separation | **Co-solvents / Emulsifiers** |
| 7 | Methanol & ethanol lower **vapour pressure blends** | **Volatility Modifiers** |
| 8 | CNG/LPG need **leak detection** | **Odorants** (THT, mercaptans) |
| 9 | Hydrogen causes **embrittlement** of steel | **Anti-embrittlement inhibitors** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Dosage Metric (KTU 2024):** Additives are reported in three ways — (a) **mass %** (mg/kg or ppm), (b) **volume %** (mL/L), and (c) **treat rate** (e.g., **1 lb/1000 bbl ≈ 2.85 g/L ≈ 2850 ppm** for petroleum additives). The conversion is critical for KTU numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Broad Classification of Additives (KTU 2024 Scheme)

1. **Performance Improvers**
   - **Cetane Improvers** — reduce ignition delay in CI engines
     - *Examples:* 2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate (2-EHN), di-tert-butyl peroxide, amyl nitrate
   - **Octane Improvers / Anti-knock additives** — increase RON/MON
     - *Examples:* MTBE, ETBE, TAME, MMT (Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl)
   - **Lubricity Improvers** — restore HFRR wear scar
     - *Examples:* oleic acid, fatty acid esters, dimer acid
2. **Stability Improvers**
   - **Antioxidants** — primary (radical scavengers: BHT, BHA, TBHQ, PG, PY) and secondary (peroxide decomposers: phosphites, thioesters)
   - **Metal Deactivators** — chelate trace Fe, Cu, Mn
3. **Cold-Flow Improvers (CFPP/PPD)**
   - *Examples:* ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA) copolymers, polyalkyl methacrylates (PAMA), styrene–maleic anhydride copolymers
4. **Handling / Storage Improvers**
   - **Anti-foam agents** (silicone polymers), **demulsifiers** (block copolymers), **anti-icing agents** (alcohols, glycols)
5. **Emission-Control Additives**
   - **Oxygenates** (ethanol, methanol, MTBE) for HC/CO reduction
   - **Particulate suppressors** (ferrocene — though controversial)
6. **Fuel-Specific Additives for Alternative Fuels**
   - **Denaturants** for ethanol (methanol, MEK, gasoline)
   - **Odorants** for CNG/LPG (tetrahydrothiophene – THT, ethyl mercaptan)
   - **Anti-embrittlement** for hydrogen (CO, H₂S, O₂ in ppm levels)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Mechanism — Why an Antioxidant Works

Step 1: Biodiesel contains **unsaturated fatty acid chains** (C=C double bonds) — e.g., linoleic acid (C18:2), linolenic (C18:3).
Step 2: In the presence of **dissolved O₂, heat, light, and trace metals (Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺)**, a **hydrogen atom** is abstracted from the allylic position, forming a **carbon-centred radical R•**.
Step 3: R• + O₂ → **ROO•** (peroxy radical) — the **propagation** step.
Step 4: ROO• + RH → **ROOH + R•** — chain-branching leads to aldehydes, acids, polymers, gums.
Step 5: **Primary antioxidant (BHT)** donates an H•: ROO• + ArOH → **ROOH + ArO•** (stable resonance-stabilised radical that does not propagate the chain).
Step 6: The chain is **terminated**, oxidation induction period (Rancimat) is extended from ~3 h (neat biodiesel) to > 8 h (EN 14112 / EN 15751 limit for B100).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application / Boundary Condition |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | $CN_{blend} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot CN_i$ | $x_i$ = volume/mass fraction; $CN_i$ = cetane number of component *i* | Linear blending rule for cetane number; valid when $CN_i$ differ by < 15 units |
| 2 | $ON_{blend} \;=\; \sum_{i} x_i \cdot ON_i$ | $ON$ = octane number (RON/MON) | Linear approximation for RON blending |
| 3 | $BI_{blend} \;=\; \dfrac{ \sum_i x_i \cdot CN_i - CN_{base} }{ CN_{additive} - CN_{base} } \cdot 100$ | $BI$ = Blend Index / Boost Index (%) | Used to compute cetane improvement from additive dose |
| 4 | $D_{add} \;=\; \dfrac{ m_{add} }{ m_{fuel} } \times 10^{6}$ | $D_{add}$ in **ppm (mg/kg)** | Standard KTU dosage metric |
| 5 | $1\;\text{lb/1000 bbl} \;\approx\; 2.853\;\text{g/L} \;\approx\; 2853\;\text{ppm}$ | Universal additive dose conversion | Treat rate for petroleum additives |
| 6 | $\text{ASTM D1500 (Colour)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Residue Index}$ | Visual colour scale 0.5 – 8.0 | Detects oxidation in biodiesel |
| 7 | $HFRR_{WS1.4} \;<\; 520\;\mu m$ (EN 590), $<\; 460\;\mu m$ (EN 14214) | High-Frequency Reciprocating Rig wear scar diameter, µm | Lubricity standard — KTU important |
| 8 | $CFPP \;=\; T_{filter\,plugging}$ | Cold Filter Plugging Point (°C) | EN 116 method — KTU standard |
| 9 | $\rho_{blend} \;=\; \sum_{i} x_i \rho_i$ | Density (kg/m³) | Mass/volume base matters in KTU problems |
| 10 | $\eta_{blend} \;\approx\; \exp\!\left( \sum_{i} x_i \ln \eta_i \right)$ | Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) | Grunberg–Nissan logarithmic mixing rule |
| 11 | $R_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; 2.31 \cdot \left[1 + 1.5\left(\dfrac{x_{O_2}}{100}\right) - 0.01\left(T - 298\right)\right]$ | Octane sensitivity approximation | Used when RON is blended with oxygenates |
| 12 | $\text{IP} \;>\; 8\;\text{h}$ (Rancimat, EN 14112) | Induction period (h) | B100 oxidation stability limit — KTU mandatory |
| 13 | $T_{flash}^{blend} \;\approx\; \sum_{i} x_i T_{flash,i}$ | Flash point (°C, K, closed cup) | Linear (only as a first estimate) |
| 14 | $\text{Bottom line threshold: dose} \;\le\; 1\;\text{wt\%}$ | Treat rate for most additives | Universal KTU engineering rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** When a question asks for additive blending in **biodiesel-diesel blends**, always check whether volume % or mass % is specified. **Mass fraction** is used in cetane blending equations, while **volume fraction** is used for density and flash point linear rules.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Maruti / Tata diesel engines running on B20** depend on antioxidant dosing to pass the 8-hour Rancimat limit.
- **Kerala cold-storage trucks** rely on CFPP depressants during the *December-January* cold spell when neat biodiesel gels.
- **Piped CNG (PCNG) in Kochi/Bengaluru** uses THT odorisation at **5–25 mg/m³ of gas** for leak detection.
- **E20 petrol launch in India (2025–2026)** requires **corrosion inhibitors and demulsifiers** to protect millions of legacy vehicles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are in the **linear-blending regime** valid for dilute additives and ideal solutions, as is standard at KTU board level. Non-linear corrections (e.g., RON blending with non-linearity index) are out of scope for Module 1.

### 3.1 Derivation A — Cetane Number of a Biodiesel–Diesel Blend

**Given:** A blend of **20 % v/v biodiesel (CN = 55)** in **80 % v/v petroleum diesel (CN = 50)**. An additive **2-EHN** is dosed at **0.3 % mass** with a measured cetane number contribution of **+6 units** to the base blend.

**Find:** Effective cetane number of the final doped fuel.

**Step 1 — Convert volume % to mass fractions** using densities $\rho_{bio} = 880$ kg/m³ and $\rho_{diesel} = 840$ kg/m³.

$$m_{bio} = 0.20 \times 880 = 176\;\text{kg}$$

$$m_{diesel} = 0.80 \times 840 = 672\;\text{kg}$$

$$m_{total,\,fuel\,base} = 176 + 672 = 848\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass fractions of the fuel base (excluding additive, since additive dose is < 0.5 %):**

$$x_{bio} = \dfrac{176}{848} = 0.2075$$

$$x_{diesel} = \dfrac{672}{848} = 0.7925$$

**Step 3 — Apply linear cetane blending:**

$$CN_{blend,\,base} = (0.2075)(55) + (0.7925)(50)$$

$$CN_{blend,\,base} = 11.41 + 39.63 = 51.04$$

**Step 4 — Add the cetane boost from 2-EHN (linear additive on CN):**

$$CN_{final} = CN_{blend,\,base} + \Delta CN_{additive}$$

$$CN_{final} = 51.04 + 6 = 57.04$$

**Result:** $\boxed{CN_{final} \approx 57.0}$ — comfortably above the **EN 590 minimum of 51** for European diesel and the **BIS IS 1460:2017 limit of 48** for Indian HSD.

**Reasoning check (Why this is acceptable):** A 2-EHN dose of 0.3 % mass corresponds to **~3 mL/L**, which in real engines extends ignition delay shortening by **~10–15 %** of the base value, equivalent to a CN boost of 5–8 units — consistent with our $\Delta CN = 6$ assumption.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Additive Dosage in ppm from Treat Rate

**Given:** A petroleum refiner specifies a cetane improver treat rate of **3.0 lb / 1000 bbl**. Find the dosage in **g/L** and in **ppm (mg/kg)**. Assume $\rho_{fuel} = 850$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert lb to grams:**

$$1\;\text{lb} = 453.592\;\text{g}$$

$$3.0\;\text{lb} = 1360.78\;\text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Convert 1000 bbl to litres:**

$$1\;\text{bbl} = 158.987\;\text{L}$$

$$1000\;\text{bbl} = 158{,}987\;\text{L}$$

**Step 3 — Compute concentration in g/L:**

$$C = \dfrac{1360.78\;\text{g}}{158{,}987\;\text{L}} = 0.008558\;\text{g/L} = 8.56\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mg/kg (ppm) using fuel density:**

$$ppm = \dfrac{C\,[\text{mg/L}] \times 1000}{\rho\,[\text{kg/m}^3]}$$

$$ppm = \dfrac{8.558 \times 1000}{850} = 10.07\;\text{mg/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-verify using the universal rule:**

$$1\;\text{lb/1000 bbl} \approx 2853\;\text{ppm}$$

$$\therefore 3\;\text{lb/1000 bbl} \approx 8559\;\text{ppm}$$

Wait — this is **3 orders of magnitude higher** than our Step 4 result. The reason is the *petroleum-industry convention* treats 1 lb/1000 bbl as a per-bbl measure where the bbl is **42 US gal of oil** but the additive mass is per **1 bbl** — the *petroleum barrel* of 158.987 L. The conversion factor 2853 ppm corresponds to a **density-relative-to-water** basis ($\rho_{oil} \approx 1000$ kg/m³), not the actual $\rho_{fuel} = 850$ kg/m³.

**Correct universal conversion (for a generic fuel):**

$$1\;\text{lb/1000 bbl} = \dfrac{453.592}{158.987 \times 10^{-3}}\;\text{mg/L} \approx 2853\;\text{mg/L}$$

In ppm (mg/kg) using $\rho = 850$ kg/m³:

$$ppm = \dfrac{2853 \times 1000}{850} \approx 3356\;\text{ppm/lb/1000 bbl}$$

So **3 lb/1000 bbl ≈ 10,068 ppm** — which agrees with the direct step-by-step. The earlier "2853 ppm" figure is the *volume* concentration in mg/L, NOT mass ppm. The KTU examiner will award full marks only if you clearly distinguish the two.

**Final boxed answer:** $\boxed{3\;\text{lb/1000 bbl} \approx 0.00856\;\text{g/L} \approx 3356\;\text{ppm (for }\rho_{fuel}=850\;\text{kg/m}^3\text{)}}$

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Cold-Flow Improvement of B100 with EVA Copolymer

**Given:** A soybean-biodiesel (B100) has CFPP = **+4 °C**. On adding **0.05 % mass of an EVA-based PPD**, the CFPP drops to **−6 °C**.

**Find:**
(a) $\Delta T_{CFPP}$ = CFPP depression
(b) Percentage improvement in cold-flow operability
(c) Estimated CFPP at 0.10 % dose (assuming linear response in dilute regime)

**Step 1 — Absolute depression:**

$$\Delta T_{CFPP} = T_{CFPP,\,neat} - T_{CFPP,\,doped} = 4 - (-6) = 10\;\text{°C}$$

**Step 2 — Relative improvement (operability temperature range):**

$$\%\;\text{improvement} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{CFPP}}{T_{CFPP,\,neat}} \times 100 = \dfrac{10}{4+273.15} \times 100 \approx 3.6\;\text{% (K basis)}$$

$$\text{Or, in °C basis: } \dfrac{10}{4} \times 100 = 250\;\text{%}$$

(The KTU-accepted metric is the **absolute ΔT = 10 °C**, since relative % is meaningless in °C; use Kelvin only for relative %.)

**Step 3 — Linear extrapolation to 0.10 % dose:**

$$T_{CFPP}\,(0.10\%) \approx -6 - 10 = -16\;\text{°C}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\Delta T_{CFPP} = 10\;\text{°C};\; T_{CFPP}(0.10\%\,\text{PPD}) \approx -16\;\text{°C}}$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property Changes Induced by Additives

| Additive Class | Property Improved | Typical Dose | Δ Magnitude | Test Method |
|----------------|-------------------|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| 2-EHN (Cetane) | CN, ignition delay | 0.05 – 0.3 % mass | +3 to +8 CN | ASTM D613, IP 41 |
| TBHQ (Antioxidant) | Oxidation stability | 200 – 1000 ppm | IP: 3 h → 10 h | EN 14112 (Rancimat) |
| EVA / PAMA (PPD) | CFPP, PP | 100 – 1000 ppm | −5 to −20 °C | EN 116, ASTM D2500 |
| Oleic acid (Lubricity) | HFRR wear scar | 50 – 200 ppm | 700 → 200 µm | EN ISO 12156-1 |
| MTBE (Octane booster) | RON | 5 – 15 % vol | +6 to +10 RON | ASTM D2699 |
| THT (Odorant for CNG/LPG) | Leak detectability | 5 – 25 mg/m³ | 0 → detectable | EN 16726 |
| Corrosion inhibitor (ethanol) | Cu/Al corrosion | 50 – 250 ppm | 1b → 1a | ASTM D130 |
| Metal deactivator (salicylidene) | Cu-catalysed oxidation | 5 – 50 ppm | stabilises | ASTM D4048 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Tree of Fuel Additives (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fuel Additives for Alternative Fuels] --> B[Performance Improvers]
    A --> C[Stability Improvers]
    A --> D[Cold Flow Improvers]
    A --> E[Handling Improvers]
    A --> F[Emission Control Additives]
    A --> G[Fuel Specific Additives]

    B --> B1[Cetane Improvers 2-EHN Amyl Nitrate]
    B --> B2[Octane Boosters MTBE ETBE MMT]
    B --> B3[Lubricity Improvers Oleic Acid Fatty Esters]

    C --> C1[Primary Antioxidants BHT BHA TBHQ PY]
    C --> C2[Secondary Antioxidants Phosphites Thioesters]
    C --> C3[Metal Deactivators Salicylidene EDTA]

    D --> D1[EVA Copolymers]
    D --> D2[Polyalkyl Methacrylates PAMA]
    D --> D3[Styrene Maleic Anhydride]

    E --> E1[Anti Foam Silicones]
    E --> E2[Demulsifiers Block Copolymers]
    E --> E3[Anti Icing Alcohols Glycols]

    F --> F1[Oxygenates Ethanol Methanol]
    F --> F2[Particulate Suppressors Ferrocene]

    G --> G1[Denaturants for Ethanol]
    G --> G2[Odorants for CNG LPG THT Mercaptan]
    G --> G3[Anti Embrittlement for H2 CO ppm levels]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — How an Antioxidant Functions Inside a Biodiesel Storage Tank

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Neat Biodiesel B100] --> B[Dissolved O2 Heat Light Trace Metals]
    B --> C[H Abstraction from Allylic C-H]
    C --> D[R Radical formed]
    D --> E[O2 attacks R radical]
    E --> F[ROO Peroxy Radical]
    F --> G[Chain Propagation ROOH Acids Aldehydes Gums]
    G --> H[Quality Failure EN 14112]

    I[Antioxidant TBHQ BHT] --> J[H Donation to ROO radical]
    J --> K[Stable ArO Radical formed]
    K --> L[Chain Terminated]
    L --> M[Induction Period IP > 8 h]
    M --> N[Passes EN 14112 / EN 15751]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Integrated Additive Package for B20 Diesel Blend

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph BaseFuels
        B1[Petroleum Diesel 80 percent]
        B2[Biodiesel B100 20 percent]
    end

    B1 --> Mix[Blending Tank with Stirrer]
    B2 --> Mix

    Mix --> AD1[Antioxidant TBHQ 500 ppm]
    Mix --> AD2[Cetane Improver 2 EHN 0.2 percent]
    Mix --> AD3[PPD EVA Copolymer 250 ppm]
    Mix --> AD4[Lubricity Improver Oleic Acid 100 ppm]

    AD1 --> P[Final B20 Blend]
    AD2 --> P
    AD3 --> P
    AD4 --> P

    P --> Q1[CN > 51 EN 590]
    P --> Q2[IP > 8 h Rancimat]
    P --> Q3[CFPP less than minus 5 deg C]
    P --> Q4[HFRR less than 460 micrometre]

    classDef good fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef add fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

    class B1,B2 base
    class AD1,AD2,AD3,AD4 add
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 good
```

### 4.4 Decision Logic — Choosing the Right Additive for an Alternative Fuel

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start Fuel Selection] --> S1{What is the host fuel}
    S1 -->|Biodiesel| S2{Problem}
    S1 -->|Ethanol| E1{Problem}
    S1 -->|CNG or LPG| G1{Problem}
    S1 -->|Hydrogen| H1{Problem}

    S2 --> S2A[Oxidation] --> A1[Antioxidant TBHQ BHT]
    S2 --> S2B[Cold Flow] --> A2[PPD EVA PAMA]
    S2 --> S2C[Low CN] --> A3[2 EHN]
    S2 --> S2D[Low Lubricity] --> A4[Oleic Acid]

    E1 --> E1A[Corrosion] --> B1[Corrosion Inhibitor]
    E1 --> E1B[Phase Separation] --> B2[Co solvent C4 to C5 alcohol]
    E1 --> E1C[Vapor Lock] --> B3[Higher Boiling Cosolvent]

    G1 --> G1A[Leak Detection] --> C1[THT or Ethyl Mercaptan]
    G1 --> G1B[Compressor Wear] --> C2[Compressor Oil Additive]

    H1 --> H1A[Embrittlement] --> D1[O2 or CO ppm level Inhibitor]
    H1 --> H1B[Static Discharge] --> D2[Anti static Stadis 450]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define a fuel additive. Why are additives particularly important for biodiesel (FAME) compared to petroleum diesel? [3 marks]**
**Model Answer:**
A fuel additive is a chemical compound blended in small concentration (typically < 1 % mass, occasionally up to 5–10 %) with a base fuel to enhance specific properties such as cetane number, oxidation stability, cold-flow behaviour, or lubricity without materially altering the bulk energy content.
Biodiesel (FAME) is *particularly dependent* on additives because:
(i) its unsaturated fatty acid chains (C18:2, C18:3) make it highly prone to **oxidative degradation** → needs antioxidants (BHT, TBHQ);
(ii) it has **poor cold-flow properties** (high CFPP, high pour point) → needs PPDs (EVA, PAMA);
(iii) modern ultra-low-sulfur diesel (ULSD) and biodiesel both exhibit **low lubricity** → needs fatty-acid-based lubricity improvers.
Petroleum diesel, being more saturated and naturally lubricious, requires fewer additives.

---

**Q2. List any three antioxidants commonly used in biodiesel with their typical dosage range. [3 marks]**
**Model Answer:**
The three most common primary antioxidants for biodiesel (FAME) are:
1. **Butylated Hydroxytoluene (BHT)** — typical dose **200 – 1000 ppm**, low cost, moderate performance.
2. **Tert-Butyl Hydroquinone (TBHQ)** — typical dose **250 – 1000 ppm**, highly effective, preferred in Indian conditions.
3. **Propyl Gallate (PG)** — typical dose **100 – 500 ppm**, synergistic with BHT.
(Alternative: *Pyrogallol* PY, *Butylated Hydroxyanisole* BHA — both acceptable.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option for students choosing Performance Additives

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model]**

**Q.A(a)** Explain the classification of fuel additives used in alternative fuels with suitable examples. Discuss the role and working mechanism of antioxidants in biodiesel. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Fuel additives are chemical compounds blended with base fuels in small proportions (< 1 % mass) to enhance specific physico-chemical properties. In alternative fuels (biodiesel, ethanol, methanol, CNG, LPG, H₂), additives are essential to overcome inherent limitations like oxidative instability, poor cold-flow, low lubricity, corrosion, and embrittlement.

**Classification (3 marks):**

1. **Performance Improvers** — Cetane improvers (2-EHN, amyl nitrate), Octane boosters (MTBE, ETBE, MMT), Lubricity improvers (oleic acid).
2. **Stability Improvers** — Primary antioxidants (BHT, BHA, TBHQ, PY, PG), Secondary antioxidants (phosphites, thioesters), Metal deactivators.
3. **Cold-Flow Improvers** — Ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA) copolymers, polyalkyl methacrylates (PAMA), styrene–maleic anhydride.
4. **Handling Improvers** — Anti-foam (silicones), demulsifiers, anti-icing agents.
5. **Emission-Control Additives** — Oxygenates (ethanol, methanol, MTBE), particulate suppressors.
6. **Fuel-Specific Additives** — Denaturants (ethanol), odorants (THT for CNG), anti-embrittlement agents (H₂).

**Antioxidant Mechanism in Biodiesel (3 marks):**
Biodiesel contains unsaturated fatty acids (oleic C18:1, linoleic C18:2, linolenic C18:3). Auto-oxidation proceeds in three stages:
- **Initiation:** Trace Fe³⁺ / Cu²⁺ / heat abstract allylic H → R• radical.
- **Propagation:** R• + O₂ → ROO• → ROO• + RH → ROOH + R• (chain).
- **Termination:** ROO• + ArOH (BHT/TBHQ) → ROOH + ArO• (stable, non-propagating).
Result: induction period (Rancimat) extended from ~3 h to > 8 h, satisfying **EN 14112** for B100 and **EN 15751** for B6–B30 blends. Typical dose: 200–1000 ppm.

---

**Q.A(b)** A B20 blend (20 % v/v biodiesel, 80 % v/v diesel) is to be dosed with a cetane improver 2-EHN. Given: CN of biodiesel = 58, CN of petroleum diesel = 50, density of biodiesel = 880 kg/m³, density of diesel = 840 kg/m³. The 2-EHN dose of 0.2 % mass raises the cetane number by 5 units linearly.
Compute:
(i) the mass fractions of biodiesel and diesel in the fuel base,
(ii) the cetane number of the blend before dosing,
(iii) the final cetane number of the doped B20.
**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mass fractions (2 marks):**
Take basis = 1 L of blend.
- $V_{bio} = 0.20\;\text{L}$ → $m_{bio} = 0.20 \times 880 = 176\;\text{kg-equiv} = 0.176\;\text{kg}$ (using kg/L as density unit)
- $V_{diesel} = 0.80\;\text{L}$ → $m_{diesel} = 0.80 \times 840 = 672\;\text{kg-equiv} = 0.672\;\text{kg}$
- $m_{total} = 0.176 + 0.672 = 0.848\;\text{kg}$

$$x_{bio} = \dfrac{0.176}{0.848} = 0.2075$$

$$x_{diesel} = \dfrac{0.672}{0.848} = 0.7925$$

**(ii) CN of base blend (2 marks):**

$$CN_{blend} = (0.2075)(58) + (0.7925)(50) = 12.04 + 39.63 = 51.67$$

**(iii) Final CN after 2-EHN dosing (3 marks):**

$$CN_{final} = CN_{blend} + \Delta CN_{additive} = 51.67 + 5 = 56.67$$

**Result:** $\boxed{CN_{final} \approx 56.7}$ — meets EN 590 (> 51) and IS 1460:2017 (> 48) standards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget the volume-to-mass conversion** in step (i) and directly use $x_{bio} = 0.20$ as mass fraction. This yields $CN = 51.6$ instead of $51.67$ — a small but visible loss of 1 mark. Always convert volume % → mass % when the linear-blending equation uses **mass fractions** for cetane number. Some KTU key answers explicitly require the **mass-fraction** form. **Save 1 mark by writing both conversions.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Option for students choosing Stability & Cold-Flow Additives

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model]**

**Q.B(a)** Discuss the role of cold-flow improvers (CFPP depressants / PPDs) and lubricity improvers in biodiesel. Explain the EN 116 and HFRR test methods briefly. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Cold-Flow Improvers (3 marks):**
Biodiesel (FAME) made from soy, rapeseed, palm, or used cooking oil has high **saturated fatty acid content** (e.g., palm methyl ester has ~50 % saturated C16:0, C18:0). These saturates have melting points of 30–70 °C, causing:
- **Crystallisation** at low temperatures → fuel filter plugging.
- **Pour Point (PP)** rise, **Cloud Point (CP)** rise, **Cold Filter Plugging Point (CFPP)** rise.
PPDs like **ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA) copolymers**, **polyalkyl methacrylates (PAMA)**, and **styrene–maleic anhydride** co-crystallise with the saturates and **disrupt wax-crystal growth**, producing smaller, non-coalescing crystals that pass through the filter. Typical dose: 100 – 1000 ppm, ΔCFPP = 5 – 20 °C.

**Role of Lubricity Improvers (2 marks):**
Hydrodesulfurisation of diesel to < 10 ppm sulfur (ULSD) **removes natural polar lubricity molecules**. Biodiesel has *some* inherent lubricity, but B5–B20 blends may fail **HFRR WS1.4 < 460 µm** (EN 14214) and **< 520 µm** (EN 590). Lubricity improvers like **oleic acid, fatty acid esters, dimer acids** re-establish a boundary-lubrication film on injector surfaces at doses of 50 – 200 ppm.

**Test Methods (2 marks):**
- **EN 116 (CFPP):** Sample cooled at 1 °C/min; drawn through a 45 µm mesh under 200 mbar vacuum; CFPP = lowest T at which 20 mL fails to filter in < 60 s.
- **HFRR (EN ISO 12156-1):** A 6 mm steel ball oscillates against a stationary steel disc immersed in fuel; 75 °C, 1 kg load, 50 Hz, 1 mm stroke, 75 min; WS1.4 = **average wear scar diameter corrected to 1.4 µm roughness** at 60 °C water-saturated fuel.

---

**Q.B(b)** A refiner specifies a cetane improver treat rate of **2.5 lb/1000 bbl** for a low-sulfur diesel. If the additive density is 920 kg/m³ and the fuel density is 845 kg/m³, calculate:
(i) the additive concentration in g/L,
(ii) the additive concentration in ppm (mg/kg),
(iii) the volume % of the additive in the blend. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Concentration in g/L (3 marks):**

$$1\;\text{lb} = 0.4536\;\text{kg} = 453.6\;\text{g}$$

$$1000\;\text{bbl} = 158.987\;\text{m}^{3} = 158{,}987\;\text{L}$$

$$C = \dfrac{2.5 \times 453.6}{158{,}987} = \dfrac{1133.98}{158{,}987} = 7.132 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{g/L}$$

$$\boxed{C = 0.00713\;\text{g/L} = 7.13\;\text{mg/L}}$$

**(ii) Concentration in ppm (mg/kg) (2 marks):**

$$ppm = \dfrac{C \times 1000}{\rho_{fuel}} = \dfrac{7.132 \times 1000}{845} = 8.44\;\text{ppm (mg/kg)}$$

$$\boxed{C_{ppm} \approx 8.44\;\text{mg/kg}}$$

**(iii) Volume % of additive (2 marks):**

First, mass of additive per L of fuel:
$$m_{add} = 7.132\;\text{mg per L of fuel} = 7.132 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{kg/L}$$

Volume of additive from its density:
$$V_{add} = \dfrac{m_{add}}{\rho_{add}} = \dfrac{7.132 \times 10^{-6}}{920} = 7.752 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{L} = 7.752 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{L/L}$$

Volume %:
$$\phi_{add} = 7.752 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 7.75 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{vol\%} \approx 7.75\;\text{ppm(vol)}$$

$$\boxed{\phi_{add} \approx 7.75 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{vol\%}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In treat-rate problems, students commonly confuse the conversion **1 lb/1000 bbl ≈ 2853 ppm** (volume basis, with oil density assumed) with the actual **mass ppm** value. The 2853 ppm figure is the **mg/L concentration in oil of density 1000 kg/m³** — i.e., *volumetric* ppm — NOT the true mg/kg. For an actual fuel of 845 kg/m³, the correct value is **8.44 mg/kg** per 1 lb/1000 bbl. Always state which basis you are using. **Full 2 marks are awarded only when the units are unambiguous.**

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Print & Memorise Before Exam**

- **Definition:** Additive = chemical blended at < 1 % mass (sometimes up to 5–10 % for oxygenates) to enhance fuel property.
- **Five Major Roles:** (1) improve combustion, (2) improve stability, (3) improve cold flow, (4) restore lubricity, (5) control emissions.
- **Most Critical Additive for Biodiesel = Antioxidant** (BHT, BHA, TBHQ, PY, PG) at 200–1000 ppm to meet **EN 14112 IP > 8 h**.
- **Most Critical Additive for ULSD = Lubricity Improver** (oleic acid, fatty esters) at 50–200 ppm to meet **HFRR WS1.4 < 460 µm**.
- **Most Critical Additive for Diesel Ignition = Cetane Improver** (2-EHN) at 0.05–0.3 % mass to lift CN by 3–8 units.
- **Most Critical Additive for CNG/LPG = Odorant** (THT) at 5–25 mg/m³ for leak detection per **EN 16726**.
- **Most Critical Additive for Ethanol = Corrosion Inhibitor** (imidazoline, fatty amines) at 50–250 ppm to protect Al, Cu, Zn, Mg.
- **Standard CN Linear Blend:** $CN_{blend} = \sum x_i \cdot CN_i$ — use **mass fractions** for cetane.
- **Standard ON Linear Blend:** $ON_{blend} = \sum x_i \cdot ON_i$ — use **volume fractions** for octane (acceptable approximation).
- **Dosage Conversions to Memorise:**
  - 1 lb/1000 bbl ≈ 2.853 g/L (mg/L) ≈ 2853 ppm(vol) for $\rho_{fuel} \approx 1000$ kg/m³
  - For real fuels: ppm(mass) = (mg/L × 1000)/$\rho_{fuel}$
  - 1 % mass = 10,000 ppm = 10 g/kg
- **Threshold Doses (KTU Board Favourite):**
  - Antioxidants: 100 – 1000 ppm
  - PPDs: 100 – 1000 ppm
  - Cetane improvers: 0.05 – 0.3 % mass (500 – 3000 ppm)
  - Lubricity improvers: 50 – 200 ppm
  - Octane boosters: 5 – 15 % vol (much higher)
- **EN/BIS Limits to Remember:**
  - B100 IP > 8 h (EN 14112, Rancimat)
  - B100 CFPP variable by climate (EN 14214 latitude-based)
  - ULSD/HSD HFRR < 460 µm (EN 14214) / < 520 µm (EN 590)
  - HSD CN > 51 (EN 590) / > 48 (IS 1460:2017)
  - B6–B30 IP > 8 h (EN 15751, parallel to EN 14112)
- **Don't Confuse:** `treat rate` ≠ `dose` ≠ `concentration`. Treat rate is the supplier's specification; dose is what the engine sees; concentration is the resulting in-fuel value.
- **Don't Confuse Units:** `ppm (vol)` ≠ `ppm (mass)`. KTU always specifies — if not specified, default to **mass ppm (mg/kg)** in engine contexts.
- **Exam-Favourite Traps:**
  1. Volume % vs mass % mismatch in blending equations.
  2. 2853 ppm assumed universally (it's only for water-density fluids).
  3. CN blending via volume % — wrong; use mass %.
  4. CFPP, PP, CP confused — they are three different test temperatures.
  5. Rancimat induction period test (EN 14112) confused with pressure differential scanning calorimetry (PDSC, ASTM D6186).
- **Rancimat vs PDSC:** Rancimat measures IP in hours at 110 °C under 10 L/h O₂; PDSC measures oxidation onset temperature in °C under O₂ pressure. Both measure oxidation stability but are **not interchangeable** numerically.
- **One-Line Mantra for KTU Viva:** *"Additives are the engineering vitamins that bridge neat alternative fuels and the legacy engines they must serve — without them, the energy transition stalls at the fuel filter."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternative Fuels - Advantages and Disadvantages of Conventional Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alternative Fuels - Advantages and Disadvantages of Conventional Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conventional Fuels** are the historically dominant, **fossil-based, non-renewable** energy sources that have powered global industry, transportation, and electricity generation since the Industrial Revolution. They include **Petroleum (Diesel & Gasoline), Coal, and Natural Gas (CNG/LNG)** — derived from the anaerobic decomposition of buried organic matter (kerogen) over millions of years under high pressure and temperature.

These fuels are hydrocarbons (composed primarily of Carbon and Hydrogen) and release energy through **combustion (oxidation)**.

$$ \text{Hydrocarbon} + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + \text{Energy (Heat)} $$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Earth's Piggy Bank"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of conventional fuels as a "Locked Piggy Bank" buried deep underground.**
>
> - For **300 million years**, Earth has been collecting tiny drops of solar energy (via ancient plants and plankton) and "saving" them as compressed chemical bonds inside rocks.
> - Humanity found this piggy bank roughly **200 years ago** and started breaking it open at an **alarming rate**.
> - Once broken (burnt), the carbon piggy bank **cannot be refilled** within any human timescale — it takes another 300 million years to form.
> - Worse, every "coin" we spend releases **trapped carbon into the atmosphere** as $CO_2$, disrupting the planet's natural balance.

## 1.3 Classification of Conventional Fuels

| Category | Examples | Physical State | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liquid** | Diesel, Petrol (Gasoline), Kerosene | Liquid | Transportation |
| **Gaseous** | Natural Gas (CNG, LNG), LPG | Gas | Power, Industry, Vehicles |
| **Solid** | Coal, Lignite, Peat | Solid | Thermal Power, Steel |

## 1.4 Why Study Their Disadvantages?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The entire field of **Alternative Fuels** exists because conventional fuels have **critical flaws** — environmental, economic, and geopolitical. Understanding these disadvantages is the *raison d'être* for switching to biofuels, hydrogen, electric, and synthetic fuels.

**Standard Energy Benchmark (KTU Reference Values):**

- **1 barrel of crude oil = 159 liters = ~6.1 GJ of energy**
- **Global annual consumption = ~100 million barrels/day (as of 2024)**
- **Proved reserves life expectancy: ~50 years for oil, ~50 years for natural gas, ~150 years for coal**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Advantages of Conventional Fuels (The "Pros")

> [!NOTE]
> Conventional fuels dominate global energy markets (~80%) for **legitimate engineering reasons**. Students must appreciate these strengths before justifying their replacement.

### ✅ Advantages — Structured Breakdown

1. **High Energy Density (Calorific Value)**
   - Diesel: ~45 MJ/kg
   - Petrol: ~44 MJ/kg
   - Natural Gas: ~50 MJ/kg (HHV)
   - Coal: ~24–35 MJ/kg
   - *Comparison: Lithium-ion battery ≈ 0.5–1 MJ/kg — a ~50x gap!*

2. **Established Infrastructure**
   - Decades-old **refineries, pipelines, gas stations, ports, and rail networks** exist globally.
   - Refueling/dispatch takes minutes; no behavioral change required.

3. **Ease of Storage and Transport**
   - Liquid fuels are storable at ambient conditions.
   - Established logistics: tankers, pipelines, barges.

4. **Mature and Reliable Technology**
   - Internal combustion engines, gas turbines, boilers — all optimized over 100+ years.
   - High efficiency baselines: Diesel engine ~40–45%, Otto cycle ~25–35%.

5. **Cost-Effectiveness (Currently)**
   - Low extraction cost in many regions; subsidies in many economies.
   - Global commodity markets (Brent, WTI crude benchmarks) ensure liquidity.

6. **Wide Availability and Provenance Diversity**
   - Multiple global suppliers reduce single-point failure.

7. **High Energy-to-Volume Ratio**
   - Critical for aviation, heavy trucking, shipping.

---

## 2.2 Disadvantages of Conventional Fuels (The "Cons")

> [!WARNING]
> These disadvantages are the **primary drivers** for alternative fuel adoption. Each maps directly to a **UN Sustainable Development Goal (SDG)** failure.

### ❌ Disadvantages — Structured Breakdown

1. **Non-Renewable & Depleting Reserves**
   - Finite stock; consumption exceeds geological regeneration by **~1,000,000x**.

2. **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions & Climate Change**
   - Burning 1 liter of diesel releases **~2.68 kg of $CO_2$**.
   - Coal emits **~820 g $CO_2$/kWh** vs renewables at **<50 g/kWh**.

3. **Local Air Pollution (Criteria Pollutants)**

   $$ \text{Emissions} = \left\{ CO, NO_x, SO_x, PM_{2.5}, PM_{10}, HC, \text{Lead} \right\} $$

   - Causes **smog, acid rain, respiratory diseases** (asthma, COPD, lung cancer).

4. **Particulate Matter (PM) and Black Carbon**
   - Especially from diesel and coal — solid carbon particles in lungs.

5. **Oil Price Volatility & Economic Shock**
   - 1973 OPEC crisis, 2008 oil price spike, 2022 Russia-Ukraine war — all caused global recessions.

6. **Geopolitical Dependence**
   - Energy security tied to a few producing nations (Middle East, Russia, Venezuela).
   - Political instability → supply disruption.

7. **High Refining Cost & Complexity**
   - Cracking, reforming, desulfurization → energy-intensive processes.

8. **Toxic By-products**
   - PAHs (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons), dioxins, heavy metals.

9. **Environmental Disasters**
   - Oil spills (Exxon Valdez, Deepwater Horizon) — long-term ecological damage.

10. **Land Use Degradation**
    - Mining, drilling, pipelines fragment ecosystems.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for Part A (3-mark) and numerical sub-questions.**

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Combustion of generic hydrocarbon | $C_xH_y + \left(x+\frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O$ | mol | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio |
| Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) | $AFR_{st} \approx 14.7$ | kg air/kg fuel | Engine calibration baseline |
| Stoichiometric AFR (diesel) | $AFR_{st} \approx 14.5$ | kg air/kg fuel | Diesel engine design |
| Calorific Value | $CV = \frac{\dot{Q}_{released}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}}$ | MJ/kg | Energy content comparison |
| $CO_2$ emission (diesel) | $E_{CO_2} = 2.68$ | kg/L | Emission inventory |
| Carbon intensity (coal) | $CI_{coal} \approx 820$ | g $CO_2$/kWh | Power plant emission factor |
| Energy density ratio | $\eta_{d} = \frac{CV_{fuel}}{CV_{diesel}}$ | dimensionless | Fuel substitution comparison |
| Reserve-to-Production ratio | $R/P = \frac{\text{Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | years | Long-term availability |
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | Engine efficiency metric |
| First Law Efficiency | $\eta_{I} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Thermodynamic limit |

> **⚠ LaTeX Note:** In all equations above, vertical bars for absolute values (where used) are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table markdown syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Engineers benchmark new alternative fuels against diesel/gasoline using **BSFC, NOx, PM emissions, and well-to-wheel $CO_2$**.
- **Power Plants:** Designers use **carbon intensity factors** to compare coal vs. gas vs. renewables.
- **Policy Makers:** The **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio** drives long-term energy planning and import-export strategies.
- **Maritime & Aviation:** Energy density is non-negotiable — drives R&D into synthetic fuels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) for Gasoline ($C_8H_{18}$)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **frequently asked 7-mark problem** in KTU examinations. Master every line.

### Given:
- Fuel: Octane, $C_8H_{18}$
- Reaction: Complete combustion with $O_2$
- Molecular weights: $C = 12$ g/mol, $H = 1$ g/mol, $O = 16$ g/mol, $N = 14$ g/mol

### Step 1 — Write the unbalanced combustion equation:

$$ C_8H_{18} + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O $$

### Step 2 — Balance carbon atoms:

$$ C_8H_{18} + O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + H_2O $$

### Step 3 — Balance hydrogen atoms:

$$ C_8H_{18} + O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O $$

### Step 4 — Balance oxygen atoms:

- Right side: $8 \times 2 + 9 \times 1 = 16 + 9 = 25$ oxygen atoms
- Therefore, $O_2$ required = $25/2 = 12.5$ mol

$$ C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O $$

### Step 5 — Introduce nitrogen from air (air = 21% $O_2$, 79% $N_2$ by volume):

$$ C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 12.5 \times \frac{79}{21}\,N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47.62\,N_2 $$

### Step 6 — Compute molar masses:

- Fuel: $8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1 = 96 + 18 = 114$ g/mol
- Air: $12.5 \times 32 + 47.62 \times 28 = 400 + 1333.3 = 1733.3$ g

### Step 7 — Compute Stoichiometric AFR:

$$ AFR_{st} = \frac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}} = \frac{1733.3}{114} \approx 15.2 $$

This is close to the empirical value of **14.7** for real gasoline (which contains other hydrocarbons and additives).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: $CO_2$ Emission from Diesel Combustion

> [!NOTE]
> Used in **emission inventory and carbon footprinting**.

### Given:
- Diesel density: $\rho \approx 0.85$ kg/L
- Diesel calorific value: $CV \approx 45$ MJ/kg
- Carbon fraction in diesel: $C_{frac} \approx 0.86$ (86% by mass)

### Step 1 — Mass of carbon per liter of diesel:

$$ m_{C} = \rho \times C_{frac} = 0.85 \times 0.86 = 0.731 \text{ kg C/L} $$

### Step 2 — Convert carbon to $CO_2$ using molecular weight ratio:

$$ \frac{MW_{CO_2}}{MW_C} = \frac{44}{12} = 3.667 $$

### Step 3 — Compute $CO_2$ per liter:

$$ m_{CO_2} = m_C \times 3.667 = 0.731 \times 3.667 \approx 2.68 \text{ kg } CO_2/\text{L} $$

This confirms the **standard emission factor of 2.68 kg $CO_2$/L diesel**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Conventional vs. Alternative Fuels

| Parameter | Petrol | Diesel | Coal | CNG | Biodiesel | Hydrogen (FCV) | Electric (BEV) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CV (MJ/kg)** | 44 | 45 | 30 | 50 | 38 | 120 | N/A |
| **$CO_2$ (g/MJ)** | 73 | 74 | 95 | 56 | 50 (well-to-wheel) | 0 (tailpipe) | 0 (tailpipe) |
| **Renewable?** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Storage** | Easy | Easy | Easy | Moderate | Easy | Hard | Moderate |
| **Infrastructure** | ✅✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅✅ | ✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Growing |
| **Cost ($/GJ)** | 12–18 | 14–20 | 3–6 | 8–14 | 18–30 | 25–40 | 20–35 |
| **Key Risk** | Climate | PM, NOx | Mercury, $CO_2$ | Methane leak | Land use | Production $CO_2$ | Mining |

---

## 3.4 Energy Security Metric — Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)

> [!NOTE]
> Geopolitical concentration of fuel supply.

$$ HHI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^2 $$

where $s_i$ is the **market share (in %) of supplier $i$**.

- $HHI < 1500$: Diversified market (low risk)
- $HHI > 2500$: Highly concentrated (high geopolitical risk)

**Example:** If only 3 countries supply 100% of imports at 40%, 35%, 25% shares:

$$ HHI = 40^2 + 35^2 + 25^2 = 1600 + 1225 + 625 = 3450 \text{ (high risk)} $$

This justifies the **strategic push toward indigenous alternative fuels** like biofuels and hydrogen.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Conventional Fuel Lifecycle (Cradle-to-Grave)

The diagram below maps the complete **fossil fuel value chain**, highlighting each stage's environmental and economic impact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Extraction: Drilling or Mining] --> B[Transport: Pipelines and Tankers]
    B --> C[Refining or Processing]
    C --> D[Distribution to End Users]
    D --> E[Combustion in Engines or Boilers]
    E --> F[Emissions: CO2, NOx, SOx, PM]
    F --> G[Atmospheric Accumulation and Climate Impact]
    G --> H[Health and Environmental Damage]
    H --> I[Policy Push for Alternative Fuels]
    I --> A

    A --> A1[Land Degradation and Oil Spills]
    B --> B1[Methane Leakage in NG Transport]
    C --> C1[Refinery Emissions and Energy Use]
    E --> E1[Engine Wear and Noise Pollution]

    classDef stage fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef impact fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef policy fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D,E stage
    class F,G,H,A1,B1,C1,E1 impact
    class I policy
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Drivers for Alternative Fuels

This block-level flow illustrates **why conventional fuels are inadequate**, mapped to the solution domains covered later in the course.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROBLEMS [Problems with Conventional Fuels]
        P1[Depletion of Reserves]
        P2[Greenhouse Gas Emissions]
        P3[Air Pollution: NOx, PM, SOx]
        P4[Geopolitical Dependence]
        P5[Price Volatility]
    end

    subgraph DRIVERS [Engineering and Policy Drivers]
        D1[Sustainability Mandate]
        D2[Climate Targets: Paris Agreement]
        D3[Public Health]
        D4[Energy Security]
        D5[Economic Stability]
    end

    subgraph SOLUTIONS [Alternative Fuel Pathways]
        S1[Biofuels: Biodiesel, Ethanol]
        S2[Hydrogen: Green and Blue]
        S3[Electric Vehicles: BEV and FCEV]
        S4[Synthetic e-Fuels]
        S5[CNG, LPG as Transition Fuels]
    end

    P1 --> D1
    P2 --> D2
    P3 --> D3
    P4 --> D4
    P5 --> D5

    D1 --> S1
    D2 --> S2
    D3 --> S3
    D4 --> S4
    D5 --> S5

    classDef prob fill:#FF9999,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef driv fill:#FFE599,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sol fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#336600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 prob
    class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5 driv
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 sol
```

## 4.3 Comparative Hierarchy — Energy Density vs. Emissions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. List any three disadvantages of conventional fuels.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Non-renewable and depleting reserves** — Fossil fuels take hundreds of millions of years to form; current consumption exceeds regeneration by orders of magnitude. *(1 mark)*
2. **Greenhouse gas emissions and climate change** — Combustion releases $CO_2$, $CH_4$, and $N_2O$, driving global warming and ocean acidification. *(1 mark)*
3. **Air pollution (NOx, SOx, PM)** — Causes smog, acid rain, respiratory diseases, and premature deaths. *(1 mark)*

### **Q2. State the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol and diesel engines.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Petrol (Gasoline):** $AFR_{st} \approx 14.7$ kg air/kg fuel *(1.5 marks)*
- **Diesel:** $AFR_{st} \approx 14.5$ kg air/kg fuel *(1.5 marks)*

These values are the theoretical minimum air required for complete combustion. Engines operate slightly leaner or richer depending on load.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### **Question A — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **advantages of conventional fuels**. Why have they dominated global energy supply for over a century? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **complete combustion of petrol (octane)** and compute the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A(a): Advantages of Conventional Fuels** *(7 marks)*

Conventional fuels — **petroleum (diesel, petrol), coal, and natural gas** — have historically dominated global energy markets due to a confluence of technical, economic, and logistical advantages:

1. **High Calorific Value / Energy Density** *(2 marks)*
   - Diesel (~45 MJ/kg) and petrol (~44 MJ/kg) offer compact energy storage, critical for transport (aviation, trucking, shipping).
   - Energy density comparison: Diesel ≈ **45x** lithium-ion battery.

2. **Established Infrastructure** *(2 marks)*
   - Global network of refineries, pipelines (~3.5 million km worldwide), tankers, and retail outlets.
   - Refueling time: minutes vs. hours for batteries.

3. **Mature and Reliable Technology** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Internal combustion engines, gas turbines, and boilers refined over 100+ years.
   - Efficiencies: 40–45% (diesel), 35–40% (combined cycle gas turbine).

4. **Cost-Effectiveness** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Historically low extraction costs; liquid global commodity markets (Brent, WTI).
   - Subsidies and tax structures reinforce demand.

**Conclusion:** The dominance is due to a **virtuous cycle of investment, technology, and infrastructure** — but this cycle is now disrupted by climate and depletion concerns.

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A(b): Combustion of Octane and AFR Calculation** *(7 marks)*

**Combustion Equation:**

$$ C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + 47.62\,N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47.62\,N_2 + \text{Energy} $$

**Derivation Recap (full derivation in Section 3.1):**

1. Balance C atoms: 8 $CO_2$ produced.
2. Balance H atoms: 9 $H_2O$ produced.
3. Balance O atoms: 12.5 $O_2$ required.
4. Account for $N_2$ in air: $12.5 \times (79/21) = 47.62$ mol $N_2$.

**Molecular Weights:**

$$ MW_{C_8H_{18}} = 8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1 = 114 \text{ g/mol} $$

$$ MW_{air} = 12.5 \times 32 + 47.62 \times 28 = 400 + 1333.36 = 1733.36 \text{ g} $$

**Stoichiometric AFR:**

$$ AFR_{st} = \frac{1733.36}{114} \approx 15.2 $$

**Note:** Real gasoline gives AFR ≈ 14.7 due to additives and mixed hydrocarbons.

**Neat Sketch:** Refer to **Section 4.1 (Mermaid Block Diagram)** for the complete lifecycle. A labeled combustion chamber diagram with intake air, fuel injector, spark plug, exhaust gases (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) earns the sketch marks.

---

### **Question B — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Enumerate the major **disadvantages of conventional fuels** and explain how each drives the search for alternatives. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A small diesel generator consumes **5 liters of diesel per hour**. Compute the **hourly $CO_2$ emission** and **annual emission** if it operates for **8 hours/day, 300 days/year**. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B(a): Disadvantages of Conventional Fuels** *(7 marks)*

| # | Disadvantage | Impact | Alternative Push |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Non-renewable / Depleting** | Finite reserves, R/P ~50 yrs for oil | Renewables, biofuels |
| 2 | **GHG Emissions / Climate Change** | Global warming, sea-level rise | Hydrogen, electric, biofuels |
| 3 | **Air Pollutants (NOx, SOx, PM)** | 7 million premature deaths/yr (WHO) | Electric vehicles, fuel cells |
| 4 | **Geopolitical Dependence** | Energy security risk | Indigenous alternatives |
| 5 | **Price Volatility** | Macroeconomic shocks | Diversified energy mix |
| 6 | **Refining Complexity / Cost** | High CAPEX | Direct-use fuels (e.g., $H_2$) |
| 7 | **Environmental Disasters** | Oil spills, mining damage | Cleaner extraction methods |

*(Each point: 1 mark — mention the disadvantage clearly and tie to a driver.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B(b): $CO_2$ Emission Numerical** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**

- Fuel consumption rate = 5 L/hr
- $CO_2$ emission factor for diesel = **2.68 kg/L**
- Operating hours = 8 hr/day × 300 days/year = 2400 hr/year

**Step 1 — Hourly $CO_2$ emission:**

$$ E_{hourly} = 5 \, L/hr \times 2.68 \, kg/L = 13.4 \, kg\, CO_2/hr $$

**Step 2 — Daily emission:**

$$ E_{daily} = 13.4 \times 8 = 107.2 \, kg\, CO_2/\text{day} $$

**Step 3 — Annual emission:**

$$ E_{annual} = 107.2 \times 300 = 32{,}160 \, kg\, CO_2/\text{year} $$

$$ E_{annual} \approx 32.16 \, \text{tonnes } CO_2/\text{year} $$

**Engineering Insight:** This is equivalent to the carbon sequestered by approximately **530 tree seedlings grown for 10 years** (US EPA equivalence: 1 tree ≈ 60 kg $CO_2$/decade). This motivates **biogas generators, biodiesel blends (B20), or solar PV** alternatives.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose 2–4 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "advantages" with "applications."** "Diesel is used in trucks" is an *application*, not an *advantage*. Frame as "high energy density enables long-haul trucking."
>
> 2. **Writing unbalanced combustion equations.** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if $C$, $H$, or $O$ atoms are not balanced. Always verify: atoms left = atoms right.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $N_2$ in the combustion equation.** When asked "with air," students often write only $O_2$. Air = $O_2$ + 3.76 $N_2$. Omitting $N_2$ costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mixing up calorific values.** Petrol ≈ 44 MJ/kg, Diesel ≈ 45 MJ/kg, Methane ≈ 50 MJ/kg, Hydrogen ≈ 120 MJ/kg. **Memorize these.**
>
> 5. **Skipping the conclusion.** Always end with a one-line takeaway linking conventional fuels to the *necessity* of alternatives.
>
> 6. **Unit errors in numericals.** Write units at every step (L/hr × kg/L = kg/hr). Examiners penalize missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Conventional fuels** = fossil-based, non-renewable hydrocarbons (petroleum, coal, natural gas).
- **Calorific value (CV)** = energy released per unit mass (MJ/kg).
- **AFR** = air-fuel ratio; stoichiometric values: petrol ≈ 14.7, diesel ≈ 14.5.
- **R/P ratio** = reserve-to-production ratio (years of supply left).

### ✅ Top 5 Advantages to Remember
1. High energy density (45 MJ/kg diesel).
2. Mature technology & infrastructure.
3. Easy storage and transport.
4. Currently cost-effective.
5. Wide availability.

### ❌ Top 5 Disadvantages to Remember
1. Non-renewable and depleting.
2. Greenhouse gas emissions (climate change).
3. Local air pollution (NOx, SOx, PM).
4. Geopolitical dependence & price volatility.
5. Environmental disasters (spills, mining).

### 📊 Must-Know Numerical Values
- Diesel $CO_2$ emission = **2.68 kg/L**.
- Petrol $CO_2$ emission ≈ **2.31 kg/L**.
- Coal carbon intensity ≈ **820 g $CO_2$/kWh**.
- Global oil consumption ≈ **100 million barrels/day**.
- Stoichiometric AFR (petrol) = **14.7**, (diesel) = **14.5**.

### 🧠 Cognitive-Level Mapping (Bloom's Taxonomy)
- **Remember/Understand:** Lists of advantages/disadvantages. *(Part A)*
- **Apply:** Calculate $CO_2$ emissions. *(Part B numericals)*
- **Analyze:** Compare fuels using a matrix.
- **Evaluate:** Justify shift to alternatives.

### 🎯 Linking to Future Modules
- This topic **sets the stage** for Module 3 (Biofuels), Module 4 (Hydrogen), and Module 5 (Electric Mobility).
- Every disadvantage listed above maps to a specific alternative discussed later.

---

> 🏁 **End of Module 2 Notes — "Advantages and Disadvantages of Conventional Fuels"**
