topics.title: Need for Alternative Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Need for Alternative Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Alternative fuels** are non-conventional, renewable, or substantially modified energy carriers that can be used in place of, or in conjunction with, traditional petroleum-based fossil fuels (gasoline, diesel, kerosene, LPG, CNG) for transportation, power generation, heating, or industrial applications. The term encompasses biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel, biogas), gaseous fuels (hydrogen, CNG, LPG, producer gas), synthetic fuels, and electric/hybrid energy systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMUT411, Module 2):**
> *Alternative fuels are energy sources which are not derived from the conventional crude oil refining process and which can be used to power vehicles, generate electricity, or meet industrial energy demand with reduced environmental impact and improved sustainability.*

The "need" for alternative fuels arises from a convergence of **four global crises**:
1. **Depletion of Fossil Reserves** (resource finiteness)
2. **Environmental Degradation** (greenhouse gas emissions, local pollution)
3. **Energy Security & Geopolitical Instability** (import dependency)
4. **Economic Volatility** (crude oil price fluctuations)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Bank Account of Planet Earth"**
> Imagine Earth has a fixed bank account of fossil fuel (coal, oil, gas) deposits. Humanity has been recklessly **withdrawing** from this account for over 150 years while the *interest rate* (regeneration time) is in **millions of years**. The balance is depleting rapidly. Additionally, every "withdrawal" (combustion) releases harmful "transaction fees" called **emissions** that pollute the atmosphere.
>
> **Alternative fuels** are like opening a *new, renewable income source* — solar, wind, biomass, hydrogen — that earns daily, costs nothing to withdraw, and leaves zero pollution. The "need" is essentially the realization that the old account is overdrawn, and the planet is showing symptoms of this financial stress in the form of climate change, smog, and energy wars.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value of Diesel | $CV_d$ | **~ 43 MJ/kg** |
| Calorific Value of Petrol | $CV_p$ | **~ 44 MJ/kg** |
| Density of Diesel | $\rho_d$ | **~ 840 kg/m³** |
| Atmospheric CO₂ Concentration (2024) | $C_{atm}$ | **~ 421 ppm** |
| Pre-industrial CO₂ Level | $C_0$ | **~ 280 ppm** |
| Carbon Content of Diesel | $C_{frac}$ | **~ 0.86 (86 % by mass)** |
| Stoichiometric Mass Ratio O₂/Fuel | $r_{stoich}$ | **~ 3.4 (for diesel)** |
| World Crude Oil Reserve Life | $R_{life}$ | **~ 50 years** (at current production rates) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical values listed above are **engineering approximations** aligned with standard KTU textbook tables and are suitable for board examination calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of "Need" — Structured Logic Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Resource Depletion (The Supply-Side Crisis)
- **Why?** Crude oil is a **non-renewable**, finite resource formed over geological timescales of **~ 100–400 million years**.
- **How?** Global consumption rate exceeds discovery rate. Proven reserves follow the **Hubbert Peak Curve** — production will inevitably decline.
- **Evidence:** OPEC and BP Statistical Review consistently report **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratios** falling below 60 years.

### Pillar 2: Environmental Concerns (The Sustainability-Side Crisis)
- **Why?** Combustion of fossil fuels releases:
  - **CO₂** (primary greenhouse gas causing global warming)
  - **NOx, SOx** (acid rain, respiratory ailments)
  - **Particulate Matter (PM2.5, PM10)** (smog, lung disease)
  - **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (UHC)** and **CO** (toxic)
- **How?** Carbon-to-CO₂ stoichiometry governs CO₂ emission. Cleaner fuels have lower **C/H ratio** and reduced emission footprint.

### Pillar 3: Energy Security (The Geopolitical Crisis)
- **Why?** India imports **> 85 %** of its crude oil requirement. This creates:
  - Foreign exchange drain
  - Vulnerability to supply disruptions
  - Strategic dependence on politically unstable regions
- **How?** Indigenous alternative fuels (biofuels, solar, wind) reduce import dependency and improve **energy independence ratio**.

### Pillar 4: Economic Sustainability (The Affordability Crisis)
- **Why?** Crude oil prices are highly volatile (e.g., 2008 peak: **\$147/barrel**, 2020 crash: **\$-37/barrel**).
- **How?** Alternative fuels, once infrastructure matures, can be produced domestically at **predictable, stable costs** (e.g., biodiesel from waste cooking oil, ethanol from sugarcane).

## 2.2 Engineering Classification of Alternative Fuels

| Category | Examples | Primary Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Alcohols** | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH), Methanol (CH₃OH) | High octane, renewable |
| **Vegetable Oils & Biodiesel** | Jatropha oil, Pongamia, FAME | Biodegradable, low SOx |
| **Gaseous Fuels** | CNG, LPG, H₂, Biogas | High C-efficiency, low emissions |
| **Synthetic Fuels** | Fischer-Tropsch diesel, syngas | Made from coal/biomass |
| **Electric & Hybrid** | Battery EVs, Fuel Cells | Zero tailpipe emission |
| **Hydrogen** | Green H₂, Blue H₂ | Zero CO₂ at point of use |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute value or norm symbols are rendered using `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times \dfrac{44}{12}$ | Mass flow rate of CO₂ emitted from combustion | kg/hr |
| 2 | $CO_2^{\text{fuel}} = 3.667 \times C_{frac} \times \rho_{fuel}$ | CO₂ emission per unit volume of fuel (factor 44/12 ≈ 3.667) | kg/L |
| 3 | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{CV_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times LHV}$ | Combustion efficiency of the system | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_{sec} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Imports}}{\text{Total Consumption}}$ | Energy security index (1 = fully secure) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R/P = \dfrac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | Reserve-to-Production ratio (years of supply left) | years |
| 6 | $AFR_{stoich} = r_{stoich} \times \dfrac{29}{32}$ | Air-Fuel Ratio on mass basis (molecular weight correction) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_{den} = \dfrac{CV}{\rho}$ | Energy density of fuel (key metric for vehicle range) | MJ/L |
| 8 | $R_{CO_2} = \dfrac{CO_2^{\text{alt}} - CO_2^{\text{conv}}}{CO_2^{\text{conv}}} \times 100$ | Percentage CO₂ reduction of alternative fuel vs. conventional | % |
| 9 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (engine performance) | kg/kWh |
| 10 | $CE_{ratio} = \dfrac{(C/H)_{alt}}{(C/H)_{conv}}$ | Carbon-to-Hydrogen ratio (lower = cleaner) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of emission auditing** in thermal power plants, IC engine design, and policy-level decisions such as **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** star ratings in India.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CO₂ Emission from Complete Combustion of a Hydrocarbon Fuel

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The General Combustion Equation

For a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y$ undergoing complete combustion with oxygen:

$$C_x H_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\, CO_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\, H_2 O$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Atomic Mass Balance for Carbon:**
One mole of fuel produces $x$ moles of CO₂. Therefore, the mass of CO₂ produced per kg of fuel is:

$$\dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{fuel}} = x \times \dfrac{M_{CO_2}}{M_{fuel}} = x \times \dfrac{44}{12x + y}$$

For **Diesel (approx. C₁₂H₂₃)**: $x = 12$, $y = 23$.

**Step 2 — Substitution for Diesel:**

$$\dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{fuel}} = 12 \times \dfrac{44}{(12 \times 12) + 23} = 12 \times \dfrac{44}{144 + 23} = 12 \times \dfrac{44}{167}$$

$$\dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{528}{167} \approx 3.162 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg diesel}$$

**Step 3 — Mass-Based Carbon Fraction Method (Quicker, KTU-Standard):**

If $C_{frac}$ is the mass fraction of carbon in the fuel (for diesel, $C_{frac} = 0.86$):

$$m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times \dfrac{44}{12} = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times 3.667$$

For 1 kg of diesel:

$$m_{CO_2} = 1 \times 0.86 \times 3.667 \approx 3.154 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the factor 44/12:** Each carbon atom (atomic mass 12) becomes one CO₂ molecule (molecular mass 44). So **1 kg of carbon always yields 44/12 = 3.667 kg of CO₂**. This is independent of the fuel.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU 2-Mark / 7-Mark Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A diesel generator consumes **10 kg/hr** of diesel. The carbon content is **86 % by mass**. Calculate:
> (a) The mass flow rate of CO₂ emitted.
> (b) The annual CO₂ emission if operated **8 hours/day, 300 days/year**.

### Solution (a) — Mass flow rate of CO₂

**Step 1:** Identify given data:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 10 \text{ kg/hr}, \quad C_{frac} = 0.86$$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$\dot{m}_{CO_2} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times \dfrac{44}{12}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$\dot{m}_{CO_2} = 10 \times 0.86 \times \dfrac{44}{12} = 8.6 \times 3.667$$

$$\dot{m}_{CO_2} = 31.536 \text{ kg/hr of CO}_2$$

### Solution (b) — Annual emission

**Step 1:** Total hours of operation per year:

$$t_{annual} = 8 \times 300 = 2400 \text{ hr/year}$$

**Step 2:** Total annual CO₂:

$$M_{CO_2}^{annual} = \dot{m}_{CO_2} \times t_{annual} = 31.536 \times 2400$$

$$M_{CO_2}^{annual} = 75,686.4 \text{ kg/year} \approx 75.7 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Conventional vs. Alternative Fuels

| Parameter | Diesel (Conventional) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | Biodiesel (FAME) | Hydrogen (H₂) | CNG (CH₄) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chemical Formula | $C_{12}H_{23}$ | $C_2H_5OH$ | $C_{19}H_{36}O_2$ (avg) | $H_2$ | $CH_4$ |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 43 | 26.9 | 37.2 | 120 (LHV) | 50 |
| C/H Ratio (by mass) | ~ 6.3 | 1.71 | 6.4 | 0 | 2.99 |
| CO₂ Emission (kg/kg fuel) | 3.16 | 1.91 | 3.04 | 0 | 2.75 |
| Energy Density (MJ/L) | ~ 36 | ~ 21 | ~ 33 | ~ 8.5 (liq.) | ~ 21 |
| Octane Number | ~ 25 (Cetane) | 108+ | ~ 50 (Cetane) | N/A (SI) | 120 |
| Renewable? | No | Yes | Yes | Yes (if green) | No |
| Primary Emission Concern | CO₂, PM, NOx | NOx, aldehydes | NOx, slight CO₂ | NOx (with air) | Methane slip |

### 3.3.1 Sample Calculation — CO₂ Reduction Percentage (Ethanol vs. Petrol)

Given: Petrol emission = **3.16 kg CO₂/kg**, Ethanol emission = **1.91 kg CO₂/kg**.

$$R_{CO_2} = \dfrac{1.91 - 3.16}{3.16} \times 100 = \dfrac{-1.25}{3.16} \times 100 \approx -39.6 \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Ethanol blended fuel (E20 — 20 % ethanol) achieves a **~ 39.6 % net CO₂ reduction** vs. pure petrol. This is the empirical basis for India's **Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme** targeting **E20 by 2025–26**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Four Pillars of "Need" and Their Cascading Consequences

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **interlinked causes** driving the global shift toward alternative fuels, mapping each driver to its primary consequence and the alternative-fuel response:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need for Alternative Fuels] --> B1[Pillar 1 Fossil Depletion]
    A --> B2[Pillar 2 Environmental Damage]
    A --> B3[Pillar 3 Energy Security Risk]
    A --> B4[Pillar 4 Economic Volatility]

    B1 --> C1[Hubbert Peak Reserve Depletion]
    B1 --> C2[Reserves 50 years or less]
    B2 --> C3[GHG Emissions Global Warming]
    B2 --> C4[Local Pollution Smog Health Issues]
    B3 --> C5[Import Dependency 85 percent]
    B3 --> C6[Geopolitical Supply Disruption]
    B4 --> C7[Crude Price Volatility 2022 Spike]
    B4 --> C8[Foreign Exchange Drain]

    C1 --> D1[Response Biofuels Ethanol Biodiesel]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2[Response Hydrogen and Electric Mobility]
    C4 --> D2
    C5 --> D3[Response Domestic Renewable Feedstock]
    C6 --> D3
    C7 --> D4[Response Stable Indigenous Energy]
    C8 --> D4

    D1 --> E[Outcome Sustainable Low Carbon Energy Mix]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F[Net Result Carbon Neutrality Energy Independence]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Indian National Mission Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[National Policy Push] --> M1[National Biofuel Policy 2018]
    P1 --> M2[National Hydrogen Mission 2021]
    P1 --> M3[FAME India Phase II EV Push]
    P1 --> M4[Ethanol Blended Petrol EBP 2025]

    M1 --> O1[Reduced Import Bill 87 percent Crude Dependent]
    M2 --> O2[Green Hydrogen Hub by 2030]
    M3 --> O3[EV Penetration 30 percent by 2030]
    M4 --> O4[20 percent Ethanol Blending Target]

    O1 --> GOAL[Energy Transition Achievement]
    O2 --> GOAL
    O3 --> GOAL
    O4 --> GOAL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Aid for Section 4:**
> - The first diagram uses a **top-down tree topology** to show root causes (Pillars) → symptoms (Consequences) → responses (Alternative Fuels) → outcome (Sustainability).
> - The second diagram shows the **horizontal policy-to-outcome mapping** specific to India's national missions, linking government initiatives to measurable targets.
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`A`, `B1`, `C1`, etc.) to comply with Mermaid's safe-syntax protocol.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Model answers follow the **definition → elaboration → example** structure expected by KTU 2024 scheme evaluators.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**
**"List any six reasons for the need of alternative fuels."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The six principal reasons are:
1. **Depletion of fossil fuel reserves** (finite, non-renewable resources, R/P < 60 years).
2. **Environmental pollution** (CO₂, NOx, SOx, particulate matter).
3. **Global warming and climate change** (rising atmospheric CO₂ ~ 421 ppm).
4. **Energy security concerns** (India imports > 85 % of crude oil).
5. **Economic instability** due to volatile crude oil prices.
6. **Need for sustainable development** (UN SDG-7: Affordable and Clean Energy).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**
**"Define the term 'Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio'. Why is it a key indicator of fossil fuel scarcity?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio** is defined as the ratio of the remaining proven reserves of a fossil fuel to its current annual production rate. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$R/P = \dfrac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production Rate}}$$

The unit is **years**, and it represents the **theoretical number of years** the resource will last at the current rate of consumption. It is a key scarcity indicator because a falling R/P ratio signals **approaching peak oil/gas** and motivates the search for alternative energy sources. As of recent BP Statistical Review, global oil R/P is approximately **~ 50 years**, indicating a pressing need for alternatives.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Two alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below. **Attempt EITHER Q-A OR Q-B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain in detail the various reasons necessitating the search for alternative fuels. Discuss the depletion of fossil fuels and the environmental impact with relevant data. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Depletion of Fossil Fuels (3.5 marks):**
Fossil fuels — coal, petroleum, and natural gas — are **non-renewable** resources formed over millions of years from organic matter. Global consumption is approximately **~ 100 million barrels of oil per day**, while new discoveries have not kept pace. The **Hubbert Peak Theory** predicts that oil production follows a bell curve, and global production has either peaked or will peak soon. The R/P ratio for oil is **~ 50 years**, for natural gas **~ 50 years**, and for coal **~ 130 years**. With rising energy demand from developing economies like India and China, the depletion is accelerated.

**2. Environmental Impact (3.5 marks):**
Combustion of fossil fuels releases:
- **CO₂ (~ 3.16 kg/kg of diesel)**: A primary greenhouse gas causing **global warming**. Atmospheric CO₂ has risen from **280 ppm (pre-industrial)** to **~ 421 ppm (2024)**, a 50 % increase.
- **NOx and SOx**: Cause acid rain and respiratory diseases.
- **Particulate Matter (PM2.5)**: Linked to lung cancer and cardiovascular diseases.
- **Unburnt Hydrocarbons**: Photochemical smog formation.
The **Paris Agreement (2015)** mandates limiting global temperature rise to 1.5 °C above pre-industrial levels, requiring a rapid transition to cleaner fuels.

---

**(b) With the help of a table, compare the properties of conventional diesel, ethanol, biodiesel, and hydrogen as fuels. Discuss the CO₂ emission reduction achieved by switching to ethanol blend (E20). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Comparative Property Table (3.5 marks):**

| Property | Diesel | Ethanol | Biodiesel | Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 43 | 26.9 | 37.2 | 120 (LHV) |
| Density (kg/L) | 0.84 | 0.79 | 0.88 | 0.071 (gas) |
| Octane/Cetane Number | 45 (Cetane) | 108 (Octane) | 50 (Cetane) | N/A |
| CO₂ Emission (kg/kg) | 3.16 | 1.91 | 3.04 | 0 |
| Flash Point (°C) | 55 | 13 | 130 | N/A |
| Renewable | No | Yes | Yes | Yes (if green) |

**2. E20 CO₂ Reduction Calculation (3.5 marks):**

For pure petrol: $CO_2^{petrol} \approx 3.16$ kg/kg
For pure ethanol: $CO_2^{ethanol} \approx 1.91$ kg/kg

For E20 (20 % ethanol + 80 % petrol by volume, approximated by mass):

$$CO_2^{E20} = 0.8 \times 3.16 + 0.2 \times 1.91 = 2.528 + 0.382 = 2.91 \text{ kg/kg}$$

Reduction:

$$R_{CO_2} = \dfrac{3.16 - 2.91}{3.16} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.25}{3.16} \times 100 \approx 7.9 \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Students:** Using exact E20 mass-energy weighted blending and lifecycle CO₂ accounting (which credits the **biogenic carbon** absorbed during sugarcane growth), the *net lifecycle* CO₂ reduction is significantly higher — **~ 20–40 %** depending on feedstock and process.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the energy security challenges faced by India. How do alternative fuels contribute to addressing these challenges? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Energy Security Challenges (3.5 marks):**
India is the **world's third-largest energy consumer** and **third-largest oil importer**. Key challenges include:
- **> 85 % crude oil import dependency**, exposing the economy to global price shocks.
- **Foreign exchange outflow** exceeding **\$160 billion annually** on oil imports.
- **Geopolitical vulnerability** — dependence on West Asia (Middle East) and Russia, regions prone to political instability.
- **Refining capacity constraints** during global supply disruptions (e.g., 2022 Russia-Ukraine war price spike).
- **Domestic demand-supply gap** widening due to rapid motorization (~ 4 % annual vehicle growth).

**2. Role of Alternative Fuels (3.5 marks):**
- **Biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel)**: Indigenously produced from sugarcane, jatropha, waste cooking oil → reduces crude import demand.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**: Powered by domestic electricity (increasingly from solar/wind) → eliminates petroleum dependence for transport.
- **Green Hydrogen**: A future clean fuel that can be produced domestically via electrolysis using solar/wind power.
- **CNG/Biogas**: Domestic natural gas reserves and agricultural/animal waste → reduces LPG import.
- **Government Initiatives**: National Hydrogen Mission (₹ 19,744 crore outlay), FAME-II scheme, EBP 20 % target — all aimed at **Atmanirbhar Bharat** (energy self-reliance).

---

**(b) Calculate the annual CO₂ emission from a fleet of 1000 diesel cars, each consuming on average 1.5 litres of fuel per day, operated 350 days/year. Assume diesel density 0.84 kg/L and carbon content 86 %. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Number of cars: $N = 1000$
- Daily fuel consumption per car: $V_{day} = 1.5$ L/day
- Operational days: $t = 350$ days/year
- Diesel density: $\rho_d = 0.84$ kg/L
- Carbon fraction: $C_{frac} = 0.86$

**Step 1: Annual fuel volume per car:**

$$V_{annual} = V_{day} \times t = 1.5 \times 350 = 525 \text{ L/year per car}$$

**Step 2: Annual mass of fuel per car:**

$$m_{annual} = V_{annual} \times \rho_d = 525 \times 0.84 = 441 \text{ kg/year per car}$$

**Step 3: Annual mass of CO₂ per car:**

$$m_{CO_2} = m_{annual} \times C_{frac} \times \dfrac{44}{12} = 441 \times 0.86 \times 3.667$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 441 \times 0.86 \times 3.667 = 1390.36 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{car/year}$$

**Step 4: Annual CO₂ for the fleet of 1000 cars:**

$$M_{fleet} = 1390.36 \times 1000 = 1,390,360 \text{ kg/year} = 1390.36 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** The fleet of 1000 diesel cars emits approximately **1390.36 tonnes of CO₂ per year**, equivalent to the CO₂ absorbed by roughly **22,500 mature trees** in a year — a strong justification for EV adoption.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Need for Alternative Fuels" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the formula 44/12 (3.667):** Many students write $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac}$ instead of multiplying by 3.667. This results in a **2-mark deduction** per wrong calculation. **Always remember: 1 kg of C → 3.667 kg of CO₂.**
>
> 2. **Confusing R/P ratio with consumption rate:** R/P is **reserves divided by annual production** (unit: years), NOT a dimensionless fraction. Writing it without units loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners often deduct 0.5–1 mark if final answers are written without proper units (kg/hr, tonnes/year, MJ/kg, etc.).
>
> 4. **Listing without elaboration:** In 7-mark questions, **listing points without explaining the "why"** leads to partial scoring (only 3–4 marks out of 7). Each point must be supported with a **data figure or example**.
>
> 5. **Mixing mass-based and volume-based AFSR:** Always verify whether the problem uses mass or volume basis before applying density. Read the question twice.
>
> 6. **Ignoring biogenic CO₂ in biofuel comparison:** A common conceptual error. Biofuel CO₂ is **carbon-neutral over its lifecycle** (CO₂ absorbed during growth ≈ CO₂ released during combustion), so net lifecycle emission is **near zero**, even though tailpipe emission may be similar.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Revise Before Exam:**

- **Alternative fuels** are non-conventional energy carriers replacing petroleum-based fossil fuels in transport, power, and industry.
- **Four Pillars of Need:** Depletion, Environment, Energy Security, Economy (Mnemonic: **"DEEE"**).
- **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio** = Proven Reserves ÷ Annual Production → units: **years**. Current global oil R/P ≈ **50 years**.
- **Key CO₂ Formula:** $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times \dfrac{44}{12}$, where the factor 3.667 converts carbon mass to CO₂ mass.
- **Atmospheric CO₂** has risen from 280 ppm (pre-industrial) to **~ 421 ppm (2024)**.
- **India's crude oil import dependency > 85 %** — a strategic energy security vulnerability.
- **Hubbert Peak Theory** predicts finite-resource production follows a bell curve, with peak followed by decline.
- **Energy Security Index:** $E_{sec} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Imports}}{\text{Total Consumption}}$. Higher is better; **1 = fully energy independent**.
- **E20 (20 % ethanol blend)** can reduce tailpipe CO₂ by **~ 8–10 %** (simple blending) and **~ 20–40 %** on **lifecycle basis** (biogenic carbon credit).
- **Major Indian Missions:** National Hydrogen Mission, FAME-II (EVs), EBP (Ethanol), SATAT (Biogas), National Biofuel Policy 2018.
- **Greenhouse Gases from Combustion:** CO₂, CH₄, N₂O — primarily **CO₂** is the most significant.
- **C/H Ratio** is a quick cleanliness metric: **Lower C/H → Lower CO₂ per unit energy**.
- **Renewable Energy Targets (India 2030):** 500 GW non-fossil capacity, **50 %** energy from renewables, net-zero by **2070**.
- **Major Alternative Fuel Categories:** Alcohols, Biodiesel, Gaseous (CNG/LPG/H₂), Synthetic (FT-diesel), Electric, Hydrogen.
- **Hydrogen as the "Fuel of the Future":** Zero CO₂ at point of use, but production must be **"green"** (renewable-powered electrolysis) to be truly clean.
- **Calorific Values to Memorize (approx.):** Diesel = 43 MJ/kg, Petrol = 44 MJ/kg, Ethanol = 27 MJ/kg, Biodiesel = 37 MJ/kg, H₂ = 120 MJ/kg, CNG = 50 MJ/kg.
- **BEE Star Rating** in India is based on emission efficiency, directly influenced by fuel choice.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Recap.** Students are advised to reproduce the formula sheet (Section 2.3) and the comparison matrix (Section 3.3) from memory during revision. These two artifacts together cover **> 70 %** of typical KTU Module 2 questions on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Gaseous Alternate Fuels - LPG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Gaseous Alternate Fuels - LPG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** is a flammable mixture of hydrocarbon gases, primarily **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)**, which is liquefied under moderate pressure (typically **7–8 bar at 25 °C**) for ease of storage and transport. As per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, LPG is classified as a **gaseous alternate fuel** because, although stored as a liquid, it exists in the gaseous phase at atmospheric pressure and room temperature. It is obtained as a by-product during **natural gas processing** and **crude oil refining**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Gaseous Alternate Fuels):**
> LPG is one of the four pillars of gaseous fuels studied in this module, the others being **CNG, LNG, and Biogas/Hydrogen.** Examiners frequently test LPG alongside CNG, so a thorough comparison is essential.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of LPG as a **"high-pressure thermos flask"** for cooking gas.

- When you pour water into a balloon, the balloon stretches and stores a lot of water in a small space.
- Similarly, propane and butane molecules are **squeezed together under pressure**, occupying only **1/260th of their gaseous volume**.
- The moment the valve is opened, the pressure drops, and the liquid **"boils" into gas** at room temperature — that is the gas you see and use in your kitchen stove or automobile.

In essence: **LPG = A liquid that wants to be a gas; we keep it liquid only by holding it under pressure.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Primary constituents | Propane (C₃H₈) and Butane (C₄H₁₀) |
| Storage pressure (commercial cylinders) | **7–8 bar (gauge)** at 25 °C |
| Boiling point of Propane | **−42 °C** |
| Boiling point of n-Butane | **−0.5 °C** |
| Density (liquid) of Propane | **0.51 g/cm³** at 15 °C |
| Density (liquid) of Butane | **0.58 g/cm³** at 15 °C |
| Calorific value of Propane (GCV) | **46.3 MJ/kg** |
| Calorific value of Butane (GCV) | **45.7 MJ/kg** |
| Octane rating of Propane | ~105 |
| Octane rating of Butane | ~92 |
| Auto-ignition temperature (Propane) | **450 °C** |
| Auto-ignition temperature (Butane) | **405 °C** |
| Flammability limits in air | **2.1 % – 9.6 %** by volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the Examiner:** A 14.2 kg domestic LPG cylinder (the standard Bharat Gas / Indane / HP cylinder) contains roughly **270 MJ of energy** — enough to cook for ~30 days for an average Indian family.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Composition of LPG

Commercial LPG is not a pure compound but a **carefully blended mixture**:

- **Propane (C₃H₈):** Winter-grade LPG is richer in propane (lower boiling point) to ensure vaporization in cold climates.
- **n-Butane (C₄H₁₀) and iso-Butane (i-C₄H₁₀):** Summer-grade LPG is richer in butane (higher boiling point), preventing excessive pressure build-up in cylinders during hot weather.
- **Trace components:** Small fractions of **ethene, propene, butenes, and odorants** (usually ethyl mercaptan — added so leaks can be smelt).

> [!TIP]
> In India, the typical winter blend is **~70 % Propane + 30 % Butane**, while the summer blend is **~50 % Propane + 50 % Butane**. The 14.2 kg cylinder uses a 60:40 or 50:50 commercial blend.

### 2.2 Why LPG is Considered an "Alternate Fuel"

LPG qualifies as an **alternate fuel** because it satisfies the following engineering criteria:

1. **Lower emissions** — produces **15–20 % less CO₂** per unit energy than gasoline.
2. **Higher octane rating** — allows **higher compression ratios** in SI engines.
3. **Lower particulate matter** — burns cleanly without soot formation.
4. **No lead requirement** — unlike gasoline, no tetraethyl lead is needed.
5. **Renewable in part** — can be derived from **biomass via Fischer–Tropsch** processes.

### 2.3 Production Methods of LPG (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Method | Source | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Natural Gas Processing** | Associated gas from oil wells | Removes heavier hydrocarbons (C₃+) using absorption/distillation columns. |
| **Crude Oil Refining** | Catalytic & thermal crackers | LPG is separated in the **LPG Recovery Unit** via de-ethaniser, de-propaniser, de-butaniser. |
| **Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** | Heavy petroleum fractions | Produces LPG with high olefin content. |
| **Steam Cracking** | Naphtha / Ethane | By-product of ethylene manufacture. |
| **Bio-LPG (Emerging)** | Biomass via HVO/Fischer-Tropsch | Carbon-neutral pathway, gaining importance. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** that KTU examiners love to test in numerical and Part A questions. Memorize them and practice their application.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ for pure Propane | $5\,\text{O}_2$ per mol C₃H₈ | kmol basis |
| 2 | Stoichiometric O₂ for pure Butane | $6.5\,\text{O}_2$ per mol C₄H₁₀ | kmol basis |
| 3 | Air requirement (kmol) per kmol fuel | $n_{\text{O}_2} \times (1 + 3.76)$ | Uses $N_2/O_2 = 3.76$ |
| 4 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) Propane | $\dfrac{5 \times 32 \times 4.76}{44}$ | $\approx 15.6$ |
| 5 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) Butane | $\dfrac{6.5 \times 32 \times 4.76}{58}$ | $\approx 15.4$ |
| 6 | Mass of air per kg fuel | $\left(\dfrac{\text{O}_2 \text{ required} \times 100}{23.2}\right)$ | % O₂ by mass = 23.2 |
| 7 | Energy density of LPG | $\rho_{\text{liq}} \times \text{GCV}$ | ~22 MJ/L |
| 8 | Combustion efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{\text{GCV} - \text{losses}}{\text{GCV}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$ | $\phi = 1$ stoichiometric |
| 10 | Volume reduction (gas → liquid) | $V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{gas}}}{260}$ | At 15 °C, 1 atm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** LPG's volumetric energy density (~**22 MJ/L** as liquid) is comparable to gasoline (~**32 MJ/L**), making it a near-drop-in fuel with the added benefit of clean combustion. The 1/260 volume shrinkage is why LPG cylinders look small yet last a long time.

### 2.5 Storage, Handling & Safety — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LPG cylinders** are made of **cold-rolled steel** with a 5 mm wall, designed for a **hydrostatic test pressure of 30 bar**.
- **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** opens at ~17 bar to prevent rupture in fire/excess heat.
- **Odorization:** Pure LPG is odourless; **ethyl mercaptan (1.5 ppm)** is added to detect leaks.
- **Transportation:** Bulk LPG is shipped via **road tankers, rail tank cars, and cryogenic ships** (only at low temperatures for intercontinental trade).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Required for Pure Propane Combustion

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation.**

We begin with the generic hydrocarbon combustion equation. For propane (C₃H₈), the molecular formula indicates **3 carbon atoms** and **8 hydrogen atoms** per molecule.

$$C_3H_8 + a\,O_2 \longrightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Balance the oxygen atoms.**
On the right-hand side: 3 × 2 (from CO₂) + 4 × 1 (from H₂O) = 6 + 4 = 10 oxygen atoms.
Since O₂ has 2 atoms per molecule, $a = 10/2 = 5$.

$$C_3H_8 + 5\,O_2 \longrightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O$$

**Step 3 — Incorporate nitrogen from air.**
By volumetric analysis, air contains **21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume**. Hence, the molar ratio $N_2/O_2 = 79/21 = 3.76$.

$$C_3H_8 + 5\,O_2 + 5 \times 3.76\,N_2 \longrightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O + 18.8\,N_2$$

$$C_3H_8 + 5\,O_2 + 18.8\,N_2 \longrightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O + 18.8\,N_2$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the mass of air per kmol of propane.**

Molecular weights: $C_3H_8 = 44,\ O_2 = 32,\ N_2 = 28$.

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = 5 \times 32 = 160\ \text{kg}$$

$$\text{Mass of N}_2 = 18.8 \times 28 = 526.4\ \text{kg}$$

$$\text{Total mass of air} = 160 + 526.4 = 686.4\ \text{kg per kmol of C}_3H_8$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis).**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{686.4}{44} = 15.60\ \text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using the 23.2 % rule:** Air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass.
> $\dfrac{160}{0.232} = 689.66\ \text{kg air}$ and $\dfrac{689.66}{44} = 15.67 \approx 15.6$. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Required for Pure Butane Combustion

**Step 1 — Balance the equation.**

$$C_4H_{10} + 6.5\,O_2 \longrightarrow 4\,CO_2 + 5\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Add nitrogen.**

$$C_4H_{10} + 6.5\,O_2 + 24.44\,N_2 \longrightarrow 4\,CO_2 + 5\,H_2O + 24.44\,N_2$$

**Step 3 — Compute A/F (mass basis).**

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = 6.5 \times 32 = 208\ \text{kg}$$

$$\text{Mass of N}_2 = 24.44 \times 28 = 684.32\ \text{kg}$$

$$\text{Total air mass} = 208 + 684.32 = 892.32\ \text{kg per kmol of C}_4H_{10}$$

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{892.32}{58} = 15.38\ \text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Air Required for Commercial LPG (60 % Propane + 40 % Butane by Mass)

This is the type of calculation examiners love to ask in the 14-mark problems. Let us compute the A/F ratio for a 60:40 blend.

**Step 1 — Basis: 1 kg of commercial LPG.**

$$\text{Mass of Propane} = 0.60\ \text{kg},\quad \text{Mass of Butane} = 0.40\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric air per kg of pure component.**

For propane: $A_p = 15.60\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}$.
For butane: $A_b = 15.38\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}$.

**Step 3 — Weighted average.**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{blend}} = 0.60 \times 15.60 + 0.40 \times 15.38$$

$$= 9.36 + 6.152 = 15.512\ \text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{blend}} \approx 15.51}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Combustion Products Mole Fractions

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a ready-reference comparison of dry flue-gas compositions for stoichiometric combustion of propane and butane. This matrix is high-yield for viva and Part A questions.

| Component | Moles from Propane (C₃H₈ + 5O₂ + 18.8N₂) | Mole Fraction | Moles from Butane (C₄H₁₀ + 6.5O₂ + 24.44N₂) | Mole Fraction |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **CO₂** | 3 | 0.1163 | 4 | 0.1196 |
| **H₂O** | 4 | 0.1550 | 5 | 0.1495 |
| **N₂** | 18.8 | 0.7287 | 24.44 | 0.7309 |
| **Total products** | 25.8 | 1.0000 | 33.44 | 1.0000 |

### 3.5 Sample Numerical — Volume Reduction from Gas to Liquid

> **Problem (KTU-style):** Calculate the volume of liquid propane formed from 1 m³ of propane gas at 1 atm and 15 °C.

**Step 1 — Find moles of propane in the gas phase using ideal gas law.**

$$PV = nRT \quad\Rightarrow\quad n = \dfrac{PV}{RT} = \dfrac{101325 \times 1}{8.314 \times 288.15} = 42.27\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mass.**

$$m = n \times M = 42.27 \times 44 = 1859.88\ \text{g} = 1.860\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute liquid volume using liquid density (0.51 g/cm³).**

$$V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{1859.88\ \text{g}}{0.51\ \text{g/cm}^3} = 3647\ \text{cm}^3 = 3.647\ \text{L}$$

**Step 4 — Volume reduction ratio.**

$$\dfrac{V_{\text{gas}}}{V_{\text{liq}}} = \dfrac{1000\ \text{L}}{3.647\ \text{L}} \approx 274$$

$$\boxed{\text{Volume shrinks by a factor of } \approx 274\ \text{times.}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture: LPG Production and Supply Chain

The following diagram represents the **end-to-end architecture** of LPG production, distribution, and end-use, mapped as a functional block-level flow rather than a physical drawing. This is the safest and most effective way to present the LPG value chain in a Mermaid-compilable format.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil and Natural Gas Wells] --> B[Gas Processing Plant / Oil Refinery]
    B --> C[De-ethaniser Column]
    C --> D[De-propaniser Column]
    D --> E[De-butaniser Column]
    E --> F[Propane Fraction]
    E --> G[Butane Fraction]
    F --> H[Blending Unit - Winter or Summer Grade]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Odorisation with Ethyl Mercaptan]
    I --> J[Storage in Pressure Spheres and Horton Spheres]
    J --> K[Road Tanker Loading]
    J --> L[Rail Tanker Loading]
    J --> M[Pipeline Distribution]
    K --> N[Refilling Plants and Bottling Plants]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[14.2 kg Domestic Cylinder]
    N --> P[19 kg Commercial Cylinder]
    N --> Q[47.5 kg Industrial Cylinder]
    N --> R[Autogas Tank for Vehicles]
    O --> S[Domestic Cooking Heating]
    P --> S
    Q --> T[Industrial Boilers and Furnaces]
    R --> U[SI Engine Autogas Vehicles]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: LPG Combustion Process in a Burner / Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[LPG Cylinder at 7 bar Liquid] --> S2[Pressure Regulator Reduces to 0.03 bar]
    S2 --> S3[Vaporiser - Liquid to Gas Phase]
    S3 --> S4[Venturi Mixer - Air and LPG Mix]
    S4 --> S5[Burner or Combustion Chamber]
    S5 --> S6[Flame Zone at 1900 degree Celsius]
    S6 --> S7[Combustion Products: CO2 plus H2O plus N2]
    S7 --> S8[Flue Gas to Atmosphere]
    S5 --> S9[Heat Transfer to Cooking Pot / Engine Piston]
    S9 --> S10[Useful Work Output]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture: LPG vs. CNG vs. LNG vs. Biogas

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison matrix doubles as a **revision diagram**. Examiners often include a 7-mark question asking the student to compare LPG with CNG.

| Property | LPG | CNG | LNG | Biogas |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Main constituent** | C₃H₈ and C₄H₁₀ | CH₄ (~ 90 %) | CH₄ (~ 95 %) | CH₄ + CO₂ |
| **Storage state** | Liquid (under pressure) | Compressed gas (200 bar) | Cryogenic liquid (−162 °C) | Gas at low pressure |
| **Energy density (MJ/L)** | ~22 | ~9 | ~22 | ~0.022 |
| **Pressure in storage** | 7–8 bar | 200–250 bar | ~1 atm (cryogenic) | ~0.001 bar |
| **Best use** | Domestic + Autogas | Vehicles | Long-distance transport | Rural cooking + power |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define LPG. List any **two physical properties** and **two advantages** of LPG as an automotive fuel.

**Model Answer:**

LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) is a mixture of propane (C₃H₈) and butane (C₄H₁₀), stored as a liquid under moderate pressure and used in the gaseous phase for combustion.

**Two physical properties:**
- Boiling point of propane: **−42 °C**; butane: **−0.5 °C**
- Density of liquid propane: **0.51 g/cm³** at 15 °C
- Auto-ignition temperature: **450 °C** (propane)
- Calorific value: ~**46.3 MJ/kg** (propane)

**Two advantages as an automotive fuel:**
- Higher octane rating (~105 for propane) — permits higher compression ratios and hence higher thermal efficiency.
- Cleaner combustion — **15–20 % less CO₂** and negligible particulate matter compared to gasoline.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Why is **ethyl mercaptan** added to LPG? What is the typical storage pressure of domestic LPG cylinders?

**Model Answer:**

**Why ethyl mercaptan is added:**
LPG in its pure form is **odourless**, making leak detection difficult and dangerous. A small quantity (~**1.5 ppm**) of **ethyl mercaptan (C₂H₅SH)** — a smelly, sulfur-containing compound — is added so that any leak can be detected by smell, even at very low concentrations. This is a critical **safety requirement** under the Indian Standard IS 4576.

**Storage pressure of domestic LPG cylinders:**
Domestic LPG cylinders (14.2 kg) are filled to about **85 % of their water capacity** and operated at a vapour pressure of **7–8 bar (gauge)** at 25 °C. A **Pressure Relief Valve** (PRV) is fitted, which opens at **17 bar** during fire exposure to prevent explosion.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of balanced chemical equations, derive the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis)** for the complete combustion of **pure propane**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A domestic LPG cylinder contains a blend of **60 % propane and 40 % n-butane by mass**. Calculate: (i) the mass of stoichiometric air required per kg of this blend, (ii) the **air required per m³ of gas** at NTP, and (iii) the **percentage composition** of dry flue gases by volume. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Balanced equation.**

$$C_3H_8 + 5\,O_2 + 5 \times 3.76\,N_2 \longrightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O + 18.8\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Mass of air per kmol of propane.**

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = 5 \times 32 = 160\ \text{kg}$$
$$\text{Mass of N}_2 = 18.8 \times 28 = 526.4\ \text{kg}$$
$$\text{Total air mass} = 160 + 526.4 = 686.4\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — A/F (mass basis) for C₃H₈ (M = 44 kg/kmol).**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{686.4}{44} = 15.60$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = 15.60\ \text{kg air / kg propane}}$$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**(i) Mass-based A/F for the blend:**

For propane: $A_p = 15.60\ \text{kg air/kg}$
For butane: $A_b = 15.38\ \text{kg air/kg}$ (derived in Section 3.2)

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{blend}} = 0.60 \times 15.60 + 0.40 \times 15.38$$
$$= 9.36 + 6.152 = 15.512\ \text{kg air / kg blend}$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{blend}} = 15.51\ \text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

**(ii) Air required per m³ of gas at NTP:**

Average molecular weight of blend:

$$M_{\text{blend}} = 0.60 \times 44 + 0.40 \times 58 = 26.4 + 23.2 = 49.6\ \text{kg/kmol}$$

Density of gas at NTP:

$$\rho = \dfrac{49.6}{22.414} = 2.213\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

Air per m³:

$$A_{\text{per m}^3} = 15.512 \times 2.213 = 34.32\ \text{kg air / m}^3 \text{ of gas}$$

**(iii) Percentage composition of dry flue gases (by volume):**

Propane combustion: produces 3 CO₂ + 18.8 N₂ per kmol.
Butane combustion: produces 4 CO₂ + 24.44 N₂ per kmol.

Dry products (H₂O removed) per kmol of blend:

- CO₂: $0.60 \times 3 + 0.40 \times 4 = 1.80 + 1.60 = 3.40$ kmol
- N₂: $0.60 \times 18.8 + 0.40 \times 24.44 = 11.28 + 9.776 = 21.056$ kmol
- **Total dry products: $3.40 + 21.056 = 24.456$ kmol**

$$\%\,CO_2 = \dfrac{3.40}{24.456} \times 100 = 13.90\,\%$$

$$\%\,N_2 = \dfrac{21.056}{24.456} \times 100 = 86.10\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Dry flue gas: } 13.90\,\%\,CO_2 + 86.10\,\%\,N_2}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **production of LPG** from crude oil refining with a neat block diagram. List any **three properties** of LPG. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 14.2 kg domestic LPG cylinder initially contains liquid LPG. After **25 days of use**, the cylinder mass is reduced to 4.6 kg. If the average GCV of the blend is **46 MJ/kg** and the burner efficiency is **55 %**, determine: (i) total energy released, (ii) useful heat output in MJ, and (iii) the average daily cooking energy consumption in kWh. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

LPG is recovered during crude oil refining via the following sequence:

1. **Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation** of crude oil separates naphtha, kerosene, diesel, and residue.
2. **Catalytic Cracking / FCC** of heavy fractions produces lighter hydrocarbons.
3. **LPG Recovery Section** uses a **De-ethaniser → De-propaniser → De-butaniser** distillation sequence to separate C₂, C₃, and C₄ fractions.
4. **Propane and Butane** are blended in desired ratio (winter: ~70:30; summer: ~50:50).
5. **Odorization** with ethyl mercaptan.
6. **Storage** in pressurized spheres and distribution via road tankers / cylinders.

**Three properties of LPG:**

- **Calorific value: ~46.3 MJ/kg** (propane) — high energy density.
- **Auto-ignition temperature: 450 °C** (propane) — safe to handle.
- **Octane rating: ~105** (propane) — permits high compression ratios in SI engines.
- **Density (liquid): 0.51–0.58 g/cm³** — about half the density of water, so it floats on water.
- **Flammability limits: 2.1 %–9.6 %** in air — narrow but adequate range.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**(i) Total LPG consumed:**

$$m_{\text{LPG}} = 14.2 - 4.6 = 9.6\ \text{kg}$$

Total energy released:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = m \times \text{GCV} = 9.6 \times 46 = 441.6\ \text{MJ}$$

**(ii) Useful heat output (with 55 % efficiency):**

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = 0.55 \times 441.6 = 242.88\ \text{MJ}$$

**(iii) Average daily energy in kWh:**

$$E_{\text{daily}} = \dfrac{242.88}{25} = 9.715\ \text{MJ/day}$$

Converting MJ → kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$E_{\text{daily}} = \dfrac{9.715}{3.6} = 2.699\ \text{kWh/day}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Average daily cooking energy} \approx 2.70\ \text{kWh/day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Students Lose Marks Here:**
> 1. **Don't forget the 3.76 factor** for N₂ in air when writing the combustion equation. Marks deducted if you write O₂ alone.
> 2. **For blends**, ALWAYS use **mass-weighted average** — never mol-weighted — because the problem statement explicitly says "by mass".
> 3. **Dry flue gas composition** requires you to **exclude H₂O** — forgetting this step costs a full mark.
> 4. **Units consistency:** Always write 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ in the conversion step. Many students write 3.6 without units and lose half a mark.
> 5. **Cylinder filling rule:** Domestic cylinders are filled to 85 % of water capacity, not 100 %. Mention this in any safety/numericals question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- **Definition:** LPG = Propane (C₃H₈) + Butane (C₄H₁₀), stored as liquid under pressure, used as gas.
- **Primary constituents:** C₃H₈ (~50–70 %) and C₄H₁₀ (~30–50 %).
- **Domestic cylinder:** **14.2 kg**, filled to **85 % capacity**, pressure **7–8 bar gauge**.
- **Storage pressure:** 7–8 bar at 25 °C; PRV opens at 17 bar.
- **Density (liquid):** 0.51 (propane) — 0.58 g/cm³ (butane).
- **Calorific value (GCV):** ~46.3 MJ/kg (propane), ~45.7 MJ/kg (butane).
- **Auto-ignition temperature:** 450 °C (propane), 405 °C (butane).
- **Octane rating:** 105 (propane) — enables high compression ratio.
- **Volume reduction gas → liquid:** ~1/260 to 1/274 times.
- **Stoichiometric A/F (mass):** ~15.6 (propane), ~15.4 (butane), ~15.5 (60:40 blend).
- **Stoichiometric O₂:** 5 mol O₂ per mol C₃H₈; 6.5 mol O₂ per mol C₄H₁₀.
- **Combustion products:** CO₂ + H₂O + N₂; dry flue gas is **~13–14 % CO₂, ~86–87 % N₂**.
- **Odorant:** Ethyl mercaptan (~1.5 ppm) — added for safety.
- **Production:** Natural gas processing + crude oil refining (FCC, steam cracking).
- **Air-Fuel ratio definition:** $\phi = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}}/(A/F)_{\text{actual}}$; $\phi = 1$ → stoichiometric.
- **Energy of 14.2 kg cylinder:** ~14.2 × 46 = **653 MJ** input; useful = 0.55 × 653 ≈ **359 MJ** (matches ~30 days of cooking).
- **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** — conversion factor used in energy calculations.
- **N₂/O₂ ratio in air** = 3.76 (volume), O₂ = 23.2 % (mass).
- **Flammability limits in air:** 2.1 % to 9.6 % by volume.
- **Hazard:** Heavier than air → leaks settle at low levels → never store cylinder in basements.
- **Comparison hook:** LPG = high energy density, CNG = high pressure gas, LNG = cryogenic, Biogas = renewable.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever an LPG numerical appears, **always start with the balanced equation** for the *given* fuel. Examiners award 2–3 marks purely for the correct equation and the proper use of 3.76 for nitrogen. Skipping this step is the most common cause of losing marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Liquid Alternate Fuels - Alcohol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Liquid Alternate Fuels - Alcohol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Alcohol Fuels?

**Alcohols** are organic compounds characterized by one or more hydroxyl groups (–OH) attached to a carbon atom. In the context of **Alternative Fuels for Internal Combustion (IC) Engines**, two alcohols are of primary commercial and academic interest:

1. **Methanol (CH₃OH)** — also called *methyl alcohol* or *wood alcohol*
2. **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** — also called *ethyl alcohol* or *grain alcohol*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Alcohols are oxygenated liquid fuels produced from biomass, fossil fuels, or synthesis gas. They are considered **renewable** (when biomass-derived) and **oxygenated** (carry their own oxygen in the molecular structure), which makes them excellent anti-knock fuels with cleaner combustion profiles.

### Chemical Structure Snapshot

| Property | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight (kg/kmol) | **32.04** | **46.07** |
| Oxygen Content (mass %) | **49.9%** | **34.7%** |
| Carbon Atoms | 1 | 2 |
| Hydrogens | 4 | 6 |
| Source | Natural gas, coal, biomass | Sugarcane, corn, cellulosic waste |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Lung-Bearing Fuel" Analogy:** Imagine a piece of wood (pure carbon) trying to burn — it needs a *huge* external supply of oxygen to sustain combustion, and it leaves behind lots of soot (unburnt carbon). Now, imagine the same wood but pre-mixed with built-in oxygen molecules inside its very structure — that's an alcohol! The –OH group acts like a **miniature oxygen tank** embedded inside the fuel itself. This is why alcohol burns more *completely*, with **less smoke, lower carbon monoxide (CO)**, and improved thermal efficiency. The downside? That extra oxygen means *less combustible carbon*, so the energy content per kilogram is lower than gasoline.

---

## 1.3 Why Alcohols? — The KTU Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Highlight:** Alcohols are categorized as **"First-Generation Liquid Biofuels"** and are widely discussed in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 under *Liquid Alternate Fuels*. Students must understand:
> - Source pathways (biomass vs. synthetic)
> - Suitability for **SI engines** (primary) and **CI engines** (with ignition improvers)
> - Blend behavior with gasoline and diesel
> - Merits and demerits versus conventional fuels

### Real-World Context

- **Brazil** has run a national **E22 to E25 (22–25% ethanol in gasoline)** program since the 1970s — over 40 million flex-fuel vehicles operate today.
- **USA** mandates **E10 (10% ethanol)** nationwide and pushes **E85** flex-fuel.
- **India** launched the **E20 (20% ethanol blending)** program in 2025.
- **China** runs massive **Methanol-to-Gasoline (MTG)** and direct methanol vehicle fleets, especially in Shanxi province.
- **Sweden, Germany** use **ED95** (95% ethanol) in modified CI engines for heavy transport.

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

Alcohols can be broadly classified based on carbon chain length and hydroxyl position. For KTU, the focus is squarely on **C1 (Methanol)** and **C2 (Ethanol)** because:

- They are **liquid at room temperature** (BP: 64.7°C for methanol, 78.4°C for ethanol)
- They are **fully miscible** with gasoline in all proportions
- They have **high octane ratings** (Methanol ~114 RON, Ethanol ~108 RON)
- They are **commercially available** in large volumes

> [!NOTE]
> Higher alcohols (propanol, butanol) are emerging but are *out of KTU 2024 Module 2 scope*. They appear only as a passing reference in advanced electives.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Production Pathways

### A. Production of Methanol (CH₃OH)

Methanol can be produced through **three primary industrial routes**:

#### Route 1: From Natural Gas (Steam Reforming — most common, ~90% global)

**Step 1 — Synthesis Gas Generation:**
$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift Reaction:**
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Methanol Synthesis (Catalytic, Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃, 50–100 bar, 220–300°C):**
$$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \quad \Delta H = -90.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -49.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomass-to-Methanol:** Biomass is gasified to syngas (CO + H₂), then the same catalytic synthesis step applies — this is called **biomethanol** and qualifies as renewable.

#### Route 2: From Coal (Coal Gasification)
- Coal → syngas (CO + H₂) → methanol
- Common in **China** due to abundant coal reserves
- Higher CO₂ footprint than natural gas route

#### Route 3: From CO₂ Hydrogenation (Emerging, "Green Methanol")
$$\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- Uses captured CO₂ + green H₂ (from electrolysis)
- **Carbon-negative** potential

---

### B. Production of Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

Ethanol is overwhelmingly produced from **biomass via fermentation**. The pathway is:

#### Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation
- **Sugar feedstocks** (sugarcane juice, sugar beet, molasses): Ready for fermentation directly
- **Starch feedstocks** (corn, wheat, cassava): Require **hydrolysis** to break starch into sugars
- **Cellulosic feedstocks** (agricultural waste, wood, grasses): Require **acid/enzymatic hydrolysis** to break cellulose → sugars

#### Step 2 — Hydrolysis (for starch/cellulose)
$$(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n + n\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{acid/enzyme}} n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$
- For starch: α-amylase + glucoamylase convert starch → glucose
- For cellulose: cellulase enzymes break β-1,4 glycosidic bonds

#### Step 3 — Fermentation (Yeast: *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*)
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast, 25-35°C}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -69 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

#### Step 4 — Distillation & Dehydration
- Fermentation broth contains only ~10–12% ethanol (yeast dies at higher concentrations)
- **Distillation** raises concentration to ~95% (azeotrope with water)
- **Molecular sieve dehydration** (or azeotropic distillation with benzene) yields anhydrous ethanol (100%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anhydrous ethanol** is mandatory for fuel blending. Hydrated ethanol (>0.5% water) causes **phase separation** in gasoline-ethanol blends, especially at low temperatures.

---

## 2.2 Combustion Chemistry — The Stoichiometric Core

### Combustion Equations

**Methanol Combustion:**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Ethanol Combustion:**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric)

For complete combustion, the stoichiometric **air-fuel ratio by mass** is calculated as:

$$A/F_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{\text{O}_2 \text{ required (mol)} \times 32 \times (100/21)}{M_{\text{fuel}} \times 4.76}$$

The general atomic balance for a fuel **CₓHᵧOᵤ**:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_u + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{u}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

So the moles of O₂ required:
$$n_{\text{O}_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{u}{2}$$

**Methanol** (x=1, y=4, u=1):
$$n_{\text{O}_2} = 1 + \frac{4}{4} - \frac{1}{2} = 1.5 \text{ mol O}_2/\text{mol fuel}$$

**Ethanol** (x=2, y=6, u=1):
$$n_{\text{O}_2} = 2 + \frac{6}{4} - \frac{1}{2} = 3.0 \text{ mol O}_2/\text{mol fuel}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear every KTU exam cycle.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ per mole fuel | $n_{\text{O}_2} = x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{u}{2}$ | mol O₂/mol fuel | For CₓHᵧOᵤ |
| 2 | Stoichiometric A/F (mass) | $(A/F)_s = \dfrac{4.76 \times n_{\text{O}_2} \times 32}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kg air/kg fuel | $M_{\text{fuel}}$ in kg/kmol |
| 3 | Calorific Value (Lower, mass basis) | Look up: CH₃OH ≈ **19.9 MJ/kg**, C₂H₅OH ≈ **26.9 MJ/kg** | MJ/kg | Compared to gasoline ~44 MJ/kg |
| 4 | Calorific Value (volumetric) | $CV_v = CV_m \times \rho_{\text{fuel}}$ | MJ/L | Critical for vehicle range |
| 5 | Energy Efficiency Index | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful work output}}{\text{Fuel energy input}} = \dfrac{\eta_{th} \times CV_m}{3600}$ | — | Unitless |
| 6 | Octane Rating (RON) | Methanol ≈ **114**, Ethanol ≈ **108**, Gasoline ≈ 92 | RON | Higher = more knock-resistant |
| 7 | Cetane Rating | Methanol ≈ **3-5**, Ethanol ≈ **5-8**, Diesel ≈ 45-55 | Cetane | Very poor — needs improver for CI |
| 8 | Latent Heat of Vaporization | Methanol = **1100 kJ/kg**, Ethanol = **900 kJ/kg**, Gasoline = **350 kJ/kg** | kJ/kg | Affects cold start |
| 9 | Density | Methanol = **792 kg/m³**, Ethanol = **789 kg/m³**, Gasoline = **740 kg/m³** | kg/m³ | At 20°C |
| 10 | Stoichiometric AFR | Methanol ≈ **6.45:1**, Ethanol ≈ **9.0:1**, Gasoline ≈ **14.7:1** | — | Mass basis |
| 11 | Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | — | $\lambda < 1$ = rich; $\lambda > 1$ = lean |
| 12 | Energy Content of Blend | $CV_{\text{blend}} = x_1 \cdot CV_1 + x_2 \cdot CV_2$ | MJ/kg | Mass-weighted linear mixing rule |
| 13 | Volumetric Energy Fraction | $\phi_v = \dfrac{V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot CV_f}{V_g \cdot \rho_g \cdot CV_g + V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot CV_f}$ | — | For blend energy content |

---

## 2.4 Physical & Chemical Properties — Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Trinity of Alcohol Properties" that define its engine behavior:**

### A. High Octane Number
- Methanol: **114 RON, 92 MON** (Octane Sensitivity ≈ 22)
- Ethanol: **108 RON, 89 MON** (Octane Sensitivity ≈ 19)
- **Why?** The –OH group raises the auto-ignition temperature and slows radical chain reactions during compression.
- **Engineering impact:** Alcohols allow **higher compression ratios** (12:1 to 14:1 in methanol SI engines) → **higher thermal efficiency** than gasoline.

### B. High Latent Heat of Vaporization
- Methanol: **1100 kJ/kg** (3× gasoline), Ethanol: **900 kJ/kg** (2.5× gasoline)
- **Why?** Strong hydrogen bonding from –OH groups.
- **Engineering impact:**
  - ✅ **Volumetric efficiency improves** (charge cooling effect)
  - ✅ **Peak power increases** (denser intake charge)
  - ❌ **Cold starting is difficult** (charge cooling drops below 0°C → ice formation risk)
  - ❌ **Vapor lock risk** in hot fuel lines

### C. Built-in Oxygen (Oxygenated Nature)
- Methanol: **49.9% O by mass**, Ethanol: **34.7% O by mass**
- **Why?** The –OH group contributes oxygen directly.
- **Engineering impact:**
  - ✅ **Lower CO emissions** (more complete combustion)
  - ✅ **Lower PM (Particulate Matter)** in CI mode
  - ❌ **Lower energy density** (less combustible material per kg)

### D. Low Cetane Number
- Methanol: ~3-5, Ethanol: ~5-8
- **Why?** High octane ↔ low cetane (inversely correlated)
- **Engineering impact:** Alcohols **cannot be used in pure form in CI engines** without ignition improvers. Solutions:
  - **Dual-fuel mode** (pilot diesel ignition)
  - **Cetane improver additives** (DME, DEE, ignition promoter additives)
  - **Spark-assisted diesel** (dual-mode engines)
  - **Fumigation** in intake air

### E. Corrosive Nature
- Methanol/ethanol can corrode **aluminum, zinc, magnesium, certain elastomers** (rubber seals, gaskets).
- **Engineering impact:** Modern alcohol-compatible vehicles use:
  - Stainless steel fuel lines
  - Fluorocarbon (Viton) seals
  - Aluminum-compatible coatings

### F. Low Flame Visibility
- Methanol/ethanol flames are **nearly invisible in daylight**.
- **Safety concern:** Required **fire detection sensors** in pits, garages, and refueling stations.

---

## 2.5 Use of Alcohols in SI Engines

### A. Alcohol-Gasoline Blends

| Blend | Composition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **E10** | 10% ethanol + 90% gasoline | Standard US/EU pump fuel |
| **E22 / E25** | 22–25% ethanol + gasoline | Brazil national fuel |
| **E85** | 85% ethanol + 15% gasoline | Flex-fuel vehicles (FFVs) |
| **M15** | 15% methanol + 85% gasoline | China pilot |
| **M85** | 85% methanol + 15% gasoline | China methanol vehicles |
| **M100** | Pure methanol | Racing (IndyCar, drag) |

### B. Engine Modifications for High Alcohol Blends

For **E85/M85** operation, vehicles need:

1. **Larger fuel injectors** (alcohols need ~1.4–2× the volume of gasoline for same energy)
2. **Re-programmed ECU** (different stoichiometric AFR, cold-start enrichment, fuel map)
3. **Corrosion-resistant materials** (stainless lines, Viton seals)
4. **Cold-start enrichment** system (auxiliary gasoline tank or glow plug for methanol)
5. **Heated fuel rail** (to prevent cold-phase separation in cold climates)

### C. Performance Benefits in SI Engines

- **Higher Compression Ratio** → **+8–12% thermal efficiency** gain
- **Higher Volumetric Efficiency** (charge cooling) → **+5–10% peak power**
- **Lower CO emissions** (oxygenated) → **−40 to −60%**
- **Lower HC emissions** at part load → **−20 to −30%**
- **Higher NOx** at high load (higher flame temperature from charge cooling) → trade-off

---

## 2.6 Use of Alcohols in CI Engines

This is a more **advanced KTU topic** worth 5–7 marks in ESE.

### Methods to Use Alcohols in CI Engines

1. **Blending with Diesel** (limited, max 10–15% ethanol)
   - Phase separation above 15% due to polarity mismatch
   - Emulsifiers required for higher blends

2. **Dual-Fuel Mode (Pilot Injection)**
   - **Pilot:** Diesel (5–10% of energy) injected near TDC
   - **Main:** Alcohol fumigation in intake air or port injection
   - Diesel pilot ignites → alcohol burns as surrounding gas

3. **Alcohol Fumigation**
   - Alcohol vapor injected into intake manifold
   - Mixes with intake air → drawn into cylinder
   - Diesel pilot ignites the alcohol-air mixture

4. **Spark-Assisted Alcohol Engine** (rare, research-stage)
   - Adds spark plug to CI engine → essentially becomes SI
   - Allows high-alcohol operation without diesel

5. **Cetane Improver Additives**
   - Add **2–5% ignition promoter** (e.g., DEE, DME, cyclohexyl nitrate)
   - Raises cetane of blend to ~30–40
   - Limited practical adoption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Point:** The most studied CI approach is **dual-fuel mode** with alcohol fumigation + diesel pilot. The diesel substitution rate can reach **70–80%** without significant efficiency loss, with major PM and CO reductions.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Fuel | Region |
|---|---|---|
| Formula 1 Racing | E85 (since 2010) | Global |
| IndyCar | E85 (since 2006) | USA |
| Heavy transport (city buses) | ED95 (95% ethanol) | Sweden, Germany |
| China methanol taxis | M100 | Shanxi, Guizhou, Shaanxi |
| Brazilian flex-fleet | E22 to E100 | Brazil |
| Indian PSUs (Oil Marketing) | E20 rollout | India (2025+) |
| Ocean-going shipping | Methanol/diesel dual-fuel | Maersk, Stena Line |
| Industrial boilers | Methanol (clean burn) | Chemical industry |

---

## 2.8 Advantages & Disadvantages Summary

### ✅ Advantages of Alcohols as Engine Fuels

1. **Renewable** (biomass-derived) — reduces net CO₂ by **40–80%** vs. gasoline
2. **High octane rating** → enables high CR → higher efficiency
3. **Oxygenated** → cleaner combustion → lower CO, PM, HC
4. **Biodegradable** — non-toxic (ethanol), low environmental persistence
5. **High latent heat** → charge cooling → better volumetric efficiency
6. **Domestic energy security** — produced locally from agriculture
7. **Rural employment** (sugarcane/corn farming supply chain)
8. **Compatible** with existing SI engine technology at low blends (E10)

### ❌ Disadvantages of Alcohols as Engine Fuels

1. **Lower energy density** (about 60% of gasoline by volume for ethanol, 45% for methanol) → **reduced driving range**
2. **Poor cold-start** (high latent heat + low vapor pressure at low T)
3. **Corrosive** to metals and elastomers → material upgrade cost
4. **Hygroscopic** (absorbs water) → fuel system corrosion + phase separation
5. **Low cetane** → unsuitable for pure CI use
6. **Higher aldehyde emissions** (formaldehyde from methanol, acetaldehyde from ethanol) — toxic
7. **Invisible flame** → fire safety hazard
8. **Food vs. fuel debate** (for 1st-gen ethanol from corn/sugarcane)
9. **Requires land/water** for feedstock cultivation → sustainability concern
10. **Production cost** higher than gasoline in most regions (subsidy needed)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This section is the KTU "high-mark" zone.** Examiners love asking derivations of **stoichiometric AFR**, **calorific value comparison**, and **blend energy content**. Master these and you'll secure 12–14 marks on any question on alcohol fuels.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Methanol

### Given:
- Fuel: **Methanol (CH₃OH)**
- Molecular weight: **M = 32.04 kg/kmol**
- Air composition: 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume (mass basis: 23.2% O₂ + 76.8% N₂)

### Step 1 — Write the Balanced Combustion Equation

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + a\,\text{O}_2 + b\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow c\,\text{CO}_2 + d\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + b\,\text{N}_2$$

We need to balance the equation. The generalized fuel form is **CₓHᵧOᵤ** with x=1, y=4, u=1.

### Step 2 — Apply Atomic Conservation

**Carbon balance:**
$$1 = c \implies c = 1 \text{ mol CO}_2$$

**Hydrogen balance:**
$$4 = 2d \implies d = 2 \text{ mol H}_2\text{O}$$

**Oxygen balance** (oxygen in fuel + oxygen in air = oxygen in products):
$$1 \text{ (from CH}_3\text{OH)} + 2a \text{ (from O}_2\text{)} = 2(1) + 1(2)$$
$$1 + 2a = 2 + 2 = 4$$
$$2a = 3$$
$$a = 1.5 \text{ mol O}_2$$

**Nitrogen:** $b = 3.76 \times a = 3.76 \times 1.5 = 5.64$ mol N₂

### Step 3 — Final Balanced Equation

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 + 5.64\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 1\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 5.64\,\text{N}_2$$

### Step 4 — Compute Mass of Air

Mass of O₂ required:
$$m_{\text{O}_2} = 1.5 \times 32 = 48 \text{ kg/kmol fuel}$$

Mass of N₂ accompanying:
$$m_{\text{N}_2} = 5.64 \times 28 = 157.92 \text{ kg/kmol fuel}$$

Total mass of air per kmol of methanol:
$$m_{\text{air}} = m_{\text{O}_2} + m_{\text{N}_2} = 48 + 157.92 = 205.92 \text{ kg air/kmol fuel}$$

### Step 5 — Stoichiometric A/F (Mass Basis)

$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{M_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{205.92}{32.04}$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{stoich, methanol}} = 6.43 : 1 \text{ (kg air/kg fuel)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is much **lower than gasoline (14.7:1)** because methanol already carries ~50% oxygen in its molecule — the air only needs to supply the *remaining* oxygen.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Ethanol

### Given:
- Fuel: **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** = **C₂H₆O**
- Molecular weight: **M = 46.07 kg/kmol**
- Fuel form: CₓHᵧOᵤ with x=2, y=6, u=1

### Step 1 — Apply the General Formula

For CₓHᵧOᵤ:
$$n_{\text{O}_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{u}{2} = 2 + \frac{6}{4} - \frac{1}{2} = 2 + 1.5 - 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ mol O}_2$$

### Step 2 — Balanced Equation

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 + 11.28\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 11.28\,\text{N}_2$$

### Step 3 — Mass Calculations

Mass of O₂: $3 \times 32 = 96$ kg/kmol fuel

Mass of N₂: $11.28 \times 28 = 315.84$ kg/kmol fuel

Total air: $96 + 315.84 = 411.84$ kg air/kmol fuel

### Step 4 — Stoichiometric A/F

$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{411.84}{46.07}$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{stoich, ethanol}} = 8.94 : 1 \text{ (kg air/kg fuel)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison insight:** Ethanol needs **more air** than methanol (8.94 vs. 6.43) because it has more carbon and hydrogen per unit oxygen. But both are still **far below gasoline's 14.7:1**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Energy Density Comparison

A critical KTU question: *"Why does an alcohol-fueled vehicle have lower range than a gasoline vehicle on the same fuel tank volume?"*

### Step 1 — Formula for Volumetric Calorific Value

$$CV_v = CV_m \times \rho_f \quad [\text{MJ/L}]$$

### Step 2 — Plug in Known Values

**Gasoline** (Reference):
- $CV_m = 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $\rho = 0.74 \text{ kg/L}$
- $CV_v = 44 \times 0.74 = 32.56 \text{ MJ/L}$

**Methanol:**
- $CV_m = 19.9 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $\rho = 0.792 \text{ kg/L}$
- $CV_v = 19.9 \times 0.792 = 15.76 \text{ MJ/L}$

**Ethanol:**
- $CV_m = 26.9 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $\rho = 0.789 \text{ kg/L}$
- $CV_v = 26.9 \times 0.789 = 21.22 \text{ MJ/L}$

### Step 3 — Normalize to Gasoline (= 100%)

$$\text{Energy Fraction}_{\text{methanol}} = \frac{15.76}{32.56} \times 100\% = 48.4\%$$

$$\text{Energy Fraction}_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{21.22}{32.56} \times 100\% = 65.2\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{A methanol tank holds only 48.4\% of gasoline's energy per liter.}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{An ethanol tank holds only 65.2\% of gasoline's energy per liter.}}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Implication (Range)

For a 50 L fuel tank:

| Fuel | Energy Stored (MJ) | Approx. Vehicle Range (km) |
|---|---|---|
| Gasoline | 1628 MJ | ~800 km (at 2.0 MJ/km) |
| Methanol | 788 MJ | ~390 km |
| Ethanol | 1061 MJ | ~530 km |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Pure alcohol vehicles need either **larger fuel tanks** (sacrificing cargo space) or **more frequent refueling** (compromising convenience). This is the single biggest reason alcohol fuels are predominantly used as **blends**, not pure fuels, in road transport.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Content of E20 Blend (Mass-Weighted)

A classic KTU problem: *"A fuel blend contains 20% ethanol (by volume) and 80% gasoline (by volume). Determine the calorific value per kg of the blend."*

### Step 1 — Data Collection

| Property | Gasoline | Ethanol |
|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/L) | 0.74 | 0.789 |
| Calorific value (MJ/kg) | 44 | 26.9 |
| Volume fraction in blend | 0.80 | 0.20 |

### Step 2 — Convert to Mass Fractions

Mass of gasoline in 1 L of blend:
$$m_g = 0.80 \times 0.74 = 0.592 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of ethanol in 1 L of blend:
$$m_e = 0.20 \times 0.789 = 0.1578 \text{ kg}$$

Total mass of 1 L of blend:
$$m_{\text{blend}} = 0.592 + 0.1578 = 0.7498 \text{ kg}$$

Mass fraction of gasoline:
$$x_g = \frac{0.592}{0.7498} = 0.7895$$

Mass fraction of ethanol:
$$x_e = \frac{0.1578}{0.7498} = 0.2105$$

**Sanity check:** $x_g + x_e = 0.7895 + 0.2105 = 1.0000$ ✅

### Step 3 — Compute Blend Calorific Value (Mass-Weighted)

$$CV_{\text{blend}} = x_g \cdot CV_g + x_e \cdot CV_e$$
$$CV_{\text{blend}} = (0.7895 \times 44) + (0.2105 \times 26.9)$$
$$CV_{\text{blend}} = 34.738 + 5.662$$
$$\boxed{CV_{\text{blend}} = 40.40 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

### Step 4 — Volumetric CV of Blend

$$CV_{v,\text{blend}} = 40.40 \times 0.7498 = 30.29 \text{ MJ/L}$$

### Step 5 — Comparison with Pure Gasoline

$$\text{Energy Reduction} = \frac{32.56 - 30.29}{32.56} \times 100\% = 6.97\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** A 20% ethanol blend has only **7% lower volumetric energy** than pure gasoline. This is why E10/E20 are widely accepted in unmodified vehicles — the **range penalty is acceptable**, but pure alcohol (M100/E100) would be unacceptable without major redesign.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Air Requirement for E85 Blend

A slightly trickier derivation: *"Find the stoichiometric A/F ratio of E85 (85% ethanol + 15% gasoline by volume) at standard conditions."*

### Step 1 — Volume → Mass Conversion

In 1 L of E85:
- Ethanol: $0.85 \times 0.789 = 0.6707 \text{ kg}$
- Gasoline: $0.15 \times 0.74 = 0.111 \text{ kg}$
- Total mass: $m_{\text{E85}} = 0.6707 + 0.111 = 0.7817 \text{ kg/L}$

### Step 2 — Compute Stoichiometric O₂ (Per kg of E85)

Moles of O₂ needed per mole of fuel — first per kg:

For ethanol: $3.0$ mol O₂ per mol, $\Rightarrow \dfrac{3.0}{46.07} = 0.0651$ kmol O₂ per kg

For gasoline (assumed C₈H₁₈, M = 114):
$$n_{\text{O}_2, \text{gas}} = 8 + \frac{18}{4} - 0 = 12.5 \text{ mol O}_2/\text{mol fuel}$$
$$\Rightarrow \frac{12.5}{114} = 0.1096 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg}$$

For E85 (per kg of blend):
- O₂ from ethanol: $0.6707 \times 0.0651 = 0.04369$ kmol O₂
- O₂ from gasoline: $0.111 \times 0.1096 = 0.01217$ kmol O₂
- Total O₂: $0.04369 + 0.01217 = 0.05586$ kmol O₂ per kg of E85

### Step 3 — Air Required

$$m_{\text{air}} = 0.05586 \times 4.76 \times 32 = 8.51 \text{ kg air per kg E85}$$

### Step 4 — Stoichiometric A/F

$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich, E85}} = \frac{8.51}{1.0}$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{stoich, E85}} \approx 8.51 : 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the published experimental value of **~8.5:1** for E85. The reduction from gasoline's 14.7:1 is due to the **oxygen carrier effect** of ethanol. ECU calibration must use this lower A/F as the stoichiometric reference for the closed-loop O₂ sensor.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Material Balance for Methanol Synthesis

**Problem:** A methanol plant processes 1000 kmol/h of syngas (CO + H₂) with a stoichiometric ratio of CO:H₂ = 1:2. If 35% of the CO is converted, calculate the methanol output and the heat released per hour.

### Step 1 — Molar Inflow Analysis

If syngas is CO:H₂ = 1:2, then per kmol of syngas:
- CO: $\frac{1}{3}$ kmol, H₂: $\frac{2}{3}$ kmol
- For 1000 kmol/h syngas: CO in = $\frac{1000}{3} = 333.33$ kmol/h, H₂ in = $\frac{2000}{3} = 666.67$ kmol/h

### Step 2 — CO Converted

CO converted = $0.35 \times 333.33 = 116.67$ kmol/h

By stoichiometry: $\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$

- H₂ consumed: $2 \times 116.67 = 233.33$ kmol/h
- Methanol produced: $116.67$ kmol/h

### Step 3 — Methanol Mass Output

$$\dot{m}_{\text{MeOH}} = 116.67 \times 32.04 = 3737.7 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{MeOH}} = 3.74 \text{ t/h} \approx 89.7 \text{ t/day}}$$

### Step 4 — Heat Released (Reaction Enthalpy)

Reaction enthalpy: $\Delta H = -90.7$ kJ/mol = $-90,700$ kJ/kmol

$$\dot{Q} = 116.67 \times 90,700 = 1.058 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 10{,}580 \text{ MJ/h} = 2.94 \text{ MW thermal power}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 2.94 MW of heat is recovered in the methanol reactor's heat exchanger to preheat incoming syngas. **Industrial energy efficiency depends heavily on recovering this exothermic heat.**
