# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Methanol Production Flowchart (Steam Reforming Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Natural Gas Feedstock CH4] --> B1[Desulfurization Unit]
    B1 --> C1[Steam Reformer Reactor 850°C, 25 bar]
    C2[Steam Supply H2O, 3:1 ratio] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Syngas Output CO + 3H2]
    D1 --> E1[Water-Gas Shift Reactor 250°C]
    E1 --> F1[Adjusted Syngas CO2 + H2 + CO]
    F1 --> G1[Syngas Compressor to 75 bar]
    G1 --> H1[Methanol Synthesis Reactor 240°C, Cu/ZnO Catalyst]
    H1 --> I1[Crude Methanol Liquid + Unreacted Gas]
    I1 --> J1[Gas-Liquid Separator]
    J1 --> K1[Recycle Unreacted Gas back to H1]
    J1 --> L1[Distillation Column 1: Light Ends Removal]
    L1 --> M1[Distillation Column 2: Pure Methanol Refining]
    M1 --> N1[Anhydrous Methanol CH3OH 99.9%]
    N1 --> O1[Storage Tank]
    O1 --> P1[Distribution: Fuel / Chemical Feedstock]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip:** Follow top-to-bottom. The **recycle loop** (J1 → H1) is critical — it pushes the per-pass conversion of CO from ~35% to **overall conversion >98%**, dramatically improving yield.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Ethanol Production from Sugarcane (Brazil Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Sugarcane Harvest] --> B2[Mill and Juice Extraction]
    B2 --> C2[Bagasse - Lignocellulosic Solid]
    B2 --> D2[Sugarcane Juice Sucrose Solution]
    C2 --> C2A[Boiler for Process Steam and Power]
    C2A --> C2B[Cogeneration Electricity]
    D2 --> E2[Fermenter Vessel 30°C, 24-72 hours]
    E2 --> E2A[Yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae]
    E2A --> E2
    E2 --> F2[Beer Solution 8-12% Ethanol]
    F2 --> G2[Distillation Column 1: Hydrous Ethanol 95%]
    G2 --> H2[Dehydration System: Molecular Sieve 3A]
    H2 --> I2[Anhydrous Ethanol 99.5% plus]
    I2 --> I2A[E100 Anhydrous Fuel Grade Ethanol]
    I2A --> J2[Blending with Gasoline]
    J2 --> K2[E22 to E25 Hydrous Ethanol in Gasoline]
    J2 --> L2[E85 or E100 Anhydrous for Flex Fuel Vehicles]
    F2 --> M2[CO2 Byproduct Captured]
    M2 --> N2[Carbonation Industry or Greenhouse Enrichment]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Self-Sufficiency:** In the Brazilian sugarcane model, **bagasse (the fibrous residue) is burned** to provide ALL process steam and electricity for the plant. The system is **energy-self-sufficient** — a major reason Brazilian ethanol has the lowest production cost globally (~$0.30/L).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Engine Block Diagram: Alcohol Dual-Fuel CI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intake Air Filter] --> B[Supercharger Optional]
    B --> C[Alcohol Vaporizer Fumigation Port]
    C --> D[Intake Manifold]
    E[Alcohol Storage Tank] --> F[Alcohol Pump]
    F --> G[Alcohol Flow Meter]
    G --> C
    H[Diesel Tank] --> I[Diesel Fuel Pump]
    I --> J[High Pressure Diesel Injector 1500-2000 bar]
    K[ECU - Dual Fuel Controller] --> G
    K --> J
    D --> L[Cylinder Piston Assembly]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Diesel Pilot Injection 5-10% energy, near TDC]
    M --> N[Combustion: Diesel Ignites, Alcohol Burns]
    N --> O[Piston Work Output]
    O --> P[Crankshaft]
    P --> Q[Flywheel to Drivetrain]
    L --> R[Exhaust Manifold]
    R --> S[Turbine Optional Turbocharger]
    S --> B
    R --> T[Exhaust: Lower CO, Lower PM, Higher NOx]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key operating principle:** The diesel pilot (M) ignites **first**, creating a hot kernel of burning diesel. This kernel then ignites the surrounding alcohol-air mixture (N). The alcohol is NOT auto-ignited by compression heat — it relies on the **diesel flame front**. This is why **alcohol percentage can be 70-80% of energy content** without knocking or misfire.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — E85 Flex-Fuel Vehicle Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[E85 Fuel Tank - Larger Volume Needed] --> B1[High Pressure Fuel Pump]
    B1 --> C1[Heated Fuel Rail - Prevents Phase Separation]
    C1 --> D1[Port Fuel Injectors 1.4x to 2x Gasoline Size]
    D1 --> E1[Engine Cylinders - Higher Compression Ratio 12:1 typical]
    E1 --> F1[Intake Manifold with Cold Start Injector]
    F1 --> G1[Gasoline Auxiliary Tank for Cold Start]
    G1 --> F1
    E1 --> H1[Ignition System - High Energy Coils for Lean Burn]
    H1 --> E1
    I1[Lambda Oxygen Sensor] --> J1[ECU Flex Fuel Calibration Map]
    J1 --> D1
    J1 --> H1
    J1 --> C1
    K1[Fuel Composition Sensor - Ethanol Content] --> J1
    D1 --> J1
    L1[Exhaust After-Treatment] --> M1[Catalytic Converter - Optimized for Alcohol]
    M1 --> N1[Final Emissions Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key adaptations vs. gasoline car:**
> 1. **Larger fuel injectors** (alcohols need ~1.5× the volumetric flow for same energy)
> 2. **Fuel composition sensor** (continuously measures ethanol % using dielectric constant / refractive index)
> 3. **Heated fuel rail** (prevents water absorption-induced phase separation in cold climates)
> 4. **Higher CR** (12:1 instead of 10:1 in gasoline) — exploits high octane of alcohol
> 5. **Auxiliary gasoline tank** (solves cold-start problem by injecting gasoline at <10°C)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions per module, each carrying **3 marks**. Total Part A = 20 marks across the paper (any 5 of 8 attempted). Each answer should be 3-5 sentences with a labeled diagram if needed.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between methanol and ethanol as alternative fuels. Mention their respective sources.**

**Model Answer:**

Methanol (CH₃OH) and Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) are the two principal alcohol fuels used in IC engines. They differ across several parameters:

| Parameter | Methanol | Ethanol |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Natural gas, coal, biomass (syngas route) | Sugarcane, corn, molasses (fermentation) |
| **Calorific value** | 19.9 MJ/kg (lower) | 26.9 MJ/kg (higher) |
| **Octane (RON)** | ~114 (higher) | ~108 |
| **Oxygen content** | 49.9% by mass | 34.7% by mass |
| **Cetane number** | ~3-5 (very poor) | ~5-8 (very poor) |
| **Latent heat of vaporization** | 1100 kJ/kg (higher) | 900 kJ/kg |

Methanol is primarily produced **synthetically** from syngas, while ethanol is produced **biologically** by yeast fermentation of sugars. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**List any six advantages of using alcohols as fuels for SI engines.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High octane rating** (Methanol: 114 RON, Ethanol: 108 RON) — enables higher compression ratios and higher thermal efficiency.
2. **Renewable & biodegradable** — biomass-derived ethanol has a closed CO₂ cycle, reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
3. **High latent heat of vaporization** (1100 kJ/kg for methanol) — charge cooling effect increases volumetric efficiency and peak power.
4. **Oxygenated nature** — built-in oxygen (49.9% in methanol) ensures more complete combustion, reducing CO and HC emissions significantly.
5. **Cleaner burning** — no sulfur, no aromatics, no lead → no black smoke, no SO₂ emissions.
6. **Domestic production** — reduces dependence on imported crude oil; promotes rural economy through agricultural feedstock supply chain. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** One full question (with internal choice) per module, carrying **14 marks**. The question has TWO sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Below we provide **TWO complete alternative question sets** (Q-A and Q-B) — examiner will offer one OR the other.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q-A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the production of methanol from natural gas with neat chemical equations. What is the role of the water-gas shift reaction in this process? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a balanced chemical equation, derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of methanol. Compare it with that of gasoline and discuss the engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO Q-A:

**(a) Methanol Production from Natural Gas (7 marks)**

Methanol is industrially produced from natural gas (methane, CH₄) through a **three-step process**:

**Step 1 — Desulfurization:**
Natural gas is passed through zinc oxide beds to remove sulfur compounds (H₂S) that would otherwise poison the catalyst in the reformer.

**Step 2 — Steam Reforming (Primary Reformer):**
Methane is reacted with superheated steam in nickel-catalyst tubes at **850°C and 25 bar**:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni, 850°C, 25 bar}} \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This is an **endothermic** reaction requiring external heat input. The product gas is called **synthesis gas (syngas)** with an H₂:CO ratio of **3:1**.

**Step 3 — Water-Gas Shift Reaction:**
The H₂:CO ratio in syngas (3:1) is **higher** than the stoichiometric ratio required for methanol synthesis (2:1). Hence, part of the CO is converted to additional H₂ by reacting with steam:

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Role of the Water-Gas Shift Reaction:**
- Adjusts the **H₂:CO ratio** to 2:1 (optimal for methanol synthesis).
- Increases **H₂ yield** for the methanol synthesis reactor.
- Exothermic heat can be recovered for feed preheating.

**Step 4 — Methanol Synthesis Loop:**
The adjusted syngas is compressed to **75-100 bar** and fed to a catalytic reactor (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃) at **220-280°C**:

$$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \quad \Delta H = -90.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -49.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Separation & Recycle:**
The crude methanol is cooled, condensed, and separated from unreacted gases, which are **recycled back** to the reactor to push overall conversion > 95%. Final purification by distillation yields fuel-grade methanol (99.9%).

> **[7 marks breakdown: Production steps = 3, Equations = 2, Role of WGS = 2]**

---

**(b) Stoichiometric A/F of Methanol and Comparison (7 marks)**

**Balanced Combustion Equation:**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 + 5.64\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 5.64\,\text{N}_2$$

**Mass of O₂ per kmol of methanol:**
$$m_{\text{O}_2} = 1.5 \times 32 = 48 \text{ kg}$$

**Mass of N₂ per kmol of methanol:**
$$m_{\text{N}_2} = 5.64 \times 28 = 157.92 \text{ kg}$$

**Total air mass per kmol fuel:**
$$m_{\text{air}} = 48 + 157.92 = 205.92 \text{ kg}$$

**Stoichiometric A/F:**
$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{205.92}{32.04} = \boxed{6.43 : 1}$$

**Comparison with Gasoline:**

| Fuel | Stoich A/F (kg/kg) |
|---|---|
| Gasoline (C₈H₁₈) | ~14.7 : 1 |
| Methanol (CH₃OH) | ~6.43 : 1 |
| **Ratio** | Gasoline needs **2.3× more air** than methanol |

**Engineering Implications:**

1. **Carburetor / Injector Resizing:** For the same energy content, methanol needs ~2× the volumetric flow → fuel system must be **upsized**.
2. **Lower Flame Temperature:** With more fuel and less air, the adiabatic flame temperature drops slightly → potential **lower NOx** in SI mode.
3. **Lean Combustion Capability:** Methanol's high octane (114 RON) allows **leaner-than-stoichiometric** operation → higher efficiency, lower CO/HC.
4. **Emissions Profile:** High CO₂ dilution from built-in oxygen → more complete combustion → **CO ↓ 50%, HC ↓ 30%**.
5. **Charge Cooling Effect:** High latent heat (1100 kJ/kg) increases volumetric efficiency → **5-10% power gain** in turbo/SI configurations.

> **[7 marks breakdown: Equation = 1, A/F derivation = 3, Comparison = 1, Implications = 2]**

---

### 📘 QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q-B. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the production of ethanol by fermentation route. What are the limitations of the fermentation process? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the use of alcohol fuels in CI engines. Explain the dual-fuel mode with neat sketches. What are the emissions trade-offs? **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO Q-B:

**(a) Ethanol Production by Fermentation (7 marks)**

Ethanol is produced from biomass containing **sugars, starch, or cellulose** by **yeast-mediated anaerobic fermentation**.

**Step 1 — Feedstock Classification:**
- **Sugars** (sugarcane juice, molasses, sugar beet): Direct fermentation possible.
- **Starch** (corn, wheat, cassava): Requires **hydrolysis** to convert starch → sugars.
- **Cellulose** (wood, agricultural waste, grasses): Requires **acid + enzymatic hydrolysis** to convert cellulose → glucose.

**Step 2 — Hydrolysis (for starch/cellulose):**
$$(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{acid/enzyme}} n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$

**Step 3 — Fermentation:**
The sugar solution is cooled to **25-35°C** and inoculated with yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*):

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -69 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Distillation:**
The fermented "beer" contains only 8-12% ethanol (yeast self-inhibits at higher concentrations). Multi-stage distillation raises this to **95.6% (azeotrope)**.

**Step 5 — Dehydration:**
The remaining 4.4% water is removed by **molecular sieve adsorption** (3Å zeolite) or **azeotropic distillation**, yielding **anhydrous ethanol (99.5%+)** suitable for fuel blending.

**Limitations of Fermentation:**

1. **Low yield:** Only ~50% of glucose mass converts to ethanol (theoretical).
2. **Self-inhibition:** Yeast dies at ethanol concentrations above ~12%, limiting batch concentration.
3. **Slow kinetics:** Fermentation takes 24-72 hours per batch (vs. minutes for catalytic methanol synthesis).
4. **Feedstock seasonality:** Sugarcane harvest limits continuous production in some regions.
5. **Food vs. fuel debate:** First-generation ethanol from corn/sugarcane competes with food supply.
6. **Energy intensive:** Distillation alone consumes ~30-40% of the energy content of the ethanol produced (mitigated by using bagasse as fuel in Brazilian model).

> **[7 marks breakdown: Process steps = 3, Equations = 1, Limitations = 3]**

---

**(b) Use of Alcohols in CI Engines — Dual-Fuel Mode (7 marks)**

**Why pure alcohol fails in CI engines:**
- Alcohols have **very low cetane numbers** (Methanol ~3-5, Ethanol ~5-8)
- They do **not auto-ignite** under typical diesel compression ratios (CR 15-20)
- A **cetane improver, pilot fuel, or spark assist** is mandatory

**Dual-Fuel Mode (Most Common Approach):**

In dual-fuel operation:
- **Primary fuel:** Alcohol (methanol or ethanol) supplied as vapor or liquid into the **intake air** (fumigation)
- **Pilot fuel:** A small quantity of **diesel (5-10% of total energy)** is injected near TDC to provide the ignition source

**Working Principle:**

1. Alcohol is vaporized in a **fumigation port** upstream of the intake manifold.
2. The alcohol-air mixture enters the cylinder during the **intake stroke**.
3. Near TDC, the **diesel pilot is injected directly** into the cylinder at high pressure.
4. The diesel auto-ignites due to compression heat → forms a small diffusion flame.
5. The alcohol-air premixed mixture is **ignited by the diesel flame front** and burns in a partially premixed mode.

**Sketches** (describe in your answer): Draw a labeled schematic showing alcohol tank → vaporizer → intake manifold, and diesel tank → injection pump → injector → cylinder. Show the diesel pilot jet surrounded by the alcohol-air premixed gas.

**Emissions Trade-offs:**

| Pollutant | Trend vs. Pure Diesel | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **CO** | ↓ 20-50% | Premixed lean combustion of alcohol |
| **HC** | ↑ May increase | Incomplete premixed burn at low loads |
| **NOx** | ↑ 10-30% | Higher peak temperatures from premixed burn |
| **PM (Smoke)** | ↓ 40-70% | Oxygenated alcohol reduces soot formation |
| **Aldehydes** | ↑ New pollutant | Formaldehyde (methanol) / acetaldehyde (ethanol) — unregulated but toxic |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common mistake on dual-fuel questions:**
> Students often write "alcohol is injected directly into the cylinder" — this is **WRONG**. Alcohol goes into the **intake air** (fumigation or port injection). Only the diesel is direct-injected into the cylinder. Failing to clarify this loses 2-3 marks.

> **[7 marks breakdown: Why pure alcohol fails = 1.5, Dual-fuel working = 3, Sketch = 1, Emissions = 1.5]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students LOSE marks on Alcohol Fuels questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "mass A/F" with "volumetric A/F"** — always specify the basis! Methanol stoich A/F = **6.43 by mass** but ~**9.8 by volume** (because density differs).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the oxygen in alcohol** — the formula $n_{\text{O}_2} = x + y/4 - u/2$ has the **$-u/2$** term. Many students write $x + y/4$ (forgetting it), getting **wrong A/F** for both methanol (9.0 vs 6.43) and ethanol (11.5 vs 8.94).
> 
> 3. **Mixing up "octane" and "cetane"** — alcohol has **high octane, low cetane**. They are **inverse** properties. State the *numerical value* with the *property name*.
> 
> 4. **Saying alcohol "increases NOx because of oxygen"** — partial credit only. Correct reasoning: alcohol has **high latent heat** → lower flame temp in SI → **lower NOx**. The lower NOx effect dominates in SI; higher NOx occurs in CI dual-fuel due to premixed combustion.
> 
> 5. **Not specifying "by volume" or "by mass" for blends** — E85 means **85% ethanol by volume**. Always state the basis. Cost of not stating: 1 mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before the exam — it covers ~80% of marks on this topic.**

### 🧠 Must-Know Definitions
- ☐ **Alcohol** = organic compound with one or more –OH groups
- ☐ **Methanol** = CH₃OH (methyl alcohol) — from syngas route
- ☐ **Ethanol** = C₂H₅OH (ethyl alcohol) — from fermentation
- ☐ **Biomass-to-Liquid (BTL)** = syngas route to methanol/ethanol from biomass
- ☐ **E85 / M85** = 85% alcohol + 15% gasoline (by volume)

### 🔢 Must-Know Numerical Values
- ☐ Stoich A/F: **Methanol = 6.43**, **Ethanol = 8.94**, **Gasoline = 14.7**
- ☐ Calorific value: **Methanol = 19.9 MJ/kg**, **Ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg**, **Gasoline = 44 MJ/kg**
- ☐ Volumetric CV: **Methanol = 15.8 MJ/L**, **Ethanol = 21.2 MJ/L**, **Gasoline = 32.6 MJ/L**
- ☐ RON: **Methanol = 114**, **Ethanol = 108**
- ☐ Cetane: **Methanol ≈ 5**, **Ethanol ≈ 8** (very low!)
- ☐ Latent heat: **Methanol = 1100 kJ/kg**, **Ethanol = 900 kJ/kg** (vs. gasoline = 350)
- ☐ Oxygen content: **Methanol = 49.9%**, **Ethanol = 34.7%**
- ☐ Density: **Methanol = 792 kg/m³**, **Ethanol = 789 kg/m³**

### ⚗️ Must-Know Reactions
- ☐ Methanol synthesis: $\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$
- ☐ Methanol combustion: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 1.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- ☐ Ethanol combustion: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- ☐ Fermentation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$
- ☐ General O₂ formula for CₓHᵧOᵤ: $n_{\text{O}_2} = x + y/4 - u/2$

### 🚗 Engine Application Points
- ☐ Alcohols are **SI-favorable** (high octane); **CI-difficult** (low cetane)
- ☐ CI options: dual-fuel, fumigation, cetane additives, spark-assist
- ☐ Cold start problem: high latent heat → need auxiliary system
- ☐ Material compatibility: alcohol corrodes aluminum, zinc, rubber → use stainless steel + Viton seals
- ☐ Energy density penalty: 50% of gasoline for methanol, 65% for ethanol → **larger fuel tank needed**

### 📊 Comparison Triggers
- ☐ **Higher CR** possible (12:1 with alcohol vs 10:1 gasoline) → **higher η_th**
- ☐ **Charge cooling** → +5-10% peak power in SI engines
- ☐ **Lower CO** by 40-60%, **lower HC** by 20-30% in SI mode
- ☐ **Higher NOx** in dual-fuel CI mode (premixed combustion)

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ☐ Don't confuse **latent heat** with **calorific value** — they are different
- ☐ Don't say "alcohol has lower energy than gasoline, so it's bad" — it's *biomass-derived renewable*, the energy density must be viewed holistically
- ☐ Don't write "alcohol is more efficient than gasoline" without context — yes in SI mode (high CR), but in dual-fuel CI the gains are modest
- ☐ Always specify **"by mass"** or **"by volume"** when stating blend ratios
- ☐ Always write the **negative sign** in $-u/2$ when computing stoichiometric O₂

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Hot Topics (Likely to appear in ESE)
- ☐ Stoichiometric A/F derivation for methanol or ethanol
- ☐ Comparison table: methanol vs ethanol (5+ properties)
- ☐ Methanol production from natural gas (3 steps + equations)
- ☐ Ethanol fermentation process + limitations
- ☐ Dual-fuel mode in CI engines — working principle + emissions
- ☐ Energy content of E10/E20/E85 blends (numerical)
- ☐ Use of alcohols in SI engines — modifications required
- ☐ Advantages/disadvantages of alcohols (at least 5 each)

---

> **END OF NOTES — TYPES OF LIQUID ALTERNATE FUELS: ALCOHOL (MODULE 2)**
> **Total content coverage: Production pathways, Combustion chemistry, Engine applications (SI & CI), Derivations, KTU exam pattern, Valuation tips**
> **Expected KTU contribution: 14 marks (1 full question with choice) + 3 marks (Part A) = 17 marks/100**

topics.title: Ethanol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ethanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**, also chemically termed *ethyl alcohol* or *grain alcohol*, is a clear, colourless, volatile, flammable, oxygenated, mono-alcoholic biofuel with the molecular formula **C₂H₆O** and a molecular weight of **46.07 g/mol**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (Module 2: Alcohol Fuels), ethanol is classified as the **first-generation liquid biofuel of highest global industrial maturity**, produced predominantly through the **fermentation of sugars (from sugarcane, sugar beet, maize, molasses)** or via the **catalytic hydration of petroleum-derived ethylene (C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH)**.

For spark-ignition (SI) engine applications, ethanol is conventionally deployed as a **gasoline blendstock** under volumetric designations such as **E5, E10, E20, E85, and E100** — where the numerical suffix represents the volume percentage of ethanol in the blend (e.g., E10 = **10 % ethanol + 90 % gasoline**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ethanol is the *only* alcohol fuel in the syllabus explicitly associated with *both* SI engine blending (E10–E85) *and* CI engine dual-fuel fumigation strategies. Master the dual-role aspect.

## 1.2 Physical–Chemical Identity Card (At a Glance)

| Property | Symbol / Unit | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | — | **C₂H₅OH** | Two-carbon mono-alcohol |
| Molecular Weight | M | **46.07 kg/kmol** | Low → high vapour mass flux |
| Density (20 °C) | ρ | **0.789 kg/L** | Energy content per litre ↓ |
| Lower Heating Value | LHV | **26.9 MJ/kg** | ~ 33 % lower than gasoline (44 MJ/kg) |
| LHV (volumetric) | LHV_v | **21.2 MJ/L** | Tank size must increase ~ 50 % |
| Research Octane Number | RON | **108–110** | Allows ↑ compression ratio |
| Motor Octane Number | MON | **89–92** | Sensitive rating still high |
| Anti-Knock Index | AKI = (RON+MON)/2 | **~ 99** | Premium-grade substitute |
| Stoichiometric AFR | A/F_st | **9.0 : 1** | Leaner than gasoline (14.7:1) |
| Heat of Vaporisation | h_fg | **~ 840 kJ/kg** | High → strong charge cooling |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | T_ai | **363 °C** | Resistant to compression ignition |
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | A/F | **9.0** | Oxygenates combustion mixture |
| Laminar Flame Speed | S_L | **~ 50 cm/s** | Higher than gasoline (38 cm/s) |
| Flash Point (closed cup) | T_fp | **12.8 °C** | Cold-start friendly, fire hazard ↑ |
| Latent Heat of Vaporisation | λ | **0.9 MJ/kg** | Charge cooling + knock resistance |
| Carbon-to-Hydrogen Ratio | C/H | **2 : 6 = 1 : 3** | Lower C → CO₂ emissions ↓ |
| Oxygen content (by mass) | %O | **34.8 %** | Built-in oxygenate |
| Reid Vapour Pressure | RVP | **~ 17 psi** | Exists for vapour-lock concern |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorable Tip for KTU Board:** Ethanol = *“high octane, high latent heat, high flame speed, high oxygen, low energy density.”* The five “highs and lows” decide its engine behaviour.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cool Burner" of Internal Combustion

Imagine you are lighting a **charcoal barbecue** using **kerosene versus rubbing alcohol (which is denatured ethanol)**. Kerosene, though energy-rich, is sluggish to light, burns with a long lazy yellow flame, and produces soot. Alcohol, by contrast, lights almost instantly with a *quiet, near-invisible blue flame*, yet you can feel the *intense cold* on your hand above the bottle because the liquid is greedily absorbing heat just to vaporise.

Translate this kitchen experience into a combustion engine:

- The **instant blue flame** = ethanol's **high laminar flame speed** and **high octane number**, which means the engine can run on a *tighter, more efficient* compression ratio without knocking.
- The **intense cold from vaporising** = ethanol's **high latent heat of vaporisation (h_fg ≈ 840 kJ/kg)**, which cools the incoming intake charge, *increases its density*, and provides additional **knock resistance** (thermal anti-knock).
- The **near-invisible combustion** (no soot) = ethanol's **34.8 % bound oxygen**, which enables *more complete combustion* and *lower particulate, CO, and unburnt-HC emissions*.
- The **smaller portion of meat cooked per litre of fuel** (you finish the bottle quickly with little energy) = ethanol's **lower heating value (LHV = 26.9 MJ/kg)**, which forces the vehicle to carry *larger fuel tanks* or accept *lower range*.

This single analogy consolidates **five key engineering trade-offs** of ethanol that the KTU examiner will repeatedly test.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU valuation desk, students who can weave such a real-world analogy into a 14-mark answer often secure the full 14/14 because it demonstrates *application-level understanding* (Bloom Level 3–4).

## 1.4 Ethanol's Place in the KTU Module 2 Roadmap

Module 2 of PEMUT411 typically groups ethanol alongside **methanol, butanol, and other alcohols** under the umbrella of *Alcohol Fuels*. Within this group, ethanol is **the largest-volume commercial alcohol fuel globally**, supported by national policies such as **India's E20 programme (Petroleum Ministry, 2025 roadmap)**, **Brazil's ProÁlcool (since 1975)**, and the **US Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Molecular Logic of Ethanol as a Fuel

### Step 1 — The "Built-in Oxygen" Architecture
Ethanol's molecular structure, C₂H₅–O–H, contains a **hydroxyl (–OH) functional group** bonded to an ethyl radical. The **C–O–H bond** carries one oxygen atom *inside the fuel molecule itself*. This means during combustion, **~ 34.8 % of the stoichiometric oxygen required already travels with the fuel**. The remainder (~ 65.2 %) must still be supplied by induction air.

### Step 2 — The Combustion Equation (Stoichiometric)
The complete combustion of ethanol with air proceeds as:

$$ \mathrm{C_2H_5OH \; + \; 3\,O_2 \; \longrightarrow \; 2\,CO_2 \; + \; 3\,H_2O} $$

Adjusting for air (where air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂ by mole):

$$ \mathrm{C_2H_5OH \; + \; 3\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \; \longrightarrow \; 2\,CO_2 \; + \; 3\,H_2O \; + \; 11.28\,N_2} $$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio Derivation
For **1 kg of ethanol**:
- C atoms → 24/46 kg → CO₂ mass = (24/46) × (44/12) = **1.913 kg CO₂**
- H atoms → 6/46 kg → H₂O mass = (6/46) × (18/2) = **1.174 kg H₂O**
- O atoms internal → supplies 1 mole O₂ equivalent → 32 kg O₂ per kmol ethanol
- External O₂ required = 3 × 32 − 16 (from ethanol) = 80 kg/kmol ethanol
- Air required = 80 / 0.232 = **344.8 kg air / 1000 kg fuel**
- **A/F_stoich = 9.0 : 1 (mass basis)** — matches the property table.

### Step 4 — The Energy Density Story
Heating value is governed by the **lower heating value (LHV)**, which for ethanol equals **26.9 MJ/kg**. Compare with gasoline (44 MJ/kg) — ethanol carries **~ 39 % less energy per kilogram**. On a *volumetric* basis, the deficit is **~ 34 %** because ethanol's density (0.789 kg/L) is also lower than gasoline (0.74–0.78 kg/L). This directly mandates **larger fuel tanks** for equivalent driving range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Consequence:** For every **100 km of range** a gasoline vehicle achieves on a 50 L tank, an E100 (pure ethanol) vehicle requires **~ 75 L** of tank capacity — a **50 % volumetric penalty**. The Brazilian flex-fuel fleet mitigates this with **smaller auxiliary gasoline tanks** for cold-start.

### Step 5 — The Octane Advantage Explained
The **Research Octane Number (RON) of 108–110** for ethanol arises from two distinct mechanisms:
1. **Chemical Anti-Knock:** Ethanol's high self-ignition resistance (auto-ignition T = 363 °C, much higher than gasoline's ~ 250 °C).
2. **Thermal Anti-Knock:** The **high latent heat of vaporisation (840 kJ/kg)** cools the end-gas, reducing its temperature and pressure at the end of compression, thereby suppressing auto-ignition.

### Step 6 — The Flame Speed Premium
The **laminar flame speed of ethanol (≈ 50 cm/s) is ~ 30 % higher than gasoline (≈ 38 cm/s)** at stoichiometric conditions. Faster flame propagation:
- Shortens the combustion duration.
- Allows the engine to operate at **higher speeds without misfire**.
- Improves the **thermal efficiency of the Otto cycle** by allowing the combustion peak to occur closer to top-dead-centre.

### Step 7 — The Volatility Trade-off
Ethanol's **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP ≈ 17 psi)** sits in the moderate range. Pure ethanol (RVP ≈ 2 psi) is *too low* to start a cold engine; blends such as **E10 (RVP ≈ 11–13 psi) exhibit non-linear RVP blending behaviour** with a *synergistic spike* (called the **"azeotropic bump"**). This is why E10 vehicles have *good* cold start, but **E85 and E100 vehicles need a gasoline cold-start injector**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often assume ethanol has "very high volatility" because of its low flash point. *Volatility = vapour pressure at 38 °C = RVP*, not flash point. Ethanol's *flash point is low* (fire hazard) but *RVP is moderate* (cold-start issue only at very high ethanol fractions).

## 2.2 Production Pathways — The Two Industrial Routes

### Route A — Fermentation (Bio-Ethanol)
Sugars from **sugarcane molasses, sugar beet, corn, sorghum, or cellulosic biomass** are fermented by yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) under anaerobic conditions:

$$ \mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\xrightarrow{yeast}\; 2\,C_2H_5OH \; + \; 2\,CO_2 \; + \; energy} $$

> Theoretical yield = 51.1 % by mass of glucose → ethanol. Industrial plants achieve **90–95 %** of theoretical yield.

The fermentation broth is then **distilled and dehydrated** (via molecular sieves or azeotropic distillation) to produce anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %+ purity) suitable for fuel blending.

### Route B — Catalytic Hydration of Ethylene (Synthetic Ethanol)
Petroleum-derived ethylene is hydrated over a **phosphoric-acid catalyst (H₃PO₄ on SiO₂)** at **300 °C and 70 atm**:

$$ \mathrm{C_2H_4 \; + \; H_2O \;\xrightleftharpoons[H_3PO_4]{300\,°C,\,70\,atm}\; C_2H_5OH} $$

This **synthetic route** is *not renewable* (it consumes fossil feedstock) and is therefore excluded from "green" biofuel credits in most KTU exam settings.

### Route C — Cellulosic (Second-Generation) Ethanol
Lignocellulosic biomass (agricultural residues, wood waste, grasses) is **hydrolysed** to release C5/C6 sugars, which are then fermented. KTU expects students to mention that India is piloting **second-generation (2G) ethanol plants** under the **Pradhan Mantri JI-VAN Yojana**.

## 2.3 Ethanol–Gasoline Blends — The Tiered Spectrum

| Blend | Ethanol % (vol) | Typical Application | Engine Modifications Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **E5** | 5 % | Conventional petrol, no change | None — "drop-in" |
| **E10** | 10 % | Standard blend, India target 2025 | None on most modern engines |
| **E20** | 20 % | India's 2025 target blend | Minor ECU recalibration, fuel-system material check |
| **E25** | 25 % | Brazil transition blend | ECU mapping required |
| **E85** | 85 % | US & Brazilian flex-fuel vehicles | Fuel injectors, FFV fuel system, cold-start |
| **E100** | 100 % | Pure ethanol (Brazil fleet, racing) | High compression, cold-start enrichment |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Buzzword — "Flex-Fuel Vehicle (FFV)":** A vehicle equipped with **fuel composition sensors** and **adaptive ECU maps** capable of running on *any blend from E0 to E85* without driver intervention. The 2014 Honda Fit, Ford Taurus, and all Brazilian post-2003 light-duty vehicles are FFVs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Saver Table** — Commit these relationships to memory. The KTU valuation key explicitly penalises missing units and omitting the energy-density correction.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric A/F | (A/F) = 34.8 × (m_O2 / m_fuel) | kg/kg | Ethanol = 9.0 |
| 2 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) | ΔH_comb − h_fg(water) | MJ/kg | 26.9 for ethanol |
| 3 | Volumetric Energy Density | LHV_v = LHV × ρ | MJ/L | 21.2 for ethanol |
| 4 | Energy-Equivalence Ratio | Φ_E = LHV_blend / LHV_gasoline | — | Compare blend to gasoline |
| 5 | Brake-Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC) | BSEC = (ṁ_f × LHV) / P_b | kg/MJ | Ethanol ≈ 1.5× gasoline |
| 6 | Volumetric Fuel Consumption | V̇_f = ṁ_f / ρ | L/s | Ethanol ↑ 50 % vs gasoline |
| 7 | Carbon Monoxide Emissions | CO ∝ (1/φ) for lean burn | ppm | Ethanol ↓ due to O₂ |
| 8 | Octane Number of Blend | RON_blend = Σ(x_i · RON_i) | — | Linear-by-volume |
| 9 | RVP Blend (E10 azeotrope) | RVP_blend ≈ 1.4 × RVP_components | psi | Non-linear in 5–25 % range |
| 10 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | η_bth = P_b / (ṁ_f × LHV) | % | Ethanol ↑ 2–5 % over gasoline |
| 11 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | (A/F) = (138 × (O + N/2)) / (12C + H + 16O − 32O) | kg/kg | General hydrocarbon fuel |
| 12 | Energy Yield per Hectare | E_hectare = m_ethanol × LHV_ethanol | GJ/ha | Sugarcane ≈ 100–120 GJ/ha |
| 13 | Stoichiometric Combustion Air | A_st = (8/3)C + 8H − O | kg O₂/kg fuel | Elemental mass balance |
| 14 | Calorific Equivalence of Blend | CE = (V_E × ρ_E × LHV_E) / (V_g × ρ_g × LHV_g) | % | Compare energy per volume |

> **Note on absolute values:** In the table above, the symbol `\vert` (vertical bar) is intentionally rendered as a literal vertical bar *only when not in a markdown table cell*. Inside table cells, use `(x)` parentheses or `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Ethanol's Edge | Practical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **SI Engine Octane Booster** | 108 RON allows ↑ CR from 9:1 to 12:1 | + 8–12 % thermal efficiency |
| **CI Engine Dual-Fuel** | High cetane deficit → fumigation or port injection | 70–80 % diesel substitution possible |
| **Cold-Start Improver (high blends)** | High RVP of E10; pure ethanol needs gasoline priming | Flex-fuel cold-start injection |
| **Emissions After-Treatment** | Built-in O₂ → ↓ CO, ↓ HC, ↓ PM | Slightly ↑ NO_x (from faster flame) |
| **Renewable Carbon Pathway** | CO₂ released = CO₂ absorbed by sugarcane | Carbon-neutral (well-to-wheel) |
| **Oxygenate for Reformulated Gasoline (RFG)** | 2 wt % O₂ as E10 → mandated by EPA | Reduced CO, ozone precursors |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio of Ethanol (Mass Basis)

**Given:** C₂H₅OH, M = 46.07 kg/kmol, atomic masses C = 12, H = 1, O = 16.

**Step 1:** Write the stoichiometric equation with O₂:

$$ \mathrm{C_2H_5OH \; + \; a\,O_2 \; \longrightarrow \; b\,CO_2 \; + \; c\,H_2O} $$

**Step 2:** Apply atomic balance:
- C: 2 = b ⇒ **b = 2**
- H: 6 = 2c ⇒ **c = 3**
- O: 1 (from ethanol) + 2a = 2b + c = 4 + 3 = 7 ⇒ 2a = 6 ⇒ **a = 3**

**Step 3:** Write the balanced equation:

$$ \mathrm{C_2H_5OH \; + \; 3\,O_2 \; \longrightarrow \; 2\,CO_2 \; + \; 3\,H_2O} $$

**Step 4:** Compute O₂ required per kg of ethanol:
- Moles of ethanol per kg = 1000 / 46.07 = **21.706 mol/kg**
- Moles of O₂ per kg ethanol = 3 × 21.706 = 65.118 mol/kg
- Mass of O₂ per kg ethanol = 65.118 × 32 = **2083.8 g = 2.084 kg O₂ per kg ethanol**

**Step 5:** Convert O₂ mass to air mass (air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass):

$$ m_{air} \;=\; \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} \;=\; \frac{2.084}{0.232} \;=\; 8.984 \; \mathrm{kg \; air/kg \; ethanol} $$

**Step 6:** Compute the air–fuel ratio on a mass basis:

$$ (A/F)_{stoich} \;\approx\; 9.0 \; \mathrm{kg \; air/kg \; ethanol} $$

> **Sanity check:** This matches the textbook value of 9.0 : 1. The minor discrepancy (8.984 vs 9.0) arises from rounding of atomic masses and the assumption that air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass (precise value 23.3 %).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Density Comparison: E10 vs Pure Gasoline

**Given:**
- LHV_ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg, ρ_ethanol = 0.789 kg/L
- LHV_gasoline = 44.0 MJ/kg, ρ_gasoline = 0.745 kg/L

**Step 1:** Compute volumetric LHV of each:

$$ LHV_{E,vol} \;=\; 26.9 \,\times\, 0.789 \;=\; 21.22 \; \mathrm{MJ/L} $$

$$ LHV_{G,vol} \;=\; 44.0 \,\times\, 0.745 \;=\; 32.78 \; \mathrm{MJ/L} $$

**Step 2:** Energy per litre of E10 blend (linear-by-volume blend rule — an approximation, since true blending is *not* strictly linear due to volume contraction on mixing, but is the convention KTU uses):

$$ LHV_{E10} \;=\; 0.10 \,\times\, 21.22 \;+\; 0.90 \,\times\, 32.78 $$

$$ LHV_{E10} \;=\; 2.122 \;+\; 29.502 \;=\; 31.62 \; \mathrm{MJ/L} $$

**Step 3:** Compute the energy-equivalence ratio:

$$ \Phi_E \;=\; \frac{31.62}{32.78} \;=\; 0.965 $$

**Step 4:** Compute the volumetric fuel consumption penalty:

$$ \Delta V_f \;=\; \left(1 - \Phi_E\right) \,\times\, 100 \;=\; 3.5 \,\% $$

**Step 5:** For an E100 (pure ethanol) vehicle:

$$ \Phi_E^{(E100)} \;=\; \frac{21.22}{32.78} \;=\; 0.647 $$

**Step 6:** Volumetric penalty for E100:

$$ \Delta V_f^{(E100)} \;=\; (1 - 0.647) \,\times\, 100 \;=\; 35.3 \,\% $$

**Conclusion:** E10 costs **~ 3.5 %** of the gasoline range; E100 costs **~ 35 %** of the gasoline range for the same tank volume. This is why India's E20 programme has acceptable range impact, while a full E100 fleet is impractical without a **larger tank** or **higher-density energy carrier**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Octane Number of an Ethanol–Gasoline Blend (Linear-by-Volume Rule)

**Given:** Blend is 20 % ethanol (E20) with 80 % gasoline.
- RON_ethanol = 110 (assumed)
- RON_gasoline = 88 (typical Indian regular)

**Step 1:** Apply the linear-blending rule (valid for moderate ethanol fractions, ≤ 30 %):

$$ RON_{blend} \;=\; x_E \cdot RON_E \;+\; x_G \cdot RON_G $$

$$ RON_{E20} \;=\; 0.20 \,\times\, 110 \;+\; 0.80 \,\times\, 88 $$

$$ RON_{E20} \;=\; 22.0 \;+\; 70.4 \;=\; 92.4 $$

**Step 2:** Octane uplift over base gasoline:

$$ \Delta RON \;=\; 92.4 \;-\; 88.0 \;=\; 4.4 \; \mathrm{points} $$

> **Engineering use:** A 4-point RON gain is sufficient to **recover a 0.5–1.0 unit compression-ratio increase** without knock, translating to a **2–4 %** improvement in indicated thermal efficiency.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cold-Start Heat-Balance for an E85 Engine

**Given:**
- 4-cylinder, 1.5 L SI engine, CR = 12:1, P_imep at idle ≈ 1.5 bar
- Ambient T = 5 °C, ethanol T = 20 °C
- Latent heat of vaporisation λ_E = 0.84 MJ/kg
- Specific heat c_p,air = 1.005 kJ/kg·K

**Step 1:** Mass of air ingested per intake stroke (ideal gas, V_d = 1.5 L, η_vol = 0.5, P_amb = 100 kPa, T_amb = 278 K):

$$ m_{air} \;=\; \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T} \;\approx\; \frac{100 \,\times\, 0.00075}{0.287 \,\times\, 278} \;\approx\; 9.4 \,\times\, 10^{-4} \; \mathrm{kg/cylinder} $$

**Step 2:** Stoichiometric ethanol mass per cylinder:

$$ m_E \;=\; \frac{m_{air}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} \;=\; \frac{9.4 \,\times\, 10^{-4}}{9.0} \;=\; 1.04 \,\times\, 10^{-4} \; \mathrm{kg} $$

**Step 3:** Heat required to vaporise this ethanol from 20 °C → vapour at intake-manifold T (say 30 °C):

$$ Q_{vap} \;=\; m_E \,\times\, \left[c_{p,l} (T_{boil} - T_{fuel}) + \lambda_E + c_{p,v} (T_{man} - T_{boil})\right] $$

Where T_boil of ethanol = **78.4 °C**, c_p,l = 2.43 kJ/kg·K, c_p,v = 1.43 kJ/kg·K.

$$ Q_{vap} \;=\; 1.04 \,\times\, 10^{-4} \;\times\; \left[2.43 \,\times\, 58.4 \;+\; 840 \;+\; 1.43 \,\times\, (-48.4)\right] $$

$$ Q_{vap} \;=\; 1.04 \,\times\, 10^{-4} \;\times\; \left[141.9 \;+\; 840 \;-\; 69.2\right] $$

$$ Q_{vap} \;=\; 1.04 \,\times\, 10^{-4} \,\times\, 912.7 \;=\; 0.0949 \; \mathrm{kJ/cylinder} $$

**Step 4:** For all 4 cylinders per intake stroke cycle = 0.3796 kJ. Per second at 1000 rpm (33 cycles/s, 4 cyl = 132 cyl-events/s):

$$ \dot{Q}_{vap} \;\approx\; 50 \; \mathrm{kW \; of \; heat \; required} $$

**Step 5:** This is the *wallpaper* calculation that reveals **why a pure-ethanol engine cannot cold-start without auxiliary heating** — the **50 kW of vaporisation heat** would freeze the intake manifold and deplete the wall-film fuel. Brazilian E100 vehicles therefore use a small **gasoline cold-start reservoir** (typically 1.5–3 L) that primes the engine for the first 60–90 seconds until the engine warms up.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Emissions of an E20 SI Engine (Ratio Analysis)

**Given baseline (E0) emissions per km:**
- CO = 2.5 g/km, HC = 0.20 g/km, NO_x = 0.10 g/km, CO₂ = 180 g/km

**E20 expected change factors** (literature / empirical factors):
- CO: ↓ 30 % (oxygenation)
- HC: ↓ 5–10 %
- NO_x: ↑ 5 % (faster flame → higher T_peak)
- CO₂: ↓ ~ 3–4 % per MJ, but volume ↑ → net ↓ 1–2 % per km

**Step 1:** Apply correction factors:

$$ CO_{E20} \;=\; 2.5 \,\times\, 0.70 \;=\; 1.75 \; \mathrm{g/km} $$

$$ HC_{E20} \;=\; 0.20 \,\times\, 0.92 \;=\; 0.184 \; \mathrm{g/km} $$

$$ NO_{x,E20} \;=\; 0.10 \,\times\, 1.05 \;=\; 0.105 \; \mathrm{g/km} $$

$$ CO_{2,E20} \;\approx\; 178 \; \mathrm{g/km} $$

> **Net assessment:** E20 is an *emissions-friendly* fuel for **CO, HC, and CO₂**; the **modest NO_x rise** is the only drawback, easily managed by a recalibrated ECU with a 2–3 % retarded spark advance during the cold-start and warm-up phases.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Ethanol Performance in Different Engine Types

| Engine Type | Ethanol Form | Mode of Introduction | Substitution Rate | Key Performance Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SI (Port Injection) | E10–E20 | In-line blending with gasoline | 10–20 % | + 2–4 % η_bth, ↓ CO/HC |
| SI (Direct Injection) | E20–E85 | Direct injection in cylinder | 20–85 % | Higher CR possible (12–14:1) |
| SI (Dedicated E100) | E100 | Port injection | 100 % | 12:1 CR, gasoline cold-start |
| SI (FFV) | E0–E85 | Adaptive ECU | Variable | Sensor-controlled |
| CI (Diesel) Dual-Fuel | 80–90 % diesel + ethanol fumigation | Port fumigation | 70–80 % diesel | ↓ PM, ↓ NO_x, ↑ HC & CO |
| CI (Diesel) PPC | E-diesel emulsions | In-line emulsion | 10–15 % | ↓ smoke, ↓ NO_x |
| CI (HCCI / RCCI) | E85 + diesel pilot | Dual injection | 80 %+ | Premixed low-T combustion |
| CI (Spark-Assisted) | E100 with glow plug | Compression + glow | 100 % | Difficult cold-start |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignosis) using ethanol–diesel blends is a hot 14-mark topic, blending *advanced combustion concepts* with *alternative fuels* — perfect for a CO3/CO4 level answer.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: List any three advantages and two disadvantages of using ethanol as a fuel in SI engines.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Higher Research Octane Number (RON ≈ 108–110)** compared to gasoline (RON ≈ 88–98), allowing higher compression ratios (up to 12:1) and improved thermal efficiency.
2. **High latent heat of vaporisation (840 kJ/kg)** provides significant charge cooling, reducing the tendency of knock and enabling leaner operation.
3. **Built-in oxygen (34.8 % by mass)** promotes more complete combustion, reducing CO and unburnt HC emissions; also enables reduction of conventional toxic additives.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Lower heating value (LHV = 26.9 MJ/kg, ~ 39 % less than gasoline)** leads to ~ 50 % higher volumetric fuel consumption, requiring larger fuel tanks.
2. **Cold-start difficulty** with blends above E70 due to high latent heat of vaporisation and low RVP of pure ethanol; auxiliary gasoline injection is required.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU typical):** 1 mark × 3 advantages + 1 mark × 2 disadvantages = 5 marks → scaled to 3. (For 3-mark version, list 2 advantages + 1 disadvantage with one-line justification each.)

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between bio-ethanol and synthetic ethanol in 5 lines.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bio-Ethanol | Synthetic Ethanol |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Material** | Sugarcane, molasses, corn, cellulosic biomass | Petroleum-derived ethylene (C₂H₄) |
| **Process** | Fermentation by yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae), then distillation | Catalytic hydration of ethylene over H₃PO₄ at 300 °C, 70 atm |
| **Renewability** | Renewable — CO₂ cycle is closed within a season | Non-renewable — fossil feedstock |
| **Carbon Credit Eligibility** | Eligible (RFS, RED II) | Ineligible |
| **Cost (2010s)** | Higher ($0.45–0.60/L) | Lower ($0.35–0.50/L) |

> **Key KTU line:** *Bio-ethanol is renewable and carbon-neutral; synthetic ethanol is not. Both yield chemically identical C₂H₅OH.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various production routes of ethanol as a transportation fuel, with neat flow diagrams. Discuss the Indian National Policy on Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP). **[7 Marks]**

**Q: (b)** A flex-fuel vehicle (FFV) is fuelled with E85 (85 % ethanol + 15 % gasoline by volume). Given:
- LHV of ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg, ρ_ethanol = 0.789 kg/L
- LHV of gasoline = 44.0 MJ/kg, ρ_gasoline = 0.745 kg/L

Compute:
(i) The volumetric lower heating value of E85.
(ii) The energy-equivalence ratio of E85 with respect to gasoline.
(iii) The percentage increase in fuel-tank volume required to maintain the same driving range as a pure gasoline vehicle of 50 L tank capacity.
(iv) Comment on the cold-start strategy needed for an E85 vehicle. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

Ethanol is produced industrially via **two principal routes** plus emerging third-generation routes.

**Route 1 — Fermentation of Sugar (Bio-Ethanol):**
- **Feedstocks:** Sugarcane juice, sugar beet juice, molasses, corn starch, sorghum.
- **Process:** (i) Juice extraction → (ii) Sugar syrup preparation → (iii) Fermentation by yeast (*S. cerevisiae*) at 30–35 °C for 48–72 hours → (iv) Distillation to 95 % v/v ethanol → (v) Dehydration to 99.5 %+ (anhydrous) via molecular sieves or azeotropic distillation.
- **Reaction:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂

**Route 2 — Catalytic Hydration of Ethylene (Synthetic):**
- **Feedstock:** Cracked petroleum ethylene (C₂H₄).
- **Catalyst:** Phosphoric acid on silica support.
- **Conditions:** 300 °C, 70 atm.
- **Reaction:** C₂H₄ + H₂O ⇌ C₂H₅OH (exothermic, equilibrium-limited).

**Route 3 — Cellulosic (2G) Ethanol:**
- **Feedstock:** Bagasse, rice straw, corn stover, woody biomass.
- **Process:** Pretreatment (steam explosion / dilute acid) → Enzymatic hydrolysis (cellulase) → C5/C6 sugar fermentation → Distillation.
- **Significance:** Uses non-food biomass; India's 2G plants at Panipat and Bhatinda are operational.

**Indian National Policy on Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP):**
- Launched in **2003** by the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas.
- **Original target:** 5 % blending (E5) by 2005.
- **Revised roadmap (2018 onwards):** E10 by 2022, **E20 by 2025/2026**.
- **National Policy on Biofuels 2018** allows 2G feedstocks; OMEs (oil marketing companies) float tenders for ethanol procurement.
- **Ethanol procurement price** is set annually by the Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs; 2024–25 price is ~ ₹60.73/L for sugarcane-based ethanol.
- **Distinction:** EBP covers *ethanol*; the *Biodiesel Blending Programme* covers FAME and is separate.

> *(7 marks awarded: 1 mark for naming routes, 2 marks for fermentation process, 1 mark for synthetic route, 1 mark for cellulosic route, 2 marks for EBP policy.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step (i) — Volumetric LHV of E85:**

$$ LHV_{E,vol} = 26.9 \,\times\, 0.789 = 21.22 \; \mathrm{MJ/L} $$

$$ LHV_{G,vol} = 44.0 \,\times\, 0.745 = 32.78 \; \mathrm{MJ/L} $$

$$ LHV_{E85,vol} = 0.85 \,\times\, 21.22 \;+\; 0.15 \,\times\, 32.78 $$

$$ LHV_{E85,vol} = 18.04 \;+\; 4.92 \;=\; 22.96 \; \mathrm{MJ/L} $$

**Step (ii) — Energy-Equivalence Ratio:**

$$ \Phi_E \;=\; \frac{LHV_{E85,vol}}{LHV_{G,vol}} \;=\; \frac{22.96}{32.78} \;=\; 0.7004 $$

**Step (iii) — Required Tank Volume for Same Range:**

$$ V_{E85} \;=\; \frac{V_{gasoline}}{\Phi_E} \;=\; \frac{50}{0.7004} \;=\; 71.39 \; \mathrm{L} $$

$$ \Delta V \;=\; \frac{71.39 - 50}{50} \,\times\, 100 \;=\; 42.8 \;\% $$

The E85 vehicle needs a **~ 43 % larger fuel tank** to maintain the same driving range.

**Step (iv) — Cold-Start Strategy for E85:**

E85's effective RVP is too low for cold-start below ~ 10 °C because the high latent heat of vaporisation (840 kJ/kg) chills the intake manifold. The **flex-fuel cold-start strategy** uses:
- A small **gasoline auxiliary tank (~ 1.5–3 L)** feeding a dedicated cold-start injector.
- The ECU runs gasoline for the first **60–90 seconds** until the engine coolant reaches ~ 50 °C, then transitions to E85 via the **fuel composition sensor** signal.

> *(7 marks awarded: 1.5 marks for each sub-step (i)–(iii), 2.5 marks for the qualitative answer in (iv) including mention of auxiliary tank and fuel composition sensor.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **dual-fuel CI engine** using ethanol fumigation. Discuss its performance, emissions, and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**Q: (b)** Derive the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (mass basis) for ethanol. If an engine operates on E20 (20 % ethanol + 80 % gasoline by volume), compute the new stoichiometric A/F ratio. Given: ρ_ethanol = 0.789 kg/L, ρ_gasoline = 0.745 kg/L, A/F of pure gasoline = 14.7. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A **dual-fuel CI engine** uses **two fuels simultaneously** — a primary gaseous/liquid fuel of low cetane (e.g., ethanol) inducted through the intake, and a pilot injection of high-cetane **diesel** injected directly into the cylinder near top-dead-centre to ignite the premixed ethanol–air charge.

**Block Diagram (Refer to SECTION 4.5 above):**
1. Primary fuel: **Diesel** → tank → injection pump → direct injection.
2. Secondary fuel: **Ethanol** → tank → vaporiser (or port injector) → intake manifold fumigation → premixed with air → enters cylinder.
3. The diesel pilot auto-ignites under compression → ignites the ethanol–air mixture.

**Performance:**
- **Brake thermal efficiency:** Comparable to diesel up to 80 % diesel substitution; falls beyond this due to slow ethanol flame initiation.
- **Brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC):** Increases ~ 15–25 % because of ethanol's lower LHV.
- **Power output:** Up to 85–90 % of pure-diesel rated power at 80 % substitution; lower at higher substitution.

**Emissions:**
- **NO_x:** ↓ 20–40 % (lower peak flame temperature due to high ethanol h_fg and lower flame speed during premixed phase).
- **Smoke/PM:** ↓ 50–70 % (oxygenated ethanol + lower diesel pilot quantity).
- **CO and HC:** ↑ 30–60 % (incomplete combustion of ethanol at low loads due to cold-flame regions).
- **CO₂:** ↓ 5–10 % per unit work (renewable carbon credit).

**Limitations:**
1. **Cold-start difficulty** — ethanol's high latent heat and low cetane cause misfire at low coolant temperatures; pilot diesel must be 100 % at start.
2. **Limited load range** — high substitution fails at low loads due to incomplete combustion; maximum efficiency only at 60–80 % load.
3. **Fuel system complexity** — two fuel tanks, two injection systems, dual ECU control.
4. **Material compatibility** — ethanol corrodes aluminium, magnesium, and certain elastomers; requires stainless-steel components.

> *(7 marks: 1 mark for sketch, 1.5 marks for working principle, 1.5 marks for performance/emissions, 3 marks for limitations.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric A/F of pure ethanol (already derived in Section 3.1):**

$$ (A/F)_{ethanol} \;=\; 9.0 \; \mathrm{kg \; air/kg \; ethanol} $$

**Step 2 — Convert volumetric composition of E20 to mass composition:**

In **1 L of E20 blend**:
- V_ethanol = 0.20 L → m_ethanol = 0.20 × 0.789 = 0.1578 kg
- V_gasoline = 0.80 L → m_gasoline = 0.80 × 0.745 = 0.5960 kg
- Total mass = 0.1578 + 0.5960 = 0.7538 kg

**Mass fractions:**
- x_E = 0.1578 / 0.7538 = 0.2094
- x_G = 0.5960 / 0.7538 = 0.7906

**Step 3 — Compute the air required per kg of E20 blend:**

$$ m_{air,E20} = x_E \,\times\, (A/F)_E \;+\; x_G \,\times\, (A/F)_G $$

$$ m_{air,E20} = 0.2094 \,\times\, 9.0 \;+\; 0.7906 \,\times\, 14.7 $$

$$ m_{air,E20} = 1.885 \;+\; 11.622 \;=\; 13.507 \; \mathrm{kg \; air/kg \; E20} $$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric A/F of E20:**

$$ (A/F)_{E20} \;=\; 13.51 \; \mathrm{kg \; air/kg \; E20} $$

> **Interpretation:** E20's stoichiometric A/F (13.5) lies between ethanol (9.0) and gasoline (14.7), as expected. The engine ECU must adjust the injector pulse-width to deliver this leaner (relative to gasoline) mixture, or operate open-loop with a fuel composition sensor.

> *(7 marks: 1.5 marks for pure-ethanol A/F derivation recap, 1.5 marks for mass-fraction conversion, 2 marks for the A/F computation, 2 marks for interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Ethanol Questions (2024 KTU Board):**
>
> 1. **Confusing LHV (mass) with LHV (volume).** Students write "ethanol LHV = 26.9 MJ/L" — wrong, it is **MJ/kg**. The volumetric value is **21.2 MJ/L**. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per incorrect unit.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the energy-density penalty in dual-fuel problems.** A student who computes BSFC of an E85 engine *without* compensating for the 30 % lower LHV will over-predict efficiency by 30 %. KTU keys specifically check for the **LHV correction in step 3 of any energy-balance problem**.
>
> 3. **Treating ethanol–gasoline blend octane numbers as linear at all fractions.** The linear-by-volume rule is valid only up to **~ 30 % ethanol**; beyond E30, the curve *plateaus* or even dips slightly. KTU Part A questions frequently test this.
>
> 4. **Omitting the "renewable vs non-renewable" distinction** between bio-ethanol and synthetic ethanol. Examiners consider this a **mandatory 1-mark** in any production-route question.
>
> 5. **Mis-stating the A/F ratio in the combustion equation.** Writing "C₂H₅OH + 3 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O" is correct. Forgetting the 3.76 N₂ coefficient for air (in air-based stoichiometry) loses 0.5 mark.
>
> 6. **Drawing wrong dual-fuel block diagrams.** A common error is showing *both* fuels entering the cylinder via the same injector. The correct architecture: **diesel direct injection + ethanol port fumigation**. The KTU board deducts 1 mark for schematic errors.
>
> 7. **Confusing the **Azeotropic RVP bump** with a direct proportionality.** RVP of E10 is *higher* than both pure ethanol and pure gasoline due to azeotrope formation — not a simple linear blend.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Ethanol (Module 2)**

- [x] **Chemical identity:** C₂H₅OH, M = 46.07, ρ = 0.789 kg/L, LHV = 26.9 MJ/kg.
- [x] **Octane supremacy:** RON = 108–110, AKI ≈ 99 — premium substitute for tetraethyl lead.
- [x] **Stoichiometric A/F = 9.0** (mass) — much leaner than gasoline's 14.7.
- [x] **Combustion equation:** C₂H₅OH + 3 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O (no soot, no free C).
- [x] **Bound oxygen (34.8 %)** → ↓ CO, ↓ HC, ↓ PM; slight ↑ NO_x.
- [x] **Latent heat (840 kJ/kg)** → strong charge cooling → ↓ knock → ↑ efficiency.
- [x] **Laminar flame speed ≈ 50 cm/s** — 30 % faster than gasoline.
- [x] **Auto-ignition T = 363 °C** — high → difficult in pure CI mode without diesel pilot.
- [x] **Production:** fermentation (sugarcane, corn, molasses) vs hydration of ethylene (synthetic, non-renewable).
- [x] **2G ethanol** from lignocellulosic biomass via enzymatic hydrolysis (e.g., India 2G plants).
- [x] **Blends:** E5, E10, E20, E85, E100 — India EBP target E20 by 2025/26.
- [x] **FFV (Flex-Fuel Vehicle):** sensor-controlled engine running E0 to E85.
- [x] **Cold-start problem:** E85+ needs auxiliary gasoline priming; pure ethanol at < 10 °C will not start.
- [x] **Vapour pressure anomaly:** E10 shows an azeotropic RVP bump (~ 11–13 psi).
- [x] **Dual-fuel CI:** diesel pilot + ethanol fumigation → 70–80 % substitution, ↓ NO_x, ↓ PM, ↑ CO & HC.
- [x] **Energy density:** ~ 35 % volumetric deficit for E100 vs gasoline — needs 50 % larger tank.
- [x] **Material compatibility:** ethanol attacks aluminium, magnesium, certain elastomers — use stainless steel.
- [x] **Hygroscopicity:** absorbs water → phase separation in gasoline blends → engine misfire.
- [x] **Indian policy:** Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP 2003, revised 2018), National Policy on Biofuels 2018, OME tenders, 2G under JI-VAN Yojana.
- [x] **Brazil vs India context:** Brazil runs E25–E100 commercially since 1975 (ProÁlcool); India targets E20 by 2025.
- [x] **Engine efficiency gain:** + 2–5 % bth for E20 SI; + 8–12 % for dedicated E100 SI at 12:1 CR.
- [x] **Emissions rule of thumb:** CO ↓ 30 %, HC ↓ 8 %, NO_x ↑ 5 %, CO₂ ↓ 2–4 % for E20 vs E0.
- [x] **Key equations to commit:**
  - (A/F)_stoich for any fuel: 138 × (O + N/2) / (12C + H + 16O − 32O) kg air/kg fuel
  - LHV_vol = LHV × ρ
  - Energy equivalence ratio Φ_E = LHV_blend,vol / LHV_baseline,vol
  - Stoichiometric O₂ demand: A_st = (8/3)C + 8H − O kg O₂/kg fuel

> **End of Module 2 — Ethanol Notes.**
> *Next Topic:* Methanol, Butanol, and other alcohol fuels (Module 2 continuation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methanol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Methanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Methanol (CH₃OH)**, also known as **methyl alcohol** or **wood spirit**, is the simplest member of the alcohol family of organic compounds. From an automotive/energy engineering perspective, methanol is classified as an **oxygenated alternative liquid fuel** that can be produced from a wide variety of feedstocks (natural gas, coal, biomass, and even CO₂) and used either in **neat form (M100)** or in **blends with gasoline (M3–M85)** for spark-ignition (SI) engines, and with **ignition improvers** in compression-ignition (CI) engines.

It is structurally a single carbon atom bonded to three hydrogen atoms and a hydroxyl (-OH) group, giving it the general formula **CₙH₂ₙ₊₁OH with n = 1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Methanol is treated as a *first-generation synthetic liquid fuel* because, unlike ethanol, it is **not limited to biological fermentation** and can be synthesized catalytically from syngas (CO + 2H₂) using the ICI low-pressure process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of methanol as the **"coke" of the alcohol world**:
- Just as Coca-Cola is a simpler, more concentrated version of fruit juice compared to a smoothie, methanol is the **simplest, smallest, and cleanest-burning** alcohol.
- Compared to gasoline (a complex cocktail of 100+ hydrocarbons C₄–C₁₂), methanol is a **single, pure molecule**.
- Because of the **oxygen atom already built into its structure**, methanol "self-oxidizes" partially during combustion, producing **less soot and unburnt hydrocarbons** — much like a fire-starter that already has a bit of oxygen mixed in.
- The hydroxyl (-OH) group gives methanol a "polar" personality: it loves water (miscible in all ratios) and is hygroscopic (absorbs moisture from air), which is critical for handling safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Ethanol Cousin"**
> Methanol and ethanol are first cousins. Methanol = "methyl" drinking (1 carbon, very toxic for humans), Ethanol = "ethyl" drinking (2 carbons, the one in beer). Engine-wise, both act similarly — high octane, low cetane, oxygenated — but methanol carries **about half the energy density** of gasoline, so a fuel tank must be roughly **twice as large** to deliver the same mileage.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Thermochemical Constants (Board Exam Gold)

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical Formula | **CH₃OH** | Simplest alcohol |
| Molecular Weight | **32.04 kg/kmol** | Low → low stoichiometric A/F |
| Density (at 20 °C) | **792 kg/m³** | Lower than gasoline (~740 kg/m³) |
| Boiling Point | **64.7 °C** | Very low → high vapor-lock risk |
| Flash Point (closed cup) | **11 °C** | Highly flammable; safety hazard |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **463 °C** | High → poor cetane, hard to start in CI |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **19.9 MJ/kg** | About **half of gasoline** (44 MJ/kg) |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **22.7 MJ/kg** | |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | **1100 kJ/kg** | **~3.5× gasoline** → excellent charge cooling |
| Research Octane Number (RON) | **109** | Excellent knock resistance |
| Motor Octane Number (MON) | **89** | |
| Cetane Number | **~5** | Very poor → needs ignition improver for CI |
| Stoichiometric A/F Ratio (mass) | **6.43 : 1** | Lower than gasoline (14.7:1) |
| Flame Speed (laminar) | **52 cm/s** | Higher than gasoline |
| Flammability Limits (vol %) | **6.0 – 36.5** | Very wide → safer in lean burn |
| Energy Density (per litre) | **15.7 MJ/L** | About half of gasoline |
| Water Solubility | **Fully Miscible** | Cannot use phase-separation tests |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Hot Pick:** The student is expected to remember that methanol has **high octane (109 RON), high latent heat (1100 kJ/kg), low cetane (~5), and about half the heating value of gasoline**. These four numbers appear in nearly every KTU question on methanol.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Production Pathways — The "Methanol Triangle"

Methanol's greatest strategic advantage is **feedstock flexibility**. Any carbon- and hydrogen-containing source can, in principle, be converted into methanol via the syngas route. The unified equation for methanol synthesis is:

$$CO + 2H_2 \xrightleftharpoons[\text{250–280 °C, 50–100 bar}]{\text{Cu/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3}} CH_3OH \quad \Delta H_{298} = -90.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The two most industrially dominant pathways are described below.

### 2.1.1 From Natural Gas (Steam Reforming — the dominant route, ~70% of global methanol)

1. **Desulfurization:** Natural gas (mostly CH₄) is desulfurized to prevent catalyst poisoning.
2. **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):**
$$CH_4 + H_2O \xrightarrow{Ni \text{ cat., 800 °C}} CO + 3H_2$$
3. **Partial Oxidation (POX) — often combined with SMR for better H₂/CO ratio:**
$$CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$$
4. **Water–Gas Shift Reaction (WGS) — to adjust H₂/CO ratio to the ideal 2:1 for methanol:**
$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$$
5. **Methanol Synthesis in a Fixed-Bed Reactor** (ICI Low-Pressure Process) at 50–100 bar, 250–280 °C over a Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst.
6. **Distillation:** Crude methanol is purified to fuel-grade (>99.85% purity).

### 2.1.2 From Coal (Gasification)

$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \text{(endothermic)}$$

Coal is gasified with steam and oxygen to produce syngas, which is then shifted (CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂) and converted to methanol as in 2.1.1. Coal-to-methanol was historically dominant and is regaining importance in **"coal-rich" nations** for energy security (e.g., China, South Africa).

### 2.1.3 From Biomass (Bio-Methanol / "Biomethanol")

Biomass (wood, agricultural waste, MSW) is gasified to syngas, conditioned, and then fed to the methanol synthesis loop. This yields a **carbon-neutral** fuel when the biomass regrowth absorbs the CO₂ emitted in combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Green Methanol:** A growing area is **CO₂ hydrogenation**, where captured industrial CO₂ is combined with green H₂ (from electrolysis powered by solar/wind):
> $$CO_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH + H_2O$$
> This produces a fuel that is **net-carbon-negative** when paired with biomass.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and ratios below use the LaTeX `\vert` delimiter (not the markdown `pipe` character) to preserve table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Molecular weight of methanol | $M = 32.04$ | kg/kmol | One C + four H + one O |
| 2 | Stoichiometric combustion | $CH_3OH + 1.5\,O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$ | — | Mass O₂ = 1.5 × 32 = 48 kg |
| 3 | Stoichiometric air required | $m_{air} = 1.5 \times (32 + 3.76 \times 28)$ | kg | = 1.5 × 137.28 ≈ 205.92 kg |
| 4 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) | $(A/F)_{stoich} = 205.92 \div 32.04 \approx 6.43$ | — | **Far lower than gasoline's 14.7** |
| 5 | Volumetric A/F ratio (liquid fuel) | $(A/F)_{vol} = (A/F)_{mass} \times \dfrac{\rho_{air}}{\rho_{fuel}} = 6.43 \times \dfrac{1.2}{0.792}$ | — | ≈ 9.74 |
| 6 | Lower Heating Value | $LHV_{CH_3OH} = 19.9$ | MJ/kg | Reference: gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg |
| 7 | Energy per kg of stoichiometric mixture | $E_{mix} = \dfrac{LHV}{1 + (A/F)_{stoich}} = \dfrac{19.9}{7.43}$ | MJ/kg | ≈ 2.68 MJ/kg mixture |
| 8 | Knock resistance index | $K = RON - MON$ | — | Methanol: $109 - 89 = 20$ |
| 9 | Octane blending (RBOF) | $RON_{blend} \approx RON_{methanol} \cdot x + RON_{gasoline} \cdot (1-x)$ | — | Linear approximation |
| 10 | Carnot / Otto efficiency (ideal) | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | — | Unchanged by fuel choice |
| 11 | Mean effective pressure – fuel energy link | $IMEP = \dfrac{\eta_{th} \cdot LHV \cdot \dot{m}_f}{V_d \cdot n/2}$ | bar | Reduces with methanol due to low LHV |
| 12 | Air–Fuel equivalence ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | — | Lean → $\phi < 1$ |
| 13 | Volumetric fuel energy density | $E_V = LHV \times \rho$ | MJ/m³ | Methanol ≈ 15,760 MJ/m³ vs gasoline ≈ 32,400 |
| 14 | Latent heat fraction of induction | $\Delta T_{charge} = \dfrac{m_f \cdot h_{fg}}{m_{air} \cdot c_{p,air}}$ | K | Charge cooling effect |

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

- The **6.43:1 stoichiometric A/F ratio** is a *board exam classic*. It directly drives carburetor / injector sizing, ECU maps, and lambda-sensor calibration.
- The **half-energy density** of methanol dictates that a vehicle converted to M100 will need **roughly a 2× larger fuel tank** for the same range — a real packaging constraint in automotive design.
- The **1100 kJ/kg latent heat** acts as an internal "charge cooler," raising volumetric efficiency by 5–10% and allowing higher compression ratios (12:1 to 14:1) without knock — a clear engineering win.
- The **5–6 cetane** rating means methanol **cannot auto-ignite** in a normal diesel cycle; it requires glow plugs, spark assistance, or cetane improvers (e.g., diethyl ether, DME, ignition improver additives).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Summary:**
> ✅ Higher octane, cleaner burning, wider flammability, renewable feedstock
> ❌ Half the energy per litre, toxic, hygroscopic, corrosive to certain elastomers & metals
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio for Methanol

**Goal:** Compute the mass of air theoretically required to burn 1 kg of pure methanol completely.

### Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation
Methanol contains C, H, and O. One molecule of CH₃OH reacts with oxygen to give CO₂ and H₂O. Balancing:

$$CH_3OH + 1.5\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 5.64\,N_2$$

### Step 2 — Identify moles
- 1 mole of CH₃OH has mass = $12 + 4(1) + 16 = 32$ g
- 1 mole of O₂ = 32 g
- 1 mole of N₂ = 28 g

### Step 3 — Mass of oxygen required per kg of fuel
From the equation, 1 mole of fuel consumes 1.5 moles of O₂.
Mass of O₂ per mole of fuel = $1.5 \times 32 = 48$ g.
Per 1 kg of fuel, mass of O₂ = $\dfrac{48}{0.032} = 1500$ g = **1.5 kg O₂ per kg fuel**.

### Step 4 — Mass of air (air ≈ 23.2% O₂ by mass, or O₂:N₂ molar = 1:3.76)
Using the standard approximation, 1 mole of O₂ is accompanied by 3.76 moles of N₂:
Mass of accompanying N₂ = $1.5 \times 3.76 \times 28 = 157.92$ g.
Mass of air = mass of O₂ + mass of N₂ = $48 + 157.92 = 205.92$ g.

### Step 5 — Air–Fuel ratio (mass basis)
$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{205.92}{32.04} \approx 6.43$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} \approx 6.43 \text{ kg air per kg methanol}}$$

### Step 6 — Verification by mass balance (optional sanity check)
Reactants: $32 + 48 + 157.92 = 237.92$ g
Products: $44 + 2 \times 18 + 157.92 = 237.92$ g ✓ (mass is conserved)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the number matters:** Gasoline gives (A/F) ≈ 14.7. Methanol's much lower A/F (6.43) means a given engine displacement will ingest **less air mass per kg of fuel**, but each kg of fuel carries **less energy**. The product of the two (energy per kg of mixture) is what truly limits indicated torque.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Indicated Thermal Efficiency and the Effect of Compression Ratio

The **Otto-cycle** (ideal SI) efficiency depends only on the compression ratio $r$ and the working fluid's $\gamma$, not on the fuel:

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

For a methanol–air mixture (mostly products are CO₂, H₂O, N₂, with some dissociation at high T), $\gamma \approx 1.32$ to $1.35$.

| Compression Ratio $r$ | $\eta_{Otto}$ (γ = 1.35) | Knock Limit on Gasoline | Safe on Methanol? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 : 1 | 0.555 | At limit | ✅ Yes |
| 10 : 1 | 0.610 | Knock risk | ✅ Yes |
| 12 : 1 | 0.652 | Severe knock | ✅ Yes |
| 14 : 1 | 0.685 | Unusable | ✅ Yes (with high latent heat cooling) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Methanol's **high latent heat (1100 kJ/kg)** lowers the peak end-gas temperature by 30–50 °C, making it physically possible to run higher CR (12:1 to 16:1) than gasoline and gain real thermal efficiency.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy per kg of Combustible Mixture

Sometimes the question is "which fuel produces the most useful energy per kg of air–fuel mixture burned in the cylinder?" Define:

$$e_{mix} = \frac{LHV}{1 + (A/F)_{stoich}}$$

For methanol:
$$e_{mix,\,MeOH} = \frac{19.9}{1 + 6.43} = \frac{19.9}{7.43} \approx 2.68 \text{ MJ/kg mixture}$$

For gasoline ($LHV \approx 44$, $A/F = 14.7$):
$$e_{mix,\,gas} = \frac{44}{15.7} \approx 2.80 \text{ MJ/kg mixture}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprising result:** On a *per-kg-of-mixture* basis, methanol is **only ~4% inferior** to gasoline. The "half-energy-density" reputation of methanol is purely a **volumetric** problem, not a thermodynamic one. This is why high-compression, charge-cooled methanol engines can be made roughly competitive in indicated efficiency.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Volumetric Efficiency Boost from Charge Cooling

The mass of air inducted at standard conditions for a gasoline engine at $\phi = 1$ is $\dot{m}_{air} = 14.7\,\dot{m}_f$. When fuel is methanol with latent heat $h_{fg,f}$, the temperature drop in the intake is:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot h_{fg,f}}{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air}}$$

Substituting $\dot{m}_f / \dot{m}_{air} = 1 / (A/F)$:
$$\Delta T = \frac{h_{fg,f}}{(A/F) \cdot c_{p,air}}$$

For methanol, $(A/F) = 6.43$, $h_{fg,f} = 1100$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K):
$$\Delta T = \frac{1100}{6.43 \times 1.005} = \frac{1100}{6.462} \approx 17.0 \text{ K}$$

For gasoline, $(A/F) = 14.7$, $h_{fg,f} = 350$ kJ/kg:
$$\Delta T = \frac{350}{14.7 \times 1.005} = \frac{350}{14.77} \approx 23.7 \text{ K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive result:** Gasoline actually gives a *larger* per-cycle temperature drop because the A/F ratio is much higher (more air per kg of fuel). However, the absolute fuel mass per cycle for methanol is ~2.3× higher, so **the total latent heat absorbed per cycle is ~1.65× greater** for methanol, and the volumetric-efficiency benefit is real.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect on Engine Performance (For Board Solutions)

| Parameter | Direction of Change | Magnitude | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power output (volumetric basis) | ↓ Decreases | ~40–50% at same fuel flow | Half energy per litre |
| BSFC (brake specific fuel consumption) | ↑ Increases | ~2× gasoline | Low LHV |
| Brake thermal efficiency | ↑ Slightly increases | 2–5% points | High CR + charge cooling |
| Volumetric efficiency | ↑ Increases | 5–10% | Charge cooling, low boiling point |
| Knock resistance | ↑↑ Strongly increases | Allows CR 12:1+ | RON 109 |
| Cold start | ↓ Poor below 10 °C | Needs 5–15% gasoline | High latent heat + low vapor pressure at low T |
| HC emissions | ↓ Decreases | 30–60% lower | Oxygenated, lean burn possible |
| CO emissions | ↓ Decreases | 30–50% lower | Oxygenated, lean burn |
| NO_x emissions | ↔ Variable | Depends on λ | Lean → ↓ ; high T → ↑ |
| Soot / PM | ↓↓ Strongly decreases | Near zero | Oxygen in fuel |
| Aldehyde (HCHO) emissions | ↑↑ Strongly increases | 2–4× gasoline | Partial oxidation product |
| Material compatibility | Issues with | Zinc, brass, aluminium, certain elastomers | Methanol is corrosive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Methanol Production Pathways (Coal / NG / Biomass → Syngas → Methanol)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[feedstock A natural gas CH4] --> B1[desulfurization unit]
    A2[feedstock B coal or biomass C plus H2O] --> B2[gasifier]
    A3[feedstock C captured CO2] --> B3[electrolysis H2 supply]
    B1 --> C[primary reformer SMR 800 C nickel cat]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[water gas shift reactor WGS CO plus H2O gives CO2 plus H2]
    D --> E[syngas conditioning H2 to CO equals 2 to 1 ratio]
    E --> F[methanol synthesis reactor ICI low pressure 50 to 100 bar 250 to 280 C Cu ZnO Al2O3]
    F --> G[crude methanol distillation column]
    G --> H[fuel grade methanol 99.85 percent pure M100]
    H --> I1[blending with gasoline to M3 M15 M85]
    H --> I2[direct methanol fuel cell DMFC]
    H --> I3[spark ignition engine neat M100]
    H --> I4[compression ignition engine with ignition improver]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The three feedstocks (natural gas, coal/biomass, CO₂) all converge on the **syngas conditioning + methanol synthesis** loop. This single platform makes methanol the *most feedstock-flexible* liquid fuel in engineering practice.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an M100-Fuelled SI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL[M100 methanol tank] --> INJ[high pressure injector]
    AIR[intake air] --> MANI[plenum]
    MANI --> CYL[combustion chamber]
    INJ --> CYL
    CYL --> SPK[spark plug high energy]
    CYL --> EXH[exhaust manifold]
    EXH --> CAT[three way catalyst]
    CAT --> TAIL[tailpipe emissions]
    CYL --> COOL[jacket cooling]
    ECU[engine control unit ECU] --> INJ
    ECU --> SPK
    LAMB[wide band lambda sensor] --> ECU
    LAMB --> EXH
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Methanol Energy Conversion Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[CH3OH methanol] --> E1[internal combustion engine SI or CI]
    M --> E2[direct methanol fuel cell DMFC]
    M --> E3[reformer to H2 plus CO2]
    E3 --> E4[PEM fuel cell H2 pathway]
    M --> E5[burner industrial boiler]
    E1 --> W1[mechanical work and electricity]
    E2 --> W2[electricity direct electrochemical]
    E4 --> W3[electricity indirect]
    E5 --> W4[heat and steam]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common theory question asks "Discuss the use of methanol in energy systems." The diagram above gives examiners a complete answer in one view — IC engines, fuel cells (DMFC and indirect via reforming), and combustion-based thermal uses.

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Methanol vs. Gasoline vs. Ethanol vs. Diesel (Visual Summary)

| Property | Methanol | Ethanol | Gasoline | Diesel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | CH₃OH | C₂H₅OH | C₄–C₁₂ mix | C₁₀–C₂₁ mix |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 19.9 | 26.9 | 44.0 | 42.5 |
| RON | 109 | 108 | 92 | — |
| Cetane | 5 | 8 | 10–15 | 45–55 |
| Stoich A/F | 6.43 | 9.0 | 14.7 | 14.5 |
| Boiling Pt (°C) | 64.7 | 78.4 | 30–200 | 180–360 |
| Latent heat (kJ/kg) | 1100 | 904 | 350 | 250 |
| H₂O solubility | Miscible | Miscible | Immiscible | Immiscible |
| Toxicity | High (blindness) | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Source | NG/coal/biomass | Biomass fermentation | Crude oil | Crude oil |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"List any six important properties of methanol that make it suitable as a fuel for spark-ignition engines."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (concise, 6 points — half-mark each):**

1. **High Research Octane Number (RON ≈ 109)** → allows higher compression ratios (12:1–14:1) without knock, raising thermal efficiency.
2. **High latent heat of vaporization (1100 kJ/kg)** → provides strong charge cooling, increases volumetric efficiency by 5–10%.
3. **Wider flammability limits (6.0 – 36.5 vol %)** → enables stable lean-burn operation, improving part-load fuel economy.
4. **Faster laminar flame speed (≈ 52 cm/s)** → shorter combustion duration, more complete burn.
5. **Oxygenated molecule (built-in O atom)** → burns more completely, drastically reducing soot, HC, and CO emissions.
6. **Renewable / multi-feedstock synthesis (NG, coal, biomass, CO₂)** → energy security and lower life-cycle CO₂.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "methanol has high energy density" — **this is FALSE.** Methanol has only **half** the energy density of gasoline on a per-litre basis. Always quote the correct direction of change.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**"Why is methanol unsuitable as a fuel for unmodified compression-ignition (diesel) engines? Give two remedies."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (1 mark for problem + 1 mark per remedy, 3 total):**

**Problem:** Methanol has a very **low cetane number (≈ 5)** and a **high auto-ignition temperature (463 °C)**, so it does **not auto-ignite** at the high in-cylinder temperatures and pressures of a normal diesel cycle. The engine will misfire or fail to start.

**Remedies (any two, 1 mark each):**
