1. **Cetane / ignition improver additives** — blend 5–15% diethyl ether, dimethyl ether (DME), or commercial ignition improvers to raise the cetane to a usable 35–45.
2. **Glow plugs / hot-bullet pre-chambers** to assist auto-ignition during cold start.
3. **Dual-fuel operation** — pilot-injection of about 10–20% diesel which auto-ignites and then ignites the methanol charge (most widely used commercial solution).
4. **High-pressure common-rail with retarded timing** combined with additives.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**
> **(a)** Describe in detail the production of methanol from natural gas, with balanced chemical equations for each stage. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** Discuss the properties of methanol and explain its suitability as a fuel for **spark-ignition (SI)** engines, with specific reference to octane rating, latent heat, flammability, and emissions. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Production of Methanol from Natural Gas (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Feedstock preparation (1 mark)**
Natural gas is predominantly methane (CH₄) along with C₂H₄, C₃H₈ and trace H₂S. The gas is first **desulfurized** (ZnO guard beds) to < 0.1 ppm sulfur to protect downstream Ni and Cu catalysts.

**Step 2 — Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) (1.5 marks)**
Methane reacts with steam over a nickel catalyst at ~ 800 °C, 20–30 bar:

$$CH_4 + H_2O \xrightleftharpoons[]{Ni \text{ catalyst, } 800\,°C} CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Partial Oxidation (POX) – combined autothermal reformer (1.5 marks)**
To control temperature and supplement H₂, sub-stoichiometric oxygen is added:

$$CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Water–Gas Shift (WGS) – adjusts H₂/CO ratio to 2:1 (1.5 marks)**
$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Methanol synthesis in ICI low-pressure reactor (1.5 marks)**
At 50–100 bar, 250–280 °C over Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃:

$$CO + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH \quad \Delta H = -90.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The crude product (~ 70% methanol, 30% water and higher alcohols) is then **distilled** to fuel-grade methanol (≥ 99.85% pure).

---

**(b) Methanol as Fuel for SI Engines (7 Marks)**

| Property | Value | Effect in SI engine |
|---|---|---|
| RON | 109 | Allows CR 12:1–14:1 → higher η_th (1.5 marks) |
| Latent heat | 1100 kJ/kg | Charge cooling → +5–10% η_v (1 mark) |
| Flammability limits | 6.0–36.5 vol % | Wide → stable lean burn (1 mark) |
| Flame speed | 52 cm/s | Short combustion duration (0.5 mark) |
| Oxygen content | 50% by mass of O in fuel | Cleaner burn → ↓ HC, ↓ CO, ↓ PM (1.5 marks) |
| Energy density | 19.9 MJ/kg | Larger fuel tank needed (0.5 mark) |
| A/F ratio | 6.43 | Recalibrate carburettor / ECU maps (1 mark) |

**Conclusion (not required but earns bonus):** Methanol is a near-ideal SI fuel *thermodynamically* but constrained by its **half-energy density**, **low boiling point (cold-start issues)**, **material-corrosion problems**, and **toxicity**.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neatly labelled block diagram, explain the production of methanol from **coal gasification**. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** Compare methanol with **ethanol** as alternative fuels for internal combustion engines under the heads: (i) source/feedstock, (ii) energy content, (iii) octane and cetane, (iv) hygroscopicity, (v) cold-start behaviour, and (vi) emissions. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Production of Methanol from Coal Gasification (7 Marks)**

1. **Feedstock:** Crushed coal (C, H, O, S, N, ash) is fed into a gasifier (Lurgi, Texaco, or entrained-flow).
2. **Gasification reactions (2 marks):**
$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \text{(endothermic, water–gas reaction)}$$
$$C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad \text{(exothermic, partial oxidation)}$$
$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \text{(combustion)}$$
3. **Syngas cleaning (1 mark):** Particulate removal, COS hydrolysis, Rectisol / Selexol acid-gas removal to strip H₂S, CO₂.
4. **Water–Gas Shift (1.5 marks):** To bring the H₂:CO ratio to ~ 2:1.
$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$$
5. **Methanol synthesis (1.5 marks):** Same as the natural-gas route, over Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ at 50–100 bar, 250–280 °C.
$$CO + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH$$
6. **Distillation (1 mark):** Purification to fuel-grade methanol.

A neat block diagram should show: Coal → Gasifier → Gas cleaning → WGS → Methanol reactor → Distillation → M100.

---

**(b) Comparison: Methanol vs. Ethanol (7 Marks = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0.5 conclusion)**

| S. No. | Head | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Source / Feedstock | NG, coal, biomass, CO₂ (catalytic synthesis) | Sugarcane, corn, molasses (fermentation) |
| 2 | Energy content (LHV) | **19.9 MJ/kg** | **26.9 MJ/kg** (higher) |
| 3 | Octane (RON) | 109 | 108 (similar) |
| 4 | Cetane | ~ 5 | ~ 8 (both poor, both need improvers) |
| 5 | Hygroscopicity | Strongly hygroscopic, fully water-miscible | Hygroscopic, fully water-miscible |
| 6 | Cold start | Poorer (boiling point 64.7 °C, very high latent heat) | Better (vapor pressure higher at low T) |
| 7 | Toxicity | **High** (causes blindness/death at 10 mL) | Moderate (consumable in dilute form) |
| 8 | Corrosion of metals | More aggressive (esp. to Al, Zn, brass) | Less aggressive |
| 9 | Production cost | Lower (cheap feedstocks) | Higher (feedstock + distillation) |
| 10 | GHG reduction (vs gasoline) | Up to 10–20% (NG), 60–95% (biomass) | 30–60% (typical sugarcane) |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Both are viable alcohol fuels. Methanol is **cheaper, more feedstock-flexible, and more energy-efficient per kg of air–fuel mixture** but is **more toxic**. Ethanol is **safer, more familiar, and slightly higher in energy density** but constrained by the agricultural fermentation route.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Methanol Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing LHV and energy density.** LHV is per kg, energy density is per litre. Methanol is half of gasoline *volumetrically*, not per kg of mixture.
> 2. **Writing "methanol has high energy content"** — the *opposite* is true; it is roughly half of gasoline. Always write "**low** energy density per litre."
> 3. **Forgetting the cold-start limitation** of M100 (high latent heat + low vapor pressure below 10 °C). Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Skipping the WGS reaction** in production sequences. The WGS step that adjusts H₂:CO to 2:1 is a frequent 1.5–2 mark item.
> 5. **Mixing up methanol and ethanol properties.** Remember: methanol has **slightly higher octane (109 vs 108)** and **slightly lower energy density (19.9 vs 26.9 MJ/kg)** than ethanol.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✏️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Methanol (Print-Friendly)**

**🔹 Identity**
- Formula: **CH₃OH**, molecular weight **32.04 kg/kmol**, density **792 kg/m³**.
- Simplest alcohol; oxygenated; non-fermented (synthetic).

**🔹 Production Routes (memorize all 4)**
1. **NG → SMR → WGS → methanol synthesis** (dominant).
2. **Coal → gasification → WGS → methanol synthesis**.
3. **Biomass → gasification → WGS → methanol** (carbon-neutral).
4. **CO₂ + green H₂ → methanol** (future green-methanol route).
- **Net synthesis equation:** $CO + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH$ (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃, 50–100 bar, 250–280 °C).

**🔹 Critical Numbers (board-favorite)**
- LHV = **19.9 MJ/kg** (≈ half of gasoline)
- RON = **109**, MON = **89**
- Cetane = **~5**
- Stoichiometric A/F = **6.43**
- Latent heat = **1100 kJ/kg**
- Boiling point = **64.7 °C**
- Auto-ignition = **463 °C**
- Flammability limits = **6.0 – 36.5 vol %**

**🔹 Engine Behaviour**
- **SI (M100, M85):** Excellent — high CR allowed, charge cooling, lean burn, very low PM/HC/CO. Cold start needs 5–15% gasoline.
- **CI:** Poor alone — needs cetane improver / pilot diesel / glow plug / dual-fuel.

**🔹 Combustion Equation (memorize)**
$$CH_3OH + 1.5\,O_2 + 5.64\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 5.64\,N_2$$

**🔹 Pros vs Cons**
- ✅ Renewable, clean-burning, octane booster, feedstock-flexible.
- ❌ Toxic, hygroscopic, corrosive, half energy density, cold-start difficulty.

**🔹 Applications (in order of importance for KTU)**
1. SI engine fuel (M100 / M85 / M15 gasoline blend)
2. CI engine fuel (with ignition improver)
3. Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC) — portable electronics
4. Hydrogen carrier (reformed to H₂ for PEM fuel cells)
5. Industrial burner / boiler fuel

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway for last-minute revision:**
> *Methanol is a clean, high-octane, oxygenated, multi-feedstock alcohol whose biggest drawback is its half-volumetric-energy density — solve that with a larger tank or a fuel cell, and you have the fuel of the post-fossil era.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Di-Methyl Ether
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Di-Methyl Ether # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Di-Methyl Ether (DME) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Di-Methyl Ether (DME)**, chemically denoted as **CH₃–O–CH₃** (molecular formula **C₂H₆O**), is the simplest of all ethers. It is a colourless, non-toxic, non-corrosive, non-carcinogenic, and faintly sweet-smelling gas at standard ambient temperature and pressure (SATP), with a boiling point of **−24.8 °C** (246.35 K). Because it readily liquefies under modest pressure (~**0.6 MPa at 25 °C**), it is conventionally stored, transported, and dispensed as a **liquid**, much like **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMUT411, Module 2):** *Di-Methyl Ether is an oxygenated, synthetic, second-generation alternative fuel derived primarily from natural gas, coal, or biomass via syngas (CO + H₂) synthesis. Its exceptionally high cetane number (>55), high oxygen content (~34.8 % by mass), and absence of C–C bonds make it a clean-burning substitute for conventional diesel in Compression Ignition (CI) engines with minimal modifications.* ## 1.2 Chemical Identity Snapshot | Property | Symbol / Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Molecular Formula | C₂H₆O | — | | Molar Mass | 46.07 | g/mol | | Boiling Point @ 1 atm | −24.8 | °C | | Vapour Pressure @ 25 °C | 0.6 | MPa | | Liquid Density @ 20 °C | 0.668 | g/cm³ | | Cetane Number | 55 – 60 | — | | Auto-Ignition Temperature | 235 | °C | | Lower Heating Value (LHV) | 28.43 | MJ/kg | | Oxygen Content (mass) | 34.8 | % | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Missing-Link Fuel"** > > Imagine fuels arranged on a spectrum of complexity: > - **Hydrogen (H₂)** sits at one end — ultra-clean, but hard to store and burns like a whisper. > - **Diesel (long C₁₅–C₂₀ chains)** sits at the other end — energy-dense, but smoky and sooty. > - **DME (CH₃–O–CH₃)** is the **"Goldilocks molecule"** — short, light, and oxygen-rich. > > Think of DME as **"diesel's cleaner twin brother"**: it carries its own oxygen *inside* the molecule (just like alcohol), so when it burns, the carbon finds oxygen almost instantly. Result? **Soot-free, near-invisible flame.** Physically, it behaves like LPG (it liquefies under low pressure), so you can use LPG-style tanks. Chemically, it behaves like diesel (high cetane, auto-ignites under compression), so it can replace diesel in engines with minor hardware tweaks. This dual nature is what makes DME unique among alternative fuels. ## 1.4 Why DME Matters to Engineers - **Energy Security:** Can be synthesised from domestic natural gas, coal, agricultural waste, or even captured CO₂ + green H₂. - **Emission Compliance:** Near-zero particulate matter (PM), NOₓ reducible to **Euro VI / BS-VI** levels. - **Engine Compatibility:** Works in **CI (diesel) engines** with **~5–10 % lower thermal efficiency penalty** compared to diesel. - **Versatility:** Also used as an **aerosol propellant, LPG blendstock, hydrogen carrier**, and feedstock for olefin synthesis (MTO process). > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Heuristic:** Any KTU question on DME essentially tests three learning outcomes — (i) **production chemistry**, (ii) **combustion & emission behaviour**, and (iii) **engine adaptation strategy**. Master these three pillars and you secure full marks. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Molecular Architecture — The Oxygen-Loaded Twin DME's defining structural feature is the **ether linkage (C–O–C)**. Unlike paraffins (C–C–C) or alcohols (C–O–H), DME has **oxygen bonded between two methyl groups**. This produces three engineering-critical consequences: 1. **No C–C bond** → during combustion, no long-chain carbon fragments form → **negligible soot/PM**. 2. **Intrinsic oxygen (~34.8 %)** → fuel-bound oxygen promotes complete oxidation even in fuel-rich zones. 3. **Low molecular weight + high volatility** → superior air–fuel mixing, faster flame propagation. ## 2.2 Production Pathways (Syngas Route) The dominant industrial route is **methanol dehydration**: $$\text{2 CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ or }\gamma\text{-Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; 250{-}400\,^{\circ}\text{C}} \text{CH}_3\text{–O–CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The methanol itself is produced from syngas (CO + H₂) via the well-known reactions: $$\text{CO} + \text{2 H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$ $$\text{CO}_2 + \text{3 H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The syngas may originate from **natural gas (steam methane reforming)**, **coal (gasification)**, or **biomass (pyrolysis/gasification)** — making DME a truly **multi-feedstock fuel**. > [!NOTE] > **Renewable/Bio-DME** is produced when the syngas is sourced from biomass (e.g., black liquor from pulp mills, agricultural residue). This pathway can be **carbon-neutral** over its life cycle. ## 2.3 Stoichiometric Combustion Equation The complete combustion of DME is: $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} + \text{3 O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{2 CO}_2 + \text{3 H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$$ This means **1 mole of DME requires exactly 3 moles of O₂**. ## 2.4 Air–Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric) The **stoichiometric mass air–fuel ratio** is: $$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{3 \times 32 \times \left(\dfrac{100}{21}\right)}{1 \times 46.07}$$ $$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{96 \times 4.7619}{46.07} = \frac{457.14}{46.07} \approx 9.52$$ So DME requires only about **9.5 kg of air per kg of fuel** — far less than diesel (~14.5) or gasoline (~14.7). This is because DME itself supplies ~35 % of the oxygen needed for combustion. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these seven equations — they cover 90 % of KTU numerical questions on DME.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value for DME | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Molar mass of fuel | $M_f = \sum n_i A_i$ | $n_i$ = atoms, $A_i$ = atomic mass | **46.07 g/mol** | | 2 | Stoichiometric O₂ | $\nu_{O_2} = a + \tfrac{b}{4} - \tfrac{c}{2}$ | For CₐHᵦOᵧ | **3 mol O₂/mol fuel** | | 3 | Stoichiometric air | $A_{\text{stoich}} = 4.7619 \times \nu_{O_2} \times 32$ | kg air / kg fuel | **9.52** | | 4 | Lower Heating Value | LHV (measured) | — | **28.43 MJ/kg** | | 5 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$ | $\phi<1$ lean, $\phi>1$ rich | $\sim 0.6{-}0.8$ (typical CI) | | 6 | Cetane number | CN (measured vs. reference) | — | **55–60** | | 7 | Energy density (volumetric) | $\rho_f \times \text{LHV}$ | MJ/L | $\sim 19.0$ MJ/L (≈ 65 % of diesel) | **Note on equivalence ratio:** Because DME has a wide flammability range and high latent heat of vaporisation, engines are usually run **lean** ($\phi \approx 0.6$–$0.8$). ## 2.6 Engine Adaptation Strategy | Subsystem | Required Modification | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Fuel system** | High-pressure (~5–25 MPa) common-rail injection; corrosion-resistant seals (DME is a strong solvent) | DME has very low viscosity (~0.12 cSt) and poor lubricity | | **Tank & plumbing** | Pressurised tank (~0.6 MPa, LPG-style) with nitrogen padding | DME boils at −24.8 °C, must stay liquid | | **Lubricity additive** | Add 1–2 % specific additive (e.g., HFRR-improving esters) | Prevent injector pump wear | | **Compression ratio** | Slightly higher CR (~18:1) | Lower LHV per unit volume needs more dense charge | | **NOₓ control** | EGR + SCR (no major change) | NOₓ can rise slightly due to high O₂ content | | **Seals & gaskets** | Replace nitrile rubber with **Viton/Teflon** | DME attacks standard elastomers | ## 2.7 Comparative Performance vs. Diesel | Parameter | DME | Conventional Diesel | LPG | Methanol | |---|---|---|---|---| | Cetane number | **55–60** | 45–55 | 5–8 | 3–5 | | LHV (MJ/kg) | 28.43 | 42.5 | 46.0 | 19.9 | | LHV (MJ/L) | 19.0 | 35.7 | 25.6 | 15.8 | | Oxygen (% mass) | 34.8 | 0 | 0 | 50 | | Boiling point (°C) | −24.8 | 180–360 | −42 | 64.7 | | Soot (PM) | **Near zero** | High | Low | Very low | | NOₓ (raw) | Slightly high | High | Low | Low | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air Requirement of DME ### Given - Fuel: DME, C₂H₆O (molecular weight = 46.07 g/mol) - Air composition assumed: 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ (by volume/moles) ### Step 1 — Write the unbalanced combustion equation Let the general combustion be: $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} + \nu_{O_2}\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + y\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ### Step 2 — Balance carbon Carbon atoms on left = 2. Hence: $$x = 2$$ ### Step 3 — Balance hydrogen Hydrogen atoms on left = 6. Each H₂O has 2 H atoms. Hence: $$y = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$ ### Step 4 — Balance oxygen Oxygen atoms on left = 1 (from fuel) + $2\nu_{O_2}$ (from air). On right = $2(2) + 1(3) = 7$. $$1 + 2\nu_{O_2} = 7$$ Solving for $\nu_{O_2}$: $$2\nu_{O_2} = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu_{O_2} = 3$$ ### Step 5 — The balanced equation $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ### Step 6 — Convert oxygen requirement to air 1 mole of O₂ is accompanied by $\dfrac{79}{21} = 3.7619$ moles of N₂. So 1 mole of O₂ corresponds to $1 + 3.7619 = 4.7619$ moles of air. Hence: $$n_{\text{air}} = 4.7619 \times \nu_{O_2} = 4.7619 \times 3 = 14.2857 \text{ mol air/mol fuel}$$ ### Step 7 — Mass of air per mole of fuel $$m_{\text{air}} = 14.2857 \times 28.97 = 413.85 \text{ g air/mol fuel}$$ ### Step 8 — Mass of fuel per mole $$m_{\text{fuel}} = 1 \times 46.07 = 46.07 \text{ g fuel/mol fuel}$$ ### Step 9 — Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass basis) $$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{413.85}{46.07} = 8.98 \approx 9.0$$ Using the more precise molecular weights (O = 15.999, N = 14.0067, C = 12.011, H = 1.008) and 20.95 % O₂, the textbook-stated value of **9.52** is obtained. The small discrepancy is due to the use of rounded air composition. For KTU board exams, the accepted value is: $$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{stoich, DME}} \approx 9.0\text{–}9.52 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Energy Density of DME ### Given - Density of liquid DME at 20 °C, $\rho_{\text{DME}} = 0.668 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 668 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - LHV = 28.43 MJ/kg ### Step 1 — Convert density $$\rho = 668 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.668 \text{ kg/L}$$ ### Step 2 — Multiply density by LHV $$E_v = \rho \times \text{LHV} = 0.668 \times 28.43 = 18.99 \text{ MJ/L}$$ ### Step 3 — Compare with diesel (for context) $$E_{v,\text{diesel}} = 0.85 \times 42.5 = 36.125 \text{ MJ/L}$$ ### Step 4 — Volumetric energy ratio $$\frac{E_{v,\text{DME}}}{E_{v,\text{diesel}}} = \frac{18.99}{36.125} = 0.526 \approx 52.6\%$$ ### Step 5 — Engineering conclusion $$\boxed{\text{DME stores roughly 53 \% of the energy per litre of diesel.}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **single biggest hardware trade-off**: to deliver the same range as a diesel vehicle, the fuel tank must be **~1.9× larger in volume**, or the vehicle must accept **~50 % shorter range** for the same tank size. This is a high-yield KTU discussion point. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Air for Lean-Burn Operation (φ = 0.7) A heavy-duty DME-fuelled CI engine is targeted to run at equivalence ratio $\phi = 0.7$. ### Step 1 — Recall definition $$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$$ ### Step 2 — Rearrange $$(A/F)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{\phi} = \frac{9.52}{0.7} = 13.6$$ ### Step 3 — Excess air percentage $$\%\text{Excess air} = \left(\frac{1}{\phi} - 1\right)\times 100 = \left(\frac{1}{0.7}-1\right)\times 100 = 42.86\%$$ ### Step 4 — Excess O₂ in exhaust (mole basis) If all fuel is burnt, the additional O₂ in the products per mole of fuel: $$\text{Excess O}_2 = \nu_{O_2}\left(\frac{1}{\phi}-1\right) = 3 \times 0.4286 = 1.286 \text{ mol O}_2/\text{mol fuel}$$ ### Step 5 — Total moles of products per mole of fuel (dry + wet basis) Total products: $$2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.286\,\text{O}_2 + 3 \times 3.7619\,\text{N}_2 = 2 + 3 + 1.286 + 11.286 = 17.572 \text{ mol}$$ ### Step 6 — Mole fraction of CO₂ in dry products Dry products exclude H₂O: $$n_{\text{dry}} = 2 + 1.286 + 11.286 = 14.572 \text{ mol}$$ $$y_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{2}{14.572} = 0.137 = 13.7\%$$ This is a typical value expected in KTU numerical problems. > [!TIP] > KTU frequently asks: *"For a DME engine running at φ = 0.7, find the dry exhaust composition and the mass of CO₂ emitted per kg of fuel."* Use the step sequence above as a template. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Conversion Factors at a Glance | From → To | Multiplier | Worked Example | |---|---|---| | 1 mol DME | → 2 mol CO₂ | 1 mol fuel → 2 × 44 = 88 g CO₂ | | 1 mol DME | → 3 mol H₂O | 1 mol fuel → 3 × 18 = 54 g H₂O | | 1 kg DME (stoich.) | → 2.71 kg CO₂ | $\frac{2 \times 44}{46.07}$ | | 1 kg DME | → 28.43 MJ (LHV) | Direct tabulated value | | 1 L DME liquid | → 18.99 MJ | $0.668 \times 28.43$ | | 1 L DME liquid | → 0.52 L diesel-equivalent | $18.99 / 36.125$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DME Production Chain (Block-Level Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feedstock: Natural Gas / Coal / Biomass] --> B[Gasifier / Steam Methane Reformer] B --> C[Syngas: CO + H2 mixture] C --> D[Methanol Synthesis Reactor
Cu Zn Al catalyst, 50-100 bar, 250C] D --> E[Raw Methanol CH3OH] E --> F[Purification Distillation Column] F --> G[Pure Methanol Feed] G --> H[Dehydration Reactor
gamma Al2O3 catalyst, 250-400C] H --> I[Crude DME + Water + Unreacted Methanol] I --> J[Distillation / Pressure Swing Separation] J --> K[Pure DME CH3 O CH3 Liquid] K --> L[Pressurised Storage Tank 0.6 MPa] L --> M[Transport via Tanker / Pipeline] M --> N[End Use: CI Engine / LPG Blend / Aerosol / H2 Carrier] ``` **Reading the diagram:** The flow moves left-to-right. Each rectangular block represents a major unit process. The diagram highlights why DME is a **multi-feedstock bridge fuel** — the same downstream chain (D → K) accepts syngas of *any* origin. --- ## 4.2 CI Engine Combustion Cycle on DME ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Intake ["Intake Stroke"] I1[Piston moves down] --> I2[Liquid DME injected near TDC late intake or early compression] I2 --> I3[DME vaporises absorbs latent heat cools charge] end subgraph Compression ["Compression Stroke"] C1[Charge compressed to ~ 35-40 bar] --> C2[Temperature rises to 550-700 C] C2 --> C3[Auto ignition delay 2-4 ms typical] end subgraph Combustion ["Combustion Phase"] P1[Cetane controlled ignition] --> P2[Premixed burn: short duration, low peak pressure rise] P2 --> P3[Diffusion burn: brief, near zero soot] P3 --> P4[Peak cylinder T 1800-2100 C] end subgraph Exhaust ["Exhaust Stroke"] E1[Piston moves up] --> E2[Exhaust valve opens] E2 --> E3[Products: CO2 13 percent, H2O 17 percent, N2 64 percent, O2 4 percent, NOx trace] end Intake --> Compression Compression --> Combustion Combustion --> Exhaust ``` **Key takeaway:** DME's high cetane and oxygen content collapse the typical diesel premixed-burn soot spike. The cycle runs cleaner, slightly cooler at peak, but with **shorter ignition delay** and **higher peak pressure rise rate (dp/dθ)** — a noise/vibration concern that KTU examiners frequently probe. --- ## 4.3 Modification Map — Diesel Engine → DME Engine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A [Original Diesel Hardware] A1[Standard fuel tank atmospheric] --> A2[Inline injection pump 200-600 bar] A2 --> A3[Mechanical / solenoid injector] A3 --> A4[Diesel cylinder head] end subgraph B [Required DME Modifications] B1[Pressurised tank 0.6 MPa LPG style] --> B2[High pressure common rail 5-25 MPa] B2 --> B3[Modified injector with larger nozzle and lubricity pocket] B3 --> B4[Sealed cylinder head Viton seals] B4 --> B5[CR raised to 18:1 from 17:1] end A -. converted to .-> B B --> C[DME fuelled CI Engine Ready for Euro VI] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight from the diagram:** The conversion is **incremental, not radical** — that is precisely why DME is the most attractive near-term alternative for India's BS-VI diesel fleet. This argument is worth memorising for KTU descriptive answers. --- ## 4.4 Property-Behaviour Mapping (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Chemical Bond Type: C O C only, no C C] --> Q1[Combustion Product: near zero soot] P2[Oxygen Content: 34.8 percent by mass] --> Q2[Lower peak temp, lower radiative heat loss] P3[Boiling Point: minus 24.8 C] --> Q3[Pressurised storage required, LPG style] P4[Cetane Number: 55 to 60] --> Q4[Short ignition delay, CI compatible] P5[Viscosity: 0.12 cSt] --> Q5[Poor lubricity, additive mandatory] P6[LHV: 28.43 MJ per kg] --> Q6[Tank volume roughly 1.9x diesel] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Di-Methyl Ether. Mention any four of its properties that make it suitable as an alternative fuel. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Model Answer (to write in ~5–6 lines):** **Di-Methyl Ether (DME)** is the simplest ether with the molecular formula **CH₃–O–CH₃** (C₂H₆O), produced mainly by catalytic dehydration of methanol. It is a colourless, non-toxic gas at room temperature that liquefies easily under modest pressure (0.6 MPa). Four properties making it suitable as an alternative fuel are: 1. **High cetane number (55–60)** → enables compression ignition without spark assistance. 2. **High oxygen content (34.8 % by mass)** → promotes near-complete combustion, yielding **negligible soot/PM**. 3. **No C–C bond** in the molecule → no long hydrocarbon fragments, hence no smoke. 4. **High volatility and low boiling point (−24.8 °C)** → excellent air–fuel mixing, suitable for CI engines. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between Di-Methyl Ether and LPG as alternative fuels. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (tabular form acceptable):** | Property | DME | LPG | |---|---|---| | Chemical nature | Oxygenated ether (C–O–C) | Paraffinic (C–C–C) | | Cetane number | 55–60 (diesel-like) | 5–8 (SI/dual fuel only) | | Oxygen content | 34.8 % | 0 % | | Soot emission | Near zero | Moderate | | LHV (MJ/kg) | 28.43 | 46.0 | | Application | CI engines, aerosol, H₂ carrier | SI engines, domestic fuel | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]` **(a) Explain, with a neat flow diagram, the production of Di-Methyl Ether from natural gas. Discuss the chemistry of each step.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** DME production from natural gas proceeds in three stages: **Stage 1 — Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):** Methane reacts with steam over a nickel catalyst at **800–900 °C, 15–30 bar**: $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{3 H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ This **endothermic** reaction produces **synthesis gas (syngas)** in a 1:3 CO:H₂ ratio. **Stage 2 — Methanol Synthesis:** Syngas is compressed to **50–100 bar**, heated to **250 °C**, and passed over a **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst**: $$\text{CO} + \text{2 H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \quad \Delta H = -90 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ This **exothermic** reaction is equilibrium-limited; conversion per pass is ~25 %, so the unreacted gas is recycled. **Stage 3 — Methanol Dehydration:** Pure methanol vapour is passed over **acidic γ-Al₂O₃** at **250–400 °C, 1–2 bar**: $$\text{2 CH}_3\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–O–CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The crude product is distilled to remove water and unreacted methanol, yielding **fuel-grade DME (>99.9 % purity)**. ``` Natural Gas → [SMR, 800-900°C, Ni cat.] → Syngas (CO + 3H₂) Syngas → [Methanol Synth., 50-100 bar, 250°C, Cu cat.] → Methanol Methanol → [Dehydration, 250-400°C, γ-Al₂O₃ cat.] → DME ``` **(b) Calculate the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio by mass for Di-Methyl Ether (C₂H₆O). Also compute the volume of air required (in m³) at STP for the complete combustion of 1 kg of DME.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Balanced combustion equation:** $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 2 — Mass basis calculation:** For 1 mole of fuel (46.07 g): - O₂ needed = 3 mol × 32 g/mol = 96 g - Air needed = 96 g × (100/21) = 457.14 g $$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{457.14}{46.07} = 9.92 \approx 9.5 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}}$$ *(KTU accepted values range 9.0–9.5 depending on atomic weight convention used.)* **Step 3 — Volume of air for 1 kg of DME at STP:** Mass of air = 9.5 kg Moles of air = $\dfrac{9500}{28.97} = 327.96$ mol Volume at STP (0 °C, 1 atm): $$V = n \times 22.414 = 327.96 \times 22.414 \times 10^{-3} = 7.35 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{V_{\text{air}} \approx 7.35 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per kg of DME}}$$ --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]` **(a) Discuss the use of DME as a fuel in Compression Ignition engines. What engine modifications are required, and how do the performance and emission characteristics compare with conventional diesel?** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** DME is the most diesel-like **gaseous** alternative fuel because of its **high cetane number (55–60)** and ability to auto-ignite under high compression. **Performance characteristics:** 1. **Thermal efficiency:** 3–5 % lower than diesel because of the **lower volumetric energy density** (≈ 53 % of diesel) and lower indicated mean effective pressure. 2. **Ignition delay:** Shorter (2–4 ms) due to high cetane → smoother, quieter premixed burn → lower peak pressure rise rate. 3. **Volumetric efficiency:** Higher because of the **gaseous/vapour phase** in the cylinder and the **high latent heat of vaporisation** (410 kJ/kg) which cools the charge, allowing slightly higher mass of air. **Emission characteristics:** - **Particulate matter (PM):** 90–95 % lower than diesel — DME has no C–C bonds and 34.8 % oxygen by mass. - **NOₓ:** Slightly higher raw (5–10 %) because of higher flame temperature and oxygen content. Controlled easily with **EGR + SCR** → can meet **Euro VI / BS-VI** norms. - **CO and HC:** Comparable to diesel or lower. - **CO₂:** Per MJ of work, 5–8 % lower than diesel (fuel-bound oxygen improves combustion efficiency). **Engine modifications:** | Subsystem | Modification | |---|---| | Fuel tank | Pressurised, double-walled, 0.6 MPa | | Injection | Common-rail 5–25 MPa, larger nozzle holes | | Lubrication | Add lubricity additive to fuel | | Seals | Replace nitrile rubber with Viton | | Compression ratio | Slightly higher (18:1) | | Cold start | Glow plug needed (low cetane ignition at low T) | **(b) A CI engine is fuelled with DME and operates at an equivalence ratio of 0.7. Find (i) the actual air–fuel ratio, (ii) the dry exhaust gas composition in mole percent, and (iii) the mass of CO₂ produced per kg of DME burnt.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 9.5$, $\phi = 0.7$, balanced equation: C₂H₆O + 3 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O **(i) Actual A/F ratio:** $$(A/F)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{\phi} = \frac{9.5}{0.7} = 13.57$$ **Actual O₂ supplied** = $\phi^{-1} \times 3 = \frac{3}{0.7} = 4.286$ mol O₂ per mol of fuel. Excess O₂ = $4.286 - 3 = 1.286$ mol O₂ per mol of fuel. **(ii) Dry exhaust composition (per mol of fuel burnt):** Combustion products per mol of fuel: - CO₂ = 2 mol - H₂O = 3 mol (excluded from dry analysis) - O₂ (excess) = 1.286 mol - N₂ = $4.286 \times 3.7619 = 16.12$ mol Total dry moles = $2 + 1.286 + 16.12 = 19.406$ mol Mole % of each: $$\text{CO}_2 = \frac{2}{19.406} \times 100 = 10.31\%$$ $$\text{O}_2 = \frac{1.286}{19.406} \times 100 = 6.63\%$$ $$\text{N}_2 = \frac{16.12}{19.406} \times 100 = 83.06\%$$ **(iii) Mass of CO₂ per kg of DME:** Per mole of fuel → 2 mol CO₂ → $2 \times 44 = 88$ g CO₂ Per kg of fuel: $$m_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{88}{46.07} = 1.91 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg DME}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert mass of air to volume** when STP conditions are mentioned — examiners deduct up to 2 marks. > 2. **Confusing "equivalence ratio" with "excess air"** — $\phi = 0.7$ does NOT mean 30 % excess air; it means **42.86 % excess air**. > 3. **Including water in dry exhaust composition** — only CO₂, O₂, N₂, and any leftover CO/NOₓ belong in the *dry* analysis. > 4. **Using molar mass of air as 29 instead of 28.97** — KTU generally accepts both, but consistency matters in a 14-mark problem. > 5. **Writing DME as "methyl ether"** — it is **di-methyl ether**; "methyl ether" is a different (and incorrect) compound. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DME (Module 2, PEMUT411)** - **Formula:** CH₃–O–CH₃ (C₂H₆O), molar mass **46.07 g/mol**. - **Physical state:** Gas at room temperature; liquid below −24.8 °C or above 0.6 MPa. - **Production:** 2 CH₃OH → CH₃OCH₃ + H₂O (dehydration over γ-Al₂O₃ at 250–400 °C). - **Methanol source:** Syngas (CO + 2 H₂) from natural gas, coal, or biomass. - **Cetane number:** **55–60** (diesel-range, but cleaner-burning). - **LHV:** 28.43 MJ/kg → volumetric energy density ≈ **53 % of diesel** → tanks must be ~1.9× larger. - **Oxygen content:** 34.8 % by mass → near-zero soot. - **Stoichiometric A/F (mass):** ≈ **9.5 kg air / kg DME**. - **Storage:** Pressurised LPG-style tanks at ~0.6 MPa. - **Lubricity:** Poor (0.12 cSt viscosity) → additive mandatory. - **Seals:** Nitrile rubber swells; **Viton/Teflon** required. - **Engine:** CI engine with high-pressure common-rail (5–25 MPa). - **Emissions:** PM ↓ 90–95 %, NOₓ ↑ 5–10 % raw, CO₂ ↓ 5–8 % per MJ, total HC comparable. - **Bio-DME:** Made from biomass-derived syngas → can be **carbon-neutral**. - **Other names:** Methoxymethane, methyl oxide, wood ether (obsolete), DME. - **Key exam numbers to memorise:** Boiling point −24.8 °C, cetane 55–60, A/F 9.5, LHV 28.43 MJ/kg, O₂ mass 34.8 %, 1 kg DME → 1.91 kg CO₂ (stoich). - **Key disadvantages to mention in any KTU essay:** Low viscosity/lubricity, high tank volume, attacks elastomers, pressurised storage, slight NOₓ rise, fuel cost higher than diesel (currently). > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "D-M-E C-L-O-S-E"** > **D**iesel-like cetane, **M**ethanol precursor, **E**ther linkage, **C**lean burning, **L**PG-style storage, **O**xygen-rich, **S**oot-free, **E**ngine friendly (CI).

topics.title: Di-Ethyl Ether - Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Di-Ethyl Ether - Availability # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Di-Ethyl Ether (DEE)**, also known as **ethoxyethane**, **ethyl ether**, or simply **ether**, is an organic compound belonging to the **ether functional group family**. Its chemical formula is **C₂H₅–O–C₂H₅** (or C₄H₁₀O), and it is structurally derived from two ethyl groups linked through a central oxygen atom. In the KTU 2024 Alternative Fuels syllabus (PEMUT411, Module 2), DEE is classified as a **bio-derived alternative fuel** for **Compression Ignition (CI) engines**, often employed either as a pure fuel or as a **high-cetane blending additive** for neat diesel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Di-ethyl ether is emphasized in Module 2 as one of the most promising **alcohol-derivative oxygenated fuels** because it can be manufactured entirely from **renewable bio-ethanol**, making it carbon-neutral in a closed-loop agricultural economy. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Twin Ethyl Molecule" Imagine a water molecule (H–O–H). Now, replace each of those two hydrogen atoms with a small "ethyl handle" (C₂H₅). What you get is exactly DEE: a central oxygen atom holding hands with **two identical ethyl groups**. A simpler way to picture it: Take a molecule of **ethanol** (C₂H₅–OH, the alcohol in beverages). Now, swap the hydrogen of its –OH group with another ethyl group (C₂H₅). That substitution produces **C₂H₅–O–C₂H₅**, which is diethyl ether. So DEE is literally a **"twin-ethyl handshake through an oxygen bridge."** In a kitchen analogy: If ethanol is "watered-down alcohol," then DEE is "anhydrous ether" — a much more volatile, energetic cousin that the engine loves because of its high cetane rating and built-in oxygen content. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Identity | Property | Value | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | **IUPAC Name** | Ethoxyethane | Board-exam friendly term | | **Chemical Formula** | C₄H₁₀O | Empirical: C₂H₅OC₂H₅ | | **Molecular Weight (M)** | **74.12 g/mol** | Used in all stoichiometric calculations | | **Boiling Point** | **34.6 °C** | Extremely volatile — major storage challenge | | **Density (ρ) at 20 °C** | **0.713 g/cm³** | Lighter than diesel (0.84 g/cm³) | | **Cetane Number** | **~ 125 – 160** | Higher than diesel (~45–55) — excellent ignition quality | | **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** | **~ 33.9 MJ/kg** | Lower than diesel (~42.5 MJ/kg) | | **Self-Ignition Temperature** | **160 °C** | Below diesel (~250 °C) — easier cold start | | **Flash Point** | **−45 °C** | Highly hazardous; **safety-critical** | | **Kinematic Viscosity (20 °C)** | **0.23 cSt** | Much lower than diesel — poor lubricity | | **Stoichiometric AFR (mass)** | **~ 11.7** | Lower than diesel's 14.5 — oxygenated fuel | | **Oxygen Content (by mass)** | **~ 21.6 %** | Built-in oxygen improves combustion completeness | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Convention:** All properties above are at **1 atm and 25 °C** unless stated otherwise. The low boiling point (34.6 °C) means DEE must be stored in **pressurized, refrigerated, or sealed containers** — a major practical limitation for widespread adoption as a transport fuel. ## 1.4 Why DEE is Relevant in the KTU Context DEE is positioned in the curriculum between **alcohols (ethanol/methanol)** and **gaseous fuels (CNG/LPG/H₂)**. It bridges them because: - It is **liquid at ambient conditions** (unlike CNG/H₂). - It can be **stored as a liquid at moderate pressure** (unlike LPG which needs −42 °C or 8 bar). - It has **engine-knock resistance** comparable to alcohols in SI engines. - It has **cetane rating** comparable to or exceeding diesel in CI engines. - It is a **bio-derived fuel** — fits the KTU "renewable alternative fuel" theme. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Perspective:** When a KTU question asks "Why is DEE considered a promising alternative fuel?", the answer should mention **three keywords** that almost guarantee full marks: **renewable (bio-ethanol origin), oxygenated (built-in O₂), and high cetane (CI-friendly).** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core "Why & How" — Availability Logic The **availability** of Di-Ethyl Ether as a transportation fuel hinges on **three logical pillars**: ### Pillar 1 — The Raw Material (Ethanol) DEE is industrially produced by the **catalytic dehydration of ethanol**. Because ethanol itself can be produced from **biomass fermentation** (sugarcane molasses, corn, cassava, agricultural waste), DEE is fundamentally a **bio-derived fuel**. Therefore, its availability is tied directly to the **global ethanol production capacity**. - **Global ethanol production (2023–2024):** ~ 110–120 billion litres/year. - **Top producers:** USA (corn), Brazil (sugarcane), India (molasses), China, EU (beet/wheat). - **India-specific:** India has set a **20 % ethanol-blending target (E20)** under the Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) programme, creating a robust upstream ethanol supply. ### Pillar 2 — The Conversion Process The conversion of ethanol to DEE is a **well-established industrial chemistry reaction**, requiring moderate temperature (140–250 °C) and an acid catalyst. The yield is high (~80–90 %), and the process is energy-efficient. ### Pillar 3 — Safety & Logistics DEE is **already manufactured at industrial scale** (millions of tonnes/year globally) for use as a **solvent, pharmaceutical intermediate, and starting fluid** in gasoline engines. This existing supply chain gives DEE an **infrastructural head-start** over newer alternative fuels. ## 2.2 Production Routes — Step-by-Step Chemical Logic ### Route A (Primary) — Vapor-Phase Catalytic Dehydration of Ethanol $$\begin{aligned} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\,\text{or Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; 140\text{–}250\,^\circ\text{C}} (\text{C}_2\text{H}_5)_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned}$$ - **Catalyst options:** Concentrated H₂SO₄ (140–150 °C) or Alumina/Al₂O₃ (250 °C, vapor phase). - **Why it works:** At high temperature in the presence of an acid, the –OH group of one ethanol molecule is protonated and leaves as water, allowing another ethanol's –O– to bond to the ethyl carbocation, forming the ether linkage. - **Industrial preference:** Vapor-phase alumina route is preferred for **continuous production** and **cleaner separation**. ### Route B (Secondary) — From Ethylene via Wacker-type Oxidation $$\begin{aligned} 2\,\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pd/Cu catalyst}} (\text{C}_2\text{H}_5)_2\text{O} \end{aligned}$$ This is a **petrochemical route** using ethylene from naphtha cracking. It is the dominant route in regions with abundant ethylene (USA, Middle East, parts of Europe). ### Route C (Historical) — Williamson's Ether Synthesis (Lab-scale) $$\begin{aligned} \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{ONa} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Cl} \rightarrow (\text{C}_2\text{H}_5)_2\text{O} + \text{NaCl} \end{aligned}$$ Used in laboratories; not commercially relevant for fuel production. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Property Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Markdown Rule:** All absolute-value/size bars are rendered with `\vert` to keep tables valid. | # | Formula / Property | Expression | Numeric Value | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Molecular Weight | $M_{\text{DEE}} = 4(12) + 10(1) + 1(16)$ | **74 g/mol** | Stoichiometry, AFR | | 2 | Combustion Equation | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\text{O} + 6\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | — | Energy balance, emissions | | 3 | Stoichiometric O₂ (mol) | $a = \dfrac{2n_{\text{C}} + \tfrac{n_{\text{H}}}{2} - n_{\text{O}}}{2}$ | **6 mol** | Combustion analysis | | 4 | Stoichiometric Air (mol) | $n_{\text{air}} = 4.76 \cdot a$ | **28.56 mol** | AFR calculation | | 5 | Stoichiometric AFR (mass) | $\text{AFR}_{\text{st}} = \dfrac{a \cdot 32 \cdot 4.76}{M_{\text{DEE}}}$ | **11.7 kg air/kg fuel** | Engine calibration | | 6 | Theoretical DEE from 1 kg Ethanol | $m_{\text{DEE}} = m_{\text{EtOH}} \cdot \dfrac{74}{92}$ | **0.804 kg DEE** | Mass-balance for plant design | | 7 | Ethanol needed for 1 kg DEE | $m_{\text{EtOH}} = m_{\text{DEE}} \cdot \dfrac{92}{74}$ | **1.243 kg ethanol** | Procurement planning | | 8 | Oxygen Content (by mass) | $\%O = \dfrac{16}{74} \times 100$ | **21.6 %** | Smoke reduction in diesel | | 9 | Cetane Number | Empirical | **125 – 160** | CI engine suitability | | 10 | Volumetric Energy Density | $\rho \cdot \text{LHV}$ | **24.2 MJ/L** | Storage comparison | | 11 | Vapor Pressure (20 °C) | Antoine eqn. | **~ 440 mmHg** | Storage tank design | | 12 | Flash Point (closed cup) | Empirical | **−45 °C** | Safety classification (Class IA) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Diesel Engine Fuel:** A blend of **5–15 % DEE in diesel** dramatically reduces **smoke (particulate matter)** and **NOₓ** while improving **thermal efficiency** by 1–3 %. - **Starting Fluid:** DEE is the **active ingredient in engine "starting fluids"** sold worldwide — its low auto-ignition temperature (160 °C) makes cold-starting easy. - **Oxygenated Fuel Additive:** Replaces toxic aromatics (benzene, toluene) for cleaner combustion. - **Pharmaceutical Solvent:** Used to manufacture drugs — explains why the **global production infrastructure** already exists. - **Bio-refinery Feedstock:** A bio-refinery producing both ethanol and DEE can be integrated with **sugarcane mills** for maximum value addition. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** When asked "Discuss the availability of DEE", examiners expect you to cover — **(a) raw material sources, (b) production technology, (c) current global/Indian production statistics, (d) challenges to widespread fuel-grade availability, and (e) potential to scale up.** # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Molecular Weight of DEE **Given:** Chemical formula C₄H₁₀O. **Required:** Molecular weight $M_{\text{DEE}}$ in g/mol. **Atomic masses:** C = 12, H = 1, O = 16. $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{DEE}} &= (4 \times 12) + (10 \times 1) + (1 \times 16) \\ &= 48 + 10 + 16 \\ &= 74 \;\text{g/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Engineering meaning:** This 74 g/mol value is the denominator in every AFR and energy calculation involving DEE. **Memorize it.** ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Combustion Equation **Given:** Complete combustion of C₄H₁₀O in pure O₂. **Required:** Balanced equation and oxygen requirement. **Step 1 — Write the unbalanced form:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\text{O} + a\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Balance Carbon atoms:** - LHS: 4 C. RHS: $b$ C in CO₂. So $b = 4$. **Step 3 — Balance Hydrogen atoms:** - LHS: 10 H. RHS: $2c$ H in H₂O. So $2c = 10 \Rightarrow c = 5$. **Step 4 — Balance Oxygen atoms:** - LHS: $2a + 1$ (the 1 comes from DEE). RHS: $2b + c = 2(4) + 5 = 13$. - So $2a + 1 = 13 \Rightarrow 2a = 12 \Rightarrow a = 6$. **Step 5 — Final balanced equation:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\text{O} + 6\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned}$$ **Engineering meaning:** One mole of DEE needs exactly **6 moles of O₂** for complete combustion. Partial combustion (insufficient O₂) will release CO and soot — a major problem in pure DEE operation. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Mass Basis) **Given:** 1 mole DEE needs 6 mol O₂. Air contains 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ by volume (mole basis), giving the **4.76 air/O₂ molar ratio**. **Step 1 — Mass of O₂ required:** $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{O}_2} &= 6 \times 32 = 192 \;\text{g} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent mass of air (using $m_{\text{air}} = m_{\text{O}_2} \times \dfrac{100}{23}$, since O₂ is 23 % of air by mass):** $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{air}} &= 192 \times \dfrac{100}{23} = 834.78 \;\text{g} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — AFR on mass basis:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{AFR}_{\text{st}} &= \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{DEE}}} = \dfrac{834.78}{74} = 11.28 \end{aligned}$$ So **1 kg of DEE requires ~ 11.28 kg of air** for stoichiometric combustion. > [!NOTE] > **Alternative quick formula** (using $4.76 \times 32 = 152.32$ as "molar mass of air"): > > $$\begin{aligned} > \text{AFR}_{\text{st}} = \dfrac{a \times 152.32}{M_{\text{DEE}}} = \dfrac{6 \times 152.32}{74} = 12.35 > \end{aligned}$$ > > This small variation (~11.3 vs 12.3) comes from using 23 % vs 21 % for O₂ mass-fraction — both are **accepted in KTU**. Be consistent in your exam. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ethanol-to-DEE Mass Balance **Given:** Reaction: $2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \rightarrow (\text{C}_2\text{H}_5)_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ **Required:** How much DEE is obtained from 1 kg of ethanol, assuming 100 % conversion. **Step 1 — Molecular weights:** - Ethanol: $M_{\text{EtOH}} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46 \;\text{g/mol}$ - DEE: $M_{\text{DEE}} = 74 \;\text{g/mol}$ **Step 2 — Mass of 2 moles of ethanol:** $$\begin{aligned} 2 \times 46 = 92 \;\text{g} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of 1 mole of DEE produced:** $$\begin{aligned} 1 \times 74 = 74 \;\text{g} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Mass conversion ratio:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Yield ratio} = \dfrac{74}{92} = 0.804 = 80.4\,\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Practical answer:** - From **1 kg ethanol** → **0.804 kg DEE** (theoretical) - For **1 kg DEE** → need **1.243 kg ethanol** (theoretical) - Industrial yield is typically **75–85 %** due to side reactions (ethylene formation). **Engineering meaning:** This number is critical for **economic feasibility studies** of a bio-DEE plant. If ethanol costs ₹50/L and DEE sells at ₹80/L, you must account for the **20 % mass loss** during conversion. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Why DEE Availability is a Boon for India | Parameter | Status in India | KTU-Relevant Insight | |---|---|---| | Ethanol production capacity | ~ 6 billion litres/year (2023) | Surplus capacity → DEE feasible | | EBP programme | E20 mandate (2025) | Co-product stream for DEE | | Sugarcane/molasses availability | Year-round in Maharashtra, UP, Karnataka | Continuous feedstock | | Existing ether plants | Pharma-grade DEE already produced | Low incremental capex | | Policy support | National Policy on Biofuels (2018) | DEE listed as advanced biofuel | | Logistics for fuel-grade | Limited (very low flash point) | Major hurdle to scale-up | | Engine compatibility | Tested at IIT Madras, IIP Dehradun | Proven for 5–15 % diesel blends | ## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Calorific Value Comparison (LHV per kg of air) To compare fuels fairly, we can compute **energy released per kg of stoichiometric air**, since this represents what an engine actually "sees": $$\begin{aligned} \text{Specific Energy}_{\text{DEE}} &= \dfrac{33.9 \;\text{MJ/kg}}{11.28} = 3.00 \;\text{MJ/kg air} \\ \text{Specific Energy}_{\text{Diesel}} &= \dfrac{42.5 \;\text{MJ/kg}}{14.5} = 2.93 \;\text{MJ/kg air} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** DEE delivers **slightly more energy per kg of air** than diesel, which means the engine's indicated efficiency on DEE can marginally exceed that on diesel — a frequent KTU viva question. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Production Process Flow — DEE from Bio-Ethanol The following Mermaid diagram traces the **industrial availability chain** of DEE, from agricultural feedstock to engine-ready fuel blend. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK
Sugarcane molasses, corn,
cassava, agricultural waste] --> B[FERMENTATION
Sugar to ethanol via yeast
30-35 deg C, 48-72 h] B --> C[BIO-ETHANOL
C2H5OH, 95-99.5 percent pure] C --> D1[ROUTE A
Vapor-phase dehydration
Al2O3 catalyst, 250 deg C] C --> D2[ROUTE B
Liquid-phase dehydration
H2SO4 catalyst, 140 deg C] C --> D3[ROUTE C
From ethylene via
Wacker oxidation, Pd Cu] D1 --> E[CRUDE DI-ETHYL ETHER
C4H10O plus water and ethanol] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F[DISTILLATION COLUMN
Separate at 34.6 deg C boiling point] F --> G[FUEL GRADE DEE
Purity greater than 99 percent] G --> H1[BLEND STOCK
Mix 5-15 percent with diesel] G --> H2[PURE DEE
For SI engine starting fluid] G --> H3[PHARMA GRADE
Solvent and drug intermediate] H1 --> I[DIESEL-CI ENGINE
Reduced smoke, improved efficiency] H2 --> J[GASOLINE-SI ENGINE
Cold-start aid] H3 --> K[PHARMACEUTICAL INDUSTRY
Existing market, stable demand] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Routes A and B (from ethanol) are the **renewable** pathways and are the focus of KTU Module 2. Route C is the **fossil-derived** pathway using ethylene and is included only for completeness. Fuel-grade DEE is the **end-product** relevant to engine applications. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Availability Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RAW["RAW MATERIAL AVAILABILITY"] R1[Bio-ethanol surplus] R2[Ethylene from petrochemicals] R3[Biomass fermentation capacity] end subgraph PROC["PRODUCTION TECHNOLOGY"] P1[Alumina catalyst route] P2[Sulfuric acid route] P3[Wacker oxidation route] end subgraph APPL["END-USE APPLICATIONS"] A1[CI engine fuel blend] A2[SI engine starting fluid] A3[Pharmaceutical solvent] end subgraph CHAL["AVAILABILITY CHALLENGES"] C1[Low flash point minus 45 deg C] C2[High vapor pressure] C3[Storage and transport safety] C4[Lubricity loss in pure form] end RAW --> PROC PROC --> APPL CHAL -.->|limits widespread
fuel deployment| APPL ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Mass Flow for 1000 kg DEE/day Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Input: 1243 kg ethanol/day] --> M2[Reactor: 80.4 percent conversion] M2 --> M3[Output: 1000 kg DEE plus 243 kg H2O/day] M3 --> M4[Distillation: 99 percent pure DEE] M4 --> M5[Final: 990 kg fuel-grade DEE
plus 253 kg by-product streams] ``` > [!TIP] > **For KTU answer sheets:** Always draw a **simple block flow diagram** like the one above. It carries 1–2 marks even if your text is incomplete, and it demonstrates to the examiner that you understand the **process train**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"What is Di-Ethyl Ether? Why is it considered a renewable alternative fuel?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Di-Ethyl Ether (DEE), chemical formula C₄H₁₀O (C₂H₅–O–C₂H₅), is an organic ether produced by the catalytic dehydration of ethanol. It is considered a **renewable alternative fuel** because **(i)** the feedstock ethanol can be obtained from **biomass fermentation** of sugarcane molasses, corn, or agricultural waste, **(ii)** it contains **21.6 % oxygen by mass** which promotes cleaner combustion with reduced particulate emissions, and **(iii)** it has a **high cetane number (~125)** making it suitable for **CI engines** as a blend stock with diesel. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **"List the four main sources of DEE and state its molecular weight."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four main sources/routes for DEE production are: 1. **Vapor-phase catalytic dehydration of ethanol** (Al₂O₃ catalyst, ~250 °C). 2. **Liquid-phase dehydration of ethanol** (H₂SO₄ catalyst, ~140 °C). 3. **Wacker-type oxidation of ethylene** (Pd/Cu catalyst). 4. **Williamson ether synthesis** (lab-scale only, using sodium ethoxide and ethyl chloride). The **molecular weight of DEE is 74 g/mol**. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by writing only the chemical equation for DEE production **without mentioning the catalyst, temperature, and pressure**. Always include all three. > - A frequent error is **confusing DEE (di-ethyl ether) with DME (di-methyl ether)**. They are different molecules. DEE = C₄H₁₀O; DME = C₂H₆O. > - Many students forget to mention that DEE's **flash point of −45 °C** is a **safety hazard** — examiners award marks for both pros and cons. > - For derivation questions, **write each step explicitly** — partial marks are awarded for correct intermediate steps even if the final answer has an arithmetic slip. ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme]** **(a) "With a neat flow diagram, explain the industrial production of Di-Ethyl Ether from bio-ethanol. Discuss the role of catalyst and reaction conditions." (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Raw Material:** Bio-ethanol (C₂H₅OH) of 95–99.5 % purity, obtained from fermentation of sugarcane molasses, corn, or cellulosic biomass. **Step 2 — Reaction:** $$\begin{aligned} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; 250\,^\circ\text{C}} (\text{C}_2\text{H}_5)_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Catalyst Role:** Alumina (Al₂O₃) acts as a **solid acid catalyst**, providing surface acidic sites that protonate the –OH group of ethanol, facilitating its removal as water. Alternative catalyst: **concentrated H₂SO₄** at 140–150 °C in the liquid phase. **Step 4 — Reaction Conditions:** - **Temperature:** 140–250 °C (vapor phase preferred for continuous operation). - **Pressure:** Atmospheric to 3 bar. - **Conversion:** 80–90 % per pass; unreacted ethanol is recycled. - **Selectivity:** 85–95 % to DEE; side products include ethylene (dehydration byproduct). **Step 5 — Process Train:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Ethanol feed} \rightarrow \text{Vaporizer} \rightarrow \text{Fixed-bed reactor (Al}_2\text{O}_3\text{)} \rightarrow \text{Condenser} \rightarrow \text{Decanter} \rightarrow \text{Distillation column} \rightarrow \text{Pure DEE} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Mass Yield:** Theoretical yield = 80.4 % (74 g DEE per 92 g ethanol); industrial yield = 75–85 %. **(7 marks — 1 for raw material, 2 for reaction, 2 for catalyst/conditions, 1 for process train, 1 for yield)** --- **(b) "Calculate the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis) for DEE combustion. Comment on how this value affects diesel engine operation when DEE is used as a blend." (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Combustion Equation:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\text{O} + 6\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Mass of O₂ per mole of DEE:** $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{O}_2} = 6 \times 32 = 192 \;\text{g} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of air (using air = 23 % O₂ by mass):** $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{192}{0.23} = 834.78 \;\text{g} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Stoichiometric AFR:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{AFR}_{\text{st}} = \dfrac{834.78}{74} = 11.28 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Comparison with diesel (AFR = 14.5):** DEE requires **less air** for complete combustion because it contains **21.6 % oxygen by mass** (built-in oxygen). **Step 6 — Engine Implications when DEE is blended (5–15 %) with diesel:** - **Smoke/PM reduction:** Built-in oxygen promotes more complete combustion of diesel's heavy hydrocarbons, **reducing soot by 30–50 %**. - **NOₓ impact:** Slight increase due to higher flame temperatures; can be controlled with EGR. - **BSFC change:** Brake specific fuel consumption increases by 3–6 % due to DEE's lower LHV (33.9 vs 42.5 MJ/kg). - **Cold-start:** Improved — DEE's lower cetane ignition delay helps start diesel engines in cold weather. - **Lubricity concern:** Pure DEE has very low viscosity (0.23 cSt) — must be blended, not used neat, in CI engines. **(7 marks — 2 for derivation, 2 for comparison, 3 for engine implications)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice) **(a) "Discuss the availability of Di-Ethyl Ether as a transportation fuel in the Indian context. Compare its production from bio-ethanol versus petrochemical routes." (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Indian Context for DEE Availability (3.5 marks):** - **Ethanol production capacity:** India produced ~ 6 billion litres of ethanol in 2023, with a target of ~ 10 billion litres by 2025 (E20 mandate). - **Surplus availability:** Once E20 is saturated, surplus ethanol can be diverted to DEE production. - **Policy push:** National Policy on Biofuels (2018) classifies DEE as an "advanced biofuel" eligible for incentives. - **Existing infrastructure:** India already produces pharma-grade DEE (~ 5,000–10,000 tonnes/year) — fuel-grade scale-up is feasible with limited capex. - **Geographical concentration:** Maharashtra, Uttar Pradesh, Karnataka have sugarcane mills co-located with potential DEE plants — ideal for **integrated bio-refineries**. **2. Comparison of Production Routes (3.5 marks):** | Aspect | Bio-ethanol Route | Petrochemical Route | |---|---|---| | Feedstock | Sugarcane/molasses/corn | Ethylene from naphtha | | Renewable | **Yes** | No (fossil-derived) | | Reaction | Dehydration (140–250 °C) | Wacker oxidation (~100 °C) | | Catalyst | Al₂O₃ or H₂SO₄ | PdCl₂/CuCl₂ | | Mass yield | 80.4 % of ethanol | ~ 90 % of ethylene | | Carbon footprint | **Near-zero (closed loop)** | Positive (~ 2 kg CO₂/kg DEE) | | Suitability for India | **High** (abundant biomass) | Low (India imports ethylene) | | Cost volatility | Tied to monsoon/sugar cycle | Tied to crude oil price | **Conclusion:** For India's renewable fuel programme, the **bio-ethanol route** is preferred due to its **domestic feedstock availability, carbon neutrality, and policy alignment**. --- **(b) "Derive the molecular weight, combustion equation, and stoichiometric air requirement for Di-Ethyl Ether. Why is DEE considered oxygenated, and how does this help in reducing diesel engine emissions?" (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Molecular Weight Derivation:** $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{DEE}} &= 4(12) + 10(1) + 1(16) = 48 + 10 + 16 = 74 \;\text{g/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Combustion Equation (derivation shown in Section 3.2):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\text{O} + 6\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Stoichiometric Air:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{AFR}_{\text{st}} = \dfrac{6 \times 32 \times 4.76}{74} = \dfrac{913.92}{74} = 12.35 \;\text{(molar basis)} \end{aligned}$$ Or on mass basis: **11.28 kg air/kg fuel** (using 23 % O₂ in air). **Step 4 — Oxygenated Nature of DEE:** $$\begin{aligned} \%O_{\text{by mass}} = \dfrac{16}{74} \times 100 = 21.6\,\% \end{aligned}$$ DEE contains **~21.6 % oxygen chemically bound** in its molecular structure, hence "oxygenated." This is **inherent oxygen** that participates in combustion. **Step 5 — Emission Reduction Mechanism in Diesel Engines:** - Diesel fuel (C₁₂H₂₃) contains **no oxygen**, so combustion in fuel-rich zones (inside the diesel spray core) is **incomplete**, producing **particulate matter (PM) and soot**. - When DEE is blended, its **bound oxygen** diffuses into fuel-rich zones, supplying O atoms **at the molecular level**. - Result: **More complete oxidation of soot precursors** → **30–50 % reduction in PM/smoke**. - Additionally, **CO emissions drop by 20–40 %** and **unburned HC by 10–25 %**. - Trade-off: Slight **NOₓ increase** (5–10 %) due to higher local flame temperatures. **(7 marks — 1.5 for MW, 1.5 for combustion, 1 for AFR, 1 for oxygen content, 2 for emission mechanism)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These:** - ✅ **Chemical formula of DEE:** C₄H₁₀O or C₂H₅–O–C₂H₅. - ✅ **Molecular weight:** 74 g/mol (C: 48, H: 10, O: 16). - ✅ **Cetane number:** ~ 125–160 (very high; superior to diesel). - ✅ **Oxygen content:** 21.6 % by mass (built-in O₂). - ✅ **LHV:** 33.9 MJ/kg (lower than diesel's 42.5 MJ/kg). - ✅ **Boiling point:** 34.6 °C (very volatile — storage hazard). - ✅ **Flash point:** −45 °C (Class IA flammable liquid). - ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR:** 11.28 (mass) or 12.35 (molar). - ✅ **Combustion equation:** C₄H₁₀O + 6 O₂ → 4 CO₂ + 5 H₂O. - ✅ **Primary production route:** Catalytic dehydration of ethanol (2 C₂H₅OH → C₄H₁₀O + H₂O). - ✅ **Catalysts:** Al₂O₃ at 250 °C (vapor) or H₂SO₄ at 140 °C (liquid). - ✅ **Theoretical mass yield:** 74/92 = 80.4 % of ethanol. - ✅ **Ethanol needed per kg DEE:** 1.243 kg. - ✅ **Engine benefits:** Reduced smoke, PM, CO; improved cold start. - ✅ **Engine drawbacks:** Low flash point, low viscosity, lower energy density, NOₓ increase. - ✅ **India connection:** Ethanol Blending Programme (E20) + National Policy on Biofuels (2018) → DEE is a "future fuel." - ✅ **Difference from DME:** DEE = C₄H₁₀O (ethyl groups); DME = C₂H₆O (methyl groups) — **do not confuse in exams**. - ✅ **Safety:** Class IA flammable — requires pressurized/refrigerated storage and explosion-proof handling.

topics.title: Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of "Performance" in Alternative Fuels

In the context of **Alternative Fuels and Energy Systems**, **Engine Performance** refers to the quantitative evaluation of an internal combustion (IC) engine's ability to convert the chemical energy stored in an alternative fuel (ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, CNG, LPG, hydrogen, biogas, etc.) into useful mechanical work at the output shaft, measured against standardized parameters under varying operating conditions (load, speed, equivalence ratio).

According to the **KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMUT411, Module 2)**, performance assessment of alternative fuels encompasses:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> "The measurement and analysis of brake power, brake thermal efficiency, brake specific fuel/energy consumption, volumetric efficiency, mean effective pressure, and exhaust emissions of an engine operating on a non-conventional (alternative) fuel, with direct comparison against baseline conventional fuel data."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy:** Imagine two athletes running a marathon. Athlete A carries a heavy, calorie-dense food bar (like diesel — high energy density ~45 MJ/kg). Athlete B carries a lightweight but lower-calorie sports drink (like ethanol — energy density ~26.8 MJ/kg). Even if both runners digest their fuel perfectly and burn the same calories, **Athlete A will run faster and longer per gram of food**. This is the essence of "fuel performance": the same engine will produce different power, efficiency, and fuel consumption depending on the **energy density** and **combustion characteristics** of the fuel you feed it.

### 1.3 Why Performance Evaluation Matters for Alternative Fuels

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Performance Metrics (Must Memorize):**
> - **Brake Power (BP)** — measured in **kW**
> - **Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)** — measured in **%** (typically 25–40%)
> - **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** — measured in **kg/kWh**
> - **Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC)** — measured in **MJ/kWh**
> - **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** — measured in **%**
> - **Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)** — measured in **bar or kPa**

Alternative fuels generally have **lower heating values (LHV)** than diesel/petrol, which directly impacts performance. Engineers must quantify this loss to determine if the fuel is economically and technically viable.

### 1.4 Performance Comparison Snapshot

| Fuel Type | LHV (MJ/kg) | Typical BTE (%) | BSFC (kg/kWh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diesel (baseline) | 42–45 | 30–38 | 0.22–0.26 |
| Biodiesel (B100) | 37–40 | 30–36 | 0.26–0.30 |
| Ethanol | 26.8 | 28–34 | 0.42–0.48 |
| Methanol | 19.9 | 26–32 | 0.55–0.65 |
| CNG | 50 (per kg) | 28–34 | 0.20–0.24 (kg/kWh) |
| Hydrogen | 120 | 32–42 | 0.08–0.12 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Performance Parameters

#### 2.1.1 Brake Power (BP)

Brake Power is the actual usable power available at the engine's output shaft, measured using a dynamometer (eddy current, hydraulic, or electrical).

**Why it matters:** It excludes friction losses, so it tells us what the *useful* output is. Alternative fuels often produce slightly lower BP due to lower heating values or incomplete combustion at certain loads.

#### 2.1.2 Indicated Power (IP) and Friction Power (FP)

$$IP = BP + FP$$

Where friction power represents the power absorbed by engine internals (piston friction, bearings, pumping losses, accessory drives).

#### 2.1.3 Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} \times 100\%$$

**For alternative fuels:** Mechanical efficiency is largely unchanged (since it's a mechanical/physical property), but slight variations occur due to differences in cylinder pressure profiles.

#### 2.1.4 Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)

**The single most important performance parameter for alternative fuels.**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/s)
- $LHV$ = Lower Heating Value (MJ/kg)

**Higher BTE = better fuel-to-work conversion.** Alternative fuels like ethanol have higher octane rating, allowing higher compression ratios → potentially higher BTE despite lower LHV.

#### 2.1.5 Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \quad [\text{kg/kWh}]$$

**Lower BSFC = better.** Since alternative fuels have lower LHV, more mass is required to produce the same energy → BSFC is **higher** for most alternative fuels compared to diesel.

#### 2.1.6 Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC) — *Critical for Alternative Fuels*

$$BSEC = BSFC \times LHV \quad [\text{MJ/kWh}]$$

**This is the fair comparison metric!** Unlike BSFC, BSEC allows direct comparison across fuels of different energy densities. If BSEC of an alternative fuel ≈ BSEC of diesel, the engine converts energy equally well.

#### 2.1.7 Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)

$$\eta_v = \frac{\text{Actual mass of air inducted}}{\text{Theoretical mass of air at atmospheric conditions}} \times 100\%$$

**For gaseous fuels (CNG, LPG, H₂):** Volumetric efficiency drops significantly because the fuel displaces air in the intake manifold. This is a **major performance loss** for gaseous alternative fuels.

#### 2.1.8 Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)

$$BMEP = \frac{BP \times n_r \times 60}{V_d \times N \times k} \quad [\text{kPa}]$$

Where:
- $n_r$ = number of revolutions per power stroke (2 for 4-stroke, 1 for 2-stroke)
- $V_d$ = displacement volume (m³)
- $N$ = engine speed (rpm)
- $k$ = number of cylinders

BMEP normalizes engine output — independent of engine size. Useful for comparing fuels across different engine platforms.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — appears in nearly every Part B question on Performance.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Unit | Boundary/Normal Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake Power | $BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | kW | 5–500 kW |
| 2 | Indicated Power | $IP = BP + FP$ | kW | > BP |
| 3 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = BP/IP \times 100$ | % | 70–90% |
| 4 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV} \times 100$ | % | 25–40% |
| 5 | BSFC | $BSFC = \dot{m}_f / BP$ | kg/kWh | 0.22–0.65 |
| 6 | BSEC | $BSEC = BSFC \times LHV$ | MJ/kWh | 8.5–12 |
| 7 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \frac{\dot{m}_{a,actual}}{\dot{m}_{a,theoretical}}$ | % | 75–100% (liq), 70–90% (gas) |
| 8 | BMEP | $BMEP = \frac{BP \cdot n_r \cdot 60}{V_d \cdot N \cdot k}$ | kPa | 600–1200 |
| 9 | IMEP | $IMEP = \frac{IP \cdot n_r \cdot 60}{V_d \cdot N \cdot k}$ | kPa | 700–1400 |
| 10 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $A/F = \dot{m}_a / \dot{m}_f$ | — | 14.5 (diesel), 9 (ethanol) |
| 11 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = (A/F)_{stoich} / (A/F)_{actual}$ | — | 0.7–1.3 |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Performance curves of CNG vs petrol vehicles inform consumer choice and OEM design.
- **Aviation:** Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF) performance is benchmarked against Jet-A1.
- **Marine:** Biodiesel blends in ship engines require BSEC analysis for IMO compliance.
- **Power generation:** Gensets running on biogas need BTE and η_v evaluation for ROI calculations.
- **Policy:** India's Ethanol Blending Programme (E20) is justified based on BSEC and emission performance studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Brake Power from Torque and Speed

**Starting premise:** Power is the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft:

$$\text{Power} = \text{Force} \times \text{Velocity} = F \times (\omega \times r)$$

Where $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = radius.

Since Torque $T = F \times r$:

$$P = T \times \omega$$

**Step 1:** Convert engine speed from rpm (revolutions per minute) to rad/s.

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back:

$$P = T \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 3:** If T is in N·m and N in rpm, the result is in Watts. Convert to kW:

$$\boxed{BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000} \quad [\text{kW}]}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency from First Principles

**Energy balance on the engine (steady state, control volume):**

$$\text{Energy In (Fuel)} = \text{Energy Out (BP + FP + Exhaust + Cooling + Losses)}$$

**Step 1:** Energy input rate (per second):

$$\dot{E}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times LHV \quad [\text{kJ/s or kW}]$$

**Step 2:** Useful output is Brake Power (BP).

**Step 3:** Define thermal efficiency as the ratio of useful energy out to chemical energy in:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Useful Work Output}}{\text{Energy Input}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to percentage:

$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV} \times 100 \quad [\%]}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between BSFC and BTE

**Step 1:** Start with BSFC definition:

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$$

**Step 2:** Start with BTE definition:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}$$

**Step 3:** Invert BTE:

$$\frac{1}{\eta_{bth}} = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}{BP} = \frac{LHV}{BP/\dot{m}_f} = \frac{LHV}{(1/BSFC)}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} = \frac{1}{BSFC \times LHV} = \frac{3600}{BSFC \times LHV}}$$

The factor 3600 converts kJ to kWh (1 kWh = 3600 kJ).

**Numerical validation:** For diesel, BSFC ≈ 0.25 kg/kWh, LHV ≈ 43 MJ/kg:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{3600}{0.25 \times 43000} = \frac{3600}{10750} = 0.3349 = 33.49\%$$

✓ Matches typical diesel engine BTE range.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style — 14-mark caliber)

**Problem Statement:**
A single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine running on **B20 biodiesel blend (20% biodiesel + 80% diesel)** operates at 1500 rpm with a brake torque of 50 N·m. The fuel consumption rate is 3.5 kg/hr. The calorific value of B20 is 42.5 MJ/kg. Calculate:
1. Brake Power
2. Brake Thermal Efficiency
3. BSFC
4. BSEC
5. BMEP (assume displacement = 0.5 L)

---

**Solution — Part (a): Brake Power and BTE (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Brake Power**

$$BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$BP = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 50}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$BP = \frac{471238.9}{60000}$$

$$\boxed{BP = 7.854 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 2: Convert fuel flow to kg/s**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{3.5}{3600} = 9.722 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Brake Thermal Efficiency**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV} = \frac{7.854}{9.722 \times 10^{-4} \times 42.5 \times 10^3}$$

$$= \frac{7.854}{41.32}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} = 19.01\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** BTE is lower than pure diesel (~33%) due to lower heating value of biodiesel blend and slightly higher viscosity causing incomplete atomization.

---

**Solution — Part (b): BSFC, BSEC, and BMEP (7 marks)**

**Step 1: BSFC**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{9.722 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854}$$

$$\boxed{BSFC = 1.238 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/W·s} = 0.4457 \text{ kg/kWh}}$$

(Converting: $1.238 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 0.4457$)

**Step 2: BSEC**

$$BSEC = BSFC \times LHV = 0.4457 \times 42.5$$

$$\boxed{BSEC = 18.94 \text{ MJ/kWh}}$$

**Step 3: BMEP**

For 4-stroke engine, $n_r = 2$:

$$BMEP = \frac{BP \times n_r \times 60}{V_d \times N \times k}$$

$$= \frac{7.854 \times 2 \times 60}{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 \times 1}$$

$$= \frac{942.48}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{BMEP = 1256.6 \text{ kPa} = 12.57 \text{ bar}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Performance Impact of Major Alternative Fuels

| Fuel | Effect on BP | Effect on BTE | Effect on BSFC | Effect on η_v | Overall Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Biodiesel (B100)** | Slight ↓ (5–8%) | Comparable | ↑ ~15% | Slightly ↓ (high visc.) | Acceptable, drop-in |
| **Ethanol (E85)** | ↓ 10–15% | ↑ (high octane, high CR) | ↑ ~80% | ↓ (latent heat cooling) | Good with engine mods |
| **Methanol (M85)** | ↓ 20–25% | ↑ (very high octane) | ↑ ~150% | ↓↓↓ | Needs dedicated engine |
| **CNG** | ↓ 10–20% | ↑ (high octane) | Comparable (kg) | ↓↓ (displaces air) | Good, lower power |
| **LPG** | ↓ 5–10% | ↑ (high octane) | ↓ | Slight ↓ | Near drop-in |
| **Hydrogen** | ↑ or comparable | ↑↑↑ (fast flame) | ↓↓↓ | ↓↓ (very low density) | Excellent BTE, storage issue |
| **Biogas** | ↓ 15–25% (low LHV) | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | Needs purification |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Engine Performance Evaluation Block Diagram

This diagram maps the workflow for evaluating performance of an alternative fuel in an IC engine test cell.

```mermaid
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G

    H --> I[Final Performance Report]
```
