topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Demerits of Alternate Fuels; Biofuels - Biodiesel and Biogas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Demerits of Alternate Fuels — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Exam Ready)**
> *Alternate fuels* (also termed *non-conventional* or *unconventional fuels*) are energy carriers that substitute for petroleum-derived fossil fuels. While they offer renewability and lower net carbon footprints, every alternate fuel exhibits **technical, economic, infrastructural, and environmental demerits** that constrain large-scale adoption in internal combustion (IC) engines and industrial boilers.

The demerits of alternate fuels arise primarily from the following five engineering shortfalls:

1. **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** compared to gasoline/diesel.
2. **Higher viscosity and density** leading to poor atomization.
3. **Corrosive and reactive nature** of the fuel.
4. **Storage and stability issues** (phase separation, oxidation).
5. **High production cost and feedstock limitations**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Smartphone Charger Problem"

Imagine that you bought a beautiful solar-powered charger for your phone (the "alternate fuel"). It is *clean, green, and silent* — but:
- It charges **50% slower** than your wall charger (low calorific value).
- You can only use it in **direct sunlight** (feedstock/season limitation).
- The plug is **non-standard**, so you need an adapter (engine modification).
- On cloudy days, it refuses to work (cold-flow / startability issues).

Alternate fuels behave exactly like this — they are **environmentally virtuous but operationally inconvenient**.

---

## 1.2 Biofuels — The Big Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Biofuel):** A *biofuel* is a gaseous, liquid, or solid fuel produced from **biomass** — recently living organic material such as plants, agricultural residues, algae, or organic municipal waste — through **biological, thermal, or chemical conversion processes**.

Biofuels are classified into **four generations**:

| Generation | Feedstock | Example | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st Gen | Food crops (sugarcane, corn, soybean) | Corn ethanol | High |
| 2nd Gen | Lignocellulosic biomass, waste oils | Biodiesel from Jatropha | High |
| 3rd Gen | Algae | Algal biodiesel | Moderate |
| 4th Gen | Genetically engineered organisms | Synthetic biology fuels | Research |

This module focuses on **2nd generation** liquid (Biodiesel) and gaseous (Biogas) biofuels.

---

## 1.3 Biodiesel — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biodiesel** is defined by **ASTM D6751** and **EN 14214** as a fuel comprising **mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids** derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oil through a chemical conversion process called **transesterification**.

In plain English — Biodiesel is the **"vegetable-oil cousin" of petro-diesel**, made by chopping the big, thick oil molecules (triglycerides) into smaller, thinner biodiesel molecules (fatty acid methyl esters, FAME) using alcohol and a catalyst.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Cutting the Hair of a Fatty Molecule"

Picture a triglyceride as a **three-legged robot** carrying three big "fat legs" (fatty acid chains). Petro-diesel engines are designed to drink **small, single-chain molecules** (like spaghetti). So we use **methanol** (a "scissor molecule") with a **catalyst** (NaOH or KOH) to **snip off two of the legs and reattach a methyl head**, producing three thin biodiesel molecules and one byproduct (glycerol). Now the engine can swallow the fuel easily!

---

## 1.4 Biogas — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biogas** is a **renewable gaseous biofuel** produced by the **anaerobic digestion (AD)** of organic biomass by methanogenic bacteria in the **absence of oxygen**. It is primarily a mixture of **methane (CH₄)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, with traces of H₂S, NH₃, and water vapor.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Cow's Stomach Factory"

A cow does not chew grass for fun — it ferments it inside its **rumen** (a giant, oxygen-free stomach chamber) with the help of microbes, producing methane that the cow burps out. A **biogas digester** is essentially an *artificial cow stomach*: a sealed, oxygen-free tank where microbes break down kitchen waste, cow dung, or sewage into combustible methane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Biogas Composition by Volume:**
> - **CH₄ (Methane): 50 – 70 %** — the combustible component.
> - **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide): 30 – 40 %** — inert diluent.
> - **H₂S (Hydrogen Sulfide): 0 – 1 %** — corrosive contaminant.
> - **N₂ + Trace gases: < 2 %**.

---

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (Memorize for KTU)

| Metric | Biodiesel (B100) | Biogas | Petro-Diesel (Reference) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Calorific Value** | **≈ 37 – 40 MJ/kg** | **≈ 18 – 25 MJ/m³** | ≈ 42 – 45 MJ/kg |
| **Density** | 860 – 900 kg/m³ | 1.10 – 1.25 kg/m³ | 820 – 860 kg/m³ |
| **Cetane Number** | 50 – 60 | N/A | 45 – 55 |
| **Octane Number** | Low (~ 25) | 120 – 130 | N/A |
| **Flash Point** | **> 130 °C** (safe) | Flammable (CH₄) | 55 – 65 °C |
| **Auto-ignition Temp.** | ~ 250 °C | ~ 595 °C | ~ 210 °C |

> [!WARNING]
> The **flash point of biodiesel is dramatically higher** than diesel — this is a *safety advantage* (less fire hazard during storage) but a *cold-start disadvantage* (harder atomization at low temperature).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Universal Demerits of Alternate Fuels (KTU Board Favourite)

Every alternate fuel — whether CNG, LPG, hydrogen, ethanol, biodiesel, biogas, or producer gas — exhibits a **common basket of disadvantages**. KTU examiners love asking *"Discuss the demerits of alternate fuels"* as a 7-mark question. Memorize this structured list:

### A. Energy Density Demerits

- **Lower calorific value (CV) per unit mass/volume** than gasoline/diesel.
- Requires **larger fuel tanks** for the same driving range.
- Hydrogen is the *worst offender*: LHV ≈ **120 MJ/kg** (highest) but volumetric energy density is **abysmal** because H₂ is the lightest molecule.

### B. Storage & Distribution Demerits

- **Hydrogen** requires cryogenic tanks at **−253 °C** or 700-bar compression.
- **CNG** needs 200 – 250 bar cylinders.
- **LPG** needs pressurized bullets.
- **Biodiesel** oxidizes on long storage; **phase separation** occurs in B20 blends below 0 °C.
- **Biogas** cannot be liquefied easily at ambient conditions due to high CO₂ content.

### C. Engine Modification Demerits

- Higher **viscosity** of biodiesel → poor atomization → injector coking, ring sticking.
- **CNG** displaces ~10 – 12 % of intake air → loss of volumetric efficiency.
- **Hydrogen** causes **abnormal combustion** (pre-ignition, backfire) due to wide flammability limits.
- **Biodiesel** attacks rubber seals and natural-rubber hoses (elastomer compatibility issue).

### D. Cold-Start & Vaporization Demerits

- **CNG, LPG, biogas** need vaporizers; cold-start emissions rise sharply.
- **Biodiesel** has pour point **+2 to +15 °C** versus diesel's **−15 °C**.
- **Ethanol** has high **heat of vaporization** (~ 900 kJ/kg) — carburetor icing in cold weather.

### E. Production, Cost & Feedstock Demerits

- **Hydrogen** from electrolysis is 2 – 3× costlier than gasoline equivalent.
- **Biodiesel** from virgin vegetable oil is **1.5 – 2× costlier** than petro-diesel (feedstock = 60 – 80 % of cost).
- **1st-gen biofuels** compete with food supply (the *food-vs-fuel* debate).
- Land-use change for biodiesel crops causes **deforestation** (e.g., palm oil in Indonesia).

### F. Emission Trade-Offs (NOₓ, Unburned HC, Aldehydes)

- **Biodiesel** reduces CO, PM, HC but **increases NOₓ** by ~5 – 10 % due to higher in-cylinder temperature and bound oxygen.
- **CNG** reduces CO and HC but methane slip (unburned CH₄) is a potent greenhouse gas (GWP = 28 – 34 over 100 years).
- **Hydrogen** has near-zero CO/HC/CO₂ but high NOₓ.

### G. Safety & Material Compatibility

- **Hydrogen** embrittles metals; leak detection is hard (odorless, colorless, explosive 4 – 75 % vol).
- **Biogas** contains H₂S → corrosion of pipelines and IC engine parts.
- **Ethanol** is hygroscopic — absorbs water, corrodes aluminum fuel tanks.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Mnemonic: "EVSCCP"**
> *E*nergy density ↓, *V*aporization issues, *S*torage costs ↑, *C*old-start poor, *C*ost high, *P*roduction pollution/feedstock.

---

## 2.2 Biodiesel — Transesterification Theory

### 2.2.1 The Chemistry

Biodiesel is made by reacting a **triglyceride** (TG) with a **short-chain alcohol** (methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a **base catalyst** (NaOH, KOH, or NaOCH₃).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Transesterification Reaction (KTU 2024 — High Yield):**
> **Triglyceride + 3 Alcohol → 3 Fatty Acid Alkyl Esters (Biodiesel) + Glycerol**
>
> i.e. *TG + 3 ROH ⇌ 3 FAME + Glycerol*, with catalyst and ~60 °C.

### 2.2.2 Why Methanol, Not Ethanol?

| Property | Methanol | Ethanol |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Reactivity with TG | Higher (shorter chain) | Lower |
| Separation of glycerol | Easy (immiscible) | Hard (forms emulsion) |
| Biodiesel product | FAME | FAEE (ethyl ester) |
| KTU answer | **Methanol preferred** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Molar ratio used in industry: 6 : 1 (methanol : oil)**, i.e., a 100 % excess over stoichiometric, to push the equilibrium toward the product side (Le Chatelier's Principle).

### 2.2.3 Catalysts — Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous

- **Homogeneous base catalysis (NaOH, KOH):** Fast, mild conditions (60 °C), but **soap formation** if FFAs > 2 %.
- **Homogeneous acid catalysis (H₂SO₄):** Slow (4 – 8 h), but tolerates high FFA feedstock.
- **Two-step acid-base catalysis:** Best for waste cooking oil (high FFA).
- **Heterogeneous (CaO, MgO, zeolites):** Reusable, no soap, but slower and mass-transfer limited.

### 2.2.4 Process Steps

1. **Feedstock pretreatment** (degumming, dewaxing, drying).
2. **Acid esterification** (if FFA > 2 %) — H₂SO₄ + methanol at 60 °C.
3. **Base transesterification** — NaOH + methanol at 60 °C for 1 – 2 h.
4. **Phase separation** — biodiesel (top) and glycerol (bottom) layers.
5. **Washing** — water wash to remove catalyst and soap.
6. **Drying** — vacuum drying to remove residual water/methanol.

### 2.2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Biodiesel Production

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equations every KTU student must know:**

| Equation | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yield (Y)** | $\displaystyle Y = \frac{m_{biodiesel}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$ | m = mass | % |
| **Molar Conversion** | $\displaystyle X = \frac{n_{FAME,out}}{n_{TG,initial} \times 3} \times 100$ | n = moles | % |
| **Stoichiometric alcohol mass** | $\displaystyle m_{alc} = 3 \times M_{ROH} \times \frac{m_{oil}}{M_{oil}}$ | M = molar mass | kg |
| **Catalyst loading** | $\displaystyle w_{cat} = \frac{m_{cat}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$ | w = weight % | % |
| **Density of blend Bx** | $\displaystyle \rho_{Bx} = \frac{x \cdot \rho_{b} + (100-x) \cdot \rho_{d}}{100}$ | x = vol% biodiesel; b = biodiesel; d = diesel | kg/m³ |
| **Cetane number of blend** | $\displaystyle CN_{Bx} = \frac{x \cdot CN_{b} + (100-x) \cdot CN_{d}}{100}$ | linear mixing | — |
| **Lower Heating Value of blend** | $\displaystyle LHV_{Bx} = \frac{x \cdot LHV_{b} + (100-x) \cdot LHV_{d}}{100}$ | linear mixing | MJ/kg |
| **Viscosity (Arrhenius blend)** | $\displaystyle \ln\mu_{blend} = \sum x_i \ln\mu_i$ | x = mole/volume fraction | cSt |
| **Oxygen content of blend** | $\displaystyle O_{Bx} = \frac{x \cdot O_{b} + (100-x) \cdot O_{d}}{100}$ | linear | wt % |
| **Calorific value of FAME** | $\displaystyle LHV = 49.43 - 0.015 \cdot N_c$ | N_c = carbon number of FAME chain | MJ/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute value rule:** For absolute values in formulas, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, never bare pipes inside markdown tables.

---

## 2.3 Biogas — Anaerobic Digestion Theory

### 2.3.1 The Four Biochemical Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Anaerobic digestion is a **four-stage biochemical cascade** carried out by **four consortia of microorganisms**:

1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) → monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).
   - Slowest stage, rate-limiting for cellulosic feedstocks.
2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers → Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) + H₂ + CO₂.
   - By acidogenic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*).
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs + H₂ + CO₂ → Acetic acid (CH₃COOH).
   - By acetogenic bacteria (e.g., *Syntrophobacter*).
4. **Methanogenesis** — CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ (most common, ~70 % of CH₄).
   - By methanogens (e.g., *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta*).
   - Also: CO₂ + 4 H₂ → CH₄ + 2 H₂O (hydrogenotrophic, ~30 %).

### 2.3.2 Operating Parameters (Critical for KTU)

| Parameter | Optimal Range | Effect of Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | **Mesophilic: 30 – 40 °C** OR **Thermophilic: 50 – 60 °C** | Below 20 °C → methanogens die |
| **pH** | **6.8 – 7.5** | < 6.3 → acid-crash (VFA accumulation) |
| **C/N ratio** | **20 – 30 : 1** | Low C/N → NH₃ toxicity; High C/N → slow digestion |
| **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time)** | 15 – 40 days | Shorter HRT → washout of methanogens |
| **SRT (Solid Retention Time)** | ≥ 20 days | — |
| **OLR (Organic Loading Rate)** | 1 – 5 kg VS/m³·day | Higher OLR → VFA accumulation |
| **Moisture (TS)** | 7 – 12 % (for solid-state AD) | Too dry → mass-transfer limit |

### 2.3.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Biogas Production

| Equation | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Buswell Equation (Ideal AD)** | $\displaystyle C_n H_a O_b N_c + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4}\right) H_2O \to \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8}\right) CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8}\right) CO_2 + c\, NH_3$ | General substrate formula | mol |
| **Buswell (Carbohydrate: C₆H₁₀O₅)** | $\displaystyle C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \to 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$ | 1 mol → 3 mol CH₄ | mol |
| **Buswell (Lipid: C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆)** | $\displaystyle C_{57}H_{104}O_6 + 27\,H_2O \to 40\,CH_4 + 17\,CO_2$ | 1 mol → 40 mol CH₄ | mol |
| **Buswell (Protein: C₅H₇NO₂)** | $\displaystyle C_5H_7NO_2 + 3\,H_2O \to 2.5\,CH_4 + 2.5\,CO_2 + NH_3$ | 1 mol → 2.5 mol CH₄ | mol |
| **Theoretical CH₄ Yield** | $\displaystyle Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{22.4 \cdot V_{CH_4}}{m_{VS}}$ | Standard molar volume = 22.4 L/mol at STP | L CH₄/kg VS |
| **Theoretical CO₂ Yield** | $\displaystyle Y_{CO_2} = \dfrac{22.4 \cdot V_{CO_2}}{m_{VS}}$ | — | L CO₂/kg VS |
| **Energy from Biogas** | $\displaystyle E = V_{biogas} \times \eta \times LHV_{CH_4} \times x_{CH_4}$ | η = engine efficiency; x = methane fraction | MJ |
| **HRT** | $\displaystyle HRT = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{Q_{feed}}$ | V = volume; Q = volumetric flow | days |
| **OLR** | $\displaystyle OLR = \dfrac{m_{VS,feed}}{V_{reactor}}$ | Mass feed / Volume reactor | kg VS/m³·day |
| **C/N ratio** | $\displaystyle (C/N) = \dfrac{\%C}{\%N}$ | Elemental analysis | — |

### 2.3.4 Real-World Application — Why Biogas Matters

- **Kerala Scenario (KTU Context):** The *Suchitwa Mission* and *NABARD* promote small (1 – 6 m³) household biogas plants in Wayanad, Idukki, and Palakkad districts. Cow dung + kitchen waste → cooking gas + slurry biofertilizer.
- **Industrial scale:** Germany has **> 9,500 biogas plants** generating **> 33 TWh** of electricity (≈ 5 % of German renewable electricity).
- **Global:** India has **> 5 million** family-size biogas plants under the *National Biogas Programme (NNBP)*.

---

## 2.4 Why Biodiesel & Biogas Are *Better* (Counter-Argument for KTU Essays)

| Advantage | Biodiesel | Biogas |
|---|---|---|
| **Renewable** | Yes | Yes |
| **Biodegradable** | Yes (~ 98 % in 28 days) | N/A (gas) |
| **Toxicity** | Non-toxic | Non-toxic (after H₂S scrubbing) |
| **Lubricity** | Excellent (10× diesel) | N/A |
| **Carbon neutrality** | ~ 78 % closed loop | ~ 80 – 90 % |
| **Engine wear** | Reduces | Reduces (synthetic oil in CH₄ use) |
| **Cetane number** | Higher (50 – 60) | N/A (octane ~130) |
| **CO, PM, HC** | ↓ 40 – 60 % | ↓ 70 – 90 % |

> [!TIP]
> A complete KTU answer always includes **both demerits and mitigations** — examiners award full marks only when students show *engineering trade-off awareness*.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transesterification Stoichiometry for Biodiesel

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** Calculate the theoretical mass of methanol and NaOH catalyst required to produce **1,000 kg of biodiesel** (FAME) from soybean oil (triglyceride, average molecular weight **885 g/mol**, FFA = 0.8 %) by base-catalyzed transesterification. Also compute the mass of glycerol byproduct.

**Step 1: Define molecular weights.**

For soybean oil (predominantly tri-linolein and tri-olein), average molar mass:

$$M_{oil} \approx 885 \text{ g/mol} = 0.885 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

Methanol: $M_{MeOH} = 32 \text{ g/mol} = 0.032 \text{ kg/kmol}$.

NaOH: $M_{NaOH} = 40 \text{ g/mol}$.

Glycerol: $M_{gly} = 92 \text{ g/mol} = 0.092 \text{ kg/kmol}$.

Average FAME (methyl oleate, C₁₉H₃₆O₂): $M_{FAME} \approx 296 \text{ g/mol} = 0.296 \text{ kg/kmol}$.

**Step 2: Write the balanced stoichiometric reaction (per mole of TG).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1 mol TG} + \text{3 mol MeOH} &\xrightarrow{\text{NaOH, 60°C}} \text{3 mol FAME} + \text{1 mol Glycerol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Relate the FAME mass produced to TG consumed.**

From stoichiometry, 1 mol TG → 3 mol FAME.

Therefore, mass of TG needed for 1,000 kg FAME:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{FAME} &= \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{0.296 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 3378.38 \text{ kmol} \\
n_{TG} &= \frac{n_{FAME}}{3} = \frac{3378.38}{3} = 1126.13 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{TG} &= n_{TG} \times M_{oil} = 1126.13 \times 0.885 = 996.62 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: 996.62 kg of oil is consumed to make 1,000 kg of biodiesel. (≈ 100 % conversion, as expected for stoichiometric limit.)

**Step 4: Stoichiometric methanol requirement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{MeOH,\,stoich} &= 3 \times n_{TG} = 3 \times 1126.13 = 3378.38 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{MeOH,\,stoich} &= n_{MeOH,\,stoich} \times M_{MeOH} = 3378.38 \times 0.032 = 108.11 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Industrial methanol (with 6 : 1 molar ratio = 100 % excess).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{MeOH,\,actual} &= 6 \times n_{TG} = 6 \times 1126.13 = 6756.76 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{MeOH,\,actual} &= 6756.76 \times 0.032 = 216.22 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: NaOH catalyst loading (1 % w/w of oil is industrial standard).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{NaOH} &= 0.01 \times m_{oil} = 0.01 \times 996.62 = 9.97 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Glycerol byproduct mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{gly} &= n_{TG} = 1126.13 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{gly} &= n_{gly} \times M_{gly} = 1126.13 \times 0.092 = 103.60 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final mass balance table (per 1,000 kg biodiesel):**

| Stream | Input (kg) | Output (kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Soybean oil | 996.62 | — |
| Methanol (with 100 % excess) | 216.22 | — |
| NaOH catalyst | 9.97 | — |
| Biodiesel (FAME) | — | 1000.00 |
| Glycerol | — | 103.60 |
| Excess methanol (recovered) | — | 108.11 |
| Water wash losses | — | ≈ 11.10 (balances) |
| **Total** | **1222.81** | **1222.81** ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> This mass balance is the **gold standard** for KTU Part B 14-mark questions on transesterification. Always show *all seven* steps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Biogas Yield by Buswell Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A cattle farm produces **1,000 kg/day** of fresh cow dung (Total Solids = 18 %, VS/TS = 0.80). Calculate: (a) the daily methane production in m³ at STP, and (b) the equivalent electrical energy in kWh if a biogas genset has 35 % electrical efficiency.

**Step 1: Compute VS feed per day.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TS &= 0.18 \times 1000 = 180 \text{ kg/day} \\
VS &= 0.80 \times TS = 0.80 \times 180 = 144 \text{ kg VS/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply empirical methane yield for cow dung (standard literature value).**

For cattle manure, the empirical biochemical methane potential (BMP) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{CH_4,\,cow} &= 0.20 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS} \quad \text{(commonly used value)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CH_4} &= 144 \text{ kg VS/day} \times 0.20 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg VS} = 28.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute total biogas volume (assuming CH₄ = 60 % by volume).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{CH_4} &= 0.60 \\
V_{biogas} &= \frac{V_{CH_4}}{x_{CH_4}} = \frac{28.8}{0.60} = 48.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute energy content (LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³ at STP).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{CH_4} &= V_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4} = 28.8 \times 35.8 = 1031.04 \text{ MJ/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute electrical output at 35 % efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{elec} &= \eta_e \times E_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 1031.04 = 360.86 \text{ MJ/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{elec,\,kWh} &= \frac{360.86}{3.6} = 100.24 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Sanity check using Buswell equation for a generic carbohydrate-rich substrate.**

The Buswell equation for C₆H₁₀O₅ (cellulose model) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \to 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2
\end{aligned}
$$

From 1 mol cellulose (162 g) → 3 mol CH₄ (3 × 22.4 L = 67.2 L at STP).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{CH_4,\,theo,\,cell} &= \frac{67.2 \text{ L}}{0.162 \text{ kg}} = 414.8 \text{ L/kg cellulose} = 0.415 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a lipid-rich substrate (C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆, average fat), Buswell gives 40 mol CH₄ from 1 mol substrate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{CH_4,\,lipid} &= \frac{40 \times 22.4}{860} = 1.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Lipid-rich feedstocks yield **2 – 3× more CH₄ per kg VS** than carbohydrate-rich ones, but are slower to digest and may cause VFA accumulation.

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| VS feed | 144 | kg VS/day |
| CH₄ produced | 28.8 | m³/day |
| Biogas produced | 48.0 | m³/day |
| Energy in CH₄ | 1031.04 | MJ/day |
| Electrical output | 100.24 | kWh/day |
| Annual electricity (× 365) | **36,587 kWh/year** | — |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Biodiesel Blend Property Calculator

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A B20 blend (20 % biodiesel + 80 % petro-diesel by volume) is to be analyzed. Given: $\rho_b = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_d = 840 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $LHV_b = 37.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $LHV_d = 43.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $CN_b = 55$, $CN_d = 50$.

**Step 1: Density of blend.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{B20} &= \frac{20 \times 880 + 80 \times 840}{100} = \frac{17600 + 67200}{100} = 848 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cetane number of blend (linear).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CN_{B20} &= \frac{20 \times 55 + 80 \times 50}{100} = \frac{1100 + 4000}{100} = 51
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Lower heating value (mass basis).**

To use the linear formula on a *mass* basis, first convert volume fractions to mass fractions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{b,\,mass} &= \frac{0.20 \times 880}{0.20 \times 880 + 0.80 \times 840} = \frac{176}{176 + 672} = 0.2075 \\
LHV_{B20} &= 0.2075 \times 37.5 + 0.7925 \times 43.0 = 7.78 + 34.08 = 41.86 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Volumetric energy density (for range estimation).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{vol} &= \rho_{B20} \times LHV_{B20} = 848 \times 41.86 = 35,498 \text{ MJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always convert vol% → mass% before applying the LHV mixing rule if the densities differ. Students lose marks by blindly multiplying vol% × LHV.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Biodiesel Production Process Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below is a **process flow block diagram** representing the transesterification process. It is rendered as a block-level functional architecture to comply with Mermaid's no-complex-physical-drawing restriction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedstockA[Feedstock Storage<br/>Vegetable Oil or Used Cooking Oil]
    pretreatA[Pre-Treatment<br/>Degumming Dewaxing Drying]
    acidstepA[Acid Esterification<br/>H2SO4 plus Methanol at 60 C<br/>Required if FFA greater than 2 percent]
    basestepA[Base Transesterification<br/>NaOH or KOH plus Methanol at 60 C<br/>Molar Ratio 6 to 1]
    mixA[Mixing Reactor<br/>Stirred Tank 200 rpm<br/>Residence Time 1 to 2 h]
    sepA[Gravity Separator<br/>Decanter Funnel<br/>Biodiesel Top Layer<br/>Glycerol Bottom Layer]
    washA[Water Wash Column<br/>3 Stage Soft Water<br/>Removes Soap and Catalyst]
    dryA[Vacuum Dryer<br/>Residual Moisture less than 500 ppm]
    fuelA[Purified Biodiesel B100<br/>Storage Tank 25 C]
    glyA[Glycerol Purification<br/>Pharma Grade Byproduct]
    recA[Methanol Recovery<br/>Distillation Column 64 C Boiling]

    feedstockA --> pretreatA
    pretreatA --> acidstepA
    acidstepA --> basestepA
    basestepA --> mixA
    mixA --> sepA
    sepA --> washA
    washA --> dryA
    dryA --> fuelA
    sepA --> glyA
    mixA --> recA
    recA --> basestepA
```

**Engineering Reading Guide (KTU Board-Exam Style):**
- The **acid step** is the *gatekeeper* — only needed for high-FFA waste oils.
- The **separation step** exploits density difference ($\rho_{FAME} \approx 880$, $\rho_{gly} \approx 1260 \text{ kg/m}^3$).
- The **recovered methanol** is recycled back, reducing operating cost by 30 – 40 %.

---

## 4.2 Biogas Anaerobic Digester Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bioA[Biomass Feed<br/>Cow Dung Kitchen Waste Sewage]
    feedprepA[Feed Preparation<br/>Grinding Mixing Water Dilution<br/>TS adjusted to 8 to 12 percent]
    digesterA[Anaerobic Digester<br/>Sealed Oxygen Free Tank<br/>Mesophilic 35 C or Thermophilic 55 C<br/>HRT 20 to 40 days]
    biochemA[Four Stage Microbiology<br/>Hydrolysis Acidogenesis<br/>Acetogenesis Methanogenesis]
    gasA[Biogas Collection Dome<br/>CH4 60 percent CO2 38 percent<br/>H2S traces]
    scrubA[Gas Scrubbing Unit<br/>Iron Oxide H2S Removal<br/>Activated Carbon Siloxane Removal]
    compA[Gas Compressor and Storage<br/>Low Pressure Float Ball Gas Holder]
    utilA[End Use Applications<br/>Cooking Burner SI Engine Genset<br/>Boiler Boiler Upgrade to CNG Pipeline]

    slurryA[Digestate Slurry Outlet<br/>Liquid and Solid Phase]
    dewatA[Dewatering Screw Press<br/>Solid Cake and Liquid Fraction]
    fertA[Biofertilizer Storage<br/>Nitrogen Phosphorus Potassium Rich]

    bioA --> feedprepA
    feedprepA --> digesterA
    digesterA --> biochemA
    biochemA --> gasA
    gasA --> scrubA
    scrubA --> compA
    compA --> utilA
    digesterA --> slurryA
    slurryA --> dewatA
    dewatA --> fertA
```

**KTU Reading Tip:** Always label the **four microbial stages** in the digester — examiners award bonus marks for showing that you understand the *biochemistry*, not just the hardware.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Biodiesel vs Biogas

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIOD[BIODIESEL PATHWAY]
        oilA[Vegetable Oil] --> transA[Transesterification]
        transA --> famA[Liquid FAME]
        famA --> engineA[CI Diesel Engine]
    end

    subgraph BIOG[BIOGAS PATHWAY]
        wetA[Wet Biomass] --> adA[Anaerobic Digestion]
        adA --> metA[Gas CH4 plus CO2]
        metA --> siengineA[SI Engine or Genset]
    end

    style BIOD fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#D4A017
    style BIOG fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
```

**Insight:** Biodiesel is a *direct-swap* liquid fuel (minor engine mods). Biogas needs a **gas train** (scrubber → compressor → mixer → regulator) — more complex hardware.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and demands a concise but technically precise answer (typically 80 – 120 words + a labelled diagram or equation).

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PEMUT411]

**"List any six demerits of alternate fuels used in IC engines."**

#### Model Answer (Board-Exam Standard, 3 marks):

1. **Lower calorific value (CV):** Most alternate fuels (CNG, biogas, producer gas, ethanol) have 30 – 60 % lower CV per unit mass than gasoline, requiring larger fuel tanks.
2. **High storage pressure/temperature:** CNG at 200 bar, LPG at 17 bar, LH₂ at −253 °C — high capital cost.
3. **Cold-start difficulties:** Poor vaporization of CNG, LPG, and high-pour-point biodiesel at low ambient temperature.
4. **Engine modifications required:** Compressed tanks, gas mixers, injectors, and sensors increase vehicle cost by 8 – 15 %.
5. **Production cost:** Hydrogen by electrolysis is 2 – 3× costlier than gasoline equivalent; biodiesel from virgin oil is 1.5× costlier than diesel.
6. **Emission trade-offs:** NOₓ rises in biodiesel; methane slip (CH₄ unburned) in CNG is a 28× greenhouse gas; H₂O in ethanol corrodes aluminum.

> [!TIP]
> 1 mark for correct identification of each demarit, 0.5 mark for justification.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT411]

**"Differentiate between biodiesel and biogas based on physical state, production process, and feedstock."**

#### Model Answer (Board-Exam Standard, 3 marks):

| Parameter | **Biodiesel** | **Biogas** |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical state** | Liquid (FAME, C₁₆ – C₁₉) | Gaseous (CH₄ + CO₂) |
| **Production process** | Transesterification (TG + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + glycerol) at 60 °C with NaOH/KOH catalyst | Anaerobic digestion (4-stage microbial breakdown) in sealed digester at 30 – 55 °C |
| **Feedstock** | Vegetable oils (soybean, Jatropha, palm), used cooking oil, animal fats | Wet biomass (cow dung, sewage, food waste, energy crops) |
| **Engine compatibility** | Direct CI engine use (B100); minor injector mods | Needs gas train; used in SI engine or modified dual-fuel CI |
| **Calorific value** | 37 – 40 MJ/kg | 18 – 25 MJ/m³ |
| **Main byproduct** | Glycerol (10 % w/w) | Digestate slurry (biofertilizer) |

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B 14-mark questions carry **internal choice** — the student answers **either OR both** of the two alternatives (typically part-a 7 marks + part-b 7 marks).

---

### Q3A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PEMUT411] — 14 Marks

**(a) With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the production of biodiesel by transesterification. State the role of the catalyst and the reason for using 6:1 molar ratio of methanol to oil. (7 marks)**

**(b) A feedstock of 1,500 kg of used cooking oil (M = 870 g/mol, FFA = 3.5 %) is to be converted to biodiesel. Calculate: (i) the mass of methanol needed at 6:1 molar ratio, (ii) the mass of NaOH catalyst at 1 % w/w, and (iii) the mass of glycerol byproduct expected. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Define transesterification.
> Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which an alcohol (methanol/ethanol) reacts with a triglyceride in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH, KOH, NaOCH₃) to produce fatty acid alkyl esters (biodiesel) and glycerol.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Provide balanced chemical equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,CH_3OH &\xrightleftharpoons[\text{60 °C, 1 h}]{\text{NaOH (1 wt \%)}} \text{3 FAME} + \text{Glycerol} \\
C_{57}H_{104}O_6 + 3\,CH_3OH &\to 3\,C_{19}H_{36}O_2 + C_3H_8O_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Draw flow diagram (refer SECTION 4.1 of these notes).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Role of catalyst — *lowers activation energy* by abstracting the proton from methanol to form the **methoxide ion** (CH₃O⁻), the actual nucleophile that attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride.

**Step 5 (0.5 mark):** Why 6:1 molar ratio — *stoichiometric ratio is 3:1; the 6:1 ratio provides 100 % excess methanol to **shift the equilibrium toward the product side (Le Chatelier's Principle)** and compensate for soap formation losses.*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**Given:** $m_{oil} = 1500 \text{ kg}$, $M_{oil} = 870 \text{ g/mol} = 0.870 \text{ kg/kmol}$, $M_{MeOH} = 32 \text{ g/mol} = 0.032 \text{ kg/kmol}$, $M_{NaOH} = 40 \text{ g/mol}$, $M_{gly} = 92 \text{ g/mol} = 0.092 \text{ kg/kmol}$.

**Step 1 (2 marks): Moles of oil.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{oil} &= \frac{1500}{0.870} = 1724.14 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Mass of methanol (6:1 ratio).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{MeOH} &= 6 \times 1724.14 = 10344.83 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{MeOH} &= 10344.83 \times 0.032 = 331.04 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Mass of NaOH catalyst (1 % w/w).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{NaOH} &= 0.01 \times 1500 = 15.0 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Mass of glycerol byproduct (1:1 stoichiometry with TG).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{gly} &= 1724.14 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{gly} &= 1724.14 \times 0.092 = 158.62 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Mass of methanol (at 6:1) | **331.04 kg** |
| Mass of NaOH catalyst | **15.00 kg** |
| Mass of glycerol byproduct | **158.62 kg** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** use mass ratio instead of molar ratio — this is the most common KTU mistake and costs 2 – 3 marks.
> - Do **not** forget to multiply by 3 (or 6) for stoichiometric — students often write $n_{MeOH} = n_{oil}$, which is wrong.
> - Always state the **units** in the final answer (kg, not g).

---

### Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT411] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) Explain the four stages of anaerobic digestion with a labelled diagram. Discuss the role of methanogenic bacteria and the conditions (pH, temperature, C/N) that affect biogas yield. (7 marks)**

**(b) A 50 m³ biogas digester is fed with 2,000 kg/day of kitchen waste (TS = 22 %, VS/TS = 0.85). The empirical methane yield is 0.32 m³ CH₄/kg VS. The methane fraction in biogas is 60 %. Compute: (i) the daily methane volume, (ii) the total daily biogas volume, and (iii) the HRT in days. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Name and describe the four stages.

| Stage | Reaction | Microbe | Slowest? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. **Hydrolysis** | Polymers (carbohydrate, protein, lipid) → Monomers (sugar, AA, FA) | *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides* | ✓ (rate-limiting) |
| 2. **Acidogenesis** | Monomers → VFAs (acetic, propionic, butyric) + H₂ + CO₂ | Acidogenic bacteria | — |
| 3. **Acetogenesis** | VFAs + H₂ + CO₂ → Acetic acid | *Syntrophobacter* | — |
| 4. **Methanogenesis** | CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ (or H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄) | *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta* | — |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Label the block diagram (refer SECTION 4.2 of these notes).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Role of methanogens + conditions.

- **Methanogens** are *archaea* that perform the final step: conversion of acetate (70 %) and H₂/CO₂ (30 %) into CH₄. They are **strict anaerobes** and very slow-growing (doubling time 2 – 25 days).
- **pH:** 6.8 – 7.5 (acid-crash below 6.3).
- **Temperature:** Mesophilic (30 – 40 °C) or thermophilic (50 – 60 °C).
- **C/N ratio:** 20 – 30 : 1 (too low → NH₃ toxicity; too high → N starvation).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion — methanogens are the *bottleneck* of the entire AD process.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**Given:** $V_{reactor} = 50 \text{ m}^3$, $m_{feed} = 2000 \text{ kg/day}$, TS = 22 %, VS/TS = 0.85, $Y_{CH_4} = 0.32 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg VS}$, $x_{CH_4} = 0.60$.

**Step 1 (2 marks): Compute VS feed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VS_{feed} &= m_{feed} \times TS \times (VS/TS) = 2000 \times 0.22 \times 0.85 \\
&= 374 \text{ kg VS/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Compute daily CH₄ volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CH_4} &= Y_{CH_4} \times VS_{feed} = 0.32 \times 374 = 119.68 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Compute total daily biogas volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{biogas} &= \frac{V_{CH_4}}{x_{CH_4}} = \frac{119.68}{0.60} = 199.47 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Compute HRT.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRT &= \frac{V_{reactor}}{Q_{feed}}
\end{aligned}
$$

We need $Q_{feed}$ in m³/day. Assuming feed density ≈ 1000 kg/m³ (dilute kitchen waste slurry):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{feed} &\approx \frac{2000 \text{ kg/day}}{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 2.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
HRT &= \frac{50}{2.0} = 25 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| VS feed | 374 kg VS/day |
| Daily CH₄ volume | **119.68 m³/day** |
| Total daily biogas | **199.47 m³/day** |
| HRT | **25 days** (within optimum 20 – 40 days ✓) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget to **multiply TS × VS/TS** to get VS — they use TS directly and over-estimate CH₄ yield by ~ 15 %.
> - Unit conversion of HRT: students often write "25" without "days" — KTU deducts 0.5 mark.
> - Do **not** mix feed mass (kg/day) with reactor volume (m³) without a density assumption — explicitly state "assuming ρ_feed ≈ 1000 kg/m³".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Print & Pin on Wall!**

### A. Demerits of Alternate Fuels (Universal)

- ❌ Low calorific value (CNG, biogas, producer gas)
- ❌ Storage complexity (cryogenic H₂, high-pressure CNG)
- ❌ Engine modification cost (8 – 15 % of vehicle cost)
- ❌ Cold-start difficulties (high latent heat of vaporization)
- ❌ NOₓ emission trade-off (biodiesel, H₂)
- ❌ Production cost (electrolysis H₂, virgin-oil biodiesel)
- ❌ Food-vs-fuel conflict (1st-gen bio-ethanol)
- ❌ Safety hazards (H₂ leak/embrittlement, H₂S in biogas)
- ❌ Methane slip in CNG (GWP = 28)

### B. Biodiesel Essentials

- ✅ Definition: **Mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids (FAME)**.
- ✅ Reaction: **TG + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + glycerol** (base-catalyzed, 60 °C).
- ✅ **Methanol : Oil = 6 : 1** (industrial standard).
- ✅ **Catalyst: NaOH or KOH (1 % w/w)**.
- ✅ **Methanol preferred over ethanol** (cheaper, no emulsion).
- ✅ **FAME (C₁₆ – C₁₉) chain** → similar to diesel (C₁₂ – C₂₅).
- ✅ Cetane number: **50 – 60** (higher than diesel).
- ✅ Calorific value: **37 – 40 MJ/kg** (lower than diesel's 43 MJ/kg).
- ✅ Lube oil: miscible → never let neat biodiesel sit in injector lines.
- ✅ Byproducts: **Glycerol (~ 10 % w/w)** — pharma/cosmetic grade.

### C. Biogas Essentials

- ✅ Definition: **CH₄ (50 – 70 %) + CO₂ (30 – 40 %) + H₂S traces**.
- ✅ Process: **Anaerobic digestion (AD)** — 4 stages.
- ✅ Stages: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**.
- ✅ Methanogens: *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta* — *strict anaerobes*, very slow.
- ✅ **Mesophilic (30 – 40 °C)** OR **Thermophilic (50 – 60 °C)**.
- ✅ **pH 6.8 – 7.5** (acid-crash below 6.3).
- ✅ **C/N ratio 20 – 30 : 1**.
- ✅ **HRT 20 – 40 days**; **OLR 1 – 5 kg VS/m³·day**.
- ✅ Calorific value: **18 – 25 MJ/m³** (biogas); **35.8 MJ/m³** (pure CH₄).
- ✅ **Buswell equation:** C₆H₁₀O₅ + H₂O → 3 CH₄ + 3 CO₂.
- ✅ **Lipid substrates yield 2 – 3× more CH₄** than carbohydrates.
- ✅ Byproduct: **digestate slurry** (rich NPK biofertilizer).
- ✅ H₂S scrubbing mandatory (iron oxide beds, activated carbon).

### D. Stoichiometric Constants (Memorize!)

| Constant | Value |
|---|---|
| Molar volume at STP | **22.4 L/mol** |
| LHV of methane | **35.8 MJ/m³** |
| LHV of methanol | **19.9 MJ/kg** |
| Density of glycerol | **1260 kg/m³** |
| Density of FAME | **~ 880 kg/m³** |
| 1 kWh | **3.6 MJ** |
| GWP of methane (100-yr) | **28** |

### E. Numerical Signatures (Spot-and-Solve Triggers)

| Question Cue | Equation |
|---|---|
| "Methanol required" | $m_{MeOH} = 6 \times n_{oil} \times M_{MeOH}$ |
| "Catalyst loading" | $m_{cat} = w_{cat} \times m_{oil}$ |
| "Glycerol produced" | $m_{gly} = n_{oil} \times M_{gly}$ |
| "Daily methane" | $V_{CH_4} = VS_{feed} \times Y_{CH_4}$ |
| "Total biogas" | $V_{biogas} = V_{CH_4} / x_{CH_4}$ |
| "HRT" | $HRT = V_{reactor} / Q_{feed}$ |
| "Blend property" | Use linear (vol or mass) mixing rule |

### F. KTU 2024 Bloom's-Level Distribution (Expected)

| Bloom's Level | Marks Weightage | Question Type |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Remember** | 15 % | Definitions, abbreviations |
| **L2 — Understand** | 25 % | Explain reactions, processes |
| **L3 — Apply** | 30 % | Numerical, formulas |
| **L4 — Analyze** | 20 % | Compare, contrast, justify |
| **L5 — Evaluate** | 10 % | Demerits + trade-offs, critiques |

### G. Mnemonics for Quick Recall

- **Biodiesel: "TAG-Me-Cat-Hex"** → *Triglyceride + Alcohol → Glycerol + FAME; Catalyst = base; 6:1 hex ratio.*
- **Biogas: "HAAM"** → *Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.*
- **Demerits: "EVSCCP"** → *Energy, Vaporization, Storage, Cold-start, Cost, Production.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board-Exam Mantra:**
> *"Every biodiesel calculation is a stoichiometry problem. Every biogas calculation is a mass balance problem. Every demerits answer needs the 'EVSCCP' framework."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production of Alternative Fuels** refers to the set of chemical, biochemical, and thermochemical processes employed to convert feedstocks (vegetable oils, animal fats, biomass, organic waste, water, coal, etc.) into usable secondary energy carriers such as **biodiesel, bioethanol, biogas, producer gas, hydrogen, and synthetic liquid fuels**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this is examined under **Module 2** as the *engineer's gateway* into renewable-fuel manufacturing — covering the reaction engineering, stoichiometry, process flow, and yield economics of the most commercially relevant alternative fuels.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Recipe" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of fuel production exactly like cooking in a kitchen.
> - The **feedstock** (oil, sugarcane, kitchen waste) is the *raw ingredient*.
> - The **process** (transesterification, fermentation, digestion, gasification, electrolysis) is the *cooking method*.
> - The **catalyst** (NaOH, enzymes, heat, electricity) is the *chef's secret spice*.
> - The **reactor** (stirred tank, digester, gasifier) is the *cooking vessel*.
> - The **product** (biodiesel, ethanol, methane, H₂) is the *finished dish* served to the engine.

Just as a chef must control **temperature, time, pH, and catalyst dose** to get a good dish, a fuel engineer must control the same variables to get a high yield of clean-burning fuel.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Metric | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Heating value of biodiesel | ≈ **37.27 MJ/L** | ~90% of petro-diesel |
| Calorific value of biogas (CH₄) | ≈ **55.5 MJ/kg** | Pure methane standard |
| Density of bioethanol | **0.789 kg/L** | Used for blending ratios |
| Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (H₂) | **34.3 : 1 (mass)** | Highest among common fuels |
| Energy density of producer gas | **4.5 – 5.5 MJ/m³** | Low — limits long-distance use |
| Typical biodiesel yield (transesterification) | **80 – 95 %** | Function of catalyst & FFAs |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Production Routes (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 2 Scope:**
> 1. **Biodiesel** — Transesterification of vegetable oils / animal fats
> 2. **Bioethanol** — Fermentation of sugars / starch / lignocellulose
> 3. **Biogas** — Anaerobic digestion of organic matter
> 4. **Producer Gas** — Gasification of solid biomass
> 5. **Hydrogen** — Electrolysis, steam reforming, biomass routes

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biodiesel Production — Transesterification (Step-by-Step Logic)

Transesterification is a **three-step, reversible, equilibrium-controlled** reaction in which a **triglyceride (TG)** reacts with a **short-chain alcohol** (methanol/ethanol) in the presence of an **alkali/acid catalyst** to yield **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME = biodiesel)** and **glycerol** as a co-product.

### 2.1.1 The Three Sequential Reactions

1. **TG + ROH → DG + R'COOR** (Triglyceride → Diglyceride)
2. **DG + ROH → MG + R'COOR** (Diglyceride → Monoglyceride)
3. **MG + ROH → GL + R'COOR** (Monoglyceride → Glycerol)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three steps?** Because the glyceride backbone is cleaved *one fatty acid at a time*. Each cleavage releases one FAME molecule, ultimately giving **3 FAME + 1 glycerol** per triglyceride.

### 2.1.2 Overall Stoichiometry

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{ROH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{R'COOR} + \text{Glycerol}$$

### 2.1.3 Process Parameters (Why and How)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Molar ratio (alcohol : oil) | **6 : 1** (theoretical = 3:1) | Drives equilibrium *forward* (Le Chatelier) |
| Catalyst (NaOH / KOH) | **0.5 – 1.5 % wt of oil** | Accelerates reaction; too much → saponification |
| Reaction temperature | **60 – 65 °C** (methanol) | Near methanol boiling point; higher → loss of alcohol |
| Reaction time | **1 – 4 hours** | Determines conversion to FAME |
| Mixing intensity | **400 – 600 rpm** | Mass-transfer limited step; poor mixing → low yield |
| Water / FFA content of oil | **< 0.5 % / < 2 %** | Water + alkali → soap (saponification, undesirable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** If the oil has **FFA > 2 %**, a **two-step acid-base catalysis** is preferred — first H₂SO₄ esterifies the FFA, then NaOH completes transesterification.

---

## 2.2 Bioethanol Production — Fermentation

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast (Zymomonas / Saccharomyces)}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{Heat}$$

### 2.2.1 Process Steps (Why Each Step)
1. **Feedstock preparation** — milling, hydrolysis (lignocellulose needs acid/enzymatic hydrolysis to release sugars).
2. **Fermentation** — yeast converts C₆/C₅ sugars to ethanol at **30 – 37 °C**, pH 4 – 5, anaerobic.
3. **Distillation** — separates ethanol (bp 78.4 °C) from water.
4. **Dehydration** — molecular sieves / azeotropic distillation produce **anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %+)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical yield of ethanol from glucose** is derived from stoichiometry:
> 1 mole glucose (180 g) → 2 moles ethanol (92 g) = **51.1 % by mass**.

---

## 2.3 Biogas Production — Anaerobic Digestion

$$\text{Organic Matter} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Anaerobic microbes}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{Heat}$$

### 2.3.1 Four Biological Stages
1. **Hydrolysis** — polymers → monomers (proteins, fats, sugars).
2. **Acidogenesis** — monomers → volatile fatty acids (VFAs) + H₂ + CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs → acetic acid + H₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ (by *Methanobacterium*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biogas composition (typical):** CH₄ = **50 – 70 %**, CO₂ = **30 – 40 %**, H₂S = **0.1 – 0.5 %**, trace N₂ & water vapor.

---

## 2.4 Producer Gas — Gasification

$$2\,\text{C} + \text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO} + 3.76\,\text{N}_2 + \text{Heat}$$

The gasifier operates with sub-stoichiometric air, **Equivalence Ratio (ER) = 0.2 – 0.4**, producing a low-BTU fuel gas.

### 2.4.1 Producer Gas Composition
- **CO = 20 – 25 %**
- **H₂ = 10 – 15 %**
- **CH₄ = 2 – 5 %**
- **CO₂ = 8 – 12 %**
- **N₂ = 45 – 55 %** (from air dilution)
- **CV ≈ 4.5 – 5.5 MJ/Nm³**

---

## 2.5 Hydrogen Production

| Route | Reaction | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrolysis** | 2 H₂O → 2 H₂ + O₂ (DC current, alkaline/PEM cell) | Green H₂ if renewable electricity |
| **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** | CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3 H₂ (Ni catalyst, 800 °C) | 95 % of global H₂ |
| **Water-Gas Shift** | CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (Fe/Cr catalyst) | Follows SMR for more H₂ |
| **Biomass gasification** | Biomass + H₂O → H₂ + CO + CO₂ + CH₄ | Carbon-neutral route |
| **Photo-biological** | Algae + sunlight → H₂ (in research) | Future tech |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FAME yield | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100$ | % | Practical: 80 – 95 % |
| 2 | Stoichiometric alcohol : oil | $n_{\text{ROH}} : n_{\text{TG}} = 3 : 1$ | mol/mol | Used = 6:1 (excess) |
| 3 | Glycerol mass balance | $m_{\text{glycerol}} = m_{\text{oil}} \times \dfrac{92}{890}$ | kg | For pure TG (M = 890 g/mol) |
| 4 | Theoretical ethanol from glucose | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.5111$ | mass frac | 51.11 % by mass |
| 5 | Practical ethanol yield (fermentation) | $\eta_{\text{prac}} \approx 90$ – $95 \% \times \eta_{\text{th}}$ | — | Inhibited at high [EtOH] |
| 6 | Biogas yield (cattle dung) | **0.02 – 0.05 m³/kg/day** | m³/kg·d | Varies with HRT |
| 7 | Equivalence Ratio (gasifier) | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | — | Gasifier: 0.2 – 0.4 |
| 8 | Air–Fuel ratio for producer gas | **1.5 – 2.0 kg/kg biomass** | kg/kg | Sub-stoichiometric |
| 9 | H₂ from electrolysis | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | Faraday constant $F = 96485$ C/mol |
| 10 | Energy in biogas | $E = V_{\text{CH}_4} \times 55.5$ | MJ | 55.5 MJ/kg × ρ_CH₄ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Whenever asked for *yield*, show the formula, substitute values, mention the unit, and state if it is *theoretical vs. practical*. Examiners award method marks generously.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Alcohol Requirement for Transesterification

**Given:** A batch of 1000 kg refined soybean oil (assumed pure triolein, M = 884.4 g/mol, density ≈ 920 kg/m³) is to be transesterified with methanol (M = 32 g/mol) using NaOH catalyst.

**Step 1 — Moles of triglyceride in oil.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{oil}} &= 1000\ \text{kg} = 1{,}000{,}000\ \text{g} \\
n_{\text{TG}} &= \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{TG}}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000\ \text{g}}{884.4\ \text{g/mol}} \\
n_{\text{TG}} &\approx 1130.66\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric methanol requirement (3 moles methanol per mole TG).**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{MeOH, stoich}} &= 3 \times n_{\text{TG}} \\
&= 3 \times 1130.66 \\
&= 3391.98\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert moles of methanol to mass.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{MeOH, stoich}} &= n_{\text{MeOH, stoich}} \times M_{\text{MeOH}} \\
&= 3391.98 \times 32 \\
&\approx 108{,}543\ \text{g} \\
&\approx 108.54\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply 100 % excess (6 : 1 molar ratio is standard).**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{MeOH, actual}} &= 2 \times 108.54 \\
&\approx 217.09\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Catalyst (NaOH) at 1 % wt of oil.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{NaOH}} &= 0.01 \times 1000 \\
&= 10\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical FAME (methyl oleate, M = 296.5 g/mol) yield.**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{FAME}} &= 3 \times n_{\text{TG}} = 3 \times 1130.66 = 3391.98\ \text{mol} \\
m_{\text{FAME}} &= 3391.98 \times 296.5 \\
&\approx 1{,}005{,}722\ \text{g} \\
&\approx 1005.72\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 1000 kg oil theoretically yields **~1006 kg FAME** because the methyl (15 g/mol) replaces each glycerol-backbone oxygen bond (≈ –41 g/mol) — so the FAME is actually *heavier* per mole than the triglyceride backbone it replaces in net mass. Real-world yield is **~85 %** → ~855 kg biodiesel per 1000 kg oil.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

**Given:** 1 kg of pure glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, M = 180 g/mol) is fermented.

**Step 1 — Moles of glucose.**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{glucose}} &= \frac{1000\ \text{g}}{180\ \text{g/mol}} \\
&\approx 5.556\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometry: 1 mol glucose → 2 mol ethanol.**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{EtOH}} &= 2 \times 5.556 \\
&\approx 11.111\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of ethanol (M = 46 g/mol).**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{EtOH, th}} &= 11.111 \times 46 \\
&\approx 511.1\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Express as mass fraction.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{th}} &= \frac{511.1}{1000} \\
&= 0.5111 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 51.11 \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Practical yield (assume 90 % of theoretical).**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{prac}} &= 0.90 \times 0.5111 \\
&\approx 0.460 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 46.0 \%
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Production Routes

| Aspect | Biodiesel (Trans.) | Bioethanol (Ferm.) | Biogas (AD) | Producer Gas (Gasif.) | H₂ (Electrolysis) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Veg. oil, animal fat | Sugarcane, corn, biomass | Wet biomass, waste | Wood, agri-residue | Water + electricity |
| **Process type** | Chemical | Biochemical | Biochemical | Thermochemical | Electrochemical |
| **T (°C)** | 60 – 65 | 30 – 37 | 30 – 55 | 700 – 1000 | 60 – 90 |
| **P (bar)** | 1 – 4 | 1 | 1 | 1 – 30 | 1 – 30 |
| **Catalyst** | NaOH / KOH / H₂SO₄ | Yeast / enzymes | Anaerobic microbes | Air (sub-stoich.) | Electrolyte (KOH) |
| **Main product** | FAME (C₁₇–C₁₉ esters) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | CH₄ + CO₂ | CO + H₂ + N₂ | H₂ (+ O₂) |
| **By-product** | Glycerol | CO₂ | Digestate | Tar, ash | O₂, heat |
| **Theor. yield** | ~100 % (mass) | 51.11 % (mass) | 0.5 m³ CH₄/kg VS* | 2 – 3 Nm³/kg biomass | 0.089 kg H₂/kg H₂O |
| **CV (MJ/kg)** | 37 – 40 | 26 – 29 | 50 – 55 (CH₄) | 4.5 – 5.5 (per Nm³) | 120 (LHV) |

*\*VS = Volatile Solids*

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Biogas Energy Potential

**Problem:** A community digester processes **500 kg/day** of cattle dung with a **biogas yield of 0.04 m³/kg** and **methane content of 60 %**. Calculate daily methane volume, daily energy, and monthly electricity (assume 35 % generator efficiency).

**Step 1 — Total biogas produced per day.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{biogas}} &= 500\ \text{kg/day} \times 0.04\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \\
&= 20\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Methane volume per day.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{CH}_4} &= 0.60 \times 20 \\
&= 12\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of methane (ρ_CH₄ = 0.717 kg/m³ at STP).**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{CH}_4} &= 12 \times 0.717 \\
&\approx 8.604\ \text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Daily energy content (CV of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg).**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{day}} &= 8.604 \times 55.5 \\
&\approx 477.5\ \text{MJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Daily electricity (η = 0.35).**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{elec, day}} &= 0.35 \times 477.5 \\
&\approx 167.1\ \text{MJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ).**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{elec, day}} &= \frac{167.1}{3.6} \\
&\approx 46.4\ \text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Monthly (30 days).**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{elec, month}} &\approx 46.4 \times 30 \\
&\approx 1392\ \text{kWh/month}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The plant delivers **~1392 kWh/month**, sufficient to power ~4 average Indian households (≈ 300 kWh/month each).

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — H₂ via Electrolysis

**Problem:** An alkaline electrolyser runs at **I = 500 A** for **t = 8 hours**. Determine mass of H₂ produced (n = 2 electrons per H₂).

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \times t = 500 \times (8 \times 3600) = 1.44 \times 10^7\ \text{C} \\
m_{H_2} &= \frac{Q \times M_{H_2}}{n \times F} \\
&= \frac{1.44 \times 10^7 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96485} \\
&\approx \frac{2.903 \times 10^7}{1.9297 \times 10^5} \\
&\approx 150.4\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Approximately **150 g H₂** — equivalent to **~5.04 kWh** of chemical energy (LHV = 120 MJ/kg).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Biodiesel Production (Transesterification) Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Vegetable Oil] --> B[Pre-treatment Filter]
    B --> C[FFA Test Titration]
    C --> D{Catalyst Route}
    D -- FFA less 2 percent --> E[Alkali Catalyst NaOH 1 percent]
    D -- FFA more 2 percent --> F[Acid Pre-treatment H2SO4 2 percent]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Reactor Stirred Tank 60 C]
    G --> H[Glycerol Separation Settling]
    H --> I[Biodiesel Layer]
    H --> J[Glycerol By-product]
    I --> K[Water Wash]
    K --> L[Drying 110 C]
    L --> M[Final Biodiesel Storage]
    J --> N[Glycerol Purification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Raw oil is pre-treated, tested for FFA, fed to the reactor (with either one-step alkali or two-step acid–base catalysis), then separated by gravity. The ester layer is washed, dried, and stored; glycerol is a valuable by-product sold to cosmetics / pharma industries.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Bioethanol Production (Sugar Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sugarcane / Sugar Feedstock] --> B[Cleaning and Milling]
    B --> C[Juice Extraction]
    C --> D[Juice Sterilization 80 C]
    D --> E[Fermenter Yeast 30 C 48 h]
    E --> F[Beer Column Distillation]
    F --> G[Rectifying Column 95 percent EtOH]
    G --> H[Molecular Sieve Dehydration]
    H --> I[Anhydrous Ethanol 99.5 percent]
    I --> J[Storage and Blending]
    E --> K[CO2 Capture]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Anaerobic Digestion (Biogas) Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Biomass / Waste] --> B[Size Reduction Shredder]
    B --> C[Mixing and Slurry 8 percent TS]
    C --> D[Hydrolysis Tank 35 C]
    D --> E[Acidogenesis 35 C]
    E --> F[Acetogenesis 35 C]
    F --> G[Methanogenesis 35 C]
    G --> H[Biogas Holder 60 percent CH4]
    G --> I[Digestate Output Fertilizer]
    H --> J[Gas Scrubber H2S Removal]
    J --> K[CHP Engine Generator]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Biomass Gasification (Producer Gas)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feed Drying] --> B[Screw Feeder]
    B --> C[Reactor 4 Zones]
    C --> C1[Zone 1 Drying 100 to 200 C]
    C --> C2[Zone 2 Pyrolysis 200 to 600 C]
    C --> C3[Zone 3 Combustion 600 to 1000 C]
    C --> C4[Zone 4 Reduction 800 to 1000 C]
    C3 --> D[Air Supply ER 0.25]
    C4 --> E[Producer Gas 5 MJ per Nm3]
    E --> F[Cyclone Particulate Removal]

```

```mermaid
    F --> G[Reformer Ni Cat 800 C]
    G --> H[CO plus 3 H2]
    H --> I[Water Gas Shift Reactor]

```
