# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define *transesterification* and write the overall reaction for biodiesel production.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which an **ester** (triglyceride) reacts with a **short-chain alcohol** (methanol/ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst (alkali or acid) to produce **biodiesel (FAME)** and **glycerol** as a co-product.

$$\text{TG} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{NaOH}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

The reaction is **reversible, three-step, and equilibrium-controlled**, requiring excess alcohol and a catalyst to drive the reaction toward completion.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** What is producer gas? List its typical composition and calorific value.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Producer gas** is a **low-BTU fuel gas** obtained by the **partial combustion / gasification** of solid biomass (wood, charcoal, agri-residue) in a reactor with **sub-stoichiometric air** (Equivalence Ratio = 0.2 – 0.4).

**Typical composition:**
- CO: 20 – 25 %
- H₂: 10 – 15 %
- CH₄: 2 – 5 %
- CO₂: 8 – 12 %
- N₂: 45 – 55 % (diluent from air)

**Calorific value:** **4.5 – 5.5 MJ/Nm³**

It is widely used in **dual-fuel engines**, **gas turbines**, and **industrial furnaces**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module 2 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat flow diagram the **production of biodiesel by transesterification**. Discuss the role of **catalyst, temperature, molar ratio, and FFA content**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **theoretical yield of biodiesel** from **1000 kg of soybean oil** (assumed pure triolein, M = 884.4 g/mol). Also compute the **practical yield** assuming **85 % efficiency**. State the **mass of glycerol** produced. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Transesterification is the catalytic reaction of a triglyceride (vegetable oil/animal fat) with a short-chain alcohol (methanol) to produce **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME) — biodiesel** and **glycerol**.

**Step 2 — Flow Diagram (2 marks):**

Raw Oil → Pre-treatment (degumming, dewaxing) → FFA titration → Reactor (60 °C, 1 hr, NaOH catalyst, 6:1 MeOH:oil) → Glycerol settling (4–8 hr) → Biodiesel washing → Drying (110 °C) → Storage.

**Step 3 — Process Parameters (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Optimum | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 60 – 65 °C | Above bp of methanol → loss |
| Molar ratio (MeOH:oil) | 6 : 1 | Drives equilibrium forward |
| Catalyst (NaOH) | 1 % wt | Below 0.5 % → slow; above 1.5 % → soap |
| FFA content | < 2 % | Above → saponification; use two-step acid–base |

**Step 4 — Advantages / Limitations (2 marks):**
Renewable, biodegradable, low emissions, but **raw-oil cost is ~70 %** of biodiesel price; **glycerol glut** on the global market is both a by-product revenue source and a price depressor.

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Moles of triglyceride (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{TG}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000\ \text{g}}{884.4\ \text{g/mol}} \approx 1130.66\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of FAME produced (3:1 stoichiometry) (1 mark):**

$$n_{\text{FAME}} = 3 \times 1130.66 = 3391.98\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical mass of FAME (methyl oleate, M = 296.5 g/mol) (1 mark):**

$$m_{\text{FAME, th}} = 3391.98 \times 296.5 \approx 1{,}005{,}722\ \text{g} \approx 1005.7\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Practical yield @ 85 % (1 mark):**

$$m_{\text{FAME, prac}} = 0.85 \times 1005.7 \approx 854.85\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of glycerol (M = 92 g/mol, 1 mol per TG) (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{glycerol}} &= 1130.66\ \text{mol} \\
m_{\text{glycerol, th}} &= 1130.66 \times 92 \approx 104{,}021\ \text{g} \approx 104.02\ \text{kg} \\
m_{\text{glycerol, prac}} &\approx 0.85 \times 104.02 \approx 88.42\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Theoretical FAME = **1005.7 kg**, Practical = **854.85 kg**, Glycerol (practical) = **88.42 kg**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Option 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module 2 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Describe the **anaerobic digestion process** for biogas production. State the **four biological stages**, the **typical composition of biogas**, and the **optimum process conditions** (pH, temperature, HRT). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A community biogas plant processes **800 kg/day of kitchen waste** with a methane yield of **0.25 m³ CH₄/kg VS** and **VS = 0.85**. Compute the **daily methane volume, energy in MJ, and equivalent kWh of electricity** at 30 % generation efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Anaerobic digestion (AD) is the **biochemical decomposition of organic matter by microorganisms in the absence of oxygen** to produce **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** and **digestate** (a nutrient-rich slurry).

**Step 2 — Four Biological Stages (3 marks):**

| Stage | Input | Output | Microbe Group |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrolysis** | Polymers (proteins, fats, carbs) | Monomers (amino acids, sugars, fatty acids) | Hydrolytic bacteria |
| **Acidogenesis** | Monomers | VFAs, H₂, CO₂ | Acidogenic bacteria |
| **Acetogenesis** | VFAs, alcohols | Acetic acid, H₂, CO₂ | Acetogenic bacteria |
| **Methanogenesis** | Acetic acid, H₂, CO₂ | CH₄, CO₂ | *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus* |

**Step 3 — Optimum Conditions (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Mesophilic | Thermophilic |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 30 – 40 °C | 50 – 55 °C |
| pH | 6.8 – 7.4 | 6.8 – 7.4 |
| HRT | 15 – 30 days | 12 – 20 days |
| C : N ratio | **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** | Same |
| TS (Total Solids) | 8 – 12 % | 8 – 12 % |

**Step 4 — Biogas Composition (1 mark):** CH₄: **50 – 70 %**, CO₂: **30 – 40 %**, H₂S: 0.1 – 0.5 %, trace N₂ + H₂O.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — VS in feed (1 mark):**

$$m_{\text{VS}} = 800 \times 0.85 = 680\ \text{kg VS/day}$$

**Step 2 — Daily methane volume (2 marks):**

$$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 680 \times 0.25 = 170\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to mass (ρ_CH₄ = 0.717 kg/m³) (1 mark):**

$$m_{\text{CH}_4} = 170 \times 0.717 \approx 121.89\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Energy per day (CV = 55.5 MJ/kg) (1 mark):**

$$E_{\text{day}} = 121.89 \times 55.5 \approx 6765\ \text{MJ/day}$$

**Step 5 — Electricity (η = 0.30) (1 mark):**

$$E_{\text{elec, day}} = 0.30 \times 6765 \approx 2029.5\ \text{MJ/day}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ) (1 mark):**

$$E_{\text{elec, day}} = \frac{2029.5}{3.6} \approx 563.75\ \text{kWh/day}$$

**Final Answer:** **170 m³ CH₄/day**, **6765 MJ/day**, **~563.75 kWh/day**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 3:1 stoichiometry** — always state *3 mol alcohol per mol triglyceride*.
> 2. **Not converting % to mass** — when using 1 % catalyst, multiply by oil mass, not alcohol mass.
> 3. **Mixing up theoretical vs. practical yield** — examiners expect both.
> 4. **Using 22.4 L/mol at STP for biogas** — methane density **0.717 kg/m³** is more direct at NTP.
> 5. **Forgetting the 6:1 actual ratio** (vs. 3:1 stoichiometric) for biodiesel — examiners mark it as a *conceptual lapse*.
> 6. **Conflating CO and CO₂** in producer-gas composition — N₂ dominates only because of *air dilution*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — "Production" : High-Density Recap**
> - **Biodiesel** = Transesterification → FAME + glycerol → Catalyst NaOH 1 %, MeOH:oil = 6:1, 60 °C, < 2 % FFA.
> - **Two-step acid–base** when FFA > 2 % (saponification guard).
> - **Bioethanol** = Fermentation (yeast) → C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ → **51.1 % theoretical yield by mass**; practical ~46 %.
> - **Distillation + molecular sieves** needed for **anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %)**.
> - **Biogas** = Anaerobic digestion → **4 stages** (hydrolysis, acidogenesis, acetogenesis, methanogenesis) → CH₄:CO₂ ≈ **60:40** → CV ≈ 55.5 MJ/kg.
> - **Producer gas** = Gasification of biomass at **ER 0.2 – 0.4** → CV = **4.5 – 5.5 MJ/Nm³** → N₂-diluted, ideal for dual-fuel engines.
> - **Hydrogen** routes: **Electrolysis** (green), **SMR** (CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3 H₂), **water-gas shift** for extra H₂ → CV = 120 MJ/kg.
> - **Key stoichiometry:** 1 mol TG → 3 mol FAME + 1 mol glycerol; 1 mol glucose → 2 mol ethanol.
> - **Key constants to memorize:** 55.5 MJ/kg (CH₄), 0.717 kg/m³ (CH₄ density), 884.4 g/mol (triolein), 296.5 g/mol (methyl oleate), 51.1 % (EtOH theor. yield), F = 96485 C/mol.
> - **Sustainability hook:** Every production route is judged on **net energy ratio (NER)** and **life-cycle CO₂** for KTU descriptive answers.
> - **Quick yield formula:** FAME yield % = (m_FAME / m_oil) × 100; EtOH yield = (2 × 46 / 180) × glucose mass × efficiency.
> - **Common KTU mistake:** Not distinguishing **theoretical vs. practical yield** — always quantify the gap (typically 10 – 15 %).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blending Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blending Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Blending Criteria** refers to the set of standardized physical, chemical, and performance parameters (along with their permissible upper/lower limits) that a fuel blend must satisfy to be considered acceptable for use in existing internal combustion engines, distribution infrastructure, and storage systems. These criteria are codified by national and international agencies such as **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM)**, **European Committee for Standardization (EN/CEN)**, and **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**.

In the context of KTU Module 2 (Alternative Fuels), blending criteria govern how renewable/alternative fuels such as **ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, dimethyl ether (DME), and hydrogen** are mixed with conventional fossil fuels (gasoline/diesel) to obtain a hybrid fuel with acceptable performance, durability, and emissions characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEMUT411):**
> Blending criteria are *the* gateway to commercial deployment of alternative fuels. A new fuel is rarely used in its pure form (B100/E100); instead, it is **blended** with a base fuel. The student must understand *which property* limits the blend ratio, *why* it limits it, and *how* standards codify the limits.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Chai Masala" Principle

Imagine making a cup of tea. You mix water, milk, sugar, tea leaves, ginger, and cardamom. If you add too much ginger, the chai becomes undrinkable (limits on flavour). If you add too little sugar, it is not sweet enough (minimum limit). If the milk is too thick (viscosity), the tea doesn't pour well. If it is too watery (water content), the "body" is lost.

In the same way, when an alternative fuel is mixed with a base fuel:
- The **properties of the resulting blend** (octane, cetane, viscosity, flash point, calorific value, water content, sulfur, etc.) must lie **within a defined "taste window"** specified by the engine manufacturer and the regulatory standard.
- The **limiting property** dictates the **maximum blend ratio** permissible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** There is no "one-size-fits-all" blending limit. The maximum allowed percentage of ethanol in gasoline (E10 in India under the Ethanol Blended Petrol Programme) is decided by the *worst-case* property of the resulting blend — which, for ethanol, is its **hygroscopic nature (water affinity)** and **corrosivity**, not its octane number (which is actually beneficial).

### 1.3 Why Blending Is Done — The Three Engineering Drivers

| Engineering Driver | Technical Purpose | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance Enhancement** | Raise octane, reduce knock, improve combustion | Ethanol in gasoline (raises RON) |
| **Emissions Reduction** | Lower PM, CO, HC, CO₂ footprint | Biodiesel in diesel (lowers PM & CO) |
| **Renewable Substitution** | Displace fossil fuel, comply with biofuel mandates | E20 (20% ethanol in petrol) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Blending Triangle:** Any alternative fuel blend must simultaneously satisfy three constraints:
> 1. **Engine compatibility** (knock, cold-start, materials).
> 2. **Storage & distribution compatibility** (miscibility, water tolerance, phase separation).
> 3. **Emissions & regulatory compliance** (BIS/ASTM/EN norms).
> If even *one* constraint is violated, the blend is *rejected*, irrespective of how attractive the other two are.

### 1.4 The Governing Standards — The "Rule Book"

| Fuel / Blend | Indian Standard (BIS) | International Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Ethanol (anhydrous) for blending | **IS 15464** | ASTM D4806 |
| Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) | **IS 1460** (amended) | EN 228 (Europe) |
| Biodiesel (FAME) – neat | **IS 15607** | ASTM D6751 / EN 14214 |
| Biodiesel-Diesel blend (B5/B20) | **IS 1460 / IS 1460 (Part 1)** | ASTM D7467 / EN 590 |
| Methanol as fuel | – | ASTM D5797 / D4814 |
| Hydrogen (gaseous) | ISO 14687 | ISO 14687 / SAE J2719 |

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Remember)

- **Density of water** $\rho_w = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 1\ \text{g/cc}$ (reference for specific gravity).
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR):** Gasoline ≈ 14.7, Diesel ≈ 14.5, Ethanol ≈ 9.0, Methanol ≈ 6.43, Biodiesel (FAME) ≈ 12.5.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** Gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg, Diesel ≈ 43 MJ/kg, Ethanol ≈ 26.9 MJ/kg, Methanol ≈ 20.1 MJ/kg, Biodiesel ≈ 37.2 MJ/kg.
- **RON (Research Octane Number):** Gasoline base 84–98, Ethanol ≈ 108–110, Methanol ≈ 106–112.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchy of Blending Properties

When ethanol is added to gasoline, the resulting blend does *not* obey a simple linear rule for all properties. The student must recognize that some properties blend **linearly**, some **non-linearly**, and some are governed by **co-solvency behaviour**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear (ideal) blending** is the exception, not the rule. Most fuel properties exhibit synergistic or antagonistic effects.

### 2.2 Classification of Fuel Properties by Blending Behaviour

**A. Linearly Blendable Properties (volume-additive or mass-additive):**
- Density (mass-fraction averaged)
- Calorific value (mass-fraction averaged)
- Sulfur content
- Carbon/hydrogen content
- Aromatic content

**B. Non-Linearly Blendable Properties (synergistic/antagonistic):**
- Octane number (sensitive to hydrocarbon type)
- Cetane number
- Viscosity (often follows Refutas or log-mixing rule)
- Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) — shows non-ideal positive deviation
- Flash point
- Cloud point / Pour point (for biodiesel blends)

**C. Critically Constraining Properties (the "deal breakers"):**
- Water content (ethanol attracts water → phase separation)
- Acid value (biodiesel FAME → corrosion)
- Oxidation stability (FAME → gum formation)
- Cold flow properties (FAME → crystallization)
- Lubricity (low-sulfur diesel → wear)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the following tables, the vertical bar symbol `\vert` is used for absolute value to **preserve markdown table integrity**. Do **NOT** use the bare pipe character `\vert` inside a row.

| # | Property | Blending Equation | Basis | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density (linear) | $\rho_{blend} = \sum_i w_i \cdot \rho_i$ | Mass fraction $w_i$ | Strictly linear by mass |
| 2 | Density (volume mixing) | $\dfrac{1}{\rho_{blend}} = \sum_i \dfrac{v_i}{\rho_i}$ | Volume fraction $v_i$ | Reciprocal additivity |
| 3 | Calorific Value (LHV/HHV) | $CV_{blend} = \sum_i w_i \cdot CV_i$ | Mass fraction | Energy is mass-additive |
| 4 | Octane Number (linear approx.) | $ON_{blend} \approx \sum_i v_i \cdot ON_i$ | Volume fraction | Approximate; for oxygenates it is **non-linear** |
| 5 | Cetane Number (linear approx.) | $CN_{blend} \approx \sum_i v_i \cdot CN_i$ | Volume fraction | Reasonable for FAME-diesel |
| 6 | Viscosity (Refutas Eq.) | $L_\nu =sum_i v_i L_{\nu,i};\ \nu = e^{e^{L_\nu}} - 0.7$ | Volume fraction | Used for non-ideal mixtures |
| 7 | Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) — Ethanol blend | $RVP_{blend} \approx RVP_{base} + (\Delta RVP)_{ethanol} \cdot v_{eth}$ | Volume fraction | $\Delta RVP$ for ethanol ≈ 1.5–2.0 psi per 10% vol. |
| 8 | Flash Point (Le Chatelier approx.) | $\sum_i v_i \cdot \dfrac{1}{FP_i - C} = \dfrac{1}{FP_{blend} - C}$ | Volume fraction | $C \approx - 273.15\ ^\circ C$ when in Kelvin |
| 9 | Water Content (mass balance) | $W_{blend} = \sum_i w_i \cdot W_i$ | Mass fraction | Ethanol attracts water |
| 10 | Acid Value (mass balance) | $AV_{blend} = \sum_i w_i \cdot AV_i$ | Mass fraction | FAME acid value is a corrosion driver |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Critical Property

**1. Octane Number & Engine Knock**
- Pure iso-octane = 100, n-heptane = 0. Real gasoline is referenced between these.
- Ethanol (RON ≈ 108) raises blend octane non-linearly because it is an **oxygenate** that disrupts the auto-ignition chain.
- For E10 (10% ethanol + 90% gasoline), the effective RON of the blend often exceeds what a simple linear mixture rule predicts by **2–3 RON points** — this is the **"blending octane" or "synergistic effect"**.

**2. Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) — The Ethanol Anomaly**
- Ethanol's pure RVP is low (~17 psi), yet when blended at 5–10%, it *increases* the base gasoline's RVP by 1–2 psi. This is because ethanol forms **non-ideal azeotropes** with light hydrocarbons.
- This has a critical consequence: high RVP → evaporative emissions (HC) → increased ozone formation → winter/summer RVP caps mandated by BIS/ASTM.

**3. Viscosity & Lubricity**
- Biodiesel (FAME) has viscosity ~4–5 mm²/s at 40 °C vs. diesel at ~2–3 mm²/s. Hence blend viscosity is non-linear.
- Lubricity (HFRR wear scar) — pure ethanol/methanol have **zero lubricity**, so blend lubricity must be verified.

**4. Cold Flow Properties — Cloud Point, Pour Point, CFPP**
- Biodiesel (saturated FAME) crystallizes at 0–15 °C, blocking fuel lines.
- **B5** is the practical limit for cold climates to avoid gelling. **B100** requires heaters or flow improvers.
- Cloud Point: $CP_{blend} \approx \sum_i v_i \cdot CP_i$ (approximately linear for FAME).

**5. Water Content & Phase Separation**
- Ethanol is **hygroscopic** — it absorbs water from atmosphere and from pipeline residue.
- If water content exceeds the **solubility limit** of ethanol in gasoline (~0.5% vol. for E10), the blend **phase-separates** into an aqueous-ethanol layer (bottom) and a hydrocarbon layer (top).
- The aqueous layer causes **engine corrosion** and **fuel system damage**. This is the single most important reason why anhydrous ethanol (≤ 0.2% water) is mandated by **IS 15464**.

**6. Acid Value — The FAME Corrosion Driver**
- FAME contains **free fatty acids (FFA)** and can hydrolyze to form more FFA during storage.
- Acid value limit: typically **≤ 0.5 mg KOH/g** for neat FAME (EN 14214) and ≤ 0.25 for B5 blend stocks.
- The blend value follows mass averaging; however, the **corrosive effect on copper, bronze, and certain elastomers** is the *true* engine hardware constraint.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Blending Criteria |
|---|---|
| **Refinery Engineering** | Deciding optimum bio-ethanol pump price and pipeline integration |
| **Engine Design** | Recalibrating ECU maps for E20/B20 compatibility |
| **Logistics & Storage** | Selecting tank coatings (epoxy vs. carbon steel) for ethanol blends |
| **Policy / Regulation** | Setting national blending mandates (E20 by 2025 in India) |
| **Tribology** | Adding lubricity improvers when blending with low-sulfur diesel |

### 2.6 The "Worst Property" Decision Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Limiting Property:** The maximum permissible blend ratio is fixed by the *most restrictive* of all the blend properties. For ethanol in gasoline, water tolerance is the binding constraint. For biodiesel in diesel, cold flow and oxidation stability are the binding constraints. For methanol in gasoline, vapor pressure and corrosion are the binding constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Density Blending (Mass Basis)

**Statement:** Density of a fuel blend is the mass-weighted average of component densities.

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Let two fuels A and B be mixed. Let $m_A$ be the mass of A, $m_B$ the mass of B, and $V_{blend}$ the total volume of the blend.

$$
V_{blend} = \dfrac{m_A}{\rho_A} + \dfrac{m_B}{\rho_B}
$$

**Step 2 — Express blend density:**

$$
\rho_{blend} = \dfrac{m_A + m_B}{V_{blend}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\rho_{blend} = \dfrac{m_A + m_B}{\dfrac{m_A}{\rho_A} + \dfrac{m_B}{\rho_B}}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce mass fractions** $w_A = m_A / (m_A + m_B)$ and $w_B = m_B / (m_A + m_B)$:

$$
\rho_{blend} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_A}{\rho_A} + \dfrac{w_B}{\rho_B}}
$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to n components:**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\rho_{blend}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{w_i}{\rho_i}}
$$

**Validation:** If $w_A = 1$ and $w_B = 0$, then $\rho_{blend} = \rho_A$ ✓. Symmetric in components ✓.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calorific Value (LHV) of Blend

**Statement:** Energy content of a blend equals the mass-weighted sum of component energies.

**Step 1 — Energy balance:** Total energy of blend equals sum of component energies.

$$
E_{blend} = m_A \cdot CV_A + m_B \cdot CV_B
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by total mass to get blend calorific value:**

$$
CV_{blend} = \dfrac{m_A \cdot CV_A + m_B \cdot CV_B}{m_A + m_B}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in mass fractions:**

$$
\boxed{CV_{blend} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot CV_i}
$$

**Engineering Implication:** Adding ethanol (CV = 26.9 MJ/kg) to gasoline (CV = 44 MJ/kg) *reduces* the blend's energy per kg. To deliver the same energy, **more fuel volume** must be injected. The stoichiometric mass ratio of air also drops (ethanol carries its own oxygen), partially offsetting the energy penalty in terms of volumetric fuel economy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Octane Blending (Linear Approximation & Synergy Correction)

**Linear (ideal) blending rule:**

$$
ON_{blend, \ linear} = v_{gasoline} \cdot ON_{gasoline} + v_{ethanol} \cdot ON_{ethanol}
$$

where $v_i$ are volume fractions.

**Empirical Synergy Correction (Ethanol in Gasoline):**
For oxygenates, the *blending octane number (BON)* is defined as the slope:

$$
BON_{ethanol} = \dfrac{ON_{blend} - (1 - v_{eth}) \cdot ON_{base, \ linear}}{v_{eth}}
$$

**Step-by-step worked example (B5 → E10 problem):**

A base gasoline has RON = 88. Ethanol is added to make E10 (10% by volume). Ethanol's BON is empirically determined as **112** (pure RON is ~108, but synergistic effect raises it to 112 in blends).

**Step 1 — Apply linear blending with BON:**

$$
ON_{E10} = 0.9 \times 88 + 0.1 \times 112
$$

$$
ON_{E10} = 79.2 + 11.2 = 90.4
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:** A 2.4 RON gain over the base gasoline. This is the *octane benefit* that allows refineries to use lower-octane blend stocks while still meeting the E20 mandate of 91/95 RON (Indian specification).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Viscosity Blending (Refutas Method)

The Refutas equation is used for non-ideal viscosity blending, especially for lubricants and biodiesel-diesel mixtures.

**Step 1 — Compute the viscosity blending number (VBN) for each component:**

$$
VBN_i = 14.534 \cdot \ln[\ln(\nu_i + 0.7)] + 10.975
$$

where $\nu_i$ is kinematic viscosity in cSt (mm²/s).

**Step 2 — Volume-blend the VBN:**

$$
VBN_{blend} = \sum_i v_i \cdot VBN_i
$$

**Step 3 — Back-calculate blend viscosity:**

$$
\nu_{blend} = e^{e^{\frac{VBN_{blend} - 10.975}{14.534}}} - 0.7
$$

**Worked Example — B20 blend:**
- Diesel: $\nu_D = 2.5$ cSt → $VBN_D \approx 15.4$
- Biodiesel (FAME): $\nu_B = 4.5$ cSt → $VBN_B \approx 24.2$
- Volume fractions: $v_D = 0.8$, $v_B = 0.2$

**Compute blend VBN:**

$$
VBN_{blend} = 0.8 \times 15.4 + 0.2 \times 24.2 = 12.32 + 4.84 = 17.16
$$

**Compute blend viscosity:**

$$
\nu_{blend} = e^{e^{(17.16 - 10.975)/14.534}} - 0.7
$$

$$
\nu_{blend} = e^{e^{0.4254}} - 0.7 = e^{1.530} - 0.7 = 4.618 - 0.7 \approx 3.92\ \text{cSt}
$$

**Validation:** Lies between 2.5 cSt and 4.5 cSt, closer to diesel side, which is exactly the engineering expectation for B20 (suitable for warm climates).

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) of Ethanol-Gasoline Blend

Pure-component RVP values:
- Gasoline base: $RVP_{base} = 9.0$ psi
- Ethanol: $RVP_{eth} = 2.3$ psi (low due to hydrogen bonding)

**Empirical correlation (ASTM D4814 inspired):**

$$
RVP_{blend} = RVP_{base} + K \cdot v_{eth} \cdot (1 - v_{eth})
$$

where $K$ is an empirical constant ≈ 12–15 psi for typical hydrocarbon-ethanol systems.

**Worked Example — E10:**

$$
RVP_{E10} = 9.0 + 13 \times 0.10 \times 0.90
$$

$$
RVP_{E10} = 9.0 + 1.17 = 10.17\ \text{psi}
$$

**Engineering Implication:** RVP increased by 1.17 psi. In summer, Indian RVP cap is ~9.0 psi (ASTM Class AA summer); the blend would **exceed** the cap. Hence base gasoline is de-blended (RVP reduced) to accommodate ethanol — a refinery logistics cost.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Property Limits the Blend?"

| Alternative Fuel | Limiting Property | Effect | Typical Max Blend |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ethanol in gasoline | Water tolerance / Phase separation | Engine corrosion, fuel system damage | E10 (E20 possible in dedicated vehicles) |
| Methanol in gasoline | Vapor pressure, cold-start, material compatibility | Cold-start failure, aluminum corrosion | M15 (FFV up to M85) |
| Biodiesel (FAME) in diesel | Cold flow, oxidation stability | Filter plugging, gum formation | B5 (universal), B20 (warm climates) |
| Hydrogen in CNG | Flame speed, storage | Pre-ignition, embrittlement | HCNG 30 (research only) |
| DME in diesel | Low viscosity, low lubricity | Pump wear, injector leakage | D30 (research/pilot) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — The Blending Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Select base fuel] --> B[Identify target properties: ON, CN, CV, viscosity, flash, RVP]
    B --> C[Choose alternative fuel component]
    C --> D[Compute blend ratio candidates]
    D --> E[Calculate blend properties using linear / non-linear mixing rules]
    E --> F{Does blend satisfy BIS / ASTM / EN standard limits?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Acceptable blend ratio found]
    F -- No --> H[Identify the limiting property]
    H --> I[Reduce blend ratio of alternative fuel]
    I --> E
    G --> J[Validate engine test: power, emissions, wear]
    J --> K[Final blend specification approved]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Property Classification for Blending

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Blend Properties] --> B[Linearly Blendable]
    A --> C[Non-Linearly Blendable]
    A --> D[Binding Constraints]
    B --> B1[Density]
    B --> B2[Calorific Value]
    B --> B3[Sulfur Content]
    B --> B4[Carbon Residue]
    C --> C1[Octane Number]
    C --> C2[Cetane Number]
    C --> C3[Viscosity]
    C --> C4[RVP]
    C --> C5[Flash Point]
    D --> D1[Water Content]
    D --> D2[Acid Value]
    D --> D3[Oxidation Stability]
    D --> D4[Cold Flow CFPP]
    D --> D5[Material Compatibility]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Ethanol Blending Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Gasoline base RON 88] --> P2{Add 5% Ethanol?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[E5: RON approx 90.5, RVP approx 9.6 psi]
    P3 --> P4{Water content within 0.2%?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[E5 ACCEPTED under IS 1460]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Phase separation risk - REJECT]
    P2 -- Add 10% --> P7[E10: RON approx 91.0, RVP approx 10.2 psi]
    P7 --> P8{RVP within summer cap 9.0 psi?}
    P8 -- No --> P9[De-blend base gasoline to lower RVP, then accept]
    P8 -- Yes --> P10[E10 ACCEPTED]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Why Each Blend Has a Different Limit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Ethanol-Gasoline] --> L1[Limit: Water Tolerance]
    F2[Methanol-Gasoline] --> L2[Limit: Cold Start and Vapor Lock]
    F3[FAME-Diesel] --> L3[Limit: Cold Flow and Oxidation]
    F4[Hydrogen-CNG] --> L4[Limit: Hydrogen Embrittlement]
    F5[DME-Diesel] --> L5[Limit: Low Lubricity]
    L1 --> S1[Max E10 to E20 in modern vehicles]
    L2 --> S2[Max M15 for SI engines]
    L3 --> S3[Max B5 to B20 depending on climate]
    L4 --> S4[Max HCNG 30 percent in research fleets]
    L5 --> S5[Max D30 pilot stage only]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define "Blending Criteria" in the context of alternative fuels. List any four properties that limit the blend ratio of ethanol in gasoline.**
*(Model Question – KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)*

**Model Answer:**
**Blending Criteria** are the standardized physical, chemical, and performance parameters with prescribed upper and lower limits that a fuel blend must satisfy to be acceptable for use in engines, storage, and distribution, as codified by BIS, ASTM, EN, and ISO.

The four key properties that **limit ethanol-gasoline blend ratio** are:
1. **Water content / phase separation tolerance** (hygroscopic nature of ethanol)
2. **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** (non-linear increase upon ethanol addition, causes evaporative emissions)
3. **Material compatibility** (corrosion of aluminum, magnesium, certain elastomers and plastics)
4. **Cold-start capability** (high heat of vaporization of ethanol causes cold-start difficulty above E20)

> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award ½ mark for the definition and ½ mark × 4 for the four properties. Use a numbered list. Do not exceed four properties.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] Explain why biodiesel (FAME) blend ratio in diesel is generally limited to B5–B20 in commercial deployments.**
*(Model Question – KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)*

**Model Answer:**
The blend ratio of biodiesel (FAME) in conventional diesel is limited by the following factors:
1. **Cold flow properties:** FAME has a high cloud point (0–15 °C) and pour point. At low ambient temperatures, saturated FAME crystallizes, blocking fuel lines and filters. B5 is the practical limit for cold-climate operation without additives.
2. **Oxidation stability:** FAME is prone to oxidation due to unsaturation in the fatty acid chain, leading to gum, sediment, and acid formation during storage. ASTM D6751 / EN 14214 mandate oxidation stability > 3 h (Rancimat) for neat FAME.
3. **Material compatibility:** FAME attacks certain elastomers, plastics, and natural rubber seals used in older fuel systems.
4. **Solvent action:** FAME cleans deposits in fuel tanks, which can clog filters initially (fuel filter plugging).

> **Valuation Tip:** State 3–4 reasons with brief technical justification. The examiner awards 1 mark per well-justified reason.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**
*(a)* Discuss the major physical and chemical properties used as blending criteria for ethanol-gasoline blends. Explain how each property is determined and its permissible limits as per IS 1460 / IS 15464. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* A refinery blends 10% (by volume) anhydrous ethanol with a base gasoline. The base gasoline has RON = 86, density = 740 kg/m³, and LHV = 44 MJ/kg. Ethanol has RON = 108, density = 789 kg/m³, LHV = 26.9 MJ/kg. Calculate:
   (i) The density of the resulting blend (mass-basis averaging). **(2 Marks)**
   (ii) The LHV of the blend per kg. **(2 Marks)**
   (iii) The RON of the blend using linear blending (BON = 108 for ethanol). **(2 Marks)**
   (iv) Comment on whether the RON of the blend meets the IS 1460 specification of minimum 91 RON. **(1 Mark)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Blending Criteria for Ethanol-Gasoline:**

| Property | Test Method | Permissible Limit (IS 1460 / IS 15464) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Research Octane Number (RON)** | ASTM D2699 | ≥ 91 (regular), ≥ 95 (premium) | Anti-knock quality |
| **Motor Octane Number (MON)** | ASTM D2700 | ≥ 81 (regular), ≥ 85 (premium) | High-speed knock resistance |
| **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** | ASTM D323 | 9.0 psi (summer class) | Cold start, evaporative emissions |
| **Density at 15 °C** | ASTM D1298 | 720–775 kg/m³ (varies with class) | Energy content per litre |
| **Water Content** | ASTM D6304 | ≤ 0.2 % vol. (anhydrous ethanol) | Prevents phase separation |
| **Sulfur Content** | ASTM D5453 | ≤ 50 ppm (BS-VI), ≤ 10 ppm (BS-VI+) | Emission of SOₓ |
| **Existent Gum (washed)** | ASTM D381 | ≤ 5 mg/100 mL | Storage stability |
| **Copper Strip Corrosion** | ASTM D130 | Class 1 (3 h at 50 °C) | Material compatibility |
| **Aromatics / Olefins** | EN 14517 | ≤ 35 % (aromatics) | Emissions control |
| **Ethanol Content** | ASTM D5501 | 5–20 % vol. (as per EBP grade) | Miscibility & stability |

**Engineering Insight:** These parameters must be tested at **blending terminal** and again at **retail outlet** (after pipeline transit) since ethanol can absorb water in transit.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Density of E10 Blend (Mass Basis)**

Volume of base gasoline: 90 mL (assume 100 mL blend basis)
Volume of ethanol: 10 mL
Mass of base gasoline: $m_g = 90 \times 0.740 = 66.6$ g (assuming 1 mL ≈ 1 cm³; convert densities: 740 kg/m³ = 0.740 g/cm³)
Mass of ethanol: $m_e = 10 \times 0.789 = 7.89$ g
Total mass: $66.6 + 7.89 = 74.49$ g
Total volume: 100 mL (assumed, no volume contraction)
Density of blend: $\rho_{blend} = 74.49 / 100 = 0.7449\ \text{g/cm}^3 = 744.9\ \text{kg/m}^3$

**Note on Volume Contraction:** Mixing ethanol with gasoline causes a small **volume contraction** (~0.4–0.6% for E10), so the actual blend volume is slightly less than 100 mL. For 14-mark exam purposes, this contraction is generally neglected unless specifically mentioned.

**(ii) LHV of Blend per kg**

Mass fractions: $w_g = 66.6/74.49 = 0.8941$, $w_e = 7.89/74.49 = 0.1059$

$$
LHV_{blend} = w_g \cdot LHV_g + w_e \cdot LHV_e
$$

$$
LHV_{blend} = 0.8941 \times 44 + 0.1059 \times 26.9
$$

$$
LHV_{blend} = 39.34 + 2.85 = 42.19\ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Interpretation:** Energy penalty of ~1.81 MJ/kg compared to pure gasoline. This means ~4% more fuel (by mass) must be burned for the same energy output, partially offset by the higher thermodynamic efficiency of ethanol (due to its high octane and fast flame speed).

**(iii) RON of Blend (Linear)**

$$
RON_{E10} = 0.90 \times 86 + 0.10 \times 108
$$

$$
RON_{E10} = 77.4 + 10.8 = 88.2
$$

**Refinement (Synergy Correction):** Actual measured RON is typically 1.5–2.5 RON higher due to the *blending octane number* (BON) effect of ethanol being greater than its pure RON. For exam purposes, the linear estimate is acceptable unless the question explicitly mentions BON.

**(iv) Compliance Check**

IS 1460 mandates **RON ≥ 91** for regular grade petrol in India. Our calculated RON = 88.2 falls **short by 2.8 RON points**. Hence the blend **does NOT meet** the IS 1460 specification for regular grade.

**To meet RON ≥ 91, the refinery must either:**
- Use a higher-octane base gasoline (RON ≥ 89, using reformate/isomerate),
- Use higher ethanol content (E20 raises RON further),
- Use octane boosters (MTBE, ETBE, toluene).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse mass fraction with volume fraction** in density calculations. Always state which basis you are using.
> 2. **For RON blending, state the assumption** (linear vs. non-linear). In the absence of data, linear is acceptable. Real-world values are higher.
> 3. **Show units in the final answer** — examiners deduct ½ mark if units are missing.
> 4. **Do not forget the volume contraction** when high precision is required (mention it as a refinement).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
*(a)* Discuss the major physical and chemical properties used as blending criteria for biodiesel (FAME)-diesel blends. Compare the IS 15607 / IS 1460 limits with the corresponding EN 14214 / ASTM D6751 standards. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* A B20 blend is prepared by mixing 20% (by volume) biodiesel (FAME) with 80% (by volume) conventional diesel. The properties are:
   - FAME: $\rho = 880\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $LHV = 37.2\ \text{MJ/kg}$, $CN = 52$, $\nu = 4.5\ \text{cSt}$
   - Diesel: $\rho = 835\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $LHV = 42.5\ \text{MJ/kg}$, $CN = 50$, $\nu = 2.8\ \text{cSt}$
   
   Calculate:
   (i) The density of the B20 blend. **(2 Marks)**
   (ii) The LHV of the blend. **(2 Marks)**
   (iii) The cetane number of the blend (linear). **(1.5 Marks)**
   (iv) The kinematic viscosity of the blend using the Refutas equation. **(1.5 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Biodiesel-Diesel Blending Criteria:**

| Property | Test Method | IS 15607 / IS 1460 (B5) | EN 14214 / ASTM D6751 (Neat FAME) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cetane Number (CN)** | ASTM D613 | ≥ 51 (B5), ≥ 48 (diesel base) | ≥ 51 (EN), ≥ 47 (ASTM) | Ignition quality |
| **Kinematic Viscosity (40 °C)** | ASTM D445 | 2.0–4.5 mm²/s | 3.5–5.0 mm²/s | Atomization, lubrication |
| **Density (15 °C)** | ASTM D1298 | 820–860 kg/m³ | 860–900 kg/m³ | Energy per litre |
| **Flash Point** | ASTM D93 | ≥ 35 °C (B5) | ≥ 120 °C (FAME) | Safety in handling |
| **Acid Value** | ASTM D664 | ≤ 0.5 mg KOH/g | ≤ 0.5 mg KOH/g (EN), ≤ 0.8 (ASTM) | Corrosion |
| **Water & Sediment** | ASTM D2709 | ≤ 0.05 % vol. | ≤ 0.05 % vol. | Microbial growth |
| **Oxidation Stability** | EN 15751 / EN 14112 | ≥ 6 h (B5) | ≥ 8 h (EN) | Storage life |
| **Iodine Value** | EN 14111 | ≤ 120 g I₂/100g | ≤ 120 g I₂/100g | Oxidation tendency |
| **Methanol Content** | EN 14110 | ≤ 0.2 % wt | ≤ 0.2 % wt | Flash point, corrosion |
| **Cloud Point** | ASTM D2500 | Report (climate-specific) | Report | Cold flow |
| **Sulfur Content** | ASTM D5453 | ≤ 50 ppm (BS-VI) | ≤ 10 ppm | Emissions |

**Key Takeaway:** The Indian BIS limits are calibrated to align with EN/ASTM norms, with minor relaxations for climate and feedstock variability. The cetane number requirement is the most critical for diesel engine performance; without a cetane improver, B20 typically meets IS 1460 limits.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass of components (per 100 mL blend):**
- Volume of FAME = 20 mL, Mass of FAME = $20 \times 0.880 = 17.6$ g
- Volume of diesel = 80 mL, Mass of diesel = $80 \times 0.835 = 66.8$ g
- Total mass = 84.4 g
- Total volume = 100 mL (neglecting contraction)

**(i) Density of B20 Blend**

$$
\rho_{B20} = \dfrac{84.4}{100} = 0.844\ \text{g/cm}^3 = 844\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

This lies within the IS 1460 specification for diesel blends (820–860 kg/m³).

**(ii) LHV of B20 Blend**

Mass fractions: $w_F = 17.6/84.4 = 0.2085$, $w_D = 66.8/84.4 = 0.7915$

$$
LHV_{B20} = 0.2085 \times 37.2 + 0.7915 \times 42.5
$$

$$
LHV_{B20} = 7.76 + 33.64 = 41.40\ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Interpretation:** ~2.6% energy penalty per kg. B20 vehicles typically show a 1–2% drop in fuel economy by volume, which is partially offset by the higher energy density per litre (since B20 is denser than diesel).

**(iii) Cetane Number of B20 Blend (Linear)**

$$
CN_{B20} = 0.20 \times 52 + 0.80 \times 50
$$

$$
CN_{B20} = 10.4 + 40.0 = 50.4
$$

**Interpretation:** Exceeds IS 15607 minimum of 51? Slightly below. A 0.6-point cetane improver (like 2-EHN at 0.1% vol.) would bring it into spec.

**(iv) Viscosity of B20 Blend (Refutas Method)**

**Compute VBN for diesel:**

$$
VBN_D = 14.534 \cdot \ln[\ln(2.8 + 0.7)] + 10.975
$$

$$
VBN_D = 14.534 \cdot \ln[\ln(3.5)] + 10.975 = 14.534 \cdot \ln(1.2528) + 10.975
$$

$$
VBN_D = 14.534 \times 0.2254 + 10.975 = 3.276 + 10.975 = 14.25
$$

**Compute VBN for FAME:**

$$
VBN_F = 14.534 \cdot \ln[\ln(4.5 + 0.7)] + 10.975
$$

$$
VBN_F = 14.534 \cdot \ln[\ln(5.2)] + 10.975 = 14.534 \cdot \ln(1.6487) + 10.975
$$

$$
VBN_F = 14.534 \times 0.4996 + 10.975 = 7.261 + 10.975 = 18.24
$$

**Volume-blend VBN:**

$$
VBN_{B20} = 0.20 \times 18.24 + 0.80 \times 14.25 = 3.648 + 11.40 = 15.05
$$

**Back-calculate viscosity:**

$$
\nu_{B20} = e^{e^{(15.05 - 10.975)/14.534}} - 0.7
$$

$$
\nu_{B20} = e^{e^{0.2802}} - 0.7 = e^{1.3234} - 0.7 = 3.756 - 0.7 = 3.056\ \text{cSt}
$$

**Interpretation:** 3.06 cSt is within the IS 1460 / EN 590 diesel specification (2.0–4.5 cSt at 40 °C). The blend is acceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Refutas equation is logarithmic** — students often make sign errors or use the wrong inverse form. Show all three steps explicitly: VBN per component → volume blend → back-calculate viscosity.
> 2. **Cetane number is volume-averaged**, not mass-averaged. Using mass fractions here loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Cite the relevant standard** in your final interpretation; this demonstrates KTU-aligned awareness.
> 4. **For B20, the binding constraint is cetane number** (slightly low) — comment on this rather than viscosity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Blending Criteria** = standardized property limits that govern the use of alternative fuel blends (BIS / ASTM / EN / ISO).
- [ ] **Density of a blend** is mass-fraction averaged (reciprocal additivity). For volume-blend, use the harmonic form: $1/\rho_{blend} = \sum v_i/\rho_i$.
- [ ] **Calorific value of a blend** is mass-fraction averaged: $CV_{blend} = \sum w_i \cdot CV_i$.
- [ ] **Octane number** is volume-averaged; ethanol exhibits synergistic blending (BON > pure RON).
- [ ] **Cetane number** is volume-averaged; FAME raises CN in diesel blends.
- [ ] **Viscosity** is non-linear; use the **Refutas** equation: $VBN_i = 14.534 \ln[\ln(\nu_i + 0.7)] + 10.975$.
- [ ] **RVP of ethanol blend** shows a non-linear positive deviation (azeotropic effect). Summer RVP caps in India ≈ 9.0 psi (ASTM Class AA).
- [ ] **Water content** is the binding constraint for ethanol blends (hygroscopic; phase separation risk).
- [ ] **Cold flow properties** (cloud point, pour point, CFPP) are the binding constraint for FAME blends in cold climates.
- [ ] **Oxidation stability** of FAME is measured by Rancimat (EN 14112 / EN 15751); minimum 6 h (B5) and 8 h (neat FAME).
- [ ] **Acid value** is the FAME corrosion driver; limits are 0.5 mg KOH/g (EN 14214).
- [ ] **Material compatibility**: ethanol attacks aluminum & magnesium; FAME attacks natural rubber and certain elastomers.
- [ ] **Volume contraction** (~0.4–0.6% for E10) is a real phenomenon often overlooked in exam problems.
- [ ] **India's E20 mandate** (2025) targets 20% ethanol blending; requires vehicle calibration for material compatibility.
- [ ] **Rule of Limiting Property** — the maximum blend ratio is set by the *most restrictive* blend property, not the most desirable one.
- [ ] **Master the Formulas:** Density (mass avg), LHV (mass avg), Octane (vol avg), Cetane (vol avg), Viscosity (Refutas).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Final Tip:** For full marks in 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **(i) Listing properties with standards, (ii) Tabulating the data, (iii) Step-by-step numerical solution, (iv) Engineering interpretation**. A well-tabulated answer with clear units typically scores 10–20% higher than a paragraph-only answer.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT411 – Module 2)**
> **Blending Process** is defined as the controlled, homogeneous mixing of two or more liquid fuels (typically a base fossil fuel with an alternative fuel such as ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, or dimethyl ether) in specified volumetric or mass proportions to produce a final fuel mixture that satisfies a defined set of physico-chemical, performance, and emission specifications prescribed by national/international fuel standards (e.g., **BIS, ASTM D4806, EN 590, EN 228**).

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the blending process is the **primary bridging technology** that allows alternative fuels to be introduced into the existing petroleum distribution infrastructure without requiring fundamental modifications to engine hardware, thereby acting as the **transitional pathway** from a 100% petroleum economy to a sustainable bio-based fuel economy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are preparing a cup of **filter coffee**:
- Pure black coffee (100% diesel) is strong, harsh, and has a particular viscosity and aroma.
- You add a measured quantity of warm milk (biodiesel/ethanol) and sugar to soften it.
- The final **blended coffee** (e.g., **B20 – 20% biodiesel + 80% diesel**) retains the character of black coffee but is smoother, has altered viscosity, a different aroma, and burns differently in your body (engine).

The "barista" (refinery/dispensing system) must ensure the milk is **completely emulsified or dissolved** — otherwise, you get separation, just like **phase separation** in a poorly blended fuel tank during cold weather.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for Students**
> Blending is **NOT** a chemical reaction — it is a **physical mixing** process. No new molecular bonds are formed. Hence, the energy content of the blend is a weighted sum of the energy contents of its components (with small non-linear corrections for certain properties like density and cetane number).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in KTU board examinations to characterize a blend:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Blending Ratio (volumetric) | $\phi$ | % or fraction |
| Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** (or g/cm³) |
| Calorific Value | CV | **MJ/kg** or **MJ/L** |
| Cetane Number | CN | Dimensionless |
| Octane Number | ON / RON / MON | Dimensionless |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | **mm²/s** (cSt) |
| Flash Point | FP | **°C** |
| Cloud Point | CP | **°C** |
| Pour Point | PP | **°C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Blending? — The Engineering Rationale

Blending is performed for **four principal engineering reasons** that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Octane/Cetane Enhancement** — Ethanol (RON ~**109**) boosts gasoline; biodiesel (CN ~**50–60**) boosts low-cetane diesel.
2. **Emission Reduction** — Oxygenated blends reduce **CO, HC, and PM** emissions.
3. **Renewable Content Mandate** — Compliance with biofuel blending targets (e.g., India's **E20** petrol program).
4. **Lubricity Improvement** — Biodiesel restores lubricity lost in ultra-low-sulfur diesel (ULSD).

## 2.2 Methods of Blending (KTU High-Yield Topic)

### 2.2.1 Splash Blending
- The two fuels are poured sequentially into a container (tanker, storage tank).
- Mixing occurs due to **turbulence during pumping and transport**.
- Simplest method, used widely for **ethanol–gasoline (E10)** and **biodiesel–diesel (B20)**.
- Requires fuels to be **miscible** and density-compatible.

### 2.2.2 In-Line Blending (Proportional Blending)
- Two fuel streams are metered by **positive-displacement flow meters** and combined in a static mixer or pipe tee **at the point of dispatch**.
- Precise control over blend ratio (±0.1% accuracy).
- Used at **refinery terminals and bulk depots**.

### 2.2.3 In-Tank Blending (Recirculation Blending)
- A portion of the tank contents is continuously pumped out through a **side-stream educator/nozzle** and re-injected into the tank, creating vigorous circulation until homogeneity is achieved.
- Used for **large storage tanks** where splash blending is inadequate.

### 2.2.4 Emulsification (Special Case for Biodiesel)
- Biodiesel and diesel are **immiscible in pure form**; an **emulsifier (surfactant)** such as **Span 80, Tween 80, or mono-/diglycerides** is added.
- Produces a **microemulsion** (droplet size 1–150 nm) — water-free or water-in-oil type.
- Microemulsions are thermodynamically stable and do not phase-separate.

## 2.3 Critical Properties Affected by Blending

When two fuels A (volume fraction $x_A$) and B (volume fraction $x_B$, with $x_A + x_B = 1$) are blended, properties behave differently:

### 2.3.1 Linearly Additive (Volume/Mass-Weighted) Properties
These follow the **lever rule**:
- Density (approximately linear in mass fraction)
- Calorific Value (mass-weighted for mass-basis CV; volume-weighted for volume-basis CV)
- Sulfur content, Carbon content
- Aromatic content

### 2.3.2 Non-Linearly Additive Properties
- **Cetane Number** — Non-linear; requires a **Cetane Index** calculation.
- **Octane Number** — Highly non-linear; blending octane ≠ base octane.
- **Viscosity** — Non-linear (typically follows an Arrhenius-type blend).
- **Vapor Pressure (Reid Vapor Pressure, RVP)** — Highly non-linear; ethanol blending causes **RVP spikes** at low concentrations (E5–E10).
- **Cloud Point / Pour Point** — Non-linear; biodiesel generally **worsens** cold-flow properties.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master These Equations — Direct Board Exam Favorites**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi_{B} = \dfrac{V_{B}}{V_{A}+V_{B}} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric blend % of fuel B |
| 2 | $CV_{blend} = x_A \cdot CV_A + x_B \cdot CV_B$ | Calorific value (mass basis, linear) |
| 3 | $\rho_{blend} = \dfrac{m_A+m_B}{V_A+V_B}$ | Density of blend |
| 4 | $\nu_{blend} \approx \exp(x_A \ln \nu_A + x_B \ln \nu_B)$ | Viscosity (Arrhenius blend rule, log-linear) |
| 5 | $CN_{blend} \approx \dfrac{x_A \cdot CN_A + k_B \cdot x_B \cdot CN_B}{x_A + k_B \cdot x_B}$ | Cetane number with blending factor $k_B$ |
| 6 | $\Delta RVP_{blend} \approx k_{E} \cdot \phi_E \cdot (100 - \phi_E)$ | Ethanol-induced RVP spike |
| 7 | $FP_{blend} = \dfrac{x_A \cdot FP_A + x_B \cdot FP_B}{1}$ (approx) | Flash point (approx. linear in vol%) |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Refineries** — Use linear programming (LP) models to optimize blend recipes that meet RON, MON, RVP, sulfur, and aromatics simultaneously at minimum cost.
- **Pipelines** — Blending is performed at terminals because **ethanol and biodiesel cannot be transported through multi-product pipelines** due to contamination and water-pickup risks.
- **Engines** — Blends up to **E10 / B20** are "drop-in" fuels; higher ratios (E85, B100) require **flex-fuel** or **bi-fuel** engine hardware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Blend Composition

**Problem Statement:** A storage tank contains 800 L of neat diesel. 200 L of biodiesel (B100) is added and thoroughly mixed. Find the blend designation and the volumetric fraction of biodiesel.

**Step 1 — Identify Volumes:**
Let $V_D$ = volume of diesel = **800 L**
Let $V_B$ = volume of biodiesel = **200 L**

**Step 2 — Total Blend Volume:**
$$V_{blend} = V_D + V_B$$

Substituting the values:
$$V_{blend} = 800 + 200 = 1000 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric Fraction of Biodiesel:**
$$\phi_B = \dfrac{V_B}{V_{blend}}$$

$$\phi_B = \dfrac{200}{1000} = 0.20$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Percentage:**
$$\phi_B(\%) = 0.20 \times 100\% = 20\%$$

**Step 5 — Blend Designation:**
The blend is **B20** (20% biodiesel + 80% diesel by volume).

> [!NOTE] **Key Engineering Note:** For practical KTU problems, always state both the percentage and the conventional **B/E nomenclature** (e.g., B20 = 20% biodiesel).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calorific Value of a Blend (Mass Basis)

**Problem Statement:** Compute the calorific value of a B20 blend. Given:
- Diesel: $CV_D = 43.0$ MJ/kg, density $\rho_D = 840$ kg/m³
- Biodiesel: $CV_B = 37.5$ MJ/kg, density $\rho_B = 880$ kg/m³
- Volume fractions: $x_D = 0.80$, $x_B = 0.20$ (volumetric)

**Step 1 — Mass of Each Component (per 1 m³ of blend):**
$$m_D = x_D \cdot \rho_D = 0.80 \times 840 = 672 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_B = x_B \cdot \rho_B = 0.20 \times 880 = 176 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total Mass:**
$$m_{blend} = m_D + m_B$$

$$m_{blend} = 672 + 176 = 848 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Fractions:**
$$w_D = \dfrac{m_D}{m_{blend}} = \dfrac{672}{848} = 0.7925$$

$$w_B = \dfrac{m_B}{m_{blend}} = \dfrac{176}{848} = 0.2075$$

**Step 4 — Apply Linear Blending Rule for Calorific Value (Mass Basis):**
$$CV_{blend} = w_D \cdot CV_D + w_B \cdot CV_B$$

Substituting:
$$CV_{blend} = (0.7925)(43.0) + (0.2075)(37.5)$$

$$CV_{blend} = 34.0775 + 7.78125$$

$$CV_{blend} = 41.86 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{CV_{blend} \approx 41.86 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

**Interpretation:** The B20 blend has a calorific value ~2.65% lower than neat diesel — this is why biodiesel blends exhibit marginally **higher brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC)**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Density of the Blend (Volume-Weighted vs. Mass-Weighted)

**Step 1 — Volume-Weighted (Arithmetic Mean) Density:**
$$\rho_{blend, V} = x_D \cdot \rho_D + x_B \cdot \rho_B$$

$$\rho_{blend, V} = (0.80)(840) + (0.20)(880)$$

$$\rho_{blend, V} = 672 + 176 = 848 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — True Density (from Mass/Volume):**
$$\rho_{blend, true} = \dfrac{m_{blend}}{V_{blend}} = \dfrac{848 \text{ kg}}{1 \text{ m}^3} = 848 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Check (Mass-Weighted Reciprocal Rule):**
$$\dfrac{1}{\rho_{blend, M}} = \dfrac{w_D}{\rho_D} + \dfrac{w_B}{\rho_B}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho_{blend, M}} = \dfrac{0.7925}{840} + \dfrac{0.2075}{880}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho_{blend, M}} = 9.435 \times 10^{-4} + 2.358 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho_{blend, M}} = 1.1793 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\rho_{blend, M} = 848.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Both methods agree because mass-weighted and volume-weighted density equations are **mathematically equivalent** when applied correctly. This is a key cross-check students can use in KTU board exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Viscosity Using Arrhenius Log-Linear Rule

**Problem Statement:** Compute the kinematic viscosity of B20 at 40 °C.
- Diesel: $\nu_D = 3.50$ mm²/s at 40 °C
- Biodiesel: $\nu_B = 4.80$ mm²/s at 40 °C
- Volume fractions: $x_D = 0.80$, $x_B = 0.20$

**Step 1 — Apply the Log-Linear Arrhenius Blend Equation:**
$$\ln \nu_{blend} = x_D \cdot \ln \nu_D + x_B \cdot \ln \nu_B$$

**Step 2 — Compute Individual Logarithms:**
$$\ln(3.50) = 1.2528$$

$$\ln(4.80) = 1.5686$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Log-Blend:**
$$\ln \nu_{blend} = (0.80)(1.2528) + (0.20)(1.5686)$$

$$\ln \nu_{blend} = 1.00224 + 0.31372$$

$$\ln \nu_{blend} = 1.35596$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate:**
$$\nu_{blend} = e^{1.35596}$$

$$\boxed{\nu_{blend} \approx 3.88 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Verification — Simple Arithmetic Mean (for comparison):**
$$\nu_{blend, arith} = (0.80)(3.50) + (0.20)(4.80) = 3.76 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$$

The Arrhenius value (3.88) is slightly higher than the arithmetic mean (3.76) — confirming the **non-linear blending behavior** of viscosity.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Property Behavior Summary

| Property | Blends with Biodiesel | Blends with Ethanol | Blending Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value | **Decreases** | **Decreases sharply** | Linear (mass basis) |
| Density | Slightly increases | Decreases | Linear (approx.) |
| Viscosity | Increases | Decreases | Non-linear (log) |
| Cetane Number | **Increases** | Slight decrease | Non-linear |
| Octane Number | Decreases | **Increases sharply** | Non-linear |
| Flash Point | Increases | Decreases | Non-linear |
| Cloud Point | **Increases (worse)** | Decreases (better) | Non-linear |
| RVP | Slight increase | **Spikes at low %** | Highly non-linear |
| Lubricity | **Improves** | Marginal effect | Non-linear |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Blending Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Fossil Fuel] --> C[Storage Tank A]
    B[Alternative Fuel Ethanol Biodiesel Methanol DME] --> D[Storage Tank B]
    C --> E{Mixing Method Selection}
    D --> E
    E --> F[Splash Blending]
    E --> G[In-Line Blending with Static Mixer]
    E --> H[In-Tank Recirculation Blending]
    E --> I[Emulsification with Surfactant]
    F --> J[Quality Control Sampling]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Quality Meets Specification}
    K -->|Yes| L[Dispatch to End User]
    K -->|No| M[Re-blend or Reject Batch]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an In-Line Blending System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Diesel Storage] --> nodeB[Flow Meter A]
    nodeC[Biodiesel Storage] --> nodeD[Flow Meter B]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Static Inline Mixer]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Online Density Sensor]
    nodeF --> nodeG[PLC Controller]
    nodeG --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeH[Finished Blend Storage Tank]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Quality Lab Sampling]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Material Compatibility & Phase Stability Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Select Blend Ratio X% Alternative Fuel] --> check1{Are Fuels Miscible?}
    check1 -->|Yes| check2[Direct Blending - Splash or Inline]
    check1 -->|No| check3[Add Surfactant Emulsifier]
    check3 --> check4[High-Shear Mixer]
    check4 --> check5[Microemulsion Formation]
    check2 --> check6[Cloud Point and Pour Point Test]
    check5 --> check6
    check6 --> check7{Passes Cold Flow Spec?}
    check7 -->|Yes| dispatch[Approve for Distribution]
    check7 -->|No| add[Add Cold Flow Improver Additive]
    add --> check6
```

## 4.4 Schematic Explanation (Verbal Block Description)

In a **typical petroleum terminal blending setup**, neat petroleum fuel (e.g., diesel) is held in **Tank A**, while the alternative fuel (e.g., biodiesel) is held in **Tank B**. Each tank discharges through a **dedicated positive-displacement flow meter** that sends pulse signals to a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)**. The PLC continuously compares the actual ratio to the set-point ratio and dynamically adjusts the **control valves** on each line. The two streams converge at a **static in-line mixer** (a series of baffles or helical elements) that generates turbulent mixing in laminar flow. The blended product passes through an **online density meter** for real-time quality verification before entering the finished-product storage tank.

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Note for Students**
> In KTU board exams, when asked to "draw a blending process flow diagram," you may reproduce the simplified version using labelled rectangular blocks joined by arrows with a brief description of each stage. Examiners award marks for: (i) identification of inputs, (ii) mixing method, (iii) quality check, and (iv) feedback loop.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEMUT411]
**Define the blending process. List any two methods used for blending alternative fuels with conventional fuels.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The blending process is the controlled physical mixing of an alternative fuel (such as ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, or DME) with a conventional fossil fuel (gasoline or diesel) in defined proportions to obtain a homogeneous fuel mixture that satisfies prescribed physico-chemical and performance specifications.

**Two Methods (1 Mark):**
1. **Splash Blending** — Sequential pouring into a common container with mixing by turbulence.
2. **In-Line Blending** — Continuous metering and mixing through a static mixer at the dispensing point.

*(Acceptable alternative: In-Tank Recirculation Blending or Emulsification.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEMUT411]
**Why is biodiesel blended with diesel instead of being used in its pure form (B100) in conventional engines?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **High Viscosity of B100 (~4.5–5.5 mm²/s)** — Causes poor atomization, injector coking, and increased engine deposits. Blending with diesel (ν ≈ 3.5 mm²/s) reduces viscosity to acceptable levels (e.g., B20 ≈ 3.9 mm²/s). **(1 Mark)**
2. **Cold Flow Problems** — Pure biodiesel has a cloud point of ~10–15 °C, making it unsuitable for cold climates. Blending improves operability. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Material Compatibility & Infrastructure** — Existing engines, fuel lines, and seals are designed for diesel; B100 can degrade natural rubber seals. Blends up to B20 are "drop-in" compatible. **(1 Mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEMUT411]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **splash, in-line, and in-tank recirculation** methods of blending. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A B20 blend is prepared by mixing **800 L of diesel (density 840 kg/m³, CV = 43.0 MJ/kg)** with **200 L of biodiesel (density 880 kg/m³, CV = 37.5 MJ/kg)**. Determine:
   (i) Mass fraction of each component
   (ii) Calorific value of the blend (mass basis)
   (iii) Density of the blend **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEMUT411]**

**(a)** Discuss the **effects of blending ethanol with gasoline** on the following properties: octane number, calorific value, vapor pressure (RVP), and cold-start behavior. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An **E10 blend** is to be prepared. Calculate the calorific value per litre of the blend given:
   - Gasoline: ρ = 740 kg/m³, CV = 44.0 MJ/kg
   - Ethanol: ρ = 789 kg/m³, CV = 26.8 MJ/kg
   - Blend ratio: 90% gasoline + 10% ethanol by volume. **(7 Marks)**

---

## MODEL SOLUTIONS — QUESTION A

### (a) Blending Methods Comparison (7 Marks)

| Method | Principle | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Splash Blending** | Sequential pouring; mixing by turbulence | Simple, low capital cost, suitable for miscible fuels | Inhomogeneous for viscous fuels; requires transport time |
| **In-Line Blending** | Continuous metering through flow meters + static mixer | High accuracy (±0.1%), continuous operation, real-time control | Higher capital cost; requires instrumentation |
| **In-Tank Recirculation** | Side-stream pump with educator nozzle | Excellent homogeneity for large tanks; suitable for immiscible systems | High energy consumption; long mixing time |

**Key Points to Mention (for full 7 marks):**
1. Definition of each method (3 × 1 = 3 Marks)
2. Diagram/sketch description (1 Mark)
3. Comparative table (2 Marks)
4. Application context (1 Mark)

---

### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V_D = 800$ L, $\rho_D = 840$ kg/m³, $CV_D = 43.0$ MJ/kg
- $V_B = 200$ L, $\rho_B = 880$ kg/m³, $CV_B = 37.5$ MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Mass of each component (1 Mark):**
$$m_D = V_D \cdot \rho_D = 800 \times 10^{-3} \times 840 = 672 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_B = V_B \cdot \rho_B = 200 \times 10^{-3} \times 880 = 176 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total mass and mass fractions (1 Mark):**
$$m_{blend} = 672 + 176 = 848 \text{ kg}$$

$$w_D = \dfrac{672}{848} = 0.7925, \quad w_B = \dfrac{176}{848} = 0.2075$$

**Step 3 — Calorific value of blend (2 Marks):**
$$CV_{blend} = w_D \cdot CV_D + w_B \cdot CV_B$$
$$CV_{blend} = (0.7925)(43.0) + (0.2075)(37.5)$$
$$CV_{blend} = 34.0775 + 7.7813 = 41.86 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Density of blend (1 Mark):**
$$\rho_{blend} = \dfrac{m_{blend}}{V_{blend}} = \dfrac{848}{1.0} = 848 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Calorific value per litre (2 Marks):**
$$CV_{blend, vol} = CV_{blend} \times \rho_{blend} = 41.86 \times 848 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$CV_{blend, vol} = 35.50 \text{ MJ/L}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Mass fractions: $w_D = 0.7925$, $w_B = 0.2075$
- Calorific value (mass basis): **41.86 MJ/kg**
- Density: **848 kg/m³**
- Calorific value (volume basis): **35.50 MJ/L**

---

## MODEL SOLUTIONS — QUESTION B

### (a) Effects of Ethanol–Gasoline Blending (7 Marks)

| Property | Effect of Ethanol Addition | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Octane Number (RON)** | **Increases significantly** | Ethanol has RON ~109; blending octane is non-linear and boosts anti-knock rating |
| **Calorific Value (MJ/kg or MJ/L)** | **Decreases** | Ethanol CV ~26.8 MJ/kg vs. gasoline ~44 MJ/kg; volumetric CV decreases ~3–5% for E10 |
| **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP)** | **Non-linear spike** | Maximum RVP spike at E5–E10 (~5–8 kPa increase); requires front-end volatility adjustment |
| **Cold-Start Behavior** | **Worsens at low temperatures** | Ethanol's low heat of vaporization (≈841 kJ/kg vs. gasoline ~305 kJ/kg) requires additional heat for vaporization |

**Distribution of Marks:**
- 4 properties × 1 Mark each for description (4 Marks)
- 2 Marks for a labelled diagram showing the non-linear RVP curve
- 1 Mark for application example (e.g., India's E20 policy)

---

### (b) Numerical Solution for E10 (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Gasoline: $\rho_G = 740$ kg/m³, $CV_G = 44.0$ MJ/kg
- Ethanol: $\rho_E = 789$ kg/m³, $CV_E = 26.8$ MJ/kg
- Volume fractions: $x_G = 0.90$, $x_E = 0.10$

**Step 1 — Mass per cubic metre of blend (2 Marks):**
$$m_G = x_G \cdot \rho_G = 0.90 \times 740 = 666.0 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_E = x_E \cdot \rho_E = 0.10 \times 789 = 78.9 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{blend} = 666.0 + 78.9 = 744.9 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Mass fractions (1 Mark):**
$$w_G = \dfrac{666.0}{744.9} = 0.8941$$

$$w_E = \dfrac{78.9}{744.9} = 0.1059$$

**Step 3 — Calorific value on mass basis (2 Marks):**
$$CV_{blend} = (0.8941)(44.0) + (0.1059)(26.8)$$

$$CV_{blend} = 39.340 + 2.838 = 42.18 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric basis (2 Marks):**
$$CV_{blend, vol} = CV_{blend} \times \rho_{blend} = 42.18 \times 744.9 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{CV_{blend, vol} \approx 31.42 \text{ MJ/L}}$$

**Comparison:** Pure gasoline volumetric CV = $44.0 \times 0.740 = 32.56$ MJ/L. The E10 blend shows a **~3.5% reduction** in volumetric calorific value — this is the classic energy-density penalty of ethanol blending.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing mass basis vs. volume basis CV:** When a problem gives density, students frequently forget to convert. Density is the bridge between the two bases — always show both calculations.
> 2. **Using simple arithmetic mean for viscosity:** Viscosity blends non-linearly. Using the arithmetic mean costs 1–2 marks if the question specifically asks for the "blended" viscosity.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1 m³ reference volume:** In density/calorific problems, define $V_{blend} = 1$ m³ explicitly to avoid unit confusion.
> 4. **Treating RVP as linear:** It is **not** linear with ethanol % — the curve peaks at E5–E10. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Ignoring units:** Always write units in the final answer. A numerical answer without units gets partial credit only.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Blending Process**

- **Definition:** Blending is a *physical* mixing process, not a chemical reaction.
- **Primary Methods:** Splash, In-Line, In-Tank Recirculation, Emulsification.
- **Most-Tested Blend Nomenclatures:** B20 (biodiesel), E10/E20/E85 (ethanol), M5/M15 (methanol), DME blends.
- **Linearly Blended Properties:** Density, calorific value (mass basis), sulfur content.
- **Non-Linearly Blended Properties:** Octane number, cetane number, viscosity (use log-Arrhenius), RVP, cloud/pour point.
- **Ethanol Effect on RVP:** Non-linear spike; maximum around E5–E10.
- **Biodiesel Effect on Cold Flow:** Worsens — requires additives or blending with kerosene/diesel.
- **Density Bridge Formula:** $CV_{vol} = CV_{mass} \times \rho$.
- **Engineering Reason for Blending:** Enables "drop-in" use of alternative fuels without engine modification.
- **Mass vs. Volume Basis:** Always check the basis specified in the question; convert using density.
- **Standard References:** ASTM D4806 (ethanol), ASTM D6751 (biodiesel), EN 228 (gasoline), EN 590 (diesel), IS 1460 (BIS diesel).
- **India-Specific Context:** Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP) target E20 by 2025; SATAT scheme for biodiesel from used cooking oil.
- **Examiner Pattern:** Always expect one 7-mark part on "methods comparison" and one 7-mark part on a numerical blend property calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Blending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Advantages of Blending # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Module 2):** > *Fuel blending* is the **engineered process of homogeneous mixing of two or more fuels (a base fuel and one or more secondary/alternative fuels) in specific volumetric, mass, or molar proportions** to obtain a composite fuel that combines the desirable physical, chemical, and combustion properties of the constituent fuels while suppressing their individual disadvantages. The resultant blend is intended for direct use in **Spark Ignition (SI), Compression Ignition (CI), or modified engines** without requiring major hardware changes. In the KTU context, blending is examined primarily as a **drop-in strategy** to integrate **alcohols (ethanol, methanol), biodiesel (FAME), gaseous fuels (CNG, LPG, H₂), and waste-derived fuels** into conventional petroleum-based infrastructure. ### 1.1.1 Key Nomenclature | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |:---:|:---|:---:| | $x_i$ | Volume / mass / mole fraction of component *i* | — | | $P_b$ | Property of the blend | varies | | $P_i$ | Property of pure component *i* | varies | | $\phi$ | Equivalence ratio (air–fuel stoichiometry) | dimensionless | | $\eta_{th}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | % | | RON | Research Octane Number | dimensionless | | MON | Motor Octane Number | dimensionless | | CN | Cetane Number | dimensionless | | LHV | Lower Heating Value | **kJ/kg** or **MJ/kg** | | $\rho_b$ | Density of blend | **kg/m³** | | $\mu_b$ | Dynamic viscosity of blend | **cSt** or **mm²/s** | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cocktail Analogy" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** > Imagine a **smoothie maker**. You don't want a drink that is purely banana (too thick, too sweet) or purely spinach (bitter, thin). You *blend* the two. The banana covers the bitterness of the spinach, and the spinach cuts the cloying sweetness of the banana. **The whole is greater than the sum of its parts.** > A **fuel blend** works exactly the same way. Petrol is great for energy density but has poor octane rating and high emissions. Ethanol has a high octane rating and burns cleanly, but it has very low energy density and is corrosive. Blending them (e.g., **E10, E20, E85**) gives a fuel that retains most of petrol's energy while gaining ethanol's clean-burning and anti-knock benefits. The blend's properties are **engineered**, not accidental. --- ## 1.3 Why Blending? The Strategic Pillars > [!NOTE] > **Four Strategic Pillars of Fuel Blending (per KTU Module 2 syllabus):** > 1. **Environmental Compliance** — Reduce tailpipe emissions (CO, HC, NOₓ, PM, CO₂). > 2. **Performance Augmentation** — Improve octane/cetane rating, reduce knock, improve cold-start. > 3. **Renewable Energy Integration** — Use bio-derived / waste-derived fuels at scale. > 4. **Energy Security & Economics** — Reduce dependence on imported crude oil; utilize locally produced agricultural/waste feedstocks. --- ## 1.4 Standard Blending Categories Referenced in KTU 2024 | Blend Family | Common Designation | Base Fuel | Secondary Fuel | Typical Use | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---| | **Gasohol** | E10, E20, E85 | Petrol (Gasoline) | **Ethanol** | SI engines | | **Methanol Blend** | M5, M15, M85, M100 | Petrol / Diesel | **Methanol** | SI / CI (with ignition improver) | | **Biodiesel Blend** | B2, B5, B20, B100 | Diesel | **FAME** (Methyl Esters) | CI engines | | **Gaseous Blends** | CBG–CNG blends | Diesel / Petrol | **CNG, LPG, H₂** | Dual-fuel / bi-fuel engines | | **Hydrogen Blends** | HCNG (H₂ + CNG) | CNG | **Hydrogen** | SI / dual-fuel | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — The "Why" Behind Each Property > [!IMPORTANT] > The key engineering insight is that **blend properties are not simple arithmetic averages**. Different fuel properties blend in fundamentally different ways. ### 2.1.1 Linear / Ideal Blending (Volume-Weighted Rule of Mixtures) Some physical properties blend **linearly** with respect to volume fraction. These are governed by the classical **Rule of Mixtures**: $$P_b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot P_i$$ where $x_i$ is the **volume fraction** of component $i$ and $P_i$ is the property of the pure component. **Properties that typically follow linear blending:** - **Density** ($\rho$) - **Flash Point** (approximate) - **Sulfur Content** (mass-weighted) - **Aromatic Content** (volume-weighted) - **Lower Heating Value** (LHV, on a mass basis in the ideal case) ### 2.1.2 Non-Linear / Synergistic Blending Some properties exhibit **non-linear blending behavior** because of molecular-level interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding in alcohol-hydrocarbon mixtures). **Properties that typically blend non-linearly:** - **Octane Number (RON, MON)** - **Cetane Number (CN)** - **Vapor Pressure (Reid Vapour Pressure)** - **Viscosity** - **Surface Tension** - **Water Solubility / Miscibility Limits** > [!NOTE] > **Why Octane Blends Non-Linearly:** > The **anti-knock behavior of an alcohol-hydrocarbon mixture** depends on the interaction between the alcohol's hydroxyl (–OH) group and the hydrocarbon chain during low-temperature pre-flame reactions. This interaction is *chemical*, not just volumetric. Hence, a small fraction of ethanol raises RON **disproportionately** more than what a simple volume-weighted average would predict. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for ESE/Internal Exams:** | # | Formula | Description | Units | Blending Type | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $P_b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, P_i$ | Linear Rule of Mixtures (volume-weighted) | property units | **Linear** | | 2 | $LHV_b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, LHV_i$ | Mass-weighted heating value | **kJ/kg** | **Linear (mass)** | | 3 | $LHV_{vol,b} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \, LHV_{vol,i}$ | Volume-weighted heating value | **MJ/L** | **Linear (vol)** | | 4 | $\rho_b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \, \rho_i$ | Blend density | **kg/m³** | **Linear** | | 5 | $\mu_b = \exp\!\left(\sum v_i \ln \mu_i\right)$ | Arrhenius viscosity blending | **cSt** | **Non-linear** | | 6 | $RON_b \approx f(v_i, RON_i, \text{interaction})$ | Octane (non-linear) | dimensionless | **Non-linear** | | 7 | $AFR_{st} = \sum v_i \, AFR_{st,i}$ | Stoichiometric AFR | dimensionless | **Linear (vol)** | | 8 | $\eta_{th,b} = \frac{W_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_b}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | % | **Performance metric** | | 9 | $BSFC_b = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{W_b}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | **kg/kWh** | **Performance metric** | | 10 | $E_{den,b} = \rho_b \cdot LHV_b$ | Volumetric energy density | **MJ/L** | **Derived** | | 11 | $\phi_b = \frac{(A/F)_{st}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | Equivalence ratio of blend | dimensionless | **Derived** | | 12 | $\dot{V}_b = \dot{m}_f \cdot \left(\sum \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}\right)$ | Volumetric flow of blend | **m³/s** | **Linear (mass)** | > **Critical Reminder (Markdown Integrity):** In all equations above, absolute value separators (if needed) must use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Blending > [!NOTE] > **Industry Application Map:** | Industry Sector | Typical Blend | Engineering Benefit | |:---|:---|:---| | **Road Transport (SI)** | E5–E10 (India), E85 (Brazil, USA) | Octane boosting, oxygenation, CO reduction | | **Road Transport (CI)** | B5–B20 (India: B100 pure in some states) | Renewable content, lubricity, PM reduction | | **Aviation** | SAF (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) blends | Lifecycle CO₂ reduction up to **80%** | | **Marine** | LNG + MDO dual-fuel, methanol blends | SOₓ, NOₓ reduction (IMO 2020 cap) | | **Power Generation** | CNG–H₂ blends (HCNG 5–30%) | Hydrogen economy transition pathway | | **Agriculture** | Straight vegetable oil (SVO) blends | Rural energy self-sufficiency | --- ## 2.4 The Blending Index Concept (Advanced — Frequently Asked in KTU) > [!TIP] > For non-linear properties, engineers use a **Blending Index (BI)** to linearize the calculation. The most common is the **Ethanol Blending Index** approach used in refineries: $$BI_i = RON_i \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\pi_i}{100}\right)$$ where $\pi_i$ is the **sensitivity** = (RON – MON) of component $i$. The blend index is then linearly volume-weighted: $$BI_{blend} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot BI_i$$ and the effective octane of the blend is recovered by inverting the relation (iteratively, since $\pi_b$ depends on the blend itself). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The derivations below are **frequently asked 14-mark derivations in KTU ESE** under Module 2. The step-by-step breakdown models the valuation key. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Density of a Two-Component Blend **Problem Statement:** A fuel engineer prepares a blend of **20% (v/v) ethanol** in petrol. Given: - Ethanol: $\rho_E = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $LHV_E = 26.9 \text{ MJ/kg}$ - Petrol: $\rho_P = 745 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $LHV_P = 43.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$ Compute the **(a) mass-weighted LHV, (b) blend density, (c) volumetric energy density of the blend, and (d) % loss in energy per litre** versus neat petrol. ### Step 1 — Define Volume Fractions $$v_E = 0.20, \qquad v_P = 1 -
