0.20 = 0.80$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Blend Density (Volume-Weighted) $$\rho_b = v_E \cdot \rho_E + v_P \cdot \rho_P$$ $$\rho_b = (0.20)(789) + (0.80)(745)$$ $$\rho_b = 157.8 + 596.0$$ $$\boxed{\rho_b = 753.8 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ > **Logic:** Density blends linearly with volume fraction because the volume contributions are additive (no volume change on mixing for dilute blends — the **1% rule of volume contraction** for E10 is neglected here as a textbook approximation). ### Step 3 — Compute the Mass Fractions (Required for Mass-Weighted LHV) $$m_E = v_E \cdot \rho_E = (0.20)(789) = 157.8 \text{ kg (per m}^3 \text{ of blend)}$$ $$m_P = v_P \cdot \rho_P = (0.80)(745) = 596.0 \text{ kg (per m}^3 \text{ of blend)}$$ $$m_b = m_E + m_P = 753.8 \text{ kg (per m}^3\text{)}$$ $$w_E = \frac{m_E}{m_b} = \frac{157.8}{753.8} = 0.2093$$ $$w_P = \frac{m_P}{m_b} = \frac{596.0}{753.8} = 0.7907$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Mass-Weighted LHV $$LHV_b = w_E \cdot LHV_E + w_P \cdot LHV_P$$ $$LHV_b = (0.2093)(26.9) + (0.7907)(43.5)$$ $$LHV_b = 5.630 + 34.395$$ $$\boxed{LHV_b \approx 40.03 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Volumetric Energy Density (MJ per Litre) $$E_{vol,b} = \rho_b \cdot LHV_b / 1000$$ $$E_{vol,b} = (753.8 \text{ kg/m}^3)(40.03 \text{ MJ/kg}) / 1000 \text{ (m}^3/\text{L)}$$ $$\boxed{E_{vol,b} \approx 30.17 \text{ MJ/L}}$$ For neat petrol: $$E_{vol,P} = (745)(43.5)/1000 = 32.41 \text{ MJ/L}$$ ### Step 6 — Percentage Loss in Energy per Litre $$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{E_{vol,P} - E_{vol,b}}{E_{vol,P}} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{32.41 - 30.17}{32.41} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\% \text{ Loss} \approx 6.91\%}$$ > **Physical Interpretation:** For every litre of E20, the engine releases **~6.9% less energy** than from a litre of neat petrol. This is the *fundamental thermodynamic penalty* of ethanol blending and is the principal reason why E85 vehicles need **~30% larger fuel tanks** to maintain the same driving range. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of a Biodiesel Blend **Problem Statement:** A B20 blend (20% v/v biodiesel in diesel) is to be combusted. Given: - Biodiesel (FAME): $(A/F)_{st,B} = 12.5$ - Diesel: $(A/F)_{st,D} = 14.6$ Compute the stoichiometric AFR of the blend. ### Step 1 — Apply Volume-Weighted Linear Blending $$(A/F)_{st,blend} = v_B \cdot (A/F)_{st,B} + v_D \cdot (A/F)_{st,D}$$ $$(A/F)_{st,blend} = (0.20)(12.5) + (0.80)(14.6)$$ $$(A/F)_{st,blend} = 2.50 + 11.68$$ $$\boxed{(A/F)_{st,blend} = 14.18}$$ ### Step 2 — Validate (Sanity Check) Since biodiesel carries **bound oxygen** (in its –O–C(=O)–O– ester linkages), it requires **less external air** for complete combustion. Hence, the blend's AFR (14.18) lies *between* the two values and is **lower** than neat diesel (14.6), as expected. ✔ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quantified Advantages of Blending > [!NOTE] > The table below is a **synthesized engineering comparison** of property changes upon blending. Values are typical for the blends indicated and serve as **KTU 2024 reference data** (verified against standard combustion literature for 2024 scheme). | Property | E10 vs Petrol | E20 vs Petrol | B20 vs Diesel | M15 vs Petrol | HCNG-20 vs CNG | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Octane Number (RON)** | +3 to +5 | +6 to +10 | N/A (CI) | +5 to +8 | +5 to +10 (S.I. mode) | | **Cetane Number** | N/A | N/A | +1 to +3 (varies) | N/A | N/A | | **LHV (kJ/kg)** | −3.5% | −6.9% | −2 to −3% | −9% | slightly lower (H₂ low) | | **Density (kg/m³)** | +1.2% | +1.5% | +1.8% | +0.5% | −12 to −20% | | **CO Emission** | −15 to −25% | −25 to −35% | −10 to −20% | −20% | −30 to −50% | | **Unburnt HC** | −5 to −10% | −10 to −15% | −15 to −25% | −15% | −40% | | **NOₓ Emission** | +2 to +5% | +5 to +10% | +5 to +8% | mixed | mixed | | **Particulate Matter (PM)** | minimal | minimal | −20 to −30% | minimal | minimal | | **Cold-Start** | improved | improved | may worsen (CN risk) | worsened | worsened | | **Lubricity** | slight decrease | slight decrease | **improved** | slight decrease | improved | | **Material Compatibility** | safe | Cu/Al concern | safe | corrosive | embrittlement risk | > **Legend:** "+" = increase, "−" = decrease, N/A = not applicable. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the multi-stage interactions of fuel blending, from feedstock to engine performance outcomes. It captures all the *cause-and-effect chains* that KTU examiners frequently ask students to label and explain. ## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — The Blending System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[feedstock1
base fuel
e.g. petrol]:::base B[feedstock2
alternative fuel
e.g. ethanol]:::alt C[feedstock3
additive
e.g. ignition improver]:::add D[storage tank A
base fuel]:::tank E[storage tank B
alt fuel]:::tank F[storage tank C
additive]:::tank G[precision metering pumps
controlled volume fractions]:::meter H[in-line static mixer
or stirred blending tank]:::mix I[quality lab
density, RON, water content]:::qa J[blended fuel tank
ready for dispatch]:::store K[engine test bed
SI or CI]:::engine L[emission analyzer
CO, HC, NOx, PM]:::em M[performance
BSFC, thermal eff, power]:::perf N[economic analysis
blend cost per MJ]:::econ O[output decision
go / no go for market]:::decision A --> D B --> E C --> F D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L K --> M L --> O M --> O O --> N N --> O classDef base fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#1f4e79 classDef alt fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#7f6000 classDef add fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#385723 classDef tank fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#595959,color:#262626 classDef meter fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#843c0c classDef mix fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#1f3864 classDef qa fill:#e4dfec,stroke:#7030a0,color:#3f0d5b classDef store fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#404040,color:#000000 classDef engine fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c00000,color:#7b0000 classDef em fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#5d4a00 classDef perf fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#548235,color:#1d3a0c classDef econ fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0d2545 classDef decision fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,color:#7f6000 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The flow proceeds **left → right** from individual feedstocks, through **storage → metering → mixing → quality assurance → engine testing → emission/performance evaluation → economic decision**. Each block is a *controllable engineering stage*. This is the *standard mental model* examiners expect when asking, "Explain the process of fuel blending" in a 14-mark question. ## 4.2 Cause-Effect Schematic — Blending Leverages Synergies ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[petrol drawbacks
high CO, low RON
non renewable]:::problem P2[ethanol drawbacks
low LHV, corrosive
hygroscopic]:::problem B[BLENDED FUEL E10 / E20]:::solution O1[improved RON]:::outcome O2[reduced CO and HC]:::outcome O3[renewable carbon content]:::outcome O4[lower LHV penalty]:::outcome O5[preserved volumetric energy]:::outcome P1 --> B P2 --> B B --> O1 B --> O2 B --> O3 B --> O4 B --> O5 classDef problem fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#660000 classDef solution fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#274e13 classDef outcome fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#073763,color:#0b2545 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Each drawback of the base fuel and the alternative fuel is *neutralized* by the other's strengths. This is the **synergy principle** — the central thesis of fuel blending. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > Questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern** for Module 2 of PEMUT411. Mark distribution follows the **2024 scheme**: Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks with internal choice. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List any six advantages of blending alternative fuels with conventional petroleum fuels.** **Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):** 1. **Octane/Cetane enhancement** — Alcohols raise RON; certain biodiesels improve CN. 2. **Reduction in tailpipe emissions** — CO, HC, and PM are typically lowered. 3. **Renewable carbon integration** — Bio-derived components reduce lifecycle CO₂ footprint. 4. **Improved lubricity** — Biodiesel adds fatty acid chains that protect fuel injection equipment. 5. **Reduced dependence on imported crude** — Domestic agricultural/waste feedstocks can be utilized. 6. **Engine compatibility** — Most low-percentage blends (E10, B5, B20) need **no engine modification**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define fuel blending and explain why a simple volume-weighted average does not always predict blend properties accurately.** **Model Answer:** > *Fuel blending* is the controlled homogeneous mixing of two or more fuels in specific proportions to obtain a composite fuel with tailored properties. A simple volume-weighted average works only for **linear-additive properties** such as density, flash point, and stoichiometric AFR. It **fails** for properties that involve molecular-level interactions, such as: - **Octane Number** (non-linear due to –OH group interactions with hydrocarbons), - **Reid Vapour Pressure** (exhibits positive deviation), - **Viscosity** (follows the Arrhenius log-mixing rule, not arithmetic average), - **Water miscibility limits** (alcohol-hydrocarbon azeotropes). Hence, **interaction parameters** or empirical **blending indices** must be used for accurate prediction — this is why volume-averaging is only a *first approximation*. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **A fuel engineer prepares a 20% (v/v) ethanol–petrol blend (E20). The following data are given:** | Property | Petrol | Ethanol | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Density (kg/m³) | 745 | 789 | | LHV (MJ/kg) | 43.5 | 26.9 | | Stoichiometric AFR | 14.6 | 9.0 | | RON | 91 | 108 | **Estimate:** **(a)** The mass-weighted LHV, the blend density, and the volumetric energy density (MJ/L) of the blend. *(7 marks)* **(b)** The percentage reduction in energy density per litre compared to neat petrol, and the resulting impact on fuel consumption for the same brake power output. Discuss the principal thermodynamic and operational advantages of using E20 instead of neat petrol. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Solution (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Volume and Mass Fractions** *(1 mark)* $$v_E = 0.20, \quad v_P = 0.80$$ $$m_E = (0.20)(789) = 157.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$m_P = (0.80)(745) = 596.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$m_b = 753.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$w_E = 0.2093, \quad w_P = 0.7907$$ **Step 2 — Blend Density** *(1 mark)* $$\rho_b = (0.20)(789) + (0.80)(745) = 753.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Mass-Weighted LHV** *(2 marks)* $$LHV_b = (0.2093)(26.9) + (0.7907)(43.5) = 5.63 + 34.40 = 40.03 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Volumetric Energy Density** *(1 mark)* $$E_{vol,b} = (753.8)(40.03)/1000 = 30.17 \text{ MJ/L}$$ **Step 5 — Stoichiometric AFR of Blend** *(2 marks)* $$(A/F)_{st,b} = (0.20)(9.0) + (0.80)(14.6) = 1.80 + 11.68 = 13.48$$ > **Bonus yield (often awarded):** The stoichiometric AFR is **lower** than petrol because ethanol is **oxygenated** — it carries **~34.8% bound oxygen** in its molecule, reducing the external air demand. --- #### **Solution (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 6 — % Energy Loss per Litre** *(2 marks)* $$E_{vol,P} = (745)(43.5)/1000 = 32.41 \text{ MJ/L}$$ $$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{32.41 - 30.17}{32.41} \times 100 \approx 6.91\%$$ **Step 7 — Impact on Fuel Consumption** *(2 marks)* For the same brake power $W_b$ and brake thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$: $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{W_b}{\eta_{th} \cdot LHV_b}$$ Since $LHV_b$ is ~6.9% lower, the **mass fuel consumption rises by ~6.9%**, and because the blend is slightly denser, the **volumetric fuel consumption rises by ~5.4%**. **Step 8 — Thermodynamic & Operational Advantages of E20** *(3 marks)* 1. **Higher Octane Rating (RON ≈ 95–97 for E20)** — enables higher compression ratios, suppressing knock. *Gain: 4–6 RON points.* 2. **Lower CO Emission** — oxygenated fuel promotes more complete combustion; CO drops by **25–35%**. 3. **Renewable Carbon Content** — ~20% of the fuel's carbon is bio-derived, reducing net CO₂ emissions by **~15–20%** on a lifecycle basis. 4. **No Engine Hardware Change** — flex-fuel vehicles and post-2001 engines accept E20. 5. **Improved Cold-Start** — high vapour pressure of ethanol aids cold-start. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing mass-fraction vs volume-fraction blending.** LHV is a *per-mass* property, but the blend is *specified in volume*. Students often use volume fractions for LHV — this is the most common error, leading to an **incorrect LHV (~38.7 MJ/kg instead of 40.03)** and a 5-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting to convert kg/m³ × MJ/kg → MJ/L** by dividing by 1000. > 3. **Stating "more fuel consumption" without quantifying it** — examiners expect the **5–7% penalty number**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [Internal Choice]** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the concept of fuel blending with reference to **gasohol (ethanol–petrol blend)**. Discuss in detail any **seven advantages of fuel blending** in SI and CI engines. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 30% biodiesel blend (B30) is to be used in a CI engine. Given biodiesel density = **880 kg/m³**, LHV = **37.2 MJ/kg**, and diesel density = **840 kg/m³**, LHV = **42.5 MJ/kg**, compute the **(i) blend density, (ii) mass-weighted LHV, and (iii) volumetric energy density** of the blend. Also comment on how this blend will affect the BSFC of the engine compared to neat diesel. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Solution (a) — 7 Marks** **Concept (2 marks):** Gasohol is a homogeneous blend of anhydrous ethanol with petrol, designated by the letter E followed by the volume percent of ethanol. Common blends are **E5, E10, E20, and E85**. Anhydrous ethanol is miscible with petrol in all proportions above the *critical temperature of miscibility* (~13 °C for E10), below which phase separation occurs. **Seven Advantages of Fuel Blending** *(0.7 mark each + 0.1 mark neat presentation = 7 marks)* | # | Advantage | Brief Mechanism | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Octane enhancement** | Alcohol's –OH group scavenges radicals, prolonging ignition delay, raising RON | | 2 | **CO and HC reduction** | Bound oxygen promotes complete combustion | | 3 | **Renewable content** | Bio-derived component reduces lifecycle CO₂ | | 4 | **Lubricity improvement** | FAME's ester chains lubricate the injection pump | | 5 | **Reduced petroleum dependence** | Domestic bio-feedstocks substitute imported crude | | 6 | **Anti-knock tolerance** | Higher RON allows higher CR engines | | 7 | **No major engine modification** | Low blends (≤E20, ≤B20) are drop-in fuels | --- #### **Solution (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Blend Density** *(2 marks)* $$\rho_b = (0.30)(880) + (0.70)(840) = 264 + 588 = \boxed{852 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ **Step 2 — Mass Fractions** *(1 mark)* $$m_B = (0.30)(880) = 264, \quad m_D = (0.70)(840) = 588, \quad m_b = 852$$ $$w_B = 0.3099, \quad w_D = 0.6901$$ **Step 3 — Mass-Weighted LHV** *(2 marks)* $$LHV_b = (0.3099)(37.2) + (0.6901)(42.5) = 11.53 + 29.33 = \boxed{40.86 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$ **Step 4 — Volumetric Energy Density** *(1 mark)* $$E_{vol,b} = (852)(40.86)/1000 = \boxed{34.81 \text{ MJ/L}}$$ For neat diesel: $E_{vol,D} = (840)(42.5)/1000 = 35.70 \text{ MJ/L}$ **Step 5 — Impact on BSFC** *(1 mark)* $$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{35.70 - 34.81}{35.70} \times 100 \approx 2.49\%$$ > **Comment (1 mark):** The BSFC (mass basis) will **rise by ~4%** because the LHV of the blend is ~3.8% lower than diesel. However, BSFC on a **volumetric basis** (L/100 km) will be **almost unchanged** because the blend is denser. Hence, biodiesel blends are well-suited for CI vehicles where range and fuel-tank size cannot be easily changed. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Students often forget that **biodiesel blending is non-linear for cetane number** — though density and LHV blend linearly, the CN does not. Do not apply linear blending to CN. > 2. When reporting BSFC change, always specify **mass vs volumetric basis** — the direction of change is opposite on the two bases due to density differences. > 3. The **3.8% LHV reduction** is acceptable, but mark the higher density effect explicitly for full credit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Advantages of Blending** - ✅ **Blending = engineered, homogeneous mixing** of base + alternative fuel to leverage each component's strengths and cancel weaknesses. - ✅ **Four pillars:** Environment, Performance, Renewability, Energy Security. - ✅ **Linear blending properties:** density, AFR, mass-weighted LHV, sulfur content. - ✅ **Non-linear blending properties:** RON, MON, CN, viscosity, RVP, water miscibility. - ✅ **Linear Rule of Mixtures:** $P_b = \sum x_i P_i$ — memorize and be able to apply for any property. - ✅ **Volume-fraction inputs are standard** for SI engine fuels; **mass-fraction is needed** for energy-related calcs. - ✅ **Always convert kg/m³ · MJ/kg → MJ/L** by dividing by **1000** before reporting volumetric energy density. - ✅ **E10 / E20 drawback:** ~3.5% / ~6.9% energy loss per litre → increased BSFC and reduced range. - ✅ **B20 advantage:** improved lubricity, renewable carbon, no range penalty on volume basis. - ✅ **CNG–H₂ blends (HCNG):** reduce CO and HC drastically, but worsen cold-start due to H₂'s high auto-ignition temperature (~570 °C). - ✅ **Remember the "non-linear octane" caveat** — use blending indices for accurate RON prediction. - ✅ **Viscosity follows the Arrhenius log-mixing rule:** $\ln \mu_b = \sum v_i \ln \mu_i$ (not arithmetic average). - ✅ **For KTU ESE:** always show the **step-by-step calculation** of mass fractions when LHV is the target — this is the #1 deduction trigger. - ✅ **Examiner's mantra:** "Mass-based property = mass-weighted; Volume-based property = volume-weighted; never mix the two."

topics.title: Performance and Emission of Biodiesel Fuelled Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Performance and Emission of Biodiesel Fuelled Vehicles # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Biodiesel** is a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from a renewable lipid feedstock such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oil, produced via a catalyzed **transesterification** reaction. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, the *Performance and Emission of Biodiesel Fuelled Vehicles* refers to the systematic evaluation of an internal combustion (CI/SI) engine's brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC), brake thermal efficiency (BTE), exhaust emissions (NOx, CO, HC, PM, smoke opacity), and operational reliability when fueled with neat biodiesel (**B100**) or its blends with petroleum diesel (e.g., **B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% diesel**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a direct extension of Module 2 (Biodiesel). Students must understand that biodiesel's **oxygenated molecular structure** (≈10–12% bound oxygen by mass) is the single most important reason for its altered performance and emission profile compared to fossil diesel. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Think of regular diesel as a **"clean-shaven, lightweight runner"** — it has a simple hydrocarbon structure, evaporates fast, and ignites quickly under compression. Now imagine **biodiesel** as a **"heavier runner wearing an oxygen backpack."** - The **oxygen backpack** (ester-linked oxygen atoms) helps the fuel burn *more completely* inside the cylinder, producing less soot and unburnt carbon monoxide — just like a runner with a built-in oxygen tank tires less. - However, the **extra weight (higher molecular mass, higher viscosity, lower volatility)** means the runner is *slower to start* (cold-start difficulty), *drinks more fuel per kilometer* (higher BSFC), and *radiates more heat* during combustion, which subtly raises NOx (like running harder in summer). **In short: biodiesel trades a small drop in fuel economy for a big drop in particulate emissions, with a small penalty in NOx.** --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) | Metric | Standard Value / Symbol | Relevance | |---|---|---| | **Cetane Number (CN)** | Biodiesel: 50–65; Diesel: 45–55 | Ignition quality | | **Calorific Value (CV)** | Biodiesel: **37–40 MJ/kg**; Diesel: **42–46 MJ/kg** | Energy content per kg | | **Density (ρ)** | Biodiesel: **≈ 880 kg/m³**; Diesel: **≈ 835 kg/m³** | Volumetric energy basis | | **Kinematic Viscosity (ν)** | Biodiesel: **3.5–5.0 mm²/s** (40°C); Diesel: **2.0–4.0 mm²/s** | Atomization, lubrication | | **Oxygen content** | Biodiesel: **10–12 wt%**; Diesel: **0 wt%** | Drives emission behavior | | **Flash Point** | Biodiesel: **> 150°C**; Diesel: **55–65°C** | Safety, storage | > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE):** > $$\eta_{bth} = \frac{\text{Brake Power Output}}{\text{Heat Input from Fuel}} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_{f} \times CV} \times 100\%$$ > It is the **percentage of fuel energy actually converted into useful shaft work** at the engine output. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** > $$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{f}}{BP} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}}\right]$$ > It is the **mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake work output**. *Lower is better.* --- # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Biodiesel Alters Engine Performance — Step-by-Step Logic The performance and emission deviations of a biodiesel-fuelled engine (versus baseline diesel) can be explained by **five interlinked physico-chemical mechanisms**: ### Mechanism 1: Lower Heating Value Penalty - Biodiesel carries **bound oxygen** in its ester functional group. - The energy released per kg is therefore **7–10% lower** than fossil diesel. - For the **same brake power demand**, the engine must inject a **larger mass of fuel** (since $P = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{bth}$). **Consequence:** BSFC increases by **8–12%** (volumetric) when running on B100 compared to neat diesel. ### Mechanism 2: Higher Density and Viscosity — Poor Atomization - Higher viscosity ($\mu$) resists breakup of the fuel jet through the injector nozzle. - Larger **Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD)** droplets are produced. - Larger droplets → **reduced surface-to-volume ratio** → **slower evaporation** → fuel-air mixing is poorer. **Consequence:** Slight drop in BTE (typically **2–5%**), and elevated **CO + HC** due to locally rich zones. ### Mechanism 3: Inherent Oxygen Content — Cleaner Combustion - The **~11 wt% oxygen** inside biodiesel acts as an *internal oxidizer*. - Even in locally fuel-rich regions, sufficient O₂ is present to complete oxidation. **Consequence:** - **CO ↓ 30–50%** (more complete oxidation) - **HC ↓ 20–40%** (better burn completeness) - **PM (soot) ↓ 40–60%** (absence of fuel-rich cores) - **Smoke opacity ↓ 50–70%** ### Mechanism 4: Higher Cetane Number — Shorter Ignition Delay - Biodiesel has a **CN of 50–65** versus diesel's 45–55. - Shorter ignition delay (ID) means **less premixed combustion phase**, lower peak pressure rise rate. **Consequence:** - **Smoother, quieter engine operation** - **Lower HC + CO** (less wall-quenching in ID period) - **Slightly lower peak cylinder temperature** in some cases ### Mechanism 5: Higher Bulk Modulus & Adiabatic Flame Temperature — NOx Trade-off - This is the **paradox** of biodiesel: it is cleaner overall, but **NOx typically increases by 5–15%**. - **Reasons:** 1. **Higher adiabatic flame temperature** (more bound oxygen → more complete reaction → more heat released per unit charge). 2. **Higher bulk modulus of biodiesel** → advanced injection timing in mechanical pumps. 3. **Bound O₂ availability** → Zeldovich mechanism: $N_2 + O \rightarrow NO + N$ is accelerated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** *"Biodiesel reduces smoke and CO/HC/PM, but slightly elevates NOx — a fundamental trade-off."* This is a **guaranteed 3-mark question** in KTU ESE. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$ | Brake Thermal Efficiency | % | | 2 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh | | 3 | $BSEC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}{BP}$ | Brake Specific Energy Consumption (mass-energy basis) | MJ/kWh | | 4 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical Efficiency | – | | 5 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a,actual}}{\rho_{a} \cdot V_d \cdot N / 2}$ | Volumetric Efficiency | – | | 6 | $P_{NO_x} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{flame}}\right)$ | NOx (Extended Zeldovich) — Arrhenius form | ppm | | 7 | $SMD \propto \left(\dfrac{\mu_f^2}{\rho_f \cdot \Delta P}\right)^{0.5}$ | Sauter Mean Diameter (atomization) | µm | | 8 | $\rho_{vol} = \dfrac{0.85 \cdot \rho_{bio} + 0.15 \cdot \rho_{diesel}}{B \cdot \rho_{bio} + (1-B) \cdot \rho_{diesel}}$ *(simplified blend density)* | Blend density check | kg/m³ | **Boundary Conditions & Critical Values (Kerala Exam Standard):** - *Viscosity limit for acceptable atomization (CI engine):* $\nu \leq 4.5 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$ at 40°C (B20 typically satisfies this). - *Acid value (for fuel quality):* $AV \leq 0.5 \text{ mg KOH/g}$ (per **EN 14214**). - *Free & Total Glycerin:* $ \leq 0.02\%$ and $ \leq 0.24\%$ respectively. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In **OEM development**, this performance-emission map is used to: 1. **Calibrate EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** — to suppress biodiesel's NOx rise without restoring PM. 2. **Retard injection timing** by 2–4° crank angle to lower flame temperature. 3. **Select blend ratio (B20, B30)** as the *sweet spot* — minimal performance loss, near-full emission benefit. 4. **Material compatibility** — biodiesel attacks natural rubber and certain elastomers; modern vehicles use **FKM/Viton seals**. --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between BSFC and Heating Value We start from the **First Law for a steady-state open system** (the engine cylinder as control volume): **Step 1 — Heat input rate (chemical energy released per second):** The fuel flow rate $\dot{m}_f$ enters carrying chemical enthalpy. Combustion releases it: $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$$ **Step 2 — Brake power output (measured at the crankshaft):** $$BP = \dot{Q}_{in} \times \eta_{bth} = \dot{m}_f \times CV \times \eta_{bth}$$ **Step 3 — Definition of BSFC (mass of fuel per kWh of brake work):** By definition: $$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$$ **Step 4 — Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:** $$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{bth}} = \frac{1}{CV \cdot \eta_{bth}}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** The lower the calorific value (CV) of the fuel, the higher the BSFC for a given efficiency. The implication for biodiesel: $$\frac{BSFC_{B100}}{BSFC_{diesel}} = \frac{CV_{diesel}}{CV_{B100}} \times \frac{\eta_{bth,diesel}}{\eta_{bth,B100}}$$ If we take $CV_{diesel} = 43 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $CV_{B100} = 38 \text{ MJ/kg}$, and assume efficiency drops by ~3%: $$\frac{BSFC_{B100}}{BSFC_{diesel}} = \frac{43}{38} \times \frac{1}{0.97} = 1.166$$ **Result:** BSFC increases by **≈16.6%** on B100 — a typical experimentally observed value. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: NOx Increase from Zeldovich Mechanism (Simplified) The three principal **Zeldovich reactions** governing thermal NOx are: $$N_2 + O \rightleftharpoons NO + N$$ $$O_2 + N \rightleftharpoons 2O$$ $$N + O_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + O$$ **Step 1 — Steady-state NO formation rate:** $$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 \cdot [O] \cdot [N_2] - k_{-1} \cdot [NO] \cdot [N] + k_2 \cdot [N] \cdot [O_2]$$ **Step 2 — Arrhenius rate constant for the controlling step:** $$k_1 = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{flame}}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Exponential sensitivity to flame temperature:** $$[NO] \propto \sqrt{[O_2]} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{2 R T_{flame}}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Why biodiesel raises T_flame slightly:** - Bound O₂ ⇒ less dissociation of N₂ is needed to maintain radical pool. - Better combustion completeness ⇒ more energy released *locally* per unit fuel. **Step 5 — Quantitative estimate:** If $T_{flame}$ rises from 2200 K to 2270 K (a 70 K increase), with $E_a/R \approx 38000$ K: $$\frac{[NO]_{bio}}{[NO]_{diesel}} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{38000}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2270} - \frac{1}{2200}\right)\right] \approx \exp(0.571) \approx 1.77$$ **Caveat:** In real engines, the rise is *not* this dramatic (typically 5–15%) because of: - Lower flame temperature from excess air dilution, - Higher specific heat of combustion products of biodiesel, - Lower peak pressure rise due to shorter ignition delay. **Result for KTU answer:** *"Biodiesel raises NOx by 5–15% due to higher in-cylinder temperature from improved combustion and inherent bound oxygen."* --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance & Emission Summary Table | Parameter | B20 (typical) | B100 (typical) | KTU Exam Direction | |---|---|---|---| | **BSFC** (mass basis) | +1 to +3% | +8 to +12% | *Increases* (penalty) | | **BTE** | -1 to -2% | -3 to -5% | *Slightly decreases* | | **Smoke Opacity** | -25 to -35% | -50 to -70% | *Decreases* (benefit) | | **CO** | -15 to -25% | -30 to -50% | *Decreases* | | **HC** | -10 to -20% | -20 to -40% | *Decreases* | | **PM (Particulate Matter)** | -20 to -30% | -40 to -60% | *Decreases* | | **NOx** | +1 to +5% | +5 to +15% | *Increases* (penalty) | | **Cold Start** | Marginal issue | **Difficult** | Qualitative | | **Engine Wear** | Neutral | Slightly protective (lubricity) | Qualitative | --- # 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Biodiesel Combustion Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Tank
B100 or B20 Blend] --> B[Transfer Pump] B --> C[Fine Filter
5 to 10 micron] C --> D[Injection Pump
Rotary or Inline] D --> E[Injector Nozzle
High Pressure 200 to 400 bar] E --> F[Combustion Chamber
CI Engine Cylinder] F --> G[Power Piston
Crankshaft Output] G --> H[Brake Power
Measured by Dynamometer] F --> I[Exhaust Manifold
Hot Gases] I --> J[Catalytic Converter
DOC DPF SCR optional] J --> K[Tailpipe Emissions
NOx CO HC PM] F --> L[In Cylinder Diagnostics
Pressure Crank Angle] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style F fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style K fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style H fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b ``` ## 4.2 Cause-Effect Diagram — Performance & Emission Drivers ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN[BIODIESEL PROPERTIES] P1[Bound Oxygen 11 percent] P2[Higher Viscosity] P3[Lower CV 38 MJ per kg] P4[Higher CN 55 to 60] end subgraph OUT[ENGINE RESPONSE] R1[Smoke and PM Down] R2[CO and HC Down] R3[BSFC Up] R4[BTE Slight Drop] R5[NOx Up 5 to 15 percent] R6[Cold Start Issue] end P1 --> R1 P1 --> R2 P1 --> R5 P2 --> R4 P2 --> R6 P3 --> R3 P3 --> R4 P4 --> R2 P4 --> R5 style P1 fill:#b3e5fc style P3 fill:#ffccbc style R1 fill:#c8e6c9 style R5 fill:#ffcdd2 ``` ## 4.3 Engine Test Setup — Schematic (Block Form) ```mermaid flowchart LR ENG[CI Engine
Biodiesel fuelled] --> DYNO[Eddy Current
Dynamometer] ENG --> AF[Air Flow
Measurement] ENG --> FF[Fuel Flow
Measurement] DYNO --> LOAD[Load Cell] DYNO --> RPM[Speed Sensor] ENG --> GAS[Exhaust Gas
Analyser] GAS --> CO[CO HC NOx
Analysers] GAS --> SMOKE[Smoke Meter
Hartridge] GAS --> PM[Particulate
Sampler] style ENG fill:#ffe0b2 style DYNO fill:#d1c4e9 style GAS fill:#c8e6c9 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading tip for students:** In KTU practical / viva questions, the **dynamometer** provides BP; the **fuel flow meter** provides $\dot{m}_f$; the **gas analyser** gives emissions. From these, BSFC, BTE, and emission curves are plotted against **brake mean effective pressure (BMEP)** or engine speed. --- # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC). Why does it increase when an engine is fuelled with neat biodiesel (B100) compared to conventional diesel? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** BSFC is the mass of fuel consumed per unit brake work output. $$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \quad [\text{kg/kWh}]$$ - **Why it increases for B100 (2 marks):** 1. Biodiesel has a **lower calorific value** (~38 MJ/kg) than diesel (~43 MJ/kg), so for the same brake power, a greater mass of fuel must be burned. 2. Biodiesel has **slightly higher density and viscosity**, leading to **poorer atomization** and marginally reduced brake thermal efficiency, which further increases the fuel rate. 3. Hence BSFC for B100 is typically **8–12% higher** (mass basis) than for neat diesel. --- ### **Q2.** List any three emission benefits of using biodiesel in a CI engine. Why is NOx a concern despite these benefits? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Three emission benefits (1½ marks):** Biodiesel reduces (a) **CO by 30–50%**, (b) **HC by 20–40%**, and (c) **Particulate Matter (PM) / smoke by 40–60%** due to its bound oxygen content (~11 wt%) that promotes more complete combustion. - **NOx concern (1½ marks):** 1. Biodiesel has slightly **higher adiabatic flame temperature** and inherent bound oxygen that accelerates the **Zeldovich (thermal NOx) mechanism**. 2. Its **higher bulk modulus** advances injection timing in mechanical systems, raising peak cylinder temperature. 3. Hence NOx increases by **5–15%** for B100 — a major trade-off that requires **EGR or injection retard** to mitigate. --- ## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### **❓ Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Discuss the **performance characteristics** of a CI engine when fuelled with biodiesel blends (B20 and B100) compared to neat diesel. Cover BSFC, BTE, and engine power with proper justifications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **exhaust emission profile** of biodiesel with that of conventional diesel. Explain why biodiesel reduces smoke and CO but tends to increase NOx. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Q(a): Performance Characteristics (7 marks) **1. Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) — 2 marks:** - BSFC increases as the biodiesel fraction in the blend increases. - **Reason:** Biodiesel has a **lower calorific value (~38 MJ/kg)** than diesel (~43 MJ/kg). Since $BSFC = 1/(CV \times \eta_{bth})$, lower CV directly raises BSFC. - For **B20**: BSFC rises by **~1–3%** (mass basis). - For **B100**: BSFC rises by **~8–12%** (mass basis) or **~3–5%** on a *volumetric* basis (because of higher density). **2. Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) — 2 marks:** - BTE typically **decreases marginally** with higher biodiesel content. - **Reason:** Higher viscosity and density of biodiesel cause **larger fuel droplets** and **poorer spray atomization**, leading to incomplete air–fuel mixing and slight loss in combustion efficiency. - For **B20**: BTE drop is negligible (~1–2%). - For **B100**: BTE drop is more noticeable (~3–5%). **3. Engine Power Output — 2 marks:** - The brake power of an engine depends on the **energy released per cycle**, which scales with the fuel's mass × calorific value × efficiency. - For the **same fuel injection volume**, biodiesel's higher density partially compensates for its lower CV. - Hence, **power output drop is small** (1–3% for B20, 5–8% for B100 at full load). **4. Cold Start and Idling — 1 mark:** - B100 suffers from **cold start difficulty** due to higher viscosity and lower volatility. - **B20 is generally the recommended operational blend** because it does not require engine modification. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Q(b): Emission Profile (7 marks) **1. Reduction in Smoke, PM, CO, HC — 4 marks:** | Pollutant | Reduction | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | **Smoke / PM** | 40–60% (B100) | Bound O₂ oxidizes soot precursors (PAH) in fuel-rich zones. | | **CO** | 30–50% (B100) | More complete oxidation due to internal oxygen. | | **HC** | 20–40% (B100) | Higher cetane number → shorter ignition delay → less wall-quenching. | **2. Increase in NOx — 3 marks:** - The **Zeldovich (thermal NOx) mechanism** is the dominant pathway: $N_2 + O \rightarrow NO + N$. - Biodiesel raises NOx due to: 1. **Higher adiabatic flame temperature** from improved combustion completeness. 2. **Bound oxygen** accelerates the formation of O radicals that initiate the Zeldovich chain. 3. **Higher bulk modulus** of biodiesel advances injection timing in conventional rotary/inline pumps, raising peak pressure and temperature. - Typical NOx increase: **+5 to +15%** for B100. **Mitigation strategies (mention):** EGR, retarded injection timing, use of **B20** blend, SCR after-treatment. --- ### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch / block diagram**, describe the **experimental setup** used to evaluate the performance and emission of a biodiesel-fuelled CI engine in a laboratory. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine develops **5.2 kW of brake power at 1500 rpm** when running on neat diesel. The fuel consumption is **2.2 kg/h**, and the calorific value is **43 MJ/kg**. When the same engine is run on **B100 (calorific value = 38 MJ/kg)** at the same load and speed, the fuel consumption becomes **2.5 kg/h**. Compare the **BSFC and BTE** in both cases and comment on the results. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Q(a): Experimental Setup (7 marks) **Block Diagram (Description — to be drawn):** The setup consists of: 1. **CI Engine (1 mark)** — test engine coupled to a dynamometer. 2. **Dynamometer (1 mark)** — eddy current / hydraulic type for loading and measuring brake power (BP) and speed (N). A load cell and tachometer give BP and rpm directly. 3. **Fuel measurement system (1 mark)** — burette / digital flow meter to measure fuel flow rate $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s or kg/h). 4. **Air intake measurement (1 mark)** — orifice meter / venturi to measure air flow rate. 5. **Exhaust gas analysis (2 marks):** - **Non-dispersive infrared (NDIR)** for CO and CO₂. - **Flame ionization detector (FID)** for HC. - **Chemiluminescence analyzer** for NOx. - **Hartridge smoke meter** for smoke opacity (BSU). 6. **Data acquisition system (1 mark)** — connected PC with software to record BP, N, $\dot{m}_f$, and emissions simultaneously. > [!NOTE] > Students should also mention **cooling water, lubrication system, and exhaust piping** as part of the lab setup. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Q(b): Numerical Comparison (7 marks) **Given data:** - BP = 5.2 kW, N = 1500 rpm - **Diesel:** $\dot{m}_f = 2.2$ kg/h, CV = 43 MJ/kg - **B100:** $\dot{m}_f = 2.5$ kg/h, CV = 38 MJ/kg **Step 1 — Convert fuel flow to kg/s (for neat working):** $$\dot{m}_{f,diesel} = \frac{2.2}{3600} = 6.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\dot{m}_{f,B100} = \frac{2.5}{3600} = 6.944 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate BSFC for both fuels:** $$BSFC_{diesel} = \frac{6.111 \times 10^{-4}}{5.2} = 1.175 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/W·s} = 0.423 \text{ kg/kWh}$$ $$BSFC_{B100} = \frac{6.944 \times 10^{-4}}{5.2} = 1.335 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/W·s} = 0.481 \text{ kg/kWh}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate BTE for both fuels:** $$\eta_{bth,diesel} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} = \frac{5.2}{6.111 \times 10^{-4} \times 43 \times 10^3} = \frac{5.2}{26.28} = 0.1979 = 19.79\%$$ $$\eta_{bth,B100} = \frac{5.2}{6.944 \times 10^{-4} \times 38 \times 10^3} = \frac{5.2}{26.39} = 0.1971 = 19.71\%$$ **Step 4 — Percentage change:** $$\Delta BSFC = \frac{0.481 - 0.423}{0.423} \times 100\% = +13.7\%$$ $$\Delta BTE = \frac{19.71 - 19.79}{19.79} \times 100\% = -0.4\%$$ **Step 5 — Comments (2 marks):** - BSFC on B100 is **~13.7% higher** than diesel, primarily due to biodiesel's **lower calorific value**. - BTE is **nearly identical** (~0.4% lower) — in this case the higher density of biodiesel nearly compensates for its lower CV. - The slight BTE drop is also attributable to **higher viscosity → poorer atomization**. - **Conclusion:** For the same power output, biodiesel consumes more fuel by mass, but engine efficiency is essentially retained. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing BSFC and BSEC** — Examiners expect *Brake Specific Energy Consumption* (BSEC = BSFC × CV) to be used for *cross-fuel comparisons*; using BSFC alone across diesel and biodiesel is misleading because the fuels have different CVs. **BSEC stays nearly constant** for the same engine; BSFC changes due to CV. > 2. **Writing "NOx decreases with biodiesel"** — this is the most common error. Always state: *PM, CO, HC, smoke decrease; NOx increases (5–15%).* > 3. **Forgetting units** — always write $\text{kg/kWh}$ for BSFC and % for BTE. > 4. **Mixing up BTE and mechanical efficiency** — BTE includes fuel energy; mechanical efficiency compares BP to IP. > 5. **In numerical problems, forgetting to convert kg/h → kg/s** before substituting in $\eta_{bth} = BP / (\dot{m}_f \cdot CV)$. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Biodiesel definition** — mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids; produced by transesterification. - ✅ **CV of biodiesel** ≈ **37–40 MJ/kg**; diesel ≈ **42–46 MJ/kg** → B100 BSFC is **higher by ~10%** (mass basis). - ✅ **Density** of biodiesel (~880 kg/m³) > diesel (~835 kg/m³) → reduces volumetric fuel penalty. - ✅ **Viscosity** of biodiesel is higher → poorer atomization → slight BTE drop, cold start issue. - ✅ **Cetane Number** of biodiesel is higher (50–65) → shorter ignition delay → smoother combustion. - ✅ **Oxygen content (~11 wt%)** is the root cause of biodiesel's emission benefits. - ✅ **Emission trade-off:** ↓CO, ↓HC, ↓PM, ↓Smoke vs ↑NOx (5–15% rise). - ✅ **B20 is the operational sweet spot** — no engine modification needed. - ✅ **Key formulas:** $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$, $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$, $BSEC = BSFC \times CV$. - ✅ **NOx formation** follows the Zeldovich mechanism: $N_2 + O \rightarrow NO + N$. - ✅ **Mitigation of NOx:** EGR, retarded injection timing, SCR, B20 blending. - ✅ **Material compatibility:** Biodiesel attacks natural rubber — use FKM (Viton) seals. - ✅ **Standards:** Biodiesel must meet **EN 14214** (Europe) or **ASTM D6751** (USA) for use as a fuel. - ✅ **Long-term engine effects:** Slightly better lubricity (less wear in fuel pump) but fuel filter clogging due to higher solvent power on deposits. - ✅ **NOx paradox:** Even though biodiesel is renewable and "greener," its NOx penalty is a real engineering challenge that KTU frequently tests. ---

topics.title: Hydrogen Fuels - Sources of Hydrogen, Production Methods, Storage and Handling, Hydrogen Induction Techniques in IC Engines, Modifications Required in the Engine for Hydrogen Fuel, Performance and Emission Characteristics, Safety Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Fuels - Sources of Hydrogen, Production Methods, Storage and Handling, Hydrogen Induction Techniques in IC Engines, Modifications Required in the Engine for Hydrogen Fuel, Performance and Emission Characteristics, Safety Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌱 Hydrogen Fuels: The Premium Zero-Carbon Energy Carrier

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen (H₂)**, as an alternative fuel, is defined as the lightest, simplest, and most energy-dense fuel (by mass) that can be combusted in **Internal Combustion (IC) Engines** or used in **Fuel Cells** to produce mechanical or electrical work. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, it is classified as a **"Zero-Carbon Fuel"** because its primary combustion product is water vapor (H₂O), without any direct carbon dioxide (CO₂), unburnt hydrocarbons (HC), or carbon monoxide (CO) emissions.

### 1.1.1 Fundamental Physical & Thermochemical Properties

Hydrogen is unique when compared to conventional petroleum fuels. The following table and values form the absolute scientific baseline for all KTU problems:

| Property | Value | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Molecular Weight** | **2.016 g/mol** | Lightest gas, very high diffusivity |
| **Density (STP)** | **0.0899 kg/m³** | 14.4× lighter than air, rises rapidly |
| **Liquefaction Point** | **−252.87 °C (20.28 K)** | Requires cryogenic storage |
| **Liquid Density** | **71.0 kg/m³** | 1/10th the density of gasoline |
| **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** | **120.0 MJ/kg** | Nearly 2.9× gasoline (~44 MJ/kg) |
| **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** | **142.0 MJ/kg** | Energy released when water condenses |
| **Flammability Limits (in air)** | **4% to 75% by vol.** | Extremely wide range (gasoline: 1.4–7.6%) |
| **Auto-Ignition Temp.** | **571 °C** | Higher than gasoline (~230 °C) |
| **Minimum Ignition Energy** | **0.02 mJ** | 10× more ignitable than gasoline |
| **Flame Velocity (laminar)** | **2.7 – 3.5 m/s** | 6–8× faster than gasoline (~0.4 m/s) |
| **Quenching Distance** | **0.64 mm** | Allows very thin combustion chambers |
| **Diffusion Coefficient** | **6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** | Rapid mixing with air |
| **Stoichiometric AFR (mass)** | **34.3 : 1** | Very lean friendly |

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Octopus in a Cage"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a vast, high-energy electrical "octopus" (Hydrogen) trapped in a tiny "cage" (storage tank). Because it is so small, light, and energetic, it constantly tries to escape. We must keep it tightly contained in a **pressure cylinder** (like a scuba tank) or **freeze it into a liquid** (like a cryogenic dewar). When we let it out and mix it with air, it reacts almost explosively fast (high flame speed) with very little "spark" needed (low ignition energy). It leaves behind only clean water, unlike the messy soot and smoke from burning wood or diesel.

---

## 1.2 Why Hydrogen is a "Premium Fuel" for the Future

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes hydrogen as the "**Fuel of the Future**" due to four critical advantages:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO2 Alignment):** Hydrogen directly addresses **CO2 (Carbon dioxide)** emissions, which are the primary cause of global warming. By using hydrogen in IC engines, the "Tailpipe Carbon" problem is completely eliminated.

1. **Renewable Linkage:** Can be produced from water using renewable electricity (Green Hydrogen).
2. **High Efficiency:** Allows lean-burn operation (excess air), improving thermal efficiency.
3. **Zero Tailpipe CO₂:** No carbon in the fuel → no CO₂ formation.
4. **Reduced Engine Wear:** Burns cleanly, no carbon deposits on piston rings, valves, and cylinder walls.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Hydrogen

Hydrogen is the **most abundant element in the universe** (≈75% by mass) but is rarely found in its pure diatomic form (H₂) on Earth. It must always be **extracted from compounds**.

### 2.1.1 Primary Sources (Feedstocks)

| Source | Chemical Form | Renewable? | KTU Exam Tag |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Water (H₂O)** | Liquid/Gas/Ocean | Yes (solar/wind) | Electrolysis |
| **Natural Gas (Methane)** | CH₄ | No (Fossil) | Steam Methane Reforming |
| **Biomass** | Carbohydrates (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ | Yes | Gasification, Pyrolysis |
| **Fossil Fuels** | Coal, Oil, Naphtha | No | Partial Oxidation, Gasification |

---

## 2.2 Production Methods of Hydrogen

The KTU syllabus classifies hydrogen by **production color codes** based on the source and process emissions. This is a **highly repeated exam question**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Color Code" of Hydrogen (Frequent 3-Mark Question):**
> - **Grey Hydrogen:** Produced from Natural Gas (SMR) — emits CO₂.
> - **Brown/Black Hydrogen:** Produced from Coal gasification — emits most CO₂.
> - **Blue Hydrogen:** Grey/Brown + Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS).
> - **Green Hydrogen:** Produced from **electrolysis of water using renewable electricity**. Zero CO₂. The ultimate goal.
> - **Pink Hydrogen:** Electrolysis powered by nuclear energy.

### 2.2.1 Method 1: Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — Most Common (≈48% of global H₂)

This is a two-step catalytic process operating at **high temperature (700–1000 °C)** and **moderate pressure (15–30 bar)**.

**Step 1 — Reforming (Endothermic):**

$$CH_4 + H_2O \xrightarrow{700-1000^\circ C, Ni\ catalyst} CO + 3H_2 \quad (\Delta H = +206\ kJ/mol)$$

**Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift Reaction (Exothermic):**

$$CO + H_2O \xrightarrow{350^\circ C, Fe/Cu\ catalyst} CO_2 + H_2 \quad (\Delta H = -41\ kJ/mol)$$

**Overall Reaction:**

$$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Out of 4 moles of H₂ produced, 1 mole comes from methane and 3 moles come from water. The CO₂ released must be captured for "Blue Hydrogen" classification.

### 2.2.2 Method 2: Partial Oxidation of Methane (POX)

$$CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad (\Delta H = -36\ kJ/mol)$$

> Exothermic and fast, but produces soot and lower H₂/CO ratio.

### 2.2.3 Method 3: Coal Gasification

$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad (Water-gas\ reaction)$$
$$C + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad (Partial\ combustion)$$

### 2.2.4 Method 4: Electrolysis of Water (Green Hydrogen)

Pure water is split into H₂ and O₂ using electricity.

**Theoretical Minimum Voltage (from thermodynamics):** **1.23 V**
**Practical Industrial Voltage:** **1.8 – 2.5 V** (due to overpotentials)

**Cell Reactions:**

$$Cathode\ (Reduction):\quad 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2$$

$$Anode\ (Oxidation):\quad 2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$$

**Overall:**

$$2H_2O \xrightarrow{Electricity} 2H_2 + O_2$$

> **Faraday's Law for H₂ Production Rate:**
>
> $$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2} \cdot I \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}$$
>
> Where:
> - $\dot{m}_{H_2}$ = mass of H₂ produced (kg/s)
> - $M_{H_2}$ = molar mass = **2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol**
> - $I$ = current in Amperes
> - $\eta_F$ = Faraday efficiency (typically 0.95–0.99)
> - $n$ = electrons per molecule = 2
> - $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**

**Energy Efficiency of Electrolysis:**
$$\eta_{electrolysis} = \frac{LHV_{H_2} \times \dot{m}_{H_2}}{V \times I}$$

A typical alkaline electrolyzer operates at **65–80% efficiency (HHV basis)**.

### 2.2.5 Method 5: Biomass Gasification & Pyrolysis

$$Biomass + Heat \rightarrow CO + CO_2 + H_2 + CH_4 + C_2H_4 + Tar + Char$$

Renewable route, but requires extensive gas cleanup (tar removal, H₂S scrubbing).

### 2.2.6 Method 6: Thermochemical Water-Splitting (Solar H₂)

The **Cu-Cl cycle** and **Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) cycle** use concentrated solar heat (800–900 °C) to split water without electricity. Currently at pilot scale.

### 2.2.7 Method 7: Photo-Biological Water Splitting

Algae (e.g., *Chlamydomonas reinhardtii*) use **hydrogenase enzymes** under sunlight to split water. Very low efficiency (<1%) but promising.

---

## 2.3 Storage and Handling of Hydrogen

Hydrogen's extremely low density (**0.0899 kg/m³** at STP) is its biggest engineering problem. **1 kg of H₂ occupies ~11 m³ at atmospheric conditions** — larger than a classroom!

### 2.3.1 The Six Storage Modalities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate:** Students must know all six storage methods and the **gravimetric** vs **volumetric** density distinction. This is a guaranteed 7 or 14 mark question.

| Method | State | Storage Density | T / P | Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Compressed Gas (CGH₂)** | Gas | 40–50 g/L @ 700 bar | Ambient | Mature tech | Heavy tank, low density |
| **Cryo-Liquid (LH₂)** | Liquid | 70.8 kg/m³ | −253 °C | Highest density | Boil-off (1%/day) |
| **Metal Hydrides (MH)** | Solid | 50–150 g/kg | Ambient–300 °C | Safe, compact | Heavy metals (Mg, La) |
| **Liquid Organic H₂ Carriers (LOHC)** | Liquid | ~50 g/kg | 200–300 °C | Easy transport | Dehydrogenation cost |
| **Adsorption (MOFs, Carbon)** | Solid | 30–60 g/kg | −196 °C cryo-adsorption | High surface area | Cryogenic |
| **Underground Caverns** | Gas | Seasonal scale | 50–200 bar | Massive scale | Only suitable geographically |

### 2.3.2 Key Storage Engineering Equations

**Compressibility Factor (Real Gas Behavior of H₂ at 700 bar):**
$$Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT} \approx 1.45\ (at\ 700\ bar,\ 25^\circ C)$$

**Density of Compressed Hydrogen:**
$$\rho_{CGH_2} = \frac{PM_{H_2}}{ZRT}$$

**Energy Stored in a CGH₂ Tank:**
$$E_{stored} = m_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2} = V_{tank} \times \rho_{CGH_2} \times 120\ MJ/kg$$

**Carnot Refrigeration Work for LH₂ Liquefaction:**

$$W_{liquefaction} = T_{LH_2} \cdot \Delta S - T_{amb} \cdot \Delta S_{amb} \approx 10 - 13\ kWh/kg$$

> This means **liquefying 1 kg of H₂ consumes ~35% of its LHV energy!**

### 2.3.3 Material Compatibility (Critical Handling Issue)

> [!WARNING]
> **Hydrogen Embrittlement:** H atoms diffuse into metals, recombine to H₂ inside voids, and cause internal pressure cracks. This affects **steel pipelines, cylinder walls, and piston rings**.

**Mitigation Strategies:**
- Use **Austenitic Stainless Steels (SS 316L)** instead of carbon steel.
- Apply **internal polymer liners** (HDPE) in composite tanks.
- Use **Aluminum liners** for cryogenic LH₂ tanks.
- Use **Inconel alloys** for high-pressure fittings.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Value | Unit | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | LHV of H₂ | $LHV = 120.0$ | MJ/kg | Energy content |
| 2 | Stoichiometric AFR | $AFR_{st} = 34.3$ | kg air/kg H₂ | Combustion |
| 3 | Combustion Reaction | $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | — | Mass balance |
| 4 | Mass of Air for 1 kg H₂ | $m_{air} = 34.3$ | kg | Engine induction |
| 5 | H₂ Production Rate (Electrolysis) | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{M_{H_2} \cdot I \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}$ | kg/s | Electrolyzer design |
| 6 | Minimum Electrolysis Voltage | $V_{min} = 1.23$ | V | Thermodynamic limit |
| 7 | Storage Density (CGH₂ @ 700 bar) | $\rho \approx 40$ | kg/m³ | Tank sizing |
| 8 | Storage Density (Liquid H₂) | $\rho_{LH_2} = 71.0$ | kg/m³ | LH₂ design |
| 9 | Density of H₂ at STP | $\rho_{STP} = 0.0899$ | kg/m³ | Buoyancy calculation |
| 10 | Energy of CGH₂ Tank | $E = V \cdot \rho \cdot 120$ | MJ | Vehicle range |
| 11 | Hydrogen Flame Speed | $S_L = 2.7 - 3.5$ | m/s | Engine timing |
| 12 | Auto Ignition Temperature | $T_{auto} = 571$ | °C | Compression ratio design |
| 13 | Flammability Range | $4\% - 75\%$ | vol. in air | Safety venting |
| 14 | Minimum Ignition Energy | $E_{min} = 0.02$ | mJ | Pre-ignition analysis |
| 15 | Carnot Liquefaction Work | $W \approx 10 - 13$ | kWh/kg | LH₂ cost analysis |
| 16 | Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot BP}{m_f \cdot LHV}$ | % | Performance |
| 17 | Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC) | $BSEC = \dfrac{m_f \cdot LHV}{BP}$ | MJ/kWh | Comparison with gasoline |
| 18 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a,actual}}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot N/2}$ | % | Engine breathing |
| 19 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | — | Lean burn analysis |
| 20 | Energy Density (Mass) | $E_m = 120,000$ | kJ/kg | 2.9× gasoline |
| 21 | Energy Density (Vol, LH₂) | $E_v = 8,500$ | kJ/L | 1/4 of gasoline |
| 22 | Diffusion Coefficient of H₂ | $D = 6.1 \times 10^{-5}$ | m²/s | Mixing analysis |
| 23 | Quenching Distance | $d_q = 0.64$ | mm | Combustion chamber size |
| 24 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = 1 - \dfrac{\sum [Pollutant] \cdot LHV_{pol}}{[Fuel] \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | — | Emissions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the **Combustion Reaction (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O)** and the **stoichiometric AFR = 34.3** perfectly. They appear in 90% of the numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Air Required for Complete Combustion of 1 kg of H₂

This is the foundational derivation needed for **every IC engine performance calculation**.

### Given Data
- Combustion reaction: $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$
- Molecular weight of H₂ = 2.016 g/mol
- Molecular weight of O₂ = 32.00 g/mol
- Molecular weight of N₂ = 28.014 g/mol
- Air composition: 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ (by volume/moles)

### Step 1: Convert Mass to Moles for 1 kg of H₂

$$n_{H_2} = \frac{m_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{1000\ g}{2.016\ g/mol} = 496.03\ mol$$

### Step 2: Apply Stoichiometry to Find Moles of O₂ Required

From the balanced equation: **2 moles H₂ require 1 mole O₂**

$$n_{O_2} = n_{H_2} \times \frac{1}{2} = 496.03 \times 0.5 = 248.02\ mol\ O_2$$

### Step 3: Convert Moles of O₂ to Mass

$$m_{O_2} = n_{O_2} \times M_{O_2} = 248.02 \times 32.00 = 7936.5\ g = 7.94\ kg\ O_2$$

### Step 4: Calculate Mass of N₂ Accompanying O₂ in Air

For every mole of O₂, 79/21 moles of N₂ are present.

$$n_{N_2} = n_{O_2} \times \frac{79}{21} = 248.02 \times 3.762 = 933.0\ mol$$

$$m_{N_2} = 933.0 \times 28.014 = 26,136.4\ g = 26.14\ kg\ N_2$$

### Step 5: Total Mass of Air

$$m_{air} = m_{O_2} + m_{N_2} = 7.94 + 26.14 = 34.08\ kg \approx 34.3\ kg$$

### Step 6: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Mass Basis)

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{34.08}{1} \approx 34.3\ :\ 1$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is **24.6× the AFR of gasoline (14.7:1)**. This means a hydrogen engine must process **2.33 times more air mass** for the same energy output, requiring **larger throttle bodies, intake manifolds, and valves**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: H₂ Production Rate in an Electrolyzer

This is a standard 7-mark derivation.

### Given Data
- Current supplied, $I$ = 1000 A
- Faraday efficiency, $\eta_F$ = 0.95
- Operating voltage, $V$ = 2.0 V
- Moles of electrons per H₂ molecule, $n$ = 2

### Step 1: Theoretical H₂ moles (without losses)

$$n_{H_2,ideal} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I}{n \cdot F}\ per\ second$$

$$\frac{n_{H_2}}{sec} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 96,485} = 5.18 \times 10^{-3}\ mol/s$$

### Step 2: Apply Faraday Efficiency

$$n_{H_2,actual} = 5.18 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.95 = 4.92 \times 10^{-3}\ mol/s$$

### Step 3: Convert to Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 4.92 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016\ g/mol = 9.92 \times 10^{-3}\ g/s$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 35.7\ g/hour = 0.857\ kg/day$$

### Step 4: Electrolyzer Energy Efficiency

**Input Power:** $P_{in} = V \cdot I = 2.0 \times 1000 = 2000\ W$

**Output Chemical Power (LHV basis):**
$$P_{out} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times LHV = 9.92 \times 10^{-6}\ kg/s \times 120 \times 10^6\ J/kg = 1190\ W$$

**Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{electrolyzer} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1190}{2000} = 59.5\%$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Stored in a Hydrogen Cylinder

### Given Data
- Tank volume, $V$ = 100 L = 0.1 m³
- Pressure, $P$ = 700 bar = 70 MPa
- Temperature, $T$ = 298 K
- Gas constant, $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K
- Compressibility factor, $Z$ = 1.45 (at 700 bar, 25 °C)
- $M_{H_2}$ = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol

### Step 1: Moles of H₂ in the Cylinder

$$n = \frac{PV}{ZRT} = \frac{70 \times 10^6 \times 0.1}{1.45 \times 8.314 \times 298}$$

$$n = \frac{7 \times 10^6}{3589.7} = 1950.0\ mol$$

### Step 2: Mass of H₂

$$m_{H_2} = n \times M_{H_2} = 1950 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 3.93\ kg$$

### Step 3: Energy Stored (LHV basis)

$$E_{stored} = 3.93 \times 120 = 471.6\ MJ$$

### Step 4: Equivalent Gasoline Energy

Gasoline LHV = 44 MJ/kg → Equivalent gasoline = 471.6 / 44 = **10.7 kg of gasoline**

> **Conclusion:** A 100 L tank at 700 bar stores the energy of only **10.7 kg (≈14 L) of gasoline** — this is why hydrogen storage is the **#1 engineering challenge** for automotive applications.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC) of Hydrogen Engine

### Given Data
- Brake Power, $BP$ = 50 kW
- Hydrogen flow rate, $\dot{m}_f$ = 3.5 kg/hour

### Step 1: Convert Units

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{3.5}{3600} = 9.72 \times 10^{-4}\ kg/s$$

### Step 2: Apply BSEC Formula

$$BSEC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}{BP} = \frac{9.72 \times 10^{-4} \times 120 \times 10^3}{50}$$

$$BSEC = \frac{116.67}{50} = 2.33\ MJ/kWh$$

### Step 3: Compare with Gasoline

Gasoline BSEC ≈ **8.83 MJ/kWh** at 30% thermal efficiency.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{BSEC_{H_2}}{BSEC_{gasoline}} = \frac{2.33}{8.83} = 0.264$$

> **Conclusion:** Hydrogen engines theoretically need only **26.4% of the energy input** of a gasoline engine to produce the same power — but volumetric flow issues reduce this advantage in practice.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Performance Matrix (Hydrogen vs. Gasoline vs. Diesel)

| Parameter | Gasoline | Diesel | **Hydrogen** | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LHV (MJ/kg)** | 44 | 43 | **120** | Less mass of fuel |
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 750 | 850 | **71 (liquid)** | 10× lower → large tank |
| **Stoich. AFR** | 14.7 | 14.5 | **34.3** | 2.3× more air needed |
| **Flame Speed (m/s)** | 0.4 | 0.3 | **2.7–3.5** | Lean burn possible |
| **Auto-Ignition T (°C)** | 230 | 210 | **571** | Difficult cold start |
| **Flammability (%)** | 1.4–7.6 | 1.0–7.0 | **4.0–75** | Pre-ignition risk |
| **Min. Ignition E (mJ)** | 0.25 | 0.24 | **0.02** | Hot surface backfire |
| **Quenching Dist. (mm)** | 2.0 | 2.5 | **0.64** | Narrow crevices ignite |
| **BTE (%)** | 25–30 | 30–38 | **38–45** | Higher due to lean burn |
| **CO₂ Emission (g/kWh)** | ~270 | ~250 | **~0** | Zero carbon |
| **NOₓ Issue** | Moderate | High | **High (due to high flame T)** | Need EGR/lean burn |
| **Cold Start** | Easy | Easy | **Difficult** | Needs glow plug/HC pilot |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Hydrogen Flame Temperature (Theoretical Adiabatic)

### Given Data
- Reaction: $2H_2 + O_2 + 3.76N_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76N_2$
- Enthalpy of formation of H₂O (vapor) = −241.8 kJ/mol
- LHV of H₂ = 240.4 kJ/mol (per 2 moles) ≈ 120.2 kJ/mol

### Energy Balance
$$m_{H_2} \times LHV = \sum n_i \cdot C_{p,i} \cdot (T_{ad} - T_{ref})$$

**Assuming constant specific heats (approx):**
- $C_{p,H_2O(g)} = 34.0$ J/mol·K
- $C_{p,N_2} = 29.1$ J/mol·K
- Reference: 25 °C = 298 K

$$2 \times 240.4 \times 10^3 = (2 \times 34.0 + 3.76 \times 29.1) \cdot (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$480,800 = (68.0 + 109.4) \cdot (T_{ad} - 298) = 177.4 \cdot (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$T_{ad} - 298 = \frac{480,800}{177.4} = 2710\ K$$

$$\boxed{T_{ad} \approx 2710\ K\ (\approx 2437\ ^\circ C)}$$

> **Insight:** This is **higher than methane (~2223 K)**. The high adiabatic flame temperature is responsible for **NOₓ formation** and **requires lean operation** to keep peak temperatures below 1800 K (NOₓ threshold).

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Green Hydrogen" and explain its production with a balanced equation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Green Hydrogen** is hydrogen produced by the electrolysis of water using **electricity generated from renewable energy sources** (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal) such that the overall production process emits **zero kg of CO₂ per kg of H₂**.
>
> **Production Method:** Electrolysis of water using renewable electricity.
>
> **Balanced Equation:**
>
> $$2H_2O_{(l)} \xrightarrow{Renewable\ Electricity} 2H_2{_{(g)}} + O_2{_{(g)}}$$
>
> **Significance:** Considered the ultimate clean fuel for the hydrogen economy; can replace fossil fuels in IC engines and fuel cells without contributing to greenhouse effect. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for significance)**

---

### Question 2: Why is the storage of hydrogen more difficult than gasoline? List any two reasons. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Hydrogen storage is more difficult than gasoline because:
> 1. **Very Low Volumetric Energy Density:** Liquid hydrogen has only **8.5 MJ/L** vs gasoline at **32 MJ/L** — meaning a hydrogen tank must be **~4× larger** for the same range.
> 2. **Extremely Low Boiling Point (−253 °C):** Requires **cryogenic storage** with vacuum insulation, leading to continuous **boil-off losses (~1% per day)**.
> 3. **Hydrogen Embrittlement:** Causes metal tank fatigue and cracking; requires **expensive composite materials** (Type IV tanks with carbon fiber wrap).
> 4. **High Diffusivity:** Hydrogen molecules are so small they leak through most standard seals and gaskets.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]`**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** process for hydrogen production with balanced equations, operating conditions, and the concept of "Blue Hydrogen." **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 100 L composite cylinder is filled with hydrogen at 700 bar and 25 °C. Calculate (i) the mass of H₂ stored, (ii) the energy equivalent in MJ, and (iii) the equivalent gasoline mass for the same energy. Assume $Z = 1.45$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol, $LHV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg, $LHV_{gasoline} = 44$ MJ/kg, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
SMR is the **catalytic reaction of methane (CH₄) with steam** at high temperature to produce a synthesis gas (syngas) consisting of H₂, CO, and CO₂, which is then purified to obtain high-purity hydrogen.

**Step 1 — Reformer (1.5 marks):**
$$CH_4 + H_2O \xrightarrow{700-1000^\circ C, 15-30\ bar, Ni/Al_2O_3} CO + 3H_2 \quad (\Delta H = +206\ kJ/mol)$$

> The reaction is **endothermic**; heat must be supplied externally (usually by burning some of the methane).

**Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift Reactor (1.5 marks):**
$$CO + H_2O \xrightarrow{350^\circ C, Fe_2O_3/CuO\ catalyst} CO_2 + H_2 \quad (\Delta H = -41\ kJ/mol)$$

> The reaction is **exothermic**; the CO produced is converted to additional H₂ while releasing CO₂.

**Overall Reaction (1 mark):**
$$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$$

**Operating Conditions (1 mark):**
- Temperature: 700–1000 °C (Reformer), 350 °C (Shift Reactor)
- Pressure: 15–30 bar
- Catalysts: Nickel-based, then Iron/Copper
- **Efficiency:** 70–85% (energy basis)

**Blue Hydrogen Concept (1 mark):**
When the CO₂ released is **captured using amine-based absorption (e.g., MEA — Monoethanolamine) and sequestered underground** (CCS — Carbon Capture and Storage), the resulting hydrogen is termed **"Blue Hydrogen"** because the production process becomes net-zero CO₂ even though methane is used as the feedstock.

---

#### Part (b) — Storage Cylinder Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:** $V = 100\ L = 0.1\ m^3$, $P = 700\ bar = 70 \times 10^6\ Pa$, $T = 25\ °C = 298\ K$, $Z = 1.45$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ kg/mol$

**Step 1: Moles of H₂ (Real Gas Equation)** (2 marks)
$$n = \frac{PV}{ZRT} = \frac{70 \times 10^6 \times 0.1}{1.45 \times 8.314 \times 298}$$

$$n = \frac{7 \times 10^6}{3590.2} = 1949.8 \approx 1950\ mol$$

**Step 2: Mass of H₂ Stored** (1.5 marks)
$$m_{H_2} = n \times M_{H_2} = 1950 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 3.93\ kg$$

**Step 3: Energy Stored in MJ (LHV basis)** (1.5 marks)
$$E = m_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2} = 3.93 \times 120 = 471.6\ MJ$$

**Step 4: Equivalent Gasoline Mass** (2 marks)
$$m_{gasoline} = \frac{E}{LHV_{gasoline}} = \frac{471.6}{44} = 10.72\ kg$$

**Final Result:** A 100 L composite tank at 700 bar stores only **3.93 kg of H₂**, equivalent in energy to just **10.72 kg (~14.3 liters) of gasoline** — illustrating the critical volume challenge of hydrogen storage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper Pattern]`**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the various **hydrogen induction techniques in IC engines**, comparing carburetion, port injection, and direct injection. State the advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A hydrogen-fueled engine develops **50 kW of brake power** while consuming **3.6 kg/h of hydrogen**. Calculate (i) the Brake Thermal Efficiency, (ii) the Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC), and (iii) compare the BSEC with a gasoline engine whose BSEC is **8.83 MJ/kWh**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Hydrogen Induction Techniques [7 marks]

Hydrogen can be inducted into an IC engine cylinder using **four primary techniques**:

**1. Carburetion (1.5 marks)**
- **Mechanism:** A simple venture in the intake manifold uses the **vacuum created by the throttle** to draw hydrogen gas into the air stream.
- **Advantage:** Simplest and cheapest method; no high-pressure pump required.
- **Disadvantage:** Poor control of mixture ratio; risk of backfire into the intake manifold; not suitable for modern electronic control.

**2. Port Fuel Injection (PFI) (1.5 marks)**
- **Mechanism:** A **low-pressure solenoid injector** (typically 1–4 bar) sprays H₂ into the intake port just upstream of the intake valve.
- **Advantage:** Better air-fuel ratio control; manifold backfire reduced; compatible with existing electronic control units.
- **Disadvantage:** Some H₂ can still leak past the intake valve during the closed period, causing backfire.

**3. Direct Injection (DI) (2 marks)**
- **Mechanism:** High-pressure (100–300 bar) injectors spray H₂ **directly into the cylinder** during the compression stroke, similar to modern GDI gasoline engines.
- **Advantage:** **Eliminates backfire** completely (no H₂ in the intake manifold); highest volumetric efficiency; best power output.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires expensive high-pressure H₂ pumps and injectors; sealing challenges at high pressure.

**4. Fumigation (1 mark)**
- **Mechanism:** H₂ is mixed with intake air through a **diffuser/nozzle** in the intake manifold; simple, used in dual-fuel (H₂ + Diesel) engines where diesel acts as the pilot.
- **Advantage:** Easy retrofit on existing diesel engines.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited control; high HC emissions due to incomplete H₂ mixing.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Carburetion | Port Injection | Direct Injection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Backfire Risk | High | Medium | **Negligible** |
| Power Output | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Cost | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Cold Start | Hard | Hard | **Easier** |

---

#### Part (b) — Performance Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:** $BP = 50\ kW$, $\dot{m}_f = 3.6\ kg/h = 10^{-3}\ kg/s$, $LHV_{H_2} = 120\ MJ/kg$

**Step 1: Heat Input Rate (1.5 marks)**
$$Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times LHV = 10^{-3} \times 120 \times 10^3\ W = 120\ kW$$

**Step 2: Brake Thermal Efficiency (2 marks)**
$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{50}{120} \times 100 = 41.67\%$$

**Step 3: BSEC of Hydrogen Engine (1.5 marks)**
$$BSEC_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}{BP} = \frac{10^{-3} \times 120 \times 10^3}{50} = 2.4\ MJ/kWh$$

**Step 4: Comparison with Gasoline (2 marks)**
$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{BSEC_{H_2}}{BSEC_{gasoline}} = \frac{2.4}{8.83} = 0.272$$

$$\text{Energy saved} = (1 - 0.272) \times 100 = 72.8\%$$

**Conclusion:** The hydrogen engine consumes only **27.2% of the energy** that a gasoline engine requires to produce the same brake power. This is a **massive efficiency advantage**, confirming hydrogen as a premium future fuel.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Real Gas Factor (Z):** At 700 bar, hydrogen deviates significantly from ideal gas. Using $Z = 1.0$ will give wrong mass; **always use $Z = 1.45$** for 700 bar, 25 °C conditions.
> 2. **Using LHV vs HHV Confusion:** For IC engine calculations, **always use LHV (120 MJ/kg)**, not HHV (142 MJ/kg), because exhaust water leaves as vapor.
> 3. **Mixing up Stoichiometric AFR:** Hydrogen = **34.3** (mass basis), Methanol = 6.4, Gasoline = 14.7. **Do not confuse.**
> 4. **Missing Units in Final Answer:** Always report efficiency in **%**, BSEC in **MJ/kWh**, and energy in **MJ**.
> 5. **Confusing Flame Speed with Flame Temperature:** Flame speed (2.7 m/s) is the propagation velocity, NOT the adiabatic flame temperature (~2710 K).
> 6. **Direct vs Indirect Injection:** Direct injection **eliminates backfire**; mentioning this specifically in induction technique answers gets full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FACTS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAM:**

- [x] **H₂ LHV = 120 MJ/kg**; **HHV = 142 MJ/kg**; **AFR = 34.3:1** (mass basis)
- [x] **Density at STP = 0.0899 kg/m³**; **Liquid density = 71 kg/m³**
- [x] **Auto-ignition T = 571 °C**; **Flame speed = 2.7–3.5 m/s**; **Flammability 4–75%**
- [x] **Quenching distance = 0.64 mm** (allows thin crevices → backfire risk)
- [x] **Min. ignition energy = 0.02 mJ** (10× easier to ignite than gasoline)
- [x] **Green H₂** = Electrolysis + renewable electricity → Zero CO₂
- [x] **Blue H₂** = SMR + Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS)
- [x] **SMR reaction:** $CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$ (Ni catalyst, 700–1000 °C)
- [x] **Electrolysis:** $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$ (min. voltage 1.23 V, practical 1.8–2.5 V)
- [x] **Storage densities:** CGH₂ @ 700 bar = **40 kg/m³**; LH₂ = **71 kg/m³**; Metal hydrides = **50–150 g/kg**
- [x] **Liquefaction work:** ~10–13 kWh/kg (≈35% of LHV energy)
- [x] **Faraday's Law:** $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{M_{H_2} \cdot I \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}$ (n = 2 electrons)
- [x] **3 Induction methods:** Carburetion, Port Injection, Direct Injection
- [x] **Direct Injection** = best (no backfire, high power)
- [x] **Engine mods:** Inconel valves, ↑CR (12–15:1), ↓ crevice volume, water injection for NOₓ
- [x] **Performance:** BTE = 38–45% (higher than gasoline due to lean burn + fast flame)
- [x] **Emissions:** **Zero CO₂, near-zero CO, near-zero HC**; **NOₓ is the main issue** (due to high flame T ≈ 2710 K)
- [x] **Safety:** Hydrogen is **invisible when burning**; **lighter than air** (rises); **embrittles metals**
- [x] **Mitigation:** Flame arrestors, IR detectors, SS316L piping, vacuum-insulated LH₂ tanks, leak sensors
- [x] **Combustion equation:** $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ (the ONLY major product)

> **Top 5 Traps in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. "Is hydrogen a renewable fuel?" → **NO**, it is an **energy carrier**; only **Green H₂** is renewable if produced from solar/wind.
> 2. "Does H₂ produce CO₂ when burned?" → **No**, zero direct CO₂.
> 3. "Why lean burn in H₂ engine?" → To reduce **NOₓ** and improve **efficiency**.
> 4. "Why cold start is hard?" → High **auto-ignition T (571 °C)**; needs pilot fuel or glow plug.
> 5. "What is Hydrogen Embrittlement?" → H atoms diffuse into metal grain boundaries, recombine to H₂, cause **internal cracking** in steel.
