# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV)?

A **Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV)** is a zero-emission electric vehicle that derives its motive power from a **proton-exchange-membrane (PEM) fuel cell stack**, which electrochemically converts compressed hydrogen (H₂) gas and atmospheric oxygen (O₂) directly into electricity, water, and heat. Unlike a battery-electric vehicle, an FCV does not store electricity; it **generates** electricity onboard continuously as long as hydrogen is supplied, giving it a refuelling profile similar to a conventional petrol/diesel car while emitting only water vapour from the tailpipe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** A Fuel Cell Vehicle is an electric vehicle that uses a **fuel cell stack** as the primary power source, often hybridised with a **battery or supercapacitor** to handle transient loads. The fuel cell stack converts chemical energy of H₂ into electrical energy through an electrochemical reaction, producing **water as the only by-product**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of energy conversion in an FCV:**
> Chemical Energy (H₂) → Electrical Energy (Fuel Cell) → Mechanical Energy (Traction Motor) → Kinetic Energy (Vehicle motion)

---

## 1.2 What is a Fuel Cell?

A **fuel cell** is an **electrochemical energy-conversion device** that continuously converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) into **direct-current (DC) electrical energy**, water, and heat, as long as reactants are supplied. It is *not* a heat engine — therefore it is **not limited by the Carnot efficiency**, and its theoretical efficiency (≈ 83 % for H₂/O₂ at 25 °C, HHV basis) far exceeds that of an internal combustion engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single fuel cell produces only **0.6 V to 1.0 V** under load. To power a vehicle (≈ 300–400 V traction bus), **hundreds of cells are stacked in series** to form a *fuel cell stack*, giving the stack its name.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Battery That Never Runs Out"

Imagine a **conventional rechargeable battery**: it stores energy inside sealed plates, and once the chemicals are used up, you must plug it in for hours to refill.

Now imagine a **transparent battery**:
- The plates are still there, separated by an electrolyte.
- But instead of being sealed, two hoses are connected: one feeding **hydrogen gas**, the other feeding **air**.
- As long as gas flows in, electricity flows out — and the only "exhaust" is pure water dripping from the air-side hose.
- You never "recharge" the battery; you simply **refuel the gas tank** in **3–5 minutes**, just like a petrol car.

That transparent, gas-fed battery **is a fuel cell**. The vehicle wrapping it — with its hydrogen tank, power electronics, electric traction motor, and battery buffer — is the **Fuel Cell Vehicle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Frequently Used in FCV Calculations:**
> - Faraday's constant: **F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹**
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
> - Standard cell potential for H₂/O₂: **E° = 1.229 V** (at 25 °C, 1 atm, liquid water product)
> - Lower Heating Value of H₂: **LHV = 33.33 kWh/kg = 120 MJ/kg**
> - Higher Heating Value of H₂: **HHV = 39.4 kWh/kg = 142 MJ/kg**

---

## 1.4 The Six Major Fuel Cell Types — Bird's-Eye View

Fuel cells are classified by their **electrolyte material**, which dictates the operating temperature, the ion transported, and therefore the application suitability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Six Fuel Cell Types to be Studied:**
> 1. **AFC** — Alkaline Fuel Cell
> 2. **PEMFC** — Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell
> 3. **DMFC** — Direct Methanol Fuel Cell
> 4. **PAFC** — Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell
> 5. **MCFC** — Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell
> 6. **SOFC** — Solid Oxide Fuel Cell

A mnemonic: **"Alkaline People Don't Prefer Molten Salty"** → **A**FC, **P**EM, **D**MFC, **P**AFC, **M**CFC, **S**OFC.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Layout of a Fuel Cell Vehicle — Complete Architecture

A modern FCV integrates four major sub-systems, often described in KTU textbooks as the **"4-Pillars of an FCV"**:

### Pillar 1 — Hydrogen Storage Subsystem
- **Type- IV composite tanks** (carbon-fibre overwrap on a polymer liner) storing H₂ at **350 bar (5,000 psi)** or **700 bar (10,000 psi)**.
- Tank capacity typically **4–6 kg H₂**, giving a driving range of **500–650 km**.
- A **pressure regulator** steps the pressure down to **10–30 bar** for the stack.
- **Safety components**: Thermal Pressure Relief Device (TPRD), Excess Flow Valve, solenoid shut-off.

### Pillar 2 — Fuel Cell Stack (Power Generation)
- **PEM fuel cell stack** (most common in automotive — low temperature, fast start-up).
- Power output: **80–120 kW** for a passenger car; up to **300 kW** for heavy trucks/buses.
- Operates at **60–80 °C** with a polymer membrane (e.g., Nafion™) as electrolyte.
- **BoP (Balance of Plant)** supporting the stack:
  - Air compressor / blower
  - Hydrogen recirculation blower
  - Humidifier (membrane requires hydration)
  - Water separator
  - Coolant pump + radiator
  - Stack voltage monitoring & individual cell voltage monitors (CVM)

### Pillar 3 — Electrical Storage & Power Electronics
- **Li-ion battery pack** (≈ 1–2 kWh) or **supercapacitor module** acts as:
  - *Load leveller* (absorbs regenerative braking energy).
  - *Peak power buffer* (delivers boost during acceleration).
  - *Cold-start assist* for the fuel cell.
- **DC-DC converter** (boost type) raises the stack's variable voltage (≈ 250–450 V) to a stable traction bus voltage.
- **Traction inverter** converts DC to 3-phase AC for the drive motor.
- **Battery Management System (BMS)** controls state-of-charge (SOC) of the buffer battery.

### Pillar 4 — Electric Drivetrain (Traction)
- **Permanent-magnet synchronous motor (PMSM)** or induction motor.
- Power rating: **100–200 kW** (passenger car); single-speed or two-speed reduction gearbox.
- **Regenerative braking** charges the buffer battery during deceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Subsystems:** Cabin HVAC (uses waste heat from the stack), power steering (electro-hydraulic or fully electric), vacuum pump (for brake booster), 12 V DC-DC converter for auxiliary loads.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Analysis of Each Fuel Cell Type

### 2.2.1 Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)
- **Electrolyte:** Aqueous **KOH** solution (30–45 % by weight) held in a porous matrix (asbestos historically; modern versions use a polymer gel).
- **Operating Temperature:** **60–200 °C** (low-temperature AFC: 60–120 °C; high-temperature AFC: up to 200 °C).
- **Mobile Ion:** **OH⁻** (hydroxide ion) — moves from cathode to anode.
- **Half-reactions:**
  - Anode: H₂ + 2 OH⁻ → 2 H₂O + 2 e⁻
  - Cathode: ½ O₂ + H₂O + 2 e⁻ → 2 OH⁻
  - Net: H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O
- **Charge Carrier:** OH⁻ (anionic conduction).
- **Catalyst:** Non-precious metals such as **Nickel (Ni)**, silver (Ag) — much cheaper than platinum, giving AFC the **lowest cost per kW** of all fuel cell types.
- **Efficiency:** 60–70 % electrical; up to 85 % with CHP.
- **Key Disadvantage:** **CO₂ poisoning** — atmospheric CO₂ reacts with KOH to form K₂CO₃, which clogs the electrolyte pores. Requires **pure O₂** (not air) and ultra-pure H₂.
- **Applications:** **Space programs** (Apollo, Space Shuttle Orbiter), military submarines.

### 2.2.2 Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)
- **Electrolyte:** Solid **perfluorinated sulfonic-acid polymer membrane** (e.g., **Nafion™ 117**).
- **Operating Temperature:** **50–100 °C** (typically 60–80 °C).
- **Mobile Ion:** **H⁺** (proton) — moves from anode to cathode.
- **Half-reactions:**
  - Anode: H₂ → 2 H⁺ + 2 e⁻
  - Cathode: ½ O₂ + 2 H⁺ + 2 e⁻ → H₂O
  - Net: H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O
- **Charge Carrier:** H⁺ (cationic conduction).
- **Catalyst:** **Platinum (Pt)** nanoparticles (≈ 0.1–0.4 mg/cm² loading) on carbon support.
- **Membrane Hydration:** Critical — Nafion conducts H⁺ only when hydrated. **Humidification of inlet gases is mandatory**.
- **Efficiency:** 50–60 % electrical; up to 85 % with CHP.
- **Key Advantages:** Fast start-up (suitable for vehicles), high power density (1–2 kW/L, 0.5–1 kW/kg), solid electrolyte (no leakage).
- **Key Disadvantages:** Expensive Pt catalyst; intolerant to CO (>10 ppm) — CO poisons Pt.
- **Applications:** **Automotive FCVs** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity), backup power, portable power.

### 2.2.3 Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC)
- **Electrolyte:** Same **PEM membrane** as PEMFC (e.g., Nafion).
- **Operating Temperature:** **60–200 °C** (typically 60–130 °C).
- **Fuel:** **Liquid methanol** (CH₃OH) mixed with water — fed directly at the anode. **No reformer required** — methanol is oxidised directly.
- **Mobile Ion:** **H⁺** (proton).
- **Half-reactions:**
  - Anode: CH₃OH + H₂O → CO₂ + 6 H⁺ + 6 e⁻
  - Cathode: 3/2 O₂ + 6 H⁺ + 6 e⁻ → 3 H₂O
  - Net: CH₃OH + 3/2 O₂ → CO₂ + 2 H₂O
- **Charge Carrier:** H⁺.
- **Catalyst:** Pt-Ru alloy at the anode (Ru helps tolerate CO intermediate); Pt at the cathode.
- **Efficiency:** **Low** — only 20–30 % electrical (kinetics of methanol oxidation are sluggish; methanol crossover through the membrane reduces efficiency).
- **Key Advantage:** **High energy density of liquid methanol** (6 kWh/kg) → no pressurised tank; fuel is easy to handle and transport.
- **Key Disadvantages:** Methanol crossover reduces performance; CO₂ emitted (rejected as a gas at the anode); lower power density.
- **Applications:** Portable electronics (laptops, military field radios), small forklifts, backup power for remote sites.

### 2.2.4 Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC)
- **Electrolyte:** **Concentrated phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄, ~100 %)** held in a **silicon carbide (SiC) matrix**.
- **Operating Temperature:** **150–200 °C** (typically 180–200 °C).
- **Mobile Ion:** **H⁺** (proton).
- **Half-reactions:** Same as PEMFC (H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O).
- **Catalyst:** Pt (slightly higher loading than PEMFC — ≈ 0.5 mg/cm², because higher temperature gives more tolerance to CO, allowing reformer-fed natural gas).
- **Charge Carrier:** H⁺.
- **Efficiency:** 40–45 % electrical; up to **85–90 % with CHP** (combined heat and power).
- **Key Advantage:** More tolerant to **CO (up to ~1.5 %)** than PEMFC; waste heat is high-quality (suitable for cogeneration).
- **Key Disadvantages:** Lower power density than PEMFC; phosphoric acid is corrosive; large and heavy.
- **Applications:** **Stationary CHP systems** (200 kW to 11 MW), hospitals, hotels, industrial sites. (Commercial: ONSI PC-25, Fuji Electric FP-100).

### 2.2.5 Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)
- **Electrolyte:** **Molten mixture of lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃) and potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃)** held in a porous **LiAlO₂** matrix.
- **Operating Temperature:** **600–700 °C** (typically 650 °C).
- **Mobile Ion:** **CO₃²⁻** (carbonate ion) — moves from cathode to anode.
- **Half-reactions:**
  - Anode: H₂ + CO₃²⁻ → H₂O + CO₂ + 2 e⁻
  - Cathode: ½ O₂ + CO₂ + 2 e⁻ → CO₃²⁻
  - Net: H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O
- **Charge Carrier:** CO₃²⁻.
- **Catalyst:** **Nickel** (Ni) — no precious metals needed. Anode is porous Ni-Cr, cathode is porous NiO.
- **CO₂ Requirement:** **CO₂ must be supplied at the cathode** (recycled from anode exhaust) to form the carbonate ion.
- **Efficiency:** 50–60 % electrical; up to **85 % with CHP/CHHP** (combined heat, hydrogen, and power).
- **Key Advantage:** Can use **CO directly as fuel** (CO + CO₃²⁻ → 2 CO₂ + 2 e⁻); can internally reform **natural gas / methane / coal gas** at 650 °C — no external reformer.
- **Key Disadvantages:** Very slow start-up (hours); electrolyte is corrosive liquid; high-temperature materials are expensive; cathode flooding by molten electrolyte.
- **Applications:** **Large-scale stationary power** (250 kW to 10 MW), industrial power plants.

### 2.2.6 Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)
- **Electrolyte:** **Solid ceramic** — **Yttria-Stabilised Zirconia (YSZ)**, an O²⁻ conductor.
- **Operating Temperature:** **800–1000 °C** (typically 850–900 °C); intermediate-temperature SOFCs (IT-SOFCs) operate at 500–750 °C.
- **Mobile Ion:** **O²⁻** (oxide ion) — moves from cathode to anode.
- **Half-reactions:**
  - Anode: H₂ + O²⁻ → H₂O + 2 e⁻ (and similarly: CO + O²⁻ → CO₂ + 2 e⁻)
  - Cathode: ½ O₂ + 2 e⁻ → O²⁻
  - Net: H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O
- **Charge Carrier:** O²⁻.
- **Catalyst:** **Perovskite oxides** (e.g., Lanthanum Strontium Manganite, LSM) at the cathode; Ni-YSZ cermet at the anode. **No precious metals**.
- **Efficiency:** 50–60 % electrical; up to **85–90 % with CHP/GT** (combined with a gas turbine).
- **Key Advantage:** Highest electrical efficiency of all fuel cell types when combined with a gas turbine (hybrid SOFC-GT cycle can reach **70 %**); fuel-flexible (H₂, CO, CH₄, hydrocarbons); solid electrolyte (no leakage, no corrosion).
- **Key Disadvantages:** Very slow start-up (hours to reach operating temp.); thermal cycling degrades ceramic components; high cost of high-temperature materials.
- **Applications:** **Stationary power generation** (kW to multi-MW), residential micro-CHP (Bloom Energy Server), auxiliary power units (APU) for heavy trucks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the temperature differences?** The ionic conductivity of the electrolyte dictates the operating temperature:
> - Aqueous KOH → ambient–200 °C
> - Hydrated polymer (Nafion) → 60–80 °C
> - Phosphoric acid → 150–200 °C
> - Molten carbonates → 650 °C
> - YSZ ceramic → 800–1000 °C

| Electrical Efficiency | 60–70 % | 50–60 % | 20–30 % | 40–45 % | 50–60 % | 50–60 % |

| Primary Application | Space, military | Automotive, portable | Portable electronics | Stationary CHP | Large stationary | Stationary, APU |
| CO Tolerance | None | <10 ppm | N/A | ~1.5 % | Fuel (uses CO) | Fuel (uses CO) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Thermodynamics of Fuel Cells**

| Equation | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$ | Open-circuit cell voltage under non-standard conditions |
| Standard Nernst (H₂/O₂, 25 °C) | $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V}$ (liquid water) ; $E^{\circ} = 1.18 \text{ V}$ (vapour) | Theoretical OCV at STP |
| Electrical Efficiency (HHV) | $\eta_{el} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} \times 100 = 83 \%$ | Max theoretical H₂ FC efficiency |
| Electrical Efficiency (LHV) | $\eta_{el} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{LHV_{H_2}} \approx 94.5 \%$ | Max practical efficiency at high T |
| Voltage Efficiency | $\eta_{V} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{Nernst}}$ | Ratio of actual to thermodynamic voltage |
| Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Upper bound for heat engines (NOT for FCs) |
| Gibbs Free Energy (H₂) | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (25 °C, liq. water) | Electrical work available per mole H₂ |
| Enthalpy of Combustion (H₂) | $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (HHV) ; $-241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (LHV) | Total chemical energy released per mole |
| Stack Voltage | $V_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}$ | Total stack voltage (cells in series) |
| Stack Power | $P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack}$ | Electrical output of the stack |
| Energy Density (H₂, 700 bar) | $E_{d} \approx 4.5 \text{ kWh/kg (tank system)}$ | Practical on-board H₂ energy density |
| Faraday's First Law | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of substance reacted electrochemically |
| Fuel Consumption Rate | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{stack}}{\eta_{el} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}$ | H₂ mass flow rate (kg/s) for a given power |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder (KTU Convention):**
> - Pressure in **atm** (for Nernst) or **bar** (for tank).
> - Temperature in **K (Kelvin)** for all gas-law and Nernst equations.
> - n = number of electrons transferred per mole of fuel (= 2 for H₂).
> - All voltages in **Volts (V)**; currents in **Amperes (A)**; power in **Watts (W)** or **kW**.

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for an H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell

The Nernst equation gives the **thermodynamically reversible open-circuit voltage** of a fuel cell as a function of reactant partial pressures, temperature, and product conditions. The cell reaction is:

$$H_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$$

For a general electrochemical reaction: $\sum_i \nu_i M_i = 0$ (where $\nu_i$ is the stoichiometric coefficient, negative for reactants, positive for products), the change in Gibbs free energy at non-standard conditions is given by:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**. For our cell reaction, $Q$ takes the form:

$$Q = \dfrac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{0.5}}$$

For ideal gases, activities equal partial pressures (in atm); for liquid water, activity = 1.

Substituting into the Gibbs relation:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}} \right)$$

The electrical work done by the cell equals $-nFE$ (negative because the system does work on the surroundings). At equilibrium (reversible), $-nFE = \Delta G$, therefore:

$$-nFE = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}} \right)$$

Dividing both sides by $-nF$:

$$E = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}} \right)$$

By definition, the standard cell potential $E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$. Using the log identity $-\ln(A/B) = \ln(B/A)$:

$$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Nernst Equation for the H₂/O₂ fuel cell**. At standard temperature and pressure with liquid water product, $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V and the pressure terms all equal 1, so $E = 1.229$ V. This is the *theoretical maximum* open-circuit voltage of a single H₂/O₂ cell.

### 3.1.1 Worked Example — Nernst Voltage Under Non-Standard Conditions

**Problem:** A PEM fuel cell operates at 80 °C with $P_{H_2} = 1.2 \text{ atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \text{ atm}$ (air at 1 atm), and product water in vapour form. Calculate the Nernst voltage.

**Given:**
- $T = 80\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 353$ K
- $n = 2$ electrons
- $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
- $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹
- For vapour water product at 80 °C, $E^{\circ} \approx 1.18$ V
- $P_{H_2O}$ at 80 °C = saturation pressure = 0.467 atm

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the Nernst term $\dfrac{RT}{nF}$:

$$\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \dfrac{2{,}934.84}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

Step 2 — Compute the logarithm argument:

$$\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times (0.21)^{0.5}}{0.467} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.4583}{0.467} = \dfrac{0.5499}{0.467} = 1.1776$$

Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(1.1776) = 0.1635$$

Step 4 — Substitute into the Nernst equation:

$$\begin{aligned} E &= E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} \right) \\ E &= 1.18 + (0.01521) \times (0.1635) \\ E &= 1.18 + 0.002487 \\ E &= 1.1825 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The actual open-circuit voltage (~1.18 V) is slightly *higher* than the standard value because the H₂ partial pressure is above 1 atm. In practice, the OCV of a real PEM cell is closer to 0.95–1.05 V due to activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Theoretical Efficiency of a Fuel Cell

The maximum (reversible) electrical efficiency of an electrochemical converter is the ratio of the **Gibbs free energy change** to the **enthalpy change** of the reaction.

For the H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O(l) reaction at 25 °C, 1 atm:
- $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2$ kJ mol⁻¹ (electrical work obtainable)
- $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8$ kJ mol⁻¹ (total chemical energy released — HHV)

The maximum efficiency (HHV basis) is:

$$\begin{aligned} \eta_{max,HHV} &= \dfrac{\vert \Delta G^{\circ} \vert}{\vert \Delta H^{\circ} \vert} \\ &= \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} \\ &= 0.830 \end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max,HHV} = 83.0\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this not 100 %?** Even in an *ideal* fuel cell, some of the reaction enthalpy is *always* released as **heat** — the term $T\Delta S$. For H₂/O₂ at 25 °C, $\Delta S < 0$ (gas moles decrease from 1.5 to 0 if water is liquid), so $-T\Delta S > 0$, meaning heat is generated. This is the fundamental reason a fuel cell *always* produces waste heat, no matter how perfectly it is built.

If we use the **LHV** of H₂ (water as vapour) — $\Delta H_{LHV} = 241.8$ kJ mol⁻¹ — and the corresponding $\Delta G = 228.6$ kJ mol⁻¹:

$$\eta_{max,LHV} = \dfrac{228.6}{241.8} = 0.945 = 94.5\%$$

### 3.2.1 Comparison with Carnot Efficiency

For a heat engine operating between the same $T_{hot} = 1000$ K and $T_{cold} = 300$ K:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{300}{1000} = 0.70 = 70\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fuel cell (83–94.5 %) **exceeds** the Carnot limit (70 %) of a heat engine operating between similar temperatures — because a fuel cell converts chemical energy **directly** to electricity, bypassing the heat-to-work step. This is the *fundamental* thermodynamic advantage of FCs.

---

## 3.3 Stack Sizing Calculation — Worked Analytical Problem

**Problem:** A passenger car requires a traction power of 90 kW. The fuel cell stack consists of PEM cells, each operating at 0.65 V under load. The design stack current is 200 A. Calculate:
(a) Number of cells required
(b) Active area per cell (given current density 1.0 A/cm²)
(c) H₂ consumption rate (kg/h) at this power output (assume 50 % overall efficiency)

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Number of cells:**
Each cell gives 0.65 V at 200 A, so each cell power = $0.65 \times 200 = 130$ W.

$$N_{cells} = \dfrac{P_{stack}}{P_{cell}} = \dfrac{90{,}000 \text{ W}}{130 \text{ W}} = 692.3 \approx \mathbf{693 \text{ cells}}$$

Alternatively, the stack voltage needed (assume traction bus = 450 V DC):

$$N_{cells} = \dfrac{V_{stack}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{450}{0.65} = 692.3 \approx \mathbf{693 \text{ cells}}$$

**Part (b) — Active area per cell:**

$$A_{cell} = \dfrac{I_{stack}}{j} = \dfrac{200 \text{ A}}{1.0 \text{ A/cm}^2} = \mathbf{200 \text{ cm}^2}$$

**Part (c) — H₂ consumption rate:**

Chemical power required:

$$P_{chem} = \dfrac{P_{stack}}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{90}{0.50} = 180 \text{ kW}$$

Using LHV of H₂ = 33.33 kWh/kg:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{180 \text{ kW}}{33.33 \text{ kWh/kg}} = 5.4 \text{ kg/h} = \mathbf{5.4 \text{ kg/h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At 5.4 kg/h, with a 5 kg tank, the car can run ≈ 55 minutes at *full 90 kW* power. In real-world driving (average power ≈ 15 kW), the range becomes ≈ 3 hours or **~250 km** per fill — but modern 700-bar tanks with 5–6 kg H₂ push this to **500–650 km**.

---

## 3.4 Polarisation Curve — Performance Characterisation

The **polarisation curve** (V vs. I or V vs. current density) is the primary performance map of any fuel cell. The actual cell voltage is:

$$V_{cell}(j) = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act}(j) - \eta_{ohmic}(j) - \eta_{conc}(j)$$

where the three **overpotential losses** are:

1. **Activation Loss** (η_act) — slow electrode kinetics, especially the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Modelled by the Tafel equation:

$$\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \left( \dfrac{j}{j_0} \right)$$

where $\alpha$ is the charge-transfer coefficient (≈ 0.5 for Pt) and $j_0$ is the exchange current density (≈ 10⁻⁹ A/cm² for ORR).

2. **Ohmic Loss** (η_ohmic) — ionic resistance of the membrane + electronic resistance of electrodes, bipolar plates, and contacts. Linear in current:

$$\eta_{ohmic} = j \cdot ASR$$

where **ASR** (Area-Specific Resistance) is in Ω·cm² (typical Nafion 117: ASR ≈ 0.05–0.15 Ω·cm² at full hydration).

3. **Concentration Loss** (η_conc) — mass-transport limitation at high current. Modelled as:

$$\eta_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( 1 - \dfrac{j}{j_L} \right)$$

where $j_L$ is the **limiting current density** (where reactant supply collapses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw/sketch the polarisation curve, mark the **three loss regions** on the graph and identify where each dominates. Activation loss dominates at **low current**; ohmic loss dominates in the **linear mid-range**; concentration loss dominates at **high current** near $j_L$.
