# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fuel Cell Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[H2 Storage Tank 700 bar] --> B[Pressure Regulator 10-30 bar]
    B --> C[Humidifier]
    C --> D[Fuel Cell Stack PEM]
    E[Air Compressor] --> F[Air Filter and Humidifier]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Coolant Loop and Radiator]
    D --> H[DC-DC Boost Converter]
    I[Li-ion Buffer Battery 1 to 2 kWh] --> H
    H --> J[DC Traction Bus 300 to 450 V]
    J --> K[Traction Inverter DC to AC]
    K --> L[PMSM Traction Motor]
    L --> M[Transmission and Wheels]
    J --> N[Regenerative Braking Interface]
    N --> I
    D --> O[Water and Heat Exhaust]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Hydrogen flows from the high-pressure tank (left) through a regulator and humidifier into the anode side of the stack. Air enters through a compressor and humidifier into the cathode side. The stack produces DC electricity, which is boosted by the DC-DC converter (assisted by the buffer battery) to a stable traction bus voltage, inverted to 3-phase AC, and fed to the traction motor that drives the wheels. Heat and product water are exhausted via the coolant loop.

---

## 4.2 Single-Cell Operation of a PEM Fuel Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[H2 In] --> B[Anode Flow Channel]
    B --> C[Anode Catalyst Layer Pt on C]
    C --> D[PEM Membrane Nafion]
    D --> E[Cathode Catalyst Layer Pt on C]
    E --> F[Cathode Flow Channel]
    F --> G[O2 In from Air]
    H[H2O Out] --> F
    I[e Out External Circuit] --> C
    C --> J[H plus migrates through membrane]
    J --> D
    D --> E
    E --> K[e plus H2O produced at cathode]
    K --> I
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electron flow vs. ion flow:** Electrons (e⁻) travel through the **external circuit** (doing useful work), while protons (H⁺) travel **through the membrane** (from anode to cathode). The two flows are equal in magnitude but spatially separated — this separation is what makes a fuel cell work.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — All Six Fuel Cell Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LT ["LOW TEMPERATURE 60 to 200 C"]
        AFC["AFC - KOH electrolyte - OH minus ion"]
        PEM["PEMFC - Nafion membrane - H plus ion"]
        DMF["DMFC - Nafion membrane - CH3OH fuel"]
    end
    subgraph MT ["MEDIUM TEMPERATURE 150 to 250 C"]
        PAF["PAFC - H3PO4 - H plus ion"]
    end
    subgraph HT ["HIGH TEMPERATURE 600 to 1000 C"]
        MCF["MCFC - Molten Carbonate - CO3 2- ion"]
        SOF["SOFC - YSZ Ceramic - O 2- ion"]
    end
    LT --> MT
    MT --> HT
    AFC --> APP1["Applications - Space and Military"]
    PEM --> APP2["Applications - Automotive FCVs"]
    DMF --> APP3["Applications - Portable Electronics"]
    PAF --> APP4["Applications - Stationary CHP"]
    MCF --> APP5["Applications - Large Stationary Power"]
    SOF --> APP6["Applications - Stationary and APU"]
```

---

## 4.4 Hydrogen Energy Flow Inside an FCV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressed H2 Tank] --> B[Stack OCV 1.18 V per cell]
    B --> C[Operating Voltage 0.6 to 0.7 V per cell]
    C --> D[Stack Power 80 to 120 kW]
    D --> E[DC-DC Boost to 400 V bus]
    E --> F[Inverter 3 phase AC]
    F --> G[Motor Mechanical Output]
    G --> H[Wheels Kinetic Energy]
    D --> I[Waste Heat via Coolant]
    I --> J[Cabin HVAC Cabin Heating]
    I --> K[Radiator Heat Rejection]
    C --> L[Exhaust H2O vapour only]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short Answer (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3]**
*List the six major types of fuel cells and identify the electrolyte and mobile ion used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — full credit allocation):**

| # | Fuel Cell | Electrolyte | Mobile Ion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC) | Aqueous KOH | OH⁻ |
| 2 | Proton Exchange Membrane FC (PEMFC) | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | H⁺ |
| 3 | Direct Methanol FC (DMFC) | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | H⁺ |
| 4 | Phosphoric Acid FC (PAFC) | Concentrated H₃PO₄ | H⁺ |
| 5 | Molten Carbonate FC (MCFC) | Molten Li/K carbonates | CO₃²⁻ |
| 6 | Solid Oxide FC (SOFC) | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilised Zirconia) | O²⁻ |

> Allocation: (1 mark) correct listing of 6 types; (1 mark) correct electrolytes; (1 mark) correct mobile ions.

---

### Question 2: Short Answer (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]**
*Why is a fuel cell exempt from the Carnot limit on thermal efficiency? Compare the theoretical efficiency of an H₂/O₂ fuel cell with a heat engine operating between 1000 K and 300 K.*

**Model Answer:**

A fuel cell converts chemical energy **directly into electrical energy** through an electrochemical reaction, **bypassing the intermediate heat-to-work conversion** step that limits a heat engine. The Carnot limit applies only to engines that operate on a thermodynamic cycle involving heat absorption and rejection. Since a fuel cell does not use a heat cycle, it is **not Carnot-limited**.

Theoretical H₂/O₂ fuel cell efficiency (HHV basis) is:

$$\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = 83.0\%$$

Carnot efficiency of a heat engine between 1000 K and 300 K:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{300}{1000} = 70.0\%$$

Therefore, the fuel cell (83 %) **exceeds the Carnot limit** (70 %) — a clear thermodynamic advantage of electrochemical conversion.

> Allocation: (1 mark) Carnot exemption reasoning; (1 mark) FC efficiency calculation; (1 mark) Carnot efficiency calculation and comparison.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Part B Choice 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the **layout of a fuel cell vehicle (FCV)**. Explain the function of the buffer battery, the DC-DC converter, and the regenerative braking system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **construction, working, and performance characteristics of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**. Include the half-cell reactions, the role of the catalyst, the operating temperature range, and the reasons for membrane humidification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)

**FCV Layout — Block Diagram (described textually; 3 marks for diagram description):**

A modern FCV consists of four inter-connected sub-systems:

1. **Hydrogen Storage System** — Type-IV composite tanks store H₂ at 700 bar. A pressure regulator reduces pressure to ≈10 bar before the stack. A thermal pressure relief device (TPRD) ensures safety in crashes.

2. **Fuel Cell Stack System** — A PEM fuel cell stack of 300–400 cells in series produces 250–450 V DC at 80–120 kW. The stack is supported by a Balance of Plant (BoP): air compressor, hydrogen recirculation blower, humidifier, water separator, and coolant pump.

3. **Electrical Power System** — A small Li-ion buffer battery (1–2 kWh) is connected via a DC-DC converter to the stack output. The DC-DC converter boosts the variable stack voltage to a stable 400 V traction bus. A traction inverter converts DC to 3-phase AC.

4. **Drivetrain** — A permanent-magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drives the wheels through a single-speed gearbox. The motor is reversible for regenerative braking.

**Function of the Buffer Battery (1.5 marks):**
- Acts as a *load leveller* — during sudden acceleration, the battery delivers peak current (boost) so the fuel cell can operate at a steady, efficient point.
- During regenerative braking, the battery absorbs the recovered kinetic energy (the fuel cell cannot accept fast current changes).
- Provides cold-start assist to warm up the stack.
- Typical rating: 1–2 kWh at 200–400 V.

**Function of the DC-DC Converter (1.5 marks):**
- The fuel cell's voltage drops with load (polarisation curve). The DC-DC converter is a **boost (step-up) converter** that maintains a constant traction bus voltage (≈400 V) regardless of the stack's instantaneous voltage.
- It also allows *bidirectional* power flow between the battery and the bus.

**Function of Regenerative Braking (1 mark):**
- During deceleration, the traction motor operates as a generator, converting kinetic energy to electrical energy.
- This energy is fed back to the buffer battery (not the fuel cell, which cannot accept rapid transients).
- Typical energy recovery: 30–50 % of braking energy, improving overall vehicle efficiency by 10–20 %.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)

**Construction of a PEMFC (2 marks):**

A single PEMFC cell consists of:
- **Anode** — A porous gas diffusion layer (GDL, typically carbon paper) coated with a Pt/C catalyst layer.
- **Cathode** — Same structure as anode (GDL + Pt/C catalyst).
- **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA)** — The polymer membrane (e.g., Nafion 117) sandwiched between the two catalyst layers. The MEA is the heart of the cell.
- **Bipolar plates** — Graphite or metal plates with machined flow channels for H₂ (anode side) and air (cathode side). They also collect current and distribute gas uniformly. (Plates are shared between adjacent cells in a stack.)

**Working — Half-cell reactions (2 marks):**

- **Anode (H₂ side):** $\quad H_2 \longrightarrow 2 H^+ + 2 e^-$
- **Cathode (O₂ side):** $\quad \dfrac{1}{2} O_2 + 2 H^+ + 2 e^- \longrightarrow H_2O$
- **Net cell reaction:** $\quad H_2 + \dfrac{1}{2} O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$

Hydrogen dissociates at the Pt anode, releasing protons and electrons. Protons migrate through the hydrated Nafion membrane to the cathode. Electrons travel through the external circuit (doing useful work) to reach the cathode. At the cathode, O₂ combines with the protons and electrons to form water and heat.

**Role of the Platinum Catalyst (1 mark):**
- The O₂ reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode has very slow kinetics. Pt lowers the activation energy, allowing the reaction to proceed at useful rates at 60–80 °C. Typical Pt loading: 0.1–0.4 mg/cm² per electrode.

**Operating Temperature and Membrane Humidification (2 marks):**
- Operating temperature: **60–80 °C** (low-temperature PEMFC).
- Nafion conducts H⁺ *only when hydrated* — water molecules form channels that transport protons via the Grotthuss mechanism. If the membrane dries out, internal resistance rises sharply and performance collapses.
- Therefore, **inlet gases are externally humidified** before entering the stack, and product water at the cathode is recirculated. Operating above 80 °C is difficult because water vapour pressure rises, risking membrane dehydration.

**Performance Characteristics (in summary table):**

| Metric | PEMFC Value |
|---|---|
| Power density | 0.5–1.0 kW/L; 0.5–1.0 kW/kg |
| Electrical efficiency | 50–60 % |
| Cell voltage under load | 0.6–0.7 V |
| Stack lifetime | 5,000–20,000 hours |
| Cold-start capability | Yes (with battery assist) |
| CO tolerance | < 10 ppm (poisoning Pt) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing AFC and PEMFC charge carriers** — AFC uses OH⁻ (anionic); PEMFC uses H⁺ (cationic). Marks lost if reversed.
> 2. **Forgetting water in PEMFC half-reactions** — the cathode reaction *must* show H⁺ + e⁻ + O₂ → H₂O. Marks lost if water is omitted.
> 3. **Stating "Carnot limit applies to fuel cells"** — this is *wrong*. The Carnot limit applies only to heat engines, not to electrochemical converters.
> 4. **Drawing PEMFC without a humidifier** — examiners deduct marks because humidification is essential to PEMFC operation.
> 5. **Conflating PAFC with PEMFC** — both use H⁺, but PAFC operates at 150–200 °C with liquid H₃PO₄ (not a polymer).

---

### Question B — Part B Choice 2 (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the **construction, working, and performance of a Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)**. What are the advantages and disadvantages compared to a PEMFC? Why is SOFC not used in passenger cars but preferred for stationary applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **polarisation curve (V–I characteristics)**, explain the three major voltage losses in a fuel cell. Derive the **Nernst equation** for the H₂/O₂ cell and compute the open-circuit voltage at 80 °C, 1.2 atm H₂, 0.21 atm O₂, and water as vapour. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks)

**SOFC Construction (2 marks):**

A single SOFC consists of:
- **Electrolyte** — Solid ceramic **Yttria-Stabilised Zirconia (YSZ)**, an O²⁻ conductor; thickness ≈ 10–100 µm.
- **Anode** — Porous **Ni-YSZ cermet** (nickel impregnated in YSZ matrix). The nickel catalyses H₂ oxidation; the YSZ provides mechanical strength and matches the thermal expansion of the electrolyte.
- **Cathode** — Porous perovskite oxide, typically **Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (LSM, La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃)**.
- **Interconnects** — Lanthanum chromite (LaCrO₃) ceramic for high-temp cells; stainless steel for IT-SOFCs.

**Working (2 marks):**

- **Anode (H₂ side):** $\quad H_2 + O^{2-} \longrightarrow H_2O + 2 e^-$
- **Cathode (O₂ side):** $\quad \dfrac{1}{2} O_2 + 2 e^- \longrightarrow O^{2-}$
- **Net cell reaction:** $\quad H_2 + \dfrac{1}{2} O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O$

Oxygen molecules adsorb at the porous cathode, gain electrons from the external circuit, and split into O²⁻ ions. These ions migrate through the dense YSZ electrolyte to the anode, where they oxidise H₂ to form water and release electrons back to the external circuit. The cell is operated at **800–1000 °C** to make YSZ a usable O²⁻ conductor (its ionic conductivity is negligible below 600 °C).

**Performance Characteristics (1 mark):**
- Electrical efficiency: 50–60 %; up to 85 % with CHP and 70 % when hybridised with a gas turbine.
- Power density: 200–400 mW/cm².
- Cell voltage: 0.7–0.8 V under load.
- **No precious metal catalysts** required (cost advantage at scale).
- **Fuel-flexible** — can use H₂, CO, CH₄, or even heavier hydrocarbons directly.

**Advantages over PEMFC (1 mark):**
- Higher electrical efficiency.
- Cheaper catalysts (no Pt).
- Higher-grade waste heat suitable for CHP/GT cycles.
- CO is a *fuel* (not a poison).
- Solid electrolyte — no leakage.

**Disadvantages vs. PEMFC (1 mark):**
- Very slow start-up (hours to reach 800 °C).
- Thermal cycling degrades ceramic components.
- Expensive high-temperature materials and sealing.
- Larger and heavier — unsuitable for mobile applications.

**Why SOFC is not used in passenger cars (1 mark):**
- Cars require fast start-up (seconds, not hours).
- Repeated thermal cycling (every drive cycle) causes ceramic cracking and stack failure.
- Heavy, bulky, and expensive for an automotive form factor.
- Instead, **PEMFC is used** in cars (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) due to its low temperature, fast start-up, and high power density.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks)

**Three Major Voltage Losses in a Fuel Cell (3 marks):**

The actual operating voltage of a fuel cell is always less than the thermodynamic (Nernst) voltage. The losses are quantified as **overpotentials**:

1. **Activation Overpotential (η_act)** — Caused by the sluggish kinetics of the electrode reactions, especially the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode. Dominates at *low current densities*. Modelled by the Tafel equation:

$$\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \left( \dfrac{j}{j_0} \right)$$

2. **Ohmic Overpotential (η_ohmic)** — Caused by ionic resistance in the electrolyte and electronic resistance in electrodes, bipolar plates, and contacts. Dominates in the *linear mid-range* of the polarisation curve.

$$\eta_{ohmic} = j \cdot ASR$$

3. **Concentration Overpotential (η_conc)** — Caused by mass-transport limitations: reactant depletion at the catalyst surface at *high current densities* near the limiting current $j_L$.

$$\eta_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( 1 - \dfrac{j}{j_L} \right)$$

**Net cell voltage equation:**

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

**Derivation of the Nernst Equation (3 marks):**

[Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.] The final result is:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$$

For the H₂/O₂ system, n = 2, and at 25 °C, $E^{\circ} = 1.18$ V (vapour) or 1.229 V (liquid water).

**Numerical Computation (1 mark):**

Given: $T = 80\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 353$ K, $P_{H_2} = 1.2$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm, water as vapour ($P_{H_2O} = 0.467$ atm at 80 °C), $E^{\circ} = 1.18$ V.

Step 1 — Nernst term:

$$\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

Step 2 — Log argument:

$$\dfrac{1.2 \times (0.21)^{0.5}}{0.467} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.4583}{0.467} = 1.1776$$

Step 3 — Result:

$$E = 1.18 + 0.01521 \times \ln(1.1776) = 1.18 + 0.01521 \times 0.1635 = \mathbf{1.1825 \text{ V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers on Question B(b):**
> 1. **Wrong sign in Nernst equation** — students often write $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$. The correct form has the log *with the product of reactants in the numerator*. Marks lost for sign error.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** — Nernst equation *requires* absolute temperature in K.
> 3. **Confusing liquid vs. vapour $E^{\circ}$** — 1.229 V for liquid water (25 °C); 1.18 V for vapour. The problem statement must be read carefully.
> 4. **Stating "no losses at open circuit"** — wrong. Even at OCV, the *activation* overpotential is present (the Nernst equation does not include kinetic losses; the actual OCV is lower than the Nernst value due to internal currents and fuel crossover).
> 5. **Mixing up activation, ohmic, and concentration loss regions** on the polarisation curve.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Fuel Cell Vehicles**

**🔹 Definitions & Fundamentals**
- ☐ FCV = electric vehicle powered by a fuel cell stack + buffer battery.
- ☐ Fuel cell = electrochemical device converting chemical energy directly to electrical energy + water + heat.
- ☐ Single cell voltage ≈ 0.6–1.0 V under load; hundreds of cells are stacked in series.
- ☐ Fuel cell is **not** Carnot-limited; theoretical efficiency = 83 % (HHV) / 94.5 % (LHV).

**🔹 FCV Architecture — 4 Pillars**
- ☐ Hydrogen storage (700 bar Type-IV tanks)
- ☐ Fuel cell stack + Balance of Plant (BoP)
- ☐ Buffer battery (1–2 kWh Li-ion) + DC-DC converter + traction inverter
- ☐ PMSM traction motor + single-speed gearbox + regenerative braking

**🔹 Six Fuel Cells — Key Differentiators**
- ☐ **AFC** — KOH electrolyte, OH⁻ ion, 60–200 °C, Ni catalyst, CO₂ poisoning, used in space.
- ☐ **PEMFC** — Nafion membrane, H⁺ ion, 50–100 °C, Pt catalyst, requires humidification, **used in FCVs**.
- ☐ **DMFC** — Same as PEMFC but liquid CH₃OH fuel; 20–30 % efficiency; portable electronics.
- ☐ **PAFC** — H₃PO₄ electrolyte, H⁺ ion, 150–200 °C, Pt catalyst, stationary CHP.
- ☐ **MCFC** — Molten Li/K carbonates, CO₃²⁻ ion, 600–700 °C, Ni catalyst, CO-tolerant, large stationary.
- ☐ **SOFC** — YSZ ceramic, O²⁻ ion, 800–1000 °C, perovskite/Ni catalysts, highest efficiency with GT hybrid.

**🔹 Key Equations to Memorise**
- ☐ Nernst: $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$
- ☐ Efficiency: $\eta_{el} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} = 83\%$ (HHV)
- ☐ Stack voltage: $V_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}$
- ☐ Stack power: $P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack}$
- ☐ Three losses: Activation (Tafel), Ohmic (j × ASR), Concentration (limiting current $j_L$)

**🔹 Polarisation Curve — Loss Regions**
- ☐ Low current → activation loss dominates (curved drop).
- ☐ Mid current → ohmic loss dominates (linear drop).
- ☐ High current → concentration loss dominates (sharp drop near $j_L$).

**🔹 Numerical Constants — Carry in Memory**
- ☐ $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹
- ☐ $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
- ☐ $E^{\circ}_{H_2/O_2} = 1.229$ V (liquid) / 1.18 V (vapour)
- ☐ LHV H₂ = 33.33 kWh/kg ; HHV H₂ = 39.4 kWh/kg
- ☐ 700 bar H₂ tank → ≈ 4.5 kWh/kg (system-level)

**🔹 Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ☐ Do not state "Carnot applies to fuel cells."
- ☐ Do not confuse the mobile ion (OH⁻ vs H⁺ vs CO₃²⁻ vs O²⁻).
- ☐ Do not omit product water in PEMFC cathode half-reaction.
- ☐ Do not forget to convert °C → K in the Nernst equation.
- ☐ Do not state that PEMFC uses Ni catalyst (it uses Pt).
- ☐ Do not state that SOFC uses liquid electrolyte (it is solid ceramic).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concept of Hybrid Traction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Concept of Hybrid Traction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Traction** is a propulsion philosophy in road and rail vehicles wherein the tractive effort (driving force) at the wheels is supplied by **two or more distinct on-board energy sources or power converters**, most commonly a combination of a **Conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (EMs)** working in a coordinated manner through a power management controller.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, hybrid traction is treated as a **dual-energy propulsion architecture** where the prime mover set is heterogeneous — meaning the conversion paths from chemical energy (fuel) and electrical energy (battery/ultracapacitor) to mechanical energy at the wheels are *parallel*, *series*, or *series-parallel* combined.

### 1.2 Taxonomy of Hybrid Vehicles (KTU Nomenclature)

| Hybrid Class | Symbol | Defining Trait |
|---|---|---|
| **Micro Hybrid** | μHEV | Start/Stop + smart alternator; 12 V architecture |
| **Mild Hybrid** | MHEV | Belt-driven Integrated Starter Generator (ISG); **48 V** architecture |
| **Full Hybrid** | FHEV | Both ICE & EM can propel vehicle independently (e.g., Toyota Prius) |
| **Plug-in Hybrid** | PHEV | Externally rechargeable ESS; **≥ 30 km** all-electric range |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Horses & One Cart" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a cart pulled by a single horse on flat roads.** Add a second, smaller, swifter horse that joins only when the cart climbs a steep hill. The bigger horse (ICE) handles steady cruising, while the smaller horse (Electric Motor) provides **burst assistance** during high-demand transients (acceleration, hill climbing). When going downhill, the smaller horse actually *pushes back* on the wheels to **regenerate** energy — equivalent to **regenerative braking**. This collaborative, demand-responsive, two-horse arrangement is exactly what hybrid traction does at the engineering level.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Air density (ISA): $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Standard drive cycle metric: **Fuel Economy in km/L or mpg**
- Energy density of petrol: **~ 44 MJ/kg**
- Energy density of Li-ion traction battery: **~ 0.5 – 0.8 MJ/kg**

### 1.5 Why Hybrid Traction? — KTU Board Favourite Question

1. **Reduction in fuel consumption** by 20–40 % over urban drive cycles.
2. **Lower tailpipe emissions** (CO₂, NOₓ, PM) — bridges the gap until full BEV adoption.
3. **Improved torque response** (EM delivers peak torque at zero rpm).
4. **Regenerative braking** recovers kinetic energy normally dissipated as heat.
5. **Range anxiety mitigation** vs pure battery EVs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Generic Hybrid Powertrain

A hybrid traction system is built around **five fundamental building blocks**:

1. **Energy Source 1** → Liquid/gaseous fuel tank + ICE.
2. **Energy Source 2** → Rechargeable Energy Storage System (RESS) — battery, ultracapacitor, or fuel cell.
3. **Power Converter 1** → ICE (chemical → mechanical).
4. **Power Converter 2** → Electric Motor-Generator (electrical ↔ mechanical, *bidirectional*).
5. **Energy Management Controller (EMC)** → Decides *instantaneous power split* between sources.

### 2.2 Three Canonical Hybrid Configurations

#### (a) Series Hybrid (Range-Extender Topology)
- ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from wheels. ICE drives a **generator** only.
- All tractive power to wheels flows through the **electrical bus → EM → wheels**.
- Analogy: ICE is a "portable power plant" charging a battery that runs a treadmill motor.

#### (b) Parallel Hybrid
- ICE and EM are **mechanically coupled** to wheels via a clutch/gear train.
- Either source can propel the vehicle *independently or simultaneously*.
- Analogy: Two engines strapped to the same driveshaft.

#### (c) Series-Parallel Hybrid (Power-Split / Toyota THS)
- Combines both topologies using an **Epicyclic (planetary) gear set** as a power-split device (PSD).
- ICE can either send power to wheels *directly* (parallel mode) or to the generator *indirectly* (series mode).
- This is the **industry standard for modern FHEVs**.

### 2.3 Modes of Operation (KTU High-Yield)

| Mode | Active Source | Typical Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure Electric Drive** | EM only | Low speed, low load, cold start |
| **Pure ICE Drive** | ICE only | Steady high-speed cruising |
| **Combined Drive** | ICE + EM | Hard acceleration, hill climbing |
| **ICE Charging Battery** | ICE → Generator → RESS | Battery SOC low, cruising |
| **Regenerative Braking** | Wheels → EM (as generator) → RESS | Braking / deceleration |
| **Hybrid Idle / Start-Stop** | Both OFF | Vehicle stationary |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready** — memorize these expressions verbatim. Note the use of `\vert` instead of literal pipes to preserve table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive Force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | N | Resistive + inertial components |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$ | N | $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.008$ to $0.015$ |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$ | N | $C_d A_f$ = drag area |
| 4 | Hill Climbing Force | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$ | N | $\alpha$ = slope angle |
| 5 | Linear Acceleration Force | $F_{la} = m \cdot \delta_m \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | N | $\delta_m$ = mass factor ($\geq 1$) |
| 6 | Tractive Power | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | W | At wheels |
| 7 | ICE Output Power | $P_{ICE} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV}$ | W | $\eta_{ICE} \approx 0.30$ to $0.40$ |
| 8 | EM Output Power | $P_{EM} = T_{EM} \cdot \omega_{EM}$ | W | $T$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 9 | Regen Braking Power | $P_{reg} = \eta_{EM} \cdot T_{gen} \cdot \omega_{w}$ | W | $\omega_w$ = wheel angular speed |
| 10 | Degree of Hybridization | $DoH = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}$ | dimensionless | $0 < DoH < 1$ |
| 11 | SOC Dynamics | $\dot{SOC} = \dfrac{I_{batt}}{Q_{batt}}$ | s⁻¹ | For charge balance |
| 12 | Fuel Consumption Reduction | $\Delta FC \, [\%] = \dfrac{FC_{conv} - FC_{hyb}}{FC_{conv}} \times 100$ | % | Compare to baseline |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Toyota, Honda, Hyundai mass-produce FHEVs achieving **> 25 km/L**.
- **Railways:** Diesel-Electric locomotives (Series Hybrid) — e.g., Indian Railways WDP class.
- **Buses:** Tata Starbus Hybrid, Volvo 7900 Hybrid for urban transit.
- **Heavy machinery:** Caterpillar & Komatsu mining dump trucks (Series-Diesel Electric).
- **Naval:** Submarines use **diesel-electric series hybrid** for silent running.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tractive Force & Power Demand (Full Working)

Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ on a road of slope angle $\alpha$ with acceleration $a = dv/dt$. The total tractive force $F_t$ required at the wheels is the **algebraic sum** of all resistive forces plus the inertia force.

**Step 1 — Identify the resistive force components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Rolling resistance:} \quad & F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha \\[4pt]
\text{(ii) Aerodynamic drag:} \quad & F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 \\[4pt]
\text{(iii) Hill-climb component:} \quad & F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha \\[4pt]
\text{(iv) Linear acceleration:} \quad & F_{la} = m \cdot \delta_m \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all components to obtain the total tractive force.**

$$
F_t \;=\; \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{air}C_d A_f v^2 \;+\; m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha \;+\; m \cdot \delta_m \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply force by velocity to obtain tractive power demand at the wheels.**

$$
\boxed{\,P_t \;=\; F_t \cdot v \;=\; v \!\left[\mu_{rr} m g \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta_m \dfrac{dv}{dt}\right]\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret units & limits.**
- Units: $N \times m/s = W$ ✓
- At constant cruising speed on flat road ($\alpha = 0$, $dv/dt = 0$): $P_t = v \cdot \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^3$
  - The **v³ aerodynamic term dominates at highway speeds**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Degree of Hybridization (DoH)

**Step 1 — Define total installed propulsion power.**

$$
P_{total} \;=\; P_{ICE} \;+\; P_{EM}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the contribution of the electric motor as a fraction of the total.**

$$
DoH \;=\; \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check with vehicle classes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Micro Hybrid:} \quad & DoH \approx 0.05 \text{ – } 0.10 \\
\text{Mild Hybrid (48 V ISG):} \quad & DoH \approx 0.15 \text{ – } 0.25 \\
\text{Full Hybrid:} \quad & DoH \approx 0.30 \text{ – } 0.50 \\
\text{Plug-in Hybrid:} \quad & DoH \approx 0.60 \text{ – } 0.85 \\
\text{BEV (reference):} \quad & DoH = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Power Split in a Series Hybrid (Worked Numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GATE/KTU Style Problem.** A series hybrid bus has $P_{ICE} = 80 \text{ kW}$, generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.92$, motor efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$, battery internal resistance losses neglected. If the demand at the wheels is $P_t = 60 \text{ kW}$ and the EM draws from both ICE-generator and battery in the ratio 70:30, find (a) fuel power input, (b) power supplied by battery.

**Step 1 — Power flowing through the electrical bus from ICE-generator side.**

$$
P_{bus,ICE} \;=\; 0.70 \times P_t \;\times\; \frac{1}{\eta_m}
$$

$$
P_{bus,ICE} \;=\; 0.70 \times 60 \times \frac{1}{0.90} \;=\; 46.67 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Power required from ICE shaft (before generator).**

$$
P_{ICE,shaft} \;=\; \frac{P_{bus,ICE}}{\eta_g} \;=\; \frac{46.67}{0.92} \;=\; 50.73 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel chemical power input (using thermal efficiency $\eta_{ICE} = 0.38$).**

$$
\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV} \;=\; \frac{P_{ICE,shaft}}{\eta_{ICE}} \;=\; \frac{50.73}{0.38} \;=\; 133.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Battery-supplied share (30 % of wheel demand).**

$$
P_{batt,bus} \;=\; 0.30 \times P_t \times \frac{1}{\eta_m} \;=\; 0.30 \times 60 \times \frac{1}{0.90} \;=\; 20.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answers.**

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV} \;\approx\; 133.5 \text{ kW (fuel input)}, \qquad P_{batt,bus} \;=\; 20.0 \text{ kW (battery share)}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Energy Recovery via Regenerative Braking

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy of a moving vehicle.**

$$
E_{kin} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2
$$

**Step 2 — Decelerating from $v_1$ to $v_2$ releases a recoverable energy.**

$$
\Delta E_{rec} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot (v_1^2 - v_2^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Of this, only a fraction $\eta_{reg}$ (round-trip) reaches the battery.**

$$
E_{stored} \;=\; \eta_{reg} \cdot \Delta E_{rec}, \qquad \eta_{reg} \approx 0.55 \text{ – } 0.70
$$

**Numerical example.** A 1500 kg car braking from 100 km/h (27.78 m/s) to 0 km/h:

$$
\Delta E_{rec} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (27.78)^2 \;=\; 578{,}704 \text{ J} \;\approx\; 0.161 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
E_{stored} \;=\; 0.65 \times 578{,}704 \;\approx\; 376 \text{ kJ} \;\approx\; 0.105 \text{ kWh}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Hybrid Traction System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Internal Combustion Engine]
    H[Braking Input] --> G[Wheel / Drivetrain]
    B --> C[Mechanical Coupling / Clutch]
    D[Rechargeable Energy Storage System] --> E[Power Electronics Inverter]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Electric Motor Generator]
    F --> G
    G --> D
    I[Energy Management Controller EMC] -.-> B
    I -.-> E
    I -.-> F
```

### 4.2 Series Hybrid Power Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT[Fuel Tank] --> ICE[ICE Engine]
    ICE --> GEN[Generator]
    GEN --> BUS1[DC Electrical Bus]
    BAT[Battery RESS] --> BUS1
    BUS1 --> INV[Inverter]
    INV --> EM[Electric Motor]
    EM --> WHEELS[Drive Wheels]
    WHEELS -.-> REG[Regenerative Feedback] -.-> BUS1
```

### 4.3 Parallel Hybrid Power Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT2[Fuel Tank] --> ICE2[ICE Engine]
    ICE2 --> CLUTCH[Clutch or CVT]
    BAT2[Battery RESS] --> INV2[Inverter]
    INV2 --> EM2[Electric Motor Generator]
    EM2 --> CLUTCH
    CLUTCH --> TRANS[Transmission]
    TRANS --> WHEELS2[Drive Wheels]
    WHEELS2 -.-> REG2[Regen Braking] -.-> INV2
```

### 4.4 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT3[Fuel Tank] --> ICE3[ICE Engine]
    ICE3 --> PSD[Planetary Gear Power Split Device]
    PSD --> WHEELS3[Drive Wheels]
    PSD --> GEN3[Generator MG1]
    GEN3 --> BUS3[DC Bus]
    BAT3[Battery] --> BUS3
    BUS3 --> MOTOR3[Traction Motor MG2]
    MOTOR3 --> WHEELS3
    EMC3[Energy Management Controller] -.-> ICE3
    EMC3 -.-> GEN3
    EMC3 -.-> MOTOR3
```

### 4.5 Operating Mode Decision Matrix (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Vehicle Start] --> Q1{Battery SOC Sufficient?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Torque Demand High?}
    Q1 -- No --> ICEONLY[ICE Only Mode]
    Q2 -- No --> EV[Pure Electric Drive]
    Q2 -- Yes --> COMBO[Combined ICE plus EM Drive]
    WHEEL[Wheel Brake Applied] --> REG[Regenerative Braking Mode]
    CRUISE[Highway Cruise] --> ICEONLY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above follow the alphanumeric-prefixed convention (e.g., `FT`, `ICE`, `GEN`, `BUS1`). Reserved keywords like `end`, `graph`, `style` have been avoided in raw form. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold or HTML tables — ensuring reliable rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**  
*Define hybrid traction. List the three fundamental hybrid powertrain configurations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Hybrid traction is a propulsion architecture where the **driving force (tractive effort) at the wheels is delivered by a combination of two or more distinct on-board energy conversion systems**, typically an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and an Electric Motor-Generator (EM), coordinated by an Energy Management Controller.

The **three fundamental configurations** are:

1. **Series Hybrid** — ICE drives a generator; wheels receive power *only* through the electric motor.
2. **Parallel Hybrid** — ICE and EM are mechanically coupled to the wheels; either can drive independently.
3. **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid** — uses an epicyclic gear set to combine series and parallel paths (e.g., Toyota THS).

*(1 mark definition + ½ mark per configuration = 3 marks)*

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**  
*What is the Degree of Hybridization (DoH)? Classify Mild, Full, and Plug-in hybrids using DoH values.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$
DoH \;=\; \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}
$$

where $P_{EM}$ is the rated power of the electric motor and $P_{ICE}$ is the rated power of the internal combustion engine.

Classification:
- **Mild Hybrid** : $DoH \approx 0.15$ – $0.25$
- **Full Hybrid** : $DoH \approx 0.30$ – $0.50$
- **Plug-in Hybrid** : $DoH \approx 0.60$ – $0.85$

*(1 mark formula + 2 marks classification = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt **one** of the two internal choices provided below.

---

#### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**Q.B-A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**  

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel hybrid powertrain configurations**. Compare their advantages and limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A parallel hybrid car of mass **1500 kg** (with rotating mass factor $\delta_m = 1.05$) is cruising on a flat road at a constant **80 km/h**. Aerodynamic drag area $C_d A_f = 0.70 \text{ m}^2$, rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. If the ICE supplies 80 % of the tractive power and the EM the remaining 20 %, calculate: (i) total tractive force, (ii) total tractive power, (iii) power share of each source. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Q.B-A:**

**(a) Configuration Explanation (7 marks):**

**Series Hybrid:** ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels. It drives a generator that produces electricity, which charges the battery or directly feeds the traction motor. The traction motor alone propels the wheels. *Advantages:* simple mechanical layout, ICE can always operate at its optimum efficiency point, ideal for urban buses. *Limitations:* three energy conversions (mech→elec→mech→mech) cause cumulative losses, requires large motor and generator.

**Parallel Hybrid:** ICE and EM are both mechanically connected to the wheels via a clutch and transmission. Either can propel independently or together. *Advantages:* compact, lower component cost, lower conversion losses since ICE can drive wheels directly at highway speeds. *Limitations:* complex control as both sources must be mechanically synchronized.

**Series-Parallel Hybrid:** Combines both topologies using a planetary gear set (Power Split Device). ICE power can be split — part going to wheels mechanically, part to a generator. *Advantages:* best of both worlds — high efficiency across the entire drive cycle. *Limitations:* most expensive, complex control algorithm.

*(Distribution: 1 mark sketch + 1 mark explanation per type × 3 = 6 marks + 1 mark comparison = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg, $\delta_m = 1.05$, $v = 80$ km/h $= 22.22$ m/s
- $\alpha = 0$ (flat road), $dv/dt = 0$ (constant speed)
- $C_d A_f = 0.70$ m², $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Power split: ICE 80 %, EM 20 %

**Step 1 — Compute individual resistive forces.**

Rolling resistance:
$$
F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos 0 = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag:
$$
F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.70 \times (22.22)^2 = 211.79 \text{ N}
$$

Hill-climb: $F_{hc} = 0$ (flat road).
Acceleration: $F_{la} = 0$ (constant speed).

**Step 2 — Total tractive force.**

$$
F_t = 176.58 + 211.79 + 0 + 0 = 388.37 \text{ N} \approx 388 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive power.**

$$
P_t = F_t \cdot v = 388.37 \times 22.22 = 8630 \text{ W} \approx 8.63 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Power share.**

$$
P_{ICE} = 0.80 \times 8.63 = 6.90 \text{ kW}, \qquad P_{EM} = 0.20 \times 8.63 = 1.73 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answers (boxed):**
$$
\boxed{F_t \approx 388 \text{ N}, \quad P_t \approx 8.63 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{ICE} \approx 6.90 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{EM} \approx 1.73 \text{ kW}}
$$

*(Distribution: 2 marks force calcs, 2 marks power calcs, 2 marks power split, 1 mark final boxed answer = 7 marks)*

---

#### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**Q.B-B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**  

**(a)** Explain the **six modes of operation** of a typical full hybrid electric vehicle with a suitable block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the equation for **tractive force** required to propel a vehicle, listing all four resistive components. Hence calculate the **total tractive power** for a vehicle of mass 1800 kg climbing a 10° slope at 60 km/h. Assume $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $C_d A_f = 0.85$ m², $\delta_m = 1.08$, and that the vehicle is not accelerating. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Q.B-B:**

**(a) Six Operating Modes (7 marks):**

1. **Pure Electric Drive:** Only the electric motor propels the vehicle. Used at low speeds, low torque demand, or when ICE is cold.
2. **Pure ICE Drive:** Only the ICE drives the wheels. Used during steady high-speed cruising when ICE efficiency is high.
3. **Combined (Boost) Drive:** Both ICE and EM deliver torque simultaneously. Used during hard acceleration or hill climbing.
4. **ICE Charging Mode:** ICE drives the generator to charge the battery (or maintain SOC) while simultaneously propelling the vehicle.
5. **Regenerative Braking:** The traction motor operates as a generator, converting kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in the battery.
6. **Idle Stop / Start-Stop:** Both ICE and EM are off at traffic stops; ICE restarts when needed.

A neat block diagram of the energy management controller with arrows indicating power flow in each mode should accompany the answer.

*(Distribution: 1 mark per mode × 6 = 6 marks + 1 mark for block diagram = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3 marks):** Already covered in Section 3.1 above. The four components are:

$$
F_t = \underbrace{\mu_{rr} m g \cos\alpha}_{\text{rolling}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2}_{\text{aero drag}} + \underbrace{m g \sin\alpha}_{\text{hill climb}} + \underbrace{m \delta_m \dfrac{dv}{dt}}_{\text{inertia}}
$$

**Numerical (4 marks):**

Given: $m = 1800$ kg, $\alpha = 10°$, $v = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s, $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $C_d A_f = 0.85$ m², $\delta_m = 1.08$, $dv/dt = 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute each force.**

Rolling resistance:
$$
F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times \cos 10° = 0.015 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.9848 = 260.85 \text{ N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag:
$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.85 \times (16.67)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.85 \times 277.89 = 144.65 \text{ N}
$$

Hill-climb:
$$
F_{hc} = 1800 \times 9.81 \times \sin 10° = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.1736 = 3065.5 \text{ N}
$$

Inertia:
$$
F_{la} = 0 \quad (\text{constant speed})
$$

**Step 2 — Sum.**

$$
F_t = 260.85 + 144.65 + 3065.5 + 0 = 3471 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive power.**

$$
P_t = F_t \cdot v = 3471 \times 16.67 = 57{,}864 \text{ W} \approx 57.86 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{F_t \approx 3471 \text{ N}, \qquad P_t \approx 57.86 \text{ kW}}
$$

*(Distribution: 3 marks derivation + 1 mark force calc + 1 mark total force + 1 mark power + 1 mark final = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s before multiplying force × velocity. This single error **loses 1–2 marks** silently.
> 2. **Ignoring Hill Component:** Many students set $F_{hc} = 0$ even when $\alpha \neq 0$. Always check the problem statement for slope.
> 3. **Mass Factor Confusion:** The rotational inertia factor $\delta_m$ multiplies only the **linear acceleration** term, **not** rolling resistance or hill-climb.
> 4. **DoH Definition Trap:** Examiners often write "**ratio of electric power to total power**" — that is the DoH. Don't confuse it with **SOC** (State of Charge).
> 5. **Missing Sketch:** In 14-mark questions on hybrid configurations, **a labelled block diagram is mandatory**; absence typically costs **2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Operating Modes Listing Without Explanation:** Naming the six modes fetches partial credit; explaining *when each mode is activated* fetches full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hybrid Traction** = dual-source propulsion (ICE + EM) coordinated by an Energy Management Controller.
- [x] Three configurations: **Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split).**
- [x] Six operating modes: **Pure EV, Pure ICE, Combined, ICE-Charging, Regen Braking, Idle Stop.**
- [x] **Degree of Hybridization (DoH)** quantifies electric share: $DoH = P_{EM} / (P_{EM} + P_{ICE})$.
- [x] Tractive force has **4 components**: rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, hill climb, linear inertia.
- [x] Tractive power: $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ — always use **SI units** (N, m/s, W).
- [x] Aerodynamic term dominates at **high speeds** (∝ v³); rolling + hill dominate at **low speeds**.
- [x] Regenerative braking recovers up to **~ 65 %** of kinetic energy in modern FHEVs.
- [x] Series hybrid = **range-extender** (best for city buses); Parallel hybrid = **compact & efficient on highways**; Series-Parallel = **best overall efficiency**.
- [x] Toyota Hybrid System (THS) = industry benchmark for power-split architecture using **planetary gear set**.
- [x] Mass factor $\delta_m$ applies **only** to acceleration term, never to $F_{rr}$ or $F_{hc}$.
- [x] Standard KTU benchmark values: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.01$–$0.015$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Various Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies (Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Various Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies (Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) Drive-Train Topology** is defined as the *systematic architectural configuration* that describes the mechanical and electrical interconnection between the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), one or more Electric Machines (EM), the Energy Storage System (ESS — typically a battery pack), the Transmission, and the final Drive-Wheels, in order to deliver tractive power for vehicle propulsion.

The three canonical topologies mandated by the KTU 2024 PEMUT411 syllabus are:

1. **Series Hybrid Drive-Train**
2. **Parallel Hybrid Drive-Train**
3. **Series-Parallel (Power-Split / Combined) Hybrid Drive-Train**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *topology* describes ONLY the *connection scheme* — it does NOT define the *degree of hybridization* (micro, mild, full, plug-in) or the *energy management strategy*. These are orthogonal design parameters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Chef Kitchen"

Imagine a restaurant kitchen preparing a single dish (the vehicle's wheels need rotational energy):

- **Series Topology — "The Relay Chef":** One chef (ICE) prepares ingredients at a slow, steady rate, hands them to a *saucepan* (Generator → Battery → Motor), and a *fast sprinting waiter* (Traction Motor) delivers it to the table (wheels). The ICE **never** directly touches the plate.
- **Parallel Topology — "The Two-Chef Race":** Two chefs (ICE **AND** EM) can both deliver food directly to the table. They cooperate *side-by-side*, and you can choose to use either one, or both at once, depending on how fast the table needs to be served.
- **Series-Parallel Topology — "The Hybrid Master Kitchen":** Combines BOTH ideas. There is a relay line **AND** a direct line, managed by a smart *kitchen manager* (Power-Split Device / Planetary Gear Set).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The choice of topology directly determines the *conversion losses*, *component sizing*, *control complexity*, and *packaging constraints* of the entire vehicle.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following **bold-marked** metrics are universally used for KTU-level evaluation of hybrid topologies:

- **Specific Energy of Li-ion Battery:** $\mathbf{100\text{–}250 \ Wh/kg}$
- **Round-trip Efficiency (Battery):** $\mathbf{85\text{–}95\%}$
- **ICE Peak Thermal Efficiency:** $\mathbf{35\text{–}42\%}$ (Otto/Diesel at optimal point)
- **Electric Machine Efficiency:** $\mathbf{90\text{–}96\%}$
- **State of Charge (SOC) operating window:** $\mathbf{0.3 \le SOC \le 0.8}$ (typical)
- **Vehicle Mass Range:** $\mathbf{800\text{–}2500 \ kg}$
- **Curb Weight Penalty for Hybridization:** $\mathbf{+10\% \text{ to } +30\%}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture 1 — The Series Hybrid Drive-Train

#### 2.1.1 Configuration & Power Flow

In a **Series Hybrid**, the ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. The energy path is *unidirectional and electrical* between the ICE and the wheels.

**Component Chain:** ICE $\rightarrow$ Generator (G) $\rightarrow$ Power Electronics Rectifier $\rightarrow$ DC Bus $\rightarrow$ Battery (ESS) $\parallel$ Traction Motor Inverter $\rightarrow$ Traction Motor (TM) $\rightarrow$ Transmission (Fixed Ratio) $\rightarrow$ Wheels.

**Operating Modes:**
- **Pure Electric Mode:** ICE OFF; Battery alone powers TM (low-speed urban driving).
- **Pure ICE Mode:** ICE drives G, which feeds TM directly; Battery in standby (steady highway cruising).
- **ICE + Battery Mode:** ICE + G + Battery jointly power TM (high torque demand).
- **Regenerative Braking Mode:** TM acts as a generator; wheels → TM → Battery.

#### 2.1.2 Why the Series Topology Exists — Engineering Rationale

1. The ICE can be operated **continuously at its Best Operating Line (BOL)** — a single sweet-spot on the BSFC map — because it never has to follow transient road loads. This is called **engine downsizing + load-leveling**.
2. No mechanical transmission is required between ICE and wheels → simpler packaging, no clutch, no gearbox.
3. Multiple ICE-generator sets (range extender concept) can be stacked.

#### 2.1.3 Critical Disadvantages

- **Three Energy Conversions** (Mechanical $\rightarrow$ Electrical $\rightarrow$ Mechanical) on the main path $\Rightarrow$ cumulative losses of $\approx 15\text{–}25\%$.
- Traction motor must be sized for **peak vehicle power**, making it large, heavy, and expensive.
- Battery must absorb the *full* regenerative braking energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Example:** The **Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1)**, **BMW i3 REx**, and **Nissan e-Power (Note)** are all series hybrids. The Nissan e-Power in particular is a series hybrid without a charging plug.

---

### 2.2 Architecture 2 — The Parallel Hybrid Drive-Train

#### 2.2.1 Configuration & Power Flow

In a **Parallel Hybrid**, the ICE **and** the EM are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels (typically through a torque-coupler or a clutch-based gearbox). Both power sources can drive the wheels *in parallel* — either independently or simultaneously.

**Component Chain:** ICE $\rightarrow$ Clutch/Coupling $\rightarrow$ Transmission $\rightarrow$ Wheels  $\;\parallel\;$  EM $\rightarrow$ Coupling $\rightarrow$ same Transmission $\rightarrow$ Wheels, with Battery feeding EM through inverter.

**Operating Modes:**
- **ICE-Only Mode:** Clutch engaged, EM idle. Highway cruising.
- **EM-Only Mode:** Clutch disengaged (or ICE OFF), EM alone drives wheels. Slow urban speeds.
- **Combined Mode:** Both ICE and EM deliver torque *additively* — used for maximum acceleration (boost mode).
- **Regenerative Braking:** EM acts as generator, ICE decoupled.
- **Battery Charging Mode (Load Point Shifting):** ICE produces more power than needed; excess charges the battery.

#### 2.2.2 Why the Parallel Topology Exists — Engineering Rationale

1. The EM does **not** have to be sized for peak vehicle power (only for *boost* power) $\Rightarrow$ smaller, lighter, cheaper electric machine.
2. Only **two energy conversions** on the main path during combined mode $\Rightarrow$ higher *combined-mode efficiency* than series.
3. The vehicle can run on ICE alone if the battery fails (fail-safe operation).

#### 2.2.3 Critical Disadvantages

- ICE is *still mechanically coupled* to the wheels $\Rightarrow$ it must still respond to transient loads; it cannot always operate at its absolute best BSFC point.
- The mechanical coupling (clutch/torque-coupler) adds complexity.
- Control strategy is more complex because two torque sources must be coordinated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Example:** The **Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA system)**, **Honda Insight (1st Gen)**, and **Hyundai Hybrid Blue Drive** (early versions) are parallel hybrids. Honda in particular is famous for its *Integrated Motor Assist* parallel architecture.

---

### 2.3 Architecture 3 — The Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Drive-Train

#### 2.3.1 Configuration & Power Flow

The **Series-Parallel Hybrid** is a *combined* topology that incorporates *both* an electrical path and a mechanical path between the ICE and the wheels, with the split dynamically managed by a **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)** acting as a **Power-Split Device (PSD)**.

**Component Chain:**
- **Mechanical Path (Direct):** ICE $\rightarrow$ Ring gear of PGS $\rightarrow$ Final drive $\rightarrow$ Wheels
- **Electrical Path (Indirect):** ICE $\rightarrow$ Planet carrier of PGS $\rightarrow$ Generator (G) $\rightarrow$ Inverter $\rightarrow$ DC Bus $\rightarrow$ Battery $\parallel$ Traction Motor (TM) $\rightarrow$ Final drive $\rightarrow$ Wheels
- The TM is **always** mechanically connected to the wheels through a separate gear train (often a second planetary gear or a fixed-ratio gear).

This gives the vehicle a **continuously variable transmission (CVT)**-like behavior — the effective gear ratio between the ICE and the wheels can be *infinitely varied* by controlling the speed of the generator.

#### 2.3.2 Why the Series-Parallel Topology Exists — Engineering Rationale

1. It can operate as a **series** topology (low speeds, ICE drives G only) **or** as a **parallel** topology (highway, both ICE and TM drive wheels) — or anywhere in between.
2. The ICE can be **decoupled from wheel speed** (series behavior) OR **locked to wheel speed** (parallel behavior) at the discretion of the controller.
3. The Toyota Hybrid System (**THS**) used in the **Toyota Prius** is the most commercially successful example, with over 20 million units sold worldwide.

#### 2.3.3 Critical Disadvantages

- The planetary gear set and the second motor add cost, weight, and packaging complexity.
- Control software (Energy Management Strategy — EMS) is far more sophisticated; relies heavily on ECUs running rule-based or optimization-based (e.g., ECMS, Pontryagin's Minimum Principle, Dynamic Programming) algorithms.
- The Toyota Prius system, for example, uses **two** electric machines and **one** planetary gear set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Example:** **Toyota Prius (all generations)**, **Ford Escape Hybrid**, **Lexus Hybrid Drive**, and the **Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid** are all series-parallel hybrids.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **high-yield formulas** for solving KTU board problems on hybrid topologies. **Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value to preserve markdown table syntax.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vehicle Tractive Force | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$ | $F_t$ [N], $F_r$ rolling, $F_{ad}$ aerodynamic drag, $F_{hc}$ hill climb, $F_{ia}$ inertia | Base dynamics for any HEV |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | $C_{rr}$ ≈ 0.012 for car tires | Power demand calculation |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$ | $\rho_{air}$ = **1.225 kg/m³** (sea level), $C_d$ drag coeff, $A_f$ frontal area [m²] | Highway fuel demand |
| 4 | Hill Climbing Force | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ road grade [rad] | Gradeability test |
| 5 | Inertial Force | $F_{ia} = m \cdot \delta_m \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | $\delta_m$ mass factor (≈ 1.05–1.4) | Acceleration analysis |
| 6 | Power at Wheels | $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ | $P_w$ [W] | Sizing EM & ICE |
| 7 | Series Path Efficiency | $\eta_{series} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_G \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{TM} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | All $\eta$ are decimal efficiencies (e.g., 0.35) | Series topology losses |
| 8 | Parallel Path Efficiency | $\eta_{parallel} = \dfrac{\eta_{ICE} \cdot P_{ICE} + \eta_{EM} \cdot P_{EM}}{P_{ICE} + P_{EM}}$ | Power-weighted average | Combined-mode efficiency |
| 9 | SOC Balance Equation | $\dfrac{d(SOC)}{dt} = \dfrac{-I_{bat}}{Q_{bat} \cdot 3600}$ | $I_{bat}$ [A], $Q_{bat}$ [Ah] | Energy management |
| 10 | Equivalent Fuel Consumption (ECMS) | $\dot{m}_{f,eq} = \dot{m}_{f,ICE} + s(t) \cdot \dfrac{P_{bat}}{Q_{LHV}}$ | $s(t)$ equivalence factor, $Q_{LHV}$ ≈ **43 MJ/kg** for gasoline | Optimal control problem |
| 11 | Planetary Gear Speed Relation | $(\omega_R + \omega_S) \cdot r_R = \omega_C \cdot r_C$ | $\omega_R$ ring, $\omega_S$ sun, $\omega_C$ carrier; $r$ pitch radii | Power-split device analysis |
| 12 | Planetary Gear Carrier (Willis Eq.) | $\omega_C = \dfrac{\omega_S \cdot r_S + \omega_R \cdot r_R}{r_S + r_R}$ | Willis Equation | THS analysis |
| 13 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $bsfc = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{ICE}} \quad [g/kWh]$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | Engine selection |
| 14 | Battery Energy | $E_{bat} = V_{oc} \cdot Q_{bat}$ | $V_{oc}$ open-circuit voltage [V] | Sizing ESS |
| 15 | Regen Braking Limit | $P_{regen,max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2 \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | $\eta_{regen} \approx 0.6$ | Energy recovery calc |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection of a hybrid topology is the **first architectural decision** in HEV design. It dictates:

- **Packaging:** Series allows engine placement anywhere (no mechanical link to wheels); Parallel requires co-axial alignment.
- **Component Cost:** Parallel has the smallest EM and battery; Series-Parallel has the highest BOM cost.
- **Software Complexity:** Series < Parallel < Series-Parallel.
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Series and Series-Parallel allow ICE to run at constant RPM → superior NVH.
- **Efficiency:** Parallel is best for highway-biased duty cycles; Series is best for urban stop-and-go; Series-Parallel is a *compromise* (best *weighted average*).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Flow in a Series Hybrid

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A series hybrid vehicle has the ICE producing a steady mechanical power $P_{ICE}$. Compute the power that actually reaches the wheels $P_w$, accounting for all intermediate conversion stages.

**Given:** $P_{ICE} = 50 \text{ kW}$, $\eta_{ICE} = 0.38$ (avg), $\eta_G = 0.92$, $\eta_{inv,rect} = 0.95$, $\eta_{batt,dis} = 0.95$, $\eta_{inv,mot} = 0.95$, $\eta_{TM} = 0.93$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.97$.

**Step 1 — Mechanical power output of the ICE shaft (after ICE internal combustion losses):**
This is the *shaft* power, not the indicated power. For a series hybrid, the ICE is sized such that its *shaft* output equals $P_{ICE}$ given.

$$P_{ICE,shaft} = P_{ICE} = 50 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical power at the generator terminals (after generator conversion losses):**
The generator converts mechanical shaft power to electrical power.

$$P_{G,elec} = P_{ICE,shaft} \cdot \eta_G$$

$$P_{G,elec} = 50 \cdot 0.92 = 46.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — DC power at the DC bus (after rectifier losses):**
The rectifier converts AC from the generator to DC.

$$P_{DC,bus} = P_{G,elec} \cdot \eta_{rect}$$

$$P_{DC,bus} = 46.0 \cdot 0.95 = 43.70 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Power delivered to the traction motor inverter (assuming battery is in standby, no battery charge/discharge loss):**

$$P_{inv,in} = P_{DC,bus} = 43.70 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — AC power output of the inverter (feeding the traction motor):**

$$P_{inv,out} = P_{inv,in} \cdot \eta_{inv}$$

$$P_{inv,out} = 43.70 \cdot 0.95 = 41.515 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Mechanical shaft power of the traction motor:**

$$P_{TM,shaft} = P_{inv,out} \cdot \eta_{TM}$$

$$P_{TM,shaft} = 41.515 \cdot 0.93 = 38.609 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Power at the wheels (after transmission losses):**

$$P_w = P_{TM,shaft} \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

$$P_w = 38.609 \cdot 0.97 = 37.451 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Overall series path efficiency:**

$$\eta_{series} = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{ICE}} = \dfrac{37.451}{50} = 0.7490 = 74.90\%$$

**Interpretation:** Of the original 50 kW of fuel energy (treated as equivalent ICE power at this BSFC point), only **37.45 kW** reaches the wheels. The remaining **12.55 kW (25.1%)** is lost to multiple conversion stages. This is the **fundamental cost** of the series topology.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Flow in a Parallel Hybrid (Combined Mode)

**Problem Setup:** A parallel hybrid is in combined mode with ICE and EM both producing power. Compute $P_w$.

**Given:** $P_{ICE} = 40 \text{ kW}$ (at BSFC min, $\eta_{ICE} = 0.40$), $P_{EM} = 15 \text{ kW}$ (electric input from battery, $\eta_{EM} = 0.92$), $\eta_{trans} = 0.97$, mechanical coupling efficiency $\eta_{coup} = 0.98$.

**Step 1 — Mechanical shaft power of ICE:**

$$P_{ICE,shaft} = 40 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical shaft power of EM (after electrical-to-mechanical conversion):**

$$P_{EM,shaft} = P_{EM} \cdot \eta_{EM} = 15 \cdot 0.92 = 13.80 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Combined mechanical power at the coupling output:**

$$P_{coup,out} = (P_{ICE,shaft} + P_{EM,shaft}) \cdot \eta_{coup}$$

$$P_{coup,out} = (40 + 13.80) \cdot 0.98 = 53.80 \cdot 0.98 = 52.724 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Power at the wheels after transmission:**

$$P_w = P_{coup,out} \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

$$P_w = 52.724 \cdot 0.97 = 51.142 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Parallel combined-mode efficiency:**

$$\eta_{parallel,combined} = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{ICE} + P_{EM}} = \dfrac{51.142}{55.0} = 0.930 = 93.0\%$$

**Interpretation:** Even though the EM path is less efficient (92%), because the dominant power is delivered by the ICE *directly* (mechanically), the overall path efficiency is far higher than the series case.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Planetary Gear (Willis Equation) for Power-Split Device

**Problem Setup:** A Toyota-THS-style planetary gear has ring gear teeth $N_R = 78$, sun gear teeth $N_S = 30$, and is connected to the ICE via the carrier. Compute the generator speed when the vehicle cruises at a wheel speed equivalent of 1500 rpm (motor speed).

**Step 1 — Geometric ratio of the planetary gear:**
The fundamental Willis relation for a simple planetary gear (with ring fixed to ground) reduces to the carrier-sun relation. The general Willis equation is:

$$\omega_S \cdot N_R + \omega_R \cdot N_S = \omega_C \cdot (N_R + N_S)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the role of each member in THS:**
In the Toyota Hybrid System (THS), the **Sun gear** is connected to **Generator (MG1)**, the **Carrier** is connected to **ICE**, and the **Ring gear** is connected to the **Wheels (via final drive)**.

$$\omega_S = \omega_{MG1}, \quad \omega_C = \omega_{ICE}, \quad \omega_R = \omega_{ring} = \omega_{wheels,eq}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the generator speed:**

$$\omega_{MG1} = \dfrac{\omega_{ICE} \cdot (N_R + N_S) - \omega_{ring} \cdot N_S}{N_R}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution:** Let ICE be at 2500 rpm, ring (wheels) at 1200 rpm (after final drive ratio).

$$\omega_{MG1} = \dfrac{2500 \cdot (78 + 30) - 1200 \cdot 30}{78}$$

$$\omega_{MG1} = \dfrac{2500 \cdot 108 - 36000}{78}$$

$$\omega_{MG1} = \dfrac{270000 - 36000}{78} = \dfrac{234000}{78}$$

$$\omega_{MG1} = 3000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** With the ICE at 2500 rpm (its fuel-optimum point) and the ring gear turning at 1200 rpm (vehicle cruising at ~60 km/h), the generator must spin at 3000 rpm in the opposite direction of the ICE to balance the gear kinematics. This negative speed is what enables the **CVT-like behavior** of the THS.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of electric machines | 1 (Traction) | 1 (Traction/Gen) | 2 (MG1=Gen, MG2=Traction) |
| Power-split device | None | Torque coupler/clutch | Planetary gear set |
| Mechanical link ICE–Wheels | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes (partial)** |
| Energy conversions (main path) | 3 (M→E→M) | 2 (direct M) | Variable (1 to 3) |
| Typical efficiency | 70–80% | 85–95% | 80–92% |
| EM size | Full vehicle peak power | Boost power only | Mid-sized (with regen) |
| ICE always at optimum? | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes (in series mode)** |
| Control complexity | Low | Medium | Very High |
| Cost | Low–Medium | Low | High |
| NVH | Best | Worst | Good |
| Best suited duty cycle | Urban stop-and-go | Highway + light urban | Mixed (city + highway) |
| Real-world example | Nissan e-Power, BMW i3 REx | Honda Civic IMA, Hyundai | Toyota Prius, Ford Escape |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Series Hybrid Drive-Train (Power Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[ICE] --> B[Generator G]
    B --> C[Rectifier AC-DC]
    C --> D[DC Bus]
    D --> E[Battery ESS]
    D --> F[Inverter DC-AC]
    F --> G[Traction Motor TM]
    G --> H[Fixed Ratio Transmission]
    H --> I[Wheels]
    G -. Regenerative Braking .-> F
    F -. Charging Path .-> D
    style A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the series hybrid, there is **no** mechanical link from the ICE to the wheels. The ICE's only mechanical output goes into the generator.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Parallel Hybrid Drive-Train (Power Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[ICE] --> C[Torque Coupler Clutch]
    C --> D[Transmission]
    D --> I[Wheels]
    B[Electric Machine EM] --> C
    E[Battery ESS] --> F[Inverter DC-AC]
    F --> B
    B -. Regenerative Braking .-> F
    style A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the *parallel* structure: ICE and EM both feed into the same torque coupler, which sends combined power to the transmission. The mechanical link ICE $\to$ Wheels is *direct*.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Series-Parallel Hybrid with Planetary Gear Set (THS Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[ICE] --> C[Planet Carrier of PGS]
    C --> R[Ring Gear of PGS]
    R --> I[Final Drive]
    I --> J[Wheels]
    C --> S[Sun Gear of PGS]
    S --> G[Generator MG1]
    G --> H[Rectifier Inverter]
    H --> K[DC Bus]
    K --> L[Battery ESS]
    K --> M[Inverter]
    M --> N[Traction Motor MG2]
    N --> I
    style A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style N fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)** is the *power-split device*. The carrier (connected to ICE) is the *input*, the sun (connected to MG1) is the *electrical path*, and the ring (connected to wheels via final drive) is the *mechanical output*. This is the exact architecture of the **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)**.

### 4.4 Comparative Topology Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SERIES[Series Hybrid]
        S1[ICE] --> S2[Generator] --> S3[Rectifier] --> S4[DC Bus] --> S5[Inverter] --> S6[Traction Motor] --> S7[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph PARALLEL[Parallel Hybrid]
        P1[ICE] --> P3[Torque Coupler] --> P4[Transmission] --> P7[Wheels]
        P2[Electric Machine] --> P3
    end
    subgraph SPLIT[Series-Parallel Hybrid]
        Q1[ICE] --> Q2[Planetary Gear Set] --> Q4[Final Drive] --> Q7[Wheels]
        Q2 --> Q3[Generator MG1] --> Q5[Inverter] --> Q6[Traction Motor MG2] --> Q4
    end
    style SERIES fill:#ffe6e6
    style PARALLEL fill:#e6ffe6
    style SPLIT fill:#e6e6ff
```
