## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) drive-train topology. List the three principal hybrid topologies with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *drive-train topology* is the architectural configuration that describes the mechanical and electrical interconnection between the ICE, electric machines, energy storage system, transmission, and drive wheels of an HEV.
The three principal topologies are:
1. **Series Hybrid** — e.g., Nissan e-Power, BMW i3 REx. (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing with examples)

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a series and a parallel hybrid drive-train with respect to the mechanical coupling between the ICE and the wheels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- In a **series hybrid**, the ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. The ICE drives a generator, and the electrical energy is then used by a traction motor to propel the vehicle. (1.5 marks)
- In a **parallel hybrid**, the ICE and the electric machine are **both mechanically coupled** to the wheels through a common torque-coupler or transmission, allowing either or both to propel the vehicle directly. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle** drive-train and explain its working in all four operating modes. (7 marks)

**(b)** A series HEV has the following component efficiencies at the most-frequently-used operating point:
$\eta_{ICE} = 0.40$, $\eta_{G} = 0.92$, $\eta_{rect} = 0.95$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$, $\eta_{TM} = 0.93$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.97$.

The ICE delivers $60 \text{ kW}$ of fuel energy per second. Calculate the power available at the wheels and the overall series path efficiency. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram (3 marks):** (See Section 4.1 above for the exact block diagram.)

**Four Operating Modes (4 marks):**
1. **Pure Electric Mode** (Battery + TM, ICE OFF): Used for low-speed urban driving. The ICE is shut off to avoid inefficient low-load operation. (1 mark)
2. **ICE-Only Mode** (ICE + G + TM, Battery in standby): The ICE drives the generator, whose electrical output directly feeds the traction motor. The battery is neither charged nor discharged. This is the **steady cruising** mode. (1 mark)
3. **Combined Mode** (ICE + G + Battery + TM): The ICE output, supplemented by battery discharge, jointly power the traction motor during high torque demand (e.g., hill climbing or hard acceleration). (1 mark)
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode** (TM acts as generator, ICE OFF): The traction motor converts the kinetic energy of the decelerating vehicle into electrical energy, which is stored in the battery. (1 mark)

**(b) Power at Wheels Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — ICE shaft power:** The 60 kW of fuel energy, when delivered at ICE efficiency 0.40, yields shaft power:

$$P_{ICE,shaft} = 60 \cdot 0.40 = 24 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Generator electrical power:**

$$P_{G,elec} = 24 \cdot 0.92 = 22.08 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Rectifier DC bus power:**

$$P_{DC} = 22.08 \cdot 0.95 = 20.976 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Inverter AC output:**

$$P_{inv,out} = 20.976 \cdot 0.96 = 20.137 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Traction motor shaft power:**

$$P_{TM,shaft} = 20.137 \cdot 0.93 = 18.727 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Power at wheels (after transmission):**

$$P_w = 18.727 \cdot 0.97 = 18.165 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Overall series path efficiency (relative to fuel energy):**

$$\eta_{series} = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{fuel}} = \dfrac{18.165}{60} = 0.3028 = 30.28\%$$

**Final Answer:** $P_w = 18.17 \text{ kW}$, $\eta_{series,overall} = 30.28\%$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, describe the architecture of a **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Drive-Train** used in the Toyota Hybrid System (THS). Explain the role of the planetary gear set as a power-split device. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a THS-style planetary gear, the ring gear has 80 teeth, the sun gear has 30 teeth, and the carrier is connected to the ICE. The ICE runs at 2800 rpm and the ring gear (vehicle equivalent) rotates at 1500 rpm. Compute the generator speed using the Willis equation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & THS Description (7 marks):**
- A labeled block diagram is required. (See Section 4.3 above.) (3 marks)
- **Role of the Planetary Gear Set (4 marks):**
  - The PGS consists of three members: **sun gear**, **planet carrier** (with planet gears), and **ring gear**.
  - In THS: **Carrier = ICE input**, **Sun = MG1 (Generator)**, **Ring = MG2 (Traction Motor) and Wheels**.
  - The PGS acts as a *continuously variable mechanical differential*: it can split the ICE's mechanical power into two parts — a *mechanical path* (carrier → ring → wheels) and an *electrical path* (carrier → sun → MG1 → inverter → MG2 → wheels).
  - By controlling the speed of MG1, the effective gear ratio between the ICE and the wheels can be varied continuously, emulating a CVT.
  - This allows the ICE to operate near its Best Operating Line (BSFC minimum) regardless of vehicle speed.

**(b) Generator Speed Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the Willis Equation:**

$$\omega_S \cdot N_R + \omega_R \cdot N_S = \omega_C \cdot (N_R + N_S)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the parameters:**
$N_R = 80$, $N_S = 30$, $\omega_C = \omega_{ICE} = 2800 \text{ rpm}$, $\omega_R = 1500 \text{ rpm}$.

**Step 3 — Solve for $\omega_S$:**

$$\omega_S = \dfrac{\omega_C \cdot (N_R + N_S) - \omega_R \cdot N_S}{N_R}$$

$$\omega_S = \dfrac{2800 \cdot (80 + 30) - 1500 \cdot 30}{80}$$

$$\omega_S = \dfrac{2800 \cdot 110 - 45000}{80}$$

$$\omega_S = \dfrac{308000 - 45000}{80}$$

$$\omega_S = \dfrac{263000}{80}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_S = 3287.5 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark for interpretation):** The generator must spin at 3287.5 rpm (positive direction, same as ICE in this case) to maintain the kinematic balance. The slight over-speed of the generator relative to the ICE is what allows the ICE to operate at 2800 rpm (its BSFC optimum) even when the wheels are turning at only 1500 rpm equivalent.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ICE shaft efficiency:** Many students compute series efficiency as $\eta_G \cdot \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{TM} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ but forget $\eta_{ICE}$. This loses **1–2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Wrong sign convention in Willis Equation:** The Willis equation has signs (ring and sun rotate in *opposite directions* relative to the carrier in a simple PGS). Always verify by substituting a known case (e.g., carrier fixed, sun must spin opposite to ring).
> 3. **Confusing "topology" with "degree of hybridization":** Saying "mild hybrid" is NOT a topology — it's a *degree*. This conceptual confusion will cost you on definition questions.
> 4. **Mixing up planetary gear members:** The planet **carrier** carries the planet gears on pins. Confusing this with the ring or sun is a classic error worth 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the operating mode:** In long answers, always explicitly state which operating mode (pure electric, ICE-only, combined, regen) you are analyzing. Examiners look for this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies):**
> - **Three Topologies:** Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split). [✓]
> - **Series:** No mechanical ICE–wheel link; ICE drives a generator; three energy conversions; best for urban; e.g., Nissan e-Power. [✓]
> - **Parallel:** ICE and EM *both* mechanically coupled to wheels; smaller EM; e.g., Honda Civic IMA. [✓]
> - **Series-Parallel:** Combines both paths; uses a planetary gear set as Power-Split Device; e.g., Toyota Prius THS. [✓]
> - **Willis Equation (must memorize):** $\omega_S \cdot N_R + \omega_R \cdot N_S = \omega_C \cdot (N_R + N_S)$. [✓]
> - **In THS:** Carrier = ICE, Sun = MG1 (Generator), Ring = Wheels + MG2. [✓]
> - **Series path efficiency formula:** $\eta_{series} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_G \cdot \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{TM} \cdot \eta_{trans}$. [✓]
> - **SOC operating window:** 30% to 80% (typical). [✓]
> - **Key standard values:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $Q_{LHV,gasoline} = 43 \text{ MJ/kg}$. [✓]
> - **Real-world examples to mention in exam answers:** Toyota Prius (SP), Honda Civic IMA (P), Nissan e-Power / BMW i3 REx (S). [✓]
> - **Operating modes to know for each topology:** Pure Electric, ICE-Only, Combined, Regenerative Braking. [✓]
> - **Engine downsizing + load leveling** is the key benefit of series topology. [✓]
> - **Negative generator speed** in THS enables the CVT-like effect. [✓]
> - **Two electric machines** in series-parallel; **one** in series or parallel. [✓]

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT411 - ALTERNATIVE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Trains** refers to the systematic regulation, distribution, and management of mechanical and electrical power among multiple energy sources (Internal Combustion Engine, Battery, Ultra-Capacitor, Fuel Cell) and propulsion components (Electric Machines, Transmission, Wheels) to meet driver demand while optimizing fuel economy, emissions, component longevity, and State of Charge (SOC) of the energy storage system.

In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMUT411), this encompasses three canonical hybrid-electric vehicle (HEV) configurations:

- **Series Hybrid** — ICE drives a generator only; traction is exclusively electric.
- **Parallel Hybrid** — ICE and EM mechanically coupled to wheels; both can propel directly.
- **Series-Parallel (Power-Split / Complex)** — combines both; uses a planetary gear set (e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive).

The **Control Strategy / Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** is the brain that decides, at every instant, *how much* power flows from the ICE, the battery, or both, to satisfy the road load demand $P_{load}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Pipeline Network

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine your home receiving water from two sources — a municipal pipe (steady, slow) and a rooftop tank (fast, but limited). A smart controller opens/closes valves in real-time: when you wash dishes (low demand), use municipal supply; when you fill a bathtub (high demand), open both. The **municipal pipe = ICE (steady efficient operation)**, the **rooftank = Battery (instant peak power)**, and the **smart controller = Energy Management System (EMS)**. The *goal* is to never let the tank overflow (over-charge) or run dry (over-discharge), while keeping the municipal pipe flowing at its most efficient rate.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **State of Charge (SOC):** typically maintained between **0.3 and 0.8** for battery longevity.
- **Specific Energy of Li-ion:** ≈ **150–250 Wh/kg**.
- **ICE Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) minimum:** ≈ **220 g/kWh** (diesel), **250 g/kWh** (gasoline).
- **Typical System Voltage in HEV:** **200 V – 800 V** (high-voltage bus).
- **Regenerative Braking Efficiency:** **60% – 80%** of kinetic energy recovered in urban cycles.

### 1.4 Why Power Flow Control Matters

| Performance Metric | Without EMS | With Optimized EMS |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel Economy | Baseline | **+20% to +40%** |
| CO₂ Emissions | Baseline | **−25% to −45%** |
| Battery Cycle Life | Irregular SOC swing | **Doubled** (sustained SOC band) |
| ICE Efficiency | Operates across map | Confined to **BSFC sweet spot** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU module treats EMS as the *decisive subsystem* — the *physical* hybrid topology defines the *possibility* of power paths, but the *control algorithm* defines the *quality* of hybridization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Topologies — Operational Logic

### A. Series Hybrid Drive-Train

**Operating Principle:** The ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. It drives a generator (G) producing electricity that either charges the battery or feeds the Traction Motor (TM).

**Power Flow Equation:**

$$P_{load} = P_{TM} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{G} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{batt/dis} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{TM}$$

**Why use it:**
- ICE can operate at *any* speed/power — independent of road speed.
- Ideal for **city buses, garbage trucks** (stop-go, no long highway runs).
- **Drawback:** Three energy conversions (mech → elec → mech → mech), so cumulative losses are higher.

### B. Parallel Hybrid Drive-Train

**Operating Principle:** ICE and Electric Machine (EM) are **mechanically coupled** (via clutch/gear) to the driveshaft. Both can propel the vehicle *simultaneously* or *alternately*.

**Power Flow Equation:**

$$P_{load} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{tr} + P_{EM} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{tr}$$

**Why use it:**
- Higher overall efficiency on highways (mechanical path is direct).
- **Drawback:** Complex clutch/gear synchronization; ICE speed is tied to vehicle speed.

### C. Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid

**Operating Principle:** Uses a **planetary gear set (PGS)** as a power split device. The ICE torque is split into a *mechanical path* (to wheels via ring gear) and an *electrical path* (via generator → battery → motor).

**Power Flow Equations (PGS Kinematics):**

$$\omega_{sun} \cdot S + \omega_{ring} \cdot R = \omega_{carrier} \cdot (S + R)$$

where S = sun gear teeth, R = ring gear teeth, carrier = ICE.

**Why use it:** Combines the *efficiency of parallel* on highway with the *flexibility of series* in city. Used in **Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid, Ford Escape Hybrid**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{load}(t) = F_{trac}(t) \cdot v(t)$ | Instantaneous road load power | W; $F_{trac}$ in N, $v$ in m/s |
| 2 | $F_{trac} = m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta \tfrac{dv}{dt}$ | Tractive force (rolling + aero + grade + inertia) | N |
| 3 | $P_{ICE,opt} = \arg\min BSFC(\omega_{ICE}, T_{ICE})$ | Optimal ICE operating point | g/kWh |
| 4 | $\Delta SOC = \dfrac{-\int \eta_{batt} \cdot I_{batt} \cdot V_{batt} \, dt}{Q_{batt}}$ | SOC evolution | dimensionless; $Q$ in As |
| 5 | $P_{batt,max,disch} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{max} - I_{max}^2 \cdot R_{int}$ | Max battery discharge power | W |
| 6 | $P_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Recovered braking energy | W |
| 7 | $T_{out} = (1+k) \cdot T_{sun} = (1+k) k \cdot T_{ring}$ | PGS torque relations (PGS ratio $k = S/R$) | N·m |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{P_{fuel,chem}}$ | Well-to-wheel efficiency | % |
| 9 | $ECMS: \dot{m}_f^{eq} = \dot{m}_{f,ICE} + s \cdot \dfrac{P_{batt}}{Q_{LHV}}$ | Equivalent fuel rate (s = equivalence factor) | g/s |
| 10 | $P_{ICE,min} = \dfrac{P_{load}}{\eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mot}}$ | Series-mode min ICE power | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions (always state in answers):** SOC ∈ [0.3, 0.8], $\eta_{mech} \in [0.92, 0.97]$, $s \in [2.5, 3.5]$ for ECMS, $k_{PGS} \in [2.5, 3.0]$ for Toyota THS.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEM calibration:** Engineers use these equations to size the battery, EM, and ICE in MATLAB/Simulink ADVISOR or Autonomie.
- **Grid/utility hybrids:** Same logic applies to hybrid locomotives, ship propulsion, and hybrid mining trucks (e.g., Caterpillar 793F).
- **Policy:** Power flow control directly determines the **CO₂ fleet-average** a manufacturer reports for compliance with CAFE / Bharat Stage VI norms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Tractive Force to Required Hybrid Power

We start with the **vehicle longitudinal dynamics equation** and build up to the hybrid power management problem.

### Step 1 — Tractive Force Demand

The vehicle must overcome four resistance forces. Newton's second law on a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at speed $v$ on grade $\alpha$:

$$F_{trac}(t) = m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha + m \cdot \delta \frac{dv}{dt}$$

where:
- $f_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient (≈ 0.012 for car)
- $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³**)
- $C_d A$ = drag area (≈ 0.68 m² for sedan)
- $\delta$ = mass factor (≈ 1.05 for ICE cars)

**Logic:** First term = rolling losses, second = aerodynamic drag, third = hill climbing, fourth = inertia during acceleration.

### Step 2 — Instantaneous Power Demand

Multiply both sides by instantaneous velocity $v(t)$:

$$P_{load}(t) = F_{trac}(t) \cdot v(t)$$

At low speed (e.g., 30 km/h), $P_{load}$ is dominated by rolling + inertia; at highway (100 km/h), aerodynamic term dominates (since $P_{aero} \propto v^3$).

### Step 3 — Power Balance in a Parallel Hybrid

For a parallel topology, the power to the wheels is the sum of ICE and EM paths:

$$P_{load} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{tr} + P_{EM} \cdot \eta_{em} \cdot \eta_{tr}$$

Let the *split factor* $u(t) \in [0,1]$ denote the fraction of load handled by the ICE:

$$P_{ICE}(t) = u(t) \cdot \frac{P_{load}(t)}{\eta_{tr}}$$

$$P_{EM}(t) = [1 - u(t)] \cdot \frac{P_{load}(t)}{\eta_{tr} \cdot \eta_{em}}$$

**Logic:** $u = 1$ → pure ICE; $u = 0$ → pure EV; $u = 0.5$ → equal split.

### Step 4 — SOC Evolution from Battery Power

Battery current, in terms of battery power $P_{batt}$, open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$, and internal resistance $R_{int}$:

$$P_{batt} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{batt} - I_{batt}^2 \cdot R_{int}$$

Solve quadratic for $I_{batt}$:

$$I_{batt} = \frac{V_{oc} - \sqrt{V_{oc}^2 - 4 R_{int} P_{batt}}}{2 R_{int}}$$

Then SOC update over time step $\Delta t$:

$$SOC(t+\Delta t) = SOC(t) - \frac{\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} I_{batt} \cdot dt}{Q_{batt}}$$

### Step 3.5 — Full Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg, $f_r = 0.012$, $C_d A = 0.7$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\alpha = 0$, $v = 90$ km/h = 25 m/s, $dv/dt = 0.5$ m/s², $\delta = 1.05$. Find required hybrid power and optimal ICE/EM split.

**Step 5.1 — Compute Tractive Force:**

$$F_{roll} = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot \cos 0 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 25^2 = 267.97 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{grade} = 0 \text{ (flat road)}$$

$$F_{inertia} = 1500 \cdot 1.05 \cdot 0.5 = 787.5 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{trac} = 176.58 + 267.97 + 0 + 787.5 = 1232.05 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5.2 — Compute Load Power:**

$$P_{load} = 1232.05 \cdot 25 = 30{,}801.25 \text{ W} \approx 30.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5.3 — Optimal Split using BSFC principle:**

Suppose ICE best BSFC occurs at 25 kW, EM efficiency 0.92, transmission 0.95. Choose $u = 25/30.8 \approx 0.812$.

$$P_{ICE} = 0.812 \cdot 30.8/0.95 = 26.3 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{EM} = 0.188 \cdot 30.8/(0.95 \cdot 0.92) = 6.7 \text{ kW (assist)}$$

**Step 5.4 — Battery Energy Delivered in 10 s:**

$$\Delta E_{batt} = 6.7 \cdot 10 / 0.95 = 70.5 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\Delta SOC = \frac{70{,}500}{Q_{batt} \cdot V_{nom}} = \text{(use 5 kWh pack)} = \frac{70{,}500}{18{,}000{,}000} = 0.00392$$

$$SOC_{new} = 0.65 - 0.00392 = 0.6461$$

## 3.2 Derivation: ECMS (Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy)

The instantaneous cost function is:

$$J_{ECMS}(t) = \dot{m}_{f,ICE}(t) + s(t) \cdot \frac{P_{batt}(t)}{Q_{LHV}}$$

The optimal control is:

$$u^*(t) = \arg\min_{u} J_{ECMS}(t)$$

where the **equivalence factor** $s(t)$ adapts to keep SOC near a target:

$$s(t) = s_0 \cdot \left[1 - k_p \left(SOC(t) - SOC_{ref}\right) - k_i \int (SOC - SOC_{ref}) dt\right]$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hybrid Topology Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies] --> B[Series HEV]
    A --> C[Parallel HEV]
    A --> D[Series-Parallel Power-Split HEV]

    B --> B1[ICE drives Generator only]
    B1 --> B2[Electricity charges Battery]
    B2 --> B3[Traction Motor drives Wheels]
    B3 --> B4[No mechanical link ICE-Wheels]

    C --> C1[ICE and EM coupled mechanically]
    C1 --> C2[Clutch engages ICE when needed]
    C2 --> C3[Both propel via Transmission]
    C3 --> C4[Simpler structure]

    D --> D1[Planetary Gear Set as Power Split]
    D1 --> D2[ICE on Carrier]
    D2 --> D3[Sun gear to Generator MG1]
    D3 --> D4[Ring gear to Wheels and MG2]
    D4 --> D5[Continuous variable transmission behavior]
```

## 4.2 Power Flow Control — Centralized EMS Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    driver[Driver Input Pedal Brake] --> vcu[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    socS[State of Charge Sensor] --> vcu
    speedS[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> vcu

    vcu --> ems[Energy Management Strategy EMS]
    ems --> iceC[ICE Controller Throttle]
    ems --> emC[EM Controller Torque]
    ems --> battC[Battery Controller Power Limit]
    ems --> regC[Regen Brake Controller]

    iceC --> iceU[ICE Unit]
    emC --> emU[Electric Machine Unit]
    battC --> battU[Battery Pack]
    regC --> wheels[Drive Wheels]

    iceU --> trans[Transmission]
    emU --> trans
    trans --> wheels

    wheels --> fbS[Feedback Speed and Torque]
    fbS --> vcu
```

## 4.3 Energy Management Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[EMS Family] --> R1[Rule Based Strategies]
    R --> R2[Optimization Based Strategies]
    R --> R3[Learning Based Strategies]

    R1 --> R1A[Thermostat On Off Control]
    R1 --> R1B[Power Follower Control]
    R1 --> R1C[State Machine Control]

    R2 --> R2A[Equivalent Consumption Minimization ECMS]
    R2 --> R2B[Adaptive ECMS]
    R2 --> R2C[Dynamic Programming DP]
    R2 --> R2D[Pontryagin Minimum Principle PMP]

    R3 --> R3A[Neural Network Based]
    R3 --> R3B[Reinforcement Learning Q Learning]
    R3 --> R3C[Fuzzy Logic Control]

    R1A --> app1[Low cost simple implementation]
    R2A --> app2[Real time and near optimal]
    R2C --> app3[Global optimum benchmark only offline]
    R3B --> app4[Adaptive to driver style]
```

## 4.4 Series Hybrid Detailed Power Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    fuel[Fuel Tank] --> ice[Internal Combustion Engine]
    ice --> gen[Generator G]
    gen --> rect[AC DC Converter]
    rect --> dcBus[DC Bus High Voltage]
    battU2[Battery Pack] --> dcBus
    dcBus --> inv[DC AC Inverter]
    inv --> tm[Traction Motor TM]
    tm --> gearbox[Gearbox]
    gearbox --> wheelsU[Wheels]
    wheelsU --> fb2[Kinetic Energy Recovery]
    fb2 --> gen2[TM as Generator]
    gen2 --> dcBus
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In a **series** topology, the ICE never touches the wheels — all propulsion is electrical. The generator rectifies ICE mechanical power into DC, which either charges the battery or directly feeds the traction motor. This is why the ICE can run at its *optimal BSFC point* regardless of vehicle speed.

## 4.5 Power-Split (Series-Parallel) — Planetary Gear Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    icePS[ICE] --> carrier[Planetary Carrier]
    carrier --> sun[Sun Gear]
    sun --> mg1[MG1 Generator]
    carrier --> ring[Ring Gear]
    ring --> chain[Chain or Direct to Wheels]
    ring --> mg2[MG2 Traction Motor]
    mg2 --> wheelsPS[Wheels]
    mg1 --> battPS[Battery via Inverter]
    battPS --> mg2

    sun --> kinem[Kinematics omega_sun k equals omega_ring]
    kinem --> split[Power split mechanical and electrical path]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel hybrid drive-trains with respect to mechanical coupling and typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Topology | Mechanical Link ICE–Wheels | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Series** | None (fully decoupled) | City buses, garbage trucks, range-extender EVs |
| **Parallel** | Yes (via clutch & transmission) | Light-duty passenger cars, mild hybrids |
| **Series-Parallel** | Partial (via PGS, both paths) | Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid, full hybrids |

**Awarding logic (3 marks):** 1 mark each for correctly identifying the coupling nature and providing one valid application.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the Energy Management Strategy (EMS) in a hybrid electric vehicle? List two EMS techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** is the supervisory control algorithm that determines, in real-time, the **optimal split of power demand** between the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and the Electric Machine (EM) to minimize fuel consumption and emissions while maintaining the battery State of Charge (SOC) within its safe operating window.

**Two EMS techniques:**
1. **Rule-based (Thermostat / On-Off) Control** — switches ICE on/off based on SOC threshold.
2. **Optimization-based (ECMS — Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy)** — minimizes an instantaneous cost function combining fuel rate and equivalent fuel cost of electrical energy.

(2 marks for EMS role + 1 mark for techniques.)

---

## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Power Flow & EMS Analytical

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **power flow architecture of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Derive the overall powertrain efficiency expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel hybrid car of mass **1400 kg** climbs a **5% grade** at constant **60 km/h**. Given $f_r = 0.013$, $C_d A = 0.65$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\eta_{tr} = 0.95$, $\eta_{em} = 0.92$, $\eta_{ICE} = 0.35$. Compute: (i) total tractive force, (ii) road-load power, (iii) fuel power if the ICE supplies 80% of mechanical demand, (iv) required battery power, and (v) the equivalent fuel rate using ECMS equivalence factor $s = 3.0$ and $Q_{LHV} = 43$ MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Diagram (draw in answer sheet — 3 marks):**

```
Fuel Tank → ICE → Generator G → Rectifier → DC Bus ←→ Battery
                                                       ↓
                                                  Inverter
                                                       ↓
                                              Traction Motor TM
                                                       ↓
                                                  Gearbox → Wheels
                                                  ↑ (Regen)
```

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The chain of energy conversions in series mode has three stages:

1. ICE → Generator: efficiency $\eta_{ICE-gen} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_G$
2. Generator → DC Bus (via rectifier): $\eta_{rect}$
3. DC Bus → Traction Motor (via inverter + motor): $\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{TM}$

Mechanical output at wheels:

$$P_{wheels} = P_{fuel} \cdot \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_G \cdot \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{TM}$$

Therefore, the overall efficiency is:

$$\eta_{overall,series} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_G \cdot \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{TM}$$

Plug in typical values: $0.35 \cdot 0.92 \cdot 0.97 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.90 = 0.265$ → **26.5%**.

**Application (1 mark):** Ideal for stop-go duty cycles (urban buses) where the ICE can be sized for average load and always run at its BSFC sweet spot.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $m = 1400$ kg, grade $\alpha$ where $\tan\alpha = 0.05 \Rightarrow \alpha = 2.86°$, $v = 60$ km/h = **16.67 m/s**, $f_r = 0.013$, $C_d A = 0.65$ m², $\eta_{tr} = 0.95$, $\eta_{em} = 0.92$, $\eta_{ICE} = 0.35$, ICE share = 80%.

**(i) Tractive Force:**

$$F_{roll} = 1400 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.013 = 178.5 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 16.67^2 = 110.6 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{grade} = 1400 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.05 = 686.7 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{inertia} = 0 \text{ (constant speed)}$$

$$\boxed{F_{trac} = 178.5 + 110.6 + 686.7 = 975.8 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Road-load Power:**

$$P_{load} = F_{trac} \cdot v = 975.8 \cdot 16.67 = 16{,}265 \text{ W} \approx 16.27 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Fuel Power (ICE supplies 80% of mechanical demand):**

Mechanical power from ICE: $P_{ICE,mech} = 0.80 \cdot 16.27 / 0.95 = 13.70$ kW

Fuel power input:

$$P_{fuel} = \frac{P_{ICE,mech}}{\eta_{ICE}} = \frac{13.70}{0.35} = 39.14 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Battery Power:**

Mechanical demand from EM: $P_{EM,mech} = 0.20 \cdot 16.27 / 0.95 = 3.42$ kW

Battery output (before EM efficiency):

$$P_{batt} = \frac{P_{EM,mech}}{\eta_{em}} = \frac{3.42}{0.92} = 3.72 \text{ kW}$$

**(v) Equivalent Fuel Rate via ECMS:**

Fuel rate from ICE: $\dot{m}_{f,ICE} = P_{fuel}/Q_{LHV} = 39{,}140 / 43{,}000{,}000 = 9.10 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s = **0.91 g/s**

Equivalent electrical fuel rate: $s \cdot P_{batt}/Q_{LHV} = 3.0 \cdot 3720 / 43{,}000{,}000 = 2.60 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s = **0.26 g/s**

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_f^{eq} = 0.91 + 0.26 = 1.17 \text{ g/s}}$$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Topologies & Control Strategies

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the **rule-based** and **optimization-based** Energy Management Strategies. Explain with a flowchart how the **Thermostat (On/Off) controller** operates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **planetary gear set** in a Toyota-style power-split hybrid, derive the kinematic and torque relations. State the advantages of this topology. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Rule-Based | Optimization-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Optimality | Sub-optimal | Near-optimal / Optimal |
| Computation | Real-time, simple | ECMS real-time; DP offline |
| Knowledge of drive cycle | Not required (ECMS, Adaptive) | Required for DP, PMP |
| Implementation | Easy | Complex |
| Examples | Thermostat, Power follower | ECMS, DP, Genetic Algorithm |

**Thermostat (On-Off) Controller Flowchart (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start of Control Loop] --> B[Read SOC from BMS]
    B --> C{SOC less than SOC_low}
    C -- Yes --> D[Turn ICE ON]
    C -- No --> E{SOC greater than SOC_high}
    E -- Yes --> F[Turn ICE OFF]
    E -- No --> G[Maintain previous ICE state]
    D --> H[Charge battery until SOC_high]
    F --> I[Run on electric only]
    H --> A
    I --> A
    G --> A
```

**Logic (1 mark):** If SOC drops below 0.3, ICE turns ON and charges; once SOC reaches 0.8, ICE turns OFF; in the dead-band [0.3, 0.8], no switching occurs. This is simple but causes **hunting** (frequent on/off cycling near thresholds) and ignores road-load.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Planetary Gear Set Diagram (textual — 2 marks):**

```
                  Sun (S teeth)
                    *
                    |
                    |  MG1 (Generator)
                    |
       *-----*-----*  ← Carrier (connected to ICE)
      Ring            |
       *-----*-----*  ← Ring (R teeth) → MG2 / Wheels
```

**Kinematic Relation (Willis Equation) (2 marks):**

For a planetary gear set with sun, ring, and carrier:

$$\frac{\omega_{sun} - \omega_{carrier}}{\omega_{ring} - \omega_{carrier}} = -\frac{R}{S} = -k$$

where $k = R/S$ is the *PGS ratio* (typically 2.5–3.0 in Toyota THS).

**Torque Relation (1 mark):**

$$T_{carrier} = T_{sun} \cdot (1 + k) = -T_{ring} \cdot (1 + 1/k)$$

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Continuous variable transmission (CVT) behavior** — ICE speed decoupled from wheel speed.
2. **Simultaneous series and parallel modes** — best of both worlds.
3. **Regenerative braking** captured efficiently via MG2.
4. **No clutch required** between ICE and driveline.
5. **Compact** — single PGS replaces multi-gear transmission.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (loses 0.5–1 mark per question).
> 2. **Not stating boundary conditions** for SOC (always mention 0.3–0.8 range).
> 3. **Confusing series vs parallel** — the *decoupling* of ICE from wheels is the **single most important** distinguishing feature; examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts — KTU mandates a block diagram for full marks.
> 5. **Writing the ECMS formula without defining the equivalence factor $s$** — full mark only if you state its typical range (2.5–3.5).
> 6. **Using `tan α` directly without clarification** — always convert grade % to radians if computing exact trigonometric terms.
> 7. **Ignoring transmission efficiency** $\eta_{tr}$ — many students compute $P_{ICE} = P_{load}$ which is wrong; the load is at the wheels, not at the engine flywheel.

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three topologies:** Series (decoupled, urban), Parallel (coupled, highway-efficient), Series-Parallel (PGS-based, CVT-like).
- ✅ **EMS is the brain** of the HEV — defines fuel economy more than the hardware.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{inertia}$ — recall all four terms.
- ✅ **Power balance:** $P_{load} = P_{ICE}\eta_{tr} + P_{EM}\eta_{em}\eta_{tr}$ (parallel) — know this cold.
- ✅ **SOC band:** Always maintain **0.3 ≤ SOC ≤ 0.8** to preserve battery life.
- ✅ **ECMS cost function:** $\dot{m}_f^{eq} = \dot{m}_{f,ICE} + s \cdot P_{batt}/Q_{LHV}$ — equivalence factor $s$ adapts via PI control on SOC.
- ✅ **PGS Kinematics:** $\omega_{sun}/\omega_{ring} = -R/S$ — sign convention: sun and ring rotate in opposite directions relative to carrier.
- ✅ **Regenerative braking** can recover up to **60–80%** of kinetic energy in urban cycles.
- ✅ **Typical efficiencies:** ICE ≈ 35%, EM ≈ 90–95%, Gen ≈ 90–95%, Inverter ≈ 95–97%, Battery round-trip ≈ 85–90%.
- ✅ **Series HEV overall efficiency** ≈ 25–28%; **Parallel HEV overall efficiency** ≈ 30–35%; **Power-Split HEV** ≈ 35–40%.
- ✅ **Rule-based EMS** is simple and real-time but sub-optimal; **ECMS** is real-time near-optimal; **DP** is globally optimal but offline.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always draw the block diagram *first* before writing derivations in 7-mark questions — KTU examiners allot 2–3 marks specifically for the diagram.
- ✅ **Penalty avoidance:** Use $\eta_{tr}$ explicitly; never assume $P_{ICE} = P_{load}$.
